Name: Inception
Review: Inception was an original film amidst a time period of dominating sequels and franchises, making it a truly exceptional film, especially during it's initial release. It has some of the best cinematography seen in any movie this millennium, combining witty dialogue, fun action, psychological thrills, dazzling CGI, excellent acting, and compelling characters with heart to go alongside with their intelligence into one, long sci-fi masterpiece that remains one of my favorite movies of the decade. I feel some of the plot was too driven by personal issues, such as Cobb's (Leo DiCaprio) situation with his wife, but that is the only minor flaw I can think of.

Name: Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace
Review:
This is the clear black sheep of the entire Star Wars franchise. The first in a new trilogy of the classic series disappoints and pleases many for various reasons, and it is often overlooked when Star Wars is talked about in any situation. As Obi-Wan says as the very beginning of the movie, "I have a bad feeling about this."

What I see as the main problem with this movie is the plot. It isn't memorable or interesting at all, thanks to the majority of it being taken up by negotiating with the Trade Federation, being every once in a while punctuated by a battle scene or something of the like. It seems to be endlessly setting up different subplots and introducing new characters until the climax at the very end, which is an epic finale, but the other three-quarters of the movie are going to bore the audience.

Another major flaw with this movie is it's dialogue. There are some memorable quotes (such as "there's always a bigger fish," "I don't care what universe you're from; that's gotta hurt," etc.) but it mostly is there to either advance the plot or introduce characters, and isn't engaging or witty in any way. The quotes do make up for it, at the very least, but it really feels like something is missing from it. It just doesn't flow well with the normal style we're used to knowing in Star Wars movies.

Speaking of introducing characters, that is one thing I think The Phantom Menace does well. Whether the characters were likeable or not (as said by C-3PO, "I find that Jar Jar creature to be a little odd."), the audience is given a good sense of who they are, what side they are on and how they help make the movie. It is also great to see the origins of some beloved characters seen in later movies, such as R2-D2 and Obi-Wan. By the end of the film, I felt like I knew all the main characters very well, and how they might take part in Attack of the Clones.

The other main thing that this movie does very well is the visuals and cinematography. The Phantom Menace was nominated for 3 Oscars; sound, sound editing, and visual effects. And when watching the movie, it isn't hard to see why. Everything looks so realistic and believable, from the city of Naboo to all the ships and transports used in battles, and especially at the time of release, the CGI was especially outstanding. Those battles at the end are both visually stunning and excellent fun, and the podrace is heartstopping. If there's one thing that the Star Wars movies all excel at, this is it.

Overall, I don't think The Phantom Menace is a bad movie, thanks to it's characters and excellent visual effects. Plus, without it, we would never have the new Star Wars movies we've seen today and in the past few years. However, the plot and dialogue leave a lot to be desired, and could have definitely been improved and had more time put into it during production. Thankfully, in Star Wars today, those aspects are done much better. It shows how much the Star Wars universe has grown and improved over the years.

Name: Avengers: Endgame
Review:
This is the biggest movie of all time, taking over Avatar's nearly decade-long record, so a simple review will not be able to cover everything that takes place in this insane franchise finale.

First off, yes, this is a _great_ movie. So much is packed into the three hour runtime that is to be discussed, but I'll break it down into smaller bits. Getting the slight negatives out of the way, it starts off a little slow; a heartbreaking opening scene which we see Hawkeye's family turn to dust and Iron Man film a depressing message for Pepper, assuming she ever gets it. But thankfully the action gets up and running quickly.

The only other slight negatives don't even knock against the movie itself, they just have to do with the audience's emotions. This can be an very stressful and, for many, very emotional film. We see a few beloved characters die (including Black Widow and, the big one, Iron Man) but they died with honor, and without their brave sacrifice the movie just wouldn't be the same. There is also a happy-tear ending to the film, which we see how Captain America fulfills a promise given to Tony years ago, in honor of his passing.

Now onto the positives. As expected, the characters are extremely well known and the great actors that play them continue to entertain us and we see all of them in their finest hour at different points throughout the film. This is partially thanks to a masterfully written script, being witty and full of memorable quotes, engaging, and best of all, very humorous. While not the film's main selling point, as with a few Marvel movies in the past, this movie packs in quite a few moments where I found myself laughing pretty hard. So that's something else to look forward to.

Something that people were concerned about with this movie, especially after Infinity War, was how good the plot of the movie would be. It isn't the best plot of the Marvel Cinematic Universe movies, but it isn't bad either and gets the job done. This movie incorporates a lot of time travel into it, so thanks to a lot of time jumps through several points in MCU history, it may be confusing to some, so while this could be seen as a flaw, my advice would be to make sure that nothing is distracting you and you give the movie your full undivided attention. Then you should understand it well enough.

Lastly, of course, I have to give a nod to the visual beauty of this movie. This is to be completely expected of Marvel, and sometimes people can take this for granted, which just shows how realism in movies has become a permanent part of Hollywood. Since all Marvel movies have amazing effects and cinematography, I'm not going to bother talking any more about it, other than it really comes in hand with some epic battles, including the final final battle which involves everyone (and I MEAN EVERYONE) and will have anyone that has seen the Marvel movies totally geeking out. You're truly in for a treat.

Avengers: Endgame has built up a very divided reputation since release, with some claiming it to be the best Marvel movie and one of the best movies ever, while some rank it lower in the MCU. I'm happy to say that I'd rank it towards the top; the Russo brothers knocked it out of the park with this one, and when looking back, aside from some minor flaws, this is a truly excellent movie, and a masterpiece. It deserves to be seen by everyone with even a passing interest in doing so.

Name: Avengers: Infinity War
Review:
Infinity War is the movie every Marvel fan has been waiting for. 10 years earlier, Iron Man was released, kicking off the whole Marvel Cinematic Universe that we know and love today. It's been constantly growing and becoming a more prominent part of what nerds look forward to each year. And this is film is part 1 of the franchise's grand finale. Did it live up to the hype?

Well, to answer simply, yes and no. I think it's a well done movie, in the sense that those who made it did everything they needed to, but I wouldn't quite call it great, on terms of how much the audience will enjoy it. As expected, many beloved characters die, and while many people are going to hate to see a lot of them go, their deaths at least have a reason in the story. I'm talking more about what the characters do to cause the deaths. Many of them go annoyingly far in situations, specifically to the point where we start to like them less, and sometimes their stupid decisions have a much bigger impact than initially thought. The other major thing that will lower the audience's overall view of the movie is the ending which, without spoiling anything, will upset the audience in many ways, being a very negative way to end. Be prepared for that.

So while those negatives are hard to overlook, bumping down my rating to a 3.5, there are, of course, many positives to boot as well. The ending may suck, but the opening is chilling and exciting, kicking things off in spectacular fashion, despite being very dark overall (minor spoiler: there are some deaths right at the start). So while it isn't positive, things jump into action very quickly, which is why I think it's great. And, of course, the characters are very well known and loved by many, all being played by excellent actors, which is always a plus and, in my opinion, the greatest strength in every Marvel movie.

The plot is fairly straightforward, although because it highlights every major superhero, there are several subplots that are happening at the same time, which can be confusing to some, but it isn't terribly hard to follow along with each one. The build-up to the ending battle with Thanos is very dramatic and suspenseful, which was done very well. Along with the plot, as with most Marvel movies, the script is very well done, being witty, entertaining, and full of memorable quotes and a lot of humor that is always sure to keep the audience engaged.

And lastly, of course, the action and stunts are excellently done, coupled with stunning CGI that make Marvel movies some of the best looking, most believable movies in Hollywood today. The cinematography is overall amazing, and I don't feel the need to get into many details, because if you've seen the movie, you know what I'm talking about.

Avengers: Infinity War is a good movie, with many strong points, but because of some major flaws as listed above, I think that Infinity War didn't quite live up to the hype for me. It seems to serve more as a setup to Endgame rather than it's own film, and because of that structure, it doesn't work as well as it might have. But the positives mentioned definitely make it worth watching, especially as one of the major finales of the MCU. Fans will be satisfied with many references to previous movies and cameos as well.

Name: Roma
Review:
Roma is very sophisticated film. It was nominated for a whopping 10 Oscars last year, and now that I've had time to sit down and think about I'm starting to see why. At first, I wasn't a huge fan of the movie, and wondered why it received all it's praise, but once you realize the story behind why Roma was made, and how the plot came to be, it all starts to make sense and you see why Oscars were shoved down its throat.

The first thing I said about Roma is how sophisticated it is, and when breaking down everything in this movie, one realizes how complex it is, and to fully understand it's beauty it requires more than one watching. But to keep things short, I'll break it down by each Oscar category that it was nominated for.

Cinematography - This was Roma's first Oscar win, and it's simple to see why. The greyscale screen amplifies the sense of being in Mexico City in the early 70s, and in a way reveals the environment the family had to live with (one which is not as wealthy). The angles it was filmed at manages to keep the main focus on Cleo and the children along with everything going on in the city around them. Director Alfonso Cuarón based his film on a personal experience, as a love letter to someone who watched over him as a child, and I believe he amplified the sense of his living conditions to the best he possibly could have.

Production Design - As said in Cinematography, the filming angles give us a great view of how Cleo and the family she was staying with fit into Mexico City's society during this time, but what really gives us this view is the spectacularly made Mexico City itself. The rundown buildings, old cars and overall mood conveyed by the storytelling doesn't just show us what it was like for Alfonso Cuarón and his family in 1971; it takes us back there to experience it for ourselves. Not many movies can take this journey with their audience, but Roma succeeds in doing so.

Sound Mixing/Editing - While I'm not sure if this part of the movie is Oscar movie, there are some cool effects done with the audio. Some scenes, specifically when we are introduced to the father in his car, the sound moved between each ear on my headphones as I watched, which, in a strange way, engages us in the specific location of the camera, part of the film's immersion style. Otherwise, every sound effect is super realistic which is always good, but I don't know if it's necessarily Oscar nominee material.

Original Screenplay - This was an incredibly well-written film. Of course, I only really heard the movie through the subtitles because it's a Spanish film, but the subtitles still meant that I could know every word that was being said. It's so intelligently written, with humor, heart and power, and really is the main reason why I loved this movie. Whether you're hearing Cleo talk to the children in her care, or if she's talking to her boyfriend Fermin, the emotion and skill put into every word is very clear to the audience, in the context of the situation each character is put in (if that makes sense).

Best Actress/Supporting Actress - This is where the movie shines. Yalitza Aparicio, the actor of Cleo, more than sells the part. I fully see her as this caretaker in Mexico City in 1971. Her emotions are very real through all her struggles, mainly the whole journey through childbirth only to end in stillbirth and how she deals with Fermin's dislike of her expected baby and involvement in the El Halconazo (the large riot, which was real and resulted in 120 deaths). She connected with the children, both as actors and real people in this time, and the way she engages in the plot connects to more than the people in this film. It connects her with the audience as well. All of this adds up to a mindblowing performance, well worthy of the Oscar nomination.

Best Director - As mentioned in previous paragraphs, Alfonso Cuarón based this on a real experience, and it shows. Also previously said, this is a sophisticated movie, the vision of only a mature, experienced filmmaker. The realism and engagement with the audience is second-to-none, and every previous paragraph is a testament to how much he connected Roma to his real life experiences. He absolutely deserved this win; Cuarón did arguably his greatest work with this film.

Lastly, Best Picture - Everything mentioned so far contributes to why Roma is such a masterpiece, so I'll only cover what hasn't been said yet. The plot is so real, and while at some points it seems to be endlessly introducing characters in situations, that's the beauty of it. This is a narrative of a life experience, so everything isn't going to be action-packed, and every event in life isn't going to have a set start or finish. Everything makes sense in the end, and Cleo and the children go through a lot nonetheless, making these specific life experiences worthy of a film adaption. This is purely a beautiful movie; true art that deserved all the Oscar nominations it received. Definitely a 5 star movie that everyone should try to see.

Name: Black Panther
Review: Black Panther was a monumental superhero film. The first ever to be nominated for Best Picture. Alongside that, it also won 3 other Oscars and was nominated for 3 more, a grand total of 7 nominations. Everyone seems to really love this movie, and I have to agree, this is one of the best and one of my personal favorite Marvel movies.

As I was leaving my local theater, we drove by their display of posters, and the one for 1917 caught my attention. Across it was a large quote stating, "A 2-hour tightrope walk." This was interesting to me for two reasons. My first thought was, "That was 2 hours long?" 1917 is absolutely exhilarating, and one of the most immersive and realistic (despite being very brutal) war movies I've ever seen, to the point where I felt like the 2 hours were much shorter thanks to how fast-paced it was. But that's not a bad thing at all.
Tags: amc naperville 16, dolby, w/ owen g

Name: Joker
Review:
There's a scene about halfway through Joker where he passes under a sign that says 'Don't Forget To Smile,' which he then proceeds to scribble out the 'Forget To' so it just says 'Don't Smile.' This is reminiscent of the mood of both the Joker himself and my mood during the film. I didn't smile once through the whole 2 hours.

I don't mean the above statement in a way that I really hated the movie or anything. I'd like to describe Joker as the amazing movie that people hate watching, in the sense that it was really well done but simply isn't comfortable to sit through (for the average person). Joker was nominated for a whopping 11 Oscars, so I decided to chip in $6 to Amazon Prime so I could watch it again and give a fair review.

DC hasn't been known for making good movies the last few years, with garbage like Suicide Squad, so I'll admit that while a Joker origin story sounded interesting, as most Joker villains have been introduced quite simply in most Batman movies, I didn't have the highest hopes seeing the first trailer. An R rating got my attention, since DC movies can be very dark and violent but all have received PG-13. My first thought was 'a DC movie rated R? Maybe this is something special.' Coming to the theater to see a couple cops posted also really caught me off guard, as to just how adult this movie would be.

Another reason I had to rent it out and watch it again before reviewing it is because the film is so complex, much more than I originally expected. I really felt like a second viewing was in order to give an accurate review, and while it wasn't any more comfortable to watch the second time, it was needed for this writing.

The opening is pretty uncomfortable, getting straight away to showcasing Arthur's life, beginning with his job working as a clown actor, who gets jumped because of his appearance. Even besides random civilians, it also shows how he is treated like shit by his co-workers as well. He then goes to a therapy session, and it is both here and on his train ride home that we are introduced to his mental disorder, causing him to laugh at inappropriate times. It sets the mood of the rest of the film quite well, however, despite being a little hard to watch.

I think that while the opening is essential to describe how I feel about the movie, the rest of the plot will be described by each aspect of the film. Let's continue discussing Arthur, with character development. I don't think it's necessarily bad that the movie pretty much entirely revolves around him, though I do wish that some of the other characters were developed better. All the acting was incredible, especially in the task of being a part of the shittiness of Gotham, reflective of very good directing on the part of Todd Phillips. Despite all this, I would've loved to see some more background on characters like anchorman Murray Franklin, or his friend Sophie.

While the writing didn't do character development many favors, the script was still quite good. I feel parts of it felt a little too vanilla, but for the most part it flowed pretty well, and it really brought out some of the great acting. There are a good number of memorable quotes, especially in the second half of the film when Arthur is really into his transition into the Joker. It really takes us into the society of Gotham as if it were a real city, dealing with the problems in the movie as if they were real. Some of it's a little graphic, and takes our imagination to places we necessarily didn't want to go, but that's what the movie was intended to do.

This brings us into the set design and cinematography. Most of the movie was filmed in New York, and it is done to great effect to appear as the dirty, crime-ridden Gotham City, which is undergoing a garbage strike and a mayor who describes the lower-class as "clowns," starting several riots (SPOILER ALERT: including the large riot at the end, where gangs completely dominate the streets and all hope seems lost, including one thug who kills a young Bruce Wayne's parents. I'd also like to say that while the ending is completely insane, it's villainously majestic and the perfect way to end such a crazy film). While it's a depressing and scary perspective on society, it really takes us there, no questions asked. Despite the bleakness, it is still shot with lots of color, and is visually satisfying (except for when he's stabbing and shooting people). The sound editing/mixing is also very well done, and contributes to the Gotham immersion factor, coupled with a very well crafted soundtrack, with lots of oldies but goodies filling the lineup.

In conclusion, I'd like to restate that this movie is amazingly well done and I hate watching it, because it's hard not to turn away at quite a few points. I wouldn't call it an entertaining film by any stretch, which is the main reason why I took a star off of it's review. The technical bits of this movie are all flawless, and I agree that a lot of those aspects deserve the Oscar nominations they got, but they aren't always easy to appreciate given the incredibly dark context and themes of this movie. It's an origin story that answers a lot of questions about a well-known comic book character. Rather than a movie relating to the DC Superhero Universe, it's more of a commentary on how far we've come in dealing with mental illness and what could happen if that stopped. It's very era-appropriate, which is something I've noticed follows a theme through each Joker. Look back at Jack Nicholson's Joker in the late 80s and 90s; he resembled a terrorist, which was an issue revolving that time period. Now in the 2010s, mental illness is a pressing issue in society, and how we deal with it, including issues such as suicide and depression. I think in that aspect, the character was portrayed masterfully, thanks to the many writers and director Todd Phillips. Just aside from the discomfort and disturbance factor, this is an excellent movie.

Name: Parasite
Review:
"Making no plans at all can never be disappointing because they will never fail." This is a quote spoken by Kim Ki-taek, father of the Kim family towards the end of the film when everything seems hopeless. These words first caught my attention when I heard them in the trailer for the film, but the trailer didn't give me a great sense of what I was getting into, so when I finally got around to seeing this masterpiece, an already memorable quote became so much more of a theme.

The above paragraph may seem cliche for all movies, but the pure humbleness of Kim Ki-taek when he spoke the quote really is the moment that defines the film for me. It's a perfect example of this movie's high praise. Fantastic acting, intelligent dialogue, and excellent sound are just a mere few of several pros of Bong Joon-ho's Parasite.

Parasite tells a story of class inequality, with the Kim family, who is poor and lives in essentially what is a basement, all unemployed and struggling to find a simple wifi signal. Early on, the sense of genius writing is established, with lots of humor just in the first few minutes, and it was immediately evident to me why this movie has been nominated for best original screenplay. The movie is entirely spoken in Korean, with English subtitles, but the fact that me and several others in the theater were chuckling just based off of that shows how well written it is.

Soon after, the son of the Kim family Ki-woo is recommended by his friend Min to tutor a sophomore girl he tutored for a while he studies abroad, leading to their relations with the Park family. Min also gives him a "scholar's rock" which is supposed to bring their family wealth. To take up the job, he needs to fake being a college student, which sets up the brilliant plot point of how clever the Kim family truly is. They are amazing con artists, with the daughter photoshopping the college form, Ki-woo hiring his sister (Ki-jeong) as an art teacher for the Park's son Da-song, who she finds out is troubled with potential schizophrenia and fools them by telling them she is an "art therapist." She then removes her panties in the Parks' chauffeur, to make it look like he gets funky in Mr. Park's car, causing him to be fired, and hiring their father. They then take advantage of the family caretaker's peach allergy, causing an allergic reaction and convincing her she has TB, causing her to resign, with the mother taking up the position. It's all super entertaining and wildly fun, continuing to show the master work of Joon-ho.

Something else that shows the master work of this scene is the quality cinematography. I'm honestly a little surprised this was a category it wasn't nominated for. The perspectives and color contrast between the Kim's basement and the Park's large mansion are really well done and one of the strong points of the movie. The whole set design of the South Korean neighborhood (which we are never given the name of) is spectacularly realistic, which helps with the immersion factor which I'll mention in greater detail later. And knowing how good this film is, that's high praise. The sound quality is very crisp as well, and while it wasn't up to the quality of something like 1917 or Ford v Ferrari, I appreciate the sound mixing that was done.

The Parks family then leave on a camping trip to celebrate their son's birthday, meaning the Kim family has the house to themselves. They embrace the luxury, all getting drunk and having a good time, and while it doesn't contribute to the plot very much, it at least showcases some good acting. It all takes a strange turn, when the former housekeeper comes by to pick up something she left in the basement, only to reveal that she has been hiding her husband there for years, because they are too poor to live on their own and are hiding from "loan sharks." This is what I'd like to call the "Tarantino Twist," because it jumps into a slightly weird vibe while setting up a whole new plot point. She tries to convince Chung-sook (the Kim mother) to let it all go, because she understands what it's like to be poor, but the rest of their family accidentally falls into the basement as well, revealing the whole act they've set up. They then break into pleading each other not to reveal the other's secrets, and it gets pretty humorous, eventually leading to a mini brawl that lasts until the Parks family reveals they are returning home due to a large rainstorm. While minor, this whole reveal is the first point which we feel true suspense, as the whole situation gives off a very unsuspecting and unhinged vibe, a feeling very prominent through the rest of the movie.

This leads to what I feel is the climax, which is the two families fighting to keep their secrets and clean their messes before the Parks get home. Because the housekeeper and her wife are trying to ruin the Kim family's whole act, the Kims have to force them back into the basement by force, which unexpectedly results in the Kim mother shoving the former housekeeper down the basement stairs, which she loses her balance and hits her head on the wall. She later wakes up feeling very dizzy and falls over and dies. It also sets up her husband as the true villain in the whole story, something very fuzzy through most of the film.

After the whole situation settles down, the rest of the family has to hide, which brings us into a scene that, while uncomfortable to watch, weirdly I found to be very well done. Because their camping trip was canceled, the son blatantly takes his tent outside in the rain and sleeps their overnight, prompting the Park parents to sit on the couch, unsuspectingly inches away from where the Kims are hiding. Because both of their kids are away, the Park parents engage in, well... sexual activity. Later in the night, through walkie talkies, their son calls his father saying he can't sleep in his tent, which distracts them and allows the family to escape, only to find their street, and worse yet, their house flooded. Their plumbing is all over the place, and they barely made it out without drowning. Ki-woo brings his scholar's rock, which is going to make a prominent appearance later in the film, and not all for the good. They swim out on an old mattress with a few belongings to a shelter in a gym where they stay for the night, and Ki-woo and his father have a thoughtful conversation, where the quote from the first paragraph is said. It is here again where we have a clear, well-created contrast between the different classes.

Simply, the movie ends with the Park-Kim relationship going down in flames. The Kim father does shopping for the Park family's son's birthday party (or impromptu as they call it), and he is reminded that he is merely a servant, with no real connection. We really start to feel the emotions he is feeling, which is good and shows just how much Bong Joon-ho has immersed us into the world of the movie. As an apology for the way they were treated, at the impromptu, Ki-woo attempts to bring down the scholar's rock to give the housekeeper's husband wealth, but because of his wife's death, he has grown insane, smashing his head into buttons which turn on the lights of the house, attempting to send a morse code out to whoever may see it. Because of this, he tries to strangle Ki-woo with a cord, which causes him to drop the rock. Running up the stairs, but eventually tripping on the wire, the husband makes it up and smashes Ki-woo's head in with the rock twice, and even dropping a glass of some sort of alcoholic drink on it as well. It's very realistic, but also very brutal and super shocking. He then proceeds out to the party with a kitchen knife and stabs Ki-woo's sister in the heart, killing her. While the father tries to save her, the Park son has a seizure after seeing the housekeeper's wife, which was the cause of his schizophrenia. Instead of letting him help his dying daughter, the Park father just demands that he take his son to the hospital, prompting Mr. Kim to stab Mr. Park, another shocking but satisfying scene. The scene ends with Mrs. Kim skewering the housekeeper's husband, where Mr. Kim runs off and hides in the basement to avoid punishment. He stays down there for, what we know, indefinitely, but the movie ends with him sending a morse code to Ki-woo, who survived his head bashing thanks to brain surgery and searches for him. He plans to raise enough money and buy the house so he can free his father.

Joon-ho has spent two hours building bonds between the audience and his characters, thanks to such an intricate and intelligent story with amazing writing and dialogue. This brings me to why the movie is just so good. You can be nominated for several Oscar categories because the certain technical aspects are really good, and those are generally really great movies. But I don't remember the last time that I left a movie theater on the verge of tears. The final scene of the film, in which we see so many characters either meet their ends or let all their anger out, is so shocking to everyone who watches it, because we feel like we really know and have connected personally with the characters. When Ki-woo had his head bashed by the scholar's rock by the insane husband, I cringed and my jaw dropped, because after two hours, I thought he met his unfortunate end because he was trying to do a nice gesture. And when Ki-woo is reading off the letter from his father, even though I was only absorbing it all through the subtitles, it broke my heart to know that their father sacrificed himself for their family's wealth, and that Ki-woo sacrificed his university education and raising a family just to save him from the hidden basement. Thinking about it now as I'm writing nearly brings me to tears. Parasite has one of the largest emotional impacts I've ever felt in a movie, and therefore I'd like to go out on a limb and say it's the best movie of the year.

Name: As Above, So Below
Review:
Normally, if a female is a main character in a horror movie, she's screaming and running for her life most of the movie, with some obvious exceptions, such as Scream, Alien and Halloween. However, I'd say main character Scarlett Marlowe in As Above, So Below could belong up there with them as well. She is an archaeologist searching to learn more about her history, and does so with bravery, even in the French catacombs.

The best way for me to describe As Above, So Below is a cross between Tomb Raider and Blair Witch Project. The cheap camera footage cinematography and many jump scares combined with an adventure movie feel make for a very entertaining horror thriller. What's even better about it is that it was all filmed in the actual Paris catacombs, so a lot of what the actors are making their way through is the real thing. And instead of lots of fancy special effects, all the scares are low-budget props or scare actors, which is surprisingly effective and works well for the movie. It relies solely on that and great sound editing and acting.

The Rotten Tomatoes rating is an embarrassing 26%, saying it starts of well but descends into cliche horror. So after I was thrilled by the great opening in the Iran caves, I was worried that I would be disappointed. But thankfully, with a good cast of characters who all did very good and took their personalities to heart (including Edwin Hodge who suffered from real claustrophobia) and the unnerving unpredictability, which I don't find in most horror movies I watch these days, I was not disappointed.

The characters are all college students with not much background or introduction (other than Scarlett) but they are likeable nonetheless, with clever personalities and some good writing. While the script is just okay at best, it worked with the overall theme of the movie, with some good lines. They only entered the deep catacombs in the first place thanks to Papillon, the leader of the group who has experience in venturing the Catacombs. It started off a little weird, with a ritual of half-naked women in red and white paint and a strange phone call, but things progressively get more interesting.

I say progressively, because of a main flaw with the movie. It is paced fairly unevenly and the story starts become less and less as the scares start to come. The first half hour is mostly set up, with the next half hour beginning to kick off the last half hour, which is incredibly suspenseful and thrilling. While the last half hour is so relentless it almost makes up for it, I can't look past how there is essentially no story past all the scares.

The twist with the catacombs for the movie is that it somehow knows each character's past and reasons to feel guilt and uses it to torture them the farther down they go. The characters are haunted by a dead brother, dead father, and dead friend in a burning car, all of which lead to demises. While it's not quite clear why this is happening, there wouldn't be much horror in the movie, so I just went along with it. It supplies everything that entails the last half hour, including endless jumpscares and many brutal, graphic deaths with lots of suspense around every corner, making it seem nearly hopeless for the characters. Because of how frightening it gets, I almost feel bad for those who saw it in theaters. The audio editing is very good, which helps too.

As Above, So Below exceeds in many categories but also doesn't in many others. I love the lost-footage cinematography, characters, the fact that it was shot in the actual catacombs, and the insane final half hour. The runtime is only about 93 minutes, and the writers sure as hell packed a lot into that short duration. It was definitely well done technically. But, as said, the lack of a story at the end and uneven pace don't make it one of my favorite horror movies I've ever seen.

Name: Marriage Story
Review: I won't write a full review until a second viewing, but the acting is truly incredible, the story full of heart and humor, and the writing excellent. I was engaged the whole time, and really felt the emotion and was able to relate to every character. I was a little skeptical after seeing it the first time, but after a couple days have passed, I see the hype. This one really knocked it out of the park.

Name: Birds of Prey (and the Fantabulous Emancipation of One Harley Quinn)
Review: This was actually pretty freakin' awesome. Really, I don't have many complaints. The story may drag a bit and some parts of it may be a little unnecessary, but that's the charm of the whole thing. My favorite DC movie (sorry, Joker). It's the only one aside from maybe Wonder Woman that I actually want to see again.

Name: Batman Begins
Review: Excellent Batman movie, and the first modern comic book movie (along with The Dark Knight). There are some parts that drag a bit, and I still don't fully get why the magic blue flower was necessary in how Bruce Wayne became Batman, but otherwise I got no complaints. Very visually pleasing, entertaining story and writing, clear direction, excellent cinematography and score, and just a ton of fun from start to finish.

Name: Sonic the Hedgehog
Review: Fantastic to see Jim Carrey back as a character with similar humor and personality to The Mask, he played Dr. Robotnik really well. Sonic was pretty cute and funny as well. Otherwise, yeah it was fine. It was done in part by Warner Bros. which is probably why I felt an extremely similar vibe to all the LEGO movies, which isn't a bad thing. I enjoyed it, and there's some really good writing and pop culture references, though it is pretty forgettable and feels pretty cliche. There are some good messages in it however, along with some nice visual effects which is a saving grace. I feel like it's a 3/5 film, though I think it deserves the 3.5.
Tags: amc naperville 16, dolby, w/ owen g

Name: Knives Out
Review: Loved it when I first saw it, but damn. After seeing it today, I was somehow really blown away. Fantastic production and costume design, amazing cinematography and engaging editing, a very fitting score, excellent writing, good directing, and some pretty awesome acting. Lots of humorous bits and surprises around every corner, and the plot is very well executed. Overall a very intelligent film, which might by why I love it so much. The only critique I can think of is maybe some more character development, but compared to everything great, that's merely a nitpick.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ grandparents

Name: The Dark Knight
Review: Holy shit, where to begin? This movie is demented, non-stop, violent, thrilling, action-packed, and really will blow away every first-time viewer. I know I was when I first saw it a few years ago. Saw it again since it's on Netflix now and goddamn, I'm probably going to watch it again real soon. It's very hard to watch at times and pretty depressing, but at least it's a part of the plot, and the movie ends somewhat okay, compared to the similar-style Joker.

Name: Kill Bill: Vol. 1
Review: Hooooly shit... once again I'm blown away by how graphic yet visually well done this film is. This thing is a true work of art. Some of the best editing I've ever seen, fantastic musical selection, beautiful cinematography, super crisp sound, decent acting, great directing, love the old Chinese movie style, and just overall a very artistic film (as said earlier). The writing is a little clunky in places and the violence gets to be a little much sometimes, but it's still one of the most entertaining and fun movies I've ever seen. I have to watch it every few months or so to get my fix for a reason.

Name: Kill Bill: Vol. 2
Review: Pretty satisfying conclusion. Bill's death was pretty majestic and very well done. The first hour dragged a little bit, with some Budd boomer shit and while the Pai Mei scene was really cool, it did feel long. The writing isn't the best either, my only other main concern, except for some great one-liners and the final convo between Beatrix and Bill. Otherwise a lot more of the same, every visual aspect is excellent (production design, editing, cinematography, etc), great music selection, and very good visual effects, even if they can get gross (was stepping on the eye really necessary?)

Name: Ant-Man and the Wasp
Review: More fun than I remembered initially. I've always found this and the original Ant-Man to be a couple of the best visual Marvel movies, with all the creative and well-done shrinking/growing effects. It's very well edited as well. However, I must say that the writing isn't the best, with not many memorable quotes and it feels a bit clunky in some parts, plus the plot is fairly basic either. At the very least, though, it's entertaining and I had fun laughing a lot, so while it isn't one of the best Marvel movies I definitely enjoyed it thoroughly.

Name: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
Review: Technically speaking, this film is 5 stars and beyond. Arguably one of the most visually extensive films ever, with epic battle scenes, beautiful costume and production design, great editing, and fantastic cinematography. It still blows my mind how much was done to make this film realistic. Even beyond the visuals, the soundtrack is wonderful and the sound edited and mixed well. Not to mention solid writing and very good acting, with tons of likeable characters and familiar faces. Not every character is developed super well, which is a minor flaw I see (especially in a movie based on a book), and maybe the plot dragged a bit, but that's at least excusable because of how closely it follows the story. Overall fantastic movie, really lives up to the hype and holds a place in my heart as one of my main childhood movies.

Name: A Wrinkle in Time
Review: It's good that Disney enjoys representing equality, and the positive messages are there. But it's really confusing most of the time, and the plot is either doing too much or too little. The script is really dry as well, and the child acting was disappointing compared to most other Disney movies. Lastly, what the fuck were those costumes?

Name: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
Review: Damn

Name: The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers
Review: I was sorta tired when I watched this so I don't fully remember it all but it's very good, a lot of the same from Fellowship. This one felt a little more talky and the plot not as effective so it's not going to get the same score but I still love Battle of Helm's Deep (IMO one of the top moments in the whole franchise)

Name: Bad Taste
Review: "l think this is going a little bit over the top, Ozzy." This quote pretty much describes the whole movie. Grossest gore I've ever seen, but I have to admit they did an amazing job making it look realistic, especially for his budget. It's creative, and surprisingly well paced with lots of action each minute, but I feel like I can only rate it so high...

Name: Space Jam
Review: Always a fun time for me, one of the movies I really enjoyed growing up. Still holds up with lots of humor and cartoon fun, though the writing and acting, as expected for a family movie such as this, could've been better.

Name: The Foreigner
Review: Not very notable. Definitely very well edited and overall visually pleasing, so it's got that going for it. Though the writing isn't anything really intriguing, the plot fairly muddled, and acting talents wasted. It could've been so much more interesting if Jackie Chan used some martial arts throughout, but it was only occasionally here. It was entertaining at the least, but definitely more of a Netflix movie than anything else.

Name: GoodFellas
Review: WellregardedFellows was very good, but it's seriously #18 on the Letterboxd Top 250??

Name: Incredibles 2
Review: Excellent animation and very funny, more intelligent than the other Pixar movies, but it almost doesn't even feel like a family movie at some points.

Name: Onward
Review: Not the best Pixar but not the worst either. Disney would make a LOT of money if they made this a video game or board game. The giant Cheeto raft is my new favorite thing ever as well.

Name: The Kid Who Would Be King
Review: Fairly entertaining but pretty cliche and really long for a family movie of this style.

Name: The Interview
Review: Day 1 of my quarantine; this was for some reason one of my top choices on Netflix. Entertaining but really over the top in many places, and the pacing was really uneven. Pretty funny overall, but it tried too hard sometimes. Not a bad film, just a pretty good way to kill a couple hours. It was very strange to see Louis Huang as Kim Jong-un.

Name: Rango
Review: Fantastic and underrated. Beautiful animation, amazing music score, intelligent and memorable writing, great voice acting, very creative in the plot and attention to detail, dramatic and overall tons of fun. It can be best described as a "mature kids movie;" it's one of those addicting movies that you want to watch again right after the first time.

Name: Spenser Confidential
Review: Mark Wahlberg vs. Post Malone is one of the most poorly acted fight scenes I've ever watched.

Name: Hugo
Review: Oh my lord this film is a work of art. One of the most inspiring, intriguing and entertaining movies I've seen in a long time. As far as I saw, it didn't have any flaws, and I was smiling the whole time. If this movie doesn't make you smile, you're probably dead.

Name: GoldenEye
Review: Penis

Name: Tomorrow Never Dies
Review: The first half is a little messy and the ending is pretty cartoonish, but everything in between was better than I expected. It's still fairly relevant 23 years later and overall feels more intelligent than GoldenEye did. They both have a lot of similar qualities but I'd give this one the slight edge.

Name: Fighting with My Family
Review:
The main takeaway I got from this movie was never trust basic white girls. Though you just need to go out and live life to learn this.

It's at least a unique sports film; there aren't many cookie-cutter sports movies about wrestling. But at the same time, it's really not going to widely appeal to many people either.

Name: The Lego Movie
Review: It became one of my favorite films when I first saw it in 4th grade, still a pretty darn good movie. Very creative and funny, obviously with good animation. I also think that as the first LEGO film, they did a great job establishing what they offer. Sure the music is annoying after a while and the writing isn't the best, but overall I'm still impressed 6 years later.

Name: The World Is Not Enough
Review: I don't remember this movie and I just saw it today... It's pretty much blended in with the other three Brosnan films.

Name: Die Another Day
Review: Maybe I'm just tired of seeing all 4 Pierce Brosnan films in the last 2 days, but I was glad to finish this film. The visuals were hilariously bad, writing not as snappy as other Bond movies, and overall felt like it was lacking substance, like it followed the same formula as the last 3.

Name: Super 8
Review: One of the most visually pleasing films I've ever seen, really. You can tell this is an Abrams/Spielberg film just from that. And honestly, even outside the technical shit, this is one of the most underrated films in my opinion. The character development was fantastic, and everyone is unique in their own way. Despite being an alien film, it feels really down to earth and relevant, and everyone is going to have fun seeing it. It ends pretty stupidly and that's my main complaint, but otherwise, it's just a great time from start to finish.

Name: Casino Royale
Review: After the last two Brosnan garbage films, this is basically Parasite-level good.

Name: Raiders of the Lost Ark
Review: If you don't love this movie you're a nazi

Name: Chicken Run
Review: No chicken escapes Tweedy's Farm... why the fuck isn't this movie talked about more?

Name: Aladdin
Review:
"In 10,000 years, I've never been that embarrassed."
Same goes for the rest of the crew working on this film, genie.

Also why the fuck is there a slow jazz beat to A Whole New World?

Name: Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom
Review: I don't know what was going through Spielberg and Lucas' minds... I've watched this movie only a handful of times over the year and every time I am not very happy when doing so.

Name: Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade
Review: Lots of fun, arguably just as fun as Raiders of the Lost Ark. Main points here for costume/production design, the writing, score and just overall entertainment factor. Definitely the funniest of the Indiana Jones.

Name: WALL·E
Review: I have memories of seeing this when it first came to theaters at my local Classic Cinemas in the mall. It holds up today and has one of the best/most relevant messages of any Pixar film. Flawlessly done and entertaining, one of the best if not my favorite Pixar movie.

Name: A Quiet Place
Review:
Very creative and fairly entertaining though I was surprised by how little I was scared. I enjoyed it, definitely, though it's definitely more "suspense" than "horror." The whole silence effect worked really well in a lot of aspects. Shia LeBeouf was a really good character and better than I expected as well.

EDIT: Turns out it's actually not Shia LeBeouf, though I'm not sure how accurate the movie credits are.

Name: Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull
Review: I loved this film when I first saw it in like 2nd grade... yeah, I've changed since then.

Name: End of Watch
Review: Uniquely-told cop story, being super intense and visually stunning, though the plot goes in way too many directions.

Name: The Platform
Review:
One of the most captivating movies I've seen in a long time. The sound editing, video editing, and cinematography are insanely good. It's very well acted, with masterful character development. Better yet, it's a social commentary on prisons and how twisted the system can be. Half a star off because it felt a little like communist propaganda at many points but I was really surprised by how well and compelling the overall message / film was.

EDIT: I forgot to mention the awesome theme song. I love it so much, it's really chilling.

Name: Spider-Man: Far From Home
Review:
Peter Parker is a huge simp

Ok but in all seriousness, this and Homecoming are the teen summer movies of the Marvel franchise. The first half tried so hard to be funny though it was only like that sometimes, with the pain of Peter trying to maintain his two different lives and the uneven transition between both of them. Thankfully the second half gets a lot better and starts to get more fun.

Name: Whiplash
Review: I haven't gotten this fucking level of chills from a movie in so long.

Name: Inglourious Basterds
Review: You know something, Utivich? I think this just might be my masterpiece

Name: Star Wars
Review: A huge part of my childhood, soooo much nostalgia. I'll never forget the first time I saw it on my 6th birthday or all the times I put in the DVD and re-watched it (probably about once a week between 1st and 2nd grade) Haven't re-visited it in about a year so it was great to finally have some time to see it again.

Name: The Empire Strikes Back
Review: This was actually my least favorite of the three originals growing up, but once I got older I've started to appreciate it more. I have to appreciate all the technical bits which are a step up from A New Hope, and Darth Vader is way more badass as a villain in this one which helps with the overall plot and entertainment levels of the film.

Name: Return of the Jedi
Review:
I think this was actually the first Star Wars movie I saw all the way through (except for maybe The Clone Wars) but I can't deny the fact that it ended out the trilogy in the best way possible. I don't even know if my rating is accurate because I stopped taking notes halfway through; it's just that goddamn fun of a movie. Easily the most fun Star Wars film even today.

I also forgot to mention there's jazz music in Jabba's Palace so that automatically boosted it to a 5 star rating for me.

Name: Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace
Review: Only watched again because I'm going through the entire series in order of release date. Everything I said in my first review still stands.

Name: Star Wars: Episode II – Attack of the Clones
Review: This was my favorite Star Wars movie growing up but because I only watched the opening ship chase in Coruscant and the final battle on Geonosis. I never actually saw it all the way through until I got a little older lmao. And yeah, take into account literally every other aspect of this movie... it's shit.

Name: Star Wars: Episode III – Revenge of the Sith
Review: How can there be so much to like and so much to hate all at once?

Name: Star Wars: The Clone Wars
Review: Felt very lacking in substance (though I do credit it for sparking my interest in Star Wars), with mediocre everything. Though I'd still take it over Attack of the Clones.

Name: Django Unchained
Review: Not a perfect film, but goddamn. There's a whole lot to love here, and it's really fucking badass.

Name: South Park: Bigger, Longer & Uncut
Review: The perfect fucking hilarious twisted animated musical. It seems like the wrong description for the movie, being what it is, but it's an accurate description, I'd like to say.

Name: Death Proof
Review:
If anyone asked me which of Tarantino's films is like a showcase of what he's known for, I'd say this does it best.

Honestly way more fun than I imagined! I didn't really enjoy the first half of it, despite being very well done, but by the time we got to the halfway point, everything from there on out was pure entertainment. There are countless references to other Tarantino films and all the characters were well acted. Plus it just has that editing and cinematography that matches the style of what it's portraying, as Tarantino films always do so well. And, as expected, the writing was surprisingly good for what it was. The only reason it isn't higher is because the beginning definitely feels very uneven, as the characters are still being developed. Because of this, the first half is way less memorable than all the action of the second. Lastly, sometimes the sexiness felt like it got to be a little much (there are so many feet shots).

Name: Moonlight
Review: No matter what I'll type here, I won't be giving it enough credit. This hit different in so many ways. Emotions (one of the few movies in the past few years to bring a tear to my eye, especially when Black visits his mom at the drug center), arguably the best cinematography and editing of the 2010s, insanely compelling acting and writing, realism, spot on character development, anticipation leading to scenes that make your heart skip a beat... holy shit. What a masterpiece.

Name: The Hateful Eight
Review: It's 3 AM as I'm writing this; I couldn't sleep so I decided to knock out another Tarantino. Yeah this one wasn't great. Obviously amazing technicals and some decent writing, though a lot of the time it felt like it was just advancing the plot without substance. The acting was pretty good too but the way the plot transitioned really awkwardly between some chapters didn't help. Not to mention all the characters are dipshits. I also want to note the genre change from western to mystery thriller, which was a nice surprise and allowed for some great performances and monologues, though it almost didn't... flow?

Name: Jackie Brown
Review: The movie was decent and all... but I couldn't help but notice how crooked Jackie's mouth is whenever she talks.

Name: Reservoir Dogs
Review: I've now completed Tarantino's film lineup; just need to re-watch and rate Pulp Fiction and OUATIH then it'll be complete. This was a relentless movie all the way through; only main flaw I can think of are that the actual heist wasn't shown but even then that was intentional. What a way to debut as a director!

Name: Pulp Fiction
Review: This is probably my 5th time watching this in my life, and still entertaining as fuck. Incredible writing, beautiful cinematography, effective editing, great acting from so many big names... it's nothing new to hear. It actually isn't my favorite Tarantino, as it has flaws (Tarantino gave himself an N-word pass and the whole pawn shop ordeal...) but because the entertainment factor is soooo through the roof, I'm giving it 5 stars.

Name: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood
Review: Just as wonderful as when I last saw it in theaters. Is it a perfect film? Definitely not. But in my honest opinion, it's the second most entertaining Tarantino film, just behind Pulp Fiction. Scene after scene kept me smiling and engaged, always a great time.

Name: Extraction
Review: It's a directorial debut, and it's always great to see new faces in Hollywood. Let's hope he only gets better from here.

Name: Fear and Desire
Review: The start of my Kubrick filmography binge. Obviously it isn't a GREAT film, but we definitely see some early potential in the areas that he's known for today. Cinematography, character development, memorable dialogue... he clearly was full of ideas for the film, though they just weren't all executed very well; some of the scenes were well done while others are laughable.

Name: Killer's Kiss
Review: Kubrick improved a lot in just a couple years after Fear and Desire. Everything technical, from the editing to the sound, only improved from there. The music was surprisingly great as well, with some very well cinematographed (that's a word, right?) moments. It does suffer from having some blandness in a few moments, as well as dragging quite a bit in the middle. The one thing that Fear and Desire does better, however, is being memorable. I watched both today, and I actually remember a bit more from Fear and Desire. But it's not a forgettable movie overall, I had a fun time watching it, and I'm glad to keep on the Kubrick marathon.

Name: Parasite
Review:
Finally saw it again today. Ahhh...

My dad is the only person in my family who gets my insane love for it. I love him for that.
Tags: hulu, w/ mom, w/ dad, w/ quinn

Name: The Killing
Review: This definitely exceeded my expectations. Take everything I said about Killer's Kiss, The Killing did those things better. He REALLY doubled down on the filmmaking techniques, and created one hell of a fun movie, with surprises around every corner. Crazy to think that he went all the way to making movies this good in a few years. And considering his next movie was Paths of Glory... I'd say he really popped off.

Name: Paths of Glory
Review: This was honestly so fantastic. Gave me chills, made me feel many feelings, beautifully shot, well acted, perfectly paced and structured... the whole shebang. It's mindblowing that Kubrick was able to do this, given it was only his 4th film, he was 28, and it is less than 90 minutes long. Just an incredible piece of filmmaking that has massively exceeded my expectations.

Name: Vice
Review: Watched this for AP US Government as a final project. It fucking sucks.

Name: Spartacus
Review:
Christ, this has taken me a while to complete. I actually found it pretty easy in comparison to other 3+ hour long movies, but I was trying to finish out the school year strong.

This was a pretty solid movie. It's an astounding technical achievement, especially considering the 1960 release, and everything in the first half is pretty much flawless. Though the second half focuses a lot less on the major action and more on a love story and politics, which is definitely where it falls short. But overall this is still a pretty darn good film. Glad to finally continue Kubrick's filmography!

Name: Lolita
Review: This film is the original "he's probably thinking about other girls" meme.

Name: Dr. Strangelove or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb
Review: Not really sure what to say about this. I really liked it, especially everything going on towards the end. It's very well paced, well shot, funny, has great acting, and all around an interesting story. Some of the writing just seemed to be keeping the plot going without any character development though, which is why it loses half a star.

Name: 2001: A Space Odyssey
Review:
Never before has a movie triggered my OCD so consistently.

Ok but seriously this is a fantastic film. The visuals are some of the most incredible and breathtaking I've ever seen, and this is a film from 1968! The characters are really cool though they aren't developed as well as some of Kubrick's other films. If I had one major criticism, it feels a little slow at times and takes an hour to really introduce the plot, which I feel like others will tell me I'm wrong, but this is a near 5 star film. Maybe I'll like it more on a second watch, which hopefully isn't too far away.

Name: The Spy Who Dumped Me
Review:
Didn't need to be as long as it was. Surprisingly good cinematography and some very funny moments but a lot that I wasn't really laughing at / felt awkward, and the way it transitions between scenes felt really messy. It looks good visually as well, but most solid moments are quickly ruined by annoying one liners that don't work at all. It feels like a movie I'll probably forget a lot of in a couple days.

I also am tempted to bump it down to 1.5 stars because the song in the final credits is *awful.*

Name: A Clockwork Orange
Review: This is a deep social commentary and no one can tell me differently. I am in awe that Kubrick pulled off something so weird yet so mind-blowing. Can't decide if this beat Paths of Glory or not but it's arguably Kubrick's finest work.

Name: The Painter and the Thief
Review: Absolutely beautiful film that uses a lot of filmmaking techniques really well and develops the characters masterfully. It has also inspired me to watch more documentaries which I think is awesome, though I found the ending to lose a lot of steam, despite being entertained and engaged the whole way through up to a certain point. The story was really neat though, and I think the ambition behind the project as a whole is great.

Name: The Lovebirds
Review: Definitely brainless entertainment. It has its good moments, but overall was just really inconsistent, annoying, and messy.

Name: Barry Lyndon
Review: Thanks to my ADD, I don't take slow films super well, but I did enjoy this one a lot. The first and last 45 minutes are TRULY excellent and done super well. The whole thing is visually stunning, well written, acted and directed. They're memorable and everything. It's just that middle hour and a half that I was getting really distracted and struggling to stay awake that kind of got to me. But overall, I'm glad I finally got around to it; Kubrick done good. It's not something that I'd generally expect from him, especially compared to his last few films. But it just shows how amazing and big his range is in the grand scheme of things.

Name: The Shining
Review:
I was half expecting this to not live up to the hype if I'm being honest, but I had nothing to worry about.

My FULL REVIEW is on my Instagram (link on profile), so check that out to hear my full thoughts on this incredible movie.

Name: Full Metal Jacket
Review: Kubrick POPPED OFF at this point in his career. Holy shit, yet again I am shook by one of his films. Sorry Paths of Glory, but this is my new favorite war movie. My affinity for this movie is going to be hard to explain, so keep watch for my Instagram list once that comes out!

Name: Eyes Wide Shut
Review: I mean... it's good in a few different ways. The cinematography and editing are some of Kubrick's best; they were both REALLY effective. The performances were good as well, as was the mystery aspect. But this just wasn't a story I could connect to, nor was I fond of any of the characters. It's well done, but I didn't personally like it a whole lot. But hey, now I'm done with Kubrick's filmography!

Name: Alien
Review:
One of the BIGGEST technical achievements in Hollywood history; I'm sure of it. I can't wrap my mind around the fact that this was made in 1979. Even beyond that, the reason why it's not a full 5 stars is because it does take a little while to build up and get going. But once it gets going... it DOESN'T STOP! Pretty much everything else about this movie is excellent.

It's enough to require a full review, which I will hopefully be writing soon!

Name: Bad Boys
Review: Eh, it was fine. Messy dialogue with a lot of the same jokes used over and over, though the action and cinematography/SFX were both good. Still a definite Michael Bay movie but better than the shit he makes today. It felt exactly like how a 90s movie should feel though, and that was good, but I couldn't help but get annoyed with it's inconsistency in quality.

Name: Back to the Future
Review:
If I'm being honest this might not be quite a 5 star film, but for nostalgia purposes and arguably being the most fun movie in the history of cinema, it deserves the edge.

MASTERPIECE HOW CAN YOU NOT LOVE THIS

Name: Back to the Future Part II
Review:
Never been a huge fan of this one, and even as more of a film buff, still don't love it. For me, at least, it tries to accomplish WAY too much in its runtime. Even after seeing it countless times, it can still be hard to follow. It also just doesn't have a lot of the heart of the first one, aside from the continued relationship between Marty and Doc.

Still a good movie in many ways, don't get me wrong, but the story of the first one is just twisted so much that some of the tickle is taken out of it.

Name: Back to the Future Part III
Review: Maybe the cheesiest of the bunch, but arguably the most fun and creatively done. Some effects have aged well, some haven't. A lot improved upon the second, however. The writing, the acting, the story structure, it's just overall a shitload of fun. I don't think we'll ever see a better "summer movie" than these three.

Name: Bad Boys II
Review:
Bad Boys II? More like Bad MOVIE II! Haha, get it?

I'll show myself out.

In all seriousness, this is really similar in quality to the first one but it's longer and has some really bad dialogue.

Also, this is the 100TH MOVIE I'VE LOGGED on Letterboxd! Yay me! :D

Name: Star Wars: The Force Awakens
Review:
I know it's cool now to hate on the new Star Wars movies, but this is still a good film.

It captures the fun of the originals and develops new characters extremely well. Not to mention some absolutely chilling visuals and sound. It's a thoroughly interesting plot that brought back some of the "force" (haha, get it?) and impact that the original trilogy had on the film industry.

Is it as good as the originals? No. There is still some really clunky dialogue and it takes a little time to really get into the plot, but I finish the film smiling every time.

Lawrence Kasdan should've been on writing for all 5 of the new Star Wars films.

Name: Rogue One: A Star Wars Story
Review: Reservoir Dogs meets Star Wars. A very slept-on film; this was just as good as it was when I first saw it in theaters in 2016. Easily the best of the new Star Wars movies and very slept on. Straightforward, dark, consistently engaging with chilling visuals and sound, the best dialogue of any Star Wars film post-Return of the Jedi, there's a whole lot to love here. Not to mention an insane third act. Really, the only lost points are because sometimes it moves between scenes a little fast, and it isn't necessarily a film we needed per se, but otherwise, this is great. Edit: just bumped the rating up to a 4.5 and the film up to my #3 star wars film. The ending with Vader is the best moment of any Star Wars film.

Name: Star Wars: The Last Jedi
Review: More creative and overall a better story than Force Awakens, though it is anything but consistent. Still enjoyed it overall like I did in theaters, as it has fantastic moments, but there are definitely flaws.

Name: Solo: A Star Wars Story
Review: Great first half but the second half was frankly bad. Lots of great action and visuals plus surprisingly solid dialogue ruined by repetitive and uncreative characters. Plus the ending seemed like it tried to pull off some plot twists but got in way over its head.

Name: Mad Max: Fury Road
Review:
Ok but consider the following... this is the best movie ever made.

Also, if I were on the Fury Road, I would 101% be the guitar guy that's straight vibing the whole time. Just saying.

Name: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker
Review: Not as bad as some people say but... ehhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhh...

Name: The Irishman
Review:
Loved this WAY more the second time around. Now that I'm in summer and taking a brief break from work (thanks to the riots), I was able to just sit back, relax, and enjoy this brilliant film.

I REALLY was tempted to give it the full 5 stars, but unfortunately it does loose a bit of focus and steam in the last hour of the film, at least compared to how amazing and entertaining the rest of the movie is. It's really Scorsese going back to his roots and showing us that he's still got it even at an older age. I really love this film and am surprised that I didn't think a whole lot of it when I saw it again back in Oscars season. I was engaged the whole 3 and a half hours and I normally have a ton of trouble with long films if that says anything (thanks to my ADD).

Can't wait to binge the rest of Scorsese's films later this summer! (I just have a couple more directors I want to knock out first)

Name: Sólo con tu pareja
Review: This film is a full length version of the naked-in-public dream.

Name: Marriage Story
Review:
This film was...

I don't know if I can put it into words, as I wouldn't be doing it justice.

The films I hold to the highest standard in terms of quality and personal preference are ones that change the way I look at a certain topic. In this situation, it's marriage, love, faithfulness, strategic thinking, etc... the first time I only gave this film a 4.5 because I was so overwhelmed by all that was being thrown at me. Like The Irishman, I've come back to it many months later, and the second viewing really stuck the landing for me.

Some of THE BEST performances I've EVER seen, a nearly flawless dialogue, beautiful Randy Newman music, not a single minute wasted, easy connections to every character as a real person and not just an actor playing a part... all this and so much more could be said and I wouldn't be doing it justice. Everything feels so precise and organized and you can really tell this is the work of a mature, intelligent filmmaker. Nothing is wasted; not a single line, shot, emotion, color, nothing is out of place.

I don't know what else to say that can express my feelings towards this film. It's made me thankful for the great parents I have, encouraged me to be a dad once I get married, it's both made me want to and not want to get married... I already prefaced the way it's re-imagined any concept relating to love for me, but it feels more fitting this late in the review. Yes, the film is entirely based around some of the most saddening topics one can face in life, but the discomfort you feel from this shouldn't be knocked against the movie. Every filmmaking technique worked to the best possible degree, and if you are feeling negative emotions, that means Noah Baumbach's message got through to you.

I dunno, I'd say this is a pretty great movie?

Name: Shirley
Review:
Really beautifully shot with amazing technicals, solid writing, a creative story, unique storytelling, and one of the most down to earth films I've seen in awhile. The "POV storytelling" makes me feel like I'm in the movie itself more than any other. Thanks to this and the overall creepy vibe, this is, in my opinion, the most entertaining and engaging film of the year so far. Not to mention some great performances as well!

The reason why I have a star off is because there's a lot of build-up to thrills, but the pay off is disappointing. This is a thriller movie and I think I should've been a little more paranoid of certain things to happen, but they just... didn't.

Name: Da 5 Bloods
Review:
Spike Lee is a gift from God. I sat silent at the end of the film for a good couple minutes; I haven't done that since my first Parasite viewing in January.

I have far more thoughts than this but I need to figure out how to effectively put them into words. My rating should say a lot though.

Name: Artemis Fowl
Review:
Fartemis Fowl.

This makes Spy Kids look like a Pixar masterpiece. I love how it's automatically the lowest rated Disney film on Letterboxd upon release. I feel like this will be 2020's big Razzie winner.

I know there will be people out there like, "Ohhhh it's just simple FUN! How can you hate a movie like this?" Well, seeing as I can really only name one redeeming quality (it looks visually decent in some scenes), I hope that answers their question. The dialogue is absolutely hilarious, the acting more wooden than Attack of the Clones, many wasted talents (fucking M from James Bond is in this!), the cramming of endless plot points into 95 minutes, and the kid actor for Fartemis has a very punchable face.

It's the perfect example of Disney only making movies for money and not really giving a shit about quality, at least when it comes to these live action remakes.

I feel like I put more effort into this review than the entire crew did for this movie.

Name: Kill Bill: Vol. 1
Review: I'd like to reflect on the impact Kill Bill has had on me. Every few months I'm drawn back to this film and it's second volume. These movies are landmark cinema in what I've seen and my love for them is like a drug addiction. It's one of THE movies that got me into being a cinephile / film buff and without it, I don't know where I'd be today with my love and obsession of cinema. Today, I've seen over 100 movies this year alone, am knocking out films by director, and am looking at film schools to go to once I graduate high school in 2022. Thank you Tarantino for these masterpieces.

Name: Donnie Brasco
Review: 3.5 stars for being a good film (not without it's noticeable flaws, however), 0 stars for stealing that monologue at the end from Brooklyn Nine-Nine smh

Name: The Terminator
Review:
For me, Terminator is more than just landmark cinema and an sci-fi relic. It's nostalgic in many ways.

One of the most triumphant moments in any young boy's life is seeing his first R-rated film. For me, this was it. I was only 11 and I was amazed by this new realm of movies that I had not yet discovered. I have a lot of nostalgia from this experience also because we watched it in our Universal Orlando hotel on a Florida vacation, and had just seen the Terminator 2 3D show the day before. I'm not the same kid anymore and when I see this movie, I reflect back to those days and I still wish I was on that vacation back in June 2015.

2015 was also the year I started middle school, my family got our dog, and really got into movies. Arguably, this was the most important year of my life? You have no idea how badly I want to go back to 2015 and relive every moment of it.

As far as the movie goes, of course it's great. Some parts have held up better over the years than others. The whole love story just felt super clunky even if it mattered to the story, and not _every_ scene feels necessary. But overall, it's still an incredibly fun movie with some great practical effects, Arnold delivers one of the most memorable performances in history, the dialogue surprisingly effective, and the pacing and story structure are just great. There's so much nostalgia here all aspects considered. The way you feel Sarah's pain and anxiety as Sarah Connors are shot down one by one is inane and a feeling that never gets old. All this considered, keep in mind that this was James Cameron's FIRST FILM. It's a relic from the 1980s and will always hold a special place in my life.

Name: Terminator 2: Judgment Day
Review: Still my favorite action film ever. The landmark CGI, extremely memorable performances with perfect casting, excellent dialogue with even more classic one liners, insanely good character development that really connects in a way most other films fail to do by turning the villain into a loveable father figure, relentless pacing with an unpredictable plot, creative directing, humor, even some emotions... this is a masterpiece. Everything about this screams classic and important. It's so much fun, badass, perfectly well done... goddamn there's no words I can use to describe this film and my love for it. Every viewing feels like the first and it will never get old. Mad Max: Fury Road is said to be the action film that implicitly calls out the flaws of modern action films, though I'd say this does so a bit better. If there was one little flaw I have with this movie, it's that the T-1000 disappears from the story for a bit too long, but this is the only negative I have and it isn't enough to bump it down to a 4.5. As far as I'm concerned, this is the last Terminator film. It's a James Cameron universe and all those other posers who directed the "sequels" don't know the source material like he does. While I don't have the same nostalgia for this as the first one, I did see it in 3D when it was re-released in 2017. One of my favorite theater experiences ever.

Name: Action Point
Review: The concept is a unique one for sure; the use of color to make the film look vibrant and vintage was excellent, and I found the slapstick humor to be alright. Although while I did enjoy it, I will say that the slapstick isn't anything that I haven't seen before, and a lot of the same stunts and tricks feel the same as the movie goes on; I kind of wish they found more ways to hurt the characters than through the same three attractions, it would've spiced things up a bit. They do so much more in Jackass, why not here? I feel like the only thing setting this movie apart from other comedies is its location (which was very good looking by the way, pretty nice production design). I also found the whole crappy dad thing going on to be lame and out of place, and I have to mention that the editing at times can be pretty distracting. It just feels like a whole bunch of wasted potential that Johnny Knoxville and Tim Kirkby could've done so much more with, especially with the urban legend being much more than it is made out to be in this film.

Name: Transformers
Review: Pretty silly film overall but at least a good way to kill time and watch some cool explosions. Obviously it's visually pleasing (would say sound too but it's too fucking loud for my tastes), but I can never take the acting and dialogue that seriously. I also think the amount of product placement in every Transformers film is hilarious. I suppose I have to rewatch the rest of the franchise now, don't I...

Name: Batman
Review: Fun movie and I'm sure it was much more exciting at the time of release, though not all of it has aged well. Jack Nicholson was a fantastic Joker though I feel Michael Keaton could've been more... brooding? It also looks really good for the most part though some effects are very fake, even for 1989.
