Entertaining overall though, I'm glad I got this in a DVD set.

Name: Citizen Kane
Review:
Well I've now seen the ultimate classic for all film buffs. And it was great! Definitely earns its reputation and legacy. Brilliant acting, writing, cinematography, character development / storytelling, production design, and it's just overall super entertaining. Not a minute was wasted, though I feel like it did lose focus at a couple points.

Something else to consider is that films I consider to be masterpieces are those that are really thought provoking and make me re-think a part of life or the world. The messages in Citizen Kane did make me think a little bit, but not unlike other films I have seen previously. It's for this reason I don't think it's a 5 star film or top 10/20, though it's still good enough that this is only worth a half star off and not a full one.

Name: Sin City
Review: So basically... that was psychotic, gross, sinister, dark, depressing, thoughtful, comical, cartoonish, artistic, over the top, engaging, ridiculous, heartstopping, violent, R rated comic book madness. It takes chances like no other. Every frame a painting became every frame a comic strip panel. It's more like a comic book than anything in the MCU yet deeper and darker than Christopher Nolan's Batman trilogy. I had some minor nitpicks along the way, but in the end, I was so... I don't even know how to describe that feeling. This was a truly insane movie and I loved it. The Clockwork Orange of comic films. I'm really tired since it's almost 2:30 my time so I might update with more thoughts later but this is something I really need to sit on. I haven't said that about a comic book movie since the Dark Knight... though this is the better movie. This ain't no squirrely amateur... this is a salty dog.

Name: Alien
Review: Strangely I don't think I liked it quite as much this time around. Still great film, though I feel the time breaks in the action do take the tickle out of some of the thrill and it didn't feel as memorable or engaging this time. At least it was still entertaining as hell and, of course, a classic.

Name: Aliens
Review:
I like this one more than the first. The development of the story is longer, although it's more meaningful and the characters all have their standout traits. I'm able to tell them apart better than those in the first. Also, I'm usually more of a sucker for action rather than thriller, so I found the action to be way more effective than the creepiness. And with the exception of a couple very outdated effects, the visuals are only one-upped in this one. It's just pure fun from start to finish.

So why doesn't it have higher of a rating? Well, the one thing that I will say is NOT as good visually is the cinematography. Some of the framing of the scenes just weren't very effective at all, and I felt like there was a lot more that could've been included on screen.
I also think some of the acting could've been better directed. Every single actor here has potential, although given how intense some situations are, they seemed a bit... boring? The child actor did really well, however, and Sigourney Weaver was classic as always.

Name: 6 Underground
Review:
Mostly pointless and crude horseshit, although this is exactly what I would've loved like 3-4 years ago. Muse, endless violence, lots of swearing, and tons of pop culture references. Thank you Michael Bay for making the ultimate boys going through puberty movie!

Also the THX scene is actually really fun.

Name: Alien³
Review:
I liked the dark tone that kicked the film off, but as it progressed, it just became really messy and frankly not good. The cinematography for the most part is really distracting except for like two memorable scenes, the visual effects and production design are country miles behind the first two (the final "chase scene" looks laughably bad), the acting and dialogue are really wooden and don't flow, and all the characters are forgettable and not super well developed. It's not engaging except for the many over-the-top death scenes (including a poor dog) and just wasn't a fun watch. I got to this point, where I can start my David Fincher binge, so I don't know if I'll ever watch Resurrection after this.

It should also be brought to everyone's attention that this barely qualifies as one of Fincher's films. He was brought into the project very late after a first director's ideas fell through, and while filming and editing was subject to tons of tweaks by 20th Century Fox. They literally dismantled and reworked the film without his consent, causing him to walk out. David Fincher now disowns the film and stays as far away from it as possible thanks to this. When I make my final list, it won't have a ranked position thanks to this (it's also why it's rating is shockingly low, because it's literally a perfect example of a big studio getting in the way of a director's creative vision).

Name: Drive
Review:
Surprisingly brilliant action flick. Engaging and entertaining the whole time, with an excellent pace and some really great action and visual effects. It looked stunning as well, with a wide variety of vibrant colors and realistic deaths/kills. I had a blast the whole time.

It's not perfect because I felt like Ryan Gosling's character could've been developed through a bit more than just stares and two-word responses, and I also was disappointed with the elevator scene, but this really was a super great movie. Very re-watchable. Also the last half hour or so kicks ass.

Name: Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen
Review: Not even an hour in and I'm rating it. Haven't seen it in like 6 years and it'll be much longer before I see it again (if I ever do). Unbelievably stupid.

Name: Leap Year
Review:
Okay, so let me tell the 48 of you following me currently about why I watched this film. Today, I was bored and watched a bunch of decade lists for the 2010s. I disagreed with all of them in many ways and couldn't seem to find one that I really liked. So, of course, as our main man Bigweld says, see a need fill a need. I've also been watching a lot of YMS recently, and I love how he actually sees a majority of the films from specific years before ranking them, so that's just what I'm going to do. And when I'm through 2019, I'll have my official decade best list!

With this film being released on January 8, 2010, it is officially the start of this behemoth side project along with my directors binges that I'll probably complete by like Oscar season at the earliest! Wish me luck!

Oh yeah, I forgot I was reviewing the movie. It's a romcom with a stupid premise and even worse ending, what more could you expect?

Name: Daybreakers
Review:
I have to admit I was kind of expecting complete garbage, but this was decent. It's definitely disturbing and has some good acting (love seeing Willem Dafoe kill vampires!), and I think the concept is definitely an interesting one. Not to mention a pretty insane final scene! Though I must say the execution of the story was pretty shallow and a some of the SFX are laughable. The writing is also just ok, with decent development of story and characters but not being memorable at all.

I also have to say that it's a little freaky this film was only 1 year off with the outbreak of a sickness and major worldwide protests. Huh. Well, at least it was entertaining and I was engaged for the most part, and yay, we're moving right along on the 2010 binge!

Name: Youth in Revolt
Review:
First 20 minutes - Funny, relatable, entertaining and showing a lot of potential
Last 70 minutes - Messy, poorly written, repetitive, annoying, only funny sometimes, and cliche. Painful most of the way through.

I love Michael Cera but wow it went to shit fast. He's a mega simp in this. Maybe I'm a little biased since I've watched a lot of shit recently but I guess this is what I've gotten myself into. I thought some of the animations and the portrayed contrast between him and his French alter ego were cool though.

Name: Transformers: Dark of the Moon
Review: My favorite Transformers film, though that doesn't say much. Honestly just Avengers (2012) with stupid characters and in Chicago. Although they crashed and drove over a sign saying "I-88 to Aurora" and it was really cool to see my hometown get recognized in something that isn't Wayne's World!

Name: Birdman or (The Unexpected Virtue of Ignorance)
Review:
Incredible.

It explores themes of ego, self-worth, fame, and identity in general in such a brilliant way. I was fully engaged the whole time thanks to the thoughtfulness of the story, the excellent acting and perfectly flowed writing, some heartstopping moments (not getting into details because it'd be a mega spoiler), clear direction and vision, and just one of the most thought-provoking films of the decade.

This blew me away, if you couldn't tell.

Name: True Lies
Review: There's something so weird to me about seeing Arnold act like a semi-normal human

Name: Bad Lieutenant
Review: Spike Lee's 95 Film Recommendations Ranked Harvey Keitel = very good performance and the story = pretty solid, but everything else was forgettable. I kinda expected more risks to be taken in many places, and it's definitely creative though a little outdated. I'll probably forget this in a few days.

Name: Rashomon
Review: Spike Lee's 95 Film Recommendations Ranked My first Kurosawa film, at last! Some really good acting and writing, along with my favorite part of the film, an excellent soundtrack. It sounds like something that would come out of a Disney animated short during this time period. Not to mention it's very well paced as well. Just overall quite memorable with some very fun scenes. I must say, however, I couldn't help but notice some of the editing was pretty distracting and frankly looked cheap, though it definitely wasn't as distracting as some movies can be with this. I also wasn't necessarily engaged the whole time, but I definitely stayed interested thanks to the great music and acting. I just didn't necessarily get a whole lot out of it, but it definitely shares qualities with top tier movies which is always a good thing. Can't wait to see Ran and Yojimbo!

Name: Gravity
Review:
Not what I expected. Obviously it's a major technical achievement in many, many ways and it doesn't disappoint AT ALL in those categories like cinematography and production design. Truly brilliant stuff. Specifically, the high angle shots easily could've been overused, but each of them felt necessary to what Cuarón intended depending on the scene. The sound was obviously great as well, though I do feel the effect of a huge crescendo immediately growing silent was overused (you know what I mean if you've seen the movie). On that topic, the score was pretty great and very fitting.

Now onto the negatives. I didn't think the performances were bad, but George Clooney's character was honestly obnoxious as hell most of the time, and Sandra Bullock was really good but a bit Inconsistent in her personality. I also feel like this has to do with the writing and screenplay. It gets the job done but has like 2 memorable lines the entire movie. Lastly, I think the pacing felt very uneven, as the entire story is fit into just 90 minutes. It really feels like something that should be 2+ hours but isn't.

The movie demands your attention from start to finish and there's a lot to like, though from Alfonso Cuarón, I suppose I expected a bit more.

Name: Yojimbo
Review: Spike Lee's 95 Film Recommendations Ranked This is a damn well done movie. Once again, we have an amazing and memorable score, and I'm starting to realize that the score is going to be a strong point in any Kurosawa movie I see. I absolutely love it. Where Rashomon's score sounded like something from a Disney short, this one sounds more Hitchcockian and I'm living for it. The screenplay and acting are also considerably improved over Rashomon, which I think really works to the advantage since we have so many dynamic characters here. But they were all easy to separate and believable which was great. The production design was very well done as well, and I have to say I was overall more engaged and entertained with this one more than Rashomon. It falls a little bit short because there were times when I was a little lost and the pacing felt a little uneven. I think it started to lose a bit of focus in the second half and in turn I lost some interest. But those are my only complaints with this great film. I can say I love Kurosawa now, thanks to this and Ran (which is next).

Name: Skin
Review:
What the fuck was this? Absolutely no focus. It had a little bit of touch, but I had no idea whether or not we're supposed to connect with certain characters or not. Everyone did horrible things and almost none of it made sense. I seriously don't get what the filmmaker's message was supposed to be with this.

I hate that this was recommended to me as being "meaningful" when it shows both races being at fault and having no view of what it's trying to accomplish. There are much better things to watch in support of BLM than this confusing mess.

Name: Taxi Driver
Review: I was sorta half expecting this to not live up to expectations, but that was totally misguided. I loved this! Everything that goes into the development of Travis Bickle was literally so good, including several great performances. Not to mention some seriously good cinematography, editing, and color palate choices. You also gotta love the consistent jazz music that fits the NYC setting beautifully! I found this to be a bit of a social commentary with some great messages that really hold up even today, partially in thanks to an excellent script, and while said messages might not be anything out of the ordinary, the execution was brilliant. I don't think every scene was necessary per se, and while I did enjoy the satisfying ending, I was expecting a bit more of a shock value. But nonetheless I can safely say that I love this film for everything it does well and I can't believe it took me this long to watch it. Now I want to see King of Comedy!

Name: My Spy
Review:
Dave Bautista suggested they kill the girl and I wish he went down that path.

This tries so hard to be funny and "relevant" but this is the most cliche and annoyingly millennial thing I've watched in a long time. No one in the film is special or memorable and at most it's maybe a good birthday party movie for a kid in elementary school. I'll gladly never see this again. Admittedly I didn't even finish it; this really doesn't need to be rated PG-13. Also I live near Naperville, where the final scene takes place, and I can confirm there are no cliffs anywhere in sight.

It was originally 1 star but any movie where we have to deal with a little fucking shit of a kid (it's very clear why she has few friends) is an automatic waste and this film put me in a bad mood.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Lost in Translation
Review:
This... was nominated for best picture?

Aside from some surprisingly great cinematography and performances, this was very typical and used a fair number of romance cliches. I was never super invested, and the writing was just decent at best. The location is interesting (in Tokyo), so I think it's a bit above average, but I'm very surprised that some people enjoy it as much as they do.

I also think the portrayal of some of the Japanese people was a bit questionable, as if they were using stereotypes for humor. Not out of the ordinary for movies, but still a notable part of the movie and something that really annoyed me.

Name: Life Itself
Review:
What a film. Roger Ebert has been a personal hero of mine ever since I started on this crazy journey of being a cinephile, and this film opened my eyes to not only different parts of his life, but different parts of film as i know it. It humanizes him an Siskel in a way that I wholly appreciate as it's allowed me to connect with him on a whole different level. It's what I think makes a documentary great; being able to take a new perspective on a real world topic. The editing, cinematography, historic footage, excellent writing, it's all done to the best it can and is a truly worthwhile tribute to Ebert's life. This is a film I'll be re-visiting a lot throughout the years, and no documentary has emotionally touched me like this one.

Can't wait to watch Hoop Dreams!

Name: Palm Springs
Review: Max Barbakow's directorial debut is hella fun with really good acting, visual flash, excellent production design, and consistent humor and entertainment. Andy Samberg could do the most basic of things and I would probably crack the fuck up; he's just that that talented of a guy. J.K. Simmons also played a simple but effective character. It's the type of movie that you go to see with your friends on a nice summer day just for a good laugh. Not an original concept per se, but it has the necessary unpredictability factor needed to do it properly. The film keeps getting more and more insane and hilarious for a while (until it gets a bit lame and predictable once Sarah and Nyles go separate ways, but improves towards the end). It's really something I don't think anyone will take anything serious out of then forming a bond with friends, but that's alright. We need those movies in the world. I really wish it got a theatrical release but alas...

Name: The Lighthouse
Review:
Holy fuck. This is packed to the brim with insanity and mystery and then some. The acting is fucking great, the cinematography and editing brilliant, writing super memorable and develops much of the film very well, the grainy visuals, haunting sound design... it's so crisp and masterful and sincerely gave me chills many times, which brings me to the main point of why I'm now a fanboy of this film. It's so persistently eerie and I wasn't able to look away for a second. This is so unpredictable in its horror and thrills that my eyes were glued to my computer the whole time. The way it explores the mental health of isolation and meditative character development are so fucking good and effective. The pacing and order of events are flawless. No holds are barred, and it really blew me away.

This, Parasite and Marriage Story are obviously my top three of 2019 but in no order. Saying one is better than another is like picking a name out of a hat.

Name: Hard Eight
Review:
Aw man, the 90s were a great time for directorial debuts. This really exceeded expectations. A nice and creative vision for an Of Mice of Men-style dynamic between Philip Baker Hall and John C. Reilly in a more contemporary setting was executed well by PTA. It has tons of other great performances, a snappy and entertaining script, and some really great visuals including cinematography and usage of color. I was watching from beginning to end without looking away thanks to all the flashy colors and excellent use of camera focus.

Obviously, based on the positives I gave this movie, you can really see how PTA had a lot of potential early on. It's definitely not a perfect film, as I think it does lose a bit of focus in the second half, and there's also some uneven pacing. It also isn't necessarily a film that I feel will hold up as well in my memory, despite being a fucking fun time. But just thanks to its solid visuals and performances, I have to say this was a great film. Underrated for sure, and a great way to start my PTA binge!

Name: Boogie Nights
Review:
I'm surprised this wasn't directed by Kubrick and written by Tarantino.

Really damn good, indeed. Every technical of the film reminded me of A Clockwork Orange and Once Upon a Time in Hollywood. The color palate for each scene is so vibrant. The cinematography is excellent with some great framing of shots and a lot of masterful, well-placed one-shots. The editing is smooth and honestly reminded me most of the editing of Whiplash. The costume and production designs just scream 70s, and the soundtrack was pretty awesome as well (another line that can be drawn to Tarantino). A big part of how much I was engaged with this super creative film is because of the stunning visuals.

Then, of course, you have that Tarantino-esque screenplay with some memorable lines and lots of pop culture talk. The performances were all super great (the fight with Dirk and Jack was pretty damn awesome, as was John C. Reilly in the entire film); they were all really believable and connected with their roles extremely well. I'm feeling like this is going to be a trend in future PTA films; his strong casting. Lastly, you can tell that this was very well-directed.

I thought it might fall a bit short of the full 5 stars because, while it was fun and entertaining as hell, it really didn't change my perspective on anything. Films that I give 5 star ratings to (except some films that I gave 5 stars to towards the beginning of my Letterboxd career) generally open my third eye, if you will, to a part of life that I hadn't really thought deeply about before. It has a couple points in the film where I was less engaged than others, but the pacing was really good and it felt down-to-earth enough that I was way more connected with the story and characters than I expected. I just don't think it was necessarily deep enough for me to give it the edge.

It should also be brought up that PTA wanted this film to be 3+ hours long and rated NC-17, similar to A Clockwork Orange and Eyes Wide Shut with Kubrick. But alas, New Line told him that it had to be less than 3 and rated R. This is one of my biggest pet peeves in film, when a company gets in the way of a director's vision. Thankfully, the movie is still pretty fantastic, but one can only dream of what the 3+ hour version would've been like.

I would also be surprised if (no spoilers) the New Years scene wasn't inspired by that one iconic part of Full Metal Jacket.

Name: Jurassic Park
Review:
This was my first movie theater trip since Onward 4 months ago and it was honestly surreal, but in a good way. There were maybe 6 other people in the theater aside from me and my friend I was seeing it with, and I don't think I could've found a better movie. It just screams summer movie and vibes that way, to the point like I really felt like it was finally a normal summer. I absolutely loved it.

As for the movie itself? It's good. Really good, in fact. The acting was much better than I remembered (including the child actors), the CGI is pretty decent even by today's standards despite being groundbreaking almost 30 years ago (hard to think about), it was well written, and I absolutely love the production design; arguably my favorite part of the entire movie. Not to mention a John Williams score. It's a blast from start to finish and full of iconic scenes, still making it one of the most memorable movie experiences out there.

One thing that really stuck out to me, however, was the cinematography. It was really good and really flawed at the same time. Of course, there are some VERY well framed shots that create a lot of famous movie moments, and the angle choices worked every time. However, I found that most of the time when a dinosaur was sneaking around, they would use a scene beforehand that gives away what was about to happen. I get that this is a movie for families so they wanted to cut back on the scares a bit, but it was a bit annoying to notice how predictable some of the scares were, and I think that's why the film doesn't get a higher rating than an 8. But it's still one of the most famous movies out there for good reasons, and I have a considerable level of nostalgia for it, so I feel like a high score is warranted for anyone with a pulse who sees it.

Lastly, while this doesn't impact my feelings towards the film as a whole, it should be noticed that the ending was clearly setting up a sequel. Which makes sense because of how much money Universal knew they were gonna make off of it, although it's a shame to see what the sequels actually became :/

Name: Godzilla
Review: The OG summer movie! Rather simple overall with decent dialogue and acting, though none of the characters are really that well developed. BUT it does have a really well made titular Godzilla that honestly is fairly believable and surprisingly good production design and editing. I have to admit that I really mainly watched this because the American bastardization from 1956 (watching next) served as a heavy influence on Jurassic Park (which I saw last night) but I'm glad I did. I even saw a bit of Jaws inspiration here. True classic.

Name: The Dark Knight
Review: It was a pleasure to burn! (Marvel can kiss Christoper Nolan's ass)

Name: Magnolia
Review:
Truly a cinematic experience. The acting is frankly some of the best I've seen in any movie, the cinematography and editing clean and masterful, great directing, excellent screenplay, beautiful character development, excellent story structure, a sweet soundtrack... damn, this is a good movie. Very engaging the whole way through and it isn't a hard movie to follow at that, which is a good thing. And, of course, the themes and messages were very well conveyed. This is a PTA film, what else would you expect? Great social commentary. Everything just blends in so perfectly, and the film is a true work of art.

My only nitpick is that I feel like it should've been a bit more meditative all around. The second half of the film really helps me to tell them apart, but I feel like we didn't touch on emotional perspectives as much as we could've in the first half. I have to say, however, once we really see everyone reach their emotional peak for the film, it gets REALLY great and becomes a truly excellent film. I couldn't get my eyes off the screen around the two hour mark (my only issue with this part was when they were all singing that song at the same time. It shows their connection but didn't quite match the energy I'd come to expect of the film). I just wish it was a little bit more gradual from the introductions into mass chaos. But then you leave the mass chaos and go into good conclusions for each of the characters, but with uneven pacing. It ended well at least, but most of the final hour was a bit of a letdown.

This is firmly a 8/10 in my opinion, though some days I might feel like it's a 9. It's a movie I'll be sitting on for a while.

Name: Punch-Drunk Love
Review: Honestly fairly disappointed. Adam Sandler was good, the writing was decent, the cinematography and editing were excellent as expected (love the use of one shots in this), as well as having some real nice production design. The music was also really great and was placed really well. The character development of Barry and his girlfriend are good, though everyone else felt fairly one dimensional. I wish the piano had more symbolism than it did, it wasn't that interesting IMO, and it felt like he was just doing things to be a simp (it's a romance though, what did I expect?). I was just kinda bored through most of this, though it has its moments. Not totally sure what he was going for other than a romantic, average character study. And with a title "Punch-Drunk Love," I was expecting it to be far more intense and emotional than Adam Sandler freaking out whenever someone calls him gay or implies he's a simp. I understand we're taken into how Barry sees the world, and I think the execution of that is great, but the film really hasn't left me feeling any strong emotions. It had no impact. Well done, but probably his least interesting film that I've seen so far. I honestly wouldn't be surprised if I bumped it down to a 6 in the future.

Name: Fatal Affair
Review:
Really cliche and excruciating as hell to watch, but what did I expect from a Netflix romance in the middle of July? Typical obsessed creep horror bullshit.

It's another brick in the wall of mediocre, forgettable movies released during quarantine (Spenser Confidential, Extraction, My Spy, Artemis Fowl just to name a few) that's made waiting for the delayed releases of good films all the more painful. I was pretty much done with it about a quarter to a third of the way through.

Name: There Will Be Blood
Review:
There was, in fact, blood in this film. 10/10, perfect title.

Oh my fucking god, this was fantastic. Brilliant direction and a creative vision make for a beautifully-crafted chronicle of this nightmarish story and time period. It felt more like a communication to today's audience, and made me feel engaged and empathetic for everyone living through this movie like no film ever has. My new favorite period piece.

The acting is the best of any movie I've seen from the 2000s so far. Daniel Day-Lewis was PERFECTLY cast and rivals some of the best performances I've ever seen. Every other performance was amazing as well, and the characters are memorable as hell. You WILL remember their names at the end of the film.

The screenplay is masterful, really elevating the engagement level of the film that was already high as hell thanks to the performances, with tons of memorable lines and being snappy, to the point, and nearly flawless. The score was beautiful, and surprisingly memorable. Normally, films will have decent-to-good scores, but I'll forget them after a few days. I don't think I'll ever forget the violin parts of this. The sound was amazing; specifically in that opening scene, where there's no dialogue. Just Daniel on his own, mining in a pit. It is one of the most captivating opening scenes I've ever seen. Not to mention fantastic production design, amazing cinematography and great visual effects.

This American epic really is a classic. It rightfully deserves its high rank on the Letterboxd Top 250, and is an unforgettable watch for anyone that has a pulse. EASILY Paul Thomas Anderson's best work that I've seen so far. (Still have to see The Master, Inherent Vice, and Phantom Thread) Why are you still reading this review? Go to Netflix and watch it!

Name: The Master
Review: Really damn well done and purely beautiful. Arguably better performances than There Will Be Blood (Philip Seymour Hoffman was great and Joaquin was OUTSTANDING) with fantastic cinematography and editing, a great screenplay, stellar production design, a clear direction, a great soundtrack and score, and just being one of the more engaging films Paul Thomas Anderson has made; yet another one of his films that left me in stunned silence thoroughly. The way Freddie's mental illness portrayed is definitely one that will be ingrained in the heads of everyone who watches, and every character, even the supporting ones, still are an essential part to the story. I love how he goes all out and plays all his cards to make this a real experience. My only gripe is that it feels like it has some uneven pacing and loss of focus at a couple points (in both the plot and my attention span), but those are the only thing that makes this excellent period piece fall short. I love how the final scenes draw back to the beginning as a sort of reflection on how far he's come; reminded me of the final scenes of Moonlight and it was great! Only have two more Paul Thomas Anderson films to go! I'd also like to mention that Philip Seymour Hoffman's group was called the 1st Phoenix Congress. Is this an easter egg referencing Joaquin Phoenix?

Name: Jurassic Park
Review: Saw it again. Fun movie but I noticed that the acting isn't that great this time around.

Name: Airplane!
Review: I probably would've appreciated this more if I saw it for the first time in like middle school, but this was still good. It's definitely funny with a lot of good lines, though it sorta falls victim to most other comedies in this era in the sense that it offers little else.

Name: Inherent Vice
Review:
Huh. Well, it was interesting.

It wasn't a BAD movie by any means, but it easily felt like the least special Paul Thomas Anderson film. The acting was good (though after Joaquin's performance in The Master, this felt very 'less than' except for a couple good parts) and the dialogue was pretty good as well, though it was the least interesting and didn't really hold my attention. The plot was confusing at times despite being pretty fun, and it just didn't feel that distinct from other movies he has done like Punch-Drunk Love and Boogie Nights. It's the only PTA film that I truly feel like could've been done by a different director. I guess it deserves a second watch? But I also barely remember any character names and I just watched it, so it's definitely not a memorable film either.

Just need to knock out Phantom Thread and I can move onto bigger and better binges!

Name: Reservoir Dogs
Review: Saw with one of my friends for his first time. Goddamn this movie is so good.

Name: Full Metal Jacket
Review: Not quite as good as I remember; the second half had some meandering and questionable scenes, but the first 45 minutes are still flawless as hell. This will always hold a place in my heart as one of my favorite (war) movies, and I will never watch it and not say it's excellent.
Tags: dvd, w/ arnav, good needle drops

Name: Phantom Thread
Review:
Easily PTA's most meditative film; phenomenal cinematography, beautiful production and costume designs, mesmerizing sound, great writing, good acting, it's extremely well done, and feels like a piece of art from an experienced director like PTA himself. The development of Reynolds and Alma and their relationship is fantastic, and arguably some of his best character development. Not to mention flawless pacing, flowing and transitioning from one scene to the next in a great manner.

However, I have to say that I wasn't really able to connect with the story as much as I would've liked, and I did find myself distracted at many points. Part of the slowness is the nature of the film, though I don't think it's a top PTA overall. Highly recommended, either way!

Name: Persona
Review: My first Bergman. The most thought-provoking and tactful screenplay in any movie I've seen. Like, holy fuck. It's so haunting and eerie in a way not many films can match. The acting of Alma and Elisabet are amazing, the direction is excellent, the cinematography and editing are nearly flawless, the production design is nicely done, there are several memorable, chilling moments, and the way it explores themes through narrative and character study are nothing short of brilliant.

Name: Fish Tank
Review: Very creative film, lots of experimental filmmaking here and I love that. Katie Jarvis did an amazing job (as did Michael Fassbender) and it's a shame she hasn't gotten any more roles. One of the most down to earth films I've seen in awhile, in a great setting and a world that I can believe is real. Her character is developed extremely well from all standpoints. The cinematography and editing are really well done as well. The dialogue is decent too. There were a few parts of it that felt a little bit cartoonish and it's a bit hard to overlook, especially with a runtime that feels longer than necessary. It's worth noting there's a lot of downtime, and a lot of said scenes don't really have much of a point. So while it's great technically and acting-wise, the story really keeps it from being anything better than a 7. I frankly lost a lot of interest around the hour mark, and Katie Jarvis's character took a lot of different turns, a lot not being very good. Still underwatched, however. I think more people should check it out.

Name: Rear Window
Review: Spike Lee's 95 Film Recommendations Ranked My first Hitchcock was a fun time. The story is intriguing, and executed well with excellent cinematography and production design. It's set up exactly like a mystery should be, and I think that's where this shines brightest. The acting and writing were good, with some memorable lines and well-developed characters, especially the main few like Lisa and Jeffries. However, I will say there are some technical flaws towards the ending. The whole final scene was frankly very cheesy. Shots were cartoonishly sped up, the editing felt a bit fast, his fall out of the window looked very fake, and I would've loved to hear some upbeat, mysterious music lining it; the only sounds being those the characters were making made it feel really uneven. It also took a bit of time before it really got into the story; I love exposition and character development, though it did feel a wee long. Otherwise, however, this was entertaining and it made me excited for Vertigo and North by Northwest. Not a bad film at all.

Name: Parasite
Review: Saw it with a group of good friends, all of which except one would be watching for the first time. Everyone only had positive things to say at the end, showing how this film is NOT just for film buffs. If I'm being honest, it felt slightly less like a 10/10 film in the past, but that ending just shuts your brain off and gets your heart racing in a way like no other. Not quite perfect but damn near it. I'll never not love it.
Tags: hulu, w/ farris, w/ shrey, w/ arnav, w/ dad

Name: Percy Jackson & the Olympians: The Lightning Thief
Review: Literally only gave this film half of my full attention at best and I could still easily tell what was happening. I can definitely tell this was supposed to reflect upon Harry Potter (Sorcerer's Stone specifically, since it's the first one) and build a franchise off of that, though... yeah. It's hard to believe this was a Chris Columbus film, after he did Sorcerer's Stone and Chamber of Secrets so well. A promising cast knocked down the charts by mediocre dialogue and a lot of meh CGI. It was repetitive as hell, and the only thing it really has going for it is some fine acting and actually good cinematography. Also shockingly violent for a family movie, a lot of it unnecessary. Oh, and the scene where they get high in Las Vegas is one of the stupidest things I've ever seen and made no sense at all. It wasn't funny, it was just totally stupid. Wait, I forgot to mention it's also nothing like the books. Tons of fantasy movie cliches and a surprising amount of predictability here, even for a movie of this scale. At least it had a decent story, I guess. Only really watched it for my 2010 binge; it ain't worth your time.

Name: Shutter Island
Review: The film looks fantastic with amazing production and costume design, decent cinematography and some dazzling visual effects. I wasn't huge on some of the editing choices, though there are worse films for this. Definitely very haunting and I do like that. Leo did a good job though he's had better performances. There are some good themes explored throughout as well, which is always a plus in my book. Although I wish it was more consistently engaging than it was; the plot points felt a bit repetitive after a bit, and sometimes a bit cartoonish, which I wish wasn't the case because this is a really heavy subject matter and could be explored in so many different ways. Thinking about it on paper is anxiety-inducing on its own, so it has a TON of potential, which I don't feel all of which was unlocked. Although it does feel very real with some seriously intense and twisted moments, so it does the job. It just doesn't feel as well put together and a bit messy. My least favorite Scorsese out of the few I've seen so far, just because it really didn't amaze me like others have, but still a good movie.

Name: The Silence of the Lambs
Review: Amazing movie. Some of the best performances I've ever seen, brilliant directing and a fascinating screenplay, great cinematography and editing, excellent pacing and a story structure that's also unpredictable, and lots of deep themes conveyed in clear fashion. I was hooked from start to finish and it really felt real. The only reason why it doesn't get the full 5 stars from me is because it isn't a film I'm itching to get back to real soon; it's so intense and realistic that a second viewing sounds like a challenge. But based on the film quality itself, this definitely makes my top 20 of all time. Check it out when you can!
Tags: netflix, car dvd / on a roadtrip

Name: Vertigo
Review: Spike Lee's 95 Film Recommendations Ranked I was really tired from a weekend in St. Louis when watching it so I wasn't able to give it my full attention, but from what I remember it was good. Felt messier than Rear Window and had a less interesting plot, but otherwise my thoughts on Rear Window are pretty similar to Vertigo. Deserves a re-watch.

Name: Death Proof
Review:
Purchased the uncut/extended DVD of it a while back, it was a great experience. I think I enjoyed it a little less this time around, but I still love this movie!

I also still think this is like an ultimate showcase of what he's known for.

Name: Pulp Fiction
Review: Watching the gimp scene was insanely uncomfortable with my parents (my dad had seen it once before but it was my mom's first time). Otherwise they both enjoyed it, though this might've been my least favorite viewing of it so far. But it was amazing to see in theaters!
Tags: ncg yorkville, w/ dad, w/ mom, ncg xtreme

Name: Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets
Review:
What a way to kick off August!

Star Wars crossed with Men In Black, watered way down with overused CGI (a lot of it not good) and everything else bad you've heard about this film... bad acting, mediocre story and character development, shitty dialogue, lots of unnecessary scenes, the story doesn't really start until 40 minutes in, predictable as hell with tons of logic flaws, a hilariously stupid love story that rivals Anakin and Padme, etc. etc. It's not a good movie. I will forget about it in a few days. It at least makes an attempt to dive into deeper concepts like corrupt governments and self identity, as well as having some good cinematography and decent editing, but those can't save all the other parts of the film which missed the mark by a mile. The end.

Name: Lady Bird
Review: Beautiful character study that's thoroughly engaging and really entertaining, being funny and heartfelt. The cinematography and editing are fantastic, the production design is amazing, the dialogue is near flawless, and the messages and vision are conveyed extremely well. I really was able to connect with what was happening in most situations and that's always a good thing. After watching the first trailers, I did half expect it to be a bit overrated since they didn't seem that impressive, but I really did enjoy this a lot. It's not perfect; there are some moments of uneven pacing which is my main complaint for the film, and a few performances didn't really do it for me (some of Lady Bird's friends). I also wasn't huge per se on the ending, though I personally agree that high school love isn't real love so I think it was great and fit the style/tone perfectly. However, I do think it lived up to the hype, and the creativity and relatability as mentioned earlier make this film excellent.

Name: The Lego Batman Movie
Review: Starts off as just being ok, then it gets progressively better and more clever. Unfortunately, then, the ending is pretty cheesy and was a little bit frustrating IMO. Not quite up to the standard of the original, both for the first half and not being up to par with the original's commentary, but still a really entertaining time. Batman also made a reference to Pulp Fiction and I think that's really clever of the writers to do that in a kids movie :)

Name: Get Out
Review:
He did, indeed, get out.

This is a great movie that I didn't fully understand on first watch. I stupidly expected a regular horror movie + social commentary but it's far more special and very well made. It's not a terribly scary movie but I love the ambition. Plus the ending is very satisfying. I will also say that the fact that I was able to get so excited over the Armitage family's deaths shows how well each character was developed through the hatred of those watching, so that was great too. Yeah, It's really great; imagine having this movie as your director debut.

Name: X-Men
Review: Outdated and uninteresting, sorry. Not looking forward to the rest of the franchise, but I'm doing it for Logan.

Name: Being John Malkovich
Review:
#monkeymentalhealthmatters

My first Kaufman! I watched this on Netflix Party with some fellow friends on Letterboxd - skylerinthecine, streetsahead55, and boris2004. This film is wacky and peculiar, and the best way I can describe it is it perfectly encompasses the emotions you feel during a weird dream at night. Your brain picks the most random of things going on in your mind, and combines it into something that almost seems believable in the weirdest way, but really shouldn't. It was so inventive and unpredictable and I honestly loved it. The acting is great, the dialogue is great, the cinematography and editing are fantastic, the music is really good, there are messages of self-identity that are explored in bizarre, but effective fashion, and the many directions the plot takes really elevate it to a whole other level for me. And those last 20 minutes? S h e e s h !! It gets cut short of the 10/10 just because I do think there were some pacing issues at a few points that took me out of what was going on, but otherwise, this seriously has to be one of the best directorial debuts in film history.

And for Skyler... Kaufman said "gay rights"
Tags: netflix, virtual watch party, w/ skyler, w/ max, w/ boris

Name: Casino
Review: Absolutely insane. Beautifully shot and super vibrant (easily some of Scorsese's best cinematography), one of the best scripts I've ever heard being snappy and interesting the whole damn runtime, a fantastic performance from Joe Pesci (arguably my favorite of his), the musical score chock full of jazz, and a few other key factors really do make this a great movie. I've also always admired how Scorsese is able to seamlessly incorporate the gangster's personal lives with their criminal lives when he makes these movies. It makes people like De Niro and Pesci seem more human and a character that's easier to connect to. I haven't seen The Departed or Mean Streets, but I can't imagine they're too different in that sense. And once you get to around the 1 hour 35 to 1 hour 40 minute mark, oh boy, it just keeps escalating until the end. I have no doubt in my mind that this is Scorsese's most brutal mafia film, but again, I'll hold my final judgement until I see the others. There's a lot to love and I think he did a great job executing a crime / mafia movie in the Las Vegas setting; lots of creativity is definitely evident here. I actually saw a lot of similarities to The Irishman here, so now I'm thinking about watching The Irishman again and doing some sort of comparison post. However, as you could tell by the rating, it isn't without it's flaws. I think it definitely drags more than Irishman or GoodFellas, so I'm not big on the pacing. I lost interest a few times, which is a big red flag in my book. I also think that every minute of this wasn't quite necessary; it would've been just as good at the very least, and probably better if it was closer in length to GoodFellas. And while not really a knock on the film, it should be noted that this is far more depressing than GoodFellas and Irishman, and that says something. I don't mind that, but it becomes a repetitive almost sort of game and it really took me out of it for a bit. It loses focus towards the end and just loses its style. I think that's the main reason why I docked a whole star off despite the rest of the film being so good.

Name: 12 Angry Men
Review: I must admit, I struggle to see the merit of this film. It is very good, and it does everything well. There aren't any clear flaws to it, and I think it's dialogue paired with the story development is excellent. It is very good in many ways, most notably showing how one voice can change a crowd, and I feel that's the message of this film. But I didn't find any of the characters super dynamic or memorable, and I often couldn't tell them apart aside from maybe three of them. I didn't get anything out of what was happening, and I felt like I was just watching this film because I had to, thanks to its reputation. Watching a film with that mindset is a big red flag for me. The scenes started to blend in my head, which in a way isn't entirely the film's fault as it's in black and white so every shot looks the same and the debate went in circles for the first hour, but still something worth noting. I would still recommend it, just because of it's extremely positive reputation and you can form your own opinion, but I personally could not see why it's as highly received as it is. Don't be surprised if I bump the rating down to a 3.5, but I think a 4 is appropriate based on my first watch.

Name: Memento
Review: I had to watch this twice in a row to fully understand what was happening. In my opinion, that's the mark of a truly excellent piece of film. I loved it, and I hope to see it many more times in the near future! You can hear much more on the film in other reviews, so I won't talk about it, but this is easily one of the best films Christopher Nolan has done.

Name: The Wolf of Wall Street
Review:
Truly one hell of a trip that deserves its reputation. It really does look fantastic; throughout the cinematography, production design, color choices in every frame, you name it. The technicals aren't flawless, as there were a few editing choices that bothered me and the sound was a bit of an issue at some points, but the technicals are great nonetheless. The acting was fantastic, and I totally feel DiCaprio and Jonah Hill deserved their Oscar nominations. The dialogue was snappy and never missed a beat, going full force without holding back, and the direction of Martin Scorsese did not fail to impress. The story structure and pacing were also pretty damn great. Even outside the technicals, this was weirdly fun and entertaining as hell in a really twisted way. Scorsese really shows us Wall Street's true colors, and his portrayal of who these rich douchebags are was really effective. It's fucked up and insane, just as Wall Street can be, with drugs, prostitutes, and legal issues from head to toe.

It was hard to find flaws in this, as Scorsese outdid himself, especially considering the scale of the film as a whole. Though something I must comment on is the supporting characters. I get this is a very Jordan Belfort-based film, though I don't think Naomi (Margot Robbie) had a lot of background other than looking hot and, in turn, winning Belfort's heart and causing them a very flawed marriage. She felt pretty one-dimensional, as did most other characters. Though aside from that, yeah, as I said, I see the hype. Check this one out whenever you can, it's one of Scorsese's best!

Name: GoodFellas
Review:
Better upon re-watch for sure. Much easier for me to see the hype now - this really is the quintessential mobster movie, no doubt about that! It's so raw in its storytelling and not holding back what the lifestyle is like. It's engaging pretty much the whole time and is fantastic across all filmmaking aspects. It is nearly flawless, though it is worth commenting on that my main issue with this is the same as with The Wolf of Wall Street. Aside from Henry Hill and his wife Karen, I found the other characters to be great though a bit underdeveloped to me. I appreciate that an effort was made through Henry's narrations, so for that reason I'd give this film the slight edge over WOWS (since this is the only flaw each film has), though I do wish that we got a little more background on Tommy and Jimmy, which prevents me from giving this the full 5 stars. Even then, I think the portrayal of mobsters in GoodFellas is slightly superior to the portrayal of Wall Street titans in Wolf of Wall Street. Although no matter what, both are excellent.

Sorry that this review was mainly a WOWS comparison lol, though since this is the second official film of the Scorsese binge not sure what else to say.

Name: X2
Review: Just as boring and cookie cutter as the first one, with slightly better CGI but worse dialogue and acting. At least I'm one step closer to Logan now.

Name: Kill Bill: Vol. 1
Review: This is my go-to film whenever my depression gets really bad and I thank it for its service.

Name: Kill Bill: Vol. 2
Review: Definitely not quite as good as the first, for me at least as far as personal preference and the quality of the film overall, but it's growing on me more and more.

Name: Raging Bull
Review: Spike Lee's 95 Film Recommendations Ranked Okay so I know this isn't in the Spike Lee list order, but I'm doing my Scorsese binge and it's on the list so who gives a flying fuck? This is a really fantastic film that does every well and engaged me the whole time. It takes a character profile of a professional boxer, someone who, to me at least (and hopefully others), doesn't seem like a normal person, and humanizes them but still making them appear superhuman, similar to Jordan Belfort in Wolf of Wall Street, but this feels more important to his career because it was early on and really influenced several films after the fact. It also is made to look and feel like a movie from that era, and I love it. It's very Tarantino of Scorsese (or, since this was made so much earlier, is it very Scorsese of Tarantino to make Kill Bill and Death Proof feel like they do?). However, Jake La Motta is a character I could personally connect to much more than others Scorsese has created, and I love that about this film. Unfortunately, however, I've noticed that Scorsese loves to follow the formula of rise-and-fall with wife issues, legal issues, and losing a bunch of friends thanks to the ego of either the protagonist or supporting characters. I give this film credit because it's the first to do so, and because De Niro did a fucking great job performing his part, though it falls a bit short of perfect for me.

Name: Hoop Dreams
Review: Spike Lee's 95 Film Recommendations Ranked Simply outstanding. Will be writing my full thoughts for a review on my Instagram, so check that out when I finish it, but this really did live up to its lofty reputation. Check out this masterpiece of film whenever you can. Definitely one of my new favorite films!

Name: Fargo
Review: Yeah I mean... it was decent but holy shit where did the reputation come from? I will admit it's very well written, well shot, and has some solid acting. There were some funny moments as well, and it was pretty engaging. And yeah, it's based on a true story which is crazy. But I couldn't overlook the fact that I got little to nothing out of it. There were some noticeable sound mixing issues, uneven pacing which took me out of the story a few times, and a lot of scenes just didn't seem to benefit what was happening in any way. Nothing really amazed me about it and there were times where I frankly felt bored. Maybe a re-watch will improve my view, but it was a pretty disappointing experience.

Name: Shrek
Review: I pity the younger generation who's growing up with Illumination.
Tags: ncg yorkville, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, ncg xtreme

Name: Shrek 2
Review: Mostly good, and has a lot of the same qualities as the first and lots of satire on previous movies, though it's a lot more predictable and uses more cliches than the first did. It didn't feel as inventive as the first overall, but still a fun time. Closer to a 7 than a 5.

Name: Y Tu Mamá También
Review:
Enjoyed this quite a bit. It looks amazing, the acting is pretty great, and it's very well written. I can definitely tell Cuaron put a lot of effort into this on a personal level, and being in high school right now, I personally was able to connect to the characters. That being said, however, I found it a bit hard to take something real serious out of it. The themes of friendship and trust are pretty solid and well developed through the whole thing, though there are other films that tackle these concepts just as well, if not better. I'm glad I watched it for sure, and I loved the first third of it (before they go on the road trip), though I found it hard to keep the same opinion for the rest of it. I personally found a lot more meaning out of Roma, though it's much better than Gravity and Solo con tu pareja. Currently debating between a 7 or 8, though I'm going back and forth with the rating.

But at the end of the day, this is the ultimate boys will be boys film. They have sex, talk about sex, have group masturbation, smoke weed... pretty much every popular high school dude's life. And it was portrayed excellently and in a somewhat relatable manner, as I said.

Name: Sherlock Jr.
Review:
Maybe the age of the film has something to do with it, but Sherlock, Jr. gave me a feeling I did not expect to get; humility. I was going in with the mindset of a funny, classic, and overall purely fun classic movie with lots of good antics and such. Though as Buster Keaton slept on the projector and lots of different effects were used to make him appear as he left his body and joined the characters on screen, I could fully understand the genius of this film and why it's so highly ranked. The creative mindset beyond it all is great, and there's no doubt in my mind that this was just as much of a joy 96 years ago as it is today. It's a fabulous film in its own right, innovation and all. It fit a really interesting story into 45 minutes and kept me engaged the whole way through. Nothing short of brilliant.

Also if the ending didn't inspire the ending of Death Proof, I don't know what did.

Name: Blinded by the Light
Review: I really loved this when I saw it for the first time, though it hasn't really held up. The whole thing doesn't look very good as far as lighting and editing, and the acting, aside from Javed, is pretty mediocre. The writing was alright though it did feel clunky at times, as did the overall pacing and story structure. However, I will say I did like the messages and how they were conveyed through his family's hardships and the way the music plays into the story is pretty good. And it's just a fun movie; makes a man feel good, ya know? Even if it is hard to believe at a few points.

Name: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
Review: I liked this, but as with other romances, I just didn't connect to it. I'm not really sure why, but the romance genre isn't something I ever really get anything out of. It's an extremely well done movie in pretty much every aspect, don't get me wrong. It just doesn't feel like a movie that's worth anything higher than what I'm giving it.
