Review:
Yeah I mean... it's Scott Pilgrim! This is one of the only acceptable movies glorifying a simp.

It takes a lot of risks and gets extremely creative and I'm sad more mature family movies nowadays play things like slightly insensitive jokes so safe.

Name: A Nightmare on Elm Street
Review: I can't decide between a 6 or 7 but yeah, this was an interesting movie. Lots to like, some of it outdated, but I had an enjoyable hour and a half in the theater. I personally think Scream is a bit better but Wes Craven is one of the horror titans of history for sure.

Name: Requiem for a Dream
Review: Damn, those The Real Cost ads have nothing on this. I'm starting to do quickies on my insta so I'll write a slightly more in-depth review there. But for now, wow. I can't think of a better way to say it than this hurt to watch.

Name: Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone
Review:
I decided to watch this and begin a Wizarding World binge after a long journey through ADHD and trying to avoid summer work for school. I love my brain.

The main takeaway I got from this is that it's funnier than I remember. It's not the central focus of the film of course, but it's funny kind of like the same way Parasite is funny. It's just well written for laughs along the way. Of course there are some details that I wish stayed in the movie from the book, but I do admire that the films all have a similar vibe. Some parts felt a little uneven and more cheesy than I would like, especially a lot of the effects and just plot elements in general. Not to mention the ending which was honestly really comical and corny. But the enjoyability factor runs fairly high. And I have major nostalgia from all 8 films, serving as some of my favorites from 2010-2016. I haven't seen any in a few years so I'm glad to finally get back to them.

Admittedly, I'm only doing this binge since I wanted to finish out Cuaron, but with Prisoner of Azkaban existing, I decided to knock out two birds with one stone.

Name: Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets
Review: I mean it's good? I like it more than Sorcerer's Stone because it introduces a far more expansive story to be covered in the rest of the films, which is always good in a sequel for a cinematic universe, and I think it's visually better as well. Not to mention the ending which is a lot darker and a great finale to the mystery aspect. But it's fairly equal in my mind just with some other subtle improvements.

Name: Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban
Review:
Yeah it's great, but it's also 2:15 AM so maybe I'll write a more detailed review in the morning.

Onto Goblet of Fire now, I'll try to hit the hay around 5:00 at this rate.

Name: Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire
Review:
Easily one of the best Harry Potter endings, and some fun Triwizard Tournament scenes, but the rest of the film accomplishes very little.

Also should be noted I finished this at 5:30 AM.

Name: Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix
Review: Does little to nothing except shallowly develop Umbridge for the first hour, then picks up on and off until the ending battles, but it's pretty forgettable all around. Not really in the mood to give a more detailed Harry Potter review until I watch another truly good one.

Name: Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince
Review: Weirdly liked this a bit more than I expected. The writing improves a lot over the past two, there's a lot of development in both character and story, and yeah it struggles with pacing and being memorable at a lot of points, but a lot of scenes make up for it. I'd say it's also one of the best looking films in the franchise.

Name: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows: Part 1
Review: Not bad at all but rather messy. There are a lot of exciting and memorable moments, and it's pretty well made, but the order of events gets really confusing and uneven to the point where even after seeing it a lot growing up I couldn't make out what happened in exact order.

Name: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows: Part 2
Review: I kinda wish I took these films a little more seriously when watching, as I think some of them are better than I'm giving credit for and I don't have much to say at all, but this was pretty good.

Name: Mad Max: Fury Road
Review: Maybe I'll watch Children of Men when this is over, but for now I'm settling on this fucking masterpiece. Even better the third time around.

Name: American Psycho
Review: Really underwhelming. Everything about it is fine, but the only thing that seemed noticeably good was Christian Bale's performance and that one insane chase/murder at the end. Most of it did little to nothing for me and I was mostly half engaged. I read up that there was supposed to be a message of self-discovery, and given how high this film's reputation is, I expected something really good out of it, but as I said, any sort of message flew over my head until the last 20 minutes or so. It also was just not a story I could believe. Sorry to say, American Psycho wasn't bad, but it's one of the most overrated films I've seen.

Name: Hereditary
Review:
This is my 200th movie I've logged on Letterboxd!

Great movie, super well done, though I need to give it a second watch in a home theater environment. Watching it on my portable DVD player probably didn't give me the best experience of it lol.

The acting is one of the high points here, and Toni did sincerely deserve an Academy nomination, but I don't know why I expected it that year considering how bad all categories were. Peter did alright as well, though he was a bit less believable than Annie. For a directing debut, it's really great, as you can tell Ari Aster really knew what he was doing on set. The story is great and it's well written, with some twisted family matter messages that you don't see too often. It felt a little repetitive and forgettable at times, but for the most part I think the concept was very well executed. Not to mention some really good visuals and audio.

Can't wait to see Midsommar!

Name: Inception
Review:
As with most Nolan films, IMAX is THE way to watch this movie; fuck I forgot how good it is. The last time I watched it was actually the first movie I ever logged on Letterboxd, so I was a bit skeptical to see if my memory would hold up... yeah fuck that, totally unwarranted. If I had a list, this would easily make the 10 best looking films of all time, but that's not anything new. It looks real damn insane, that's just a fact of life if you're a cinephile. Practical effects >>>>

And ya know, the acting, writing, themes, story, and level of engagement are all still great. Third time around is the best so far, and while a part of that probably is due to the setting, it really was hard to find words as Time by Hans Zimmer cut out with the spinning top. One of my favorite moviegoing experiences ever.

Idk about you guys, but with Tenet's nationwide release 2 weeks from today and early access showings in a week and a half (will try to see it as early as August 31 but we'll see where life takes us with the new school year), I think it's time for a Nolan binge.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, imax

Name: Midsommar
Review:
Huh...

A lot to talk about here but probably not enough. I'll do my best.

Pros
-Florence Pugh did pretty amazing. For a lot of the movie I really believed her character and pain, and her performance really is one of the most memorable I've seen. The other characters did pretty good as well, though they are a lot more forgettable.
-I was engaged pretty much the whole time. The film didn't feel like it was 2 and a half hours thanks to it, and that's always a good thing. It's a very visual film overall, thanks both in part to it's excellent cinematography, editing, production design and use of color and keeping track of all that's happening, so thanks to this, it isn't a forgettable film in itself by any stretch, characters notwithstanding.
-Building on that, the visual effects are some of the best I've seen in a horror movie. The use of prosthetics and makeup on the dummies on the cliffside were very convincing in particular, though any time there's a death or general use of violence, the realism and practical effects added a whole lot. I'm glad to see a modern horror movie that doesn't rely on heavily used CGI. And the drug trip-esque scenes? Wow
-I found some social commentary at a few points on race, specifically people coming in from a different country and how they act, how they're treated, and how they adapt to that country's traditions and beliefs. Normally, people coming in from a different country and acting like they're still in said country is made in films to comedic effect (Borat comes to mind), so it's interesting to see this topic taken in a more serious and strange manner.

In the middle
-The writing was decent, though there isn't a single memorable line in the whole film (for me at least) and most of it was used to keep the story going. I learned a bit more about the characters through their actions rather than their words, and with a movie that takes it's narrative so slowly, I feel great dialogue is crucial. Nothing against the pacing or anything of the like however, I feel the slow burn is wholly necessary.

Cons
-Ari Aster intended this to be a "breakup movie dressed up as folk horror" but I fail to see how the former is that prevalent. Dani and Christian do sort of non-verbally breakup when she sees him having non-consensual sex with a Swedish girl and when she just lets him burn at the end, but if being a breakup movie was the sole theme in Ari Aster's vision, it's pretty forgettable in the grand scale of things. It's nearly never brought up from the moment the festival starts to the end, aside from a small dynamic between her and Pelle that was hard to believe. I understand a lot of the development early on in the film relies on their tense situation back in the USA, but that's it.
-Building off of this, there are very few character dynamics once all hell starts breaking loose. No one really sees any further development of their character, and they're just sort of experiencing the festival in their own ways - if the tensions continued and were somehow built into the horror of the festival's traditions, I feel like that could've helped us connect a bit more to everything.

The film in its concept is so original and creative, especially given the intended themes and narrative combined with the location. But aside from the acting, visual stun, engagement, and brief social commentary, I wish it felt more complete and consistent in what it does.

Name: Host
Review: One star for being creepy at times with some decent low budget effects, half a star for the creative concept, and half a star for not being any longer than it is. Somehow this has a 100% on Rotten Tomatoes which is hilarious to me.

Name: Interstellar
Review: Extremely disappointing and frustrating. I'll go into more details in the morning since I'm pretty beat today.

Name: Following
Review: Not a bad first film. A solid story, with some pretty good cinematography and editing, and the acting and writing aren't bad either; Nolan does show some early potential in those areas. It just really doesn't do anything nor does it make me feel any different, and the plot structure feels pretty uneven. I'll probably forget a good bit of it in a few days.

Name: Batman Begins
Review:
I was in the process of writing a really good and detailed review but my dumbass accidentally deleted the tab so I'll keep it short.

Christopher Nolan was tired of seeing formulaic, bland superhero movies for years so he decided to make one with drama and real character connection. He was also frustrated that no other Batman movie covered the origin story of his character. So therefore, this is important and influential. Even today, it has better engagement with the audience than most superhero movies. And while it gets overshadowed by Dark Knight, it's still well written, well acted, and flows very nicely. And of course it looks great, aside from a few effects which are outdated.

Name: The Dark Knight
Review: I mean, yeah. I have to admit I'm not huge on the editing and I still think some of the deaths appear a little fake. I'm also not too huge on Two-Face and his motives for being a villain, but it's the Dark fucking Knight. Really is brilliantly written, well acted, and the plot really slaps. The realism and believability of Joker's terrorism is amazing, and the setup for every scene is masterful. Maybe overrated by some, but 12 years later, Dark Knight still is excellent.

Name: Anomalisa
Review:
Before today, whenever I saw a trailer or photo from the film, I thought every character had glasses, but it's just the facial structure of each person. And it plays a big part of the story at one point. Huh!

This was so fucking beautiful. Feels weird to say since some of its content is a little bizarre, but you know; that's just the Kaufman quirk. It's a really solid character study with absolutely amazing animation, great voice acting, stunning music, and truly excellent writing, also being engaging throughout. Not to mention some seriously great symbolism and creativity in the portrayal of specific themes. And while I think the story is a very relatable one that does work, I wasn't super invested with the characters themselves. I wasn't huge on their dynamic from the start, and when he eventually lost interest in Lisa, I wasn't that surprised or torn up. Yes, it is painful to see it fall apart at the end, but I did sort of see it coming. It was very well paced and I totally believed what happened as a real story, but Michael didn't feel that special compared to similar movie characters. We didn't really touch too deeply into him as a person, and for that reason, I think the movie would've benefitted from some more runtime for us to learn more about him. Funny thing is, I found a lot of connection to Michael as a character at the beginning, though from about the 50 minute mark to the end, it just got less and less. But don't get me wrong, it's still a great movie and Charlie Kaufman did an amazing job, though it doesn't quite check all the boxes for me.

Name: Dogtooth
Review:
The best thing I can say that made this film memorable, aside from the disturbing moments, was that the cinematography was excellent. Otherwise, everything in between the disturbances left me very unmoved. There are some high energy points, but they're immediately followed by more nothing. It's an interesting concept and pretty weird at times, as well as having some pretty good social commentary, but the process of watching it just felt like a waste of time.

Also it should be noted that I'm very tired and having a hard time transitioning back into the school year so maybe I would've enjoyed this more if I was of better mind.

Name: A Grand Night In: The Story of Aardman
Review:
Turns out, I actually saw this a few years ago, but it was a fun watch for the evening. I grew up with a lot of these and while it was a nice nostalgia trip, I didn't quite take as much out of it as I expected? Aside from some of the celebrity interviews, it wasn't much that I couldn't have taken away from a Wikipedia page. But again, it's still quite the fun watch and reminds you a lot of what made these films great.

Also not afraid to admit I almost cried at a couple points. Aardman really did make my childhood happy!

Name: Insomnia
Review: Either gets a 6 or 7 so I might give it a bump depending on my mood, but this was pretty good. Fairly standard detective movie in a unique location with some great acting and decent writing. I didn't get a whole lot out of it per se, but it was a pretty good way to pass 2 hours. It should also be said that I'm not huge on the editing. Of course, this could be the fault of Nolan or the cinematographer for having it this way, but there were a lot of messy and sudden cuts that just kinda ruined the flow. I've noticed this can be a problem in other Nolan movies as well, but it was especially distracting here.

Name: The Dark Knight Rises
Review:
Glad to say I don't understand the hate for this. Pretty well acted, some seriously good direction, engaging and did not feel as long as it actually is, visually stunning, and an overall satisfying conclusion. It was just a lot of fun the whole way through and it has the Nolan touch. And that whole ending sequence was excellent.

That being said, it isn't the perfect film. Bane wasn't a bad villain, however I do think he felt a little too similar to Joker. He is a memorable villain physically, but the motives are very similar and it felt like they were going after the same thing. Nolan intended Bane to be almost a Dickensian creation, but he didn't have a very distinct dynamic. I also have to admit that while Dark Knight seemed to have some social commentary on terrorism and how it's viewed and handled as a society, Dark Knight Rises felt more like a basic comic book movie with some extra drama.

Lastly, I think it's worth noting the editing. Something I've noticed in Nolan's films are a lot of messy cuts that feel too sudden, along with a lot of camera angles to match. There was a lot in this film that was implied but not actually shown, and while Dark Knight does this as well, Dark Knight Rises does it a lot more and it's pretty obnoxious.

Despite all that, however, I fail to see why some people rate this film so low. It doesn't hold a candle to The Dark Knight, but it's a great movie in its own right.

Name: Black Panther
Review:
No review or log, I just wanted to comment on Chadwick Boseman.

He managed to give some spectacular performances, some of the most unforgettable in recent cinema. He battled colon cancer for 4 years, and did not let that drag him down. He was one hell of an actor and a true king who had great talent, which died too soon.

So long, blood. Rest in power. 😭🖤✊🏾

Name: American Beauty
Review:
Magnolia: Who are you?
American Beauty: I'm you, but stronger in every possible way.

Call me a basic film student if you want... this film was fucking exquisite. The subject matter is a pretty strange one, indeed, and I suppose that could cause people to not like this film. But I'm really not sure how more normal this could be portrayed. This is one of less than maybe 10 films I've seen that I would consider using the word 'perfection' to describe. It deserves a review of its own, but I think I'll stick with a brief Letterboxd log for now.

I never know what order to go in when writing reviews, so let's start there. The writing. The script is absolutely fantastic and to the point, being memorable and having a very natural flow. It's such a big part in development of all areas, and I can honestly say it's one of the best scripts in a movie I've seen. And, of course, another part of this is the acting. Kevin Spacey, Annette Bening, Thora Birch, and everyone in between did absolutely fantastic. I completely believed them as individual, memorable, and relatable characters.

Going along with that, the plot really is something special. It reminded me a lot of Magnolia, in the sense that it's about a few different stories that come together eventually, but it's so unpredictable and takes so many different turns along the way. The storytelling is brilliant and takes a subject matter that could easily have been too weird or complicated for enjoyment, but it was put in the right hands, and Sam Mendes put his mind to work to create this masterpiece.

The movie is thoroughly engaging and there's a lot of social commentary on identity and how we judge people. Thanks to the excellent story development and plot twists as mentioned, it's hard to know when we've truly seen each character complete a full arc. It was incredibly thought-provoking for me personally, and I went through an emotional roller coaster that has lots of inversions and airtime hills.

Lastly, I'd like to touch on some of the technical aspects. First off, the music was outstanding and was always used at the correct times, sounding like a Randy Newman soundtrack (I now realize it was written by Thomas Newman, which comes at no surprise). And finally, the cinematography was brilliant, the editing was smooth with even cuts, and the use of the color red was beautiful.

Name: Dunkirk
Review: Not necessarily bad by any means, but it didn't do anything great either. It feels very similar to a lot of war movies of recent years and it doesn't really accomplish much, at that. There wasn't much narrative so that probably had something to do with it, but I just wasn't very interested aside from the high-stress moments and battle scenes,

Name: Memento
Review:
So close to finishing my Nolan binge! Giving this another watch just to see if I'll bump up my rating to a 10. Check back in a couple hours to see!

Alright, so... strangely I actually liked it less this time. A lot of it held up from the first viewing and kept my view of this as a great movie. though there are some style inconsistencies, there isn't a whole lot of payoff and the twist behind his wife felt a little out of place towards the end. This was also Nolan's first mind-fuck film, and it feels like he's trying to find his defined style within this genre that's improved upon later in his career.

Name: The Thing
Review:
A genuinely thrilling and gross horror sci-fi that holds up today! Since spooky season started yesterday for many people (Mary Shelley's birthday), I guess you can consider this my first horror movie of the halloween season?  I have a list ready of a lot more horror movies to hit within the next two months, as I generally keep September and October horror based.

This really does have some of the best practical effects and makeup in horror movie history. It looks stunning with some great cinematography as well, and the whole thing is just super believable. It makes for some really great jumps and tense moments. However, I do have to say that outside the visuals, say hypothetically the visuals were subpar at best, this would be a very forgettable movie. The next best thing I can say is that it was pretty well paced with a good story structure. The acting and writing are decent, but nothing to write home about. The music was either very mediocre or absent from a lot of scenes; very out of character considering it was composed by Ennio Morricone (God rest his soul). It's mostly engaging, though a few points felt a bit too talky without accomplishing anything and it kinda took me out of the film for a bit. And I mean, it's an 80s horror movie, so it's not going to be really deep or anything, but given its huge reputation, I was expecting maybe a little food for thought. It's a very good movie overall, don't get me wrong, but why it's so high on the Letterboxd Top 250 is beyond me. Maybe it's just one of those films that'll get better on re-watch, as that's what it seems to do for a lot of people, but I finished the movie feeling the same as I did when I started.

Name: There Will Be Blood
Review: Still ruthless, badass and masterful as the first time, though I kinda wish I hadn't watched it the same day as Synecdoche - my brain has melted over the course of the day. But even with that in mind, I still liked it to the point of staying my favorite. It just goes to show how flawlessly made this film truly is.

Name: I'm Thinking of Ending Things
Review:
The symbolism is easily Kaufman's most complicated in any of his movies he's written or directed, and while I wish some more open ends were closed and concluded, I think the character arc of Jake is one of the best he's done. The story structure is sort of all over the place and it's frustrating at times, but a lot of questions could be asked. Synecdoche and Anomalisa feel a bit powerful thanks to as a whole, but this was still a pretty great film that deserves a re-watch. The whole thing has a very drug-trippy / psychedelic feel to it that I appreciate as well. It took me a bit of time to fully understand some of what was happening, and in turn made me feel fairly frustrated at times, but the re-watch should probably bump it up to a 9 for me. My favorite film of the year so far!

And, of course, you have to admit the acting is pretty great, the writing is excellent, and it looks fantastic visually.

Name: Annihilation
Review: Spooky Season 2020 As the first "official" movie on my spooky season watchlist, I was really excited to watch Annihilation. That and also the fact that I've been interested in this film ever since I saw previews for it in theaters a couple years back. And I have to say that I was unfortunately expecting a bit more. Ever since I first was blown away by the main trailer, I was expecting some seriously badass sci-fi thriller, as that's what many reviews had stated it was. I was aware that it would also be a bit more meditative than one might think, and I think that's totally cool, except pretty much nothing happens the first 40 minutes. Everything that was established and developed seemed to be already done in the trailer, and I didn't learn anything new about the characters. The screenplay and acting were so vanilla, and there were a lot of missed opportunities for snappy dialogue and creative cinematography, but it just felt unspecial up until they actually went into the Shimmer. To its credit, however, the Shimmer looks absolutely excellent, inside and out, and the sound mixing was great. The visual effects are all very well done, and the overall realism factor was breathtaking through and through. It's pure and clean eye candy. Not to mention a pretty epic score as well, even if it isn't always used at the correct times. And it goes with anyone who watches this that the last 20 minutes of the film are completely mesmerizing. I will say it seemed to have been done a little too easily for a sense of satisfaction or truly feeling blown away, but it looks beautiful nonetheless. The original idea behind the film was for Alex Garland to make more of a memory of the book rather than an adaptation, but to still keep the dreamlike qualities to set it apart from other similar films, and through every technical aspect, I think Garland nailed it. I just wish the characters and their development through the story didn't feel so static. And with a cast as stellar as this, you'd expect some seriously good performances, but they were weakly written and didn't seem to give much emotional expression, especially considering how stressful some of the situations they get in to are. The only true standouts to me were Gina Rodriguez and sometimes Natalie Portman, which is a shame considering some of the other names on the roster. Still, it's an interesting story with a lot of themes that are executed fairly well, even if not explored to extents as deep as the Shimmer itself. The best way for me to describe it is a really cool movie, and I think it's worth checking out.

Name: Baby Driver
Review: Watched with some friends for my birthday party.

Name: Shaun of the Dead
Review: Spooky Season 2020 Edgar Wright mastered making tonal shifts feel natural on day one. This is the perfect blend of satire, seriousness, horror, comedy, and it all feels like it's own individual genre. Sorta like Parasite, but with a real parasite (haha, get it? Because zombies?). The tonal shifts alone make this a great movie. And yet, you still have great performances and excellent characters written very similarly to the real actors, lots of connections to real life overall (Edgar Wright made this surprisingly personal) as well as movies and games that Wright and Pegg loved that serve as inspiration instead of overt copying (something that I feel is often taken for granted), themes of friendship that are honestly above average for any movie, a nice and familiar soundtrack, sweet visual effects, and a really solid screenplay. It really deserves its praise as a cult classic that pokes fun at romcoms and horror movies in a thoroughly engaging way. Can't wait to check out the rest of the Cornetto trilogy!

Name: The Nightingale
Review: UPDATE - I don't like this review anymore, but I'm keeping it up for posterity. The TL;DR for how I feel in retrospect is that the first 30 minutes are 30 of the most terrifying minutes I have EVER seen. Everything after that is good but not on the same level. I wanna see it again if I can bear it for a more solid opinion. = = = = = Spooky Season 2020 Pretty mixed feelings on this. On the surface level, this was really great. The acting is spectacular, particularly Aisling Franciosi who has a really promising future. It's well directed as well, with some solid ideas and plot elements. It also looks the part, with good cinematography and editing along with the color choices and production design, as well as having really crisp sound, though nothing creative is done with the mixing or individual sound effects. However, once you delve into the film, things go downhill a bit. Clare is a pretty inconsistent character and while I think you're supposed to root for her, the way she was written went back and forth on a lot of things up until the last half hour or so when she becomes a fairly decent person. She's an independent woman who fights for freedom and revenge, which is good, but her relationship with Billy is obnoxious. A lot of it goes back and forth between Clare being no better than other white people and her actually being considerate. The transition between each felt really messy and annoying a lot of the time. Billy is the best character in the film, even if he doesn't see much change, but unfortunately, given my other issues with the film, it was hard to feel too terribly strong for him. And the other characters, while semi-memorable, don't see any change and can often be very predictable. Racism as a theme is here, but it's hardly touched upon aside from a couple moments, and for that reason as an example, I think there's something major missing from the film. It's lacking a genuine concept + execution and just didn't feel like there was anything really good about what it was trying to say. Yes, black people are treated like shit in this, and it gives us sympathy towards them, but it felt the same every time. Worse yet, I forgot that this was even a revenge movie at a few different times. The writing as a whole is decent, but there isn't a line that I can recall as being particularly good, and the dialogue is only there to develop character and the story, without serving up anything else worthwhile. One other really notable thing is that this movie doesn't have any score, and I really don't think it would've done any harm if there was one. There are a lot of tense moments, and the most important scene which spans from the 25-30 minute mark, really did give me chills and keep me watching, but that energy is absent from the rest of the film. Every time someone is killed, there aren't really any stakes and they lack any notable shock value. The trailer for this film has that consistent energy, met with intriguing sound mixing absent from the film itself, and I was expecting this to be a legitimately scary watch, but after the first 45 minutes or so, I could just tell something was missing. The plot is a really interesting one, and with some better, more prominent themes plus improved writing all around, this could be a great film. Though it's hard to deny that there's something missing.

Name: Shame
Review:
This really hit hard in many ways, a fair amount of which were painful. It's hard to find the words to describe my thoughts on this brilliant film. If I try to dive into the messages and themes of the film and how they impacted me, I'd probably be describing the impact it had on me in a way I'm not quite comfortable putting on the internet. Plus, I feel like I haven't quite gathered all my emotions this soon after watching; it's a film I'll need to sit on for a couple days, so I'll just talk about the surface level stuff.

Excellent acting, great directing, brilliant writing, beautiful cinematography, masterful plot development and choices for tone, and everything you see on screen really does serve a thoughtful purpose. One of the best character studies ever put on to film that I'll probably come back to soon and write a full review.

Name: Shame
Review: Loved it more the second time. I'm pretty certain this is masterpiece-status cinema. About to get started on a review for insta!

Name: Hot Fuzz
Review:
I've watched too many good movies recently. I need some bad shit to keep a solid balance of movie quality.

As for the movie itself... it was good, but ya know, if Sergeant Angel owned a camera or anything that records video, this whole movie wouldn't have happened?

Name: Battle Royale
Review:
Pretty damn good movie. I'll write more thoughts in the morning.

Ok so it's a little past the morning but here we go. Great directing and acting, pretty nice writing (though Tubi had a fucking annoying dub, but I was able to adjust after a while), some really good practical effects, and the whole thing really did look excellent. The cinematography in particular really stood out. I was engaged the whole time as well, and I think the representation of high schoolers and how they would act in every situation is still accurate today (and this is coming from a 17 year old). I wish the ideas of trauma and development of characters were a little more in depth than they were, as I felt that would've pushed this to masterpiece level cinema, but overall it's absolutely worth checking out (not on Tubi, though. Watch without the dub)

Name: The Colossus of Rhodes
Review: It's a pretty decent movie, especially considering it's Sergio Leone's debut. Visually, this film is fantastic even today. Some of the best production design I've ever seen, and when there's choreography, it's brilliant. If you think Spartacus looks great, this looks even better. Unfortunately, it's not as good as Spartacus thanks to overall having a rather dull execution of the story, and forgettable characters. The slightly over 2 hour run time was a bit excruciating at points, but there were some exciting moments in between.

Name: Saw
Review:
This was a totally impulse decision to watch this, I'm staying with my friends overnight and we're watching the Saw movies based on a group vote.

This was so mixed overall. The practical effects are really good and the story wasn't half bad either. The squeamish factor is also pretty good. The acting and writing were kinda bad though, and the cinematography was frankly bad at a few points. Some parts didn't make a whole lot of sense, but ya know, it's a horror classic so I still had fun. James Wan didn't intend to have any sequels so I'm looking forward to some shitty horror after this :)

Name: Saw II
Review: Lmao so many dumb decisions and just lack of logic and closure to the story. Pretty badly paced overall, at that.

Name: Pan's Labyrinth
Review: Yeah it was really excellent and pretty much flawless. Roger Ebert's review sums up all my thoughts well. I didn't quite get the emotions I was expecting from all that happened, maybe a re-watch will do it for me, but it pretty much was a masterpiece otherwise. Guillermo has such an incredible mind, and his eye for attention-to-detail is second to none. Very intrigued to see how The Shape of Water stacks up.

Name: Revenge
Review: Spooky Season 2020 This movie is ass. I know a lot of people like it, but I could not get into this. Not a single likeable character, mediocre dialogue, alright acting (actually one of the better qualities of the film), the pacing was trash, and it honestly felt more like torture porn than a revenge story for most of the runtime. It looks decent visually, but there's nothing spectacular about that either. The editing and cinematography honestly reminded me of a Michael Bay movie with tons of sudden and uneven cuts, and the camera rarely stops moving. There isn't a whole lot of logic behind what happens, and I was frustrated most of the time, hoping it would just be over. It's tense and a bit thrilling at times, but the trailer pretty much gives away most of the story, so a lot of it wasn't really a shock. Yeah it isn't completely terrible, but is in contention with Saw II for worst movie I've seen so far this spooky season.

Name: A Fistful of Dollars
Review: A rather good movie, a bit better than I was expecting. Excellent score, good writing, great production design, and it was just a fun time the whole way through. The plot reminded me a lot of Yojimbo, and while I think Yojimbo is the better movie overall, this was a good start to the Man with No Name Trilogy. Lots of good characters line the story as well. A lot of the effects are outdated, which I think is the biggest (and really only) complaint of the film, there's not really anything else bad about it. Plus, it's Sergio Leone's shortest film he made in his career. Check it out!

Name: For a Few Dollars More
Review: Not a bad movie per se, but nothing really great about it. Sergio Leone didn't really have much of a message or theme in mind when the film was being made, other than being a second western with Clint Eastwood, and it sorta shows. What made A Fistful of Dollars so interesting was how he wanted to reform the American western genre by giving it some Spaghetti / European western charm, via the plot of Yojimbo. And while I wish the plot was a bit more original, like this one, For a Few Dollars More felt very much like a sequel to Fistful rather than its own movie. There are also a lot of cartoonish bits that felt very much like the American westerns he disliked. It looked pretty good visually, about on par with the predecessor, though I feel some uses of zooming into Clint's face were used a bit inappropriately and too often; some shots looked brilliant, however. With the exception of some improved visual effects, the score, and slightly better dialogue, everything about the film feels lesser than Fistful. Again, it isn't bad per se, but it's not as memorable or notable as its predecessor, and in turn, made it a bit of a disappointment. I will say, however, the finale is a very fun one.

Name: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly
Review:
Master Pie.

Perhaps the greatest score in history, the token Sergio Leone production value from start to finish through its incredible art direction, a fantastic screenplay, lots of memorable and cleverly executed scenes, great character and story development, and yeah. It's a true icon. A classic story with beautiful pacing and brilliant structure. The acting is really good as well, even if they aren't the best performances in the world. Yeah, it ain't perfect; as with For a Few Dollars More, there were some shot framing techniques that I feel were used a bit too often. And yes, I'm going to comment on the outdatedness of some of the effects; I get that at this point in time it's a part of the film's charm, but worth commenting on. Lastly, I think the film loses some focus at about the 1:30 to 2 hour mark, but makes up for it with everything else good in that time. Super entertaining and thoroughly engaging, making the 3 hour runtime feel worthwhile. Check this out, and I honestly don't think it's a requirement to watch the other two in the trilogy beforehand. It works super well as its own movie. Might bump it up to a 10 tomorrow morning, we'll see.

TL;DR - I know this is an unpopular opinion, but this movie is fantastic.

Name: Crip Camp: A Disability Revolution
Review: An extremely well put together documentary, it's no surprise why it won an award at Sundance. It came out on Netflix soon after the stay at home order started, which now seems like ages ago, but I finally got around to this film today. Lots of brilliant old footage paired with great commentary make for a really interesting story on the fight for rights for the disabled. There are a few "main characters" that tell some great stories of the camp itself back in the day, but the fight itself was simply just fascinating. I'm a little mad I put this off for so long, because it's easily one of my top 3 of 2020 so far. This has been a great year for documentaries.

Name: Vampire's Kiss
Review: Spooky Season 2020 I actually really liked this lol, didn't expect that! It's not very good as far as technical aspects and directing (you can tell the director is very inexperienced through the way the film is made), but the writing is pretty decent, Nicolas Cage did a surprisingly great job at his character, and I was thoroughly entertained and engaged the entire film. It's nothing to shout at, but it was just a goof of a time. I see why it's become a cult classic. And I'm honored to have seen the film that created the legendary Nick Cage meme (you don't say)!

Name: Once Upon a Time in the West
Review: I really loved this. Sergio Leone tried to move away from a tongue-in-cheek parody of American westerns to more of a slow, thoughtful, and somber western that actually borrows the best pieces of the American-made movies of the genre. It has all the Sergio Leone trademarks I've mentioned in past reviews, with some great characters, excellent visuals (though the framing of some shots were questionable towards the beginning), the connection between each story is pretty great, and it is easily the best screenplay of any of his films so far. That being said, I just can't say there were many memorable scenes. I finished it a couple hours and have sat on it since then, and not as many scenes have stuck in my head as The Good, the Bad and the Ugly. I did lose interest sometimes as well, despite being well made overall, and it was just fun. It's a well-directed, great introduction to the next chapter of Leone's career that I think deserves a good bit of its reputation. I don't think it should be as high as it is in the top 250, but I wouldn't call it overrated either.

Name: Birdman or (The Unexpected Virtue of Ignorance)
Review: I guess they were trying to remind us that that's what all our egos and self-obsession are worth. I'm still not totally sure why, but tonight I decided to rewatch this, one of my favorite movies. If I had a list of films that have really stuck with me since my first viewing, there are some notable ones to mention. Adaptation, It's Such a Beautiful Day, A Clockwork Orange, Shame, etc. Just a few to mention. This would be up here with them. Real life is lived without editing, hence the use of one shot cinematography. Moments we live are framed a certain way, based on things like our emotions and the location of said moments, and the camera reflects that. We have regrets, we may freeze up in the moment, but in the end, nothing lasts forever and we just have to find the right place for us through time. Alejandro G. Innaritu is a brilliant director and everything is placed in all the right places to keep us engaged and smiling, captivating you from one room to the next. The script is perfect with all the right words and the flow is snappy and feels natural. The actors are phenomenal and give some of the best performances I've ever seen on the screen. It's a perfect mix of surrealism and realism. The culmination up to the Michael Keatom / Riggan Thomson climax (you know what I'm talking about if you've seen the film) works stupendously for the right amount of shock value. Yet, despite all this, it still leaves the asking questions about what it could possibly all mean in the grand scheme of things. I'm not sure what happens in a life for someone to become a critic, but I know I've done something right up to this point, because I've fallen in love with this film all over again.

Name: District 9
Review: Spike Lee's 95 Film Recommendations Ranked I mean, it was fine. I watched the short film that the director made as a precursor to this film and thought it was really cool, but I wish they went more with that same story for the movie. The movie is about a man who contracted a virus that turns him into one of the aliens, and aside from that, the aliens are just sort of in the background the entire time. I think I would've liked it considerably more if the aliens themselves had a bigger part in what was going on. The themes of apartheid and racial tensions are really creative given the concept, and they are apparent in a few shallow parts, but they really aren't delved upon and it really felt like something was missing. The story itself is fine, and I think the world is developed very well, but the pacing and structure felt uneven at times and had strange transitions. It's all backed up by a script that's decent and feels very natural, but lacking in memorable lines or anything that really stands out about it. This is the director's first feature film, so he was probably still getting his feet into the pool, but with Peter Jackson on the crew as well, I guess I just expected a bit better storywise? It wasn't a very engaging movie, and with some of what's happening (notably the action sequences), I think I was supposed to be more hooked on it. That aside, there are some good qualities to the film. The characters are quite good, along is the acting, the documentary style worked very effectively (though there are some shots that just don't seem realistic, at least based on where they were filmed from), the concept itself is original as I already said and the humanization of aliens was excellent, and it looks pretty good. Some CGI is a bit outdated but a fair amount of it holds up, and the use of practical effects in a few parts was great. Overall, it's worth watching, but it's safe to say I was left underwhelmed.

Name: Jojo Rabbit
Review:
7.99 stars for Sam Rockwell as a gay nazi

0.01 stars for all that other stuff. The screenplay, acting, directing, cinematography, art direction, tonal shifts, and symbolism are cool and all, but...


Seriously though, I had a great time with this just like the first watch in theaters almost a year ago. Can't believe it's taken me this long to see it again!

Name: Call Me by Your Name
Review: I mean yeah it was awesome I loved it and I kind of want to watch it again like right now to see if it goes up to a 10

Name: Night at the Museum: Battle of the Smithsonian
Review: I loved this series growing up so its interesting to just turn it on one night. Yeah it's not good but you know... nostalgia.

Name: The House That Jack Built
Review: Spooky Season 2020 My first Lars Von Trier. It was good but I have some mixed feelings. The acting and screenplay are great, and the scenes where Jack is talking to the doctor are excellent as far as pacing and the visuals. I found the "incidents" to be predictable, even if well done, and the second and fourth ones dragged a good bit. I also just wasn't a huge fan of the setup of each murder, and I felt pretty unmoved by them. But there are some pretty good themes explored here and even if it's a bit longer than it needs to be, it's an interesting film that's worth checking out. I'll definitely be checking out some more Von Trier soon.

Name: The Social Network
Review:
Very good but I have some criticisms. I didn't really connect with the story, nor did I feel like there were many great messages at play here. I get why some people love this film so much, but for me to give it a higher rating, it needs to have a bit more under the surface level as far as its themes. It's definitely a film solely about Mark Zuckerberg and how his relationships were affected by the creation of Facebook, but I didn't really get any reaction out of what was happening. It's a bit messily put together as far as pacing and structure.

I will say, however, the screenplay was fantastic, along with great directing and acting. Loved the cinematography too.

Name: Drive
Review:
Not sure if I like it more or less than the first time, but it is a great film, no denying that. It feels like it borrows a lot of elements from Taxi Driver, Reservoir Dogs, and classic movie heroes like Clint Eastwood, but Nicolas Winding Refn offers some authenticity to it that really defines it as one of his film, and that isn't an easy feat. Los Angeles as a city is portrayed in a fairly simplistic way as well, which I appreciate, rather than either a wonderland of glamour or a crime-ridden sin city (not the movie). It's also very worth noting that between this and The Neon Demon, Refn can make a 90-120 minute film feel much shorter than that. In short, he really knows his way around the director's chair.

That being said, I don't know if I'll ever give it a rating above an 8, or a place on my list of favorites for that matter. There's no doubt that there's more than meets the Drive (haha, get it?) when comparing it to other action films of recent, from the stellar technicals to the hidden meanings, but I still don't think I'm entirely on board with the pace it takes and a fair amount of plot points. Plus, there are definitely great and not so great parts to the dialogue. I know that sounds a bit confusing, but I'm writing a comparison between this and The Neon Demon, so I'll go more in depth there.

TL;DR - With apologies to all the filmbros who may follow me or read this review, Drive is not a perfect movie. But it is outstanding.

Name: Blade Runner
Review: Good movie. I definitely think there's a lot I missed, and I did feel underwhelmed overall, but I'll rewatch it for sure.

Name: Joker
Review:

Working on a nice, big essay on how this film has impacted me and was a source of me becoming a cinephile, so look forward to that soon.

The one thing I will say is that my first review is long, but a bit embarrassing as I still didn't fully understand who I was as a film buff. I'll have more cohesive and improved commentary in the essay.

Name: Nomadland
Review: What a beautiful movie. As I'm starting to write more about all that I see, I'm starting to keep my LB reviews shorter, so go ahead and follow my insta to start seeing those thoughts. The film and everyone involved is going to get award season love, I can can guarantee, and they deserve it.
Tags: hamptons 2020, virtual cinema, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Collective
Review:
Third and final film I purchased from this years' Hamptons festival.

The story that is covered in the film is a truly fascinating one. An entire country's extremely poor health system exposed is no easy feat to cover, and I think the film does this very well. However, the information itself and what I got out of it felt just a bit shy of doing some research, in the sense that I didn't get a ton out of what was shown. The directing and way the film is shot isn't done in an engaging way, and I often felt lost. We're just simply watching each person have a conversation with subtitles, and it doesn't really accomplish anything as far as allowing the audience to get something real out of the experience. There's definitely a lot that's covered well, and for that, I'd say it's a good movie. Just the fact that the amount of what was caught on film was done makes this worth watching and something to get excited over. But a lot more could've been done to make it more interesting overall, in quite a few aspects.

Name: Minari
Review:

Best performances of the year so far as well IMO

Name: Antichrist
Review: Spooky Season 2020 I don't know how to rate this. I'll get back to it sometime later today. It isn't a bad movie but I didn't like it either. A lot of choices as far as the story and pacing that I don't necessarily agree with. The first hour is masterful, the last 45 minutes are the opposite.

Name: Suspiria
Review: Spooky Season 2020 This is a work of art. Some of the best usages of color, cinematography, and editing I've seen. Not to mention a truly amazing score, with some really good suspense throughout the whole film. The writing was pretty good too, and the ideas behind the whole thing are executed really well. That being said, I wasn't that invested in what was happening. The story is good, the characters are good, but I just couldn't keep my attention going, and I think that has to do with mixed pacing. The acting was pretty good as well, though some performances were a bit cheesy / stale. Also a lot of the blood looked more like paint, but that's more of a nitpick compared to what blood could be in the 70s. Great movie, worth watching for sure. Can't wait to see Guadagnino's remake.

Name: Dear Comrades!
Review: Chicago International Film Festival 2020 Movie #1 Interesting movie. The technical style really reminded me of that of Roma and was honestly great. It was well directed too, and had some good writing (though the English translation had some grammar issues). The acting was mostly good, though some performances were below average. The story was good, which is obligatory considering how memorable the real event is, and I think it was executed well, however a lot of the talky parts lost me a bit. The second hour got pretty dull and I was taken out until the last 20-30 minutes or so. It's worth watching, though it reminded me a lot of what we call Oscar bait here. Its engagement levels really dipped for the last half of it movie. It's also worth noting that afterwards, there was a live Q&A with the director, and a question I sent in was asked. It was really exciting and the answer I got was fantastic. I'm going to work on a set of reviews from each film I'm watching at the festival, so I'll go into more detail there.

Name: Apples
Review: Chicago International Film Festival 2020 Movie #2 I'm really confused as to why the Letterboxd average rating isn't higher. The director is/was a protege of Yorgos Lanthimos and it shows; what a quirky, charming, and overall excellent film. It reminded me of Dogtooth, but I think this is superior. It all looks and sounds FANTASTIC. Though I think some editing could've been better, that's a small nitpick on the overwhelmingly good technicals. The development of the story is great as well, and though some plot points took me out a bit, I was engaged the whole time with the memorable characters. It's very well written too, another Yorgos alignment. The story is a sad one, and the emotional delivery was good for the most part, though I have to comment on the fact that the tonal shifts seemed to be a bit inappropriate at times. The music wouldn't necessarily match the emotions, or maybe the transitions themselves just felt clunky. Still, I really did love it and I can't wait to see it again (whenever that may be).

Name: Child's Play
Review:
I forgot to log this last night, but I had a friend over and we watched this for a good laugh. And that's what we got. This honestly would've worked way better as a satirical comedy for the most part lol. It's bad and stupid. I guess the originals could be described in that sense too, but they're way more fun than this (the first six were some of the earliest horrors I watched, at way too young of an age I might add). This "contemporary Chucky" gathers a lot of the worst horror stereotypes, including mass predictability, and turns it into a typical unenjoyable, low effort reboot, as you might expect. Most of the "modern touches" if you will are all the most Gen X views on today's younger generation and they all made me cringe. The Tupac line was the only one that I laughed at.

There honestly is a lot of potential here. I'd love to see this concept taken with more depth into how we can be controlled by society and large companies and such but it's such surface level bullshit. I guess I should re-watch the originals because I'll probably think less of them now.

Name: The Columnist
Review: Chicago International Film Festival 2020 Movie #3 I hate this the same way I hated Revenge but I think this was done a bit better? Fine execution of a stupid concept with some decent themes going for it, but the whole ride was frustrating. The 6/10 rating was pretty reluctant, I'll go into more details on Instagram for my first of three Chicago 2020 posts.

Name: Detention
Review: Spooky Season 2020 No way did I expect to feel this way after watching this, but I loved it. This movie is great. It may just be the best high school movie I've ever seen. Being a junior myself, I don't think any other film I've watched in the past has been able to nail high school culture across multiple generations like this one, and I say that with full honesty. The script makes this one of a handful of movies that is able to get away with some cheesy lines, because that's the whole purpose. Each character is dynamic, funny, and well developed. All the satire was great, of both life in high school and social classes as well as horror movies themselves, and every joke stuck the landing for me. It drew a lot of parallels with Scream for me, in all that they both accomplish, though I think Scream focuses more on the slasher itself than this does. There were definitely some inappropriate tone choices and moments of rough pacing at times, and the entire premise of the film is a group of kids in detention fighting off a slasher, but that's hardly the case; they don't even go to detention until halfway through the film! Still, this is a vastly underappreciated film that I will be checking out every so often; it's one that I can also recommend to all my real life friends, so I love it for that reason too. It's hard to rank this among the other films on my spooky season watchlist, as the horror element really isn't what makes this film great. But I'm ranking the films based on my rating and how much I liked each film; so while it may not be the scariest, not even close, it's still placing high.

Name: The Special
Review: Chicago International Film Festival 2020 Movie #4 It's a director debut, and a pretty good one at that! The story is really good, the characters are excellent through both the acting and development, and it was a thoroughly engaging experience, being very heartfelt and realistic. That being said, there are definitely a few amateur aspects to it. The camerawork could be good at times, but at many times, the angles and zoom felt very awkward, and the editing at times were just simply uneven. Both thanks to this and some really weird places to cut off scenes, the pacing wasn't very good. A lot of scenes moved on without closure, and left a fair number of questions unasked. I'm sure through more movies this will be improved upon, but the poor pacing is still worth noting. No matter, this is a good film and I hope it gets the attention it deserves.
Tags: ciff 2020, w/ dad, w/ mom, virtual cinema

Name: Climax
Review: Spooky Season 2020 Great movie full of gentle, subtle metaphors; can't wait to never watch it again! :) In all seriousness, fucking hell. There's so much to be said about this, every single part of it, and I'm struggling to find the right words. Gaspar Noe wouldn't know a subtle metaphor if it slapped him in the face while this movie was in development. Every visual aspect, the acting, the directing, the emotional impact, the choreography, the pacing, it's all incredible. And that's just the surface level shit. So much could be said, and I'm honestly close to giving it a 10, but it just wasn't something that I could seriously connect to, despite being so impactful and stunning.

Name: Mean Streets
Review:
So waaaay back in late June/early July, I started a binge where I mark off all the films on Spike Lee's 95 recommendations list. That obviously has not happened, and I'm okay with that. I like spacing out classic films so I don't run out of new ones to discover. I was bored earlier today and was scavenging around for something to watch, before coming across this and deciding to watch it.

It was very good, better than I initially expected. The cinematography is excellent, the acting is really good, the directing is solid (though you can tell that Scorsese was still in his early years when this was made), and the characters are great. Robert De Niro is brash and young, and in a weird way I was able to connect with Johnny Boy. The script is good as well, the entire story being inspired by Scorsese's years growing up in Little Italy. Not to mention some really good music. However, I do feel that whenever I revisit GoodFellas, The Irishman, or even to a certain extent Casino I'd be able to point out many areas in which Scorsese has improved upon since this one. Some scenes had rough editing, the pace of the story took some sharp turns a few times and made it a bit hard to follow, and it just didn't really exceed my expectations, nor did many scenes stick with me that much. Still, it deserves it's historic reputation and is absolutely worth a watch.

Name: Feels Good Man
Review: "What's more worthless than cartoons, but what's more powerful than Mickey Mouse?" Wow this actually lived up to the hype, haha! I was a bit skeptical, both based on its content and the fact that this is a director debut, but I expected it to be competently made and overall enjoyable. I'm not sure why those expectations were where they are, what a great docu this is. A good blend of source material and creativity, this was engaging from start to finish and much more powerful than one might think. The story is a truly fascinating one, and I learned much more than I expected about internet culture during Pepe's rise to fame, as well as his prominence in the 2016 presidential election. For reference, when late 2016 rolled around, I was in 7th grade and had turned 13 in September, so I was a dumb middle schooler and into memes. I was well aware about Pepe's evolution during the school year and I guess you could say I lived through it even if I never took serious note of what was going on. It's crazy how the internet has created such a herd mentality in many situations, all through this one frog. It hit me where I felt it, and with memes being a fairly sizeable part of my social life, it's a recipe for success. There are a couple issues with it, however. The director stated that the film was intended to be mostly about how the creator, Matt Furie, negotiated with the reality of the situations, but during the second half especially, the film seemed to lose a lot of focus in that sense. It became more about the impact Pepe had on America through the eyes of so many others, a lot of which seemed to have more screen time than necessary given the context of each. The story is heartfelt and told Matt's story well nonetheless, but the road is still a little rocky. Otherwise, yeah. I suggest that everyone check this one out, as it is a really great movie and will teach you a lot about a subject I'm sure many of us are fairly familiar with.

Name: Borat: Cultural Learnings of America for Make Benefit Glorious Nation of Kazakhstan
Review:
There are movies that people genuinely are not sure how to assign a numerical value to on this site, and I have found one of those. This may just simply be a universally unrankable film. It's so funny but also full of cringe and prolonged painful moments. I laughed at loud at times and was just kinda stunned silent at others. The risks taken are frankly pretty insane. Cohen and everyone are put in serious danger for the meme, and it's... creative? It's still crazy to me that this film was made and acceptable to be released in America.

From a technical standpoint, it's actually pretty good. The Kazhik TV style was both entertaining and worked effectively, and the cinematography and editing were really solid. Of course, Sacha Baron Cohen's acting is spectacular and I read that he actually stayed in character even when being questioned by the secret service, and the candidness of everyone he encounters is really well portrayed on the camera. I totally see why this is a cult classic and it certainly won't be the last time I watch it. Not sure what else to say that hasn't already been said, you just have to watch it for yourself.

Name: Day of the Fight
Review: Not gonna give a star rating but this was pretty cool. Reminded me a lot of his first few films as far as his technical style and story material. I think I actually like this more than Fear and Desire if I'm being honest? It's nothing great of course,  but it's worth a little 12 minute watch especially if you love Kubrick.

Name: Kubrick by Kubrick
Review: This was a strange one. When I binged all 13 Kubrick films back in May, it forever changed me and the way I look at cinema. From Fear and Desire to Eyes Wide Shut, I was exposed to so many influences and examples of masterful filmmaking. Not a day has gone by where I don't think about at least one of his projects. I think he may be the greatest filmmaker ever. While I didn't necessarily expect it to, this documentary is not a good one. It introduced me to a lot of obscure interviews with him where he talked about most of his movies (the only ones not mentioned were The Killing, Killer's Kiss, and Lolita) and we got some behind the scenes looks at them. While it was a good reminder of why I loved the man and his work so much, I wasn't really touched by any of the content specific to the film. There was no structure or narrative, just a bunch of artifacts. It would've worked much better as a slideshow than a movie, and I hope a more serious documentary on Kubrick is made in the future. At most, this inspired me to get off my ass and make my 'What Defines' post for Kubrick that I've been meaning to create since July. Tons to unpack and I'm getting to work on it now so look forward to that.
Tags: ciff 2020, w/ dad, w/ mom, virtual cinema

Name: One Night in Miami...
Review:
My computer is being stupid and not loading letterboxd and my phone keeps crashing the app so this may be the hardest log I've ever done on this site.

Decent movie. Good acting, good character development, good script, good cinematography, not much else. It felt a bit like Oscar bait and it didn't really bring anything new to the table as far as its messages and themes, and I didn't feel very emotionally affected by it, but it's still worth a watch.
Tags: ciff 2020, virtual cinema, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Krisha
Review: Pretty good movie. It's a marvel in independent, low budget filmmaking and TES really showed strong talent in directing and acting. The script is really good as well and the cinematography was great. It wasn't thoroughly engaging however and there were parts where I was just waiting for the film to move on. This barely counts as a horror movie and if it technically does, it's one of the most boring ones I've ever seen. Still, it has its positives even if it felt fairly pointless to me.

Name: New Order
Review:
I thought this was a really good. It looked great, the production design was great, the acting was really good, there were a lot of good ideas, and the visual effects were really nice. I think it did everything it needed to.

So why does it have a 6/10 and not higher?

It didn't have a point.

No motive is established, none of the characters are very well developed, and it was impossible to take any sort of emotional stance. I was just watching a bunch of disgruntled lower class Mexican City citizens attacking these higher class citizens out of anger? It's hard to feel anything because we're watching a bunch of murders and other acts of torture occur (including sex, it was really uncomfortable). It has the potential to be something like Die Hard or Parasite, but with a lack of emotion and poor pacing with a clunky final act, I couldn't give it any higher of a rating, despite thinking the first half was really good. This is another example of a movie that I think would benefit from more length, the best other example in my memory is Gravity. Check this one out and see if you like it more, but the film does seem to have a very polarizing reception.

Name: Summer of 85
Review:
I'm rarely ever consistent with rating films the same score all in a row, but unfortunately it seems I've been hitting the average stuff from Chicago right at the end.

I'm pretty tired as I've had a long weekend and just don't want to rush into any more movies right now, so look for my further thoughts on this movie once I get around to all the Chicago stuff on Instagram.

Sad to be done with this all, however. My first year in the film festival experience was a good one and I look forward to it again next year.
Tags: ciff 2020, w/ dad, w/ mom, virtual cinema

Name: Freaks
Review: Spooky Season 2020 There's a surprising amount to unpack here, and I'm honestly not sure what to say. It's a really good movie but my emotions towards it are on either side of the spectrum. I'll see if I can do better than this a bit later today once I've sat on the film a bit. It wasn't scary, but I think there's a little more to why this film is labeled as a horror film.

Name: Fantastic Mr. Fox
Review:
Like, holy shit Scooby! I haven't seen this film in, like, 8 years.

Having been a more official movie bitch for almost a year now, I'm kinda shocked I haven't seen anything else from Wes Anderson. It was... *ahem*... fantastic. It deserves another watch to really grasp it all because I loved it a ton and I'm probably gonna do a full Wes binge sometime soon, but there's pretty much nothing wrong with this film. I'm not huge on the offbeat and pacing, but I feel like it's something that'll grow on me over time. Not far off from my favorite movies list.

Name: Molly's Game
Review: Watched in preparation for Trial of the Chicago 7 in the coming days, and oh boy. I hope it's less sleep-inducing than this was. A good screenplay and character development can't save the overlong runtime, lack of authenticity, poor editing, and dull, repetitive-feeling plot points. I expected more of Sorkin, even this being his directorial debut. Everything has blended together in my head at this point and there are very few memorable shots and scenes.

Name: The Texas Chain Saw Massacre
Review: Some good B-movie fun. Not sure what else to say that hasn't been said. Pretty stupid but a good way to kill and hour and a half. Plus it's a "classic" so that's cool too.

Name: Hereditary
Review:
My dad asked me how the film was towards the end and I think I summed things up pretty well, so I'll go ahead and copy paste that convo.

Dad: How is it?
Me: It's good though not this generation's Exorcist or a masterpiece like some people say
There are some fantastic aspects to it though, like the cinematography, production design, Toni Collette's performance and the practical effects
The story, parts of the dialogue and a few of the other performances are a bit subpar though

Hereditary, or teenage M. Night Shyamalan suffers schizophrenia after killing his sister on accident (symbolism for his anguish after making every movie he did in the 2010s)

Even though I watched this in a home theater environment with the sound up pretty high at night, I wasn't very affected by it, aside from some discomfort from Charlie's death and the last 10-15 minutes. Having that predictability factor made things a little less interesting and engaging, though it was fun for my friends to see it for the first time and be wrong with all their guesses. There were also some certain aspects of it that felt a bit more cartoony than I think was intended. Still, it is a great movie and I look forward to seeing it again even if I'll probably be even less affected by it.

Name: Rosemary's Baby
Review: Spooky Season 2020 Over the course of the year, there have been some disappointing films that I would label as overrated. Some examples include Interstellar, Fargo, Pulp Fiction, and The Thing. Unfortunately, I would have to place Rosemary's Baby among those four as well, maybe even on top. I entered the film with fairly high expectations along with little knowledge of the plot itself, other than Rosemary gives birth to Satan's spawn and horror movie shit happens. Turns out... aside from a couple early scenes, nothing even remotely scary happens until about an hour and 50 minutes in. And those first 110 minutes are frankly not very good. We spend so much time with the main characters, yet I didn't feel like I knew very much about them. I hardly cared about them and what was going on with other people, and it was hard to consistently pay attention. Perhaps if I knew about this going in I'd have thought a bit higher of the film, but even then I'd still find Rosemary's Baby grossly overrated. There are some positives to this movie but the film gets enough hype so I'll let someone else write about them.

Name: Audition
Review: Spooky Season 2020 When it comes to movies from 1999, you hear a lot of uproar about Fight Club, The Matrix, American Beauty, Toy Story 2, The Sixth Sense, Magnolia, Being John Malkovich, etc. etc... but you don't hear too much about Audition. Which is a bit surprising really, as it's just as good as all those films I just mentioned and is one of my new favorite Japanese films. Audition is shock violence done right. The story is so incredibly well developed and entertaining for the first hour of the film, then the second hour correctly and naturally builds off of that into a well executed thriller. The cinematography is astounding, the use of color and production design are each excellent, the pacing is terrific, the dialogue is great, and I was thoroughly engaged from start to finish. Sound isn't something I normally mention a lot with movies, but in this case, the sound design/quality was perfect. It's hard to find flaws in this, though I will admit there were some plot elements that felt a bit confusing at first and took a bit for me to understand them, and it just doesn't fully feel like a 10/10 if that makes sense? But this is a fantastic movie that is very worth watching and ranks among the best films of 1999, the best films of the 90s, and the best Japanese films. As of right now, I'd call it my third favorite of '99. Not quite up to American Beauty or Being John Malkovich, but I liked it more than Magnolia and Eyes Wide Shut.

Name: Children of Men
Review:
I started this last night but fell asleep so I finished it this morning, as I wanted a calm before the storm today of many horror movies (hopefully).

This was brilliant and blew me away. It's intense, stressful, paced amazingly well, has some of my new all time favorite cinematography, super well acted and directed, a great script, an unpredictable story that focuses on attention to detail in great fashion, and overall just left a huge emotional impression on me. The world building is so good. It's so realistic and the thought of this happening is crazy. It has some of the most effective death scenes in any movie and keeps you engaged the whole runtime. The rest of the technicals are fantastic as well.

I'd recommend everyone see this film, and I'm not sure if it's Cuaron's best above Roma (I need to give Roma a second watch) but it gives it serious competition.

Name: The Exorcist
Review: Forgot to log this, but is there really much more that needs to be said? It's The Exorcist. It was a really cool theater experience, I will say that, though I don't understand all the hype that this is the scariest movie ever. There were some really good thrills, admittedly, but there are absolutely scarier films out there. The concept was executed well and though it dragged at times, I still enjoyed myself. Not going into too many gritty details, as, like I said, many have done it and will do better than I can upon just one watch.
Tags: cinemark tinseltown north aurora, w/ matt f

Name: ParaNorman
Review: This was honestly a huge throwback to 2012 for me. I remember what life was like back then. I remember everything that I was into and how good life was and I highkey miss it. I remember this theater experience in specific, because I used to be afraid of all the policy videos that showed beforehand (stuff like no texting, no talking, safety exits, etc.) but I immediately fell in love with this movie. Looking back, there's a LOT that flew over my head and that's kay, but it's funny to re-visit with friends and pick up all the innuendos. It's a well done film, and I wouldn't call it well-written particularly as there's a lot of dialogue that feels awkward, but there's also a lot of this film that's just so much fun. Not the best claymation film ever made but worth checking out.
Tags: netflix, w/ farris, w/ josh k

Name: The Trial of the Chicago 7
Review:
Good movie, and quite a bit better than Molly's Game. Sorkin's directing has improved in pretty much every way, but despite that, he still really doesn't have a defined style. He takes a lot after David Fincher and Steven Spielberg, but his screenplay carried the movie. Much like Molly's Game, this film could easily have been directed by someone else and it would've gotten the same rating. Again, he is mainly a screenwriter so this is an expected critique, but it's worth mentioning. The technical aspects were all really good, the acting was great, the dialogue is terrific, and I think it's well paced. The scenes from the riots themselves were very well done and had the right level of emotional impact.

Despite that, this is a courtroom drama at it's core, and most of the time I was really only paying half my attention to the film and still fully understood what was happening. I put it on in the background as I was cleaning my room a bit and I think I got the same level of enjoyment out of that experience as I would've if I was fully paying attention. The characters also aren't as dynamically developed as past Sorkin characters, even if the performances all were great. Outside of the riot scenes, it didn't carry a thought-provoking message or something really seriously to take out of what was going on. It's worth watching for sure and it's going to get a lot of Oscar attention (I'm calling it now), though it isn't the next 12 Angry Men or anything.

Name: Little Women
Review: I was going pretty far back and forth on just how much I liked this movie but I ended up feeling the same connection I did the first time by the final scenes. This is another film that makes for an interesting comparison between my early cinephile days vs the present, and fortunately the re-watch was more of the same. It's still fucking beautiful across every technical aspect possible, Desplat's score is excellent, and Saoirse Ronan gives one of my favorite performances of 2019; I've figured that Scarlett Johansson in Marriage Story deserved that Oscar, but now I'm feeling a bit undecided after this viewing. The other performances didn't hold up quite as well, but were all still quite good. The screenplay was very good and had a great flow, but there aren't as many memorable / quotable lines as some people might say and in comparison to Lady Bird. The social commentary is great and the way it's told through this classic story is well done; I don't personally find it life changing, but still very good nonetheless. And as I said at the beginning, my attention was a bit flaky throughout the movie; there were times where I was locked in and others where I was taken out a bit, but overall I was fairly engaged. And going back to the basics, this is a super enjoyable film and I feel really good watching it. With that, it's one of the easiest films for me to recommend to a lot of people.

Review:
