Name: Twin Peaks
Review: Edit - I think I was only so pressed about people logging the entire show because I wasn't introduced to the concept of the show-logging loophole on the website yet lmao So I just found out this Letterboxd page existed... The runtime listed on the website matches that of the FIRST EPISODE, not the entire season. Why the fuck are people logging the entire season, some the first two seasons? That's not what this is. A few weeks back I watched the first episode and embarrassingly haven't gotten back to it. It definitely felt a lot like a soap opera as far as its portrayal of the story, but despite the fairly lofty runtime for a pilot episode, it was pretty engaging. I'm definitely gonna return and finish the rest of the season soon not just because of the insane reputation. So yes. I liked the first episode, and that's what the 7/10 rating represents. Not the entire show. In the immortal words of David Lynch himself... "Get real."

Name: Bacurau
Review: Felt exactly how I'd imagine a crossover between New Order and Dogtooth would be. Cool movie.

Name: High Life
Review:
Not going to rate yet, but it's either a 7 or an 8. I really like the concept and think it was executed pretty well. All the acting is great and it's well written with great cinematography and production design. The nonlinear structure felt pretty uneven towards the start but got better and began to make more sense as time went on. As gruesome and morally abhorrent as a lot of the scenes are, a fair amount of them are at least somewhat justified by the ideas taken from the story. For example, there's the rape scene which can be explained by the criminals just wanting to make a baby and get out of the ship, though other sex scenes with characters just looking for pleasure did NOT need to be added, or at the very least not as graphic as they are (I'm talking about one scene in particular. If you've seen the film you can probably guess which sequence I'm referring to). However, that's really my only complaint with this really good film. Check it out when you can.

As far as the "intellectual" space movies of the decade, I'd actually take this over Gravity and Interstellar, which probably will sound like blasphemy to a lot of people. It doesn't have the rewatch value of Gravity just thanks to how explicit it is along with Gravity's excellent opening, but I think the execution of the original concept and overall story are superior. I haven't seen Ad Astra yet and it's been long enough since I've seen The Martian and Arrival to compare, but I'd assume at least Arrival is better than this.

Name: The Incredibles
Review:
The film itself is an 8/10 but HOLY shit this film just hits the spot in so many ways. I've seen this film at least once a year since I was 2, MANY times early on and every time I watch this, I feel two strong emotions: nostalgia and renewal. I have great memories of watching this growing up, as you could expect, but I always feel so fresh after watching it. No matter how many times I'll watch it in my life, I can always look at where my life is at in new light. It's so powerful and I will always love this film to death.

As for the movie itself, yeah it's fantastic. I'm just now finding out that Brad Bird based this film on a midlife crisis and spun it into a superhero story without it feeling too unnatural. I love the story so much and it brought so much new meaning to an already important film for me. The animation is outdated in many places, yeah, but it still isn't bad. A fair amount of scenes do still hold up. The voice acting is great, the script is really good, the sound design is actually pretty fucking awesome (I'd love to see this in IMAX, or just a theater in general), and the score. The score might be the best in any animated movie in history, only rivaled by The Lion King. It introduced me to jazz at a young age, and oh boy can I say that it's had an effect on my life in that sense (I'm in jazz at school and listen to it regularly). I admit I found the pacing to feel a bit rushed and in return uneven a fair amount, and Syndrome is kind of a laughably stupid villain? But those are my only complaints. It's The Incredibles, man. COME ON!

Name: Pain and Glory
Review: Fantástico!

Name: Borat Subsequent Moviefilm: Delivery of Prodigious Bribe to American Regime for Make Benefit Once Glorious Nation of Kazakhstan
Review: Everything I said in my first review pretty much applies here. The pranks aren't as funny as the ones in the first film, but I like how the story is a bit more structured. However, what I liked best about this is how they were able to pull so much off without Borat getting too crowded by people. Yeah it happened a fair amount of times and probably more off-screen, but what they managed to pull off is still incredibly impressive. And yes, the twist ending is pretty good. I can also now confirm that these movies are best experienced with friends.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ matt f, w/ owen g, w/ owen l, at a friend's place

Name: The Life Ahead
Review:
Some good acting, solid technicals and a decent screenplay against a messy story execution with uneven pacing. It shows some real potential to be great at times but it failed to connect with me, and a lot of it felt unclear. I kinda checked out after 50 minutes, tolerating the rest to say I finished it. The ending is very touching, however. Enjoyable, I suppose, but don't expect much from it.

I apologize for not adding better reviews to the films I've seen lately, but the past few films have just been kinda mediocre and not really worthy of deep thoughts.

Name: Mulholland Drive
Review: I'll need to wait a bit of time to sit on this film before I'm able to write any cohesive thoughts on it. All I can say right now is that it's either a 7 or an 8, but even then I can't decide.

Name: The Ballad of Buster Scruggs
Review:
The Ballad of Buster Scruggs: 6.5/10
Near Algodones: 6/10
Meal Ticket: 6/10
All Gold Canyon: 7.5/10
The Gal Who Got Rattled: 7/10
The Mortal Remains: 5.5/10


The acting was great, writing was good, cinematography and editing really good (loved the lighting contrast in every scene, as well as the color palates), the music was excellent, but the skits themselves were a bit of a mixed bag. I think I'm being a bit generous on some of the above ratings, as I wasn't super enthused or engaged with the first half of the film, but the last three stories made up for it. The pace was a bit uneven thanks to some strange transitions between each, and it was hard to keep my interest levels up until All Gold Canyon. That being said, it is very funny and an overall entertaining good time. But I would be lying if I said I wasn't a bit underwhelmed overall. Between my opinions on this and Fargo, I might have to alleviate my expectations a bit whenever I get around to No Country, Llewyn Davis, and the Coens' other highly regarded works, though not by too terribly much.

Name: Before Sunrise
Review: Ugh... I mean, UGH. It's all so natural, isn't it? The candidness of an instant connection is executed incredibly well through the screenplay, acting and Linklater's direction. Jesse and Celine have many wonderful traits and can relate to anyone in at least one way. Their exploration of Vienna and the way they're able to interact with both each other and the city itself is brilliant. I was captivated the entire runtime and loved every minute. The only minor complaints I have are the way they met, which felt a bit forced / unrealistic, and there are some shots that I wish were a bit longer, as the cuts seemed too quick. But there's no way that those will stand in the way of how much I adored this film. Can't wait to check out the rest of the trilogy.

Name: Taxi Driver
Review: Showed my parents this film for their first time in honor of Marty's birthday. Love it so much; I need to re-watch Raging Bull but I think this is still my favorite Scorsese.

Name: Brazil
Review:
This has the potential to become one of my favorite films, so I need to give it a second watch. I don't think I fully was able to grasp all that made it great, despite loving it a lot. I absolutely adore the production design, worldbuilding, and the scale of the world itself. The acting and writing are brilliant, and every visual aspect is stellar. The story is structured so incredibly well, being chock full of surprises and having a great flow, as well as having one of the better endings I've seen in a film. The symbolism is portrayed incredibly well and this is an aspect that I think I'll get more out of and pick more up on during my second viewing. I wasn't as engaged as I think I possibly could have been and there were some parts where I was thoroughly confused, hence the points off, but if both of these are alleviated the second time, I would not be surprised. Since it technically takes place around Christmas time, I'll probably watch it again sometime within the next month lol.

EDIT: I just noticed this is my 300th film I've logged this year. Yay me :)

Name: Orgazmo
Review:
Now I don't mean to sound like a queer or nothin', but how are Trey Parker and Matt Stone not married?




I also think it's fucking hilarious that this and Boogie Nights came out in the same year, the same month, and the same festival (TIFF)

Name: RoboCop
Review:
To be honest, I expected more out of this. Between the gratuitous violence and hidden themes that are actually completely in your face, this felt like a Spike Lee-directed film with a mediocre screenwriter.

I will give it this however; I kinda dug the story development, mainly at the beginning. Plus, it has surprisingly good cinematography and solid visual effects (occasionally).

Name: Rango
Review: Forgot to log this last night, but everything that I said in my first review stands. Fantastic film, one of my favorite animated movies AND movies period. It's hard to find flaws and I honestly do lowkey want to give it the full 10/10 rating but it just doesn't entirely feel like a 10/10 film? Nonetheless, this movie is underrated as hell and you should all watch it when you can.

Name: Judy
Review: Renee was really good, but like... how did she beat Scarlett for best actress lmao

Name: Arrival
Review: Yeah, this actually is one of the best films of the decade, century, and potentially all time, no questions asked. Mindblowing in every aspect and finally seeing this again 4 years after the first time was such a redeeming experience. I was only 13 and in 7th grade when this came out in December 2016, and it was one of the first artistic films I ever saw. I remember feeling overwhelmed and confused, like I wasn't ready for media of this caliber. I can safely say the satisfaction I feel for finally understanding Arrival is a source in my speechlessness. Sometimes you watch a movie and it feels like fate made the decision to pick it, as it just fits what's going on in your life at a certain time and was exactly what you needed. This is how I feel right now. Arrival understands exactly what it's seeking to execute and portrays Louise's journey through the situation as a labyrinth leading up to the final moments where you take a step back and understand why everything the way it is. It's such a sweet emotion. Between feeling truly changed by the film and my current inability to put my thoughts into competent words, I know I've found a new favorite.

Name: Mangrove
Review: Really good as both a movie and the first episode of Small Axe. I'd argue this has the best performances of the year so far; every actor and actress did an excellent job. The screenplay is great as well, along with the production design and direction by Steve McQueen. Many iconic and memorable scenes are found in the 2 hour runtime and it's safe to say I was never bored. While some shots were excellent, I have to admit the framing of specific shots (more towards the beginning) weren't great, and many of the scenes felt like they were cut off too early, messing the pacing up a bit. That's really my only criticism with Mangrove, however. While it might not hold the universal value of The Trial of the Chicago 7, this is still an important film and the better of the two courtroom dramas. Check it out when you can, as well as the rest of the miniseries once it gets released.

Name: The Master
Review:
I lost my entire fucking review thanks to my internet so I'll keep it short.

I still thought it was fantastic and everything in the first review holds up. There are some dead spots and I wish we got a bit of a glimpse into what exactly caused Freddie's PTSD, which would've helped me connect to his character more, but this is a fabulous piece of art even disregarding those nitpicks. And those last few minutes? Holy shit... it's hard not to shed a tear or two. This will absolutely not be the last time I watch it. Honorable mention to my top 25 favorite movies.

Name: Tokyo Story
Review: I had a lot of trouble getting into this. My emotional reactions to all that happened were few and far between. I didn't find any of the characters to be super great or anything (though the main couple is memorable at least), my attention span wasn't very consistent, and I overall just didn't feel the themes that were being conveyed. The cinematography was spectacular, the dialogue was all great and I think the performances were really good as well, and there were times where I did feel for them, but overall there was a fair amount of the film that just didn't stick the landing. Maybe upon rewatch I'll enjoy it more, but for now, this was a disappointing first viewing, even if there were a lot of excellent aspects to it.

Name: Life of Brian
Review: Atheists turn away from Christianity because they don't believe in Je**vah or the bible. Little do they know, Brian is an alternate option! ALWAYS LOOK ON THE BRIIIIIGHT SIDE OF LIFE (for real though, this is one of my new favorite comedies. I loved it)

Name: Under the Silver Lake
Review: I interpreted this film as a modern take on classic movie mysteries like those from Hitchcock, and it did a pretty good job! Loved the acting, writing and directing especially. The cinematography was great for the most part, though the editing was a little problematic the whole time. The film has some seriously excellent moments and I was engaged the whole time, though I have to admit there were a few parts that just felt unevenly paced and some scenes which just felt unnecessary, but overall I wasn't bothered by the run time. Not sure what else to say lol, this is a weird movie so it's hard to write a good review of it.

Name: The Kissing Booth
Review:
I liked the plot twist where Elle's family commit group suicide, then she joins Noah and Lee on their guys trip to Sweden. The rest of the film was shit, though.




watched with Nik / crazywharf625 and Alex / alexsommer0609
Tags: netflix, virtual watch party, w/ alex s, w/ nik

Name: Se7en
Review: A good film indeed, though I feel the 8/10 rating was a bit reluctant. All the performances were good as were the characters (though I wish Somerset was as well developed as Mills), the cinematography and editing were both great, the screenplay was really good, the directing was good, and it really had a nice style to it. The story and pacing were solid as well, and for the most part I was pretty engaged; the runtime felt shorter than it was. So why only an 8 (which is more like a 7)? None of it was really amazing. It did everything well, but I wasn't really shocked and nothing really exceeded my expectations until the final scene. The way the finale was executed is fantastic and it was a real punch to the gut, but the rest of the film didn't give me the same reaction. If you know me, I always like to get a brief look at what the director of a film I'm watching intended for the film as far as themes, messages and execution. For this one, Fincher intended it to be a meditation on evil rather than a basic detective story, but the first hour and 45 minutes or so were that basic detective story; it didn't really get thought provoking until the car ride to the box. It's still a good movie and worth checking out, especially considering Mank's upcoming release, though it's safe to say I think it gets a bit more hype than it deserves.

Name: Hillbilly Elegy
Review:
Congratulations Ron Howard, you're like a C tier director at best.

BLEH... probably the most frustrating film to come out this year so far (that I've seen). It's so hard to connect with any of the characters since they're so inconsistent and change their tone and feelings on situations every few minutes (most of the performances were really good though). When any of them were in pain or going through tough times, I couldn't feel for them at all. No one is perfect, but you can't excuse how every character in this film is developed with minimal or no focus. The writing is good sometimes, and other times it's just mediocre and clunky. The pacing is awful and it feels like the story never knows what direction it's going in. The production design was good, I'll give it that, but the cinematography was not. The framing and angle of the camera in every shot set off my OCD from it's lack of balance, and most shots felt way too zoomed in. Not to mention the film feels way longer than it actually is because of it; it was tough to stay engaged. By the time the film is finished, the ride to the end doesn't feel worth it at all. There are good aspects to the film, like the ones I mentioned, but most of it is mediocre to bad, which I feel shouldn't happen from an experienced director like Ron Howard. He doesn't have the most impressive filmography but I don't know how he managed to mess a lot of this up. I might bump it down to a 3 but I'm not sure yet, I'll need to sit on it a bit.

Name: North by Northwest
Review:
Imperfect but entertaining as hell; I had so much fun with this. As with any Hitchcock, the production design, cinematography and writing were brilliant. They have so much charm and just scream his visual style. The characters were great as well, as to be expected. Sure a lot of the effects are outdated, and some parts of the film don't keep you as engaged as you might hope, but the premise is executed well and is one of the better classic films I've seen lately.

Another random thing but I've given so many films a 4/5 rating recently lol, I guess I'm just consistent.

Name: The Passion of Joan of Arc
Review:
Requested by Skyler / skylerinthecine as a film to place on my top 50 countdown on Instagram.

Wow, yeah. Pretty amazing as a whole thing. The performances, the ahead-of-its-time filmmaking, the production design and other technical aspects, it was all just excellent. Unfortunately I couldn't find an English version so I just used the French one on YouTube and Google Translate to figure out what was happening, but that had little bearing on the quality as a whole. I also wasn't as into the first hour, but boy did it get amazing fast. This is an important piece of silent film history and you should all check it out. It's currently on my top 50.

Name: The Game
Review: Honestly, the best comparison I can make to this is an early Christopher Nolan film. This gave me pretty strong Memento and Insomnia vibes, mainly during the last hour of the movie. The first hour was admittedly pretty dull and I struggled to see the film's merits, but the rest was really good. This is an exciting thriller that feels more mainstream compared to other Fincher works, but still has his distinct vibes. Some great visuals, fun action sequences, a decent plot with solid flow and pacing, and Michael Douglas gave a really good performance. I wasn't huge on the screenplay and I do think it doesn't really bring anything new to the table all aspects considered, but it's worth checking out, especially now that we're approaching Mank's Netflix release.

Name: Her
Review: I'm too tired rn so I'll update the review in the morning but know that I really REALLY loved it. I felt so much connection to the characters and all that was happening, and you should watch it if you haven't yet. The one flaw I had was the final twist at about the hour and 45 minute mark, but that wasn't enough to take away the 10/10 rating.
Tags: netflix, virtual watch party, w/ skyler, w/ max, w/ alex s

Name: Penguins of Madagascar
Review:
lmao wtf


I forgot how much this ruined my love of Madagascar when I was younger though in a weird way I can kinda appreciate it at a bit of an older age

Name: Jumanji: The Next Level
Review:
Forgot to log this earlier but I was feeling kinda nauseous this morning so I was just flipping through channels trying to find a movie and this popped up. Lmao between this and Penguins of Madagascar I've had a very whatever morning so far. But my family and I are going to watch Fantastic Mr. Fox soon so it's all good.

And I'm feeling better, don't worry

Name: Fantastic Mr. Fox
Review: The best movie to watch on Thanksgiving and I'm not going to argue.

Name: Panic Room
Review: Definitely liked it more than last time. Sarah and Meg are really well developed characters and Foster and Stewart gave great performances... I really connected to both of them. The whole thing is really well directed and I think the plot twists are all executed well; I was engaged the whole time and the tense moments worked. The screenplay is solid as well, and the entire thing had a nice flow to it. Above that, the cinematography and editing were quite good, and I just had a lot more fun with this when I expected. It's like a time capsule back to 2002 in many ways and that might be my favorite part of it. Definitely worth checking out, whether you're binging Fincher or not.

Name: Lovers Rock
Review: A fun time, indeed. Loved all the technical aspects to it as well as the performances. It was just a fun and entertaining film all around with a lot of great music. I really do wish there was a real story to it, as the characters introduced were really intriguing, but they were still well written nonetheless. Definitely not up to Mangrove for me, but worth checking out.

Name: Dog Day Afternoon
Review: Forgot to log this sooner lol but I really liked it. Excellent directing, writing and acting. The way the original story was executed was great; I was engaged the whole time and while some parts felt a little unevenly paced, the entire thing was structured pretty well. I didn't feel a very strong emotional connection to what was happening, possibly the biggest red flag for a 4.5 or 5 star rating from me, but everything that I saw on the screen was pretty much flawless. Outstanding work overall from everyone involved, highly recommended.

Name: This Is the End
Review: I love Craig Robinson

Name: Memories of Murder
Review: A truly incredible film. Above being very well directed with a brilliant screenplay and great performances, plus some stunning cinematography, it doesn't require deep messages, symbolism or commentary to reach the levels of quality it has; it's just that goddamn well made. I adore Bong Joon-ho, Song Kang-ho and cinematographer Kim Hyung-koo. Not quite up to Parasite for me, but not far off at all. I hope to go into more detail in the future.

Name: Paterson
Review: Let me tell you about what I learned while watching this film... Take a look at my film diary. I watched five films yesterday, and this one currently (it's almost 4 AM as I write this), and two of those are a part of the Letterboxd Top 250. I checked out those two as they were recommended to me by friends, as was This Is The End (what my friends and I like to do is make wheels for each other, then we spin and decide what to watch). I watched Panic Room for my David Fincher binge, then Lovers Rock because it's a new 2020 film released today. Getting onto the point, five movies is a lot to take in to one period between waking up to going to bed. Getting to the point, I write parts of stories sometimes for screenplays just to pass the time, but also because it's an interest of mine for college and careers. Adaptation is easily the film that's had the biggest film on me as a writer, and it's going to be hard to top it. But after watching this film, and the monogamy of Paterson's everyday life except with slight changes / rotations to those events, I knew I found something special. Jim Jarmusch turned the everyday life of a union worker with dreams into one of my new favorite films. I felt so touched in the moment and still do by Jarmusch's screenplay, along with the character development. Whether it be a conversation he hears while driving the bus, or someone he encounters while walking his dog in the evening, or a conversation with his bubbly wife, it's so representative of most people's lives and I'd be hard-pressed to find someone who didn't feel some sort of connection to it. Jarmusch understands life like not many screenwriters do. So how does this connect to the first part of my story? Well, it was a good reminder to go outside more, even if it's just a walk around the block. No two days are the same, and you can find inspiration to write about anything. The best screenwriters live life and learn from experience. I've had this thought watching other films, such as from Charlie Kaufman and other such personal films, but I haven't felt the same impact from any film before this one. This is such a wonderful film and I thank it for its service. Between the direction, screenplay, performances, cinematography, editing, structure and pacing, music, and overall beauty in simplicity, this is one of the best films I've seen and one of the best of the decade. I need to slow down because I'm watching so many great movies in a row, and I need to experience more of life. I feel like Paterson at the end of the film when he was conversing with the man near the waterfall. I will be showing this film to my family as soon as I possibly can; they need to experience this. TL;DR - It's like Synecdoche, New York but uplifting and only takes place over the course of the week. It's inspired me in many more ways than I expected and is easily one of my new favorite films. Not quite as good as Synecdoche but still a masterful piece of art through and through.

Name: Fight Club
Review:
I had a miserable time watching this film and I guess I need to rewatch it but nothing made sense to me. Maybe I'm stupid, or tired, but this was so fucking frustrating. I hate myself.

I actually had the film up to an 8/10 around two-thirds of the way in. I really enjoyed the film and what it had to say. Then the twist happened, along with the rest of the film. All that happened during the last act made little sense to me. And when I tried to think about the rest of the film leading up to the twist in context, I just got more frustrated and things made less sense to me. The movie was completely ruined and I just didn't know what to think.

I'll give it a rewatch for sure but even then I'm not sure if I'll consider it a great film or anything.

Name: Saving Christmas
Review: The holiday season has officially begun!

Name: FRED 3: Camp Fred
Review: When you hang out with friends... you watch shitty movies. I'm 10 minutes in and am considering quitting Letterboxd. I'm genuinely not kidding when I say this film gave me a headache so I think it's safe to say I now have a pick for the worst movie I've ever seen.

Name: Sonic the Hedgehog
Review:
SOooo let me go ahead and say that despite having watch four films today (but just this and Fight Club to completion), I actually went outside today and thought about a screenplay, so not to worry; I am living by what I said in Paterson. It was really nice and I hope this becomes a daily thing.

Anyways, Sonic... it's fun but yeah. Not seeing it in theaters made me realize some more flaws. Still worth watching though, I'd recommend this as a movie to watch when you're high with friends.
Tags: on tv, w/ dad, w/ quinn, w/ mom

Name: Oldboy
Review:
This is a rare example of a film which has made me feel real nausea. I feel weird using positive adjectives to describe it, as it's such a difficult task to watch the film on its own. The ultraviolence is shocking and ever so cringeworthy. You really can't process what you just watched until after the film is done. The filmmaking is excellent and every element is executed very well, though this might just be the darkest film I've ever seen.


Time to watch something happy!

Name: Zodiac
Review: I promise I'm genuinely giving each Fincher film a chance, but I have yet to find one that hits the mark for me. Fight Club was close to that, but then came the twist. Se7en would easily have made it if at least a bit more of the film had the same energy as the last 20 minutes. Then the rest are just what they are. Zodiac, unfortunately, is the same way. I cannot see why so many of my friends give this a 10/10. The characters were good and backed up by great performances, so this part of the film did it for me; Fincher knows how to direct actors. The story has a really great flow to it, and every plot element is structured very well. It has a strong screenplay as well (surprising, coming from the same writer as the Amazing Spider-Man movies and Independence Day: Resurgence), and goes without saying the cinematography is always stunning. However, outside of what was just mentioned, no other part of the film was really great. I understand the film needs to be as long as it is, but there were a handful of points where it could just not keep my attention. I also just didn't really feel changed by what I saw; it's a well made, good film, but not many qualities help it qualify as a favorite in my book. If you love this film, more power to you; I just didn't. Also, weirdly, watching this made me appreciate Se7en more than I did before, so thanks for that I guess?

Name: The Curious Case of Benjamin Button
Review:
Sorry it's been a few days since I last watched anything... trying to finish this film felt like a chore since I was really not that engaged with the story aside from a few points. I've also just been really busy with school and struggling with some mental health issues.

As I've said with many other films, it's competently made, but the substance just doesn't connect with me. It has a pulsating heart but it gets buried underneath several mediocre subplots and a decent-at-best screenplay. David Fincher did his best to direct, I can tell, though the story just isn't structured well. It definitely had sequences that I really liked, though not much of the film has stuck out in my memory. This year of the Oscars is known as one of the worst due to snubs such as The Dark Knight and Synecdoche, New York as well as films that didn't deserve certain nods... add this film getting 13 nominations to the list of 2008 Academy sins. Some of those noms are justified, but a lot of them aren't.

Name: The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo
Review:
I sure am glad I didn't watch this one with my parents!

This was a visual feast. The cinematography was EXCELLENT and I'd argue it's the second best shot Fincher film behind Zodiac. The art direction was really solid as well, and it sounded amazing. Another smaller thing that I noticed is how the rain and snow actually looked real. There are a lot of films out there where you can definitely tell the rain and snow are fake, but not this one. Maybe the weather was real for the shoot, but if it wasn't, they pulled it off very well. I wasn't very huge on the editing, as a lot of shots just felt cut off at the wrong time, so I'm pretty surprised that this is the category the film won an Oscar for. The writing and performances are solid as well, though they were both definitely overshadowed by the technicals. I don't think Rooney Mara was quite as good as some people say, and I can only really remember a few lines of dialogue. Plus, there were some points during the film, mainly the second half, that just lost me for a few minutes. The runtime didn't bother me per se, as I think it needed to be 2.5 hours long, though it could've taken less than halfway through to get into the bigger story. I have some other smaller issues with the film, such as Daniel Craig having a surprising amount of questions about Sweden as a country for a man who lives there, though those are more nitpicks. It's a well directed film overall, and pretty entertaining at that, though outside the visuals, it offers little else that's truly great. Nonetheless, I really liked this one and it's my third favorite Fincher thus far.

Name: The Hunt
Review:
Watched in preparation for Another Round, which I was lucky enough to find a nearby theater screening it virtually.

This was probably not a good thing to watch at the end of a tough week (I have anxiety/depression and sometimes you just have low periods of time, like this entire week for me) but it hit the spot for sure. It's difficult to endure, about a man who is wrongly accused of pedophilia, but cannot escape as the lie spreads like wildfire. However, this sensitive subject matter is handled with maturity and respect, which is why I think everyone should watch it. This isn't a film that ends up being overly preachy and feeling like a PSA; it's truly telling a personal story.

Above all, this is an excellent character study. Mads Mikkelsen gives a great performance and truly brings the character to life; all his emotion wears off onto the audience and we learn a lot about him. On that note, the amazing screenplay is memorable and feels natural, developing everything well. I'd argue it reaches depression levels of films like Synecdoche and It's Such a Beautiful Day, but not with as much symbolism. It doesn't quite live up to those films, despite coming close; with those two, I felt truly affected by what I just experienced and took something real out of what the writers and director were trying to say. While Lucas's character is developed incredibly well and you feel all the emotions he is, I just didn't take away something that I feel is going to stick with me on the same level. The only other nitpick I had with this was how a couple of the plot elements felt a bit out of place with the tone the film is going for, but for the most part, the story had a great flow to it. Maybe one day I'll realize that The Hunt had more personal meaning to it than I initially suspected, and I'll bump it up to a 10. But for now, it's at a high 9.

Lastly, I couldn't find a good place to put this in the review, but the cinematography was kinda mixed. Every shot was framed and angled well, though the lighting was very questionable. In the second to last scene, the sun is shining right on him, but then we cut to a scene looking at him, and it looks like it's about to rain or is just very cloudy.

Name: Gone Girl
Review: I gotta say... strange decision on my part to watch both The Hunt and this in the same day, but also this is easily my favorite Fincher so it's all good. I mean yeah it was pretty amazing as a whole. I pretty much agree with a lot of what's already been said so I'm not going to go into real detail but I was pretty locked in from start to finish; not even The Social Network did this. The reaction it gave me was something I was expecting from other Fincher films, but I guess I saved the best for last. Every detail is so concise and it'll be hard for my guy David to top this one.

Name: Red, White and Blue
Review: I went back and forth, mainly towards the final half hour, on whether this is better than Mangrove or not... I think it is. And, at that, it made my top 10 of the year thus far. It feels more straightforward in its storytelling and John Boyega gives a truly amazing performance. It's hard to find a flaw in this and I truly felt impacted by all the slander Leroy had to endure. Mangrove is longer and has more of a complex plot to it, but I think I got more out of what this was trying to say. It might actually go up to a 9, though I'll need to sit on it for a bit. If you could only choose one Lovers Rock film, though I haven't seen the remaining two of course, I would say choose this one.

Name: Mank
Review:
I fell asleep during the watch party last night lol, but I started a review before I dozed off. So I'll continue off of that.

Style: Fantastic, loved every part of it. The cinematography, editing, set pieces, and overall style were enthralling. I love the way it captures almost a dreamlike feel to the time period this is set in. And, as always, I loved the jazz in the score (as a jazz player myself, hearing it in a film always increases my positivity on it)
Substance: A mixed bag. The screenplay is good sometimes, clunky others, the performances are solid (except for Seyfried who did really great), the pacing is a bit weird and there are a handful of long stretches where I only gave the film my half attention... overall, while I liked the film, I didn't really take anything serious out of it. I didn't really learn a whole lot about the characters (all of which I feel had subpar development at best), and instead of being a part of the film, I just felt like a spectator. It's probably going to blow up awards season (even though I don't think it deserves to), and we're about to enter it so we'll see what happens.
Tags: netflix, virtual watch party, w/ skyler, w/ max, w/ alex s

Name: 76 Days
Review: Well shot, eye-opening, and very moving overall. It's emotional and hard to watch, but a rewarding experience in a weird kind of way. COVID is real, y'all. Healthcare workers all around the world deserve our thanks for what they've done to help throughout 2020. In general, this also reminded me that you can learn a lot from documentaries, and how many are essential watches. I should really watch more of them, or at the very least more than I have lately. Premiered at TIFF and received a Gotham Award nomination for best documentary, alongside City Hall, Our Time Machine, A Thousand Cuts, and Time.

Name: National Lampoon's Christmas Vacation
Review:
I still remember when I first saw this, it was on TV and I was like 5. I had flipped through random channels and thought it was intriguing and my parents were mortified I saw it, lmao. I did the same for The Godfather (or GoodFellas, I'm not sure; one of the gangster classics) as well as a movie called The Big Hit when I was younger. By the time I hit kindergarten, my parents couldn't leave me alone with the TV remote.

Fun movie!

Name: Die Hard
Review:
On the debate whether this is a Christmas movie or not... It takes place around Christmas but was released as a summer blockbuster. In other words, it doesn't really matter; how about we just enjoy the movie?

Brooklyn Nine-Nine has been one of my favorite shows over the course of the last three years, and I somehow haven't re-visited this film since before I started watching it! So it's really interesting to watch this again and notice all the references the show made to the film. And I mean yeah, it's a classic action flick. I don't quite think it lives up to Terminator 2 as far as the classics go, but it's about on the same level as the first Terminator and Aliens.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Die Hard 2
Review:
Not great, but better than I was expecting I guess. A fair amount of it is forgettable and poorly scripted (I can't tell you how the film was set up; I hardly remember the first hour), though some of the shots look pretty good and I enjoyed most of the fight scenes (some of the acting was really bad though). Also the film feels like it could've ended way sooner. And even then, the true ending felt a bit too coincidental for my tastes. Could definitely have been better but I mean it is what it is.

EDIT: So I guess my review didn't save last night, but I also commented on how John McClane was iconic in the first one because of his development, and how he found redemption through the Nakatomi attacks. In this one, he's just a static character, fighting off the terrorists for action movie sakes. With that, this is a mediocre action movie, functioning even worse as a sequel.

Name: Die Hard With a Vengeance
Review: This one doesn't take place around Christmas, Bonnie Bedelia is no longer a part of the story, and the opening is extremely questionable (it's sad to think that the writers actually got away with using the N word)... though once things get into action, they improve, at least. Some really tense moments and solid special effects line a lot of the exciting sequences. The way the city of New York was used for plot elements was really creative as well. A few of the moments do kind of rely on chance and in turn feel a bit cheesy and too coincidental for my tastes, and I don't think racism should've played as big a part in the script as it did, but overall this was an entertaining time. Not very close to being as good as the first but an improvement over the second.

Name: Another Round
Review: While it doesn't quite live up to The Hunt, Another Round is yet another great film from writer/director Thomas Vinterberg. Mads Mikkelsen gives yet another amazing performance, and it's backed up by several other great characters, all developed well. This is arguably just as personal a film as The Hunt, and the screenplay does everything it needs to and more. Mikkelsen is under consideration for best actor at this year's Oscars, and if he got nominated I would not be upset. The cinematography is also very good, and there wasn't a single bad shot as far as framing, lighting, and just visual pleasure in general. There were some dead spots here and there, though the pacing is good for the most part; the tone hits the mark in every scene. I also didn't quite connect to this as strongly as I did The Hunt, but for the most part, Another Round is just wildly entertaining, and watching the events unfold is bound to put a smile on your face. This is my fourth favorite film of 2020 thus far, and the rating could very well go up to a 9. It sucks we have to wait until February for a wide release of this, but that way it gets to go to theaters; how could you complain about that?

Name: Ingrid Goes West
Review: I'm not even sure what to compare this to LOL it was an absolute goof of a time. The screenplay is a mixed bag, and some of the performances were just whatever (Audrey Plaza and Elisabeth Olsen were excellent however), though I was engaged the whole time and the plot took a lot of weird twists. I think the story has an underlying message of what social media can do to you, but it's either told very subtly or not subtly at all... I can't decide. It's well shot and I think the plot and character developments are both pretty good, though at the same time I still can't figure out the point? It's a weird movie and I don't really feel an urge to re-watch but I guess I'm glad I did. Again, idrk...

Name: Hardcore Henry
Review: Russian Oldboy: The Video Game is still one hell of an entertaining time.

Name: Ex Machina
Review: I wish I enjoyed this like others did, though much like Annihilation, I had trouble getting into it. I think the overall idea for the film is fantastic, and there are some great qualities to it. It's very well shot and the screenplay is quite good. Oscar Isaac and Alicia Vikander give great performances as well (Domhnall Gleeson was a bit mixed but overall decent). It's a very visually pleasing film from start to finish. However, a lot of the earlier plot points are forgotten as the film progresses, and I think the ending was weirdly paced, as it didn't quite hit me the way I hoped. The progression of the story is well executed, though something about it just didn't really keep me engaged or amazed. I'm not very knowledgeable on the science behind the film, so that might have something to do with it, but I feel like others got something out of Ex Machina that I didn't. I'm glad I watched it but I don't really think this was my thing. This and Annihilation are pretty neck and neck for me and I'm not sure which one I prefer.

Name: The Artist
Review:
A wonderful piece of art on all fronts. This is one of those films that reminds you why you love cinema so much. The pacing is absolutely terrific and the story unfolded itself in perfect succession. The tone of each scene hit me the right way, every emotional moment has the intended effect, and I can safely say I was never not engaged. It captures the energy you feel when watching a silent film so well. Every performance was excellent (all the way down to the dogs) and Michel Hazanavicius's direction is brilliant. His vision was truly brought to life and I fell in love with it all so easily. Beyond that, it's shot amazingly well, the production design is great, and from start to finish, not a single detail is out of place. And that score? Man, WOW! I'm very tempted to bump up the rating to a 10, and who knows? I might in the future, or upon a re-watch. I'm sad that it's too late to add this to my top 50 list (it's already started on Instagram) because this would've made it. This is also one of those films where I could watch it if I'm happy, sad, mad, any emotion and I'd still enjoy it just the same. I adore films that can boast this.

Something else worth commenting on is how fulfilling of an experience this was. In 2011, I was just 7 and later turned 8 that year, but my dad introduced me to this website movieinsider.com after we went to a museum in Chicago as a family and I was fascinated by how the theater worked. I spent almost all of 2011 checking that site and tracking all the new releases from that year, and I do specifically remember looking at The Artist. While I couldn't watch every film I tracked because of my young age, I was still intrigued by the possibility that so many films could come out in one year, and how different some of them were. Because of this, my parents also let me watch the Oscars for the first time (the 84th ceremony) and it was an experience that changed my life, seeing The Artist win Best Picture. For that reason, this was one of the films highest on my watchlist and it didn't disappoint.

Another thing: People who call this Oscar bait don't know the film's history very well; when Hazanavicius was writing the screenplay, he did not believe the film would reach wide American audiences. He created The Artist as a passion project, based on his interest of Hollywood in the 1920s; this film is merely a celebration of movie culture from times past. The reason it got as far as winning Best Picture is because the Weinsteins gained distribution rights in the United States and campaigned the film for several Oscar categories. Although Harvey is an absolute piece of shit person, he definitely knew how to make a movie successful.

Name: Adaptation.
Review: I certainly enjoyed this as much as the first time, and while I don't think the rest of my family enjoyed it the same way, I believe this is all of their first Kaufman-written films (my parents saw Being John Malkovich back in the day but didn't enjoy it because they had no idea what to expect) so they're not quite used to it. And as any Kaufman film functions, I picked up on a lot more this time around that really makes you appreciate the re-watches. This is a masterful film in all that it accomplishes, and it just works so well for me. I can understand why people would prefer some of Kaufman's other films more than this one, but Adaptation remains my second favorite of his (behind Synecdoche, of course) and one of my favorite films overall.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Climax
Review: Well uhhh to say it didn't hold up is being generous. In only two months I have a pretty different opinion on this film. The thing is, because of that, I'm just not sure what to rate this film out of 10. It isn't a bad film at all and there's a lot to like about it but the adrenaline of the second half just felt more like straight discomfort this time around. That is the point of the film and most likely what Noe was going for so yes, he succeeded. I'm just rambling at this point and idk where to go from here.

Name: The Autopsy of Jane Doe
Review: A lot of my school friends told me this was really scary... actually it's one of the lamest horror movies I've ever seen. It's not even bad, but I was really bored and all the scares were fairly predictable. The ending was interesting and the practical effects (if you could even call them that) were well made... that's about it. I simply can't really comment on much more because there's no need to.

Name: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
Review: A more enjoyable watching experience than last time, though that's thanks to more alleviated expectations. This isn't a flawless film and it just doesn't hit me the same way films like Before Sunrise or Her do. Also, compared to Adaptation just yesterday, I didn't pick up on as many nuances the second watch as I expected for a Kaufman-written film. Despite that, this is still a great movie and there's a lot to like about it, even though I don't get the hype some people do.

Name: The King's Speech
Review:
For a movie telling the not very exciting story it is, I can definitely tell everyone involved tried their best to make it more interesting and enjoyable for a wider audience. The screenplay is a good one indeed, Colin Firth was excellent, and the production design is stellar. For a film of almost two hours, it actually didn't feel that, and I was engaged for the most part, so props to Tom Hooper for succeeding in making a film with strong Oscar bait vibes enticing. And I'm not going to forget to mention the subtle, but well written score. The cinematography is mostly good, though there were scenes where I feel the camera was moving a bit too sporadically and just felt out of place in comparison to the previous and upcoming shots. On that note, there aren't any huge burning flaws with this, though I have a couple criticisms. I enjoyed what I saw but I didn't connect emotionally to it. I get as a 17 year old guy I'm not the target audience, though I didn't feel seriously engaged until the end where he actually gave his speech. Also, as far as plot elements, towards the end of the film, he's rehearsing the speech itself and trying to fight off his stammer, however he reverts to saying swear words as if he has Tourette's. As someone who also has a minor speech impediment (used to be much more major when I was young) it bothers me when the line between a stammer and Tourette's is blended together. Those criticisms aside, this is a well made film and overall I did enjoy it more than I expected.


Side note: As you might expect, this is a rather safe pick for Best Picture, and unfortunately fulfills the typical Academy stereotype. I even say that without the bias of having Black Swan in my top 5; I would also rank The Social Network and Inception over this film. Maybe even Toy Story 3 whenever I get around to re-watching it. In comparison to the other BP winners I've seen since joining Letterboxd, this definitely ranks at the bottom. I've also seen Titanic and The Sound of Music, though neither recently enough to give a number to. The other seven films on the list below I all have rated at a 9 or 10, whereas this one is a 7.

For reference
1. Parasite
2. Moonlight
3. Birdman
4. American Beauty
5. Lord of the Rings: Return of the King
6. The Artist
7. The Silence of the Lambs
...
...
8. The King's Speech

Name: 127 Hours
Review: This is such a mixed bag all across the board. On one hand, James Franco gives an admittedly great performance, the long shots were all great, and I was engaged pretty much the whole time. However, the editing is so bad (I could understand how some people might like this film's style, but it is NOT for me), the tone never felt like it had a real direction, the screenplay is pretty average, and there were a lot of cringeworthy moments. The cinematography isn't bad per se, though there are a number of shots that are shaky and choppy, my guess is to represent the style of his camcorder, but this style is rarely used at the correct moments. "Why would someone be filming him from up above on a camcorder" is a question I asked myself frequently. The story itself is a great one, but I feel like the filmmaking relies too much on the real story to make up for how cartoonish a lot of it was portrayed. I was never really hooked until the final 20 minutes or so. Sure, it's a character study, but the way he contemplates his life and reflects on certain events just felt messy and I couldn't connect with him. It isn't a horrible film, and if you really like it then more power to you, but I'm sorry to say that I was disappointed.

Name: Requiem for a Dream
Review:
So let me tell you a story about how I came to watch this film tonight. I know I want to major in film once I get to college, and screenwriting has always fascinated me. Most notably after watching all the Kaufman-written films, I was waiting for an inspiration for a story. Well, on November 5, I don't remember the exact situation but something just clicked inside of me and I got inspiration. Without going into gory details, I'm writing about mental health and psychiatric drugs. I'm starting off by watching films about the effects of hard drugs, and what better place to start than this film? Throughout the 102 minute runtime, I've taken several notes on every aspect of the film imaginable, and, let's just say this rewatch could be described as life changing.


Maybe I was just so overwhelmed by the film itself the first time to fully process everything, but holy shit this film has so many deeper meanings I didn't pick up on the first time. Between that and just being exposed to how blunt the story is told once again, this is now a 10/10 film for me and rising into my all time favorites list. This is such an important film and in a weird way, I feel like it's in my life at the correct time. It's easy for me to connect to with it and even though I knew what to expect, I still felt truly heartbroken once again when it was all over. Darren Aronofsky has a power no other film director I've seen films from has - he can get a physical reaction that feels hypnotic in the sense of you feeling the pain of the characters. I could barely breathe and was trembling those last 10 minutes. This is such a real film and one that has influenced the way I look at film, drugs, and life as I know it. I could go on and on about all this film has to offer and I think I'm going to but I know I'm going to be thinking about this one for a while. Hell, there's a lot MORE I could be talking with this film but I want to save a lot of the good stuff.


Watch this movie. Please. Just do it. It might break you, but it's worth it.

Name: Alex Wheatle
Review: As expected after Mangrove and Red, White & Blue, this is another well acted, well written, and well shot film. Though as far as the structure, pacing and overall story go, it is definitely lacking in focus. I wasn't very engaged, especially in the last 20 minutes or so, and there's really no reason behind the sequence of events. I think Alex is an interesting character and it's easy to connect with him, though I wish the way his story was told was put together in a cleaner fashion.

Name: How the Grinch Stole Christmas
Review:
A film by Ron Howard. I'm sure he's a nice guy, though his filmography is something of mediocrity.


Also this is still better than Hillbilly Elegy.

Name: I, Tonya
Review: A good film overall. The screenplay is solid, Robbie gives an amazing performance and the supporting roles aren't half bad either (Janney did not deserve the Oscar though), and you definitely feel the pain of all her abuse growing up. However, I don't think the film had a strong focus throughout the entire runtime and there were many sequences that felt unnecessarily repetitive or long. It isn't an easy or fun watch, so I'm trying to keep that in mind as well. As far as cinematography, every shot was angled well, though the framing was problematic, most notably when filming Tonya perform. It was also well edited for the most part. Overall, it's a good character study and tackles the topic of abuse fairly well, though I didn't feel as moved by the end as I think I wanted.

Name: Emma.
Review: Admittedly great production design, costumes and cinematography can't quite save the story that just really couldn't make a connection to me, backed up by a rather bland screenplay. The performances were good as well, and I think Autumn de Wilde did a great job for her first directing credit, though I struggled to finish this one. Not a bad film but not one of my favorites of the year.

Name: Education
Review: The middle of the pack as far as the Small Axe films go, but that still means this is very good. Kingsley is very well developed and I found it rather easy to connect to him. As you might expect from the others in the series, the acting, direction, screenplay and cinematography are all very well done. While the performances of this one don't live up to Mangrove, Red, White & Blue or Alex Wheatle, the story has a great flow to it and I think the structure is very well made. Not sure what else to say that hasn't already been said about the other Small Axes, my thoughts are pretty similar to those.

Name: Small Axe
Review: Score is the average of all 5 films. Here are the hotlinks to my reviews of each: Mangrove Lovers Rock Red, White and Blue Alex Wheatle

Name: Time
Review: A very good documentary that tells an interesting story in a subpar way. The structure of the used footage really didn't have focus and I was never entirely sure what was happening. Despite that, I still did enjoy myself and liked what I was shown for the most part. There are better documentaries to come out of this year though.

Name: The Invisible Man
Review:
Watched yesterday, ran out of time and forgot to log it.

Good for the performance from Elisabeth Moss and the thrills of the second half. Also it didn't quite feel 2 hours which is nice.

Bad for the pacing and uncomfortable plot structure.

Mediocre for everything else.

Name: The Santa Clause
Review:
A childhood classic of mine.


Though it's safe to say that without Tim Allen's charm, this would be nothing.
Tags: disney+, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Ma Rainey's Black Bottom
Review: Interesting movie, to say the least. Chadwick does give a great performance, though all the others weren't that far behind. It was very well shot and edited, I appreciated the way it was directed for the most part, the sound design/quality was outstanding, and I think the tone was really good for the most part. Though some parts were better than others, I was really enjoying myself, always engaged, and never bored. I think it easily could've been an 8 if it didn't end the way it did. It ends with a really sad, heartfelt moment (no spoilers) but then moves onto another scene that really doesn't have anything to do with the rest of the film, then boom. End credits. Not a fan of the ending at all. Still, it's very worth checking out for the great aspects of it, and it's probably going to get a lot of Oscar attention.

Name: The Assistant
Review: A good film, though it's safe to say that when it finished I was expecting a bit more. It was very well shot, directed and written, and I think the level of realism and relatability is excellent. It gave me some similar vibes to Paterson, though not with as much heart or depth. The main complaint I have with this one is, while I think they started to develop some good #MeToo scenes, the ends were never really met (none of the subplots felt complete, in other words), and they just felt a bit empty to me. Nonetheless, this feels very accurate and I did enjoy myself. Though it didn't blow me away.

Name: Yi Yi
Review:
Yi Yi is a beautiful, charming, and deliberate vignette of masterful filmmaking. Arguably the most famous aspect of this film is the cinematography, and it is impossible to deny the merit of that aspect. Every shot is total eye candy, with flawless colors (I loved the multiple shades of crimson) and ever so precise framing. It tells a very personal story that is developed slowly, but surely. You truly feel like you understand all the main characters, backed up by amazing performances, and it's easy to find a connection. On that note, the child performances might be the best in a film I've ever seen. Yang's direction is terrific and you can tell this is exactly the movie he wanted to make. A great screenplay also helps bring it all together.

So why not a perfect score? To tell you the truth, I was definitely close to giving it the edge. While this is an emotional film, and you definitely do feel many of the scenes, I just didn't seriously take much out of it, aside from the memorable tense moments scattered throughout and those final few sucker-punch minutes. I totally get how people are affected by the abstract look at life this film offers, and why that would make this a favorite film for them. However, I've been more affected and thought about life more after watching other films than this one. Nonetheless, I had an excellent time watching this film and I'd be hard pressed to find someone who hasn't.

Name: The Host
Review: A good and enjoyable film indeed, though I must admit that I felt pretty underwhelmed by it overall. As to be expected from a Bong Joon-ho film, the screenplay is very strong and the characters are well developed. The story is quite a good one as well, and the Anti-America commentary wasn't bad at all (though at times it felt a bit too obvious for my tastes). There are also a lot of good shots, though unfortunately I think there are an equal amount of shots that just felt awkward, especially surprising coming from the same man who shot Memories of Murder. On that note, let's get into some of the critiques. First off, the tone. Especially at the beginning, I felt little focus in the tone and was never quite sure how to feel during scenes until we really got going. Bong directed this film well, though it feels as though not as much effort was put into it as Memories of Murder. Not to mention some dated CGI that looks like it came out of a video game. Lastly, this just didn't engage me as much as I'd hoped. There are some great moments to this film, and even the slower parts weren't bad at all, though my attention was not always consistently kept. Despite that, I'd still recommend this one, though don't go in expecting Memories of Murder or Parasite.

Name: In the Mood for Love
Review: Excellent overall, though I wish the film ended with the same energy I was feeling with the rest of the film. Excellent cinematography, directing, writing and performances. Much like Yi Yi, I really connected to what was happening and I was pretty much engaged the whole time. The pacing is great and I had a smile on my face from start to finish. This is kind of a bad review but I'm not sure what else to say. While I was very entertained, I didn't really get any big takeaways so it doesn't get the perfect score.

Name: Gotti
Review: I really wish I could've watched this while high on lean or some shit.

Name: Roundhay Garden Scene
Review: Parasite really is #1 on the Letterboxd top 250 when this exists. Damn...

Name: Sound of Metal
Review: While I was hoping for more of a personal / emotional impact, pretty much everything about this film is great. Riz Ahmed easily gives one of the best performances of the year (the only male performances that rival him are John Boyega in Red, White & Blue and Delroy Lindo in Da 5 Bloods, sorry Chadwick), excellent cinematography and directing with a lot of focus in the story and pacing, a great screenplay, brilliant music and sound mixing, and incredibly well developed characters. It all feels very natural and realistic. Definitely one of the best of the year and it makes me happy to see it getting considerable awards season attention.

Review: Forgot to log this lol but enjoyed it quite a bit more the second time. More of the jokes worked really well for me and I'm surprised I didn't enjoy it this much the first time.

Name: Rushmore
Review: Another example of a film that feels like it came into my life right when I needed it. It taps into a certain energy that I really connect to and I feel like a lot of my friends do as well, and it honestly just got really personal in a way that I didn't expect it to. If I had to describe this in one word it would be insightful. I loved pretty much everything about this film and it deserves a much deeper analysis than I'm giving it, which I'll do after I give it a second watch. But holy fuck yeah, it's been a few minutes and while I'm not as emotionally affected by it as my other 10/10 films, I think a re-watch will do just that. The last thing I'm going to say is that this film is preserved by the National Film Registry which is pretty dope!

Name: Broken Flowers
Review:
Liked this a lot. Obviously it's all about the characters and, though many have minimal screen time, still have solid development. All the performances were good and backed up by a great screenplay along with Jarmusch's great direction. It isn't really life changing or anything, but I was very entertained and I'm glad I checked this out. I didn't expect this to be Paterson, but I was NOT disappointed with what I got.

Also this was a watch party with Max, Joe and Alex

Review: A classic! Nostalgia! And also just kinda mediocre overall. Quite fun however, and this has been a favorite since like 3rd grade

Name: A Christmas Story
Review: "Alright, I'll get that kid to eat. Where's my screwdriver and plumber's helper? I'll open up his mouth and shove it right in." Yeah, the above quote isn't the best one to use, but that's just the thing. This is such a well-written film and every line is quotable. This is an excellent movie overall, so let me tell you my experience with it. Flashback to December 2011. I was just 8 years old, in 2nd grade. I was familiar with Christmas films like The Santa Clause trilogy and some of the classic animated ones (my parents were rather restricted and I used to be a bit close-minded when it came to watching new stuff, so the list was pretty short at this time). However, this was my first more real Christmas film I ever saw. I was so amazed by what I was viewing. I knew immediately this was going to stick in my mind for a while, and it really did. I felt such a strong connection to both Parker boys, and aside from some of the Star Wars: The Clone Wars characters (my main obsession at this age), I think Ralphie was the most memorable movie character for me. I've watched the film at least once a year since then, usually more than that. This is a time capsule from the 1940s; it's like a cross between urban legend, Playboy magazines, and really old home movies. Not a single detail is out of place, transforming you not only into suburban Chicago (Hohman is based on Hammond, Indiana) from the past, but the mind of Ralphie. Every time we see his imagination, we listen to him narrate, we just feel like we know him so well. It's absolutely timeless and I get something out of every re-watch of this classic. While you might not be exactly like him, you'd be hard-pressed to find a pre-teen boy who doesn't share at least one quality with him. I have such a strong personal connection to this film and it really hit the spot tonight. I thought about times past, all the friends from 9 years ago who I used to see everyday and talk with about this film and other such things, and just how good life was at that younger age. Yes, this doesn't have to do with the film itself but I'm just describing the emotion this re-watch invoked in me. I love this film and I'm going to rewatch is every Christmas until the day I die.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Klaus
Review:
This was a good film. Excellent animation and a solid screenplay make this special. The voice acting is rather good as well, featuring several big-name actors and actresses. It's paced very well at that. The story can be predictable and has some cheese here and there with the plot elements, and I also really hated the soundtrack (all the modern artists and songs do NOT fit the film's tone at all) but I did enjoy myself. This is a good movie with the family, check it out.

I don't really have much else to say. Normally I have a bit more edge to my reviews though I'm having a great day with my parents and siblings, so this is a more positive, straightforward review for you.

Name: The Grand Budapest Hotel
Review:
Great movie! I thoroughly enjoyed myself from start to finish. It didn't quite have the same impact on me as Fantastic Mr. Fox or Rushmore did, but I doubt Wes Anderson will disappoint me with any of his films.

As you've heard, the technical aspects of this are truly incredible. Wes has one of the most distinct visual styles of any film director I can think of, and this lives up his good name. The production design is absolutely stunning and some of the best of the decade and beyond, the cinematography is excellent as always, the costumes and paintings are gorgeous, and the use of color in general is mindblowing. I've heard some complaints about the usage of greenscreens and CGI feeling a bit off, and while neither aspect was perfect, I was never really bothered by this. The ski and sled chase towards the end of the film was fairly obviously computer generated, though it gave that scene a cartoonish vibe and I didn't mind. Wes Anderson's directing skills were put to good use and then some. Not to mention another fantastic score from Alexandre Desplat!

While none of the performances were heartstoppers or anything (I was also a bit bothered by the high amount of American accents, not quite sure if this was intentional or not), I did enjoy all the characters presented and you could tell they connected with each role. I also do wish more of the supporting characters were developed as well as Zero and Gustave, but this isn't a huge flaw. The screenplay is a good one, though I feel it didn't consistently capture the style of the time period and specific lines did take me out of the immersion briefly. The story was very well paced and engaging for the most part. I believe I got the intended effect out of each major plot element, and there were a lot of moments where I laughed out loud. However, I think some scenes haven't quite stuck out in my memory like I'd hoped, as a lot of the smaller subplots aren't as easy to piece together in my head. And this brings me to what I think the main reason why the rating isn't any higher than it is; style over substance.

While I was always entertained by what was being presented, I do wish the film tried to make more of a prominent point or have more commentary integrated with the story, and in turn I didn't think the film was very purposeful. This seemed to be more of a Wes Anderson passion project rather than a piece of art with a message behind it. This isn't necessarily a knock on the film as a whole, and I understand why that is a reason behind why so many people adore this film, though it is worth commenting that for this film to make my favorites list, it would have to say something.

All things considered, I had a great time with this one. As said, it isn't my favorite film by Wes, but it wasn't disappointing in the slightest and I'm very thankful that I got it on Criterion as a Christmas gift. I have the rating at 4/5 stars for now, but I could definitely see it going up to a 4.5/5 in the future. It's a joy from start to finish and I can't think of anyone close to me personally that I wouldn't recommend this to. Check it out when you can!
Tags: criterion blu-ray, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Primer
Review: A good film that's amazingly well made for how low the budget is, though the story itself didn't really consistently engage me. It's very well shot, written and directed, and I think the concept is a great one. The ideas discussed are good ones indeed, though I'd be lying if I said there were parts of the film which left me rather bored, despite all the positives. Nonetheless, I'm glad I checked this out, as its a very interesting piece of filmmaking that doesn't seem to be talked about very much.

Name: Good Time
Review: This is one of those films that I'm honestly just not sure how to review. It was great for the most part, I liked it. It was stressful, but that's what it was going for. Really good performances across the board and the score was amazing. The whole thing reminded me of Drive, and I'm not sure which one I prefer. The colors were also great, and I love how the borough of Queens was used as a location, almost as a sort of character. The pacing was terrific, and the story was structured very well as a whole. However, the cinematography was definitely my least favorite part of it, as a majority of the shots in the first 20-25 minutes or so were all close-ups, and most of them didn't need to be. I understand they wanted a sense of stress, but mostly the camerawork just felt a bit obnoxious. Once things slow down, it definitely improves but the cinematography still isn't very good. The search for the LSD and the ensuing events trying to avoid the police are awesome; I think it ends very well, though I just kinda wish the film rolled credits on that shot of Pattinson in the cop car. I didn't mind ending with Nick in the special needs program, though for dramatic effect I think the capture would've been a better stopping place. Either way, this is a great film overall and I'm glad I checked it out. It fits a lot into the 90-ish minute runtime and I was always engaged. Can't wait for Uncut Gems!

Name: Spider-Man: Homecoming
Review:
Goddammit, Dad. I've gone about 9 months at this point without watching a Marvel movie, but I guess you just had to invite me to watch this with you (I'm glad you want to spend time with me, but why this?)

Anyways, this was a Marvel movie. Predictable and very repetitive plot, mediocre screenplay (though this was funnier than I remember), meh CGI, and just not that original or special. I do appreciate the fact that they went a different direction with this film, as in starting with an already established Spider-Man instead of another origin story, but a lot of the plot points still do feel derivative of the first Sam Raimi Spider-Man from 2002. Not to mention inconsistent characters, as I didn't consistently really believe Tom Holland as Peter Parker. The constant awkwardness of this also made it hard to stay excited for the rest of the film. As with most Marvel movies (actually, just over half of them), this wasn't a BAD movie, but I was definitely bored. To repeat what I said at the beginning, this is a Marvel movie.

Name: Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri
Review: Wow. I wasn't really into this the first 30-45 minutes and I think that's mainly why it isn't the full 10/10 rating, but the last hour redeemed the hell out of it. Amazing performances from everyone, excellent social commentary on cop corruption, herd mentality and grieving, emotional moments that hit the spot at all the right times, a mostly good screenplay aside from some clunky dialogue at the start, great directing, I was engaged from start to finish, and the production design was terrific. The only real criticism I think I have aside from not really hooking me until a little before the halfway mark is the tonal shifts. There were a handful of moments where the tone and overall mood felt rather unclear, but most of the movie did a good job with this aspect. Quite the excellent film overall, one of the best of 2017 and it just gets better as it goes along.
Tags: blu-ray, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Relic
Review: Overall a very good film, it definitely exceeded my expectations. The story and character development in the first half is very well done, and all the thrills and chills of the second half hit the mark. A solid screenplay and direction really bring the film alive. The grandmother and daughter give great performances, and while the mother was a bit more mixed with her character, she didn't do a bad job either. The camerawork is quite good as well. There are a lot of sequences full of quick cuts that don't quite feel necessary, which might be my biggest complaint, but otherwise, there aren't many flaws with this one. I thoroughly enjoyed myself, and would definitely recommend it! Unless you count I'm Thinking of Ending Things as a horror film, I'd say this is the best horror film of 2020 I've seen thus far, and perhaps the best one overall (sorry Invisible Man).

Name: City of God
Review:
An absolute achievement. This cuts right to the bone when it comes to emotional impact and giving a physical reaction. The violence is not glamorized in this film; rather, most of the killings are not shown with much detail, and with this, they honestly have more meaning than when someone is killed in a Scorsese crime film.

On that note, the Scorsese films definitely are better technically (City of God had a number of editing choices that bothered me, and the cinematography is inferior though not bad at all), but they're all kinda the same when it comes to developing characters outside of the De Niros and the Pescis. When someone is killed in this, you actually think about how this is a real person and how depressingly inevitable it is. They were born into the ghetto and were raised in an environment of gangsters and violence. What else are they going to do except join them? I'm not even sure if this was the reaction Meirelles intended for the audience to receive, but whenever anyone was killed, I actually thought about how they could've had a full life but it was ruined and they couldn't help it. It's depressing as fuck and no other crime film has reached the emotional heights of this. Even beyond all that, there's so much more to rave about with this film. the directing is absolutely phenomenal. Only one real actor was in this entire film, and he didn't have a very big role. The way Meirelles was still able to bring each character to live and push everyone to give terrific performances is mindblowing. I was completely locked in from start to finish, and aside from one little sequence which I'll get to in the next paragraph, this had damn near flawless pacing and I knew from the start that I was going to love this.

The only reason why I hesitated to give this film a 10 at first is the final half hour. The narrator of the film is always in the background of the story being told, but he never really has a major role in the story until this point. This bothered me at first, but it's now dawned on me that this was most likely purposeful as the character did not want to get involved with all the gang shit. Then, after an hour and a half of hard-to-watch violence where so many innocents are killed, we have a HUGE break in pace, where we get a look at Rocket (the narrator) and what he's doing; he has taken a job as a newspaper photographer to help them get the story on the City of God itself. As I said, it didn't hit me until now, but this is where we finally get some development of the narrator's character. From there, Rocket returns to the City for the final battle, and is able to capture much of what happens, while the narrative still hits emotionally for the audience. It's fantastic and the perfect way of doing things.

Onto the main character of the film itself, Lil' Dice, later Lil' Z', he has been involved in crime and ruthlessly killing people from a young age. Later in life, he is doing the same as well as dealing drugs to make money.
