He has an extremely hot temper and when his best friend meets a girl and decides to leave the gang life, takes a look at himself to try and change, but ends up returning to his old agenda. He kills innocents for a rush. He has to be one of the most well-developed villains in any film, as I felt a sense of rage whenever he came on screen. But, as with the other characters, this is simply what he was born in to, and the only way he knows how to deal with it is to rise in rank. That's just the depressing reality of it.

To re-iterate the first sentence, this film is nothing short of an achievement. It is a bit flawed in its technical aspects, but the narrative and emotional impact are masterful and make up for it. I was on the verge of tears whenever anyone was killed, and it serves as a shockingly relevant example of poverty in crime-ridden neighborhoods even today. Initially I gave this film a 9, but as I have sat here thinking about the film even more and writing this review, I am reminded of how stunned I am and how flaws I had at first aren't so bad. While I'd fall just a bit short of calling this film a masterpiece, it is one of the greats of the century so far. Add this one to your watchlist if you haven't seen it yet.

Name: Punch-Drunk Love
Review:
If you know me, then you could probably guess what I'm about to say... it's 2:45, I'm tired and want to go to bed, so I'll update in the morning. All I'm gonna say is idk what I was smoking the first time I saw this, but the re-watch did it a lot of good. Way better than I remember!

Alright, it's the morning... so I don't know if I was just overcome with the excitement that I would watch There Will Be Blood after this for my P.T. Anderson binge, or if I just didn't give this film my full attention. But I'm not sure why I only gave it a 7/10. This is a high 8, not too far off of a 9. The use of the color blue is excellent. Adam Sandler is really good, and as someone with Aspergers, I'm surprised I never picked up on that the first time I saw this. There are some parts of it where Adam Sandler's "funny" side felt like it was coming in strong, and it almost gave his Aspergers personality a comical aspect to it that I wasn't a fan of, but overall this is a good performance. Otherwise, I mean, the cinematography is excellent, the screenplay and direction are great, and I just felt a much stronger connection to it. Plus, it feels quite a bit shorter than it actually is.

Name: O Brother, Where Art Thou?
Review: My favorite Coen film so far. The duo describes this as a "three stooges kind of movie" and it works really well for me. The chemistry between the main guys was really engaging and the performances elevate things a lot. The production value is really high and none of it is wasted. All the technical aspects are really good, especially Deakins' stellar cinematography which I was able to identify from the first shot alone. The screenplay is just what I'd expect from Joel and Ethan, as is the great direction. Not to mention some excellent music as well. Overall, I just had a great time with this and was smiling from start to finish. It's such an entertaining adventure and I can already say it has high rewatch value.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: The Illusionist
Review:
Bought a full year of the Criterion Channel today so this is my first official film on it! (watched because it's leaving after tomorrow)

It was fine, I think? Solid animation with great set design even if I didn't really didn't like some of the character designs at all, and an ok story but for a film of only 80 minutes, it felt like such a drag to get through. I didn't care about what was happening, and there just wasn't much focus all around. Cool ending though, and the magic scenes at the beginning were interesting.

Name: Rat Race
Review: Stupid and not very well made but entertaining and self aware. Also very fun to watch with friends.
Tags: dvd, w/ arnav, w/ shrey, w/ farris

Name: Hot Fuzz
Review: Not as good as I remember but still very absurd and funny. Didn't really love the editing and the pacing is off in some places but y'know... it's Hot Fuzz. Gotta love it!
Tags: dvd, at a friend's place, w/ arnav, w/ farris, w/ shrey

Name: Mother
Review: Pretty great all around! Not really any bad aspects to it, and pretty consistent throughout though it gets a star off since it had some trouble keeping my attention at a few parts. More thoughts coming later this morning.

Name: Bill & Ted Face the Music
Review: My family was rather negatively vocal tonight about their thoughts on my love of film and my tastes and opinions to be specific. Whether they were serious or joking, their words cut deep. Maybe I would've had more fun with this in a better state of mind but after what my family told me, but I just can't review this competently. All the jokes felt recycled and if you enjoy the nostalgia and very similar style to the first films, then more power to you. But I guess I was hoping for something a little more original.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Rushmore
Review: Still can't get over how well this film understands and taps into the demographic of Max and his persona. I was definitely not sure if this film was a 9 or 10 the first time I saw this a couple weeks ago... I'm sure now. Actually it's only been a WEEK since I saw this for the first time and I'm already rewatching it. What does that say? The emotional reaction I got out of this did it for me. Brilliant film that I would recommend to everyone in my life.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Satantango
Review:
I mean, how else would I kick off 2021?


God DAMN! Watching this was such a daunting experience and certainly a challenge, though there were definitely many rewards along with it. I filmed my thoughts upon each hour and I'm compiling them all into a video which I'll upload to YouTube and link here once it's posted. Would much rather just do that then post the same thoughts here.

What I will say for now is this isn't a perfect film, but I'm still speechless. It's a complete beast and I'm in awe that this exists. Nearly every aspect is masterful and a good number of those justify the long-ass runtime. On that note, this really didn't feel 7 hours and 20 minutes long. I'll go into more detail in the review, but a number of this film's aspects make the duration MUCH more bearable than you might expect. I understand if this isn't a film you're going to jump right on and watch, but do put it on your watchlist as it's something I think that should be on every cinephile's watchlist. Fairly tempted to give it a 10, but it falls slightly short of both the rating and top 10% list.

Technically this could be considered a film I officially watched on January 2nd because I finished it at 3 AM, but I watched most of it on the 1st so that's what I'm listing it as in my diary.


Lastly, a quick recommendation for the best times to watch. As with any long film, start in the morning, so once you eventually finish you still have a fair amount of the day remaining. The first official intermission occurs at the 2 hour, 17 minute mark so plan for that. Then, you have another 2-ish hour increment before the second intermission at 4 hours, 22 minutes. At this point, you should be around lunch time so maybe take a longer break. That saves the remaining three hours for afterwards, then you have the rest of the afternoon and evening to enjoy yourself.

Name: The Big Lebowski
Review:
I think between this, Mulholland Drive and Under the Silver Lake, it's safe to say I'm gonna add 'joining a secret society in LA' to my personal bucket list.

Not quite my favorite Coen film (I still like O Brother more), but I did really enjoy this. I was a bit divided on my opinion for like 3/4 of the film, but the final quarter is really great and it started growing on me; I started to realize that this is a better movie than I was giving it credit for initially.

This is absolutely a film for the characters. The Dude and Walter are well developed and very memorable, backed up by great performances. I was surprised to find out the two main guys were actually inspired by two of the Coens' best friends and I think the way they captured their energy is really great, the chemistry at play is excellent. However, it's also worth commenting on every other character in the film, none of which are very well developed at all; even the other, millionaire Lebowski. This is probably the area where the film suffers the most.

The screenplay isn't the best the brothers have written, but still a very good one nonetheless. There is a lot of humor and very few lines that aren't snappy. It had a great flow to it and accomplished all it needed to. There was some excessiveness in how repeated certain jokes were and that annoyed me and took me out of the story, though this thankfully wasn't prominent. On the topic of the story, I really love how they tried to incorporate a sort of noir style to it. I'd be a bit surprised if Under the Silver Lake didn't take some inspiration from this, and all the strangeness, mystery and plot twists were executed well. Even if I didn't really love what was happening until the last half hour, its safe to say I was consistently engaged. On a similar note, the direction by the Coens is great, but that's to be expected.

Some other things to note, this has a really good score, and it gave a handful of the film's scenes a Boogie Nights feel to it, though not much of the film actually has music in the background. Deakins' cinematography is good here, and I think the vibrancy of pretty much every color is amazing-looking, though there just aren't very many opportunities for Deakins to show off his skill. Easily both the best camerawork and my favorite part of the film is the Gutterballs sequence, as it's both insanely funny and beautiful looking, but I wish more of the film lived up to this visual style and energy.

While not very deep or life-changing, The Big Lebowski is thoroughly entertaining and humorous, while being much more intelligent than you might expect. It has its flaws and I wouldn't call it a masterpiece as some would, but I am very glad I got around to checking it out, as it's an easy film to recommend and I definitely see myself revisiting it. The cult classic status is warranted and if you're part of that following, more power to you.

Unrelated, but I think I've gone on a bit of a Coen high recently, so hopefully I'll get to check out some of the bigger ticket ones like No Country, A Serious Man, Llewyn Davis, etc. Look forward to those in the future.

Name: Shadow in the Cloud
Review:
Kinda like a motion simulator ride at a theme park but with misogynistic characters.


Ok but this was honestly funny. Chloë gave a decent performance but her accent changed halfway through and she didn't always have the right facial expression, which was especially noticeable given most of the film is literally just a medium close-up on her. The screenplay is so awful and excessive (one of the characters literally said "bitches be crazy" and this is a WW2 film), and all the characters were just nonstop bullying Chloë. I also found of one of the screenwriters (Max Landis) had sexual assault allegations so I fear to think about how much of the dialogue was written, um... let's say "from the heart." Beyond that, the CGI is insanely cheap, I had so much trouble staying engaged and the story took several stupid turns, the score was all mediocre synth music... I love you Chloë and you're the one reason why this was watchable, but holy shit LMAO.

Name: Paris, Texas
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 1 -- A film from 1984 As a whole movie, this is great. The cinematography is excellent, Wenders' direction is great, the characters are well developed and backed up by mostly good performances, and it has a terrific screenplay. Aside from a few sequences here and there, I was mostly engaged the whole time, and the ending has an emotional punch (though the very end felt a bit too open-ended for my taste). However, I wish there were some more strong emotional punches throughout the rest of it. I think I didn't quite get the emotional reaction I desired / is required for the film to receive a higher rating, and in turn it felt like there was something missing. There aren't really any striking flaws and that's why it has the high rating it does, but it doesn't quite qualify as a favorite. Definitely worth watching however, check it out sometime.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2021, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Never Rarely Sometimes Always
Review: Really enjoyed this one. Great performances, great screenplay, and great direction. Solid cinematography (love the colors) and decent editing as well. I was pretty engaged from start to finish and the runtime felt shorter than 100 minutes. However, my main gripe with this film has to do with the pacing. There were sequences, mainly at the beginning, that had this really captivating energy and I wish more of the film had this. In turn, the rest of the film was just good and not great, kinda like your average drama. With that, I also think the tone didn't have very much focus and I didn't really feel too strongly emotionally towards what was happening. Needless to say, very glad I finally got around to checking this film out, as it's one of the better movies to come from 2020.

Name: Inside Llewyn Davis
Review:
I'm feeling a wide variety of emotions right now so I'm gonna try my best to put thoughts into words. I think the one point I can put into words is I'm a little confused towards the end and what it symbolizes so I think a second watch will give it a bump up in rating. Even then, I'm told a second watch does this film well, kinda like a Kaufman film.

The performances are all so perfect and Oscar Isaac transforms into the character so well. The screenplay is amazing as well and engages me just the same. Beautiful cinematography lines every single shot. It's another one of those films that feels so candid and real and I can't praise it enough for that. The musical aspect is great and I love that most people are actually playing and singing real songs. I'm not sure what else to say at this point, I'm very internally overwhelmed and honestly pretty flustered but in the best way possible. I am VERY tempted to bump it up to a 10, but at the least I can say it's going on my favorites list and it's my favorite Coen film so far.


Oh yeah I also forgot to mention that I'm in the process of looking for a new therapist with my parents so uhhhhh interesting choice for a family movie night!
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Blood Simple
Review: This was a decent movie but also very much a directorial debut. Some of the scenes were GREAT as far as building tension and keeping me engaged, but most of the exposition scenes kinda lost me. Some good performances across the board (this was McDormand's debut as well) and a very Coen-esque screenplay make this worth watching especially. They definitely tried to make the plot unique, but I found it to be a bit over-complicated and hard to follow at times. The cinematography was good, but the editing was, well... not quite the same. Lastly, the pacing felt slightly off and I was never quite sure what direction the focus was going. But despite the number of flaws, this was pretty entertaining and a solid first film.

Name: Rush Hour
Review: Good action but I didn't really find it funny and the screenplay is rather questionable by today's standards. Recommended by my parents for one last movie night before school starts back up for me tomorrow. I tried to enjoy it because they both love it, but... yeah I just didn't. I don't know what else to say.
Tags: dvd rental, w/ dad, w/ mom, library

Name: Amores Perros
Review:
Alright it's later today so review time.

This was excellent and one hell of a director debut. The second and third acts (last hour and a half) are flawless, but the first act (first hour) is good, though not great and I think that's where it loses the half star. The screenplay and performances are soooo good, and Inarritu's directing is somehow terrific even though this is his first. The cinematography is mostly good though there aren't really any amazing shots until the third act. The story was incredibly well paced and was developed well enough to keep me consistently engaged. While adamantly a hard watch, both from the standpoint of seeing the characters struggle and as a lover of dogs, but the outstanding filmmaking and loose thematic elements kept me engaged, along with emotional connections to all the characters (and canines).


I would love to try and write more but this is a good reminder to stop watching films right before I sleep because my recall isn't as good and this is a film that deserves more commentary.

Name: Thor: Ragnarok
Review:
Today is my first day of school (still online at home) but it's all introductory things so I was still able to squeeze this in with my dad.

Yeah, yeah, Marvel bad I know... this is one of their better films. Screenplay is admittedly a mixed bag and I think if Taika was able to both direct and write, this would be the best Marvel movie. Even then, the dialogue is decent and not every joke landed for me, but a fair amount of them did. The CGI is also a bit mixed but most of the film looks good. It also has surprisingly great cinematography; almost every shot looked great. The performances obviously aren't much to shout at but they aren't bad. Also the opening scene is easily one of my favorite sequences in Marvel, and the ending is lots of fun as well. I'm glad to see Taika is continuing to work with Marvel as he brought a lot of life into this one, especially after a disappointing first couple of Thor movies. Overall, as you might expect with the review, I had more fun with this than I expected and I'm glad I checked it out. It's a nice change in pace from what I've been watching recently.

Name: Memento
Review:
Showed my friends Memento. Here's how it went down. (this is all my interpretation, they might have felt different)

One was on his phone half the time and ended up enjoying some parts but we had a lot of explaining to do for his sake.

One just kinda sat and enjoyed the film as it happened.

The last one figured out what was going on like 20 minutes in and really loved the rest of it.


Ah, the joys of first-time watches of postmodern cinema.
Tags: dvd, w/ shrey, w/ arnav, w/ farris, at a friend's place

Name: Short Term 12
Review: I'm so overwhelmed by how it handles the subject matter with such respect and accuracy. Everything about this film is perfect, it all lays out in flawless order, and I simply couldn't take my eyes off the screen or even check to see how far I was into it. The ending isn't overly sappy or depressing or anything, it's just bittersweet and blends both tones this film was going for in terrific fashion. One of my new favorite movies and it is going to stick with me for a long fucking time. Holy shit I can't believe this was as good as it was; I'm completely shook, crying, and just overall blown away. I understand that not everyone is going to feel the same way I do about this film, but this is an absolute highlight of the 2010s for me. Holy fuck I could just go on and on and on. MASTER. FUCKING. PIE.

Name: 21 Grams
Review:
Alejandro wtf how do you go from Amores Perros to this middle-of-the-road (literally) melodrama?

What a disappointment. The admittedly great performances carry the basic screenplay, poor structure / pacing, and annoying romances. The nonlinear storyline was executed rather badly if I'm being honest, as there were a number of sequences that were simply hard to follow and made the plot more complicated than it should've been; every transition between scenes felt like it happened at the worst moments. Worse yet, because of the changes in the timeline, the direction of the story started to feel repetitive and it lost me at a few points. At that rate, it was hard to care about what was happening or stay engaged. I expected more of Iñárritu and Guillermo Arriaga than to resort to turning the drama and grief of the characters into a hard to believe love story, but that's the direction the story took. Some of the characters were developed adequately, while others felt like they were lacking information. The cinematography wasn't bad, but apparently there's some meaning behind the look of each shot as far as filtering / colors but even having read up on that, it's beyond me. On paper, the premise sounds very reminiscent of Amores, however the location and different characters make this distinguishable enough. It's just a shame they're bogged down by a simply bad and frustrating story. And without going into too many details, the shots in the last 5 minutes are all randomly placed earlier in the film and I felt no emotional impact in turn. Once again, not a good use of the nonlinear timeline at all.

Name: 2016: Obama's America
Review: Only watched this because I bought it as a joke from a Family Video near me that was selling DVDs for $0.25 (closing sale, RIP video stores :'(

Name: Mystery Train
Review:
Maybe a hot take but I got very little out of this. The performances, writing and direction are all decent, and I definitely do appreciate the way Jarmusch portrayed Memphis as well as the purpose/meaning behind the trains and Elvis. That was all really good, but the characters made minimal impressions on me (aside from the Japanese couple) and I wasn't really engaged with what was happening. I could hardly tell you what happens in this film since there aren't many particularly memorable scenes.

Not a bad film and it has some great qualities, but one I need to visit again sometime soon to see if it changes in quality.

Name: Raising Arizona
Review: A lot more traits of the Coens we know today are in this so they definitely started to find their signature style just two films in which is impressive. The film itself is rather decent, backed up by solid performances and a decent screenplay, but it's worth mentioning that not very many of the jokes stuck the landing for me. I only really found myself laughing at the physical comedy and hardly ever at what was being spoken, so I can't say the film delivered on arguably it's main purpose. Also, as with Blood Simple, the technical aspects are fairly flawed. Some scenes are easy to forgive as the film definitely has more of a cartoony style, though only so much can be overlooked. Despite that, it was rather engaging the whole way through and I enjoyed the characters (some were a bit lacking in development however), and the ending was great, from the chases to the last monologue. It's entertaining and fun despite its flaws, so that's good. But in the grand scheme of the Coens filmography, it falls on the lower end for me.
Tags: dvd rental, w/ dad, w/ mom, library

Name: Whiplash
Review: Checked this out in preparation for a Whiplash re-watch hopefully later today, I'm planning to write something related to the film though I want to try and think of some other movie / idea to incorporate into it. Chazelle really did have early talent for directing, both the camera and the actors. You can definitely tell that a lot of the screenplay and structure of the short film was incorporated into the beginning of the feature film, and the fact that I was able to recall so much goes to show how much of the film actually sticks with you. I still remember a pretty high amount of the film and it's been about 9 months since my first watch (it doesn't feel that long though, holy shit).

Name: Red Beard
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 2 -- A film by Akira Kurosawa This was a weird watching experience for me. ADHD is a bitch to me when I'm not medicated and I'm gonna assume I forgot to take my afternoon pill since I felt the runtime at a number points. The film itself is a great one and the filmmaking is pretty much flawless. The cinematography is beautiful and the production design is amazing. Narratively, it's good and the screenplay was good, though it didn't really blow me away and I wasn't really touched by anything until the ending. There were also a few too many characters for me to connect to any of them very strongly, and I'm not sure exactly if it was something in the film itself, but I kinda feel like shit now that it's done. I'm not going to hold this against anything that Kurosawa did with the film and the story that was told, but it's worthy of note. I'm interested to rewatch the previous three Kurosawa films I've seen now since I think I prefer his Samurai work.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2021, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Babel
Review:
I am slightly hesitant to give this a 9/10 rating, but nonetheless, this was amazing. From who I follow, it seems to me that this is a fairly hit-or-miss film, but it hit the spot for me. I loved it; my expectations were vastly exceeded.

Since there is so much I could praise this film for, I'm gonna get the criticisms out of the way. While it isn't as big of a problem here as 21 Grams, there were still several transitions between the different narrative locations that just felt cut off at the wrong time and uneven. Some of the transitions were good, but a lot of them were frustrating. However, this was mainly during the first 90 minutes or so; once the stories started to blend together, this became less of a problem. The only other thing I have to say is there were some scenes that didn't need to have as many cuts as there were, so I guess the editing is the main snafu I have with Babel.

Anyways, let's get onto the good stuff, because there's a lot of it. The screenplay is a great one; all the dialogue feels entirely natural and it accomplished all that it needed to. Despite the uneven transitions mentioned in the first paragraph, I was completely invested in the story and characters. Everyone was developed incredibly well and I really cared about them... not to mention there wasn't a single bad performance; Brad Pitt, Amelia Hernandez and Rinko Kikuchi in particular did spectacular. Every emotional moment hit me the right way and I adored the way the story progressed. Pretty much every end is met and the conclusion felt truly satisfying in its own way. I was just captivated for at least the last half hour and I couldn't believe it felt like the time went by as quick as it did; the mark of a great movie for me. This is a film that's going to stick with me for a while and I love it for that.

The thematic elements aren't very apparent or complex, as are the messages, but they still serve a purpose. As the title suggests, there is crossover between countries (mainly between USA, Morocco and Mexico) and the social commentary is really solid here. There's something to be said here about racial sensitivity and how certain situations are interpreted; it only added to the experience. There are also aspects to the themes that are also sadly still prevalent today; the Mexico story had some elements about deporting immigrants and border control, and depressingly feel a lot like how the situation still is today.

Now let's tackle the remaining aspects to be discussed. As you might expect, every detail is purposeful and it isn't the best directed film ever, but it's clear Iñárritu put effort into the project. The score is a really good one and every song was used correctly; when the end credits rolled, I didn't feel like turning the film off yet... I just lost myself in the music. And lastly, let's discuss what I think is easily the best filmmaking aspect; the cinematography. There are so many memorable and well done shots which line the story and are more than just icing on the cake. There are enough to warrant an appreciation post on Instagram, so I've gotten started on working on it. It's all so visually appealing and not hard at all to lose yourself in the setting, wherever that may be.

Lastly, I'd like to touch on how Babel fits in with the other two films in the so-called "Death Trilogy," consisting of this, 21 Grams and Amores Perros. While each film has their own level of quality, I have to credit Iñárritu and Arriaga for making each film distinguishable enough, despite having very similar sounding synopses. 21 Grams was very disappointing, and Amores Perros was great initially though it has lost a bit of its grip on me. Babel feels like a timeless film that will hold up on both re-watch and in my memory.

While Babel has a bit of a mixed reception, I am on the more positive side of things, as I have found a film that defied all expectations and isn't too far off of my top 10% list. Consisting of an incredibly enticing concept executed very well, with handfuls of emotional punches backed up by great writing and excellent performances. Iñárritu and Arriaga outdid themselves and created a timeless story with solid thematic elements and a captivating plot. The progression is full of twists and surprises, rounding itself out incredibly nicely. Whether you end up loving it or not, I would recommend this film to everyone who reads this review; it's an unforgettable experience of precision and social commentary.

Name: Absurda
Review:
I've seen this before today but never logged it lmao.

This is the most David Lynch thing that David Lynch ever David Lynch-ed. Short but sweet; I love his style and will probably binge his work sometime in the next few months.

Name: The Revenant
Review: One of the most impressive films ever in my honest opinion, at least technically. I mean the cinematography, editing, set pieces, use of natural lighting and the real location, it's so unbelievable. Narratively, it's very good but prevents a higher rating. I was very engaged with like 90-95% of the film, though there were a couple distractions in the pacing and specific actions by characters that took me out of the film a bit. The other thing I have to say is the ending feels a bit too easy, and not quite as "earned" as I was hoping for. Those are slightly more nitpicks though; I'm still amazed a film like this exists, thanks to being maybe top 5 most technically impressive pieces of cinema ever and the absolutely stunning performances.
Tags: blu-ray rental, w/ dad, w/ mom, library

Name: Birdman or (The Unexpected Virtue of Ignorance)
Review: This was my third time watching it. I love this movie more than I love myself!
Tags: dvd, love this movie more than myself, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Miller's Crossing
Review:
When it comes to 1990 crime films, you hear a lot about GoodFellas, The Godfather Part III and Wild at Heart. However, the Coens also made a crime film of their own that doesn't seem to get very much attention.

I definitely liked this, though it's pretty up and down. When it's good, it's riveting. The blend of drama and humor is done shockingly well, as there were moments that were truly hilarious. The action sequences were also great, and I was consistently engaged when either of these occurred. The screenplay overall is a very good one, whether or not I'm interested. There are certain aspects of the Coens I don't like, such as repetitive dialogue, but this is made fun of and I really enjoyed that. However, I didn't feel like I knew the characters super well, and there was a lot of downtime where I just didn't have much attention put in. I also can't remember very much of the film outside of the great sequences, as it doesn't do anything great with the slower portions. It's very well shot, directed and acted, I will give it those, but is also rather Inconsistent. Nonetheless, an enjoyable watch and one of the more underrated films from the Coens.

Name: Amélie
Review:
Watched with my parents in celebration of my dad's 50th birthday.

Adored everything about this. This has so many traits of Wes Anderson with a little splash of Coens, but it still feels authentic and unique enough to be distinguishable from those filmmakers. The colors in this are so goddamn well used, it's insanely well written and directed, and the energy it has is just so heartwarming and engaging. One of the funniest movies I've seen in a while as well! Very shy of both my top 10% and a 5 star rating.
Tags: pluto tv, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: 2 or 3 Things I Know About Her
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 3 -- A film by Jean-Luc Godard I really do feel bad for this one. I wasn't really giving it my full attention, so I didn't quite absorb all it tried to say. I was able to watch a majority of it at least, so I'll give my thoughts from that. I loved the colors and cinematography it offers. It has interesting ideas and has some very engaging sections, but it felt more like a TV special than a movie. It's a bunch of interviews with random people talking about their interpretations of consumerism. Some of these were more interesting than others, and it's definitely a well written film, but I also didn't learn much about any of them. Even the main lady I didn't seem to learn much from her dialogue. The performances weren't bad, but nothing to shout at. I think just for the ambition of the film, visual stun and ideas tackled, this is a good film for sure, but it just wasn't super interesting either. Also Godard's narrations the entire film was just him whispering and it was a huge turnoff. If he actually spoke what he was saying, the film would go up to a 7 for the rating, but this was irritating to the point to actually ruin my enjoyment. At the very least, it's a cool slice of life for France in the mid-1960s.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2021, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Certain Women
Review: A welcoming introduction to Reichardt's work, indeed! It's gorgeously shot and well paced, with great performances from every leading lady. The screenplay is a very good one as well, with similar quality directing from Kelly. There aren't really any flaws with the film itself, but it also wasn't really life changing in any way; it was just an enjoyable look into the lives of these four women. They're well developed and all, though not much happens. And that's okay! I was engaged for the most part, and I can appreciate simplicity. Many people compare Reichardt's style to that of Jarmusch, and she credits Yasujiro Ozu as a major influence on her work... I can confirm this is all true. I think if I had one thing to say, it's with the location. This doesn't majorly impact my rating or opinion on the film, though as someone who spent a lot of time in the Northwest growing up (my dad is from Colorado and my mom from Washington), I guess I wish the location had more incorporation into the plot. It's pretty much just a convenience that they're from the Northwest, and the story wouldn't make much of a difference if it took place in the South, or the Mid-Atlantic, or LA, etc. etc. Anyway, this was definitely an enjoyable watch. Not quite worth buying on Criterion in my opinion, but I'd still recommend it. Maybe I'll bump it up to an 8 in the future, we'll see.

Name: Barton Fink
Review: Had mid expectations going in, but this was great! There are a lot of films that this could be compared to, like Adaptation, Under the Silver Lake or even Tower of Terror at Disney World. But as with most Coens, it's in a league of its own. Admittedly, it took me a bit of time to get into the story, as I enjoyed what I was seeing from the beginning but it didn't really demand my attention. The performances are all good, the screenplay is a great one, it's very well shot (this might be Deakins' weakest cinematography I've seen so far but that says a lot about his skill), and the art direction and overall feel of the setting are executed very well. Though at the same time, it felt a little safe for a Coen film. Once we got into the last half hour, however, things got amazing. Barton Fink easily has my favorite Coen ending I've seen thus far, and one of my new favorite endings in any film. Without spoiling anything, I was really caught off guard by how quickly everything escalated. My heartrate got significantly faster and the plot twists truly came as a surprise. The way it concludes the story in such a dark fashion really fits the tone and thematic elements, and I'm gonna be thinking about some of those images for a while. The thematic elements aren't super apparent, and I think I was expecting a little more complexity given how lengthy the film's Wikipedia page is, but they are still there. Each character has a certain symbol to them, and Fink with all his quirks and short temper is a great representation of the conditions of labor in Hollywood. Packing in a lot of what the Coens are known for and then some, Barton Fink is all around an excellent film that just gets better as it progresses. I'm tempted to move the score up to a 4.5/5, and this is my second favorite film from the pair just behind Inside Llewyn Davis (though I haven't seen No Country, Burn After Reading or A Serious Man yet).

Name: Uncut Gems
Review:
With apologies to my friends who find this film overrated... I thought this was great. It's honestly a toss-up whether I prefer this or Good Time. I have them both at an 8, and I both thoroughly enjoyed them for the most part. Good Time does certain things better, but so does Uncut Gems.

Anyways, enough comparing. Adam Sandler gave a very good performance and played his character well. I don't think it's as good as his work on Punch-Drunk Love, and for a screenplay that prides itself in incorporating Howard's Jewish faith heavily into the character, I think there was definitely room for improvement, but I'm good with what's here. Every supporting performance was excellent as well. Speaking of the screenplay, it's a great one. Every line is so crisp and flows incredibly well, and there's really not much wrong with it. It did everything it needed to and even surprised me at a handful of points. It's pretty well paced, though I don't think I felt the same exhilaration many did while watching. The first half is pretty consistent and gets better as it goes on, and while the second half starts off a bit slow to the point where I felt that the film peaked there, the ending rounded things out very well and I was satisfied. Plus, I really didn't see the last plot element coming and it had me stunned for sure. The film has a great energy to it nonetheless, and every aspect contributes to this. The cinematography is very good, and improves over the camerawork in Good Time. Not every shot is perfect, but many of them were framed and lit very well to contribute to the distinctive Safdie style.

Overall, this was a very consistently engaging film that accomplished most of what it set out to be. While I'm not sure if there are any flawless aspects, there aren't any bad aspects either, and it feels very well-rounded and complete. Would definitely recommend it, as it's one of the better films to come out in 2019 and a good example of a cinematic experience. Good Time is definitely a great movie, but I think Uncut Gems leaves more of a lasting impression so I would say I prefer it.

Name: Us
Review: This film kinda left me speechless. Not in the sense of being blown away, but in the sense of having very mixed thoughts and not exactly sure where to begin with a review. So I just kinda jumped in and let my train of thought take the reins. Read my review here.

Name: Ad Astra
Review: As hard as I tried to appreciate certain aspects, I couldn't help but just not enjoy this. As I've mentioned before, I need to get medicated for my ADHD and I can cut the film some slack for this, but there are still a lot of glaring issues in my eyes. It feels too long, the pacing is rather uneven and kinda makes Ford v Ferrari seem well executed, the screenplay was mostly mediocre and had a fair amount of cliché and cheesy lines, great set pieces but cartoonish CGI, annoying editing and lazy camerawork for every shot that wasn't a long shot, etc. etc. Also, for a film that takes itself quite seriously, there are a lot of moments that are just kinda ridiculous, the monkeys being the best example. It has some solid ideas and the sound design is excellent, as well as some effective emotional moments and really thrilling sequences. But the ideas have been tackled in much cleaner fashion in other sci-fi films and I really don't think this is a movie that'll stick in my mind for very long. This film has some fans, but for me it joins the lineup of disappointing space movies from this decade (Gravity and Interstellar the other examples; First Man is my last hope).
Tags: blu-ray rental, w/ dad, w/ mom, library

Name: First Cow
Review: Certainly a good film as expected, but with the respectable reputation it's garnered over the course of the last 6+ months, I was hoping to get a bit more out of the story and characters. As expected, it's very well directed and has excellent cinematography, editing and sound. The screenplay is a very good one as well, with lots of memorable dialogue, even if the film as a whole didn't quite engage me as consistently as I expected. The performances were solid as well, though for a film about the characters I didn't feel like I knew them super well. Otherwise, I don't think I need to say much more. I liked it and it's an enjoyable watch but nothing spectacular in my eyes.

Name: Promising Young Woman
Review:
DUDE... FUCK


I got out of the theater maybe 7 minutes ago and I'm still speechless, heart beating fast. A sensational experience that I want to watch again. Gonna let my thoughts match themselves up to sensible words then come back to it. But one of the best of the year for sure and I'm so happy I saw this on the big screen.
Tags: amc streets of woodfield 20, me time!

Name: House
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 4 -- A horror film This film was supposedly Japan's answer to Jaws. And I'm not sure what version of Jaws they got, but this was nothing like it. The first half plays out like a Buster Keaton comedy, and the second half is just 40 minutes of constant WTF. It's a great example of a movie that's so bad it's good, and in turn qualifies as a cult classic. And that's... all I got, yeah. This is one of the most unconventional movies ever and you just kinda have to experience it for yourself. Oh yeah I liked the music!

Name: Intolerable Cruelty
Review: Literally only watchable for Deakins' cinematography, but even then that feels lazy half the time. Weak screenplay and poor acting, low effort directing and wasted cast. Overall very unengaging, and maybe two joke landed for me. Having looked it up, it wasn't entirely the Coens' fault the film ended up how it did, but the result is still bad.

Name: Boyz n the Hood
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 5 -- A film released on LaserDisc Watched a week ahead since next weekend is Sundance and I wouldn't have had time for this given the amount of films I bought tickets to. This hit so hard. Dough, Tre and Ricky are developed so well and backed up by amazing performances, both the child actors along with Gooding Jr., Ice Cube and Chestnut. Not every supporting performance was great, but between that and a couple story issues, that's all I really have to criticize on it. This was Singleton's first screenplay and holy shit the man had talent. Every line just rolled right off the tongue, fit the style perfectly, and is packed full of memorable lines and dialogue that is so incredibly thoughtful. There is a ton of reflection on topics like revenge, violence, making your way through the world, and everything in between. It's genuinely sad that this film holds up so well nearly 30 years later. I actually feel like I learned things about myself from this film, and that's a mark of a great movie. The pacing and structure are terrific, every emotion hit well, and it's just really compelling on almost all fronts. Everyone should check out this film; I am comfortable calling it an essential watch.
Tags: dvd rental, criterion challenge 2021, w/ dad, w/ mom, library

Name: The Ladykillers
Review:
The Coens must've had a midlife crisis to release Intolerable Cruelty and this one after the other lmao

Pros
-Better than Intolerable Cruelty
-Deakins cinematography
-Two funny jokes, and an entertaining ending
-No Country for Old Men is next

Cons
-Pretty much everything else, which ranges from mediocre to bad. Why do my parents own this on DVD lol

Name: The Forty-Year-Old Version
Review: I enjoyed this one. I loved the musical aspect, as I'm into rap and I think the creativity of each song is fantastic. I also think the entire thing is paced very well. The screenplay is good as well; it doesn't do anything amazing, but it's snappy and its flow keeps you engaged. Most of the performances were solid, though some weren't able to fulfill every emotion. Also I'm a bit surprised this won a Sundance award for directing, as I think that's actually one of this film's weaker aspects. The cinematography is decent, but the editing is, well... not. There are also some clichés to the story that are a bit corny, and I don't quite understand the purpose of the film being B&W, but overall this felt rather genuine and I was entertained. It doesn't blow anything out of the water though I'd still recommend it. Might go up to a 7.

Name: It
Review:
I loved this film when I saw it for the first time at a sleepover a few years back, but I had also just finished middle school so I related to the kids lmao.

I haven't read the book but this wasn't really that scary lmao. My favorite part is definitely the cinematography, as it's actually a very well shot film. I understand they purposely wanted the film to look naturally lit, and for the most part that's respectable but there are a few scenes that are annoyingly dark. A solid screenplay and actually decent performances contribute to some interesting themes, but the editing isn't good and pacing felt off multiple times, tonally it's all over the place and I didn't remember every part, but the parts I didn't I was still able to predict fairly easily. I was also surprisingly unengaged for like half of it which I didn't expect lol. Some of the CGI and scares I was not able to take seriously. Not the best revisit but I mean there are worse horror films so it was fine.

Name: Borat: Cultural Learnings of America for Make Benefit Glorious Nation of Kazakhstan
Review: I always love showing my friends this film for their first time. As long as they not gypsies or from assholes Uzbekistan! Very nice!

Name: Fargo
Review: Sorry to say my original review still stands. I don't get this movie. That's all I'm gonna say. Fun to watch with friends for the first time though, but they also both weren't huge in it.

Name: Columbus
Review:
Many thanks to out to Sundance for offering this movie for free to virtual festival-goers.


Great stuff. Spectacular cinematography, a simple but effective story and an intelligent screenplay make this an excellent watch. There isn't really anything to criticize though it just doesn't quite feel like a 10/10 movie upon first watch, because it's quite a bit to take in but I'm going to put this on my top 10% list. The characters are beautiful and I'd be hard pressed to think of someone close to me in my life who wouldn't connect to them. My new favorite of 2017 and I'm not sure if anything else will beat it. Can't wait for After Yang later this year (presumably).

Name: Parasite
Review:
Watched the black & white version with the Criterion DVD. I love the aesthetic it provides, but I think there are a lot of colors that serve a purpose both visually and thematically so I would have to say I prefer the original.

Anyways, what more is there to say? Parasite is one of those films that has nuances to pick up on with each new watch. Certain elements don't get old, and any re-watch just feels the same in terms of quality. I still get the same impact out of this film and all the messages stick with me. So yeah, will probably always be one of the best pieces of cinema ever created. Another film I love more than myself!

EDIT: Forgot to mention that two days ago was the one year anniversary of my first time seeing this film, and the day that I will forever remember as the start of my bigger love for movies. So this is an important movie for me personally and I'll never forget that.
Tags: love this movie more than myself, criterion dvd

Name: American Graffiti
Review:
Very enjoyable and entertaining; I had a blast with this. I'm not sure if timeless is the right word to describe the characters, but with all their quirks and personalities, they still feel relatable and very well developed. Lucas wrote an excellent screenplay that's very funny and elevates the concept into a classic. It's very well directed and shot at that, with a lot of charm and several memorable scenes. In a sentence, this is an engaging Americana experience and it's sure to put a smile on your face.

Going into a bit of a backstory, I've been waiting to see this film for a decade. 2010 is the year I really got into Star Wars, and in turn my parents told me all about George Lucas and I learned of his other films. Of course, at that younger age, they didn't let me see American Graffiti and I understand why now, though it's always been in the back of my mind. On top of that my dad saw this in high school several times so it's cool to finally check this out with my parents while I'm at the same age!
Tags: dvd rental, w/ dad, w/ mom, library

Name: CODA
Review: Sundance 2021 Film Festival -- #1 There are many stories about characters who are special and/or different seeking more in life and hoping to write their own story. Coda is one of the best films with this premise in recent years, thanks to the creativity in utilizing deafness and sign language amplified by great directing, unique and entertaining characters, beautiful cinematography, and an excellent screenplay with an even better story. In all, it is very funny, emotionally effective and heartfelt, and has a superb flow. There are some clichés in the plot and the characters, including a rather cheesy ending that feels too cleanly resolved and kills the pace a bit, though I'm still sure it will end up being one of the festival's best. What a great way to start this year off.
Tags: sundance 2021, w/ dad, w/ mom, virtual cinema

Name: The Pink Cloud
Review: Sundance 2021 Film Festival -- #2 A very irritating watch. It's pretty well directed with some very good cinematography and a decent screenplay, but the characters are very inconsistent and trying to connect with them was an obstacle and the story is a complete mess. It starts off well enough even if a bit unengaging, but then jumps five years a little under halfway in, then commencing the annoying logic and plot holes. Plus, most of the film plays out as if the film followed the same timeline without the jump. It isn't very well paced or structured, and things just don't make a whole lot of sense. Early reviews have stated terrifying parallels to the lockdown early in 2020, but with the time jump and logical fallacies, the story really took me out of the vibe. Worse yet, I think the final minutes jump even further in time by years. In general, I wasn't hooked and a lot of the emotional impact felt kinda forced. So many questions are left unanswered, and I felt pretty much nothing at the end (which had no message and felt uneven and abrupt). I was intrigued by the premise and promo shots, and this is a directorial debut so I decided to buy a ticket. But for now all I can say is I hope the filmmaker's next film is better. Might bump this down to a 3 in the future.

Name: Strawberry Mansion
Review: Sundance 2021 Film Festival -- #3 "Sorry about the bird. Here's some Ẓ̶̈́͗̅̃ể̵̡͍z̶̥͊̑̄͛̚j̵̗̖̰̎f̵̧̛̗͕̱̗̒ë̸͍́͑̇̐͝z̴̻͆̏̌̎a̵̢̾ ̸̟̦̦̒̊͘̚m̶̹̞̽r̵̩̄̎c̸̡̺̥̬͛̋́̓̄o̶̥̦̭̬͔̾ÿ̵͍̯́̂̆̋e̵̛̫͌͂̐̆k̷̠̒́́̋̚h̸̜͇̻̎̕l̵̜̳̮͎̋̚͝ͅḇ̴̗̹̱͕̾͗͘i̷̩̲̼̻͌̅͝͝͝r̵̜͋̔j̷̹̀̅̆g̶͎͍̓͛ on the house." - the frog waiter With a range of energy and tonal shifts not unlike Hausu and Don't Hug Me, I'm Scared along with its sequels, plus a ton of creative ideas behind every aspect, I dug this film. It's so, so bizarre in everything it does, and most of it makes sense and has ends met, though the final minutes definitely lost both me and my parents. There is also just offbeat pacing through the entire film, many messages are tried to be conveyed and not everything feels purposeful. This might turn a lot of people off and it got a bit annoying at times. Also none of the VFX are necessarily that good; some of it just seemed laughable but other parts felt more purposeful, reminding me a lot of the two pieces of media I referenced in the first sentence. At the very least, I was never bored and I can certainly give this a recommendation. The surrealism and sense of how weird dreams are is captured so well and what more can you ask for out of this? Plus the score is just so cool. And as painful as my experience with The Pink Cloud was, the film being boring and actually making me start to fall asleep, being on the verge of doing so made watching this an even more indescribable trip. God, I'm going to have weird dreams tonight!

Name: Censor
Review: Sundance 2021 Film Festival -- #4 Some great moments and a solid concept, as well as some admittedly great performances, but the story and direction don't have a whole lot of focus and I wasn't super engaged. No part of it is particularly bad but it isn't an intriguing film all in all. I liked the ending though, it kinda reminded me of Audition. Really not sure what else to say; this is one of those films I was able to pay attention to only half the time and still get the same out of so I can't comment on much more.

Name: Prime Time
Review: Sundance 2021 Film Festival -- #5 The idea for the film has a fair amount of parallels with Dog Day Afternoon, though not as interesting or executed well. Admittedly, I'm thinking about a lot right now (both related and unrelated to movies) so I wasn't able to give my full attention to the film, but I got through most of it just fine. For a debut, it's actually very well directed, shot and edited, with a rather solid screenplay. The performances are very good as well, and it doesn't have any pacing issues though it had some troubles engaging me. Plus, the ending is a bit unsatisfying and, in turn, there isn't a strong message to get out of it. The plot is unpredictable and I think that a good amount of the story is still relevant today. The character study aspect is also rather solid (even if a bit inauthentic), though I think I was hoping to feel more of an impact overall. Rating might go up to an 8.

Name: Writing with Fire
Review: Sundance 2021 Film Festival -- #6 This massively exceeded my expectations. An amazingly well shot and directed documentary that is enlightening and paced brilliantly. It follows each character incredibly well and really puts things into perspective. India's society is very different to what we're all used to and this film captures it so brilliantly. It's emotional, has some moments of humor, and kept me consistently engaged. Not to mention it has a surprisingly great score when it plays as well! I really hope this gets the wide release it deserves, as I honestly found this to be fantastic. There's more I could say on this, but I'll keep it here for now.

Name: This Film Is Not Yet Rated
Review: God this really accomplishes what it sets out to do, which is to make the audience fucking hate the MPAA. For maximum anger, I'm going to spare you the details but do know that it's all full of lies and a truly fucked up system. While the information gathered is enough to call this a great movie, I can only rate the movie itself so high. But nonetheless I'm thankful this documentary exists because of how much was exposed (but at the same time a lot of it was exposed through deception), and I think a lot more people need to see it. I can't believe more action hasn't been taken to reform the way films are rated.

Name: All Light, Everywhere
Review: Sundance 2021 Film Festival -- #7 A very intriguing and informational documentary that asks big questions and engages consistently. I didn't take anything too huge away from it but I definitely learned a thing or two. It feels a bit repetitive at times as well, though the overall final product is indeed very good. I'm not really going into any more details since this is a good film to go in blind for.

Name: The Blazing World
Review: Sundance 2021 Film Festival -- #8 It seems like a lot of people hate this film judging by the early average rating, and I wouldn't call it good but it isn't necessarily bad either. There's some great cinematography, I think the concept is rather interesting and handled creatively, and I was pretty engaged at the start. It has a really good score as well, when it occurs which isn't too often. Otherwise, I can't praise much else. The performances were decent but nothing great, the screenplay felt very muddled and unoriginal, the CGI all looked bad, the scares were predictable, and it didn't really do anything amazing; it kinda just stayed the exact same tone-wise the entire film. Also the opening 10 minutes are laughable. The director clearly takes influence from other films, as I recognized some parallels with movies like Star Wars and Pan's Labyrinth, though she has a lot to learn about directing. Oh yeah, and the editing in this is REALLY bad. So overall, as I said, it isn't awful but don't go in expecting very much.

Name: Prisoners of the Ghostland
Review: Sundance 2021 Film Festival -- #9 Yeah I love this. Spitfire performances from everyone, breathtaking cinematography, imaginative storytelling and world-building backed up by a beautiful story with lots of brutal action and twists, insane pacing with consistently stressful energy, a sensational ending, and overall kickass directing by Sion Sono. Though not quite as good, I drew some comparisons to Oldboy as far as the action and character dynamics go. Plus this might just be the closest thing to it since it came out 17 years ago. Perhaps I've lost some objectivity due to the hype for this film, but I can't imagine I'll like it less upon rewatch. The only thing I have to criticize is there are a few moments that take a stab at humor butt don't quite stick the landing... even then the screenplay is pretty solid. The western-esque energy is captured so well and I loved almost every minute of this. It's batshit crazy; whenever this gets released, the world is in for a treat.

Name: Night of the Kings
Review: Sundance 2021 Film Festival -- #10 (last one :( ) While I didn't take as much out of the story itself, this was certainly more riveting than I was expecting. The directing, screenplay, and cinematography are all excellent, and the performances are really good as well. The production design is also pretty awesome at that rate, so overall it's extremely well made. I was almost always engaged, and each story being told was very intriguing. It does have a few rough patches where I just wasn't very interested at all, but those are made up for by most other scenes. Also not every character is very well developed, but the story is. I'm a little mad I bought a ticket to this since it's getting a US wide release on February 26th, but I'm glad I was able to check it out; a nice pleasant surprise is always a good way to end a film festival.

Name: No Country for Old Men
Review:
EDIT: This is an old review. I promise whenever I rewatch the film, the rating will be higher lmao (it already is, I’m just leaving this review up for posterity)


This was one of my most anticipated movies for the longest time. Between comparisons to There Will Be Blood, its MASSIVE reputation and, of course, intriguing plot and thematic ideas, I really couldn't imagine not loving this film.

When the film opened, I really did have high hopes. For the first kill, Javier Bardem has a truly unhinged look in his eye. Tommy Lee Jones' monologue is really well written as well, so between these two aspects and Deakins' sexy long shots, I was super psyched.

The cinematography throughout the rest of the film is still great, and the dialogue and performances are really good which is where this film gets its points. However, I struggled to really connect to any of the characters despite this and all my other issues with the film fall under that umbrella.

The rest of the deaths in this film, even though some came as a surprise, were not really effective for me on a personal level. Bardem does not give the same energy into the rest of the film as he did the opening, and he just kinda turned into another movie villain. While the deaths aren't glamourized or anything like that, the characters aren't super well developed to the point of feeling really depressed over them. On a similar note, for a movie that prides itself in being so badass, half the kills don't even take place on-screen. In turn the film felt surprisingly safe and the lack of showing those deaths doesn't have much of a point. The editing and structure overall just aren't very good, as a lot of transitions feel abrupt and uneven. The last 20 minutes hardly conclude the film at all and to be completely honest I felt ripped off. Not to mention I wasn't super engaged until the halfway point (though I must at least credit the film for feeling shorter than it actually is).

Perhaps a lot of the film's open-endedness can be explained by the themes of fate and circumstance, where every plot point is inevitable but it was executed in such an unconventional way that felt rushed and like a lot was left to be desired. It's a very realistic story, but that doesn't mean it works as a movie. With as many people as there are who praise the film for being complex and mind-blowing, I feel very let down because of taking nothing serious out of the final product.

I'd like to reiterate that if you love this film, I'm glad. Film is subjective and it seems like a lot of people forget that. However, I didn't care much about what was happening, and the hollow characters and story left a minimal impression on me. It's certainly a well made film, but it stops there.

I might bump the rating up to a 7 but based on this first impression I'm keeping it where it is. I'm a bit jaded on this due to the fact that I've been so hyped for the film as long as I've been a serious film lover, but my huge disappointment certainly plays into my overall opinion.

Name: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
Review:
I've seen this movie many times across my life, so finally checking it out on the big screen (in IMAX even) was pretty surreal and will certainly go down as one of the most unforgettable theater experiences I've ever had.

So yeah, I know this is an unpopular opinion but this movie is great!
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, imax

Name: What's Up: Balloon to the Rescue!
Review:
Move over Parasite. Get out of the way, Come and See. Adios The Godfather. Au revoir, Hara-kiri! There is now a new king of the movies. From the minds of Everton Rodrigues and Michelle Gabriel, not one, but TWO incredible filmmakers, comes this monumental masterpiece. This is no Gotti or Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets. This is no meme, this is factual information. The number one movie in history is the one and only What's Up: Balloon to the Rescue! This film is the definition of underrated, clouded by some very mediocre and painful "gems" like Braver, Freedom 4orce, Chop Kick Panda, and Tangled Up. Not only is this film more touching than Schindler's List or the legendary Synecdoche, New York, but the screenplay debatably beats out even the almighty Citizen Kane. Not only is this movie the best movie of the 2000s, it could possibly be the best movie ever made. Some cinephiles would argue and say 2001: A Space Odyssey or Tokyo Story, but to that, I say no. Those boring and repetitive film follies don't even come close to the beauty and grace of What's Up: Balloon to the Rescue! People say this film is overrated, and to those Pulp Fiction-sucking filmbro fucks, you have no idea what you're talking about. What's Up: Balloon to the Rescue! is by far the best movie in history, and dare I say the best piece of art in history. This film is absolutely revolutionary and can be put up in the ranks with films such as Breathless, The Gospel According to St. Matthew, and Man with a Movie Camera: some of the all-time greats. These animators really hit it out of the park with the beautiful character design, far better than that mediocre, embarrassing excuses for animated films like Spirited Away, It's Such a Beautiful Day, Toy Story, and WALL-E. I pray that Disney goes to this form someday, and it seems like with the recent incredible additions of Artemis Fowl and Mulan, I can only see the future is looking flawless for the incomparable company.


By the way, I have not seen What's Up: Balloon to the Rescue!

Name: The Florida Project
Review: A lot to love with this. Great directing, writing and performances back up the very real story told with precision and grace. It took me a bit of time to get into it, but once I did I was able to just sit back and enjoy myself. The colors are all so vibrant and Floridian which I love, and most of the cinematography is good, though I wasn't a huge fan of the digital-looking shots (however Disney is a fucking cunt and I understand it would've been far more expensive to shoot the finale with actual film). There isn't really anything to criticize, though I wasn't a fan of some of the scene transitions which made the pacing feel a bit uneven here and there (this also comes down to the editing). The message feels a bit flawed and the ending itself leaves me feeling rather conflicted, which is where I'm mainly divided between an 8 and a 9 but as a final product I think this film is excellent. It appeals to so many different people and uses the location very well, so I would highly recommend this to all my cinephile friends who haven't seen it yet.

Name: Viridiana
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 6 -- A film made in Spain Nooo, I definitely didn't forget to watch this 4 days ago for the weekend I was supposed to... (In my defense I have a new job and it's very time consuming, plus school so I'm not going to be watching as many films for the time being. I'll go more into my job soon since it involves movies) Ok so anyways this was a good film; the catholic religion sure is weird! Very well made from a filmmaking standpoint though every scene kinda feels the same as far as tone and style, so it isn't really one I see myself remembering that well months out (outside of the great ending). Solid characters but none that I were really emotionally attached to. There's also some solid commentary on various topics throughout which I appreciated. Check it out, even if it isn't mindblowing I guess. This is a quick review since I gotta get the fuck back to homework.

Name: Pi
Review: Okay, I'm not even kidding; this was awesome. Darren Aronofsky's style just works so incredibly well for me and that's even evident in his first feature film. While not as consistently captivating or emotionally effective as Requiem or Black Swan, this is still a very well paced film with strong directing, a great screenplay that's very intelligent and delivered with several confident performances, all of which delivered and tons of effort was brought into each character, both the actors and the way they are developed. I didn't strongly connect to any of them, but they were still very endearing to watch. As you might expect from Aronofsky, the plot is very intricate and a bit repetitive along the way but thoroughly interesting. I won't go into very much detail, as I went in knowing none of these and I want the same for people reading this review who haven't seen it yet. The music works well a lot of the time, and in the high energy sequences (which are executed to maximum effect thanks to the pace) it feels especially great. However, this isn't a perfect film and I must say that it feels like Aronofsky was still on the shallow end as far as directing the technical aspects such as the editing and specific camera movements, which is okay for a debut, and I also think a couple plot elements feel a little unrealistic (even considering how imaginative this film is) but these are both more nitpicks than actual criticisms. The only actual criticism I have is some of the religious themes, a number of which feel placed incorrectly and where they don't have much purpose with the rest of the story? As much as I love the film as a whole, it doesn't require as much religion as is present to be effective. Based on this, I wasn't very huge on the ending either as it doesn't quite conclude things in a natural manner, but somehow still felt satisfying which is incredibly weird. Above all, I seriously admire the dedication Aronofsky puts into his artistry, and I think right now he might be my favorite filmmaker. Not a single frame feels wasted, and the execution of the ideas and themes for the story is pretty brilliant... especially for a first project, despite some flaws. Also, can we just appreciate that this film was actually MADE? Read the Wikipedia page, the production barely survived. Something that's so interesting to me about this is, while there are certain films that are just so overwhelmingly good that they make you feel as if you'll never make something that good, this film is the opposite; it gives me hope that a film of this quality is possible from me With that, I cannot wait to check out the rest of his work.

Name: The Road to the Racetrack
Review: New Korean Cinema Park Chan-wook considers this film the first in the era of New Korean Cinema so I was curious to see if it really holds up. The film is pretty well directed and shot with solid acting and a competent screenplay, but the plot just repeats the same arguments with a random side character or sex every now and then. I got pretty bored as the film went on and didn't really connect to anything. Plus, the two things that classify New Korean Cinema are 1) excessive sex and violence, and 2) a study of the human condition. It was cool to see the characters interact with each other but it isn't a deep film by any means. At the very least, there were some solid moments of humor and places where the performances shine through that kept me interested through the ending (which is actually a very intriguing and visually stunning conclusion). Not a particularly great film, but an interesting recommendation from Park to say the least. If you're interested in checking this film out, here's where I watched it.

Name: Land
Review: Good stuff. Has great cinematography and overall really solid direction. Every performance was very good as well and I was engaged most of the time. However, the film also suffers from an average screenplay and mediocre structure / pacing that only feels natural half the time; the other half of the film feels rather abrupt. The story also has some obvious coincidences and some cheese here and there, but overall I at least enjoyed myself. Either a 6 or 7, not sure yet.

Name: Judas and the Black Messiah
Review:
As riveting and powerful as the story itself is, I found the movie to be good not great. It's objective and portrays the actual events in a respectful manner, but I wasn't really blown away by any certain aspect. Obviously Stanfield and Kaluuya are great and they portray their characters well, it's well written and overall looks fantastic. I really liked the cinematography and the production design honestly added a lot. It's very immersive as well, as in the way it defines the 70s location is excellent. Despite the positives, however, the plot isn't structured very well and I think what's actually shown doesn't always work. A lot of the longer dialogue-driven scenes felt the same and there was a drastic difference in quality between those and the high energy sections. Most of the story was established early on so the prolonged exposition kinda lost me. Along those lines, I also just did not like the way the film was edited. Many abrupt cuts that felt unnecessary were found in at least half the scenes, and this is more of a nitpick but still very worthy of note. The high energy scenes, however, were really excellent and easily serve as the highlights. Overall though, it's certainly a very well done movie and worth the price of admission. I just don't think the film itself does anything that really makes it stand out from films with similar ideas and themes.

Unrelated to the movie itself, I'd just like to say that there is a special place in hell for those who use their phone inside a theater with other people. Tommy texter didn't ruin the film but I was definitely taken out of a few scenes because of his light mode phone usage half the time. Don't pay for admission if you aren't going to actually watch the movie you goddamn bitch.
Tags: amc naperville 16, dolby, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Burn After Reading
Review: This could only have been made in 2008, and films that have that sort of nostalgic charm for a certain year are always great for me. And better yet, this is one of the Coens' best! Their charm and ability to blend genres added a lot to an already really good story; the directing is on point across pretty much every aspect, from the performances to Lubezki's excellent camerawork to bringing the script to life (the script being one of my favorite from them; most of the jokes land and *gasp* they don't rely on constant repetition more than once!). I was never bored, and the comedic take on Washington and the US government is very creative. I unironically connected to a lot of the characters, and overall it's incredibly well paced, well developed, and just really worked for me. Not a life changing movie, but a damn good time for sure.

Name: Minari
Review:
This film is nearly perfect, and seeing it in a theater was unforgettable. There are so many things I want to say, but my thoughts are still accumulating even after a second watch. I honestly struggled to keep it together at the end. For me, this is simply the best film of 2020 and could very well end up being one of the best of the decade nine years from now.

Another example of a movie I love more than myself!
Tags: cinemark seven bridges, love this movie more than myself, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Cries and Whispers
Review: Very good, though not quite what I was expecting. It's certainly very beautiful and there isn't a single bad performance, which can be explained by a rather good screenplay and Bergman's direction. The story itself is a personal one and I admire that, but I really only felt emotionally invested during two scenes, one towards the beginning and the other about halfway through. With that, this also wasn't the most consistently engaging movie ever. I didn't really connect with any other characters than Agnes and her mom, so overall I don't feel as much of an impact, but this is certainly a movie worth checking out and I admire the technicalities. I can't say it's one of Bergman's best though, and it certainly isn't up to the standard of Persona. Still, I'm very much looking forward to The Seventh Seal, Wild Strawberries, Fanny and Alexander, and the rest of his work.

Name: The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers
Review: This was my 500th review ! ! ! Another cool experience on the big screen, but also this is definitely the weakest of the trilogy. I'm pretty sure my vision got worse over the course of the three hours thanks to the awful editing; oh my god, between the constant cuts (mainly during the battles) and the camera moving way too much given how short most of the shots were, I actually have a headache after it all. Sure there are some nice long shots, but they're few and far between. I can also tell this one relied a lot more on CGI than Fellowship since a lot more of it looked outdated, but at least the makeup, costumes and art direction still hold up of course. The other aspect I have to criticize actually has to do with the narrative; in Fellowship, there are several instances where you truly feel for the characters and root for them, but The Two Towers feels way more story driven, so in turn I didn't care quite as much. Don't get me wrong; this is still a good movie, and Peter Jackson was the best person to bring the world to life. The battle scenes fundamentally are excellent, and the fact that I can say that despite the sloppy camerawork says a lot. Plus, a lot of the film outside of that still holds up today, but I was bothered by a lot more than I remembered.
Tags: amc naperville 16, bad editing, w/ dad, w/ mom, imax

Name: A Clockwork Orange
Review:
The decision to watch this was really impulsive but I found myself looking at it on Netflix, and before I knew it the movie was starting and I was already locked in.

So the first time I saw this, which already was 9 months ago, I was still rather new to my film obsession. I really hadn't seen a film that tackles the themes of A Clockwork Orange, let alone in the fashion of Kubrick’s style. So I don't blame myself at all for being completely floored by it and calling it a new favorite.

Now I've seen far more films since then, and many that hold the same or similar thematic and emotional weights. I'll get to that in a second, but first off I'd like to reiterate that this film is fucking fantastic from a technical standpoint. I mean every aspect to the visuals is so on point, especially considering this was shot 50 years ago. I fell in love with the cinematography in particular really fast; the fish eye lens and Alex's perspective shots really did it for me. And the colors and production design? This is so insanely immersive; a filmmaking anomaly.

Onto the deeper stuff... I was really hooked in the first hour. It was all the shock, pain and wretchedness that I remembered but in glorious fashion that does all it needs to, hitting all the right punches. The story is so ambitious in the way it tackles themes and ideas of prison, crime and punishment, police brutality, and government control as a whole. While the statement it makes as an entire product is certainly a good one, the second hour from the experiment to the end didn't live up the same way. By that I don't mean under the umbrellas of knowing what's coming or things no longer making sense. My issue is the message the story is trying to convey to the audience gets very blurred by all that occurs. Admittedly, in the time since the movie finished, this feels like less of an issue, though at the time I was not satisfied by how Alex dealing with his new world paid off compared to everything preceding it. The ending in particular; when he wakes up at the hospital and everything is okay, it just felt rushed. Not to mention the entire final chapter of the novel was left out. I just wasn't satisfied by how the ends were met.

Don't get me wrong; at the end of the day, this is still a truly excellent movie, way ahead of its time and nearly flawlessly made. The thematics just haven't held up quite as well, and I wasn't blown away the same way. I really do love this film despite all of this, and today certainly won't be the last time I watch it. Though maybe I'll actually read the novel before my third viewing.

Name: Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 7 -- A film from Wes Anderson's Top 10 Wow. Pretty amazing as a whole thing, with excellent filmmaking and technical aspects.
