The scenes of Mishima himself reflecting on what inspired him to write each story and how they applied to his life were pretty emotional and I felt very impacted by those, though the novels themselves didn't move me quite as much and I think I was expecting more of a physical and emotional reaction out of the ending, as great as it was. I'll go more in detail in a couple days, since I realized while watching that this is the Filmstagram Movie Club pick for this week, so I'm gonna give it a full Instagram review.

Name: Akira
Review: Not as transcendent as it was in theaters, but still fucking amazing and one of my all time favorites. It's an absolute marvel in animation and I really don't find the story confusing, even on the first watch I didn't. Everything works so perfectly for me and I adore the hell out of it.
Tags: dvd, w/ farris, w/ shrey, w/ arnav

Name: Buffalo '66
Review: This is a bizarre film but not in the way you might expect. Vincent Gallo has such a distinct style, and while it may not seem that way on the surface level as it has a lot of traits of directors we talk about today, this came out in 1998 and probably developed in the few years beforehand, so it isn't really that derivative at all. It's very well written, shot and acted, and while I wasn't always on board with the stakes or story development, I found the entire movie really endearing and definitely didn't expect to get as into it as I did. It wasn't always my thing, and there were some stylistic choices that just didn't work for me, but overall it's very ambitious and I do see why people might really love it. I have it at an 8 right now but it really does seem like one of those films that grows on me with each rewatch, so the possibility for the rating to raise is certainly there.

Name: Possessor
Review: Honestly very disappointed this wasn't better. I love the concept and the visual aspects are really great but the screenplay is very average and all the characters are pretty douchey. It acts like it's way more effective and complex than it actually is, and I wasn't amazed by any aspects in particular. I'm a fan of over-the-top action that can even border on being a bit much, which is why I thoroughly enjoy films like Hardcore Henry, the Kingsman series, Kill Bill, Birds of Prey, and of course Oldboy. With those films, it feels warranted enough based on the circumstances of the story, or the director is just having fun and is able to recognize it's absurdity. But the inane violence in this just felt unspecial and it took itself way too seriously for me to appreciate. It's not something I'll re-watch anytime soon if ever.

Name: Werckmeister Harmonies
Review:
Bela Tarr's excellent directing which is incredibly persistent and attentive to details is prevalent in this feature, which is certainly very good but not Satantango. The entire film is, of course, shot and edited to near perfection and the production design is really great as well. The film relies a lot more on the screenplay than Satantango, however, and I think this is both a blessing and a curse. The exposition comes at a quicker pace, and that's okay since this isn't as meditative of a film, however it isn't one of the best screenplays ever either. The first half starts off very strong for the first 20 minutes or so, but then very little happens that hasn't already occurred until the second half starts when the circus really starts to heat up. Then the second half increases in intensity by a bit, but it isn't anything stunning either. There are some fantastic shots, but I think I was expecting to be a little more surprised. The characters are all interesting but their development feels lacking, and the way they play into the story isn't quite as symbolic as I thought it might (this is more of a nitpick since I'm sure it was intended). So yeah, overall a very good movie though I don't quite agree with it's placement on the Letterboxd top 250 nor do I think it's a masterpiece.


I also do think some of my frustration for this film comes with the precursor that a lot of my friends lately have criticized me for not really giving any new movies a 10/10 rating in a while, and based on the reputation and my love for Satantango I was kinda expecting this to break that cycle but it simply doesn't. I really hope I check out a film worthy of a 10/10 rating in my book sometime in the future, because I'd love to add another film to the favorites list.

Name: Nomadland
Review: Pretty depressing followup to Fargo (1996), but at least Marge Gunderson becomes a very personal character, has much better development and ditches the accent! In all seriousness I loved seeing this in theaters. Even just beyond being incredibly beautiful and touching, the surround sound really came in clutch; I genuinely turned around multiple times since I thought I was hearing things in the theater. Instagram post about this coming soon, probably tomorrow night since I have school and work.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
Review: I was going to make a joke for this review about how the entire journey would've gone by a lot faster if they used cars instead, but I'm still kinda stunned even after the long car ride home. I forgot how tremendous of an experience this movie is; it's one that reminds you why you love cinema, I'm so glad I saw this in theaters. It's an experience that I'll probably keep with me forever and ranks among my very favorite theater trips. The film itself is a 9 which is where I have it for this review but the experience itself was certainly a 10. I might bump it back up real soon.
Tags: amc dine-in yorktown 18, w/ dad, w/ mom, imax

Name: Sincere Heart
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 8 -- A romance film A cute film that isn't spectacular or anything, but the fact that this is an early work from Masaki Kobayashi gives it some merit. It's rather well shot and has some solid performances, though the story is hard to follow and I was never enthralled with anything. Even being foreign and having subtitles, I was still able to put it on in the background, do other things and still get the same level of enjoyment out of it. In that sense, I wasn't too engaged either, and I think this barely qualifies as a part of the romance genre. There were some entertaining sequences, though I really wouldn't recommend it outside of a Kobayashi binge. I'm already fairly behind on the challenge and I apologize for that (this past weekend was week 9), though I have a new job at a nearby movie theater / dine-in as a cook, so that's taken up most of my free time on the weekends. Tom and Jerry kept me very busy the past few days since my manager is still in the process of hiring more cooks, and this weekend is going to be hell with Raya and the Last Dragon plus Chaos Walking. So I just wanted to explain why the weekends have me crunched on time, and that's why I'm not as active on Letterboxd as I once was. I still try to watch movies whenever I can, though it won't always happen.

Name: Fantastic Mr. Fox
Review:
I'm very depressed right now and I think I'm getting physically sick but at least I have this film. Maybe the most watchable movie ever?


I really need to get this on criterion

Name: The Fountain
Review:
Aronofsky's ambition and admittedly great ideas for the film are bogged down by a surprisingly convoluted and frustrating sequence of events and structure. While the movie is visually pleasing in every aspect, the screenplay is nothing really to shout at and the main characters, in all three time periods, are difficult to connect to. There isn't a single bad performance and Aronofsky's good direction is apparent, but it's contributing towards a story that's all bark and no bite. The plot itself doesn't really start escalating until the halfway point, and up to then the movie just isn't intuitive or very engaging. The jumps between time periods make little sense and I think if the conflict of Hugh Jackman's dying wife was established earlier on, then I would've found a much deeper appreciation for what was presented. But even then there is very little correlation between each time period established, if any. The final half hour was kinda interesting and sorta rounds things out well, but it's also rather cheesy and ineffective. There are a lot of motifs and clearly there's a great film hiding underneath, but despite my Aronofsky love, I wish the writing was in the hands of someone like Charlie Kaufman instead.

The Fountain as a project went through some serious development detractors in the years of its conception, so as always I admire Aronofsky for his ambition. But it seems to me like he got so caught up in writing such a complex and deep thematic story that the narrative itself suffered. Might go down to a 5, but it's held up by the performances (mainly from Burstyn and Jackman), visuals and imagination. Ending was pretty interesting as well.


*random but a follow-up to yesterday's FMF review, I'm feeling better physically though not quite 100%. Hopefully I'll get there in the coming days.

Name: The Pianist
Review:
Despite my alleviated expectations for Polanski's other work after being disappointed with Rosemary's Baby, I'm happy to report I had nothing to worry about here. The Pianist is a stunning and incredible film. Rather than being a film about the holocaust itself, it follows the story of Wladyslaw Szpilman and how he survived WW2. Adrien Brody is simply brilliant, and his development and progression as a character is amazing and touching. Whether it was through his emotions or even when he played the piano, you feel a really strong connection to him and all he has stood up to. It's a visual feast, featuring great cinematography and even better production design. Polanski's direction is outstanding and there isn't a single auditory or visual piece that I would criticize. The screenplay is excellent as well, both in developing everything in a natural and comfortable fashion alongside some really touching dialogue. The pacing felt a bit weird at times, as the first hour has a brisk pace and the events come by very quickly, but the remainder of the film feels much slower. The transition was noticeable, and there are sections around that transition that I wish felt longer (such as when he was separated from his family, I think they deserved more of the runtime for us to really fixate on the emotional value of the situation), but nonetheless I have no other complaints.  Even as I'm typing this while listening to the piano music of the end credits, my heart is beating really fast and I'm shaking a bit. Despite the minor criticisms, my head is still spinning and that's the sign of a masterpiece for me. Polanski has created an awe-inspiring, heart-stopping and very sad piece of film that is surely going to stick with me for a while...

I need a hug now.


EDIT: I just finished the film a half hour ago but I just found a really weird sense of comfort out of it. The movie really did take me out of everything going on in my life right now and I was able to stay enthralled with Wladyslaw. So in a strange way, The Pianist has functioned as both a masterful film AND an escape that reminds me why I love watching such powerful stories.

Name: Rango
Review: Dear modern animation companies sans Don Hertzfeldt, Please study this film. It's better than you, try and take after it. Thanks. Watched the director's cut, which was only a slightly changed ending but I enjoyed it slightly more than the theatrical cut with that.

Name: Chaos Walking
Review: It was just okay, but still exceeded my expectations! I don't think it deserves the absurd hate it's received so far.

Name: Lies
Review: New Korean Cinema "Think of my dick as shit. That will make it easier." I watched this for one reason, and that's because it was featured on the New Korean Cinema video from the Parasite Criterion. Plus it has the same director as The Road to the Racetrack, which was dubbed the birth of the Korean Cinema movement by Park Chan-wook. I was hoping for more of a story since that's what the other Letterboxd reviews tell me, but this is literally just half porn... I fucking hate myself for wasting $2 on this. I could've bought a McDonalds burger with that and I wish I did; woulda been a better use of my time and money. (I actually really like McDonalds though so no disrespect to the company or what they make, I'm just being facetious) I fast forwarded through the rest of the film, and what I got is that if you've seen the first 25 minutes, you've seen the rest. Parts of it are interesting as far as character development, but even then the two leads are very static and forgettable. There's structure to the story yet I was still either bored by the exposition. It's competently shot and directed I guess? Bleh.

Name: Spirited Away
Review:
Let's see here uhhhhh
- Amazing story that stands for childhood wonder, imagination, growing up, and probably more 
- Hand... drawn... animation
- Absolutely beautiful score
- I had a big smile on my face half the time
- The creativity of every character that came from Miyazaki's mind alone is stunning, that's goals
- It still leaves enough up to interpretation that you can theorize about what a lot of it means, I have a few ideas in mind with Haku in particular
- Many genuine gags that made me laugh
- I was never bored obv.
- So much more that I could say but I'm still kinda letting the film wash over me


So uhhh yeah. I know this is an unpopular opinion, but what a great movie!
Tags: hbo max (courtesy of friends), at a friend's place, w/ farris, w/ arnav, w/ shrey

Name: Chinatown
Review:
Great stuff! Honestly I think I was most blown away by the technical / filmmaking aspects. The production design is fantastic, cinematography and editing terrific, all the green screens are actually really effective and better than most today, and the sound is honestly some of the best in ANY movie. That's all perfect and Polanski did an excellent job directing, what more is there to say?

The performances were all very good, especially Jack Nicholson who really disappears into the role. The screenplay is a great one of course, deserving of its status; I love the tone and flow it has all the way through. There aren't very many memorable quotes that come to mind now that I've finished it, although the ending monologue is rather excellent. Overall, I think the first hour and 15 minutes of it are fantastic and at that rate I actually thought it might end in my top 30, but after that it takes a bit of a dip for about bit before really coming in strong the last half hour. The story is really compelling and I love the way it uses the city of Los Angeles (especially from the time period it's set in), and there wasn't a single moment that I was unengaged. Not to mention an ending that doesn't quite round things out the way I had hoped but was still shocking in its own right and nearly impossible to see coming.

The dips in the pace and scenes where it briefly lost me are my main criticisms, but don't get me wrong. This is an outstanding piece of filmmaking, thoroughly enjoyable, intelligent, and ahead of its time.

Name: The White Tiger
Review: More awards season catchup. This had some really good scenes that were well directed, acted and written. I was really feeling them. But unfortunately, the entire first 45 minutes are messy and forgettable and overall not much of the story or many characters are THAT well developed. Some really good ideas are tackled but nothing is really super thought-provoking during the 2 hour runtime, so I can't say the runtime was warranted; at that rate there wasn't a clear focus very often and I didn't love the pacing. I don't even know if there are any aspects I would call bad but it's mostly forgettable. As far as the two pieces of the film that are getting a lot of attention, the screenplay really wasn't that good and the lead actor did a solid job but never was amazing. Nonetheless it's still an interesting look at the caste system in modern society but uhhh I don't know what else to say. Yeah, that's the review. I know there are a lot of aspects I didn't touch on here that I usually do, but if I didn't then the unmentioned aspects are whatever. Maybe this was the point of the movie but watching it just killed my mood and I uhhh kinda feel like shit now (for both the mediocrity of the movie and other things). Might go up to a 6 but idk. (the review is intentionally messy and doesn't have a structure. If you thought it was annoying you probably won't like the film very much)

Name: The Father
Review: Stupendous performances all across the board, great cinematography, a solid screenplay and a terrifyingly realistic story structure make this a great film. And it's just growing on me the longer I think about it; it's surprisingly intuitive and many theories could be made about the actual continuity and time of the plot. And while I fully expected this, there's a strong emotional impact to be felt. Oscar nominations are tomorrow and I hope this gets the attention it deserves. I'm so glad I saw it in a theater.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Beach Bum
Review: Idk how to review this, I mean... Matthew McConaughey was great (man really has an impressive range) but not every supporting actor/actress was. The entire film has ADHD as far as pacing, structure, editing, camerawork and overall directing in the sense that nothing is conventional in the slightest though that's to be expected from Harmony Korine ig lol. Some of those aspects are really good and do work but others just feel like lazy craft, even given the film's style. The film is frustrating and has no direction at all but maybe that's the point given the characters, though at the same time I feel like excusing the weak aspects of the film as "all part of the style" can only go so far. I also didn't like the ending very much though from what I've seen not too many people do. And yeah this review has about as much structure as the movie, idk what else to say.

Name: Burrow
Review: 93rd Oscars, Animated Short Film #1 Kinda reminded me of a simpler, less quirky version of Fantastic Mr. Fox. Cute and entertaining though, I liked it.

Name: If Anything Happens I Love You
Review: 93rd Oscars, Animated Short Film #2 Very beautiful, very symbolic, and very emotional. The story pans out in such a crisp and perfect way; I still have three more to see but I can already say I really hope this wins. Even if you don't care about the short films, please check this out.

Name: Caché
Review: My first film from Michael Haneke was, well, solid. I add the 'well' into that sentence because it was rather underwhelming. It's a very well put together film, from both a filmmaking standpoint and the story structure / pacing. Tonally it was pretty consistent as well, with some good performances across the board. However, given how many people praise this film for being intricate and thought-provoking, I'm wondering what I might've missed. I've seen the ideas tackled in this film portrayed in much more riveting fashion before, and I wasn't very consistently engaged. There's some great camerawork (not a single shot looks bad and you can really tell Haneke knows what he's doing), and a decent number of scenes do get you thinking and theorizing about hidden meanings. But in the end, I just don't find very much about this film memorable, with characters that I didn't find too well developed or easy to connect to (I can't even remember their names). As a whole, I got very strong Fincher vibes from watching this, mainly from The Game and Gone Girl, though it's much closer in quality to the former of those two. For the ambition behind the film and great filmmaking, the film receives points, as well as some terrifically executed scenes sprinkled in, though I won't be thinking about this film months out from now and I don't see myself giving it a second try anytime soon. Hopefully Haneke's films get better from here.

Name: Battleship Potemkin
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 9 -- A silent film I mean I'd love to make jokes right now but this was a really special experience, watching such a hailed classic for the first time. A great film too, even by today's standards. Eisenstein's directing is excellent, not a single bad performance, a fantastic score, surprisingly intense scenes that keep you on the edge of your seat, and it really got me into the story and characters. The story is timeless and I really do see why so many critics and directors hold it in such high regard. Maybe this is a hot take but this is my new favorite silent film (over The Passion of Joan of Arc). I'm still ruminating over it which I didn't expect, this is a special film that everyone who's got even a passing interest in cinema should check it out.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2021, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Joint Security Area
Review: New Korean Cinema Some really great moments and overall good directing, this is a movie that I thoroughly enjoyed but wasn't really amazed by any particular aspect. The cinematography is good, the editing is ok but a bit annoying at times, pretty well paced and very well acted. The story is a very interesting one and the plot twists are executed in a very crisp manner. It's a relevant subject matter that's handled in a very down to earth way that I think was pulled off excellently. And how 'bout that ending ??? I didn't agree with every story decision, and I just wasn't too engaged at certain parts, but I'd recommend this nonetheless.

Name: Justice League
Review: This was hilarious.
Tags: hbo max, w/ farris, w/ shrey, w/ arnav

Name: Zack Snyder's Justice League
Review: "So that's it? What, we some kind of Zack Snyder's Justice League" 4 hours long and so much random bullshit you could call this Jimmy Kimmel hosts the Oscars! Except this was actually funny. Okay in all seriousness, I guess it was an improvement but not by much. Some parts of it were better developed (Cyborg was FAR better in particular) but a lot of the added footage was just so useless. Half the characters didn't need to be here. There is an insane amount of CGI and it was obvious every other scene, which is a LOT since there are so many cuts and mediocre editing. It didn't feel like 4 hours which is good but partially because my friends and I were laughing half the time. So it was a fun experience but that was because of how this movie is still just not good. Some decent visuals and solid battles, but you can only get so far with this. Epilogue was good though, and reminded me how I love the tone of this universe and how I wish it went to better directors. I fucking hate the MPAA as evidenced by This Film is Not Yet Rated but its also worth noting that the R rating is dumb. PG-13 as a whole has felt pointless after the entire MCU but there was nothing that excessive in this movie. I was hoping for some more impressive, brutal and bloody action but it's just not here. Or at the very least they could've shown Aquaman's dick! (Not like his character serves any better purpose in this movie)
Tags: hbo max, at a friend's place, w/ farris, w/ rami, w/ shrey, w/ arnav

Name: We Need to Talk About Kevin
Review:
I honestly loved this. The first half hour is really bad as far as how it's edited and structured; it felt like a completely different director from everything that follows. But once the plot follows a slightly more linear structure, things get so good. As far as filmmaking goes, not every shot is a good one but the camerawork is still pretty great for the most part. I don't even really need to comment on how great the performances are; Ramsey knows her shit with actors. Pretty much every emotional beat hit and all the shocks really did come to me. It's a brilliant character study that manages to accomplish all it needs to for both Kevin and Eva alike, and I seriously must praise it for that. Now that it's all over, I kinda felt like it was tackling similar ideas to No Country for Old Men as far as pure human violence and rage (from an entirely different perspective, however) but this does it soooo much better for me personally. I think there are certain elements that I wish were on camera and the message would've stayed intact, but I trust Ramsey enough to know that everything we saw was intentional. Certain scenes are a bit unsatisfying in their payoff as well, but once again; now that the movie is over, I'm not thinking about them. The entire product just feels so focused and rightfully intense, to the point where I'm very tempted to give it an even higher score. I'm going to be thinking about it for a while, and I feel like I just watched it at the right time. I could go on and on, but all I'll say is that I'll be watching it again (as much of an emotional struggle as that will be) and writing a more fleshed out analysis in the (near?) future. Aside from the first half hour, it's brilliant.


Unrelated to my review, but it's a chilling coincidence that this movie was filmed in Connecticut and released in 2011. I'll let you connect the dots.

Name: Chungking Express
Review: Not gonna give this a rating because I really did love the movie but I'm tired as fuck and just not in the best mindset from personal life shit that's been going on today. I'm really sorry that this is such a disappointing log since so many of you love this film but I just can't give any good thoughts right now. I wish I didn't watch a movie like this with so much hype and praise around it because it deserves more than what I'm currently writing.
Tags: criterion channel, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Wrestler
Review: Oh hell yes... this was fucking stupendous. It doesn't have as many Aronofsky stylistic traits as Requiem, Black Swan or even The Fountain, but he was called on as director and not the screenwriter (the first film of his where he didn't write) so I think the traits he was able to incorporate were done to the best possible extent. Everyone, from Rourke to Tomei to Evan Rachel Wood, did a fucking amazing job. Seriously some of the best performances I've ever seen; I got Birdman-level chills. The Ram's character arc is both intricate and satisfying through and through; it's the story of a product from a past decade finding himself years out from his glory days, and this detail is explored in several different ways. The music is always used appropriately, much of it being from the 80s when The Ram was hot new shit, and there's even some music from the 00s when the tone calls for it. The screenplay is a really great one, being snappy and hitting every emotional beat possible. It's nearly perfect technically, with terrific editing and very effective camerawork. The visceral cinematography adds a ton to the already brilliant production design. At that rate, the worldbuilding is excellent; it explores pro-wrestling and the typical lifestyle in brilliant fashion. I don't understand the craze for pro-wrestling and I probably never will, but I seriously connected to Randy and his struggle. Aronofsky directed as well as I would expect him to, and if it wasn't already evident I fell in love with this. It's clearly a passion project across every aspect, and it doesn't hold back; several moments had me stunned for minutes on end. And not to mention an ending that rivals Aronofsky's masterpieces that rounds things out just as well. I feel personally affected by watching this film, and as with Black Swan and Requiem for a Dream this felt like a truly special experience. I'm very tempted to bump it up to a 10, and who knows? It could very well crack that boundary on rewatch. Aronofsky seriously is one of my all time favorite filmmakers, and despite the disappointment that was The Fountain (mainly due to the studio interfering with his budget and production, however, so I don't entirely blame him), this was a revitalization of my love for his work and the artistic influence his films have had on me. Even if you don't LOVE Aronofsky, I would highly, highly recommend this masterful film because it's a character study for the ages.

Name: The United States vs. Billie Holiday
Review: 2020 and the early 2021 awards season have been full of great biopics, but this isn't one of them.

Name: Another Round
Review:
A truly awesome film. I forgot how wild of an emotional roller coaster the story actually is; one of the absolute best of the year without a doubt and you honestly feel like a changed person once it's all over. Every end is met with glorious satisfaction and the development of Martin and his friend group stands for a lot more than I remember it being on first watch.

I'm so impressed in the Academy for giving Thomas Vinterberg a directing nomination, but it's incredibly well deserved and that means it's going to get more mainstream attention. If there's anything this year has hopefully taught them, it's that a lot of the best films of the year (both this and the past) are from filmmakers who don't make millions of dollars at the box office. Let's hope they stay mostly consistent this year even though circumstances will be more normal.

Name: Stop Making Sense
Review: Not giving a star rating because this is a concert film, but what a great time this was. David Byrne and the rest of Talking Heads have such a distinct sound to them and this concert was a lot of fun to see unfold. Demme's filmmaking adds a lot as well, there isn't a single bad shot in this. Apparently this was also the first documentary to ever use digital sound? It's just... such a cool movie on all fronts.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Nobody
Review:
As a big fan of Hardcore Henry, I was really looking forward to the director's follow up feature. I'm always a sucker for over the top action and when it's incorporated into the movie well, it can add a lot to the experience for me. There are a number of hints of the Naishuller's creativity in his craft and how he elevates the action to be more entertaining, but he's working with a rather mediocre script that changes tones so often and has a uneven pace mostly because of this. I like the idea of a different main character, one who is more of a regular dad, but he was never written with a consistent goal in mind. Hardcore Henry doesn't have a world-shattering script either but it fit perfectly with what the movie was going for, so I wish Naishuller was able to write again for this. At the very least, the plot itself moves pretty well and the ridiculousness feels purposeful with mostly satisfying payoff, though it feels a bit too open ended now that I've had some time to sit on it. The editing is really... not good and I could actually tell that I would've liked longer shots because of how each stunt was choreographed, and even the close ups for facial expression felt underwhelming. The cinematographer is the same as Hereditary and Midsommar (with much stronger vibes of the former) and I could tell he was trying his best, but after all the great one shots in Hardvore Henry I'm surprised Naishuller didn't go down that path again.

Overall, I at least had a relatively good time and I'm VERY glad I finally saw something like this in theaters again. I'm doing a lot of criticizing but I feel like I got my money's worth. Though the movie itself leaves some to be desired.


Technical note: I saw this in a Dolby theater and the film doesn't feel like it was made for that compatability. The entire time, the movie looked relatively grainy and the sound felt muffled at a handful of points. I don't blame anyone involved in the filmmaking process, and I understand AMC wants a new movie to go to Dolby, though the movie as it is didn't meet the Dolby standards and it'll only be there for a week until Godzilla vs. Kong comes out.
Tags: amc naperville 16, dolby, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Häxan
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 10 -- A film from the 1920s Super inventive and creative for its time and by today's standards, this is one of the most unique films I've ever seen. The way it tells the quirky story with horror and teaches you the history of witchcraft with a documentary style is a combo of genres like I've never seen before. There were a few times where it felt really slow and unengaging, as well as some cheesy dialogue, but the dialogue is easy to pass by since this movie actually has a pretty strong comedy aspect to it. There are a few times where it looks purposely cartoonish and even pokes fun at religions who have historically opposed witches. Even beyond the successful aspects of each genre it's going for, the physical technical aspects like the production design were excellent, every costume and creature design looked very realistic, all the performances were really solid... it doesn't have the best pacing and I didn't care a WHOLE lot about the characters, but overall I had a great time with this. A super entertaining and a very out of the ordinary watch that wasn't what I was expecting. It's a movie that I will definitely remember a lot of in the future and I think that's a factor that I take for granted. Rating may go up to a 9.

Name: Moonrise Kingdom
Review: Good but my least favorite Wes movie so far. It has a lot of his usual stylistic traits but they aren't as effective as how they are in his other work. I love the story and world-building in this a lot; maybe the best I've seen from him so far. Suzy was really well developed and easy to connect to, but the same can't really be said for the rest of the characters except maybe Bruce Willis and Sam. None of the performances really did anything great and some of the child performances were frankly not good. This especially got to me knowing that this is a passion project of Wes and I really wish that at least Sam was better written than what he ended up with. The cinematography is made up of a good number of GREAT shots on all fronts, but there are also a handful of digital shots that are sometimes good and sometimes bad. Certain of these digital shots actually keep the style and flow consistent but the rest just feel really out of place. The editing bugged me at certain points as well, with a high amount of shots cut off way too fast, annoying from both a technical standpoint and making the pacing feel uneven, though the irritating shot lengths could also go down to Anderson's directing. It's a well directed movie overall, don't get me wrong, but it suffers in the areas mentioned above. The plot itself, pacing aside, is unpredictable and interesting enough to keep me thoroughly engaged, and there are some really entertaining and emotionally involved moments, though I wish the rest of the movie followed in those scenes' footsteps. I enjoyed my time with this movie and overall I'm glad I checked it out, but I'm walking away feeling rather underwhelmed.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: It Comes at Night
Review: Certain story and thematic elements can hit different for a lot of people, where one movie might completely stun one person and it'll leave another feeling underwhelmed. It Comes at Night is chock full of moments that had my heart pounding and my ass on the edge of my seat; this is truly one of the most anxiety-inducing and emotional films I've ever watched. There are a few lines of dialogue that felt clunky, and I wish the movie ended better, but that's all I can say against this incredible piece of filmmaking. It's paced perfectly, there isn't a single shot that doesn't look great, all the performances were really fantastic, Trey Edward Shults did an amazing job directing and writing, and the plot is unpredictable to the best degree. Normally I would criticize limited story and character development, but there's a purpose behind each and I didn't feel negatively about this at all; I still found an incredibly strong connection to Travis, Will and Kim in particular. Everything just feels so real and I'm going to have a lot of scenes from the movie in my head for a long time. Unrelated to everything I've said so far, I'm glad I didn't watch the trailer beforehand since I heard the movie was very mismarketed. I was mesmerized from start to finish and I seriously just don't know what to say. It Comes at Night is breathtaking, plain and simple.

Name: Judas and the Black Messiah
Review: Best Picture Showcase #1 "Looks like he's gonna make it." My group of closest friends and I agreed to watch every BP nominee before the Oscars and we decided to start here! I have lots to say but the bottom line to begin is I enjoyed the film immensely more this time, probably in part due to no morons on their phone half the time. - Every Oscar nominee is deserved. Kaluuya needs that fucking statue! - I didn't realize how good the cinematography was the first time. Sean Bobbitt uses close-ups, medium close-ups and master shots incredibly well and every angle feels so purposeful. I'm glad it got attention. - I complained about the long dialogue scenes being boring the first time and I've come around on that. The story development is so good and each character is written with precision. - Still not a huge fan of the story's structure and editing, there are a number of awkward transitions. - The "I Am... a Revolutionary" scene gave me consistent chills, somehow even more than the first time in Dolby. - Excellent technical aspects across the board, and I'm excited to see what Shaka King does next (he's clearly great with performers and bringing the writing to life) though I still do admittedly hope he finds more of a distinct voice as a filmmaker.

Name: Fight Club
Review: I used to not understand this film at all, but you all talked me into it and I gave it a second try. It's my favorite movie now.

Name: Ikiru
Review: "This day will never return." What a way to start off April. (the Fight Club review was my April Fools joke) This was fantastic and basically a masterpiece. It's technically great, amazingly well directed and has some seriously incredible dialogue. For the first 1:30-1:40 of it, I was mesmerized and seriously emotionally connected in all the best ways. Takashi Shimura did a brilliant job and I completely believed his character, and this leads me into my only complaint and only setback from a guaranteed perfect rating. After like the hour 35 minute mark, right as things really started to improve for Watanabe and his remaining time, the story takes a really jarring turn and it messed with the tone and pace a lot. Looking back, I don't mind that transition too much but the focus shifts towards a bunch of other guys that worked at city hall with him. Watanabe becomes more of an after thought for a bit and the film's emotional grip on me was lost for a bit. Thankfully the third act gets WAY better as it goes on, and the thoughtful dialogue and shared anecdotes really brought me back in. It culminates with the famous swing scene (as shown on the poster) accompanied by the "Life is Brief" song. The movie doesn't end here and I wish it did, but now that I've had time to sit on the movie I'm starting to see this less as an actual criticism since the final scene, which is a heated argument with the city hall men, represents the mark Watanabe left on everyone and I love that. I'm going back and forth on the rating but for now I think the purpose it serves for how I've been feeling mentally recently gives it the edge. Let me expand on that a bit more. One of my biggest anxieties in life is trying not to waste any time when I can. As I've grown older, the days go by faster and I can really fixate over how fleeing and insignificant certain parts of life are. Growing up scares me. The film channels that feeling incredibly well and almost to a scary extent. When Watanabe is singing and crying over Life is Brief, I felt so intensely sad for him because I've been in that position so many times. I reminisce and this often leads me into a tangent of depression because it reminds me of how long it's been since certain events have taken place. I miss the old times and worry that I'm not embracing the current times enough simultaneously, and Ikiru takes all those fears into my biggest fear in life, something I hope never happens to me. I adore the way the story pans out and how every aspect is executed masterfully. I'm going to be thinking about Ikiru for a long time and I can only see it rising up in my rankings. This is a new favorite.

Name: Pieces of a Woman
Review: Compromised of three acts, Pieces of a Woman is a decent movie full of inconsistency. When it's good, it's very good. Most of the first half hour and third act fall under this umbrella. But when it's bad, which would be the second act and little sections of the first and third, it's a real struggle to get through. Sort of like childbirth. One thing that cannot be denied is that this film has acting talent in vast quantities. Vanessa Kirby, Shia LaBeouf and even Ellen Burstyn really bring a lot to their characters, and this is especially impressive considering they're working with rather weak dialogue more often than not (I'll touch more on this later). While not every shot feels purposeful, the camerawork undoubtedly looks thoroughly great and I really like the one shots in the first act. It's quite the good movie in other technical aspects as well. Overall, the direction is solid. The first act, consisting of an extremely believable childbirth, sets the bar very high for the rest of the movie and is one hell of a way to kick things off. Unfortunately, the second act is badly paced and the character development is all over the place. I didn't give two shits about the characters or the situations they were in, and I couldn't be bothered to look forward to the rest of the movie. There's really no focus or direction in this act and I was rolling my eyes every few minutes. However, the third scene, which I would mark as beginning with Burstyn's monologue, rounds things out well and has satisfying payoff. It was a needed change and I'm happy with how the story concluded. As a final product, it's hard to deny that Pieces of a Woman is pretty messy. Despite good performances and technical aspects, there are parts of the story that feel really under developed and overall I still feel like something is missing. The pacing is clunky a majority of the time and I was able to predict many plot points. The screenplay is a mediocre one and even when I was engaged with the film, I can't deny that I never truly cared about the characters because they're written with a high amount of melodrama that feels forced mixed in with actual traits that define them. I give my 6/10 rating because the final act left a better taste in my mouth (metaphorically of course) but this isn't one of the better movies to come from 2020.

Name: Godzilla
Review:
Got HBO Max yesterday to see how I like it for a month, this is the first movie I'm watching on it. Cool!

The MonsterVerse installments are the textbook definitions of theme park movies. It feels very inspired by Spielberg and James Cameron at that, in the sense that it's packed to the brim with special effects, has an exciting music score, and trying its hardest to make big dough at the box office and start a franchise. In other words, Gareth Edwards attempted to make the next Jurassic Park.

Given the above paragraph, Godzilla has a lot of parallels to sci-fi blockbusters in history, but as a movie it's exactly what I expected. There's a surprising amount of CGI even for this kind of movie, not all of it very good, and there isn't anything too technically interesting about it aside from the striking use of the color red. There were a number of sound mixing issues as well, mainly with people talking louder than giant machines or monsters screeching. Some parts were really interesting and well written, the cinematography isn't half bad, it's directed well enough with a number of well timed and exciting sequences, but there's a fair amount of forced drama and cheese sprinkled throughout. Some of the cheese is just stupid jokes, but I don't mind them THAT much. This could very well be intentional, but it's still very worth noting. Similarly, I really didn't care that deeply about the characters (featuring a high quantity of mediocre to bad performances at that but I was expecting this), and it focuses far too much on the politics on the situation for me to be consistently engaged. The last half hour has some good action, and the intensity of the final skydive / attack on Godzilla was great, but the buildup itself wasn't really satisfying. The skydive feels like the only part of the movie that really had some serious imagination to it, and I wish there were more sequences that matched the same energy.

Admittedly I feel bad bashing the movie for certain things, since this really isn't something that's trying too hard and just having fun, so I definitely respect that. It knows the audience well and obviously it was successful, along with some very good sequences, interesting technical aspects, and pure ambition. However, it doesn't bring anything new to the genre and won't leave a mark nearly the same way Spielberg's and James Cameron's imaginative blockbusters have had on the film industry. It started the MonsterVerse, and there are some interesting ideas, but I'm probably not gonna remember much of the movie itself.


Random side note, I wonder how the Marvel casting team felt about this film, with Aaron Taylor-Johnson and Elisabeth Olsen as lovers...
Tags: hbo max, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Sympathy for Mr. Vengeance
Review: New Korean Cinema This is a film that's really difficult to review without spoiling it, but I'll try my best. Throughout the 2 hour runtime, I got vibes of Yorgos Lanthimos, Takashi Miike's Audition and some other minor parallels to other filmmakers, but I say this all in positive light. This film really is definitive of all the traits of Korean cinema, as it is a complex character study for multiple people and features some pretty extreme violence and sex. Park Chan-wook's directing and the excellent cinematography add a ton to an already intriguing story that isn't consistently paced but never bores. The first hour is fantastic and the last half hour is brilliant as well, but the middle half hour just doesn't hook me the same way. There are some not as good performances to be found as well, but Song Kang-ho and Shin Ha-kyun are both excellent. Overall this is an incredibly memorable and well done film that is a great intro to Korean cinema for someone who isn't familiar with the subgenre. At one point I had this at a 9, and I might bump it up, but I'm happy with a high 8 for now.

Name: Oldboy
Review: New Korean Cinema It's Park Chan-wook's Oldboy, I really don't have anything new to say. The gorgeous and purposeful cinematography, flawless directing, thoroughly memorable screenplay, Choi Min-sik's endlessly phenomenal performance... fucking amazing. The pacing and story are brilliant and it's such a complex, layered story that after two watches and taking a listen to some video analyses, I'm still not fully sure of what everything means. This is one that I'll be taking influence from in films I make in the future. I love a ton of what Park did to make this film memorable, with both traits of his own work and Korean Cinema entirely. I knew what was coming and yet I'm still thinking about so many of the images I just witnessed. It was a close call, but I'm gonna add this to my favorites list. While I don't feel a personal impact from the outcome of each character's arc, I feel as though that's the point because one of the main ideas discussed is how revenge is not always the answer nor is it satisfying. I just know in my heart and instincts that this is a masterpiece of filmmaking and one that has influenced me outside my movie life as well. This rating might go down to a 9, but for now it's definitely staying here. In short... I fucking love it.

Name: Dick Johnson Is Dead
Review:
I can't believe it took me so long to finally check this out. It is easily one of the most ambitious films to come out last year and such a joy to experience. In a year dominated by Minari, Nomadland and The Father, this still stands as one of the most personal and touching films of 2020. It has such a good pace and expectedly great camerawork; the way it captures the experience and emotions of creating this film and the meaning behind it is something seriously worth praising. There are several tones blended during the 90 minute runtime, all done to excellent effect, which add a lot to the already stellar filmmaking. Overall this is one of the best documentaries I've seen and certainly will find a place in the top ten for all 2020 movies.

I really do like the lineup of nominees in every category, including documentary feature at the Oscars this year, but they somehow forgot Dick Johnson. (props to them for nominating Crip Camp however)

Name: Emperor Jones
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 11 -- A film from the 1930s Holy crap I'm really behind on the challenge. There are a lot of movies I've been meaning to watch for a while that are on my list so I'm gonna see what all I can get to this weekend. Hopefully a solid number of them. Anyways, as for the movie itself, I enjoyed it. It's an incredibly interesting artifact of filmmaking from Depression-era America; there's some very questionable content to be found, but the black characters were portrayed in a much more positive light than I was expecting. Dudley Murphy had a good eye for directing and it's very well made from a technical standpoint. I was never really unengaged with the movie overall, and the performances show some really solid skill (mainly from the two leads). The runtime felt very adequate and the story has precise pacing at that, something I don't think I praise as much as I should in movies; the only issue I had in this area was a long dialogue-driven section that didn't really add much to what was already established, but the final 8-ish minutes are full of some cool visuals that round things out well. It isn't a mindblowing piece of work or incredibly ahead of its time, but I'm glad I checked it out for the challenge.

Name: American Psycho
Review:
I took the SAT earlier today and watched this...? Yeah I guess. I'm very tired right now

Having watched it a second time and now that I know a lot more about the filmmaking process and I definitely picked up on more details than the first time. I was engaged the whole time and the story somehow still felt unpredictable too which I really liked. It's not the most well written and there are a lot of story decisions I really didn't like but I haven't read the novel so I'm not going to hold this against my personal opinion.

However, the most distinct aspect to this movie for me was I actually noticed a large number of Fight Club parallels which is both a good and bad thing. They're both commentaries on masculinity and their role in society, the main characters get off on violence, they have relatively the same twist (both are executed with very ineffective follow up development and feel messy to me; frankly they both ruin the movies for me and feel largely unsatisfying), and they both have random high-energy action in their final acts. 

Fight Club has a better screenplay all things considered (note: screenplays are more than just dialogue. They also contribute to the story structure, plot progression and pacing), but Christian Bale's performance and the simple fact that the twist occurs at the very end without an largely unlikable final act to follow up (though I still hate how abruptly it ends) make this the better movie in my eyes. I certainly don't love either and I have no urge to watch them again though so that's a similarity too. I really do like the social commentary that Mary Harron was able to embed with what she was working with, and yet I'm still largely unmoved.

This is a terrible review and I'm sorry. As I said, I took the SAT this morning and then I went to go shoot hoops with friends to decompress before watching this movie. I've had a crazy day and I'm burnt out so I'm just trying to get my thoughts out while I have them.


Also I've gotten really behind on watching movies and I'm sorry about that, my school and work lives are seriously kicking my ass this month.

Name: Greener Grass
Review:
I really enjoyed this overall. Great cinematography and production design, an excellent screenplay, and overall solid directing really elevate the quirkiness of the story and themes. It blends sequences of fun and fear very well and the points got across rather well. I mostly liked the performances, and there were a few moments where I was questioning the acting but this almost feels purposeful with the rest of the movie? It's very well paced overall and I was definitely never bored.

Despite that, I didn't feel much of a connection to any such character and my emotions felt pretty consistent throughout the 90 minutes runtime. With ideas as ambitious as those tackled in Greener Grass, I was hoping to feel something stronger, but by the end I feel like the movie was worth my time and I enjoyed myself but I don't have any major takeaways. There are also a few technical issues I noticed, mainly with editing and sound mixing, but this is more of a nitpick. Nonetheless this is an overlooked comedy from 2019 that I'd definitely recommend giving a try. Maybe a second watch when I'm in a bit of a better mental state will improve my thoughts and opinion, it's close to an 8.

Name: Crack: Cocaine, Corruption & Conspiracy
Review: "I don't think they understand the trauma of poverty." While nothing amazing is done with the filmmaking or the way the story is told, Crack: Cocaine, Corruption & Conspiracy remains an informational, interesting and rather powerful documentary. It's structured and paced well, offers a lot of anecdotes from the 80s, crack and those who were impacted by it, and also offers a high amount of insight into related political issues the crack epidemic made its way into such as the Reagan administration, class divides, and even racial issues (this being the most prominently featured). The only thing I really have to say against it is that there were some sections that offered information which felt irrelevant to what was being discussed at certain points during the movie and I felt unengaged during these pieces, but otherwise I'm more than glad I checked it out; I learned more and felt more affected than I was expecting going in. And it goes without saying that the story is a fascinating one that feels surreal in several ways. I've seen drug use, specifically crack utilized as a story element in so many pieces of media but nothing really outside the culture of it. And as someone who's always interested in history, this made for a very worthwhile watch. The middle-aged crowd who actually lived through the crack epidemic might get more out of the movie, but it certainly gets my recommendation and I'm rather surprised that this being a Netflix documentary, and a rather good one at that, hasn't gotten more attention.

Name: mother!
Review: "I'll just get started on the apocalypse." I'd like to start by apologizing to the UPS delivery guy I freaked out; he rang the doorbell during a really violent scene about 45 minutes in and I screamed since I was so enamored by the movie. He asked if everything was okay and I told him what movie I was watching, and we shared a laugh over it. The movie itself... I don't even know what conclusions to draw from this. It's incredible in some aspects but others just annoy me so much. Darren Aronofsky clearly knew this would be a divisive film but I can't tell if that was a goal in mind for him while he was writing the script because there's so much to unpack both good and bad. Above all, I can't believe a human made this movie and I feel like I dreamed it all. The one thing I really just can't deny is Javier Bardem and Jennifer Lawrence blew the roof off. No matter how satisfied or pissed off I was with what was happening, I loved what they brought to the table and they were written so fucking well. I mean, they dominated every scene and bring life to everything... kinda like how Bardem wanted to bring life into the home. The beginning had weirdly short shots that didn't feel meaningful at all, especially out of place for an Aronofsky movie but as the movie goes on the filmmaking gets better and better. The first half hour just didn't hook me the same way Requiem and Black Swan command your attention from start to finish, but at least after that it gets better. The editing kinda bugged me overall, as there were a few transitions that felt REALLY weird and the way the story concludes, both going down to the writing and editing, might've been the worst of it. It's so unsatisfying and goes completely against the rest of the film leaving an even worse taste in your mouth. Outside of the ending though, I did really like the cinematography and the production design was used to the greatest extent possible. Anyways, so why do I have it at a high(ish) rating despite my major complaints? Well, you all know the metaphor so I won't delve any deeper into it but there were some incredibly intense sections that REALLY do work. There's a murder about a third into the film that felt strongly like an allegory to the Old Testament of the Bible, and once everyone starts coming into the house in the final act, I fully felt like I was embracing Aronofsky's imagination. It's disturbing and ridiculous but in a surprisingly amazing way. At these points I felt like the movie was building up to join the ranks of his masterpieces but I wish the rest of the film felt so focused. When mother! is amazing, it's amazing. When it's not, it's painful and unsatisfying. I honestly do love the concept of Mother Nature and how humanity destroys the earth as a horror movie, and the amazing sections were nearly perfect. But between the lackluster editing, ridiculous ending and inconsistent quality there's potential that's wasted at least a quarter of the movie. At the very least, I give my highest props to Aronofsky for being insane enough to actually make this movie happen... and receive distribution from a studio as big as Paramount. This is an incredibly messy review and I apologize, but I'm flustered, stunned, pissed, amazed, and just all around feeling like I'm going to have the disturbing imagery stuck in my brain maybe forever. It lives up to the reputation of being one of the most disturbing, heart-stopping movies ever... at a price.

Name: Lady Vengeance
Review: New Korean Cinema A good movie, but not as interesting as the other two entries in the Vengeance trilogy. It's as amazingly well made and visually stunning as I would expect from Park Chan-wook, and he manages to bring out some pretty fantastic performances as usual. On top of all this, it has a wonderful score that doesn't quite match that of Oldboy but comes very close; I love the baroque style it has. However, I found the pacing and structure of the story to lack the ability to keep me consistently engaged and I didn't feel much of a connection to any particular character, including Geum-ja herself. I still enjoyed myself and every once in a while there are moments of greatness, but they feel more like punctuations in the overall good, not great storyline. The ending is brilliant and I love how satisfying the film turned out to be, but I wish the build-up to that matched that same energy and power.

Name: Sing Street
Review: My first time seeing this movie since I was 12 was an interesting experience as I LOVED this at the time. This reminds me a lot of Blinded by the Light where it has the heart, ideas and ambition behind it but the actual movie itself is inconsistent. Parts of it are actually really great and really show the skill behind the screenplay, but the directing is pretty average and there are a lot of obvious filmmaking flaws including sound mixing and the actors' fingers not matching the music being played, two things that are extremely important in music-related movies. In the end, the conclusion feels forced and like it's going way too fast without development which left a bad taste in my mouth, but I'd still say it was worth watching if not to be entertained by the chemistry between the guys and just decompress after a long day and week.
Tags: blu-ray, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: The Mole Agent
Review: As a whole, this is pretty well made and has a really interesting concept behind it, but after the first 15 minutes the movie shifts focus away from what it set out to do. I was certainly very entertained by the chemistry between all the elderly, particularly Sergio and his girlfriend, but once you remember that this was supposed to be a movie about tracking down a mole, the experience felt a bit underwhelming for a while. There were aspects to appreciate in both the humor and execution of the movie itself, such as Sergio being an 83 year old man learning and interacting with newer technology and the way he reports his findings, but the findings themselves don't draw any conclusions to the film's opening. Despite that, I can cut the film some slack because this is a documentary and of course the filmmakers didn't know how it would end, so it seems more like the marketing for it was misleading. Better yet, once the movie keeps moving along, the story of Sergio and his friends get very personal and quite moving at that. Once it all finished, I almost completely forgot my frustration with the shift in focus because it's more than made up for with some great emotion. I'd definitely recommend this movie, as it doesn't achieve what it set out to but ends up standing for something else that's a rather good substitute, so alleviate your expectations with that mindset. I wouldn't call it one of the best 2020 documentaries, but it isn't too terribly far off.

Name: It's Such a Beautiful Day
Review:
Even on a second watch I still don't think I fully processed all this movie threw at me. Still figuring out if that aspect of it is genius or frustrating. All I know for sure is that this is a masterpiece and will forever be a favorite. I can't put into words how painfully relatable Bill is to me and how I see a lot of myself in his character. I hope I watch this film throughout myself so I keep finding new relevance in it as I age, as scary a concept as that is.


I don't even know what else to say. I need to live life more than I do now. That's all, thank you.
Tags: blu-ray, love this movie more than myself

Name: The Hudsucker Proxy
Review: This was... brilliant! Combining the charm of 40s like It's a Wonderful Life with the quirky portrayal of the business world from movies like Brazil, this is easily one of the most underrated movies I've seen and certainly the most underrated of the Coens' work. Even outside of the business world, the story and characters speak on so much more about the 50s era culture and the emotional roller coaster behind having a stressful and important job that many people can relate to. The screenplay is absolutely terrific, not a single bad performance in sight, all the traits of the Coen creative process are apparent and used to great effect, I was hooked from start to finish, and smiling for nearly every minute of it. Parts of the movie felt slightly unengaging and some of the drama forced, but each subsequent scene was either equal to or better than the last and every joke landed for me. And that soundtrack? I mean... goddamn, every song is used correctly and so good. The directing executed each element in beautiful fashion, Deakins' cinematography is great as always, and by the time it was all over, I both felt like I knew the characters + world incredibly well and found one of my new favorite comedies. Last night after I watched It's Such a Beautiful Day, after I had some time to think about it, I got irrationally anxious and felt really weird mentally. It eventually built up to be a true anxiety attack and I don't think before then did I know the true meaning of a piece of media hitting it close to home. Because of this, I've been in a really weird and frankly not very good headspace which I'm still recovering from; maybe I'm just so glad to have watched a movie as funny as this to enjoy a strong change in pace, but I'd imagine even if I watched this when feeling good I would enjoy it just the same. I'd rate this an 8. 9, including the mezzanine (this is also a better 1994 film than Pulp Fiction IMO, sorry not sorry)

Name: Pebbles
Review:
First film from the New Directors festival!

Pebbles is a very visually interesting film with a well developed dysfunctional father-son relationship. Every performance shows some good acting skill and I was certainly never bored. However, the editing is pretty bad and the way each event is structured feels bizarre and out of place at a few instances. Some scenes are really gripping and got me excited for what came next, but others leave you questioning the point. All around a worthwhile watch nonetheless that uses its location incredibly well, based off a real story from the director, but not one that comes without flaws.

6.5
Tags: new directors 2021, virtual cinema, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Biutiful
Review: 7.0 Holy shit I finally finished Inarritu's filmography! Iñárritu himself has said that Biutiful is heavily inspired by Akira Kurosawa's Ikiru. Kurosawa's film, which is a favorite of mine, has an incredibly focused narrative structure that doesn't waste any time with its pacing and every emotional beat hits the right amount. I can recall so many scenes from Ikiru in my mind and my positive opinion of it has only grown stronger over time. Iñárritu has made films that fit these descriptions before, in the guise of Babel and Birdman. But this entry to his filmography, which came in between those two, doesn't hold the same merit or impact behind it. An incredibly dull first hour that lacks much focus or consistency at all, keeping me mostly uninterested and feeling more like an amateur filmmaker trying to imitate the style of the Depression Trilogy than anything else is followed by a vastly improved remainder (80 minutes). Javier Bardem goes from just playing Javier Bardem to truly entering the role of Uxbal. The cinematography also goes from shaky and forgettable to beautiful and sharp, featuring several long takes and perfect color palates for each individual shot. All this and an excellent soundtrack kept me interested. Unfortunately, there's more to the remainder than this, including a lot of my same complaints from the first hour, such as long sequences of exposition which I was able to not pay attention to and not feel like I had missed anything. There are some attempts at commentaries on racism and class divides as well, the latter of these two proving to be far more effective as Uxbal spends an equal amount of time in both middle and lower class Barcelona, but I wish Iñárritu focused more on these thematic elements in tandem with the side characters. An admittedly great ending that rounds things out in great fashion and feels depressingly satisfying makes the whole experience worth it, but I can't seem to recall a huge amount of individual scenes prior to this. If the rest of the film had this same emotional power, then I think the movie could at least join the ranks of Amores Perros. The cinematography, Uxbal and the chemistry with his family make this a worthwhile watch, but I can't deny that Iñárritu has both written and directed better screenplays that are more engaging. Overall, I think a pretty high amount of minutes could've been cut out of the runtime and it would be that much better. His love letter to Kurosawa's 1952 treasure is inferior for the most part and I'd place it on the lower end of his filmography. Still, I think it has the possibility to grow on me months out, and now that it's been some time since I've finished the movie, I can safely say the emotions of Uxbal's crushing journey are really sticking with me. Going back and forth between a 6.5 and a 7.0, but I'll keep it at a 7.0 for the time being because of that lasting impact.

Name: Apples
Review:
7.5

Sadly not as interesting as it was the first time for me. I stand by the film being amazingly well shot and very well directed with a lot of interesting plot details and great production design, but I didn't find the story and characters to be as enticing as they were the first time; this time the movie just felt more like something I would put on in the background while doing other stuff. Still an enjoyable movie with a lot of big ideas and some heart, but not touching or leaving a lasting an impression like it did back at the Chicago festival in October.

I truly do wish I had more to write on it after finishing my second watch, but this is all I can really say. Worth a recommendation, though I'd be lying if I said I wasn't a little disappointed.

Name: Rome, Open City
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 12 -- A film from the 1940s Yes yes I'm 7 weeks behind on this. Shut up. The end of the school year sucks, that's all I'm gonna say. 7.5 Written and filmed during WWII, this movie provides an incredibly realistic portrayal of Nazism through fictional characters. This is a bravely written film and the fact that it came out in 1945 with these characters is super impressive. As an entire product, the film is very well directed, shot and edited (particularly impressive is the mise-en-scène and how Rossellini framed each character movement); it certainly isn't perfect on an audio level, with lots of words not matching to lips and weird mixing, though I'm not sure if this is a valid criticism because this might've been the best they were able to pull off given circumstances. However, it's safe for me to say I admire the movie more than I actually enjoyed or felt personally impacted by it. There's a lot of merit here given the time period and significance of how it came to be, though I struggled to give the film itself, the story it tells and the characters it develops my full attention. It spends a lot of time on side characters who I'm going to forget in a few days, and Manfredi, the main guy, isn't the most dynamic either. The transition between Chapters 1 and 2 an hour in is full of great moments for actors to present great performances, some really intense peril and character conflict I actually felt emotionally. Unfortunately, it then descends back into exposition that went back to the old schtick of the first chapter I didn't care much about. Just for the historical merit alone I'm glad I watched this and I'd urge you all to put it on your watchlist, but don't go in expecting anything life changing as far as the story or characters go.

Name: Saw III
Review: 3.5 With the death of someone close to one of the main characters who is tasked with the decision to fulfill his vengeance or forgive and move on with his life on numerous occasions, Saw III feels weirdly like it's trying to replicate the emotion of Se7en as its top priority. Unfortunately, this is not the excellent, heart-pounding and piercingly effective thriller it takes after; it's an abysmal sequel that keeps getting worse as it goes on. I finished the third entry in the grisly franchise minutes ago, but I've already forgotten a fair amount of the plot if that says anything to its quality, so I'll keep it simple (the movie doesn't deserve more than this anyway); it's a Saw movie. Expect blood, torture, elaborate traps with a design that packs an equal amount of cringe as it does pain to the characters, and Tobin Bell talking slow. The first 20 minutes or so serve a sadly low amount of purpose to the rest of the movie; there are two victims shown but neither have a point in the rest of the story. I'm surprised to see that the mind behind the original, James Wan, was behind the story; the original doesn't have any kills just for the sake of grossing the audience out. Even if the characters are insignificant, they still serve some sort of purpose in the worldbuilding and mythology behind Jigsaw. But this is the third entry, and we already know what to expect when going into these movies. Once we actually meet the couple the actual story centers around, things start to get interesting. The plot actually starts off pretty good, with intriguing setup and some surprisingly solid dialogue. At this point, I actually had some hope for the rest of the movie to be just as good as the first. Unfortunately, things don't last long; I can't tell you what happens for the remainder, both to avoid spoilers and because I simply don't remember "off the top of my head." What I can tell you is that both the psychological and physical labyrinths each character goes through culminates into a messy, unfocused sequence of frustration full of Darren Lynn Bousman being extremely timelapse-happy with the cinematography and already mediocre shots being punctuated by the sloppy editing, not unlike the unsatisfying conclusion that's needlessly melodramatic and completely made me forget about why I was enjoying this just 30-45 minutes earlier. To make matters worse, the final minutes are accompanied by alt-rock music that caused me to start laughing during the final death; this is comparable to the rap music in Django Unchained, but if it was so bad it ended up being funny. Reiterating the fact that James Wan helped develop the story for this movie, it seems to me he either forgot what made the original successful or he made it bad on purpose to prevent the idea to make any further sequels... I wish he put at least a little more effort into the sometimes promising premise, but I also don't blame him for wanting this franchise to end, assuming that's the case. I can't tell you the names of the characters in this, I can barely tell you the story or the motive behind why they were put in the traps in the first place, and the only takeaway I got from this is lower expectations for Spiral. Side note: Why are there teeth in the poster? None of the traps or deaths involve teeth at all, so I guess they're only there to look creepy I guess. Look, normally a movie like this isn't worth my time and brainshare to write a review this complex, but I'm trying to get back into writing full blown reviews as often as I can. Plus, it's fun to do so while talking about a movie this shitty.

Name: Gull
Review: 6.5 New Korean Cinema A simple but effective debut, Gull shows off some real directing skill from the beautiful cinematography to the rather good performances from each of the main characters, backed up by a decent screenplay (not without some dialogue lacking in engagement, though). At least a third of the runtime is fairly slow and not all that special, but when it's good, it's really good. Otherwise, there's not much more to say here; it shows promise in Kim Mi-jo and their future career but this first film isn't something I really take anything serious out of.

Name: Faya Dayi
Review: 9.0 An amazing, visually striking look at the culture of khat, one of the main exports of Ethiopia, Faya Dayi is an ambitious and all around very well executed debut by Jessica Beshir. While there isn't a particular storyline to tell of, it beautifully portrays the sights and sounds of Harar and how their civilization connects to the khat plant. Every character tells an interesting story that is aided by the black and white cinematography which continually had me stunned; there isn't a single shot in this movie that isn't mesmerizing. I can't wait to see this again because the filmmaking behind it is something that I am seriously so intrigued by and want to learn as much as I can about it; the long shots, the natural lighting and sounds, it's fantastic and totally up my alley. Easily in competition for my favorite film of the year so far (matched by Writing with Fire) and I'd be very surprised if it didn't stay in at least the top 10 by the time we get to the end of 2021. The documentary genre of film is something that has stuck out in the back of my mind as something to learn more about and explore further ever since watching Hoop Dreams for the first time last summer. Combine that with 2020 and now 2021 looking to be another great year for documentaries, I think I know what I'm going to lay a good chunk of my film-watching focus on this summer.

Name: Bo Burnham: Make Happy
Review: Very entertaining. A friend recommended this to me and within 10 minutes I knew this was COMPLETELY going to be my thing, so I gave it my full attention, though eventually it petered out a bit and just became background noise while I worked on homework before going back to being something I loved by the end. Burnham is an incredibly intriguing guy to me and I love all the passion and effort he puts into all his creative work. I'm only saying this based off of seeing this comedy special + his role in Promising Young Woman, but I feel like it's safe to make that statement just based on Make Happy. I'll keep his other work in mind if I'm ever feeling bored one day like I am today. (and no I still don't give ratings to stand-up specials or concert films or anything of the like)

Name: True Grit
Review: 7.0 Thoroughly entertaining with a lot of great production value and effort put in by each actor, though it feels rather conventional for a Coen affair. It has enough thrills, plot twists (which are all predictable by the standard of the brothers) and some solid messages to help this feel like a film they would make, but at the same time this is exactly what I was expecting; the biggest takeaway I got from it was how talented Hailee Steinfeld and Domhall Gleeson were this early on in their career. On a technical note, Roger Deakins met the standard he always sets for himself but doesn't really exceed it at any point, and the brothers were very fade transition-happy when editing which is more of a nitpick but still took me out of the movie briefly. Again, a good movie overall, but it's much more likely I'll rewatch O Brother or Buster Scruggs over this as far as Coen westerns go.
Tags: hbo max, w/ dad, w/ quinn

Name: Black Swan
Review: 10 "What happened to my sweet girl?" Generally there's about a 50/50 chance a movie I watch the second time will hold up. But with Black Swan, my former favorite movie, I am once again floored by this artistic, complex, compelling and simply beautiful masterwork. The 105 minute film and the direction it takes still brings the tragedy of Nina Sayers to glorious life, from the slow and sure buildup to the mind-bending and incredibly satisfying finale that feels bigger than the audience, moreso on rewatch. On a personal note, I was intrigued to see how this would hold up after going through the rest of Aronofsky’s filmography. I had some ups where I was reminded of why I love his work and approach to filmmaking with Pi and The Wrestler, a clear down with The Fountain, and a combination of the both with mother! which I still think about a month later. But before my second watch was over I was already reminded of why I considered this my #1 movie for months and captivated all over again. It goes without saying that Natalie Portman gives one of the greatest performances and one of my favorites of all time. Nina's emotional breakdowns took their toll but in a strangely addicting manner; the contrast between her two sides as the white and black swans are relatable and the extremes of each character’s traits strike a chord that I never expected them to on either watch.
