What I can't get enough of is the hypnotic nature of the movie. This mostly goes to Aronofsky’s direction, Libatique's spectacular camerawork and Mansell's musical talent, but every creative hand involved turns this into one of the most terrifying but striking experiences in film I've ever seen. Admittedly there were times where I had minor doubts that my original opinion wouldn't hold up but once it all wraps up I felt like I could live in the chilling final performance of Swan Lake forever. I physically can't take my eyes off the film and no matter how many times I watch Black Swan I'm going to put myself through the same emotional roller coaster all over again. And I'm going to love it. I know I already reviewed the movie after the first watch but I won't sleep well tonight without doing it all over again. This is a perennial favorite of mine and always will be; no one will change that. It's a work of art. It's phenomenal. It's Black Swan.
Tags: blu-ray, love this movie more than myself, w/ quinn

Name: Ordet
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, Week 13 -- A film from the 1950s 8.5 A great movie all around that has incredible cinematography (specifically mise-en-scene), acting and production design all brought together by the legendary Carl Theodore Dreyer's direction, though I didn't really feel emotionally involved until the final half hour. The first 90 minutes are very well made and slowly but surely develop each character, but the finale is simply incredible. I understand why this is one of the highest rated movies of the 1950s, and while I wouldn't call it a personal favorite or one that I'll take a lot of inspiration from, this felt like a very special watch and I truly got the sense that I was watching something important. The themes of religion are quite intense, ranging from the simple fact that each character lives in a Christian household to Johannes thinking he is literally Jesus Christ (his name sounds like Your Highness, and I totally think this was intentional). It totally feels like a film Scorsese would consider a favorite of his and I don't mean that in a bad way at all.

Name: Flushed Away
Review: 5.0 A movie from my childhood, so it was interesting to see how it holds up (I watched this off a friend's recommendation). For that reason I don't want to be too harsh on it, and admittedly it has incredibly imaginative world-building from Aardman as expected, with a great idea for the synopsis, but the execution of it is so incredibly rushed in its pacing and they've written much better screenplays. There are some good moments in it but they tried to fit way too much into 75 minutes (there are 10 minutes of credits for some reason) and it seems to get more unfocused as it goes along. Still, I have some good memories from this, even if it isn't a very good movie. I honestly still love Aardman today but this is their weakest work. Also it gets extra points because they make fun of British and French people! (This is ironic, sorry to any British or French users who follow me)

Name: The Good, the Bad, the Weird
Review: 7.5 Every Korean film I've seen up to this point has been deep and very thematic, so it's an interesting change in pace to watch one that's entertaining and doesn't focus on taking itself too seriously. All around I certainly enjoyed myself and there are aspects to praise. All three main performances show some impressive acting skill along with being super entertaining to watch, the score is so well composed, the production design is fantastic and the stuntwork is amazing. However, it's not easy to notice this or other aspects of the high energy, action-packed scenes... there are copious amounts of bad cuts and jarring transitions that really took me out of the movie. I felt like I was watching The Two Towers again, but I could see that the stunts and action were amazing so it was all the more annoying because of this. This and the cinematography are a mixed bag, as they're really good during the slower, more exposition driven scenes but downright irritating during the intense action. The directing isn't bad for the most part, but more cuts =/= good editing. Narrative wise, the story is enjoyable and engaging with interesting characters and a solid screenplay with a surprising amount of humor; I can imagine the cast and crew had a ball of a time on set. The scale of the plot is huge as it should be for a western, and there wasn't a single part of the story, location or characters that felt lacking. I found the ending a bit dull and it takes a little while at the start to really get into things, but all things considered Kim Jee-woon got the job done here and clearly put in a lot of creativity. Something I haven't seen anyone write about on Letterboxd, however, at least across the reviews I've read, is the historical theme aspect. The movie takes place in the 1930s when Korea was a China-owned territory, and this plays into the character dynamics pretty frequently. There are a lot of Koreans fighting for independence, both in small and big roles, and it got me interested in this era of Korean history. Don't expect anything super rich or impactful like in Parasite, but an effort was made and I'm glad for that. To re-iterate, The Good, The Bad, The Weird isn't a social commentary or complex study of any sort, but it isn't trying to be. It's a wildly entertaining western that's a lot of fun and well made in a number of areas. It certainly isn't a personal favorite, or one of the best movies I've seen, or any superlative of the sort. But it might be one of the easiest to recommend Korean films.

Name: The Meaning of Life
Review: As cerebral, bizarre, imaginative, deep, and emotional as I'd expect from Don Hertzfeldt. I love this man and I fucking love his work but even this little 12 minute short was draining and I don't understand it fully on just one watch. I can only watch his films every once in a while because if I do anything more often than this I'm genuinely going to go insane.

Name: Cruella
Review: 4.0 Bad because - the "first gay character" (can Disney please fuck right the hell of with their gay baiting bullshit it goddamn it pisses me off so much) was stereotypical and underdeveloped. His existence in the story felt so much more like a corporate order than a genuine part of the story and he deserves SO MUCH BETTER. Fuck Disney, man - holy shit the editing LOL - holy shit the soundtrack LOL - cheesy dialogue that takes the sting out of Emma Stone's and Thompson's admittedly good performances - the comic relief characters, which seemed to be really effective for everyone else in the theater but I didn't find any of it funny - gross CGI - holy shit the ending LOL - ok look I tried to get into the story, I really did. But I predicted every twist and the darkness of the story only worked half the time; this has nothing new to say if it says anything at all and I think I was hoping for a little bit of commentary or some solid themes but that might be on me - while watching the movie I kept making comparisons in my head to other movies that certain scenes reminded me of and I wish the movie had more of its own voice. It's just badly directed overall I'll leave it at that Good because - visually interesting at times - the Emmas really did try - the production design - a few moments around the halfway point that kept me hooked and I was able to briefly lose myself - the audience enjoyed the movie and I REALLY REALLY hope none of my school friends ever read this review since I know many of them will get a huge kick out of it (but I hope they agree with me on at least a few of my problems with the movie)
Tags: kendall 11, bad editing, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Snowpiercer
Review: 7.0 I actually watched this much earlier in the afternoon, but work kept me insanely busy (I'm a cook for a restaurant movie theater, so people ordering food at sold out Cruella and A Quiet Place II showings wore me the fuck out) so I'm finally logging it now. As to be expected, I loved the world building and imagination put into the design and layout of the train itself, but the narrative and some technical aspects brought the merit of this down for me. I was engaged the whole time but never cared too thoroughly for any particular character, and while there were some intense moments, I still wasn't terribly excited by them or the stakes they built. Otherwise, the CGI wasn't very good and there were a number of editing choices I didn't like, but overall it was just a fun time. Not sure what else to say, I liked but didn't loved it.

Name: Hunt for the Wilderpeople
Review: 7.5 I feel like I'm giving most films I see lately a 7/10 but I guess that just means I need to watch better (or worse movies) since I promise I don't forcibly give movies certain ratings lol Love the way the film utilizes its location and how it really does feel immersive. Parts of it aren't edited so well, but for the most part this felt like it was edited in the guise of the Vengeance trilogy (very similar cuts to Lady Vengeance) and that's a good thing. The cinematography is really great as well; even the digital shots look pleasing. And, just like with Jojo Rabbit, Taiwan Waititi knows his shit when it comes to directing actors; there isn't a single bad performance here. All around, I was entertained from start to finish but not hooked like I was Jojo Rabbit. That's not to say I wasn't thoroughly enjoying myself however, and I think this is a movie I'll enjoy even more on a second watch. Hopefully I'll show it to friends sometime this summer.

Name: A Quiet Place Part II
Review: 6.0 This was an exciting experience as my first time I got to use free employee tickets at the theater I work. Yay! (Not going to say where it is or what it's called since internet people can be weird) Movie itself... it started out strong with a really good first half hour but then started to feel more predictable and rushed as it went along. Most jumpscares were predictable or just didn't really get me, along with some movie mistakes and strange filmmaking choices. The acting was just as good if not better than the first one, so that's one thing I do have to praise this for, though it's safe to say I was pretty disappointed overall. For a movie with a $61 million budget compared to $17 million for the first, it takes no risks that the first one didn't already and just feels like another horror sequel. By the end I was pretty uninterested and that's never a sign of a good movie. The original screenwriters wanted the first to be a standalone success and I completely understand that, now that I've seen the final result of the second.

Name: The Lobster
Review: 8.0 Conflicting feelings on this. The first hour is absolutely amazing, but the second is solid but not much more, with the only real standout being the scene with the hotel manager couple. All around the cinematography is never short of brilliant, the score is amazing and the one recurring short tune continually reminded me of a classic European-style version of the Mii channel theme, and the purposefully stilted acting kept me engaged. The story and the way it's executed gave me dark fairy tale vibes and I absolutely loved the narrative in this sense. Furthermore, the first hour has some of the best tonal shifts in any film I've seen, pushing the extremes of being funny and satisfying blended with those of being disturbing, unsettling and depressing in ways that worked incredibly well for me. In result, I got a strange sense of euphoria every time this occurred and I was so locked into the story and characters. However, after they leave the hotel and go shopping, from here on out the film feels a lot more standard and consistent in tone. This is not necessarily a bad thing, but in comparison to how perfectly offbeat the rest of the film before this was, I started to feel less engaged. Strangely, the story starts to feel like it has less of a direction it's going in as well, and by the ending I just didn't feel the same way I did halfway through when the movie peaked. In total, this is a really good movie, but it's pretty frustrating too. Deserves a re-watch.

Name: The Conjuring
Review: 6.5 A very intriguing homage to 70s horror, most notably in the premise and editing, though it falls short thanks to a blend of a number of contemporary horror tropes that are done to death. I had a strange idea while watching that the use of tropes might be purposeful and self-aware, in which case props to James Wan, but nothing I've read online has lead me to believe that that's the actual case. In other words, there are a boatload of jumpscares and standard build-up, but I have mixed feelings on this. Weirdly, I think this movie is better experienced with your brain turned off. Once I stopped thinking about what I was seeing, I was able to appreciate some of the scares more and homages to The Exorcist. Although either way, I'd still say it's rather repetitive and not the most engaging given how high the energy consistently is. Good performances from everyone, Wan did a good job on a technical level, and I understand why this was a commercial success, but the cheesy dialogue, predictable subplots and hard-to-find direction in the story hurt it a lot; I think Wan as a screenwriter would've been a huge benefit. I'm no fan of Insidious, but I'd imagine the 70s style would've opened a lot more doors to his film creativity. Enjoyable overall with the exorcism at the end being the standout, I imagine I'd have a ton of fun with this in a theater.

Name: Shame
Review: Criterion Challenge 2021, week idfk I'm behind -- a film from the 1960s 9.0 1968 really was a good fucking year for film, huh? All around this was very intense, visually stunning (the cinematography is just fantastic on all fronts, from the framing to the overall look), amazingly well acted, beautifully directed and written, and just all around enticing from start to finish. A brilliant story of two characters forced to leave their homeland and everything they hold dear for survival, only for things to keep getting worse as they go along. That description is rather basic, but trust me when I say this is an amazing movie, as to be expected from Ingmar Bergman. I was emotionally attached to Jan and Eva (props to Max von Sydow and Liv Ullmann for never disappointing) and every hardship they went through I fully believed. While most people refer to Paths of Glory and Come and See as the quintessential anti-war movies, I'd say this deserves a similar amount of praise. There are some subplots towards the end that I wish had their ends meet in a more satisfying manner, but that's my only criticism on this excellent piece of filmmaking.

Name: Enter the Void
Review: This film is fucking amazing but the watching experience ended up being one of the worst of any movie in my life so I need to watch it again before I rate it. Like I seriously have so much to say on it but I'm also in the middle of a huge anxiety attack right now (from the movie itself and the watching experience) so I can't give it any sort of /10 score at all.

Name: In the Heights
Review: 7.5 I enjoyed this a lot! There were times that it was fantastic (the opening scene and 96K song in particular) and some times where it wasn't so good (felt like there were so many endings) but all around it was well made and a vibrant, entertaining love letter to the Latin culture of Manhattan. The characters are all beautiful with some excellent performances and I can safely say I was never tired of them, easily my favorite part of the narrative. Overall I definitely liked the first half more than the second half, but this will definitely go down as one of the more memorable theater experiences I've had in quite some time... I mean how can you not fall in love with the choreography and color palates of literally every scene? It's very in your face and at times it felt overwhelming but in the best way possible. I have some other nitpicks but I'm mostly using this log to get my initial thoughts out so yeah. Very interested to give it a second watch and it gets a recommendation from me.
Tags: kendall 11, w/ dad, w/ mom, gdx

Name: Milk
Review: 7.0 I'm very behind on my queer cinema for pride month! Luckily I found this playing in theaters, and enjoyed it a lot! It's incredibly well made, from the directing to the excellent 70s style across the cinematography, color palates, production design and costumes, great performances from Penn, Brolin and Franco, and a really solid screenplay that feels natural and does everything it needs to. There were a number of times around the halfway point where I lost interest, just thanks to the docile nature of that part of the movie, and all around I wasn't necessarily blown away by any particular aspect. But I feel like I got my money's worth and learned a lot about both Harvey Milk as a person and this era of the fight for gay civil rights. Side note: I was the only one in the theater so every time a crowd in the film boo'd or cheered I joined them. That honestly just made it all the more fun for the experience.

Name: Mulholland Drive
Review:
I fucking hate this movie. Trying to write down any thought for this review is just making me anxious and feel stupider than I already did once the end credits rolled. I feel like I'm being laughed at by Lynch and I never want to think about the movie again. I've never felt this way about any piece of media before, but Mulholland Drive almost makes me want to quit being a cinephile.


UPDATE -- I don't actually hate this movie, nor do these thoughts accurately represent what I think. Use this as a lesson not to watch David Lynch films at 2 in the morning.

Name: Mulholland Drive
Review: 9.5 So yeah I watched it again, during the day within 12 hours of my last viewing while all my half-assed theories were still in mind. I first watched this movie back in November and had quite a love for it but needed a second watch... I'd say 8 months in between was too long. As much as I love to theorize on my own when I watch movies, I actually read up some of the interpretations, specifically the differences between Betty in the dream and Diane in real life. I still have some ideas in mind for other meanings, but I'll elaborate below (marked with spoilers). Anyway, that made certain parts of this movie more enjoyable but I'm not going to keep doing this with cerebral and surreal films. David Lynch has stated that the film does tell a coherent, comprehensible story and after the third watch I can finally understand that this is true. His directing is simply phenomenal and in a way I can definitely tell this feels like a defining film of his career, so a part of me wishes this wasn't the first Lynch I ever saw (I've learned so much on him as a guy and I'm honestly shocked that I haven't seen any other of his movies outside of this one). The screenplay is mindblowing in every aspect and while I've read some complaints that the dialogue isn't very strong, I have an argument against this... It goes without saying that every performance in this is amazing, but what I didn't realize on the first watch is the contrast in how each character operates. In the first hour and 45 minutes (the dream), there are strange long pauses between lines spoken which are absent from the reality section at the end. It's a small detail but it added a lot to the immersion for me once I finally recognized the contrast. There are some cheesy lines earlier in the movie, specifically from Betty, but her story during the dream plays out like a fairy tale, and she's like a princess following her heart so this was entirely fitting and I didn't have a problem with this at all. As for the other filmmaking aspects... Angelo Badalamenti's score is amazing as always, the cinematography is mostly great with only a few shots feeling weirdly framed or out of place, the production design and usage of colors (more on this below) are incredible, specifically during the Club Silencio scene which I can sincerely say is one of the most captivating scenes in any movie I've ever seen, and despite coming out in 2001, every visual effect holds up today, to a point where I genuinely could not tell what was real and fake. It's amazing what Lynch is able to accomplish in everything he does visually, and this is one of the biggest reasons why I need to explore his career more. Plot and character spoilers from here on out as I share my personal interpretations and half-assed theories. If you've read a number of my reviews, you know that I'm a sucker for movies that use their location and worldbuilding to the greatest extent possible. It automatically elevates my enjoyment and engagement in a movie when this occurs, so of course Mulholland Drive in its story has always fascinated me. There are still a few story elements that I don't understand the thematic purpose of, but as a whole I feel like I have a finally have a good grasp on how the story plays out. So here are my interpretations. DREAM vs REALITY -- The endless theorization I've seen quite literally everywhere online is how Diane's dream correlates to her reality. I mentioned in an earlier paragraph how Betty's story is like that of a fairy tale, and in her dream she is living the Hollywood she has always hoped for. Each character serves the opposite position in her life from what they are in reality, such as Adam Kersher in financial ruin and struggling with casting vs. his actual success in film, engaged to Camilla / Rita, who depends on Betty to figure out who she is and ends up being her lover vs. a relationship that will never happen. The color blue serves as a grasp on reality, and this is most apparent during the Club Silencio scene once Betty starts shaking and at the end when Diane shoots herself. And the reason this happens at all is when the old people from the airport approach her again in her home, which I can only assume is a reminder of her life in Hollywood that never was, and she doesn't want this because of how much she struggles in her real Hollywood life. GENDER ROLES -- This is one I've conceived of completely on my own so it's going to be a lot shorter. With that, it's also a dead end at a certain point and I wouldn't be surprised if I'm way off with this paragraph. But between the poor treatment of women on numerous occasions and the contrasting lesbianism, the film seems to be making a statement on misogyny and the stereotypes of women in Hollywood. I can't really give any more evidence off of this, it requires some more research, writing things out and at least one more re-watch, but it's something I'm going to keep thinking about in the future. I have a couple other ideas but they aren't worthy of paragraphs since I have nothing else to say on them. But at the same time, I think that's the beauty of Mulholland Drive and the rest of Lynch's works. He loves to tease brains, create a puzzle and get you thinking as many directors do, but with his own extremely distinct style. Or maybe he doesn't; the final line of the film is "Silencio" which is theorized to be Lynch's request of the audience to not try and piece together the puzzle (he's asking the impossible). On that note, the ending of the film made me tear up but not consciously. It was one of those moments where you feel sad and want to cry but you simply don't know why, and that's the beauty of it. After last night's watch I was already going to be thinking about the movie for a while, but with two watches back to back I'm really going to make my half-assed theories a new focus. Yeah... I love this movie, and I can't consciously say it's a full-on masterpiece but there aren't any flaws either in all of its art, thought-provoking surrealism, and general wonder... to call it mindblowing would be a huge understatement.

Name: Under the Silver Lake
Review: 8.0 Moral of the story: Go outside, touch grass. You don't want to end up a horny NEET like this guy.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ shrey, w/ arnav

Name: The Sparks Brothers
Review: 8.0 So glad I saw this on the big screen! It was incredibly interesting and I really have a lot to say on it. And while I think that Wright has room to improve as a documentary filmmaker, the movie was still strongly made.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Tangerine
Review: 8.0 Not quite as good as The Florida Project for me, but still... what a fucking ride. Never before have I gone so drastically between loving and hating a movie, but at the very least it's a pretty damn impressive feat in both filmmaking and storytelling. The dialogue is awesome and the characters are all so realistic, and by the end I actually started to care for them. I really think the way the film was paced could've used a fair amount of work but in the end I was hooked from start to finish, and some of these scenes really are going to stick in my head for a while. And of course it's a very intense look at low life LA that I have a ton of admiration for; just all around a great story that has its flaws in the way its told but I'm very glad I checked this one out. Also I'm genuinely kinda embarrassed to not have watched any more LGBT films than this so far in June but I'm trying my best... life is busy

Name: The Darjeeling Limited
Review: 7.0 This film has a pretty mixed reception across the board, and I enjoyed it for the most part but I took a very small amount out of the substance. It's as visually striking as anything from Wes Anderson and I was really hooked for the first half of the movie but the last half really kinda lost me. There are some moments where the screenplay is great though I'd say Wes has written much better. Not a bad movie by any means, but it's probably the most forgettable out of all 5 films I've seen from him. I kinda feel bad saying that since I was loving it at the start, though that didn't stay consistent. Also between Jason Schwartzman in this and Javier Bardem in No County for Old Men, I'd say 2007 was a year for weird bowl cuts.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Memories of Murder
Review: 10 Still an absolute masterpiece. Not to mention there are various parts of this I'm going to take inspiration from for a screenplay I'm writing. Even though I knew every twist that was coming, this was just as draining an experience as I remember and it's honestly perplexing how well made this movie is on every single front. Each frame from beginning to end is so ripe with masterful filmmaking and tells one hell of a compelling story. Not that there's been any doubt in my mind with this next statement ever since I saw Parasite for the first time, but Bong Joon-ho continues to be one of the most influential filmmakers both of the century and my personal journey into film as a medium. WATCH THIS MOVIE WATCH THIS MOVIE WATCH THIS MOVIE
Tags: love this movie more than myself, criterion blu-ray

Name: Stalker
Review: Criterion Challenge -- a film from the 1970s. Not going to rate this one. Oh lord this was incredible in every imaginable way but it digs into way too many ideas for me to write any comprehensive thoughts on it. My head genuinely hurts and I don't know what to think. Should I cry? Should I laugh? Should I be ecstatic and watch it again immediately, or be afraid and stay away? Add all this to my ADHD and I'll need a rewatch eventually but yeah I'm glad I finally saw my first Tarkovsky. Go in prepared to be focused for 3 hours, I don't think I fully was.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2021, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Get Shorty
Review: 7.5 This is a movie my parents have been wanting me to watch for a while and it's pretty good! Interesting how I've seen a pretty good number of LA-centric films this month, and Get Shorty only continues that trend. The screenplay is much sharper and more intelligent than I expected, and it really carried the movie along; at times it almost felt Coen-esque. It's rather well directed and edited at that, so my expectations overall were very exceeded. At times I thought this might've ended up being really great, though there are a few moments where I felt the runtime (even though it was only 100 minutes long) and just wasn't as interested, but all around this was entertaining as hell and I'm glad my parents gave it a recommendation. I enjoyed it a lot, and it's a nice change in pace from a lot of what I've watched recently.
Tags: hbo max, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Bo Burnham: Inside
Review: Why do I try anymore, I'll never meet the standard Bo Burnham sets for white dudes

Name: Zola
Review: 6.5 One of the most uncomfortable movies I've ever sat through, holy shit. This is fully intentional obviously given the actual story, though the movie itself felt like a worse Tangerine. Imagine the same insane energy and sleazy style (with a lot of the same positives) but with really bad pacing. Despite this, I had kind of a blast watching this in a theaters with three stoners in the row in front of me... it was one hell of an experience. I'll add more thoughts once I've had some time to sit on it. If you love movies in the same vein as Tangerine or the works of Harmony Korine this is totally up your alley. Also Robin Thicke's Blurred Lines was playing on the radio once I got back in the car and I'm surprised the movie didn't use the song. It fits in the most horrible way.

Name: Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon
Review: 7.5 Liked this a lot but definitely appreciate the visual merit and choreography much more than the narrative itself. I didn't care too deeply about any character and was far more enthralled by how every fight was directed. My favorite part is by far the editing, Tim Squyres deserved one hell of a raise after this movie. Worthy of a recommendation for sure, though in a year full of amazing Asian cinema (Yi Yi, In the Mood for Love, Battle Royale, Joint Security Area, etc.) this one doesn't stand out for me.

Name: After Life
Review: 10 After Life is about life. After Life is about death. After Life is a story of a community full of people who are making one decision that will determine how their eternity will play out, and the emotional range of the narrative is bigger than I could've imagined. With a film so focused on amazing commentary with mortality, purpose and society, I was expecting some pretty heart-wrenching material. And while there is some of this, I somehow found an amazing new comfort film as well. I could talk about how Koreeda's style is just so human but I guarantee you've heard that before. The screenplay for After Life is exactly this, but it's so much more. The story is brilliantly postmodern, and the dialogue is some of the most natural in any movie I've ever seen. There are plenty of screenplays I've heard that match this description as well, most notably from Yasujiro Ozu, Richard Linklater and Noah Baumbach, but this still feels just so authentic. It's incredible and worthy of study; every line is thought-provoking and memorable as can be, to the point where I found myself considering which one memory I would take with me after I die. And of course it goes without saying that the directing and technical filmmaking are amazing as well, complementing the truly wonderful characters and setting to the furthest extent. Death is an incredibly sensitive topic to me and it's not something I like to think about. However, After Life combines the warmth and melancholia of death as a subject, to the point where this movie will stay firm in my memory for quite some time. It is undoubtedly a new favorite and I can only see it rising further in my rankings upon a second watch; I'll sleep well tonight knowing this movie has found its way into my life.

Name: Summer of Soul (...Or, When the Revolution Could Not Be Televised)
Review: 9.5 Amazing in every sense of the word. The cultural event itself is as vibrant as anything I've ever seen, and the movie does it justice. Questlove did an incredible job directing, and I was transfixed for the entire 2 hours... the movie didn't even feel that long. At one point I almost started applauding a band because it was just that immersive. I don't think I've ever seen a film quite like this one and it'll probably be a long time until I see another. I'm so happy I saw this on a theater and I already want to watch it again. Genuinely can't decide between a 9.5 and a 10... I'd recommend this to pretty much everyone in my life, on the biggest screen possible. It's mindblowing, plain and simple.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Ready Player One
Review: 4.0 Was on TV so I joined my parents in watching this. It's really badly written with a ton of lazy plotlines and stupid dialogue and there are a ton of messy scenes but I didn't completely hate it. Some scenes were fun and there's something about the story I like and can't put my finger on but overall yeah it's just not a good movie. Although Spielberg's made like three films that aren't forgettable Oscar bait in the last 25 years so I wouldn't mind seeing more movies like this one from him. This is a really bland review compared to what I normally write but I'm tired and feel kinda bad overall so yeah sorry this is what I can do right now. Plus I only really half-watched the movie.

Name: The Purge: Anarchy
Review: 3.5 A liiiitle better than the first one but still bad. The first half hour was actually very promising but the rest feels like a montage of missed opportunities. Stupid plot with vanilla characters and a lot of plot conveniences punctuated by cheesy dialogue. A great film to put on in the background while you do other things. The next few movies in this franchise will be one hell of a drag to get through.

Name: The Conjuring 2
Review: 7.0 I enjoyed this one quite a bit! It's not much of a formal sequel with the exception of the two detectives, but I'd still say this surpasses the original. Most notably, the story and characters are more fleshed out than those of the previous movie, and it's much easier to connect to what's happening. Wan's directing is very good especially with the performances, namely all the kids who were very convincing. The plot feels a little bit repetitive at times and the end is slightly anticlimactic, but I was definitely never bored. The dialogue and sound mixing aren't anything spectacular but they get the job done. I'm not sure what to think of the cinematography, since it's entirely digital which for the most part is enough to pass since the production design is pretty solid, though a few shots looked too digital. I would've preferred they shot this on 35 mm, both to capture more of the 1970s London atmosphere and so there would be less opportunities for the CGI (a fair amount looks rather unconvincing) but it could've been worse. On the CGI note, when it appeared it didn't look horrible, but it was still fairly obvious, especially the main villain guy who just looked like Jack Skellington in a velvet suit. Overall though, this is better than most mainstream horror movies I've seen and I had a lot of fun with it so I'm glad I finally got around to checking this out... even if it's a little long.

Name: Shiva Baby
Review: 8.5 There are some technical flaws and it took me a bit of time to get into things, but once I did, oh boy. This is a great film. An emotional roller coaster from beginning to end with an excellent screenplay, really solid direction, and not a single bad performance. I completely believed every character and event that happened, and the plot unfolds in an incredibly clever manner. I regret not watching this any sooner, especially given how much I frequented virtual cinemas last year. I certainly didn't mind forking over a few bucks to watch it, but hopefully it finds its way onto a streaming service so even more people can give it a try.

Name: Black Widow
Review: 5.5 Saw this at an exclusive early screening for the employees at my theater and it was really hype despite running from 1-3:30 AM... I'm grateful I made it home safe without falling asleep behind the wheel. Not gonna make a lot of friends for my opinion on this I think, so I'd like to reiterate that if you loved this that's your opinion and that's great. I however didn't. I was interested enough to stay engaged the whole time and the runtime felt about 30 minutes shorter than it actually was. But with a character as dark and complex as Natasha Romanoff, I was expecting some unique things to be happening, and while there were some very good moments I've already forgotten most of it and by the end of the plot I felt pretty underwhelmed by how cleanly things were resolved. It's very safe and standard and doesn't have very much of a point. There's nothing really notable about the filmmaking either; it was mostly just passable but I was surprised by the high amount of CGI even for Marvel and this is more of a personal thing for me, but as a cinematography nut I was left really disappointed. My favorite part of the movie would be Florence Pugh, I was always entertained by her presence.

Name: Bad Trip
Review: 6.0 I enjoy the hidden camera comedy genre enough but I wasn't really feeling this one. It's well made at the very least. Maybe I'm just not in the right mood to enjoy it today, but I really didn't care that much to give it my full attention at any point. I do enjoy Eric Andre's TV show as well, which I find very funny, but he really didn't push any boundaries here compared to that. I don't know... I'm going to sleep now good night.

Name: I Killed My Lesbian Wife, Hung Her on a Meat Hook, and Now I Have a Three-Picture Deal at Disney
Review: I know you most likely have 16 minutes to spare at some point during your day. I'd like to suggest you spend that precious time watching this truly... unique piece of short filmmaking.

Name: Genre
Review: Not philosophical or emotional like other Hertzfeldt creative works but I still absolutely loved this. A simple but effective postmodern short that packs tons of entertainment into 5 minutes. This is genuinely one of the funniest things I've seen in a while. I want to make something like this, like right now; I always get some sort of inspiration from watching the things he's made and this is no different.

Name: Unbreakable
Review: Holy shit I loved this. Shyamalan's screenplay was so ahead of its time and has aged like fine wine. The dialogue is really great, the characters emotionally involved me, and everything just feels so real. The camerawork is excellent, every performance hit the mark, the score is beautiful, and overall the direction is just so sharp and grounded; it feels like everyone involved in the production really cared and gave it their best effort. I was fascinated the entire runtime and even the least important characters have something to say. Aside from a few plot details, it's unforgettable and brilliant. I feel drained.

Name: Old
Review: Good enough. Annoying camerawork, stupid plot details and an anticlimactic ending are really hard to look past but I was interested the whole time and cared about the characters to a certain extent. A lot could've been fixed but at the same time it was better than I was expecting.

Name: The Royal Tenenbaums
Review: Another great film from Wes. It's interesting to see him dig even further into his style that we all know today after Rushmore, as there are a lot more of his trademarks in this. It's visually very interesting as expected, has an excellent family dynamic, and all around just a really entertaining narrative. There are a few moments that I felt were very strangely paced and not in favor of emotional payoff which is my main critique, as well as a couple editing decisions, but overall I've been looking forward to this film for quite some time and it didn't disappoint. The middle section is sheer brilliance. Might go down to an 8 but I'm keeping it here for now.
Tags: criterion dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: She's Gotta Have It
Review: Loved this! It's smart, funny, sexy, sometimes goofy sometimes serious, and just really entertaining. Spike Lee has always had a gift in writing engaging screenplays that transition from each scene to the next incredibly well. For a first feature, it's stunning. The dance scene around the first third in was excellent. I genuinely cared about everything that was happening and would gladly watch it again.

Name: Pig
Review:
First movie theater of my LA trip!

Sadly the movie didn't really live up for me. Cage did a good job and it was never really boring but I also had a lot of trouble caring a huge amount. It has weird pacing too which doesn’t help and overall I'm really not going to take anything out of watching this. I predicted a lot of plot points that seem to be hitting harder for others. There aren't any TERRIBLE flaws but I just wasn't feeling it.

Name: Weathering with You
Review:
Really good movie. Amazing animation and some really good music complement the characters and story incredibly well. It took me a little bit of time to get into it, and there are some moments of really bad pacing, but all around I was emotionally with it and enjoying myself a lot. Seeing it on the big screen was amazing.

Oh and I saw it at some random Chinese theater in LA but I doubt you've heard of it.

Name: Jungle Cruise
Review:
How did this $200 million budget end up feeling like a B movie?

I think the editors forgot to remove some outtakes from the final cut.

I tried to have as much fun as I could've with it, I was able to for the first 20-30 minutes. But then it felt like it went on for a few more hours and got old real fast.

At least my date liked it.

Name: The Green Knight
Review: Really enjoyed this one. Stunning production design and the performances really bring the story to life, as well as a number of great shots. The immersion was broken a few times for me, at times by occasional dull pacing, others by overly digital cinematography (I sound like Sean Baker when I say that), but all around I had a good time and I'm glad I finally saw it. I feel like there are a number of details that flew over my head though, so it deserves a second watch sometime.

Name: Trainspotting
Review: 8.5 I've been interested in this movie for a long time so I was hoping my expectations wouldn't get the best of me, and for the most part they didn't. It's really good, from the exceptional dialogue to the way Danny Boyle brings the novel to life. There were times when the pacing felt weird and a few moments when the surrealism didn't work for me, but all around I was pretty engulfed for the runtime. The range between stress and humor is really broad and pulled off well; at times I was really laughing, others my heart pounding. It's a very intelligent narrative and Boyle's work as a director is super solid here. This isn't a perfect movie, but it's certainly going to stick in my mind for a good while.

Name: The Suicide Squad
Review: Had a blast with this. I'm a sucker for over the top gory action, so combine that with admirable filmmaking, the story that I actually cared about, and how it felt to me like James Gunn's deconstruction of modern superhero movies (mainly the first Avengers), this is a winner and honestly one of the best films of the year so far. I'll probably be seeing it again. Considering moving it up to an 8.

Name: Kids
Review:
How did that McLovin looking guy fuck so many bitches what the hell

I love the ambition behind the film and it was executed very well. From a filmmaking and writing standpoint, this is very much my thing so I'm glad it didn't disappoint. For Larry Clark's first project as a director, he did a really good job and clearly worked well with everyone on set. The performances were mostly good but a few of them frankly weren't, mainly in line delivery. Harmony Korine's first screenplay is a very impressive one as well, keeping me engaged all 90 minutes and he creates a web of characters that I was always interested in. It develops each tone super well and the immersion is consistently on point. Otherwise, I really don't like some story decisions, but I'm glad I gave this a chance and I definitely see myself watching it again in the future.

Name: The Kid with a Bike
Review: I loved this so much, to the point that as I'm writing this log I just don't know where to start. The cinematography is amazing, in both the way it's styled and the shaky movements which I feel elevate Cyril and how he perceives every situation. The screenplay is simple on the surface but does a great job at developing the city, every character, and how they all mesh together to form the story... and somehow all this was fit into less than 90 minutes. It's funny, sad, stressful, relieving, and an overall nearly perfect film for the scale it's on. I'm tempted to give it the bump up to a 10.

Name: Melancholia
Review: Incredibly well done and beautiful to look at, though I think it peaks in the first 5 minutes. The rest of the film is really good of course, with some great monologues and entertaining scenes, though my attention span wasn't as consistent. The pacing felt pretty weird overall, and there were times when I didn't find this to be an issue but certain moments I think should've made me feel stronger than they did (the ending in particular). I love the themes Lars was going for here but I don't think I picked up on everything. Loved the music and performances, especially Charlotte Gainsbourg. My favorite Lars von Trier of the three I've seen but that wasn't too terribly high a bar.

Name: The Killing of a Sacred Deer
Review:
What if the real sacred deer is the friends we made along the way?

As a whole this is a really great film with a lot to love that kept me hooked nearly the whole time with its amazing screenplay, cinematography and performances, but as it goes on I was starting to feel it less. I'm still kinda processing what happened so these definitely aren't my final thoughts but I found the end to be a bit... unsatisfying? I feel weird describing in that way since this is such a layered story but everyone has said that the movie only gets better on rewatch (whenever that'll be for me). It's brilliant and very worth a recommendation, despite how weird it is, but I think I missed some details. No idea what to score it right now.
Tags: blu-ray, w/ dad, watched while sick

Name: Gummo
Review: A really intriguing and bizarre piece of low budget filmmaking that kept my interest the entire time with its unique style, but not one that I necessarily see myself thinking about months out from now. There really aren't any flaws for what it is, and I can see why some people love it so much, I just didn't fall for it that way. It grew on me more throughout the runtime. Not really going to go into any more detail since I feel this is a movie best experienced going in blind. About on the same league as Kids, though not quite as good for me personally.

Name: No Sudden Move
Review:
The only sudden moves I made were almost falling asleep halfway through and cringing every time fish-eye lens were used.


I was going to call it this year's stale Oscar-bait equivalent to Molly's Game, Vice, The Two Popes or Trial of the Chicago 7 but then I remembered that Adam McKay has a new movie coming out later this year so I'm not going to make that statement yet.

Name: I Saw the Devil
Review: Kim Jee-woon saw Memories of Murder and said, "Okay, let's do this... but extremely violent!" I don't think it's quite as strong a film as Bong's, but still packed one hell of an experience. As expected, it's brilliantly made with stunning performances and great music and up until the end which felt like it went on for quite a while, I wasn't bored for a second. Weirdly, I don't think it was quite as disturbing as I had built it up to be in my mind, but that's not to say that this is for the faint of heart either. Still, it's going to stick in my mind for a while and is all around an excellent movie with interesting characters and an insane story that could only be pulled by someone like Jee-woon. I can't say I'd recommend it to everyone thanks to some of the brutal shit, but if you're a fan of New Korean Cinema then this is completely up your alley. It features all the traits you've come to know from the movement and then some. Could easily go up to a 9, and I'm tempted to do that, but I think there's too much to think about on first viewing for me to give it that edge.

Name: Candyman
Review: Honestly that blew me away. An amazing modern take on the original movie that's easily one of the year's best so far, a complement on par with the 2018 Suspiria. It's amazingly well made, feels super lived in, has stunning performances, and had me HOOKED from start to finish (I'm so funny I know). With Cabrini-Green long gone from the first one, Nia DiCosta and Jordan Peele were tasked with finding a new centerpoint for a symbol the character represents, and as expected, there are a lot of themes involving racism and gentrification. The main character is a freelance painter with a lot of his work focused on this, and there are several monologues early on that have a lot more meaning later on. The screenplay is very layered and really exceeded my expectations. While I wouldn't say it quite reaches the scary heights of the original, there are plenty of great scares. None of the kills felt out of place, the makeup FX are incredible (the Candyman costume is insane), and some brought genuine chills. The cinematography adds a lot to this as well, with tons of great shots that build tension... oh and if you thought the use of mirrors in Black Swan is amazing? This movie does it even better. On top of that, the performances from Yahya Abdul-Matee II and Colman Dominigo are stunning in this respect. There are very few flaws with this film, but in particular there are some strange transitions and pacing in a few spots. The ending specifically feels like a buzzkill; it sets up an incredible chain reaction of a conclusion that ends abruptly and clichéd. This is really the only main complaint I have; it isn't enough to ruin a lot of what makes this movie so satisfying and complex, though it did take some of the sting out of my initial reaction. Overall Candyman is a big achievement and hugely exceeded my expectations. I truly believe this is one of the films of the year and it deserves a lot of attention. It's arguably the best horror film in 2 years and I can't wait to hear what everyone else has to say. It'll undoubtedly cause a lot of discussion and have mixed opinions, but that's the beauty of movies like this.

Name: Ida
Review: This is so well made and beautiful. Very few visual details look out of place, the settings all feel very lived in and for the most part I cared about what was happening (aside from a few times I lost focus), but it almost feels like a diluted version of a longer, more fleshed out story. A ton was fit into 82 minutes and I think the pacing would've benefit from more development; during the movie I was really locked in and on board with everything going on but I think after a bit of time the movie will kinda fade from my memory. Let's hope I'm wrong.

Name: School of Rock
Review: I want Richard Linklater to direct my school year.

Name: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
Review:
All around a solid movie. The first half is kinda bad and at a certain point I was close to checking out but the second half definitely makes up for it. It feels more inspired than most Marvel movies and at certain points I was actually very impressed with what I was seeing but not without its issues.

Technically speaking, the CGI was very distracting and I'm surprised that for a movie like this a lot of things didn't look more natural, but there is a lot of good cinematography here. It's well edited and well directed as well, and Tony Leung did a really great job. Fight choreography is super engaging as well.

On top of that, and this is not as prominent but still worth mentioning, is the minimal Asian cinema references. I read in a review that a lot of shots are similar to the style of Zhang Yimou (who I've always been intrigued by) and there's a fight scene that's shot very similarly to Oldboy's hammer scene and I was so hyped at that moment.

The screenplay is where my huge issues lie. The dialogue is SO FULL of comic relief and 90% of the time it didn't land for me, and I could definitely tell it had the touch of corporate meddling. It still has the typical Marvel easily-resolved ending and predictable plot points and some pacing that just didn't work for me at all. And while I cared enough about the characters, the film is so full of repeated points that at a few times I forgot that the story was even advancing.

In total I was satisfied and am looking forward to more of the future of Marvel. The movie isn't without its flaws and the first half is where most of my criticism lies but it's worth a trip to the theater and it's a step in the right direction for this franchise.

Name: First Reformed
Review:
what






Edit: I feel stupid leaving my review at that but that was my initial reaction.

The first hour and 20 minutes were perfect in every way. Like, holy shit. It feels like a movie I could learn filmmaking from. The last half hour is still really great but I'm too dumb to understand whatever it all means. I have some ideas, but overall it was both frustrating and heartstopping... give me some time.

Name: Malignant
Review:
UPDATE - I think I was in a bad mood this day


A malignant waste of 11 bucks and 2 hours.

It feels like an amateur's take on James Wan's past horror films, with elements of each, thrown into a story with characters I didn't give a shit about, filmmaking that made me laugh, and not a single scare that made my heartbeat faster than my resting pulse. Felt like I was there for at least 2.5 hours and only got more frustrated. There's no memorable character development until the last half hour and even then it mostly goes to waste just like that. I almost cared about some of the action happening towards the end but then it all goes to shit. I walked out before it finished.

Fuck this movie. I didn't think I'd say this about something that came out in 2021, but it's far more worth your time to watch this at home on HBO Max than to go to a theater.
Tags: amc naperville 16, me time!, spooky season 2021, walked out

Name: The Card Counter
Review: Frankly I found it boring and not doing very much with the interesting characters for the most part. Oscar Isaac is complex but he doesn't feel like a Paul Schrader character until the second half. Feels like a screenplay that Schrader wrote for a paycheck more than one he's really passionate about; there's nothing groundbreaking or memorable for the first hour and a half, aside from some poker-related dialogue. Every time the tension builds in a scene or something interesting happens, it just ends up slowing down and going back to the usual shtick a few moments later and this cycle keeps repeating. The last 15 minutes provide a very Schrader ending that's engaging and satisfying and I got super locked in all of a sudden, but unfortunately that's the only thing I'll remember from this months out.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ mom, w/ dad

Name: The Evil Dead
Review: I was expecting to love this, given how much I love low budget movies, and I was not disappointed at all. The camerawork, production design and practical effects are incredible. A lot of the gorier scenes seriously hold up today and made me feel more uneasy than most modern horror. The performances aren't all at a high level but I certainly believed each character. The screenplay is the weakest part of the film for sure, both in dialogue and pacing, but that's my only big flaw here. Watching it at 10 PM after a long day only added to the experience. Glad I could finally check this out as the (very late) start to my spooky season this year; very tempted to bump the rating up to a 4.5.

Name: Casino Royale
Review: "Christ, I miss the Cold War." I'm not a huge fan of the character or Bond franchise but my parents absolutely are, and I'm at least interested enough to go through all the Craig entries with them before No Time to Die comes out. I far prefer this darker re-invented style over what was previously thought of James Bond, and I think it works really well the whole movie, though the second half is just so much better than the first IMO. Pretty much every performance is really good and the action is well choreographed, but I wish Mikkelsen had more of an actual arc and the romance is forgettable until the very end; nonetheless the screenplay is still really solid. I was also really interested by the discussion of MI6 and their international relations at the beginning, I wish this took on more of a subplot. Overall a slightly underwhelming rewatch but I still did have fun (and I haven't been having nearly as much fun with movies recently as I'd like).

Name: Rocky
Review:
Watched in class.

Close to 3 stars.

Name: The Forest of Love
Review: Holy shit what a movie. It's disturbing, scary, funny, sad, and overall just not for the faint of heart. The story is impossible to predict and I was fully invested for the entire runtime. Sion Sono's style may not be for everyone but it just hits all the right notes for me. This isn't even my first film I've seen from him, but I'm still just not sure what to type here, I really do feel like I just watched something fresh and I'm struggling to put most of my thoughts into words; I feel so emotionally drained from all the characters went through and by how much it all exceeded my expectations. Aside from a few story details I'm still confused on, this is an amazing film, a new favorite as to be expected, and I can't wait to watch it again.

Name: A Trip to the Moon
Review: I liked the part where they went to the moon

Name: Santa Sangre
Review: In the same league as Antichrist, mother!, and Enter the Void as movies that have made me nauseous. It's so well made and nails nearly every scene but I'd never recommend it to anyone. There are a number of things that I'm sure I didn't pick up on, and I don't want to willingly re-watch this, but I'm sure I will one day. I feel like a sick person giving it this high of a rating but goddamn it's amazing psychological horror.

Name: Funny Games
Review:
Film is art. Art is personal. This is a film by Michael Haneke, whom I am now afraid of.

Perfect meta, from the screenplay to the performances, and it only gets better as it goes on. One of the best movies that only has one location as well, using this better than most directors could. It breaks pretty much every rule of horror movies but still manages to be scary and will stick with me for a while.

Name: The Great Train Robbery
Review: Devious lick

Name: The Kid
Review: Very well made, engaging and enjoyable

Name: Evil Dead II
Review:
Really enjoyable as expected, about on par with the first one for me. I liked the more fleshed out / focused story and the practical effects are just as good (sans the green screens but this was 1987), with an improved screenplay though at the same time, after a certain point, I felt like I was just watching the first Evil Dead again. Both movies are great, don't get me wrong, but even just weeks out from now I don't think I'll be able to differentiate the two.


(This review is really confusing I'm sorry, I finished the movie at like 11:40 PM last night then immediately fell asleep. It's 6:40 AM right now so I'm trying my best to write something readable)

Name: Raw
Review: No thoughts, stomach empty. I need some time to process all that but what the fuck did I just watch? It's brilliant and layered and terrifying and just... so well done. Throughout the whole film, it was seriously on track to be one of my favorite movies period, and maybe even my new #1 horror movie. While it didn't overtake The Shining, Possession or The Lighthouse, it's still an amazing movie that I connected to and is a lot smarter than I expected. French Extremity in the most creative form I've seen from the movement. Plot spoilers (I didn't want to mark the entire review as spoilers since it's just this one point) There's really nothing wrong with the movie itself, though the transition in focus from Justine to Alexia's cannibalism towards the end felt pretty out of place with the pace and left me with more questions than I would've liked for the film to be a full 10/10. It fits the tone and is as shocking and grotesque as the rest of the movie, so I still really liked this section and think it's necessary to the whole story, but I wish it was integrated better.

Name: Titane
Review: I genuinely can't tell if the film was genius or complete garbage but I left the theater feeling pissed off and confused and just... not feeling the film at all. After seeing so many people rave about this film and having watched Raw earlier today + anticipating this ever since it won the Palme D'or, I am unfathomably disappointed. UPDATE after a few days -- removing rating since the film is growing on me and I've recognized that I watched this while in a bad headspace. I'll give it a second try sometime.

Name: Venom
Review: The Mad Max Mathers LP The stupidity of it all held my attention and makes the movie so bad it's good, but at that same rate it's a big waste of talent given who's involved, annoying me more than anything else. Matthew Libatique's camerawork stuck out like a sore thumb given the quality of the rest of the film, and Riz Ahmed and Jenny Slate deserved better. And while I was expecting the screenplay, CGI and editing to be shit, I was surprised by how little effort Michelle Williams put in to her role; she's kinda really terrible here. Tom Hardy is entertaining at least.

Name: Halloween
Review: I wish I liked this more on my first impression, but it's starting to really grow on me in numerous aspects. Still kinda processing; not blown away but a good movie for sure. It feels like the type of film that'll be even better on second watch. For its low budget, the camerawork is really good and the performances are very solid, backed up by a very good screenplay, though certain scenes just feel really awkward and the sound design is super questionable at times. All around it's effective and it'll stick with me.

Name: Spirited Away
Review:
Holy fuck this was exactly what I needed to brighten up my week. All my senses were heightened with frequent chills, the soundtrack, the animation, the story... I won't forget it. I really don't need to say anymore since it's all been said before. Timeless movie.

Also I relate more to No Face than I'm willing to admit

Name: Double Indemnity
Review: I liked the part where the white men were yelling

Name: Quantum of Solace
Review:
Apparently I saw this in theaters when I was like 5 and my parents told me they couldn't find me or my siblings a babysitter so they just said fuck it. I have no memory of the film but I'll still mark this as a re-watch.


Anyways yes this indeed identifies as a "motion picture." When the film was in production in 2007 there was the WGA strike and a lot of people blame that on how annoying and convoluted this was, and I agree, though Marc Forster clearly wasn't passionate about this either. Don't even get me started on the theme song or atrocious editing.
Tags: dvd, bad editing, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Ex-Shaman
Review: This appeared in my recommended on Netflix and it turns out I'm watching another film from this director at CIFF. Enjoyable overall, a good movie to put on in the background while I work on my college essay. Beautifully made and interesting subject matter, though it doesn't really tell a story; it's more of a collection of people in the tribe talking about their life.

Name: Scream
Review: Awesome experience in theaters and the 4K restoration looked amazing

Name: Lamb
Review: What a colossal letdown. If you're planning on watching this, please avoid the trailer because it's nothing like the real thing... at all. Go in expecting a bizarre family drama. It begins with a beautiful set of long takes that I was really on board with and minimal dialogue. The second act introduces an uncle character who doesn't really have clear intentions but I was still enjoying myself. The story sets itself up for some interesting allegories and I was ready for a great conclusion, but I got anything but that. There's no coherency to the placement of each scene, and it jumps back between locations and characters at what felt completely random. The final scene has a little bit of set up about 20 minutes earlier but by the time it actually happens, I completely forgot about it. Even then, the finale is just so frustrating and left such a bad taste in my mouth. I could barely tell someone what happens the entire final chapter because it bounces so quickly and has no direction. TL;DR - The story takes on a structure with three chapters, with the first two being solid and the final being an absolute disaster that sours the whole thing. Anyways... it's very well shot and the performances are good, and I guess I can appreciate the ambition. Even if the final product annoyed me when I saw it and I've now forgotten most of it despite leaving the theater 30 minutes ago. It very much feels like a directorial debut so I hope he improves upon this. Or goes back to visual effects.

Name: Dheepan
Review: A really good character study that's well made and super engaging, though I understand why people disagreed with this winning the Palme D'or. It has important social commentary and a very solid screenplay, but it also has thinly written supporting characters, weird pacing, and an ending that I was really not on board with.

Name: The Last Forest
Review: More layered than I expected and I was really engaged. Beautiful cinematography as expected. Very similar to Ex-Shaman, but with a much more informative ending. A great way to kick off this year's Chicago Film Festival.

Name: Paris, 13th District
Review: This was doing such an amazing job at developing character and I really connected to the entire story for the first half, kinda reminding me of a less stylized version of She's Gotta Have It. But then character intentions start to change and the pacing gets really disjointed out of nowhere and I kinda stopped caring for a bit. There are a number of themes at play here, and for the most part they're portrayed well, but the story overall lacks consistency or focus. The muddled second half makes up for itself thanks to a very good ending and Noémie Merlant's consistently great performance, but it feels like Audiard made a movie out of an earlier draft of the screenplay. Still worth a watch, but I was pretty disappointed.

Name: Fargo
Review: Weirdly... way better on third watch? Idk why but this vibed with me way more this time around LMAO great movie. Cinematography, story structure, directing, I have no idea what I was missing the first two times but I think... I love this now. Didn't think I'd be saying that about any film in class.

Name: The Worst Person in the World
Review: It's been a while since I've seen a film that comes into and relates to my life at the right time. And while I knew that this would be my kind of movie pretty early on, I wasn't emotionally prepared for what I ended up getting. It's the most human 2021 film I've seen by a mile and nearly perfectly made. Layered screenplay, incredible pacing, great cinematography, beautiful acting, I couldn't get enough of it and genuinely felt sad when the end credits rolled. Maybe I'll feel different on a second watch without the festival crowd around me but this was such a special experience and I know this review is beyond cheesy but I don't care, this movie is a masterpiece.
Tags: amc river east 21, ciff 2021, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Power of the Dog
Review: The festival hype is not exaggerated, this really is a powerhouse of a movie. The cinematography and Jonny Greenwood's score are brilliant, there isn't a single bad performance, and the world building is stunning. Jane Campion knows what the fuck is up and I'm not sure I'll see better direction in a movie this year. The narrative aspect is really good as well, with a very precisely developed web of characters, though I only found any sort of emotional attachment every so often and the ending felt a little too ambiguous given how grounded everything preceding it is. There are definitely a number of tense moments, though I didn't find it gripping like some people did. Despite that, I still was super engaged with every character and the direction the story takes works on a number of levels. This isn't quite a masterpiece in my eyes, but it's a technical accomplishment and I'd recommend it to everyone. I can't wait to see it a second time.
Tags: ciff 2021, amc river east 21, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Cow
Review: Every cow that gave birth in this movie immediately started licking their baby after it came out. Personally I wouldn't do that, pretty gross ngl

Name: Skyfall
Review: I really should've finished the Craig Bond films by now but life has kept me busy. I promise I'll see No Time to Die... at some point. Somehow the only one of the Craig-era films I hadn't seen before recently and... yeah it's the best one. This and Fury Road are how you make a big budget action movie. The work from Deakins, Thomas Newman and Bardem are especially excellent, but everything is genuinely really good and the score is close to a 9. I really do see where the reputation comes from, I truly loved this movie!

Name: Flee
Review: Really great story told in a beautiful way. My final impression isn't quite as emotionally intense as I expected but I was still very moved by everything and I'll be thinking about Amin's story for a while. One that I'd recommend to everyone, definitely one of the best films of the year. I'll definitely give this a second watch once it hits theaters, maybe I'll love it even more then. A great reminder how blessed we all are, and how much so many other people in the world have gone through. I have huge respect for everyone involved in the production of this.
Tags: ciff 2021, virtual cinema, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Hand of God
Review:
I'm going through a lot right now, and this is the film I needed. I can't say exactly why because then I would be venturing into spoiler territory, but even if I did I couldn't do the film justice with words. It's a family drama, coming of age, slice of life, beautiful portrait of a teenage dude who wants to be a filmmaker. So naturally I saw a lot of myself here, but the direction the story takes is so special. The structure and pacing are unconventional so I don't expect everyone to love it, but with the awkwardness of the main guy and his struggle to communicate, I feel like it was intentional. The amount of films I've seen that hit so painfully close to home is a small number, but this cracks that list. By the time the end credits rolled, I had a range of mixed thoughts on the film but I knew it was great. As I walked out of the theater, I realized how much it was actually sticking with me. Once I got to the car, I nearly lost it when it hit me how much it all spoke to me.

Personal connection aside, it's hilarious, engaging from start to finish, beautifully shot, masterfully directed and acted... a movie that seeps with vibrance and personality. I don't think this will be everyone's thing, but I hope everyone gives it a chance. I'll even admit that this isn't a perfect film, but it deserves this rating. Despite all going on in my life, this film restored my outlook on the future and for that I have to give it the edge.
Tags: ciff 2021, amc river east 21, w/ mom, w/ dad

Name: Spectre
Review:
Sam Mendes made another Skyfall but this one isn't nearly as good. Not as bad as some people say and it works relatively well for me but there's also sooo much plot. Still well made though and I'm ready for No Time to Die before it leaves theaters.

Also Writing on the Wall is the best Bond song imma be real
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Halloween
Review: Not a single Halloween film past the 1978 original has had a good director at the helm. I was interested enough by the dynamic between Laurie and the rest of her family and there are some decent murders. But it's not very well made, a lot of the dialogue is pretty bad, and there's barely any creativity to be found. Not horrible, but very forgettable. Close to a 4, but I'm feeling generous today so I'll keep it here for now.

Name: Last Night in Soho
Review:
Original review: 9/10, I really fucking loved it

Edit 6/16/2022: Good vibes and good memories but I'm afraid to rewatch it because the story is really mid

Name: The French Dispatch of the Liberty, Kansas Evening Sun
Review:
Somehow forgot to review this initially my bad


But yeah I loved this. Super Wes Andersony but no way is that a bad thing. Even then he's trying new things in multiple areas, such as the consistent use of both color and B&W. Production and costume designs off the charts, beautiful cinematography, excellent directing, and his commitment to his own craft never disappoints. The first two stories were awesome though the third one didn't quite wow me the same way (still very good). I think Adrien Brody gave my favorite performance in this movie though I'm basically picking a name out of a hat since they all did great. It's also maybe the funniest Wes film so far. Had a huge smile on my face the whole time and got a lot out of the story, characters and commentary on journalism, what more is there to say? Close to 2 years of buildup for this fucking movie and I wasn't disappointed!

Right now there's no excuse to not go to a theater, the lineup of movies playing is truly incredible.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ mom, w/ dad

Name: Halloween Kills
Review:
For a while I actually thought this was decent and works for both what it's trying for and as a Michael Myers continuation story.

Then the last 15 minutes happened and I realized just how stupid this reboot series is.

Name: No Time to Die
Review: I'm sorry, I wanted to like this but just really didn't. It feels like every other Craig Bond film without much of its own creative voice and I'm going to forget about it later this week. Plus it relies so much on Spectre, this and that are going to be hard to distinguish in my memory. I like the inclusion of a daughter and the emotional value with it but that's really the only notable part of the whole movie for me.
Tags: cinemark seven bridges, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Saint Maud
Review:
These next two months I'm gonna do a lot of 2021 catchup and this was a great place to start. Super impressive debut with amazing performances and cinematography. Solid screenplay too with some great monologues. The execution of some scenes didn't quite work for me and the pacing dragged at times but I got a lot out of the movie overall.


And yes I know this isn't technically a 2021 film but I base the "release year" off of when I am first able to watch a movie, and there was no physical way for me to check this out until like January.
Tags: hulu, w/ dad, watched while sick

Name: Mass
Review: The easiest 10/10 I've given in a long time. I'm still out of breath as I was when the end credits rolled. Every aspect of this movie is perfect, and the performances, screenplay, even cinematography and editing are masterful. This is an important film, on a subject matter that could not have been handled more respectfully. I believed that every second of this story was real, and I will remember every part of this for a while and am urging everyone to watch it.

Name: Dune
Review:
Without any detail I was in a horrible headspace the first time I saw this to the point that I really couldn't focus on it at all. While today wasn't technically my first watch, in a way I consider it that.

Visually and technically, god tier. I have no idea how people can text during this in theaters. Ambition behind the whole thing, extremely admirable. Some of the scenes in this are truly mindblowing and I think that helped me pinpoint my favorite thing about the sci-fi genre, that I really can't figure out how most of this movie was pulled off. Acting, very good actually even if I struggled to connect to any one character. Musically, Hans Zimmer doesn't disappoint. Screenplay and pacing... decent but more of a mixed bag. The film left a weird impression on me where I left the theater not really feeling it, but 30 minutes later it's already growing on me. Imma be honest and say I didn't find much of an emotional attachment, but I definitely had a really fun time seeing this with a couple friends and it's a project that I'm incredibly fascinated by and would willingly rewatch to continue figuring it out. And you bet your ass I'm grateful I got to see it in IMAX one more time before Eternals takes over.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ josh k, imax

Name: Rear Window
Review:
Watched in class


My first review from a year ago is incredibly stupid lol but I don't feel like rewriting it. Idk it's a really good movie that I don’t love the end for and doesn't connect to me emotionally. Kinda like literally every film I've watched in class so far lmao

Name: Spencer
Review: "I thought I'd lighten the blue with something totally unexpected." Fascinating and unconventional in the best way possible. A very different but effective portrayal of anxiety thanks to Kristen Stewart's unbelievable performance, a fantastic score, beautiful direction and cinematography. It's a technical masterclass and thinks outside the box, something rarely seen in a biopic these days. Even more surprising for a biopic is how unpredictable this film is; more than a few times my eyes got bigger because of the direction the story took. There isn't any new information presented about the real situation, but the way the film tells it is really special. It's not hard at all to find a connection to at least one part of this movie, and surprise surprise… I'd recommend it to everyone. 2021 is an amazing year for film but you already knew that.
Tags: regal cantera 17, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Polytechnique
Review: For Denis Villeneuve’s first movie in 9 years, he tackled one hell of a subject matter, but in the end I seriously admire him for it. This is a really powerful film. The character development is very minimal given the short runtime, but still manages to be really effective. Aside from a couple issues I had with the screenplay (structure and some dialogue), I don’t have anything else really negative to say. I don’t know enough about Denis to recognize all the early traits of his work, but I can definitely say he’s always been amazing with sound design and working with actors. It’s technically great and emotionally investing overall, but a lot of that has to do with those two aspects. A lot of the images in this will stick with me for a while and I genuinely cared about everything going on. Best of all, Denis took a lot of precautions and made a number of filmmaking decisions to make this film as respectful as possible, and it really pays off. This isn’t a perfect film, and at times I was a little frustrated with the direction things were going, but overall I’m really impressed. Close to a 9.

Name: Disturbia
Review: Michael Haneke has only made slow dramas since 2007 because this probably ruined suspense for him.

Name: Train to Busan
Review: Monsters as symbolism, a train as the main location, a closing scene in a tunnel… this dude loves Bong Joon-ho. A lot of Kim Jee-woon in this too. Great movie.

Name: Hit the Road
