Review: Such a cozy little film with genuinely great cinematography and a super engaging family dynamic backed up by excellent performances. Certain moments that I think were supposed to be more emotional didn't quite work for me, but I had a smile the whole time and it's a super easy film to recommend. It didn't have my full attention but I blame that on how the last couple days have been draining far more than the movie itself, so hopefully I'll try it again some day.

Name: Belle
Review: Beautiful animation, completely believable characters, great voice acting, and the way it uses internet culture as a plot device feels fully natural and not like it’s trying too hard to be relevant. Had some issues with the pacing and lost attention at a few points but overall it’s leaving a lasting impression on me and I had a really good time. Can’t wait for the US release in January.
Tags: music box theater, chicago critics festival, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: In Time
Review: My dad put this on in the background since he was working and I was doing homework and it seemed interesting enough to half-watch. We both regret it.

Name: Heaven: To The Land of Happiness
Review:
Really really good. Not what I expected but not in a bad way at all, in the sense that it hardly has any traits of most of New Korean Cinema but it's still incredibly enjoyable. Choi Min-sik and the supporting cast all did a fantastic job, it was all well shot and directed, and the screenplay just worked really well. One of those movies that manages to have an amazing range of being sad and funny. It's kinda predictable and the ending felt rushed, but I'm really happy I finally got to see this movie and that it didn't disappoint.

Man I love Korean films. I really hope this becomes available in the US at some point.

Name: Belfast
Review: This movie left a really strange effect on me. Ultimately I think it takes a lot after big Oscar winners of the past like Roma and The Pianist, but it doesn’t quite live up to either. It offers great performances (the kid who played Buddy was amazing), beautiful production design and some really impactful sequences. However, the screenplay is good not great, it felt like an Oscar winner for best editing (not a compliment), and the middle section felt pretty unfocused. It feels like a movie that shouldn’t have worked, but once it got to the end I felt a pretty strong connection to the story. Really good movie and definitely not my favorite of the year, but it’s hard to hate. Didn’t really find a place to fit this into the main paragraph but either way I want to go in slight detail with the cinematography which may actually be my least favorite aspect of the movie. I understand why Branagh and his DP went with black and white for most of the film, but the shot composition and editing don’t reflect the style at all. I think that if you take that aspect out, the camerawork wouldn’t nearly be praised as much as it is. The opening scene felt like I was watching a Michael Bay movie, and a lot of the shots were just not well framed IMO. I don’t think the cinematography is bad, but I don’t see why so many people say it’s incredible.
Tags: regal cantera 17, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Mad God
Review: Absolutely demented. This is a film that demands your undivided fucking attention and won't let you go for 80 minutes; my legs were literally jelly by the time the credits rolled. This has some of the sickest (meaning both cool and gross) animation I've ever seen and while there's relatively no dialogue, it still tells a compelling story that I was so invested in and actually has some thematic depth to what it has to say. It's an incredible achievement in... however the hell this thing was made. All I know is that it's a fucking perfect feat in filmmaking, from the cinematography to the production design to the sound. Every scene is directed flawlessly to the last detail; you have to see it to believe it. Parts of this are really wonderful and true eye candy, while others are total nightmare fuel (the scenes in the surgical suite with the baby will haunt me for the rest of my life). You have to see this thing to believe it and I hope everyone does. I want to make a film that accomplishes what this did one day. I will not see a more creative movie this year. Probably not next year either. My mind is blown and I could never have been ready. Only reason it isn't a 10 is because I'm still processing what the hell I just saw.

Name: Marvelous and the Black Hole
Review: "Focus on your act! Have you thought of a story yet?" A really unique coming of age story that's flawed but kept me thoroughly enjoyed. Very funny and charming, well shot and the performances are solid for the most part. Some filmmaking choices I really loved, and a screenplay that was fairly inconsistent but had some great moments. Great debut, I'll be watching for Kate Tsang's future work.

Name: The Adventures of Tintin
Review: My family and I wanted to watch something short since none of us were tired but it was getting late. Having watched a video essay on this movie earlier in the day, I was in the mood for this, and thankfully they picked it. Watching this made me feel like a kid again; while I can recognize that it has problems, I had a smile the whole time and found a lot to appreciate about it. It’s just awesome and so much fun with astonishing background animation, great stunts, a brilliant John Williams score, and genuinely excellent editing. Not to mention some really solid voice acting and a screenplay that feels pretty rushed in a number of places, but is really funny, unpredictable, and has the Edgar Wright touch. Better yet, it’s really faithful to the original comics. I saw this film three times when it was in theaters and I was 8, I’ve had a poster in my room for 10 years now, and I’ve seen it multiple times since then but not for a few years. It makes me feel nostalgic but not in a sad way; watching what was my favorite movie for a while in elementary school, it just kinda hit the spot. An absolute joy that I should revisit more often, and a great example of the type of kids movie I’d want to make. By far my personal favorite, maybe the objective best Spielberg film of the 21st century.

Name: Eternals
Review:
The first hour = completely exposition and I got bored pretty quick
The second hour = still exposition but I actually felt the story was going somewhere and I was fairly interested
The final half hour = oh dear

I really appreciate that this felt like more of a standalone Marvel movie than most others, but I was really only onboard for a little while. It’s visually interesting and has the best Marvel VFX since 2019, but the screenplay repeats itself so often and introduces big ideas but never delves into them, sacrificing this for more comic relief and buildup without much satisfaction. The characters started to get really interesting but in the final half hour most of their arcs end up not making much sense. In the end it’s a movie that’s trying new things, thanks to what creative control Zhao had and a completely new set of characters, but still feels like an addition to the MCU thanks to the dialogue, story progression, and how it feels like a lot of potential went unused.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: C'mon C'mon
Review: “Are you laughing or crying? I’m not sure, I want to have the appropriate reaction.” Flawless. Perfect. A film that really makes me feel enlightened and like I learned a thing or two about myself, thanks to Mike Mills’ unbelievably human and masterful writing. The directing, editing, and camerawork are all top notch. Joaquin Phoenix and Woody Norman give two of the year’s best performances. With each new scene I became more invested and felt the real emotion behind what I was seeing and hearing. This is the type of film that I want to make. An easy recommendation for everyone, instant 10/10, new all time favorite.
Tags: amc river east 21, love this movie more than myself, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Eternally Younger Than Those Idiots
Review: Beautiful. Incredibly well made with great characters that offers a lot of emotional value. A simple premise on paper, but it works so well and hooked me the whole time. Pacing isn't the best, my attention was lost at a couple points, but that's the only reason I have it at a high 8 and not a 9. I really hope it finds some sort of US distribution so more people can see it.

Name: The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
Review: Forgot to log this last week lol whoops but I watched this in class for the first time since it came to theaters. Held up pretty well tbh, the surreal scenes mostly worked for me and I was interested the whole time. Not an unforgotten masterpiece but very enjoyable.

Name: The Handmaiden
Review:
Two years of buildup and sky high expectations led me to this very moment. It feels weird to finally say I've seen this movie, but I did.

And it was fucking fantastic.

It's only been a few minutes since the end credits rolled but I can't physically put my thoughts into words. This film is tremendous in every sense of the word. 145 minutes and not once was I ever bored or did I feel like the film was losing its direction. It's absolutely gorgeous with its Kurosawa-esque visuals and unbelievable production design and cinematography, every performance was brilliant, the screenplay is... holy fuck it's so perfect nothing I say will do it justice. Broken into a three act structure, it keeps getting better and better and by the time the film is over, you take a step back and look at the final product and the amount of times Park was able to misdirect you is astonishing. Absolutely mindblowing storytelling. I want and need to watch this many more times because this is a masterpiece. I truly don't believe there are many (if any) directors working right now that can rival Park Chan-wook.

(watched with Skyler)
Tags: amazon prime, virtual watch party, w/ skyler

Name: Shutter Island
Review: This movie is so filmbro-core and Scorsese-centric it’s not even funny. If you think this a masterpiece or one of your favorites then I’m running away from you because this is a red flag kind of movie.

Name: Venom: Let There Be Carnage
Review: Veninem 2 LOL I’m 2 months late but this is somehow still playing in theaters. After a long and frankly awful week I needed something simple and fun like this. And it was just that! Yeah it’s a comic book movie but recently I’ve found myself more interested in them than usual and I had a good time. Kinda similar in quality level overall to the first one but it’s funnier, the character motivations had me more engaged, and Andy Serkis is a stronger director than Ruben Fleischer. Also it was unironically way gayer than I expected which is an automatic plus for me. Robert Richardson deserves better, the first 15 minutes felt especially messy and this isn’t very well paced like… at all, but it was a nice break in pace from what else I’ve been watching recently. If the circumstances were different I wouldn’t have liked this as much but I’m glad I did. Stupid fun.

Name: Junior
Review: Julia Ducournau has never missed. 20 minutes of unpredictable body horror and a simple but easy to connect with web of characters.

Name: Titane
Review: I still think this movie manages tonal shifts pretty badly, and a number of shots (particularly in the first half hour) feel super gross just to be gross without contributing to the final product, but I admit this was far better on second watch. On paper, none of it should work but it all comes together really well. Adrien is an amazingly well developed character on multiple fronts, it's beautifully shot for the most part, unforgettable, and leaves me with a lot to think about, this time with what it all means rather than just my opinion of the film overall. And holy fuck the ending really struck a chord with me this time. Not quite a masterpiece and not very re-watchable, but I have a lot of admiration for what Ducournau made.

Name: Spider-Man
Review: First time watching in years. Most of the performances, Raimi’s direction, the pacing, and the comic book style really elevate this for me. Super fun film, easy to re-watch and the influence it had on comic book movies over the years is very apparent.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Spider-Man 2
Review: Genuinely great movie. Raimi utilizes a lot more of a comic book style visually than he did in the first one, I cared a lot more about the chemistry between characters, and everything else I said good about the first one still stands here. I wasn't really blown away by anything and there were a couple moments that left me scratching my head but I get the hype (I thought I had seen this one before but I actually don't recall most of these scenes from previous years so I think this was my first time watching it)
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: The Matrix
Review:
I’ve seen at least half this film before probably in montages, parodies and various videos, but this was still an insane first watch holy fuck. I had really high expectations and I was still floored. This excels in every aspect and is a masterclass in a handful. 22 years later and it still has some of the best visuals in literally any movie I’ve ever seen. The control over the craft and story is amazing and it’s executed to near perfection. And let’s be real, the movie ends with a cliche that usually takes me out of things but I don’t even care. May go down to a 9 but for now, based on my reaction, it deserves the full 10. Fuck… my brain is still back at the theater. This… man, this is a movie that reminds me why I’m going into film. I’ve had a weekend of exposing myself to creativity fuel and inspiration, and this tops that off. I loved The Matrix… so much. One of the best theater experiences of my life.

Saying the Wachowskis knocked this out of the park is an understatement. I can’t wait to see all the great films they made later in their career!
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Spider-Man 3
Review:
I actually enjoyed this tbh. The battle scenes are all kinda ass, this isn't a very good looking film and it's all over the place the entire time, but the main plot itself about Peter becoming Bully Maguire was actually done really well, it's one of the best-directed sequences of the whole trilogy. If the screenplay had a little more focus and didn't try to bite off more than it could chew, I would place it above the first one.


Also I know the whole message of the film is that you shouldn't be filled with hate and revenge, but this movie gave me new reasons to hate both Venom movies and Joker. All for the better tbh.
Tags: dvd rental, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, library

Name: The Shape of Water
Review: 9.0 This is a movie I've been putting off ever since it came out; I wanted to see it in theaters because it won best picture, but I was only 14 at the time and my parents wouldn't let me. Then the movie didn't really cross my mind again until I started seriously getting into movies, and even during a long stint where I was watching the highest rated movies of the 2010s (specifically best picture nominees), I didn't get around to watching this. Four years and a lot of hype for Nightmare Alley later, here we are. And yeah okay wow this was amazing. The production design, cinematography, editing, and Desplat's score are simply fantastic, keeping me dazzled the whole time. The performances are all incredible as well, with Octavia Spencer giving a career best and everyone else seriously transforming into their roles; I genuinely forgot I was watching Sally Hawkins for a while. Del Toro's direction and screenplay are spotless, creating the type of magical story only he could that's very fantastical but still down to earth and easy to connect with. There are a few sequences throughout the movie that are more dialogue focused between some of the smaller characters that I wasn't very invested with, but aside from that I had a wonderful time with this movie. Guillermo really is one of the best working filmmakers today, both on a technical level and in terms of bringing his stories to life. I loved it.
Tags: blu-ray rental, w/ dad, w/ quinn, library

Name: Starlet
Review: 7.5 While not Sean Baker's best film by any means, I still found this really good, bordering on great at points. It features a lot of the traits I've come to know and love about him and his unique slices of life, complete with great performances and natural dialogue which really bring his flawed characters into beautiful fruition, handheld cinematography, and high energy, off-the-rail moments that are both hilarious and at times hard to watch. I felt the runtime at a few points, and the story structure doesn’t feel quite as rewarding or satisfying as Tangerine or The Florida Project, but I enjoyed myself quite a bit. Maybe a low 8.

Name: Stand by Me
Review: 8.5 If I saw this film like 4-5 years ago it would’ve come into my life at the time where I’d relate to it on a huge level and declare it an instant favorite. Even today though I loved it; a simple slice of life with great characters and a really engaging story that I always cared about. I think as it got going I was expecting to feel like REALLY invested by the end and I walked away saying that I really liked it without being blown away, but this also feels like the type of movie to grow on me (same thing happened with Trainspotting). And of course the filmmaking and performances were excellent. Update 1 year later -- I was right. It aged like Trainspotting. I need to watch this again really really soon

Name: Nightmare Alley
Review: Just take away my Letterboxd at this point.
Tags: kendall 11, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: The Amazing Spider-Man
Review: 6.0 I’m trying to fit in all four remaining Spider-Man movies before No Way Home tonight so I’m not going to be giving a lot of thoughts but this was more fun than I remember! Very flawed but I enjoyed it overall tbh

Name: The Amazing Spider-Man 2
Review: 4.0 I didn’t hate this as much as I thought but it’s bad.

Name: Spider-Man: Homecoming
Review: 6.5 I'm glad I've been able to come at least a little more around on Marvel recently, and if I needed any proof of that, this was quite a bit better than I remember. It doesn't hook me like Raimi's trilogy, and around the middle it peters out and starts to feel a little tedious, but it's a more unique take on the Spider-Man character than any of the other movies preceding this one (both a good and bad thing, slightly more good). Maybe I'm a little biased since I'm watching this immediately after Amazing Spider-Man 2, but I like this movie now. Upper half MCU, and just a little below the 2002 Spider-Man (Dafoe > Keaton, and the apartment scene gives it the edge).

Name: Spider-Man: Far From Home
Review: 6.0 Didn't dislike this one as much as I have previously either. It feels more like another MCU entry than Homecoming (I liked how Homecoming felt more standalone), and Mysterio isn't the greatest villain, but I had fun with this. Can't decide between a 5 or 6 but I'm in a good mood and anticipating No Way Home in one hour so I'll give it the benefit for now.

Name: Spider-Man: No Way Home
Review: If Sam Raimi never directed Evil Dead, we wouldn’t be here. I’m still gathering my thoughts, especially with the ending (not in a bad way at all) and pacing (meh), but I liked it… a lot, actually! Took me a bit of time to really get into it but it’s one of the MCU’s best and in competition with Spider-Man 2 for the best movie on the character. In the end, I’m still processing everything that happened and I really do love the ambition. It’s great, the first Marvel movie in years that I genuinely want to see again. This won me over despite its flaws, and I’m truly interested in the MCU like I was before I seriously got into movies. I’m still a bit of a critic and I think about the filmmaking and all that shit, but I feel like I can just enjoy a movie for being fun now. Cool!
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ farris, w/ rami, imax

Name: Ghostbusters: Afterlife
Review: 5.5 Mid but with some heart. I was kinda expecting to hate it but it’s worth more than what I thought; some parts were horrible to get through but I had fun. I would’ve fucking loved this when I was 12 so it was an interesting nostalgia piece (they could’ve held off a little on the easter eggs though)

Name: The Matrix Reloaded
Review:
I liked the part where they fought.

I didn’t like the part where they talked.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Spider-Man: No Way Home
Review: 7.5 I, too, have felt the pain of the college searching process recently. It doesn’t hold up quite as well on second watch, but one thing I do appreciate a lot more is the story itself. I will say the first half is very flawed; it’s full of fan service, the first half is riddled with plot mistakes which turned me off for a bit, and they could’ve made the introduction of every villain smoother, but once we get into the action, the pacing is legitimately excellent great. It’s got some satisfying arcs, dialogue that stays true to Raimi and Webb (for the respective characters), the major climaxes (aka the spoilers) were still impactful, and the ending worked much better for me. It might still feel and look like every MCU movie, but it far and away has the best narrative. And I’m still here talking about It, giving it a good review, what does that say? Might bump it back up to an 8 at some point, but at the same time I'm flustered by it's place at #31 on the Letterboxd Top 250 over all my top films except for a select few.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Kingsman: The Secret Service
Review: 8.5 This movie is awesome, disagree if you have bad manners. A little overlong and it doesn't scream masterpiece but it's genuinely so well made and so entertaining. Matthew Vaughn's action directing is top-notch and the editing adds a lot to the already impressive camerawork and effects. Every location is made the most out of in each scene and the payoff is always engaging, keeping me on edge for what comes next. Every performance is really good too, I believe each character as themselves rather than the actors (except Sofia Boutella but she's an exception to this being a negative, she's one of my favorite actresses). Yeah it's very cartoonish and I understand why that would turn some people off... but that's the whole point. This movie is based on a series of graphic novels, so it has to be done like this. I love it.
Tags: blu-ray, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Kingsman: The Golden Circle
Review: 6.0 Is this how Britain views America? Definitely not as good as the first as far as pacing (especially the first half which forgets what made the first one so good), dialogue, and my overall interest levels, but I still managed to have a decent amount of fun. This kind of action is my cup of tea, I just wish there was more of that and less of the subpar plot. At least it still has really tight performances and is just as visually interesting as the first, maybe moreso.
Tags: blu-ray rental, w/ dad, w/ quinn, library

Name: The Matrix Revolutions
Review: 5.5 This wasn't so bad but I'm getting so tired of all these mid action sequels I CAN'T WAIT to see Matrix Resurrections and Kings Man so I can get back to my normal agenda of movies and not constant mainstream shit for a week.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: It's a Sin
Review: 9.0 Took me way too long to finish this (I've been watching this over the course of the last few weeks, maybe a bit longer) but I finally did and I really loved this so much. Human life is a treasure and this reminds you of that. I laughed a ton and I cried a ton; such a bittersweet and wonderfully frightening tale of a lifestyle that I want to learn more about, complete with great filmmaking, amazing performances, and beautiful writing that kept me on edge at the end of every episode. I don't do very well with miniseries or TV shows just simply because of my ADHD, but this was brilliant. Fuck right the hell off Bohemian Rhapsody, this is how you tell a story of AIDS and the LGBT community in England in the 1980s. Ranking of the episodes: 4 > 5 > 3 > 2 > 1 (but they're all really great) Random side note but at one point I said Ritchie looks like he came straight out of a Gaspar Noe movie and THE ACTOR WAS IN ENTER THE VOID???? I'M SURPRISED I GOT THAT LMAO

Name: The Matrix Resurrections
Review: 8.0 Had a really great time! The pacing doesn’t bring it all together in a smooth fashion, but it reminded me a lot more of what I love about the first than either preceding sequel. The first half is ultra meta in the guise of something like Adaptation, which got slightly annoying at times, but never enough to turn me off and looking back works pretty well, almost like Lana is speaking to the audience saying she hears all our fucking comments. Even with that out of mind, it feels kinda unfocused for a while without much direction, but once the villain is introduced, the rest of the film feels like a far better version of Reloaded. While the dialogue feels like it was read straight off the script at times, it offers a lot of philosophy that genuinely got me to care amidst all the fighting and all the returns of old characters felt entirely natural. On top of that, it has expectedly great cinematography (shot on both digital and 35 mm <3), great sound, solid visual effects, and Wachowski’s direction feels like she’s getting back to her glory days. Watching this made me feel like the energy I’ve been putting into watching all these sequels hasn’t gone to waste and I love it.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, imax

Name: Listening to Kenny G
Review: 8.0 Really informative and interesting documentary that covers a lot in a hour and a half. Super funny too and there’s a very clear passion on both sides of the camera. I can’t say I was engaged the entire time, but I definitely learned a lot and have a newfound intrigue for Kenny G so the movie really did it’s job.

Name: The Santa Clause 3: The Escape Clause

Name: Tokyo Godfathers
Review: 9.5 Really great movie with amazing animation, characters, voice acting and music. Way funnier and more unpredictable than I thought, but I loved it a lot. The dialogue is kinda fantastic and a lot of the monologues in the second half were enthralling. The only reasons it isn't a full 10 is the pacing kinda threw me off and I wish there was a little more closure on Miyuki and her parents, but this is my first Satoshi Kon so once I get more familiar with his work maybe I won't consider these to be issues. This also feels like a movie that I'll appreciate more once I have kids and understand what some of the characters are going through on a more personal level. Overall though, I'm glad I saw this and it's easily one of the best Christmas movies ever.

Name: Punch-Drunk Love
Review: Showed this to my parents since we're going to see Licorice Pizza tomorrow, and... holy fuck. The first time I saw this, I liked it but didn't understand the hype. I watched it again at 2 AM on a random night, and it grew on me as I started to get why people loved it... nearly a year ago now. This time, man it hit me hard. (about to get weirdly personal on the internet but here we go) I've changed so much in the past year, learned more about myself, and grown from an admittedly dark place to where I am now. That's not to say I'm perfect or seriously thriving, but I'm at a time where I can say I have a much stronger grasp on reality and have a direction in my life. With that, I have re-watched Punch-Drunk Love at the perfect time and this struck multiple emotional chords in me. The quirky energy in both the atmosphere and characters is so on point with how I'm feeling right now, that every aspect I had an issue with in the past now seems perfect. I see a lot of myself in Barry Egan, both good and bad, and the way his character behaves feels uncomfortably personal, almost on the same level as It's Such a Beautiful Day, but this is far more of a comfort film. That's not to say it's my new favorite movie (discussion for another time), but all the progress I've made in the last year is evident in the way this movie functions and I couldn't help but smile in every single scene. Anyways yes, this isn't a very well structured review but I'm just so excited. I love this movie so much, and on top of all this I just finished a discussion with my parents on what it means to me and they said they finally understand some of how I feel when I have anxiety. Any movie that helps me communicate is one that I'm going to place in the highest regard. How is Licorice Pizza going to top this smh 10
Tags: hbo max, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Licorice Pizza
Review: I cannot tell you how BADLY I wanted to love this. It starts off really great, I was loving it and what it was setting up. Then it kinda starts to bottom out, then the second half just doesn’t have the same energy. I found myself starting to care less about what was going on. As a whole product, it doesn’t have very good pacing but the second half has a lot of changes that didn’t really work for me and the ending was just so frustrating with how the character arcs are finished and how I felt like I gained nothing. It doesn’t resonate and won’t months out. This is a technically amazing movie with great performances and a lot of really good moments. But I just personally did not find a connection to much of anything and in the end the journey the characters go on feels empty. Maybe I’ll feel different without the sting of massive disappointment, but for now… no thanks. Generous 7.0

Review: Well there go my plans to skip town and become a pornstar :/ Seriously though, I loved this. Name: Mysterious Object at Noon
Review: Apichatpong is a director who's been in the back of my mind for quite some time now. With Memoria coming to Chicago this upcoming week, I figured I'd check out one of his films. This was really cool. I love the way its made and how it merges documentary and drama, and the story was really interesting. It never really demanded my full attention or amazed me, but I wasn't bored either. I'd say good acting, but I'm not entirely sure which scenes were acted and which scenes were real, so the movie did its job. The editing is great and there are some long takes that I loved. Rating might go up in the future, we'll see. 7.5

Name: The Tragedy of Macbeth
Review: Dude I’m not even gonna try and write an intro sentence. I expected to come out of this thinking it was really good, but I’m still shaking and my head is swirling a little bit. Completely hypnotic and at times disorienting, this is a nearly perfectly made film that manages to also have a touch to it that makes it feel like a stage play. The cinematography, production design, and sound are mindblowing. Every performance floored me and the screenplay, though hard to understand at times, stays super reliable to Shakespeare vocabulary while still retaining the impact on delivery. By the end, I was stunned and even now I’m not sure what to say as my family discusses the film. This hardly feels Coen-esque, in fact I’d compare it to Robert Eggers, but even without most of his stylistic traits, Joel made one of the best movies of the year.

Name: Scenes from a Marriage
Review:
Not sure whether this is technically my first watch of 2022 or not since I've been watching one episode a day for the past week, but goddamn I chose an amazing first thing to log.

Can't fully commit to a rating right now (but if it changes it'll go up, not down) since each episode feels so different in how it serves the story and I'm still gathering my final thoughts but I was so into this. One of the best screenplays I think I've ever seen put to film with Ullmann and Josephson being incredible. My ADHD made this a struggle to get through at times but even then I got through it at a really good rate. Not sure what else to say on this since it's still fresh in my mind so I'd just be meandering if I kept going but yeah... incredible miniseries.

Review: The precursor to The House That Jack Built Seriously, I'm not kidding

Name: Memoria
Review: There's something so weird about going into a movie knowing that it might be the only time you ever see it in your life. As excruciating as the release plan is for many people, it's turned out to be a sound business strategy and when I saw that it was showing in Chicago the second week, I sprung at the first opportunity I could to buy tickets. This is a really complex film that says a lot as it descends further, and I think Apichatpong doesn't want us to figure it out. It's unique but pretentious and I still have mixed feelings on it. The more I read about this movie the less I have to say on it which makes it all the more frustrating for me. At this point, I don't even feel like putting time into it; I admire and respect this film way more than I enjoyed it or cared about what I was looking at. I'd like to think I have a decent grasp on what it all means, but every time I try to start a review I just feel like I don't know what I'm talking about. Maybe one day I'll actually talk about it, but I spent half the movie really thinking about and piecing it together, and the other half just waiting for the next cut (my ADHD couldn't tolerate most of the shots that were fixated on for too long). I "like" this movie but I genuinely can't start any coherent review on it until I see it a second time... if that ever happens.

Name: Saraband
Review: Criterion Challenge 2022 Kickoff Week 1 -- a film from the year you were born Even in his final years, Bergman was still writing amazing screenplays that make you think about life and cry. This was fantastic as both a movie and a legacy sequel to Scenes from a Marriage. I went in expecting to think it was good, but I've come out thinking it was brilliant. Those 110 minutes take exactly how long is needed, not too much or too little is covered, and the chapter format of the story worked super well. Three major takeaways - Life is precious and terrifying - Ingmar Bergman has inevitably joined my list of favorite directors - I love this heavy shit but I need some more positive watches for my next few movies

Name: The Salesman
Review: Love the performances and cinematography. The directing is super tight as well; the story has a lot to chew on, with several amazing scenes, and the way it uses Death of a Salesman as a backdrop for the characters' lives is pretty brilliant. There is a decent amount that didn't keep me consistently interested and I wish the ending went a little differently as well, but I still got a ton out of this movie. Some very emotional moments through the whole thing, and I can definitely say I cared about the characters. Rating could definitely go up on rewatch (didn't have the best watching conditions so that's part of it too). Can't wait for A Hero. 8.5

Name: Mauvais Sang
Review: My first from Leos Carax is one of the stranger romances I've seen but I really liked it. Amazing cinematography (same guy who did Good Will Hunting and Gummo) and music; when these two go together in a scene, it always works and I LOVE the way they're directed. There's a surprising amount of genre crossing, especially between drama-romance and action. The performances by young Denis Lavant, Juliette Binoche and Julie Delpy are all really good. The skydiving scene is fantastic and I'll be thinking about it for a while; can't figure out how they accomplished that in 1986. The film wasn't super consistent though, and by the end I can't say this is a film I really cared TOO much about (especially the actual plot involving the vaccine), but I also wish I gave the movie more attention than I did since there's a solid amount to take in. Rating is pretty solid, but if it changes it'll definitely go up, not down. Overall this was a lot of fun, definitely check it out even if you don't care about Carax. 8.5

Name: Klondike
Review: Sundance 2022 Film Festival #1 Just fantastic. A modern anti-war film written and directed by a woman from a country that is currently fighting one. This was certainly a bleak start to this year’s fest, echoing Bergman's Shame, but the authenticity, atmosphere, intensity and clear passion in the making all generate an incredibly tough act to follow. It's brilliantly made from the cinematography to the production design to the sound design, amazing performances, and pacing; every aspect works to make an unforgettable slow burn with an even better ending. The movie is over, I let the credits run, but all I could do was sit and think about what I just saw. In all an exhilarating movie, I’m not going to forget this for a long time.

Name: We Met in Virtual Reality
Review: Sundance 2022 Film Festival #2 The first image on the Sundance website almost turned me off of this movie, and I'm still not sure why they chose it, but it proved to be nothing like the movie itself. I was admittedly expecting something of a joke, but I ended up loved this. It's hard to make a film of any kind on a video game, and just saying that makes it sound impossible. But this was not only a well put together documentary that highlights a lot of aspects of the platform, but it was surprisingly heartfelt as it offers a number of different perspectives from users and their experiences with each other. This isn't something like Ready Player One or movies of the like where they're speculating the future or trying to cater super hard to a certain group; these are real people finding escapism in the reality of COVID, which has been commonplace for the last year, and they have genuine stories to tell. Everything that goes on ranges from funny to sad, but it's a true experiment and one that pays off. It may drag a bit and at times feel fairly repetitive in both its message and what's shown from VRChat, but in the end it leaves a lasting impression. There were times when I actually felt both joyful and depressed hearing everyone speak and learning about their community; you really come to know and care about everyone who's highlighted as any good documentary does. An easy recommendation for pretty much anyone.

Name: Mija
Review:
Sundance 2022 Film Festival #3

It feels a little long at times, but I almost didn't mind because everyone telling their story has such a vibrant presence. So yeah really good overall, might move up a score if I think about it more.

Name: Nanny
Review:
Sundance 2022 Film Festival #6

Solid horror movie. It tackles a lot of ideas, some of which don't really come together, but it's unpredictable and has a couple good sucker punches. The ending is super tight as well. The whole thing feels very inspired by Jordan Peele, and I don't mean that in a bad way.

Name: Sharp Stick
Review:
Sundance 2022 Film Festival #7

An odd one for sure. There are a few very different tones and styles during the film, and I enjoyed each of them, but when they're put together as a final product, it feels disjointed and unfocused. A good chunk of the film is decently written and acted, with a number of well done sequences, but then there's a couple sections that are the opposite; one of them feels like it was maybe purposeful given the context, but the other was just annoying. The soundtrack to this film is also the weirdest fucking thing, both in the song choices and how none of them seemed to add to their respective scenes. Overall, good ideas are tossed around for sure, I had a decent amount of care for Sarah Jo and I can't say it wasn't entertaining, but in the end I walked away without feeling like I gained much. Not a bad movie, but fairly forgettable.

Name: Annette
Review:
Really love the filmmaking. Feeling iffy on the narrative. Glad I finally saw it, rating may go up.


2/21/2023 -- A super admirable and impressive movie but it didn't vibe with me a lot personally and I don't think about it much. I do like it overall, however. Some sequences are really fascinating, such as Adam Driver's standup routines and every scene with Simon Helberg and the cinematography and mise-en-scene are treasures, but the entire thing just feels kinda bloated in both the runtime and all it tries to accomplish.

Name: Master
Review:
Sundance 2022 Film Festival #8

Still formulating an opinion, there’s a lot to dissect here, but I liked it a lot. I’m impressed they fit in as much as they did into the fairly short runtime, and it’s a movie that I definitely want to watch again to answer the questions I have. pretty much every aspect works very well, and I’m glad this is in competition.

Name: Free Chol Soo Lee
Review:
Sundance 2022 Film Festival #9

Very well made and emotional. I learned a lot about Chol Soo Lee himself, the US prison system, and how both of these played into activism for Asian-Americans. I really hope this gets a good distributor, it deserves to be seen by a lot of people.
Tags: sundance 2022, w/ dad, w/ mom, virtual cinema

Name: Speak No Evil
Review:
Sundance 2022 Film Festival #10

A solid build-up to a vile ending which ultimately feels pointless.

The first half is 45 minutes of buildup, which feels tedious after a while thanks to a lot of fake-out suspense and overbearing music, but developed the two families pretty decently; I was rooting for the movie. Not to mention some very good cinematography and great sound design, both of which apply to the whole movie. Once the second half kicks in and the entire mystery wraps itself up in gloriously disturbing fashion from a visual standpoint that would impress even someone like Michael Haneke, I definitely felt uncomfortable and tensed up several times, but this effect wore off me quickly, as I realized that I went through all of this for a story that says close to nothing.

There's no doubt that Tafdrup has potential as a director, and he understands what makes the European horror he loves effective from a visual standpoint. The movie does its job as a horror movie to scare me, and I appreciate that it painted an enticing picture for a while. But in the end it feels more like an insult to an audience since he doesn't provide any good reason for the whole thing. The bad taste in my mouth is more due to gaining way less than I had hoped for. The 6/10 rating is a bit generous.


Side note: This guy loves Lars von Trier and it's made very clear. The end feels annoyingly like his two horror movies, from the high amount of handheld Dogme 95 cinematography, to color-drained images of the aftermath of violence, and a closing slo-mo montage over opera music. Suffice to say, he's probably a scary person.

Name: Hatching
Review: Sundance 2022 Film Festival #11 Holy shit this really exceeded my expectations. I went in nearly completely blind and ended up with a treat. Another European horror film, but on the other end of the spectrum from the mediocrity of yesterday. It's hard to talk about this film without revealing too much, but it's as bizarre as something from Julie Ducournau with a lot of similar themes actually. It's mostly very well shot, well directed, and has some great performances to back up a story that I honestly really fell for, with a number of successful emotional moments. And it’s STRESSFUL! Predictable at times, but the way it's all executed was only working for me. There were a number of times when I was worried it wouldn't stick the landing, but all the way through the end I was engaged and never really disappointed. It's seriously disturbing, as expected, but it feels a lot more rewarding than I initially thought. Not to mention surprisingly amazing effects, both CGI and makeup. It does bite off more than it can chew for only 90 minutes, as I don't think it's all resolved, and I can understand why it doesn't work for some people but I really dug this. Go in knowing little.

Name: Emily the Criminal
Review:
Sundance 2022 Film Festival #12

Starts out promising and turns into the blandest thing I've seen this entire weekend. The performances are great and definitely save it, but I have nothing else to really comment on from this movie. Very forgettable and not creative, even if intense at a couple points. Feels longer than it is, but admittedly pretty entertaining; a lot of people will like it and good for them.

Name: Happening

Sundance 2022 Film Festival #13

I’m never buying this many films from a festival again. I’m pretty burnt out on it to the point where I kept nearly falling asleep in this which sucks because it is really good from what I saw. Hopefully it hits one of the streaming services in a few months. I’m going to sleep.

Name: Sirens
Review:
Sundance 2022 Film Festival #14

At a certain point it felt so real I thought I was watching a fiction movie. Definitely my favorite doc of the festival (so far).

Name: Meet Me in the Bathroom

Sundance 2022 Film Festival #15

Name: The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou
Review: Still gathering thoughts on the movie, but one thing's for sure, I inevitably loved it. I might come back and make this log more organized but initially I have to say - absolutely love a lot of the visual style, some of the best cinematography in a Wes film (not an easy feat) - expectedly no bad performances and great dialogue for the most part - definitely one of the best Wes ending chase scenes - a ton of other fantastic scenes that really stick the landing - feels a pretty disorganized at a few points - not as consistently entertaining or emotional as his other movies, but when it hits it really hits - hard to rank among the rest of his filmography since it's all so close together in quality, but it's around the same level as Grand Budapest, Royal Tenenbaums and The French Dispatch. They're basically interchangeable and the actual spot for each will depend on my mood at any given time. Same goes for the rating on this, I could see it go up to a 9 at some point. 8.5 / 10

Name: tick, tick... BOOM!
Review: Honestly? Kinda loved this. It's flawed, sure, and the first half hour wasn't really doing it for me, but once things start ticking away (haha, get it?) I got SO into it. Andrew Garfield is obviously fantastic and I'm glad he got nominated for an Oscar, the screenplay is pretty great, it's a very well paced movie, very well shot and edited, and compared to most movie-musicals, this one had a much greater variety of singing and just talking which I appreciated. Some scenes worked far better for me than others did, but in the end the whole thing really left a lasting impression. 2021 was a great year for NYC-based musicals, and while I really like In the Heights and West Side Story, this takes the cake for me. 8.0

Name: Murder on the Orient Express
Review: Watched this because my parents really like it and wanted to re-see it before Death on the Nile. (Gonna indulge this then watch something good tonight) Starts out feeling like Wes Anderson without any personality (call it The Attention Span Limited), then it turns into a decent murder-mystery. The way the mystery itself concludes is solid, but there are a few minutes afterwards that take away from how I feel about the whole film. A handful of good moments but a lot of it that's just so uninteresting. Some hilarious visuals too, though not a bad looking movie in its entirety. 5.5
Tags: dvd rental, w/ dad, w/ mom, library

Name: Death on the Nile
Review: The second best Kenneth Branagh movie in theaters right now. Branagh’s directing goes against a lot of what I love in movies. The first hour is full on garbage. The second hour is considerably less garbage. That’s the review. Barely 4.0

Name: The House
Review: Really good and just short of being great. I adore the animation style, voice acting and tone of each story, but they don't really say a whole lot or correlate with each other. Still it's entertaining as hell and there was clearly a lot of heart put into the making of this. Very hard to hate, an easy recommendation. 7.5 - could bump it up to an 8 at some point

Name: The Fallout
Review: Great performances + characters, great dialogue, great story, great cinematography, great... everything. A ton of scenes that are just winners. One of my biggest pet peeves with dramedies lately is that the use of memes and slang always feels forced and robotic and it makes me cringe, and this handles that better than maybe 95% of those movies, with only a few moments that took me out of things. A very respectful and honest movie, I loved it. I know this is typical of me, but rating could go up (and very possibly could within the next few days. Maybe even today) 8.5

Name: The 400 Blows
Review:
Criterion challenge week #5

Really kinda wish I fell in love with it but it’s still really great. Super well made and there are a lot of scenes that really hooked me, at times I was thinking it would become a favorite, but in the end it doesn’t really leave me starstruck like I thought it might. Nonetheless I got a lot out of it, it’s really good.
Tags: criterion challenge 2022, criterion channel, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: High and Low
Review: Criterion challenge week #6 Wow. This was... ridiculously good. I had a feeling this would meet my expectations, but what an insane movie. One of the most fine-tuned movies I've ever seen in the sense that it feels so clean and complete and flawlessly made. There's no way I'm going to try and give any sort of analysis just based on this first watch, just because it's such a massive experience, but it has so much to say in every part of the plot. The way the story unfolds is so crisp and satisfying, and everything in this movie just feels so authentic. It's a visual feast, every performance is fantastic, the screenplay is phenomenal... I can't say enough on this. You can see how this paved the way for so many films with similar themes and storylines since, especially a lot of New Korean Cinema. Seriously one of the best things I've ever seen, and it lived up to the hype in every way. Has to be up there with the most entertaining and iconic films in history. And this has to be said in its own paragraph, but the ending is mindblowing. Such a perfect conclusion with every single detail in every sense of the word. Maybe my new favorite ending in any movie but I'd have to think about that more. 10+

Name: Sherlock Jr.

I definitely enjoyed it for what it was, though it didn't really hit me like a lot of the others have. Also this was finally my first watch on Mubi after having it for like a month now (I've been behind but hopefully I can watch more from them soon)

Name: Being the Ricardos
Review:
/start rant

My parents both grew up watching I Love Lucy, and they're a lot more familiar with the history of Lucy and Desi themselves. During a few scenes, I had them explain some of the context for those situations, and that helped my understanding for sure, but it also made me realize that if I was watching this with anyone else or alone, I'd have no idea what the hell was going on.

The beginning and end present a point A and B, but everything in the middle is repetitive, unfocused and just plain unlikeable Aaron Sorkin filmmaking, from both a writing and directing standpoint. In the first 15 minutes, I was almost convinced that Sorkin was actually trying to say something with this story, but it quickly descended into typical under-the-spotlight soulless shit. I cared every so often, until it was ruined by the obnoxious structure that jumps between the multiple subplots and makes the story needlessly convoluted and loses the focus early on; after a certain point, you’ll gain more from the Wikipedia page. This isn't a stupidly long movie but it feels like 2.5 hours. The dialogue is witty, really the only thing keeping my attention span afloat, but it's never remarkable like The Social Network (although none of his recent screenplays have been). The production design is very good and the best part of the movie, but even then it's dampened by the ugly colors and lighting.

The performances are good and I wasn't expecting them to be less, but they don't jump out at me as best of the year worthy. Bardem was impressive as he fit the character despite the huge age difference, and Kidman was convincing despite not really doing anything remarkable, but J.K. Simmons getting the level of attention he has particularly pisses me off. He plays a boring version of himself as a historical figure, and is getting way more recognition than Jason Isaacs and Reed Birney in Mass, Woody Norman in C'mon C'mon, and a number of other amazing supporting performances. Fucking typical.

Anyways yeah, this movie isn't a complete trainwreck but I hated it. It's a joyless experience. Aaron Sorkin isn't interesting to me, he writes and unfortunately also directs movies that cater to old Hollywood celebrities. He's obviously high profile and will inevitably be writing more screenplays, but I am BEGGING producers to pair those with good directors.

/end rant
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Wild at Heart
Review:
In true David Lynch fashion, I saw this on my FUCKING telephone.



Just kidding I saw it on 35mm (which was really awesome and added a lot to the immersion, I felt like I was actually watching this in 1990). Anyways...


The heightened Lynch reality in this presents an incredibly unwelcoming atmosphere, but I still dug the hell out of it. Love the direction, cinematography and editing, all the filmmaking works together incredibly well. Every scene is memorable, all the performances are awesome, and I wasn't bored for a second. The soundtrack is a mix of heavy metal, jazz and Angelo Badalamenti, but the way it's all used was kinda perfect. The screenplay is pretty fantastic too; the development of everything and dialogue are both amazing and blend a lot of tones naturally, making me care about characters I wouldn't normally care about.

In true Lynch fashion, I noticed a lot of underlying themes to the point where it almost felt overstuffed, but around 2/3 to 3/4 of the way through it all started to come together, which I didn't expect on a first watch; to me, it's the darkest possible coming of age story, highlighting specifically the discord between parents and their kids, reality vs expectations (not unlike Mulholland Drive), and the Wizard of Oz (watch and you'll understand). There's still a lot I want to unpack, specifically with the colors and how they're used, but that's the beauty of films like these and multiple watches.

At a certain point I was sure this was about to be a new favorite, and it stayed that way until maybe the last 10 minutes, though I still have pretty mixed feelings on the end. What happens isn't completely out of place given some of what happens, but it's super different tonally and honestly becomes pretty goofy. It didn't really ruin anything for me, but I can't say I agree with what happens either.

Goofy ending aside, this is just an awesome movie. Very exhilarating, violent, funny, strange, uncomfortable, Nic Cage, and just plain 90s. In all, it's unforgettable and it really exceeded my expectations; I loved it a lot.
Tags: gene siskel film center, 35mm, w/ oliver

Name: The Stanford Prison Experiment

Name: The Ascent
Review:
Criterion challenge week 7

I had initially planned to watch this earlier in the year, but given recent events it felt even more fitting. As heavy as it is, I’m so glad I watched this. Just a brilliant movie from start to finish in nearly every way, hypnotizing and emotional. Can’t really pick a favorite scene since they’re all so well done. Don’t really know what else to say, other than this should be on everyone’s watchlist.

Name: The Godfather
Review: I mean what do you want me to say? It’s an amazing movie, I guarantee anything I could say has always been said. Brilliant in both filmmaking and narrative. It’s surreal I’ve finally seen this, and I’m so happy that my first watch was in a big ass theater. Name: Sorry to Bother You
Review:
How do you even review a movie like this, especially on first watch? Lmao

It doesn’t all come together for me (probably will on my second viewing) but I love how insane and literal everything is. Somehow very powerful despite how surreal most of what happens is, and I respect the hell out of it. Amazing production design, editing and performances too, maybe my favorite role by Lakeith but I love his work on Short Term 12 and Judas as well. Overall a mess in the best way possible, a hard movie to give a rating to. More movies should take risks like this.

Name: The Host
Review:
Watched for a school project

Better than I initially remembered for sure, though I didn't remember much of the story at all prior to this. The first half hour is really good, the second half hour is decent but more rough around the edges, then the final hour is excellent. First there was The Host, then there was Parasite... what's next in the Bong Joon-ho virus title trilogy?

Name: The Batman
Review:
The character of Batman is explored on a higher amount of layers than even The Dark Knight.

Every actor brings their A game, no matter how small the role.

Matt Reeves and the writers channel both Fincher and the Dark Knight trilogy as they create a nearly 3 hour facade that never feels like it overstays its welcome.

The cinematography, editing and Michael Giacchino’s score are on fire.

It’s PG-13, but no part of it feels dialed back or toned down.

In conclusion: It’s a new standard for comic book movies. A clear winner and tons of people are going to fall head over heels for it. See this in IMAX.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ rhys, w/ matt g, early screener, imax

Name: Parasite
Review:
School projects have peaked for me. I will never put more effort into anything in any classes than I have for this Bong Joon-ho research project.

Things I picked up on this time
- perfect editing (having learned a lot more about technical filmmaking in the past year, I appreciated the hell out of every aspect more than I possibly could otherwise but the cutting stands out to me the most)
- “crossing the line”
- I’m convinced the Park and Kim families were named after Park Chan-wook and Kim Jee-woon and you can’t tell me otherwise
- it’s still perfect and makes me cry! I can’t believe this movie exists

Name: Columbus
Review: Had some doubts while watching this a second time that it wasn’t as good as I initially remember, but nope. Still some of my favorite cinematography ever with a brilliant chemistry between Haley Lu Richardson and John Cho. Such an excellent screenplay and an amazing example of cinema having the power to heal, and outside of the first 45 minutes which weren’t consistently grasping this remains one of my favorites. I really love it.

Name: After Yang
Review: Great movie, one that’s really left me thinking about it for a while. The concept is fantastic, and while there isn’t a whole lot of world building here, I really didn’t mind. Aside from conversations that weren’t cut very well, this is close to a technically perfect movie and the visual style is just amazing. Every shot is aesthetic, as it should be. Kogonada’s great direction extends to the actors as well, there isn’t a bad performance and everyone is restrained for maximum effect. The screenplay is where my main issues lie, but even then it’s really good. During the first third of the film, I realized Mika (the little girl) was my favorite character and the way she responded to not knowing what was going on with Yang really struck a chord with me, but unfortunately she’s not really prevalent in the rest of the story. I was still  invested in and cared about what was going on, but Colin Farrell’s development didn’t really hit me the same way. The ending also felt sudden and kinda rushed, but otherwise the dialogue is excellent and is easily one of the strongest parts of the movie. Overall, while I don’t think it’s as strong as Columbus, Kogonada created another wonderful film that’s very charming and fascinating despite some issues with the story structure. Rating: 8.5/10

Name: The Batman
Review:

Anyways it’s still a really great movie. Most of everything I said in my first review still stands, and even if it didn’t surprise me the same way it’ll always rip ass and be one of the best comic book movies.

Name: Police Story

Name: La Haine
Review: Criterion challenge week 9 A movie that's been so hyped for me as this one probably wasn't the best choice to watch today, as I'm still sick and had some trouble focusing, but I still found it fantastic and it very likely will move up to a 10 on rewatch. Perfect screenplay, brilliant style and performances, excellent pacing, stunning cinematography, and sober direction. The ultra realism aspect is really something special, and how it's used to incorporate real France events into the story worked so well. There are no bad scenes, and the ending is unforgettable. A similar movie to City of God and Do the Right Thing, two of my favorites, and I think it's on the same level. I just need to watch it again when I'm in a better headspace.

Name: Venom: The Last Dance
Review: Venom: Let There Be Cinema

Name: Atonement
Review:
Why does every British person in movies drive on the wrong side of the road? Am I missing something?

Jokes aside... not sure what to make of this movie. It's beautifully shot with great acting, an interesting score and some great moments, but overall it really just feels messy. The first 45 minutes are pretty boring, the next 45 minutes tell a completely different story from two perspectives (one of which includes an excellent long take) which I actually liked a lot and connected to, but then the film's final act kinda delegitimizes it and it's frustrating. I didn't like the ending at all, but... the movie left its mark on me, and I’m still thinking about it. I’m getting two different messages on what rating I should give and just what I think of it in general, one says 6 and the other says 7. I'm gonna give it the edge for now since I'm really logging what I think in the moment, though I'll probably bump it down at some point. In conclusion... it's not great, but it stuck with me.

Name: Cyrano
Review:
Was trying to think of a metaphor to describe how mid this film is but it’s not coming to me or worth my energy so…

Dinklage is good and the music worked at times, along with a few successfully emotional scenes, but otherwise this just isn’t very good at all. A lot of scenes that aren’t lit well and there’s some actively bad sound design in a number of places. The screenplay is really annoying too, at times it tries to be smart and funny but just didn’t work for me, complete with some dialogue that made no sense. And for a movie with such a prominent love interest, Haley Bennett was given fucking nothing to work with. I wasn’t really bored during this and it didn’t feel longer than the runtime, but the main message of the story is established maybe 25 minutes in, then nothing interesting is done with it for the rest of the movie.

Speaking of the main message, I guess true love comes from your longest friend. Wasting no more time, I’m heading two streets over to ask Farris, a close friend since childhood, on a date. Hopefully he doesn’t already have someone else in mind, but even then according to the movie I’ll just manipulate him by putting my words in their mouth lol ok bye

Name: The Mask
Review:

It’s very flawed for sure but it’s the most 90s thing ever, entertaining as hell and actually well made in a lot of aspects— also one of the earliest movies that I was really fascinated by. Hardest I’ve laughed in a while as well, I wish I made this movie (I could do better though).

Name: Turning Red
Review:
I liked this overall. It’s well animated, fun, and I think the red panda metaphor is great (the end was a little off the rails though). There was clearly a lot of heart and creativity put into every scene. I was able to look past most of the teenage cringiness since that’s just what this film’s style is, though some of it could definitely have been taken out (did she have to twerk at all, much less 4 times?). Some dialogue was frustrating, I hated the douchebag character’s arc (he was racist, got attacked then bailed out by his parents, and became Mei’s friend ??), and there were a few other story and character decisions that just felt odd or cliched. The flaws do stand out, but I liked the way the story itself was resolved even if the way it got there was kinda disjointed so it won me over.

ALSO -- really hope Pixar departs from Disney. Fuck Disney, fuck Disney so bad (the lack of a theatrical release yeah, but mostly their support of anti-gay legislation)
Tags: disney+, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Flee
Review:
So the first time I saw this movie way back at CIFF, I liked it a lot but man it grew on me. So much so that the very next day, I decided to come out to my parents, which Is obviously a huge moment for me. And while this isn’t a film that I necessarily think about everyday, every time I do I only think higher of it. Fast forward to now, I heard it was playing at a theater I’ve been wanting to go to for a long time AND it introduced me to a local film club, so I convinced my family to join me.

And… oh my god. My schedule in October was packed to the brim which explains why I had to see it at home, but man am I thankful I finally got to see this on the big screen, which it deserves. The animation… the voice acting… the pacing… the music… man it’s all so REAL. I still can’t really put elaborate thoughts into the movie because it keeps me quiet and thinking and, after a certain point, very emotional. The entire film is just beautiful, but coming back to it after how it influenced me made the coming out scene and finale all the more unforgettable for me. I could go on and on but goddamn this movie is so special to me. One of my new favorite theater experiences ever for sure.
Tags: tivoli theater, after hours film society, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: The World's End
Review: Really awesome and probably my favorite Edgar Wright movie. Laughed and loved every minute of it, the story and pacing are brilliant. Don’t know what else to say, I loved it a lot.

Name: The Witch
Review: Anya Taylor-Joy as Thomasin? Where have I seen that before… Still not sure what I just watched but I can’t move and I can’t stop thinking about any of this. Genuinely one of the most disturbing films I’ve seen in a long time and I can’t find anything wrong with it. It shook me to my core and left me in almost a limbo state. It tackles so many themes and has completely unforgettable performances and imagery. I can’t wait to watch this again real soon, I think this might be my new favorite horror film for now but there are a few I need to rewatch. I see why this was so influential for horror; not just because so many A24 horror movies that succeeded it took a lot after it (It Comes at Night is oddly similar), but just because of how much it accomplishes.

Name: Attica
Review: The Attica riot was obviously a horrific event and proved to be a landmark for prisoners' rights, and honestly I respect the simplicity in the way this film was made. It's about as typical as a documentary can get, with interviews followed by photos and footage from the time period, but the way its edited works really well, adding an extra emotional layer. Any attempt to make it super artistic or surreal would probably just backfire. The one thing that could've elevated it, however, was an added element that goes beyond just re-telling the history. I was engaged and cared about what was going on, but I just learned about Attica itself and nothing that came after it or how the legacy of it was important. What's here is still good though, and worth a recommendation. Not my favorite of this year's nominated documentaries, but I'm not mad that it got the nod.

Name: The Innkeepers
Review: Given the presumably low budget, this had a surprising amount of production value. It's got solid filmmaking, some decent scares, and characters that feel like real people (something that even a few of my favorite horror movies can't boast). The minimalism works super well, I was never really bored, and the execution of the concept was just really good. Very solid music as well, and the limited amount of humorous moments gave me mild Sean Baker vibes, which I didn't expect. It never reaches greatness, and there are a number of aspects that just didn't work for me, but this was an enjoyable watch all around. Can't wait for X tomorrow. 7.5

Name: The Breakfast Club
Review: Surprise evening watch with some friends. It mostly doesn’t hold up to be honest which kinda disappointed me but I do like a fair amount of scenes in this so I don’t want to call it bad either. John Hughes was probably a total creep.

Name: Birdman or (The Unexpected Virtue of Ignorance)
Review:
<3


(people who criticize this film for being pretentious usually sound even more pretentious themselves lmfao)

Name: Spring Breakers
Review:
Started this sometime around the beginning of the school year but for whatever reason didn't finish. Now I finally watched the whole thing, and with my dad... it made for an interesting experience to say the least.

But damn, I have to say I really did love it. I've been super intrigued by Harmony Korine and this movie since last summer, and I'm glad I finally saw this movie. I can see why it's divisive, there's a lot of weird and occasionally random shit going on (certain scenes I agree weren't needed, and I didn't love the editing), and it's not the most well made, but at the same time it has a really mesmerizing style in how its shot and how music is used (perfect score) and the directing is great, the authenticity similar to that of Sean Baker (if one of his movies was thrown into a strobe-lit washing machine).

Every performance is awesome and James Franco seriously disappeared into his character, the casting of Disney girls in their roles is perfect. The screenplay isn't brilliant on the surface, but the dialogue all fits and it's paced amazingly well; a runtime of only 90 minutes but it felt shorter, I was locked in. And the message I got, which has a lot to do with expectations vs reality, the dark side of fantasy and how far people are willing to go for their dreams, came across so effectively. The ending is so bold and out there, but Harmony made it work and I felt it rounded things out really well.

Overall, this movie is so immersive and the energy given off is extremely unwelcoming but intriguing as hell. It's kinda hanging onto the 9 rating, but despite some scenes I felt went on for too long or just wish weren't there, this was awesome and a wild kickoff to spring break. It'll stick with me.

Name: Raya and the Last Dragon
Review: The animation and music are great, but I swear I've seen this exact story and extremely similar characters before. Now to figure out where...

Name: Neon Genesis Evangelion
Review:
I'm gonna be honest and say that I watched this entire show over the course of 2 months. With ADHD, I almost never watch shows or miniseries because it requires a lot more brainshare, and just because of life in general I never have a consistent watching schedule.

That being said I can still recall most of the show despite the long time period and yeah what the fuck it's fantastic. I'm angry with myself because I don't have more to say and the schedule I watched every episode was so fucking jagged but I went through more emotions than I could've possibly imagined and my heart was pounding for most of the last 6 episodes. Makes me wonder that even if I was able to watch shows like a normal person, I still wouldn't be able to comprehend it right now. This isn't something you can summarize your thoughts with in a short log or even just a couple paragraphs.

Name: The Fugitive
Review: I wish I liked this more since this was my parents' first time in at least a couple decades, but aside from the good music, some fun action scenes and solid performances, this felt really hollow and bland to me. Every scene looked the same and I saw the ending coming from a while away (that could also be because my parents verbally hinted at it multiple times as we went along, I probably would've enjoyed it more if I watched it alone). I was really invested in all the characters at the start, but once the manhunt started the movie focused way more on the action than the story development and I progressively cared about them less, sorry :/ Good hometown representation though that was cool.
Tags: dvd rental, w/ dad, w/ mom, library

Name: Licorice Pizza
Review: I liked it more than the first time, no doubt due to the lack of massive disappointment. Closer to an 8 now than a 6 I will say, it was more fun than I remember and certain scenes had me more emotionally invested. But now that it’s all said and done again, this remains a super well made and acted film that I just don’t care much about.

Name: The Lost Daughter
Review: A heart-pounding, intricate, emotional journey. An entrancing character study that's draining and difficult but thoroughly engaging and incredibly well made. The performances are fantastic, the cinematography is stunning, the directing and editing are captivating, and it only gets better as it goes along. There are some scenes that left me with my head-scratching (specifically how the story wrapped itself up) and at times it feels long, but this hit way harder than I ever expected it to. A lot of people have complained about the flashback scenes, but honestly I didn't mind in the slightest. Watching it with my mom only made it more intense but it was a perfect experience. An unexpected but welcome entry into my top 10 movies of 2021.

Name: Bad Luck Banging or Loony Porn
Review:
I liked it a lot... I think. Still not sure what I just watched, but it's definitely unique and really funny. I didn't really fall in love with it at any point, but I respect the vision and it's anything but forgettable so the rating could go up.


Also I'm almost done with all the major award winners of 2021 (festivals and industry shows), just one more to go.

Name: The Untouchables
Review: Watched with my dad, another Chicago-based movie. Great Morricone score, great performances, good screenplay and the Union Station scene is really awesome. Some parts of this are really cheesy though, out of place for a period piece.

Name: Her
Review: So much funnier, so much sadder, so much more personal, so much more affecting than I remember. This is a truly special, and maybe perfect film to me. The complexity and layers to every character (no matter how small the role, they all feel necessary), the use of music, the ridiculously good production design, the cinematography in both aesthetic pleasure and the emotion behind every shot... it means more to me than I could put into this review. Love is an understatement, this was a MUCH needed rewatch.
Tags: blu-ray, love this movie more than myself, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: X
Review:
Just now realizing I never logged this, whoops


I was going to do a review for YouTube but now that may or may not happen so I’ll just paste my final thoughts here.


Overall, it was just fun! Made me miss summer and reminded me to keep working on my screenplays. It’s an original concept that’s executed with infectious energy and love for horror. Though it took me a while to get into, it’s very well made from every standpoint, has interesting dialogue, great performances, and discusses themes of both porn and horror meta that are interestingly tied together. Despite its flaws in pacing, some characters being better than others and too much exposition, I like X a lot, would recommend it to those interested, and can’t wait for the prequel.

Name: High Fidelity
Review: I definitely wish I liked this more than I did, but it was still definitely a good time. I was invested in the characters, how they're written and their performances, it's well made and funny, and I love how Chicago is utilized in varied ways, definitely my favorite movie that uses the city's location so far. It just felt pretty messy in getting from point A to point B with some of the (many) subplots, and because of that this felt longer than it actually is. Still very entertaining, cool to see Jack Black in his breakout role.

Name: The Lego Batman Movie
Review: Watched this with friends last night. This is a gay movie. I’m like Batman because I also don’t pay my taxes.
