Name: Dogtooth
Review: An interesting concept that’s well shot and well acted, which unfortunately peaks halfway through for me. I thought it was great the first 45 minutes, but the next 45 do more of the exact same and don’t really expand at all. I wasn’t expecting some big complex world, but I eventually found the experience tedious. I love a unique story with a deadpan style, and there are a lot of thought-provoking ideas are play, but Yorgos hadn’t mastered pacing and structure yet. I wish I liked this more, and I had high hopes that my memory was just fading and that this is actually a great movie, but sadly it was even worse.

Name: Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion
Review: Synecdoche, Tokyo Prefecture I can’t think of anything else to add at the moment and maybe I won't for a while, but this was everything I hoped it would be and more. A perfectly tragic finale full of layers upon layers and unforgettable content and some of the most beautiful animation I’ve ever seen, bar maybe Akira. Existential on the level of something like It’s Such a Beautiful Day, but arguably more dynamic and artistic (idk which I prefer, they’re both masterpieces). Who knew a movie about the end of the world would be so depressing? Why didn’t anyone warn me?

Name: The Tree of Life
Review:
I have to say, interesting choice to watch this less than a day after End of Evangelion. It wasn't as emotionally taxing as I thought it would be, which is good for my mental state, though at the same time this wasn't quite the movie I was expecting.

A year and a half ago after I watched Synecdoche, New York for the first time and quickly became obsessed, The Tree of Life started to linger in my brain more and more, as many people compared the two experiences in terms of themes and the effect it has on you. I had actually first heard of this film upon its release and I remember seeing the poster in theaters when I was just 7, but I had no idea what this movie was and didn't really think about it again until a couple/few years ago when my movie obsession really began.

I regret not watching this during that time when it was of higher interest to me, because now I'll never know if it would've lived up to the hype at the time or not, but even then I'm not sure if it would've met my super-high expectations. This is just as beautifully created and ambitiously directed as I've heard from, well, everyone, and there are tons of shots and sequences that are really unforgettable. All the acting is really great and I respect the strong personal aspect the screenplay has, though after a certain point the film just kinda... lost me.

The first 30-ish minutes, to me, felt like an Oscar-appropriate version of Enter the Void with how the characters are introduced and all the surreal natural imagery; it hooked me immediately and I already was sure that it would live up to expectations (and that I'd regret watching it the day after Evangelion). However, while the rest of the story of the family was certainly interesting and very well paced, I slowly started to feel less attached. I felt like I had a good understanding of what Malick was trying to say and I was still very engaged and fascinated by the filmmaking, but by the end I did so without the earlier emotional connection.

One trend I've noticed in many of the glowing reviews for the movie is that this is a film for multiple phases of life. So maybe this will be one of those that I rewatch when I'm older and I love it a lot more all of a sudden which could totally be possible. But for the time being, this is an incredible film that I admire more than I care about.
Tags: criterion blu-ray, criterion challenge 2022, w/ mom, w/ dad

Name: The Counterfeiters
Review: Kinda like The Pianist if it was shot like an episode of Parks and Rec. Well acted and interesting for the most part but not very well paced, feels like a movie I'd have watched in class during a WW2 unit.

Name: Ford v Ferrari

Name: Princess Mononoke
Review: Really epic and beautiful in every way. I loved it a lot and there isn't really much to say that hasn't already been said, especially on my very first watch. There's also a lot of clear inspiration for later American films from this lol Miyazaki is an unstoppable force. It didn't hit me quite as hard as I was hoping, but if I don't bump it up to a 10 in the near future it'll very likely happen on a second watch. I can’t really get it out of my mind and I’m fully stunned by the animation, music, story, voice acting… everything. I might go see it again on Wednesday, though it’ll be dubbed sadly so I'm not sure yet.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, ghibli fest

Name: Boiling Point
Review: An excellent character drama, an accurate portrayal of what working in a restaurant is like (brought back some painful memories for me), a brilliantly directed nearly one-shot film, and a damn stressful 90 minutes that heats up more and more until the water, literally and metaphorically, reaches 100 °C. This pretty quickly became one of my favorites of last year, and it never lost that steam. This is one hell of an overlooked movie and I seriously hope it becomes a sleeper hit in the near future. If this wasn't already clear... I love it. And I'm damn glad I don't work in this industry anymore.

Name: Memento
Review:
Second movie watched for Psych 1.

Having sat on it for a while, and having learned way more about both filmmaking and psychology in the past year, this was a much needed rewatch and I really do love this movie. It has almost all Nolan-isms on display, a lot of them at their best; in some ways, this is an anti-filmbro movie. I wish Nolan made more films that deal with the brain a lot more intimately and self-contained like this one is, it works way better for me than Inception and Tenet. Hopefully in 2030 when he makes his next cerebral film, it’ll be more along the lines of this, since IMHO his career peaked here (though I still haven’t seen The Prestige yet).

Name: Nightcrawler
Review: Second film today I finished watching in a class (this one for actual Film Studies). This is a movie like Tree of Life in the sense that I heard a ton about it in 2020, and I regret not watching it earlier to compare how I'd feel then vs now. I think I would've LOVED it at that time, though times have changed and the movie was pretty close to exactly what I expected. Jake Gyllenhaal is definitely impressive in his role / performance, but for me the standout was Riz Ahmed. We've seen the fed-up-by-society, deranged antihero many times before, but more often than not they're the only one on their own team. Riz adds an extra layer to Jake's character that you don't get in similarly-themed filmbro movies like Taxi Driver or Joker, a layer of sanity and normal reasoning to contrast the unhinged. He was perfectly cast as Rick, given great dialogue to work with, and I was always emotionally engaged with him. Speaking of dialogue, the screenplay isn't perfect, but it's definitely the other standout aspect for me. It's snappy, always engaging, and structured well. Even though I didn't find all the characters overly memorable, I can't deny that they're all well developed and interesting to watch. I also thought the themes of the media and the ethics of capturing a news story were tied in well, if not life-changing. It felt like the Safdies wrote a Paul Schrader-esque character study. Outside of the characters and screenplay, the rest of the film is solid but pretty unremarkable. My only real complaint is that while the camerawork itself isn't bad, the visual style is so bland, especially compared to movies with distinct LA vibes like Mulholland Drive, Tangerine, Under the Silver Lake and even Drive. I wasn't a fan of the finale either; the final chase was well done, but everything between that and the very end didn't add very much for me and was almost ruined by one scene in particular between Jake and Rene Russo (if you've seen it you know which scene I mean). I think my watching experience was also jaded by the fact that I watched this over the course of three days, so if I ever come back to this film (I probably will at some point) then my opinion will most likely change. Overall, Nightcrawler is a very good movie that's well done for the most part (if not the best directed) that has interesting themes and characters, though it's not one that blew me away by any stretch. It's a more modern take on the incel antihero character that isn't ever boring, but I won't be thinking about it for months to come either. I'm happy with the rating at a 7/10 for now, but it may go up to an 8 at some point. (writer note: I have no idea why I wrote so much, almost an essay lmfao. W for spontaneous motivation)

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
Review: Everything Everywhere All at Once is a celebration. A celebration of, fittingly, everything. It embraces the best aspects of cinema, family, sexuality, sex toys, and the human psyche, along with so much more. It’s a masterclass in every way, from filmmaking to artistically portraying what it means to live on earth, and you will literally feel like you’ve opened your third eye, whether that be a plastic googly eye, a circle on a paper stapled to your head, or the bagel you’ll eat for breakfast in the morning. It is the first film in years to truly break open the medium, and if you aren’t breaking open your wallet to pay for a ticket to see this in theaters, you’re making a big mistake and you’ve chosen the universal path that will make your day worse.

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
