Name: A Scanner Darkly
Review: I'm surprised I've only recently started getting especially interested in the works of Richard Linklater, since a lot of his career is so in line with the general type of indie film I'm most fascinated by right now, both for watching and making myself. I'm glad I found a great start to that journey with this low-budget dramedy full of lovable characters.

Name: Blue Velvet
Review:
Without fail, this was another amazing cerebral film that wasn’t what I expected but exactly what I was hoping for. Lynch films always manage the perfect medium of being surreal yet still transporting you into the lives of his characters, and every minute had me fully hooked. At a certain point, I was wondering how far into the film we were, but then I also realized that even if I was only 30 minutes in, I wouldn’t mind watching another hour and a half.

I’ll talk more about the film itself soon since I really didn’t do that at all in this review, but I also just wanted to say I’m so happy I got to see at least one Lynch film at the Music Box retrospective. I was supposed to see Mulholland Drive on Friday but that fell through, and I could’ve seen Inland Empire yesterday had it not sold out so fast, but tonight made up for all of that. The crowd was more active than any other theater experience in recent memory, everyone was so excited to be there, and this really was an unforgettable first watch. I just had a total blast.
Tags: music box theater, me time!, 35mm, david lynch retrospective

Name: Memoria
Review: This is a different movie the second time. I’ll be writing a full review when I get home, as I have a lot more theories on what it means and a lot of my questions were answered. I think I really love it now. According to a Siskel Center worker, they’re screening the film on one of three Memoria 35mm prints in existence. It was a special experience. Video idea: Memoria but every boom is replaced with James Franco whispering “spriiing breeeak” 8.5, quite possibly could be a 9 the more I sit on it.
Tags: gene siskel film center, 35mm, w/ rhys

Name: Fantastic Planet
Review: Deserves a re-watch, not sure why I thought it would be a good idea to watch this really soon after waking up LOL. I liked it a lot, not perfect but a very well animated film with a lot of interesting ideas at the helm. It wasn't quite as weird as I was expecting either, but the story is intriguing enough that I don't really care since it's still super creative and fairly ahead of its time. This is something I would really dig had I watched it at a different time in a better mood, but unfortunately I wasn't able to give it my full attention this early in the day.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2022, w/ dad

Name: The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent
Review: Why the hell does this have a 3.8 average rating?? I’m sorry to be the bearer of bad news but I didn’t find this good… at all. If you want to see a new dumb Nicholas Cage action movie, then this is for you. The entire movie tries to play the story off as meta along the lines of Birdman (the two have a shocking amount in common and Nic Cage literally mentions Iñárritu in the dialogue at one point), but at its core there’s no depth and the central story is a forgettable spy thriller with thin characters that are hard to care about, although Pedro Pascal has a fun presence for the most part. Admittedly I didn’t hate the movie for the majority of the time and had a decent amount of fun, but there was still a lot holding me back from really getting me into things, and it progressively got less fun all the way up to the AWFUL ending. It isn’t a very well made movie either, with a fair amount of awkward camerawork and distracting sound design, but these were more just mild annoyances that were briefly annoying and took me out the immersion. This isn’t a total miss of a film, but it’s really obnoxious and thinks it’s way more intelligent than it actually is (it's somehow underdeveloped and overdeveloped at the same time which is impressive in its own right) and I just feel very let down by how lazy the final product ended up. 4.0, was gonna give it something lower but he has a brief Wild at Heart moment towards the beginning that I enjoyed so it gets a very slight edge.
Tags: cinemark seven bridges, w/ dad, w/ mom, early screener

Name: I Stand Alone
Review: i really don't like movies or life or existing and i kinda wish i wasn't here (there are so many better studies of mental health out there that are engaging and interesting without all the random bullshit of this. So much unnecessary dialogue, out-of-pocket incest, painfully obvious homages to Taxi Driver, and I really never gave a shit after the first 20 minutes or so)

Name: Wings of Desire
Review: I really love the way this movie is directed, especially visually… moreso than I love the way it’s written. I still really enjoyed the story and characters Wenders created and there are many scenes that really connected with me, though the pacing felt very off at a number of points and by the end I really wasn’t sure what to get out of it. The lack of a clear plot worked very well during the first half when the angels were just spectating everything and I really loved that, though when Bruno Ganz started following the same characters I wasn’t as hooked and the structure began to feel a bit messy. And maybe this is just the English translation, but some dialogue almost felt thoughtful to a point past believability, like it was read straight off the page. Nonetheless, this is a pretty great movie overall that I want to learn more about and sit on for a while. Seeing it in theaters was a pretty cool experience as well.
Tags: gene siskel film center, 35mm, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Dancer in the Dark
Review: I don’t know what to say right now. I’m close to speechless. A brilliant movie. Aside from a couple story points that I felt were a bit underdeveloped, this is fantastic and close to a masterpiece. I absolutely loved it. The physical reaction it left on me almost hurt after a certain point, and I didn’t breathe the entire last 10 minutes. I completely forgot Björk was playing Selma for almost the entire film and this almost never happens for me; easily one of my new top 10 favorite performances. I love the style, I love the screenplay, I love the music, I just… love it. I wish I had more to say but I can’t think of anything else right now. On the next watch I’ll go deeper. High 9.5

Name: The Northman
Review:
Anya Taylor-Joy and Björk my loves <3


As expected, an amazing movie. The filmmaking craft is just brilliant and I loved almost everything about the way every scene is made. I didn't find a huge connection to the story, but I partially blame that on my energy level; I fell asleep for about 5 minutes around the 2/3 point according to my dad (But that's not the movie's fault at all, I was up until 2 AM last night). The cinematography and production design are spectacular, the sound design is mindblowing, the way the music is used oh my god, the performances are unforgettable, really all I need is a second watch when I'm more awake and focused and this will be a 9 (even then, I'm tempted to bump it up regardless).

If you can, see it in Dolby; the experience was fucking awesome as expected. I hate that Focus Features made the directing experience kinda miserable for Eggers, but without them I doubt the film would've made it past the smallest auditoriums.

Name: Clueless
Review:
Watched for a film project (on diversity, my specific topic being LGBQ representation). I’ll be using Christian’s character as an example of stereotyping.

Mediocre gay inclusivity aside, this is a good movie and hit differently than I expected, probably due to the fact I’m graduating high school in less than a month. The main characters are from a side of high school culture I’ve never been a part of, one that I’ve also seen done to death in movies and shows prior, but the development Heckerling gives them makes them more interesting than most other popular kids I’ve seen elsewhere; I felt very familiar with all of them by the end and actually remember who they are. It isn’t a spectacularly made movie, the story is fairly predictable and I didn’t love every performance, but at times it’s great and I’d be lying if I said I didn’t have a good time watching it.

Name: The Worst Person in the World
Review: A different experience the second time for reasons I didn’t fully pick up on until after the movie finished, but I still adore it the same way. I smiled my whole way through writing this review so I hope you enjoy it just as much. The first watch had the excitement of CIFF, the shock and awe of every surprise, and the joy of an overwhelmingly positive first reaction. This time, I knew what to expect and already had a preconceived idea of what would be in store, but with that I was able to figure out more of the purpose behind everything. Instead of being sad that the movie had finished like I was initially, I felt like the runtime was perfectly apt tonight; I felt reassured and, in a way, personally revitalized after joining these characters once again. Some scenes hit harder than others, a few were just as delightful as I had remembered, but what most made this watch just as special is what I took away from the whole thing. I’ve always felt a strong personal attachment to the Julie, Aksel and Eivind in unique ways, but the first time I wasn’t applying what happens to my own life very much. This time, the tone of a lot of the plot points and specifically the transitions between each struck a different emotional chord for me and felt like they were channeling emotions that I’ve felt a lot recently given what’s going on in my own life. I know this isn’t a particularly unique feeling and I’ve felt this way about other movies in the past, but this watch felt more personal than most others I’ve had in recent memory. There’s a lot of both anxiety and elation I feel with becoming an adult, and a high amount of my desires and fears appear at some point in this film. It’s an experience I want to relive every few years or so for the rest of my life so I can periodically keep finding new meaning in Joachim Trier’s world. Anyways this log is already much longer than I was planning but I love this movie so incredibly much and it deserves it. Every aspect is incredible, especially the performances, writing/directing, cinematography and editing. I can’t wait for the Criterion release so I can hopefully learn far more about this movie than I already have. It’s in a neverending battle for my favorite of the year with Flee and C’mon C’mon but all three are like top 2% tier for me so it’s like picking a name out of a hat which one is my favorite. Cinema is awesome, I can’t wait to spend the rest of my life in it.
Tags: tivoli theater, after hours film society, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Shiva Baby
Review: Second half of my LGBQ film class project (perfect bi representation IMHO). An anxious bisexual who hates crowds, feels uncomfortable at religious gatherings, makes little mistakes every few minutes, and has a difficult time communicating with their parents? I've experienced it all before (not to this extent, thankfully, but I was relating really hard). I guess I've lived enough life since my first watch last summer to be able to find a stronger attachment to Danielle, but I genuinely am so filled with simultaneous joy and fear after the second time, it's a confusing mix of emotions in the best way possible. This is the most stressful comfort film ever and I fucking love it.

Name: Girlhood
Review: Really great performances and use of music. The Diamonds scene will live rent free in my head for a while. Had some issues with the pacing and sections of the story but this was really well done and I always really cared about what was going on, the development is superb across the board. Can't wait for Petite Maman.

Name: The Lighthouse
Review:
I really need a significant other…


This movie is still excellent btw

Name: Irreversible
Review: January 2023 update -- I don't disagree with a lot of these points I made last April, but my opinions have definitely changed on this movie (for the better) and I think, as brutal as it is, I'm due for a rewatch at some point. The very first scene in this movie features a cameo from Philippe Nahon, who played the lead in I Stand Alone, remorsefully talking about what happens in the final scene from I Stand Alone. The very last scene is the camera looking up at the sky with seizure-inducing cutting to black, almost reminiscent of Enter the Void, as if he was teasing it as his next movie. This may sound like a stretch, but Gaspar himself said that Irreversible was only a side project, made to prove he was worthy of the budget required to make ETV. It feels like Noe is either trying to say that all his films happen in the same universe, or just to remind you that this is a Gaspar Noe movie. No matter what, because of that, I never really lost myself in the story or forgot I was watching a movie. Mostly just because the experience is so fucking miserable and so full of unnecessary bullshit. (mild spoilers but who gives a fuck with this movie) Within the first half hour, a gay man is mistaken for their actual target and brutally murdered by the main characters who also release a barrage of homophobic slurs throughout, a Chinese taxi driver doesn't know where the gay club is so the main guys call him a bunch of anti-Asian slurs out of frustration, and a transgender woman doesn't know who Alex is so she is beaten up and called slurs. It felt very I Stand Alone-esque, both in its content and lack of reason. If absolutely none of the slurs were said, I still would've gotten the main point from the violence and action of the characters. Not only this, but because the film is told backwards, these characters have no development so I really had no reason to give a shit. The second half hour comprises of the infamous six-minute **** scene, and there is still very little development in the characters. Everything feels overlong, and I had a mix of two desires; 1. wanting to punch Gaspar Noe in his ugly goblin face, and 2. throwing up. The final half hour is honestly just kinda boring, but the film slightly grew on me just for how Monica Bellucci is developed. Now that I've had time to sit on it though, I'm reverting back to my initial thoughts that this is just such an unnecessary film. Gaspar Noe is a self-proclaimed "provocateur" so, in addition to proving he deserved a budget for Enter the Void, I'm convinced he only made this to cause controversy and not actually say anything. It's impressively made from a filmmaking standpoint (even then, what's the point of spinning the camera around constantly?) but horribly written, using minorities as punching bags for shallow characters who I'm going to forget all about tomorrow, full of self-indulgency from who is quickly becoming one of my least favorite people in the film industry, and is the exact opposite kind of movie I want to make myself. The more I think, the more I hate it all. It means nothing, it's a waste of 90 minutes, doing nothing for me but turning my mood to total shit. I'm going to see The Northman a second time tonight because some friends invited me, hopefully that can save the day.

Name: The Northman
Review: Can't believe Robert Eggers made a Lars von Trier multiverse movie! Michael from Melancholia fights to save his mother Grace Margaret Mulligan, whilst briefly also being joined by Selma Jezkova and 'He' from Antichrist. Just insane what the possibilities of filmmaking are today! Unexpectedly even better on rewatch, my GOD. Maybe my guard was let a down by the fact that I watched a dreadful revenge film earlier today and was following that up with another (albeit very different) revenge film, but I had such a particularly awesome time tonight. I love literally everything about this movie. It's well paced, it's got a healthy mix of being both gorgeous and fucking gory, it sounds incredible from both a mixing and music standpoint, the characters and the performances to back them up are amazing, and it's just chock full of incredible sequences and nearly impossible to pick a favorite (the ending though... my god). And better yet, I didn't fall asleep this time so 5 minutes of this were brand new to me! The only reason it isn't immediately a 10 is because there are still a couple moments that had me slightly bored, but honestly I might bump it up at some point.
Tags: amc naperville 16, dolby, w/ josh k

Name: Brokeback Mountain
Review:
I haven’t done this in a long time, but… I actually read the original story prior to seeing the movie, and I think I’m going to start doing that more often. Annie Proulx’s book isn’t perfect but I was still entranced by how it was written and formed a strong connection to the characters by the end. It only made me more excited to see a film I’ve been meaning to watch for a few years now.

Ang Lee’s adaptation is very faithful with only a few small changes, but those few did make a difference. It was very satisfying to hear a lot of my favorite monologues spoken on screen by Ledger and Gyllenhaal (who both do an excellent job in general), and I was engaged throughout the exact same way. It’s a very well directed movie that captures the atmosphere and overall feel in a similar way to the book. It was effective in getting me to care about the characters the same way, though a couple plot points towards the end felt a bit off. Because of that the film lost some of its grip on me as the runtime felt kinda like it was dragging near the finale, but overall most of the heartbreak and love I felt was translated well. I feel like I would have similar thoughts on the movie had I not read the book prior, but I understand where a lot of the hype comes from regardless. Glad to have finally seen it. Might bump it up to a 9 at some point but I feel confident with the current rating.

1 month until pride month <3




(SPOILERS FROM HERE ON OUT — DISCUSSION OF WHAT WAS CHANGED THAT I DIDN’T LIKE)


Jack’s death comes about rather suddenly and I was hoping it would feel more drawn out because that’s what really allowed to me feel Ennis’s pain while reading, and I think the final conversation with Alma Jr. wasn’t entirely necessary. I was expecting a quicker end after his final convo with Jack’s parents, which really worked for me, but it was at that point that I felt like the movie was slightly overlong.
Tags: amc dine-in yorktown 18, me time!, read the source material prior

Name: Petite Maman
Review:
I’m in the car driving home with my parents now and have been for roughly 20 minutes, but I haven’t even bothered to start a review until now just because I found it so enjoyable to sit and smile in the lasting effect of this movie, and also to talk about it with my family. This isn’t a film that necessarily blew me away or really made me think about my own life, but I can’t think of a single thing wrong about it either. Just a wonderful and simple film from start to finish that I always loved and I can only imagine it getting better on future watches. I’ve been waiting roughly a year to see this now and it was worth the wait. I can’t wait to dissect it more in my head as I sit on it over the next few weeks.

Fun fact; my mom declared that if she had a list of top 10 films, this would crack it. So glad she loved it as much as she did.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: A Beautiful Mind
Review: Watched in psych. Same reaction to this as Atonement for me. A very flawed film that has a lot of glaring issues for me, but I still somehow found an emotional attachment to it ?? Russell Crowe does a great job, the screenplay is great occasionally, and it’s very well paced with some effectively tense moments. But at times it’s very cheesy, there’s no creativity in the visual style at all, and Jennifer Connolly is badly miscast. There’s a lot of the real story they didn’t get right, but I still felt for the characters felt and wasn’t really taken out of the story, until the end. I liked it a fair bit at the end of class, but now that I finally have time to review this, it’s been a couple hours and it’s already lost its grip on me. No idea how the Academy thought this was better than Mulholland Drive, Yi Yi, In the Mood for Love, or Lord of the Rings in most, if not all ways.

Name: Scream
Review: Amazing way to close out film studies. This is such a fun movie to watch in class. Also I never noticed before that the use of music in this is perfect. Glad this never happened to me in high school.

Name: Vortex
Review: Tone is everything, and Gaspar Noe films establish a tone early on that stays consistent the entire runtime. Whereas Enter the Void and Climax kick off with blaring EDM, Vortex kicks off with a slow French ballad. From the earliest minutes of the film, I could already tell that Gaspar was truly trying to make something different and far more grounded than ever before. My year-long anticipation felt warranted right away, and it never really left me. This is a slow burn in every sense in the word, but I cannot emphasize enough how much I truly felt burnt at the end. I’ve wondered for a long time what a Gaspar film would be like in theaters, and… now I know. No other theater experience has even come close to impacting me like this. By the end, I was sweaty, numb, and literally couldn’t take my eyes off the screen. I wasn’t able to catch my breath the entire last 20-ish minutes, and even as I walked out of the theater, I felt like I was re-learning how to use my hands. But somehow, this wasn’t done through typical Gaspar graphic content. It was done through the story, performances, editing, sound, and pacing. This is Gaspar Noe’s best work and I can only see him getting better with time, as he has done from the very start. It’s extremely realistic in how patient and even how tedious the characters go about their lives, it shows multiple perspectives that near flawlessly reveals character development, and every other scene I was thinking about either myself or how this thing was made. The way Noe and the two DPs film conversations in this is genuinely nothing I’ve ever seen before, and the way they move the story along works better than most movies I’ve ever seen. The split screen is absolutely perfect and the entire movie is basically parallel editing, but it all functions so well. The descent to the end is transporting and visceral, and for almost all of it the main thought on my mind was “life is so precious and we need to enjoy it.” This worked better than even Synecdoche, New York for me. I’m in awe of every aspect of this movie. It’s flawlessly made, it gave me an unforgettable reaction, it’s realistic while staying true to artistic style, it made me contemplate my life but still left me feeling positive at the end, it’s a whirlwind of everything I want out of movies. I’ll have more to say in the future but I feel different after this movie and it’ll live rent free in my head for a while. See it as soon as you can.
Tags: amc streets of woodfield 20, me time!

Name: Doctor Strange
Review:
A different movie from Vortex.

Ripped off Batman Begins and Inception but did Tenet before Tenet which is really funny. The car accident at the beginning and the Doramamu scene at the end both look like video game cutscenes and there are some other weird moments and plot conveniences but I had a fun time. It’s a cool movie.

Name: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness
Review: Genuinely don’t understand the hate for this movie. I had an awesome time, this fixes a lot of issues I’ve had with Marvel in the past and does a fair amount of new things as well. It’s not perfect and there are definitely some things it gets wrong. A fair amount of the CGI towards the start was simply just not good, there’s a fair share of bad MCU dialogue, and at times it feels pretty overstuffed. The runtime was cut down a fair bit so that might have something to do with it, but it’s a bit hard to follow and at times the amount of characters and plot points being introduced comes across as disorganized. All that said, I haven’t enjoyed myself this much with Marvel since 2019, before my film obsession really took off. I love that the story gets a lot of its propulsion from personal conflict rather than a typical hero vs villain arc; even if it isn’t very complex or layered, it mostly all worked for me and kept me really engaged the whole time. The structure isn’t perfect, but I didn’t find this a predictable movie and the payoff was generally satisfying. There are actual emotional stakes and the story really allows you to decide if you side with Wanda or Strange more. I think America Chavez is a great character as well and I can’t wait to see her solo movie; she was always a welcoming presence on screen and the representation is awesome. There is a fair amount of Marvel fan service, and in the grand scheme of the movie most of it serves little-to-no purpose, but it was nice to see this happen without feeling too terribly on the nose. Beyond the very solid narrative, this is a surprisingly well made movie. It suffers at the start from feeling like it has two different directors, but when the Raimi feel kicks in it never really lets up. There’s a lot of creative and visually cool imagery in every scene, a majority of the CGI worked, the sound design is awesome and allowed for some nice tense moments, and just in general Raimi’s style is apparent. There are a higher amount of Evil Dead references than I was expecting, and some of them are a little goofy but I enjoyed all the nods. Most of all though, the action is all stellar and every battle scene makes its case for being one of the best in the whole franchise. There’s one in particular that uses sheet music (no more details will be revealed) that I genuinely loved. The horror element is a great added touch, and I feel like the number of genres at play during the whole movie are all integrated naturally; hopefully more future MCU joints do the same. All around, even if not perfect, this is maybe my new favorite Marvel movie. Despite its flaws and stipulations, it accomplishes a lot of new things for the franchise, all of which I hope continue. It’s a blast to watch and I was always enthused by what was happening. While I’m sure I’ll notice more flaws the second time around, this is the kind of Marvel movie I’ve been waiting for. It works for me as a film on its own and re-ignited my excitement for the future of the franchise. The rating may go down fairly soon, but this experience was definitely an 8/10. Also yes I saw the Avatar 2 trailer. It was kinda underwhelming and weirdly calm in its vibes, but the movie looks pretty cool.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, imax

Name: Inland Empire
Review: Jared Leto thinks walking with crutches everywhere on set is method acting, but he should try the Inland Empire technique of literally entering the fucking world of his movie sometime. (Dawg I can’t write any more on this that’s cohesive after just one watch. It’s such a fascinating world with really cool filmmaking, the runtime is warranted, and the experience is basically everything you’ve heard. I have some theories on the themes piecing together in my head already, mostly to do with gender roles and how women are treated in Hollywood, but what I can say for sure now is I liked it a lot. It’s gonna stick with me for a while and I’m glad it will)

Name: The Northman
Review: Heimir: “And you, little cub… what are you?” Amleth: *farts* Aurvandil: “Ahhhh… I smell a clever pupil!”
Tags: cinemark tinseltown north aurora, w/ quinn

Name: The French Dispatch of the Liberty, Kansas Evening Sun
Review:
At its worst, it's well made and entertaining but emotionally ineffective.

At its best, it's a joyful celebration of the stories humans tell and how beautiful creativity is.

Not a perfect film and some parts don't work as well as others, but this was the perfect time to give it a second watch. My life is buried in a sack of shit right now and I kinda fucking hate myself, but I needed to re-enter this wonderful world to turn my mood around.

Somehow I had forgotten for a bit why this worked so well for me the first time, but now I completely remember. And while I definitely haven't forgotten why Wes Anderson is a top 3 American filmmaker and top 10 filmmaker period for me, it's been a while since I've seen anything from him. His work is necessary to my growth as both a person and an aspiring member of the film industry.
Tags: blu-ray, w/ quinn, watched while sick

Name: The Umbrellas of Cherbourg
Review: I love the effect this movie had on me. It started out super strong and I was seriously into it, then it petered out for a bit after the halfway mark, but then it returned to glory in the final chapter and... damn. This has one of the better endings I've seen in any movie recently. The way a question asked by the story early on is wrapped up so naturally, the way the music is used, the bittersweetness of how Guy and Genevieve handle the situation... the entire movie is great but the ending really stunned me. The whole thing is immaculately made from all standpoints and I only really see it growing on me on future watches. As I mentioned in my review of The French Dispatch, this has been a seriously rough week. And though it's getting better, this was a beautiful watch to help with that transition. I really got a lot out of this and I can't wait to let it resonate on me for the future, both near and far. It's a film that feels like it'll serve different emotional purposes at different points in my life. Easily the most joyful first watch I've had since Everything Everywhere All at Once (I know that wasn't too long ago, but I mean this as high praise).
Tags: criterion challenge 2022, criterion channel, w/ dad, w/ mom, watched while sick

Name: Ex Machina
Review: I was on some goofy shit the first time I saw this and didn’t like it a whole lot. I’m questioning the conditions I watched it in though, because although I was able to recall a good bit of what I saw, this almost felt like a first watch through the different way I was absorbing everything. In addition to the hype for Men coming out this week, I’ve been meaning to rewatch this recently for my screenplay, which I think allowed me an extra layer of satisfaction; I went in expecting to take a lot of notes on how the relationship between Caleb and Nathan plays out, but I ended up gaining more inspiration from the conversations between Caleb and Ava. The way the two of them form a strong bond neither of them expected, specifically how their gradual surprise is portrayed, really struck a chord with me, and every time there was a title card for a new AVA SESSION I got more excited. This isn't a super fresh concept or anything, but it's told through beautifully realistic visuals and sharp writing, and nearly every part of it is inspiring in some way. The only slight reasons it misses the full marks is because I still have some mixed feelings on the ending (probably just still resonating) and it took me a bit to really feel like I cared about Caleb, but I still fucking love it, and I’m embarrassed that I’ve been sleeping on it for a year and a half.

Name: Just Wright
Review:
How the hell does my film teacher expect me to write a paper on a movie that’s so surface level lmfao

Great for representation (which is what I’ll be writing about) but this is the most boring thing I’ve seen in at least a couple months. Super cliched and forgettable romcom story that took me two sittings to finish. Queen Latifah’s performance was the saving grace

Name: Mean Girls
Review:
Forgot to log this but THIS is the last film I watched in film studies. I know I said the same about the last two, but it turns out my teacher kept packing the year until the very end. Thankfully, however, I didn’t have to write about this at all; it was a closer movie in the sense that she just let us sit back and enjoy it.

Anyways It’s good, my first time watching this in at least three years. Reminds me more of Clueless than I thought. Not something I love, but an entertaining movie. Reminds me why I’m excited to leave high school in a couple days, but at the same time it’s an energy and environment I’ll weirdly miss in a year or so. I had a… semi-typical high school experience I’d like to think, but at the same time it’s almost too fresh to fairly assess. I dunno, I think there are better high school movies but this was the one that fate decided would be my last one I watch while I’m still in high school.

Name: Good Will Hunting
Review: Good Will Hunting, more like Good AF. I really loved this a lot, not much to say that hasn’t already been said. Very lovely little group of characters and tons of powerful moments and monologues. I still miss Robin Williams deeply. Final movie of Psych 1. 8.5

Name: 3-Iron
Review: I LOVED the first 40 minutes. There's hardly any dialogue, and the fact that Ki-duk primarily relies on what the audience sees to keep the story moving worked so incredibly well; I adore the storytelling and I'm starting to realize that I'm kind of a sucker for movies that don't rely mostly on the writing to build worlds. The remaining 45 minutes aren't bad per se, but I found them less interesting and not as well paced, up until the last 5-ish minutes which were great and felt more like the first half for me. The direction the story went left me more confused with what I was supposed to be taking away from it all, and some plot points I felt were fairly predictable. I kinda wish I was left with more of an impression since I was so moved by everything the film offered for a while, but the change in pace took me out. Overall though this offers a really interesting story and it's very strongly made in every aspect. Great character development and wonderful cinematography, even if it doesn't all come together for me. (it is already starting to grow on me though) 8.0

Name: Annihilation
Review: I didn’t quite fall in love with this like I did Ex Machina, but I’m still not sure why I wasn’t so big on this the first time. It's really a stunning movie with a lot of really amazing filmmaking (it’s the sound design and score for me, but the production design, VFX and camerawork aren’t far behind at all) and a head-spinning ending, only really held back by the screenplay along the way. Can’t say the flashbacks really added much for me and the dialogue at times kinda took me out, but it’s still a super engaging movie full of intriguing ideas. It’s a big swing that doesn’t quite bat 1000, but still leaves you thinking about a ton after it’s over. 8.?

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
Review: There are two possible outcomes to this multiverse: 1. Everything Everywhere All at Once gets nominated for Best Picture, it comes back to theaters, and I get to see it in theaters again. 2. Everything Everywhere All at Once gets snubbed, but at least I saw it a third time today so I wasn't robbed of one more theater experience. (There's a lot more I could say on how much this movie means to me but I don't think anyone will read it all so I'll just leave it at this)

Name: Men
Review: The scene where Harper is in the tunnel making a song with her echoes is fantastic. It has nothing to do with the rest of the movie. It's also my favorite scene in the movie.

Name: Zoolander
Review: Not a good watch before starting a new job, it was boring and I'm almost asleep now. Idk, I liked the production design and the 2001 scene was funny.

Name: Crimes of the Future
Review: Some interesting imagery and decent direction bogged down by a boring screenplay that’s incoherent at a handful of points. In other words, an early work from a great director. Cronenberg’s traits are at least prevalent enough in this for me to assume that I’ll really like his other movies. 5.5

Name: Wendy and Lucy
Review: The search for purpose is a rocky one, littered with the dangers of things out of our own control, but all we can do is make the smart decisions, be good to each other, and never give up. A deeply American film in a way I didn't expect. This is a beautiful little story that feels so authentic and lived in; I found genuine attachment to Wendy and her struggles, and Reichardt's singular vision brought out every emotion, making my stomach drop at every turn. I love it. 9.0

Name: Top Gun
Review: Loved this movie when I was in middle school. At age 18, it's cheesy but I still kinda liked it. Really good cinematography and cool music. Didn't leave much of an impression on me but I had fun. Not as gay as I was hoping. Glad I could finally finish a movie after being depressed enough; I started five the last few days but quit part of the way through on all of them. 6.5

Name: Top Gun: Maverick
Review: Very enjoyable movie. Great filmmaking, good performances, not really any major complaints. Some of the references to the original were a little on the nose, but overall its definitely one of the best legacy sequels. Incredible sound design, though I wish there was more music. I also wish the movie ended less predictably, but the story overall I liked quite a bit more than the original. All around I’m satisfied and it lives up to most of the hype. 7.5 pretty close to an 8 though.
Tags: kendall 11, w/ dad, w/ mom, gdx

Name: Orexin
Review: Chad is one of my friends on Letterboxd, so I was excited to see that he made a new short film. Overall, I liked it; the cinematography is impressive and I actually really love the way music was used. The acting was decent for the most part, some of it made me unintentionally laugh, but I believed the characters and was interested in watching all the way to the end. The story was a bit of a mixed bag, with a strong start and end but fairly aimless middle, but it's coherent and the plot itself is unique, executed well. Can't wait to see what Chad does next!

Name: Columbus
Review: The cinematic equivalent of waking up in the morning after a super rough night, taking a deep breath, and realizing there's a light at the end of the tunnel. A therapeutic movie.

Name: Love
Review:
The main character has posters of Saló, Taxi Driver, Angst, and Birth of a Nation in his room. He claims 2001 is his favorite movie and he wouldn’t be a director without it. He’s also just a huge asshole and verbally abuses every woman in this movie at some point.

What I’m getting at is it feels like Noe wrote himself into the character at a dangerous level. I kinda gave a shit about the story at a few points and it’s certainly a very well made movie, which makes it better than his first two films, but it’s still impossible to connect to unless you’re an incel edgelord. It’s also telling that I watched this in the middle of the day but forgot to log it until now (12 hours later). The level of self-insert was unintentionally funny at times and took me out every time he did that, of course a guy who has a fucking Saló poster in his room is going to be a piece of shit LMFAO how did any of the girls not see that as a massive red flag I can’t get over it.

Name: Sunday Bloody Sunday
Review: The first half is way better than the second as far as entertainment, pacing, and just being able to emotionally attach me to the story goes on, but the whole thing has excellent performances, directing and filmmaking. Hopefully when I watch it a second time, whenever that may be, I'm in a better headspace, since I think that had a little bit of an impact on my enjoyment as well.

Name: The Fly
Review: Wow, I mean… wow. I had a feeling early on I would really like this movie, but I’m kinda floored by it. Don’t need to say much on the visual effects that hasn’t already been said; every technical aspect is just amazing. Seth Brundle’s character arc is really interesting, and I found a surprising emotional attachment to his development. Ronnie is a really amazing co-lead, I was always on board with her and I think she complimented the central story very well. Stathis is interesting too, though I wish he had more of a redeeming moment before the end which is really my main criticism here, since he played a critical role in how the story wraps himself up. In all, the story is an unforgettable tragedy with amazing pacing, insane ending, great screenplay, brilliant aesthetics, a dope score from Howard Shore, and fascinating ideas. I loved it. I’m gonna watch some cute animal videos before I go to sleep tonight. I love body horror done right, and I’m now even more excited for the rest of Cronenberg’s work, especially Crimes of the Future tomorrow. 9.0

Name: Tangerine
Review: A needed re-watch for a film I've grown to love even more since my first time, almost a year ago. I had forgotten a lot more of this than I thought I did, but I think that just made all the more difference and I was quickly reminded why I value Tangerine so much. As I've mentioned before, Sean Baker is a filmmaker who prioritizes authenticity and ethical research when developing his stories, and that really shines here. As funny as this movie can be sometimes, it's all realistic and, under the surface, a very upsetting reality. I'm not trans myself, but the narrative still speaks to me both as someone who is openly pansexual and still isn't fully comfortable with identity. Sean respects that and tells this story in a wild but respectful manner. His directing is amazing, from getting great performances out of every actor to working with professional-looking iPhone cinematography (there were a couple moments that didn't work, including one where Sean's shadow with his phone is visible) which didn't bother me nearly as much as it did the first time around. I'm still not the biggest fan of all the music choices either, but I understand why they're all used far more on second watch. The roller coaster to the end is a wild, heart pounding journey, and once you reach the end, it's a super tender and emotional climax that is satisfying and pays off incredibly well. At this point I'm just rambling and you've heard it all before, but this movie means a lot to me even if it isn't 100% seamless. It emotionally connects me, inspires me in my own creative work, provokes thoughts, and probably won't ever leave my mind. Quite honestly, years out, I think this will end up one of the most important films to my growth as a filmmaker.

Name: Crimes of the Future
Review: At the turn of the century, David Cronenberg conceived of the story for a movie concept called Painkillers, sharing the exact same story as his latest film, but after a few years he put the project on the backburner due to a loss of interest. A decade and a half later, it's finally been made, and unfortunately all I can say is I understand why he lost interest the first time around. For a movie that's technically been "in the making" as long as I've been alive, it still doesn't feel like a complete product. The first 20 minutes showcase an intriguing start with cool ideas, even if it kicks off a little rough, but the following 30 minutes expand on this in close to every way. It really got me excited and I was fascinated by the dynamic body horror sequences. I could tell there was social commentary at play along with that, even if I wasn't sure yet what the movie was trying to say. It's all complemented by solid acting, amazing visual effects, seamless sound design and a well-paced, eerie atmosphere. All the ingredients for a successful piece of Sci-Fi horror. Unfortunately, this is where it peaks, and the movie descends into surprisingly similar territory as Men from a couple weeks ago; a great first half full of great ideas, all of which become frustrating exposition for the rest of the runtime. The last hour of Crimes of the Future isn't convoluted or flat-out bonkers like Men's second half is, but it's just so boring, which is arguably worse. Between the two halves, the film moves from a healthy mix of story and body horror to becoming ONLY story, and I suddenly realized almost none of the characters got any sort of actual development, and they're nearly impossible to connect with. Some of the stronger characters are painfully underused as well, most notably Kristen Stewart who doesn't have any more than 15 minutes of screen time. And it doesn't help that the seemingly endless dialogue in the final hour just seems to go in circles until the last 15 minutes, which briefly return to body horror but don't add much to the story, instead introducing a bunch of random subplots that all end up wasted. I really wanted to like this movie, I'd been excited about it for quite some time. The precise and complex first half is keeping me from labeling this as a bad movie, but it results in a forgettable and bland second half which feels like it came straight from a first draft of the screenplay, so I can't give it anything higher than a 5.0. P.S.: Supposedly, the movie got a 6-minute standing ovation at Cannes. My theory is that everyone who would've boo'd the film fell asleep at some point.

Name: Over the Garden Wall
Review:
:,)

Need to sit on this some more. I really loved it though.

Name: Theorem
Review:
Don’t understand a lot of this. Not sure if I’m supposed to, but I feel like a lot of it went over my head and I didn’t get very much out of it. Some parts of it are really good and really interesting, but as a final product, I found it well made but ineffective.

I based my Pride Month watchlist on a BFI article, and their definition of what qualifies a film as queer must be super broad and non-limiting since this made it on. There’s a really small subplot where one character discovers his homosexuality, but it’s barely a part of the story. I’m gonna re-adjust my list even more since, in my opinion, this doesn’t really count lmfao.

Name: Show Me Love
Review: A chaotic mess of a movie, but a very good chaotic mess at that. Every scene is apt and the story is always moving. I think this is a very accurate and honest portrayal of high school culture, and there were a number of scenes I found myself really connecting to; the story has aged well. The entire movie is told through handheld cinematography, kinda in the style of Dogme, but it still has a stark, vibrant look to it that I really dug and added to the overall feel. It's not all perfect, I can't say I love the pacing (specifically the transitions between scenes and some emotional payoff), but I'm really glad I found this film and I can only see it growing on me in the future.
Tags: youtube, pride month '22, watch for screenplay

Name: Hoop Dreams
Review: I wasn’t sure on first impression if I’d keep the movie at the 10/10 rating, but the more I think about it the more I remember both why it hit me so hard in the first place and how it hits me in different ways today. I’m too tired to write a multi-paragraph review now, but in short it’s the best Chicago movie ever, it tackles way more themes than you might assume, and the emotional delivery is incredible; you feel everything the characters are feeling in every scene. It deserves the status as both a classic and an important part of film history and seeing it in theaters in Chicago was an amazing experience.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Jurassic Park
Review:
Was too harsh on this the last time I saw it. Not perfect but a lot more to admire than I remembered, even without the major nostalgia factor I have for it. It’s also just so much fun lol.

Even if franchises get really bad (this one probably has), I hate when people say franchises are tired or need to die. Especially when there are really great first movies like this one; franchises don’t really deserve to end. If recent movies in them aren’t good, then change the creative team. I think there’s a good future in Jurassic Park, just replace Colin Trevorrow with a good director and hire some actually talented writers.
Tags: hbo max, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Spencer
Review: I get so happy rewatching 2021 movies that I love, and this one was no different. I had a similar experience to this today as I did in theaters, thinking it started off incredibly well, followed by a lull that felt not very well written and it took me out, then for the rest of the film I was locked in and in love with everything I was seeing. I forgot a lot of scenes in this, some of which did the movie favors (didn’t love the first couple Anne Boleyn appearances), but the biggest of these were the monologues that are full of a lot more emotional impact than I remembered. The beach scene at the end genuinely might be one of my favorite movie scenes of all time; my god, the chemistry between Diana and Maggie is such a small part of the movie but may just be the one that hit me the hardest. Don’t need to say much more on the performances or filmmaking details, there isn’t a single one of either that isn’t incredible. Greenwood’s score was snubbed at the Oscars, this entire movie was aside from Stewart’s nomination. I love Spencer a ton.
Tags: hulu, w/ dad, w/ mom, watch for screenplay

Name: Je Tu Il Elle
Review: The biggest takeaway I got from this is that it was probably a big influence on early American indie cinema, and I mean that as a very good thing. The simple and straightforward premise, long unbroken takes, naturalistic and aimless feel, representation that you don't really see in mainstream movies from that time period, monologues that are seemingly random but end up leaving a much bigger impression on you later, it ticks a lot of the boxes. As expected, this is a slow movie, but the pacing didn't make it feel any longer than it actually was which is impressive. I was engaged the whole time and every character holds a lot of interest for me. All around this was a very good introduction to Chantal Akerman, I'm even more intrigued by the rest of her work now. 8.0
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2022, pride month '22

Name: The Lost World: Jurassic Park
Review: I had only seen this once before when I was 9, and the memory I have is being really upset by watching the T-Rex eat that one family's dog so I was interested to see how it would hold up almost a decade later. This isn't really a boring movie, but it's a great example of a sequel introducing new ideas and doing nothing remarkable with them. There was so much great set up and a lot of opportunities for new iconic scenes, but they all end up being bland and forgettable. I still think the story is a decent one and there are some fun dinosaur moments, but overall this wasn't a huge disappointment OR a nice surprise. I'll forget about it next week.
Tags: hbo max, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Hedwig and the Angry Inch
Review: I loved it... I think. This was a shock to the system; so brash, loud, angry, colorful, just an insane experience. There's a ton to unpack and I don't really feel like talking about the movie past that, but it was definitely really well made, unique, and anything but forgettable. I need to sit on it some more before I can really have a solid opinion on it. I feel like a lot went over my head, just because so much was thrown at me and exhilarating me, but that's a good thing. Yeah.

Name: Irma Vep
Review:
Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent if it was good.

I liked this a lot but didn't fall in love with it like I was kinda hoping. I think a part of that is I’m on a roll with finishing my short film screenplay and a film about the chaos of making a movie didn’t really match my vibes, but I still found enjoyment out of it. The dialogue about film is extremely engaging, the meta aspects with French cinema and filmmaking were integrated very well, and Maggie Cheung did a really great job as expected. Unfortunately I can't really say the same about the rest of the supporting cast; there weren't necessarily any bad performances, but all their characters felt underdeveloped to me. They were able to keep the story moving and they add some layers, but I was noticeably less interested when watching scenes that featured only them and not Maggie. And in the end, I didn't feel like I gained as much as Assayas wanted me to. Despite a lot of positive aspects to the film, this falls more into the category of a movie I admire rather than one I personally connect to.
Tags: hbo max, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Idiots
Review: I wanted to watch this because I've gotten really interested in the Dogme movement recently, and it is also featured on Sean Baker's top 4. I'd also be lying if I said I haven't always been at least intrigued by everything in Lars von Trier's career, especially since I think super highly of Melancholia and Dancer in the Dark. Unfortunately, this was not either of those. I fucking hate The Idiots. I wanted to punch anything I could while watching this. This is a movie of interesting scene setups and interesting ideas... and it's all used for one of the most ableist movies I've ever fucking seen, and in the end, there's no reason for any of it. Lars von Trier could've written his characters to do fucking ANYTHING else weird to drive home the point that they're anti-bourgeois and against societal norms, but his provocative ass had to write them to constantly mimic disabled people. As an autistic person, I genuinely tried to get on his level but I just felt hurt and drained as I watched a bunch of characters who represent low self-esteem I’ve had my whole life for 117 MINUTES. Fuck the hell off. Last thing -- This was written to be a comedy and von Trier himself said he intended the movie to be amusing, but the only times I laughed were the multiple shots with a very visible boom mic; how brave of him to release a film with four (that I counted) outtakes in the final cut.

Name: The Bitter Tears of Petra von Kant
Review: For quite a while I thought I was watching the most aesthetically pleasing endurance test ever, very well made with some super enticing conversations. I didn’t get much of a personal connection or feel consistently engaged, but I recognized that there was a lot at play. Then the final half hour hit and damn, yeah I realized that everything came together and this is a GOOD movie. I’m very impressed.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2022, pride month '22

Name: The Thing
Review: This was just fantastic in theaters. I’m a sucker for a good summer movie trip (there’s a certain vibe and nostalgia to it that I crave), and it was made even better by the fact that I forgot a lot of the story. It’s 40 years old but hardly has aged. I’m still reeling from the practical effects, cinematography, production design, pacing, tension, subversion of expectations, etc. etc. I got a lot of thoughts on this movie, but I’ll keep it at this; I didn’t get much of an emotional punch out of the movie, which is one of the bigger indicators of a favorite for me, but otherwise this is one of the most complete movies I’ve ever seen. I love it. 9.5

Name: Fish Tank
Review: The second film I've watched this week for the first time in almost 2 years, and also another one that I hardly remembered anything about. I've been meaning to watch this again for quite some time now, since it checks a lot of the boxes for the films that I see myself most often falling in love with (an uncompromising look at how teenagers behave, low budget filmmaking, personal story to the director, etc.). Now that I'm working on production of a short film of my own, I figured it would be a good time to finally check it out again. This is definitely a film I need to sit on, since it left me feeling very moved but also empty and sad by the end, but I know right away that it is really great and has only grown on me. It feels like a movie Sean Baker was inspired by, and I mean that as high praise. The first hour is just fantastic and I was on board the whole way through. The second hour still kept me engaged, though because the plot makes some directional changes that come completely out of left field, I have some more mixed feelings, but I think it all works. Andrea Arnold has a unique approach to filmmaking, but I admire her style a lot. The entire thing has great directing and cinematography (DoP Robbie Ryan's other credits include C'mon C'mon, Marriage Story, and The Favourite among many others), intense performances, a very passionate screenplay, and effective pacing that leaves a lot of emotional beats lingering in my head. I never really saw myself in Mia's character, but I definitely related to a lot of her emotional journey (for the first hour and a half and the final 10 minutes, mostly). All around an excellent movie with a lot of authenticity and emotion. Side note: it sucks to see Katie Jarvis didn't go anywhere after this. Sucks even more to see she isn't a good person. 8.5 but it might be a 9.0 the more I think.

Name: Sinister
Review: I enjoyed this more than I expected! Despite a rough start, the movie eventually starts developing an interesting story that combines the found footage and supernatural horror sub-genres well. There are a fair amount of cliches, some of which do take away from the experience (it relies so much on sound even when it’s completely unnecessary, the jumpscares missed more often than not), but others are played on effectively, such as the family stupidly buying a house with a haunted past, which is later a topic of serious discussion between the parents. The characters are pretty standard and the screenplay isn’t anything to write home about, but they kept me engaged enough the whole time, even if parts of each are kinda dumb; these parts don’t stand out to me as much right now compared to the parts that were effective. Aside from the start, this is a well paced movie  It doesn’t have any groundbreaking visuals, but there are still a lot of good-looking shots and solid horror direction in that aspect. The more I think about the movie I’m sure it’ll come apart, and I’ll most likely bump the rating down soon, but I had a good enough time watching this to keep it at a 7.0.

Name: The Black Phone
Review: I’m too tired today to say anything else on this movie other than it’s an interesting concept with good performances bogged down by a stupid first draft of a screenplay. I give it a little benefit of the doubt because it was adapted from a short story with the same story elements, but every time it tried to be serious with the supernatural element, it ended up coming across as goofy to me and it took me out every time. There’s enough to enjoy that I might bump the rating up, but for now I’m keeping it where it is mostly based on my own disappointment. Some updated thoughts, 6/26 — I really do respect the fact that the middle school characters were written and performed in a way that they’re easy to connect to, which is a difficult task since I generally hate these characters. Also, part of it is the time period, but there’s a strange kind of 80s movie vibe to the atmosphere that I dig a lot; it’s almost nostalgic in a way. Also, I removed a thought I had initially that this isn’t as effective or memorable as Sinister, and won’t be talked about years out from now the same way. I still think Sinister is the better movie, but I think because of the unique concept and the fact that it’s doing really well at the box office, I was wrong. It isn’t on the money level of something like It, Us or A Quiet Place, but it’s exceeding my expectations.

Name: Pleasantville
Review: I loved the cinematography, production design and story. Feels inspired by Back to the Future and Blue Velvet.

Name: Unsane
Review: I obviously love the way Tangerine is shot both on a technical level and how it innovated low budget filmmaking, but seeing the iPhone used in a way that comes off as more controlled and polished was very interesting. This was the main reason I watched the movie, and I'm mostly not disappointed in that regard. There are some scenes where it really doesn't add much, and others where it tried something cool and just looked stupid, but these are few and far between. The story starts off well, being emotionally engaging with good performances and vague but interesting social commentary along the way. It doesn't really explore new territory, and it never really felt like a story that needed to be told, but I was interested enough in the characters to see where the plot would go. After about halfway through, every aspect that interested me up to that point was never really a big part of the story again. It turns into a frustrating mix of horror and crime, focusing solely on shock value and closing out the character arcs. But even for a film that's only 98 minutes, it feels like it outstays its welcome, building up towards an unsatisfying and pointlessly insane finale. Points for the cinematography and performances, but nothing else. I guess I should've expected this from the synopsis, but this was a surprising entry to the cinematic universe of men making horror movies about female trauma (along with Men, Last Night in Soho, and mother!). In the end, it isn't a movie that'll really stick with me though; it's more along the lines of Alex Garland's recent horror movie than the other two in that it was really just a waste of time, despite some good technical aspects. 6.0 (barely)

Name: Obi-Wan Kenobi
Review: Obi-Wan Kenobi is definitely not a story that has to be told, but it makes up for it in many ways; I'm impressed with how the creative team managed to tell the story in a way that feels like it has some soul behind it. There are a lot of plot points and large portions of episodes that really don't have stakes, purely just because I know which characters stay alive in A New Hope and other new characters have their arcs used up before the very end, but the writers still managed to turn some of this around and make some effectively memorable sequences (the confrontation between Vader and Obi-Wan in the last episode, the entirety of episode 3, etc.). It's not an amazing show overall, and it has a few other problems (a lot of CGI in this looks very unfinished, some parts are just boring), but I have to say I'm satisfied with and enjoyed the final product. 7.0
Tags: disney+, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: jeen-yuhs: A Kanye Trilogy
Review: I started this a couple days thinking I would watch it all alone. But my parents both walked in the living room maybe 15 minutes after I started, instantly were hooked, and have joined me on the ride the whole way. It’s a testament to the accessibility of this miniseries, in addition to the reasons I loved it myself. 1 > 3 > 2, but they’re all great. 8.5

Name: Apollo 10½: A Space Age Childhood
Review: It’s one interesting idea that Linklater realized wouldn’t be long enough for a full feature film so he added in an extra hour of reminiscing and nostalgia of living in Houston in the 60s. This kept me engaged for a while, but since it’s really most of the story, I didn’t see much purpose in including the Apollo 10 1/2 subplot. Not a bad movie, and I really liked parts of it, but in the end it feels like a screenplay that needed more refining. I wish I liked it more, but it has a lot in common with better movies I’ve seen before tackling similar subjects. 6.5
Tags: netflix, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Jackass: The Movie
Review: A delightful group of fine and distinguished young gentleman lollygagging with various trinkets and doodads and partaking in vast amounts of horseplay. (This is simultaneously the most homophobic and gayest movie I’ve ever seen) 7.5

Name: Jackass Number Two
Review:
It's only 1 AM and my day is already ruined courtesy of Steve-O's fish hook and leech skits. Thanks for the images I can never remove from my brain </3

Funnier than the first with its best moments but this one has also aged much worse with a couple skits to the point of ruining some enjoyment. I liked the more cinematic feel at the start and end though with the bulls and musical number.

Name: Jackass 2.5
Review:
A more cinematic BTS special feature, I don't know if I feel like rating it or not. The more I watch, the more their antics get boring and frustrating for me instead of funny; the Jackass series is definitely something I have to be in the right mood for. The most interesting part for me was to see how toxic the work environment was for some of the members, Ehren deserved much better.

For those who have seen it, is it worth my time to watch 3D or should I just jump to Forever? (the reason I started this binge in the first place)

Name: Hiroshima Mon Amour
Review: From the alluring first 15 minutes which immediately found a personal hook on me all the way to the end, I was locked into this movie in a way I haven’t really been before. The first half felt like it went by really quickly, but the second half felt very drawn out; I mean this as a good thing. Fantastic filmmaking, amazing central performances (this was the first film role for Emmanuelle Riva from Haneke's Amour; she was incredible from the start of her career), great usage of music… it’s just such an interesting movie. I’m left confused by the ending itself and questioning some parts but I guess that’s the beauty of films like this. 9.0-ish (maybe 9.5)

Name: Independence Day
Review: This is a solid movie, my parents love it and I haven't seen it since I was like 9 or 10. That being said, today is not a day worth celebrating given the current American political climate. Use today to check up on people you know whose safety and/or well-beings have been compromised by any recent US events.
Tags: hulu, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Marcel the Shell with Shoes On
Review: Non-binary representation

Name: Happy Together
Review: I loved it but I already know it deserves a re-watch. I've been doing a lot better mentally the last couple days than I have recently which is obviously a good thing, though because of that I didn't find a super strong personal connection to the movie. The cinematography is close to perfect, Tony Leung and Leslie Cheung were phenomenal, the soundtrack I probably don't need to say anything about because you already know every song is used flawlessly, Wong's direction is obviously very apparent and works incredibly well, and even though I didn't click with the depressing tone there were still a lot of emotional moments that I felt. I'll watch it again in a couple years when I've lived some more life and (hopefully) gotten some more experience with relationships, I pretty much guarantee the score will go up then. 8.5
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2022, w/ matt g, w/ rhys

Name: Thor
Review: Enjoyable watch because of nostalgia, it’s not a bad movie but I think most of what I got out of this leads back to the fact that this was one of the first PG-13 movies I ever saw. Regardless, the cast is really strong, even if a number of them are underused. Despite a lot of flaws (mostly dealing with the CGI, a lot of it has aged really badly), I was still able to stay engaged for most of the movie which is impressive, part of that might be my good mood today though. Either way, MCU movies tend to grow off me over time + this was directed by Kenneth Branagh so who knows, the rating will probably go down.

Name: Thor: The Dark World
Review:
This is a major motion picture. It was shot with a camera by a group people. Some did a good job and actually tried, others didn’t and it really shows.

Translation: I was expecting to hate it and thankfully it’s not like one of the worst things I’ve ever seen. It’s not good either though. The first half is mid and boring, the second half is slightly less boring but much worse made. I remember it being more exciting when I was 10.

Name: Chicken Run
Review: Love this movie. It’s so funny, the stop motion animation is fantastic, the concept and story are grounded and surprisingly dark for a kids/family movie, the characters are all awesome, it’s structured and paced effectively, it invokes strong emotions even though I’ve seen it countless times since I was young… such an underrated movie. Not all these aspects are perfect, there are some jokes that don’t quite land for me and some scenes make me briefly cringe, but it ticks nearly every box on animated movies for me. There are really no big issues to comment on. Fantastic Mr. Fox perfected the stop-motion animation animal crime caper, but this laid a lot of the groundwork for Wes. It’s also the funnier movie in my opinion. 9.0
Tags: car dvd / on a roadtrip, w/ quinn, dvd

Name: Thor: Ragnarok
Review: Fun movie. Everything I said in my last review holds up. It's pretty good from a visual and technical standpoint (great production design especially), Taika's style isn't as strong here as it is in other of his films but it's still apparent, and both of these things considered, it has more of its own voice than 90% of the rest of the franchise. The screenplay is still a super mixed bag though, from the humor which only worked for me half the time to the story which, not unlike The Dark World, feels too reliant on The Avengers and Age of Ultron at a number of points. Valkyrie is a good character, Hela is a decent villain, but the dynamic between Thor and Loki, especially considering they just lost their dad of 5,000 centuries, is super lacking and barely expanded upon. Nonetheless, I have fun with it and it's still one of the better movies in the MCU. 7.0

Name: Thor: Love and Thunder
Review: Have a lot more to say so I’ll add some initial spoiler-free thoughts… — The first half was a drag and really just didn’t work for me, the second half was far more enjoyable. Both have their pros and cons, but the difference between the two makes it feel like two entirely different creative teams; the screenplay and direction were all over the place but had moments where they hit well. — The dramatic scenes worked way better for me than the humorous scenes (I only laughed a handful of times and some scenes were so heavy on jokes and one-liners), though I don’t entirely blame Taika for this since there was apparently lots of studio interference (it’s Disney lmfao no surprises there). Valkyrie remains the best character in the Thor series IMO, Jane would be competing for that spot after this movie if the conflict between her and Thor was more thoroughly developed during the first half. — Christian Bale is genuinely really good. His performance reminded me a lot of Heath Ledger’s Joker which is super funny in hindsight but it works in the moment. Anyone who says Bale would’ve been better as Joker than Batman will really enjoy this aspect. (This is also the first time I’ve ever seen him play a British character) — Despite my criticisms, the one overarching criticism that I’ve had between all the Thor movies, them being too reliant on other movies in the franchise, is mostly absent here. The movie and story are far from perfect, but overall it lines up as a good closure to Thor’s individual story. There’s absolutely no reason for a fifth Thor movie to come out in the MCU. 6.0
Tags: amc naperville 16, dolby, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: The World of Us
Review: So I recently came across the TIFF theater's summer lineup, and one of their series for this year is a Korean Cinema showcase. Obviously I don't live anywhere near Toronto so I can't see any of them in theaters, but I figured there's nothing wrong with following along with their schedule at home. It isn't quite the same experience, but it's an excuse to watch more Korean films. Anyways... this was awesome. The premise immediately hooked me as something I'd love, and while it got off to a bit of a rocky start, it only gets better as it goes along, and my initial expectations were warranted. There are some aspects to this that make it feel like a directorial debut, such as there not being very much visual style and some dull pacing, but the great child performances, screenplay, themes, and buildup to the end are all super effective and memorable. By the end, I really felt a strong connection to the struggles of these girls, and all the emotional beats just hit. It's not quite like an all-time South Korean great, but it stands out among a lot of the most famous of their films for me. 8.5 for now, but it might go up the more I think about it

Name: A Grand Day Out
Review: Pretty sure this invented my imagination the first time I saw it. Still haven’t gone to the moon yet for some gourmet cheese but I’ll get there one day <3
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: The Wrong Trousers
Review: Don’t have anything witty to say here — it’s just so awesome. A great thriller in its own right with surprising emotion, amazing animation, and it’s really funny. Fairly certain this was my introduction to comedic timing from a young age. I love it so much.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: A Close Shave
Review: Not quite as good as Wrong Trousers but not too far off. It doesn’t have as coherent of a story nor is it as funny, but I’d argue it has more emotional value and more effective plot twists. I also admire how many genres it tries to tackle, most of them successfully.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Wallace & Gromit's Cracking Contraptions
Review:
Almost forgot to watch this too. Basically an anthology of micro short films but Nick Park is still playing around with a lot of genres, which is slowly becoming one of the most admirable aspects of this series for me. I’m probably gonna take some inspiration on telling a story in two minutes from some of these.

In my opinion this also features the best arrangement of the main theme song.

The ten shorts ranked:

2. The 525 Crackervac
3. The Soccamatic
4. The Autochef
5. Shopper 13
6. The Snowmanotron
7. The Tellyscope

9. The Snoozatron
10. A Christmas Cardomatic
