Name: Wallace & Gromit: The Curse of the Were-Rabbit
Review: There’s really nothing holding me back from giving this full marks, I LOVE IT! It was a favorite as a kid, and it holds up at age 18. Driving through Chicago morning traffic to see this in theaters was so worth it. MASTERPIECE!!
Tags: music box theater, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Marcel the Shell with Shoes On
Review: Very sweet movie! I love the characters, cinematography, production design, music, and just general creativity behind it all. The contrast between Marcel and Dean is what really makes it a moving experience IMO, their dynamic is fantastic. It’s just a cute little concept executed very well. I will say I didn’t get quite as strong of a reaction from it as I was hoping, in fact I think I wish the movie was a bit longer so we were able to live with certain aspects of the story for a longer time i.e. Dean’s personal life. I know he said he was making a movie about Marcel and didn’t want to talk about his past, but I wish I was able to connect to him more. I don’t think the movie was rushed, I just wish there was more of it (this is a good thing). Despite this, I had a lot of fun and would gladly recommend it to anyone for something to brighten your day or find some creative inspiration. 8.5
Tags: regal webster place, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Peppermint Candy
Review: This was my first Lee Chang-dong film and as expected, I feel like shit after watching it. I'm pretty conflicted on it, as it's REALLY well acted and made, but it didn't really leave me with any lasting thoughts except for feeling depressed. The first half hour is easily the highlight, making for a fantastic start, and from that point I figured this would be a new favorite. But as the story goes further back in time, I only found myself connecting every so often, with the exception of the army sequence near the end which was close to perfect. I was never not engaged, but as I mentioned, I left the film feeling empty without taking anything too serious out of it which I can assume is part of the point, but is also pretty frustrating. 7.5

Name: Black Mirror: The National Anthem
Review: If I didn't have subtitles on, I probably wouldn't have understood a thing they said except for "Dogme 95." Also, I'm worried the film buff characters at the hospital are similar to how I look when I talk about film. This wasn't super well made despite having some interesting ideas, but I'm definitely excited to check out more Black Mirror in the future. 6.5

Name: Get Out
Review:
A lot to think about and admire after finally giving this a second watch. The suspense aspect is insane (can’t think of many other movies that have a better slow burn — every minute is intense, I don’t know how Jordan Peele was able to keep the same propulsion going the entire runtime), the screenplay is incredible with so many subtleties and details that are easy to miss but integrated so well, Peele’s directing and almost every aspect of the production is seamless, the performances do I really need to say anything? I’m still left with some small questions about the final act, but this left a lot more images and ideas in my brain than it did the first time. Fantastic movie.

Overall, this is just quintessential horror of the movie era we’re in; a future classic for sure. It’s the first film from a unique voice (who else would’ve possibly been able to combine comedy and horror like Peele, given his Comedy Central experience and horror fascination?), it exposes and plays off horror cliches, there’s layered social commentary, writing with precise structure, and it just spins your head with every scene.
Tags: blu-ray, w/ dad, w/ josh k

Name: Us
Review: More to think about and appreciate the movie the second time, similar to Get Out a couple days ago. I remembered this better over time, but still forgot a lot of what makes up the ending so it was an interesting rewatch but, while I don’t think it’s as bad as something like Men, I do feel like Peele’s ideas could’ve used more revising so it could’ve related more to the social commentary of Hands Across America and the Underground Railroad (was this even mentioned outside of the opening title card?). In the end, it feels like nothing more than a singular idea, barely touched upon; it’s made up for by the beautiful choreography of the final fight, but I still feel like it could’ve been so much more. Nonetheless, this lies my only set of complaints. Until the end, the filmmaking is fantastic (pacing, makeup and editing are perfect, and the look of the cinematography in both this and Get Out is amazing and totally something I want to emulate in my own work), the performances are mind-blowing, the chemistry between the Wilson family is just so endearing, the soundtrack is mostly used very well, and while it overall isn’t as touching or satisfying as Get Out, I’d argue this film is both scarier and funnier. It’s captivating and feels shorter than the actual runtime. It isn’t an instant horror classic like Peele’s first film, but it’s still very thought-provoking and anything but forgettable. 8.5
Tags: blu-ray rental, w/ dad, w/ matt g, library

Name: Black Mirror: Fifteen Million Merits
Review: Watched this last night before going to bed but haven’t logged it until now because I’m still letting it ruminate. Pretty quickly I found myself enamored with this, and to be honest it never let up. The look of this movie is great as expected, Daniel Kaluuya never disappoints, and the screenplay is surprisingly layered (at least it was surprising compared to how underwhelming The National Anthem was). It’s a simple but effective and inspiring use of three-act structure, always engaging and building up to a very solid end. As a whole, I found this movie very inspiring in both its visual style, structure, general vibes, and simplicity whilst still leaving a lasting an impression. Still deciding what to rate it, but, if you couldn’t already tell, I loved it.

Name: Nope
Review: I have a strange feeling this is my Titane of 2022. It has a lot of great scenes, it’s certainly a well made movie, and there’s a good amount of Jordan Peele’s great stylistic and narrative traits that continually keep things going. But on first impression, it also feels like multiple different movies going on at the same time, with a sense of lacking plot direction and a mind-bending finale that would’ve been a lot more satisfying had everything preceding it been executed better. It’s bizarre, it’s going to be divisive, it’s far from perfect, but in the end I’m left feeling more positive than negative on it. And much like Titane last year, I’m expecting the second watch to clear a lot of this up for me. Hype is dangerous. (I already have a lot to say, so hopefully I’ll have the motivation to actually finish a full review) 7.0
Tags: amc oakbrook center 12, w/ dad, w/ matt g, imax

Name: George Washington
Review: Shoutout to unflinching looks at rural America with great characters, beautiful cinematography, impactful monologues and lots of emotional value. Gotta be one of my favorite genders.

Name: Time Bandits
Review: Very fun movie with amazing production design and solid comedy. Super engaging for the most part with a lot of great ideas to back up the concept along the way. It feels too long and didn't really know how to end, but I enjoyed it a lot. One of my dad's favorite movies and he's been trying to get me to watch it for quite some time, so it's cool to finally check it out.
Tags: hbo max, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Nope
Review: I liked it way more the second time, just knowing what to expect and being able to seriously enjoy myself with that in mind. I’ll go into a lot more depth why on Instagram as soon as I can, my life schedule just really fucking sucks right now but this is a movie I genuinely want to give a full review for. There’s a lot to marvel at all around, but for this log I’ll just mention that Hoyte van Hoytema’s cinematography is easily in the discussion for best of the year so far. Also I guarantee Sean Baker cried happy tears when he saw the film cameras at the end. 8.5
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, imax

Name: Y Tu Mamá También
Review:
Alfonso Cuaron was easily one of my favorite directors when I was starting out on my film obsession, but I haven't watched any of his since October 2020. I was super excited to finally watch one of his movies again, and I'm happy to report it didn't disappoint.

I forgot just how good Cuaron is at telling his stories; even when sections of the story should very clearly be boring and uninteresting, he finds a way to keep it engaging. This is a story of escapism, the shortcomings of those closest to you, and slowing down to take a look at life, both the good and bad. None of this hit me until closer to the end (although this felt like a first watch, I barely remembered the movie in the first place) and I wasn't quite sure how to feel for a while, but when it did hit, it was fantastic. I'm seriously teetering between an 8 and 9 on this, but I honestly kinda want to watch it again soon so we'll see what happens.
Tags: gene siskel film center, 35mm, me time!

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
Review:
I’m over the moon. I’ve missed this movie so much. I won’t be able to write anything on this without turning it into a thesis paper, so for now all I can do is thank this movie and the Daniels for creating an experience that entertains, enlightens, connects, inspires, and reminds me of a lot of what I’m missing in life and what I can do to improve. This is the cheesiest thing I’ve ever written, but this movie genuinely means so much to me.

This was the most spontaneous theater trip of my life. My sibling had to go downtown (Chicago) for a doctor’s appointment, I tagged along to do some photography since I’ve gotten really into that recently, and we were both just like “hey, we’ve got an afternoon to kill. Why not go see a movie?” Anyways, time to go home and write that thesis paper

Name: My Dinner with Andre
Review: Huh... wow, that was more of an experience than I expected. In addition to being an admirable piece of low-budget filmmaking (a budget of just $475,000), I wasn't quite prepared for how deep and thought-provoking this film gets; it was uncomfortable at times. Maybe my guard was let down by the fact that the first 40 minutes were only interesting every so often, and my preconceived idea of this movie was from Community. But for the entire last hour, I really felt like I was in their fascinating conversation, like they were my old friends too. Real-time structure, when used properly like in this and many other great films, is easily one of the most effective screenplay structures. The movie is certainly a product of its time, and while that occasionally shows itself in the wrong ways, it majorly makes this a more interesting watch. Nonetheless, it still spoke in large volumes to me and I was constantly drawing things back to my own life. I left the film feeling sad, but not in the kind of way that just drains you and leaves you with nothing to think about. It's the good kind of sad where you want to combat feeling the same way the characters do; the motivating kind of sadness. I see this as something that grows on me over time as I live more life. As much as I was listening and paying attention to all that was going on, some of their discussion topics were big question marks for me, but I just view that of the beauty of being young. 9.0

Getting my first experience with this in theaters was insane, and it was really special to meet Mary Sweeney (Lynch’s film editor) afterwards.

Name: Fresh
Review: Fresh is a decent horror film full of ideas which unfortunately only stick the landing half the time, making in an apt fit for 2022 horror thus far (even more apt before Nope). It starts out rough, with the most mediocre and typical romance arc imaginable which lasts for a dragging 38 minutes. Once the horror kicks in, you could infer the low quality was intentional to present a contrast between two genres, though the transition felt more like a cool effect than something actually affecting. As both a romance and horror, it feels unfocused but polished. From the 38 minute mark to the end, there's a potent perspective on power struggles in relationships which is admirable (especially compared to Men earlier in the year) but inconsistent, frequently losing sight of what makes it engaging and falling into cookie-cutter romance territory. It's a long-feeling movie in general, with an unexpected amount of hanging out and trailing off. When the messages hit, they really hit and make for satisfying moments, I wish this was more the overarching romantic focus. As a horror movie, it's not half-bad either, even if not very original. The relationship horror clearly takes a lot after Midsommar and Get Out, and while there are still instances of effective shock in both the violence (especially the great finale) and cannibalism, it pales in comparison to the movies that have changed the genre's game recently. (It also has brief echoes of Saw and Climax in its visual style and The House That Jack Built in Sebastian Stan's character) All around, I don't regret watching Fresh. It's generally entertaining with merits, Sebastian Stan and Daisy Edgar-Jones do a good job, but it's only really recommendable for the entertainment factor. It's a worthy pick as a time-killer, but there are several better horror movies to choose from. Either way, I'm intrigued to see what Mimi Cave does next; I think she has a lot of potential to become a prominent filmmaker. 6.0

Name: Pickpocket
Review: I've come to realize French New Wave is hit-or-miss for me, and unfortunately this falls more into the miss category. It's definitely a well made movie, and there were moments where it was interesting and really engaging for me, but in all I didn't find this to be a movie that consistently connected. I blame part of this on the fact that I had an 8.5 hour shift earlier today and I didn't quite give this 100% of my attention (I'll be sure to save slower cinema for less strenuous days in the future), but at the end I was just kinda like... "okay, cool I guess." There are lines of dialogue, well-composed shots, and aspects to the plot that normally hook me in movies, but in this case I felt unmoved. The main character has layers to him and is backed up by a good performance, but his (intentional) lack of emotion was too much for me. The style is almost nonexistent, which I get is on purpose because of French New Wave being what it is, but, similarly to the characters, contributed more in part to my boredom. I think I'll give this another try one day since I do wish I got a more memorable experience (it honestly feels like a 7 or 8), but for the time being I didn't think very highly of my first Bresson. 6.5
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2022, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Good Luck to You, Leo Grande
Review: What a fantastic movie. The filmmaking is nearly spotless, the performances are fantastic and should be praised, and the screenplay, holy shit. I don't agree with how every plot point was used (there's a gay subplot that is a bit stereotypical even if used effectively), but I haven't felt this much emotional truth in quite some time. Scene after scene I got more hooked and felt all the beats of this dynamic, and the runtime progressively felt like it was moving faster. It's not a real-time structure, as the story takes place over the course of a month, but it comprises of four real-time sessions along the way which are edited to give it a real-time feel, and I love it. Real-time is quickly becoming one of my favorite structures in film, and maybe the biggest reason for that is a naturalistic feel to every conversation. It's fantastic directing, writing and acting when done right, and this one happened to do all that while touching on several topics that hit close to home for me. I could keep going, but this was an amazing experience and I really hope that it gains some traction for awards season because it deserves it.

Name: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial
Review: 10 years ago I saw this for the first time in theaters for the 30th anniversary Fathom Event. Today, I saw it for the 40th with my family, in what might be my last theater trip with everyone before I head off to college. Sitting a row behind us was a mom with two kids who were very clearly watching it for the first time; with all the emotional beats, we heard quiet crying or laughing or gasping behind us. Normally this is annoying, but in a weird way this added to the experience and it was really fun. The movie is imperfect, in fact I was doubting for a while whether or not it held up in my memory, but things slowly grew on me and I've realized why this is a good movie and why a lot of people hold it in such high regard. To me, this is far more of a character movie than a story movie. The character writing and how the actors were directed commands the audience; even if the story leaves you scratching your head (it left me feeling that way at times), there's still an emotional connection at play because everyone on screen is so intimately developed. The movie is entirely told from the perspective of the three kids, and you can argue all you want that they're annoying and not the best actors, but Spielberg was able to get a lot out of them and, through this, added a successful extra personal edge. Everything you feel throughout the runtime sticks with you after the movie ends, flawed story and all. Emotional connection aside, it's a really well made movie. It's much more contained than Indiana Jones, Jaws or Close Encounters, but the suburban feel is utilized very well and Spielberg is able to maintain the same sense of wonder and excitement. The cinematography and sound design are great and use perspective incredibly well, the editing is effective where it needs to be, and a lot of the visual effects hold up today (everything still looks good except the flying bike green screens). In an era where sci-fi was about pushing the limits and creating the biggest spectacle possible, E.T. came along and became the biggest box office success in movie history with sci-fi on a small scale. It wasn't the most groundbreaking movie ever, but it was certainly a step forward in many ways while still being heartfelt and entertaining like any summer movie should. 8.0 (probably would be a 7.5 without the great theater experience)

Name: Hunger
Review: Finding the right words to do Hunger justice is anything but an easy task. This film floored me. It is a disgusting and desolate experience, at times heartstopping and at others slow burning, that defines a standard for biopics. Unlike cookie-cutter Oscar bait, Steve McQueen’s singular vision of an important event in recent UK history relies primarily on stunning visual storytelling (courtesy of the brilliant Sean Bobbitt) coupled with outstanding performances to tell the unforgettable true story. The Achilles heel of so many biopics is their reliance on dialogue force-feeding the audience information, but Hunger is the antithesis of this. There’s only one important conversation in the entire 90 minutes, and it’s a flawlessly written discussion that encompasses the character of Bobby Sands and says everything that can’t be said through the cinematography. Furthermore, if you haven’t yet felt strong sense of empathy from the brutal violence at this point, then this conversation is going to establish an emotional attachment in you as Bobby breaks down his chilling perspectives on all the film’s central themes. Hunger is the opposite of a spectacle, portraying every aspect as realistically and raw as possible, and it’s this ambition for accuracy that turns it into a truly incredible film. No second of the runtime feels unnecessary; you’re locked in and all your senses are attacked from start to finish, leaving you feeling like a changed person. The technical and emotional merits of McQueen’s debut feature aren’t just what biopics should strive to be, they’re what all movies should work to accomplish. 10

Name: Robots
Review: I haven't re-visited a childhood classic in quite some time so this was a fun way to start the day. Really funny and a solid message about class divides. A bit standard and predictable plotwise, but it's an easy and very entertaining watch. I hope this is me when I meet Sean Baker one day.

Name: The Lobster
Review:
Kind of a disappointing rewatch. I gave it this same score initially, but it really grew on me over time as I slowly realized a personal connection I found to it and I put it in my favorites list with a 9/10. I’ll always consider this a great movie, there’s a lot of praise in many aspects, but compared to my preconceived expectations, I admired it more than I personally connected to it.

I think part of it is I’m experiencing movie burnout, as I’ve having watched a lot of movies recently and don’t have much else to say here unfortunately. I’ll be pacing myself more in the near future, and hopefully I’ll return to my former opinion for this on third watch (whenever that may be).

Name: Pariah
Review: Pariah is loud, piercing, passionate, and honest. It’s a celebration of self expression, or more fittingly the struggle to embrace how you express yourself. A truly personal and deep understanding of the discomfort of hiding the secret of being LGBTQ+, told through an authentic and beautiful perspective that really shines through the camera. While my personal experience was never as intense as it was for Alike, Rees’ movie tells a story that feels crucial to listen to and still hits a fair amount of personal notes for me. I loved it.

Name: The Art of Self-Defense
Review: The Art of Self-Defense is confident in itself, but its courage only halfway warranted. This really didn’t leave much of an impression on me at all. The performances are all good, there are a few funny moments in the first half, it’s well shot and edited, and I related to the way karate is portrayed towards the beginning as someone with years of martial arts experience. However, I found the story to be very predictable and the themes not speaking in large volumes. The characters Eisenberg and Poots play have layers to them and I was able to connect to their individual struggles, but Novola plays a really boring villain with no real real development aside from the fact he doesn’t see a place for women in karate. Not only is this aspect undercooked, but for a film with a feminist message, why is the central plot so heavily focused on Eisenberg’s conflict and not Poots, the female character? The film briefly redeems itself with a somewhat satisfying and dryly humorous ending, but it still supports the blandness of the themes. I really wish I liked this more, but I’m going to forget I saw it in a few days. 5.5

Name: Shame
Review: Similar to how Hunger is an unbelievable directorial debut, anyone without a knowledge of Steve McQueen watching his sophomoric effort would assume it's the work of a veteran. His talent for writing, directing, and visual eye are only rivaled by a single-digit number of other filmmakers working today. And it's not just McQueen's full potential on display here; Michael Fassbender and Carey Mulligan give even better performances than I remember, and I held them incredibly high in my memory. Their bravery, vulnerability, emotional range, and line delivery are unmatched by anyone else I've watched in recent memory. On the surface, they're regular siblings with very little in common, but they align in the guise of crippling mental issues. Mulligan is generally outgoing and bubbly, a talented singer who's tough and always fights for what she wants, mostly the simple attention of her older brother. Perfectly encompassed by her sole vocal performance of "New York, New York" by Frank Sinatra, she believes moving in with her brother in NYC is the change she needs to turn her life around. For her, New York is a vibrant dreamscape. Fassbender struggles to communicate at all; from the subtext it can be inferred that the pair had a rough upbringing, and this clearly has left permanent scars on him in more ways than one. Just the sight of his sister makes him uneasy, and he is unable to commit to an emotional relationship, only seeing value in his rampant sex life. NYC is everyday life for him, full of all the prostitutes, business, and booze needed to make the world go round. DoP Sean Bobbitt portrays the city and the point-of-view of each sibling in a sleazy perspective, full of soft colors and meticulous framing that somehow capture the feelings of both dreaming and restless anxiety. As you might expect, the story is a downward spiral from beginning to end and is flawlessly paced, and given everything I've praised so far, it's damn close to a 10/10. Quite honestly, I see myself still thinking about the images and ideas of this movie days out from now, but on initial impression, I remember a stronger emotional reaction from my first watch nearly 2 years ago. The rating is honestly a gray area for me still, since I'm only taking marks off for small personal reasons, but don't be surprised if I bump it back up in the near future. It shouldn't take away from the fact that this is a fantastic and deeply wistful film that burns its way into the minds of everyone who watches it. It's brilliant in nearly every way, plain and simple. 9.5

Name: The Batman
Review: The power this film held over me on my first experience may never be matched, but The Batman remains immensely impressive on multiple levels. In the guise of every other big movie that's been released over the course of the last 5 months, Matt Reeves' smash hit has still has managed to hold its ground as one of the best films of 2022 thus far. Having gotten the chance in theaters to really take in the technical mastery more than anything else, giving it another go at home has allowed me to appreciate how tightly written the entire thing is (not to say I was any less stunned by how phenomenal all the camera, editing, and VFX work are, even off the IMAX screen). Having had quite some time to sit on the movie, I noticed a lot of the social inequality messages at play here and appreciated this more than I had previously. It's a theme we haven't really seen in a superhero film before to such a layered extent, (unless you count Joker) and it's combined with the typical spectacle and entertainment factor in excellent fashion. A lot of it is elevated by the character arcs of Riddler and Selina Kyle, providing two engaging perspectives to the same coin: deep down, they're hardened and resentful of how the corrupt Gotham political landscape affected their upbringings, but they end up with opposing end goals and only one prevails. It sounds simple in these terms, like I'm describing most hero vs. villain stories, but the runtime is fully utilized in how both characters are fleshed out without feeling like the exposition is overdone. This is what every movie's goal should be, but given the superhero movies of recent years, it's a rarity in the genre and deserves to be praised here. Moreover on the Riddler's arc, the way his character is developed also plays a large role in what makes Pattinson's portrayal of the titular role so enthralling. This isn't a by-the-books superhero movie where the end goal is to simply defeat the villain and call it a day, with maybe a small lesson learned. At the start of the movie, Batman is a flawed hero, seemingly attacking criminals more as therapy for himself than to help the greater good. As he listens to and learns about Riddler's background and motive behind what he does, he realizes that Riddler isn't alone; there is a large disadvantaged population in the city who feels the same and agrees with his views, just to a less violent extent. After taking a step back and absorbing the bigger picture, Bruce decides to fight crime as a means of making a change in the city, by genuinely learning from what drove Riddler to insanity and figuring out how he can prevent it from happening in the future. It's a moving change in character that feels emotionally layered and represents a more effective hero journey than any other superhero movie I can think of. Subtext non-withstanding, The Batman is one of the most memorable of the films of the year due to the intricate central story. Simply put, it's a great detective thriller and stands its ground against many of the subgenre's classics it was influenced by; across each viewing, I can't help but notice parallels to the work of David Fincher, and Reeves was able to extract much of what makes the mysteries of Seven and Zodiac so effective on a larger scale. Similar to how the themes of class really grew on me this time around, I picked up on a lot of details within the mystery and the roles of many characters that I previously hadn't, making a re-visit rewarding for anyone who has seen and loved it before. I was a bit skeptical on whether or not I'd still be mystified by this movie, having seen it twice before, but this exceeded my expectations in how rewarding my return to this world proved to be. Similar to The Dark Knight, there's a lot of rewatch value to be found, and because of this I believe it will become equally as legendary in the coming years. The Batman has found itself in every discussion I've had for the best of 2022 thus far, and it will remain this way even through the very promising awards season ahead of it. Unlike the Riddler's puzzles, it isn't difficult to understand why. 9.0

Name: The Scar
Review: Watching Kieslowski's first feature without much knowledge of him prior proved to be a challenge, but I was able to finish it without any problems. To its credit, it's a very well directed film and is visually interesting with almost every shot, though the story feels like one that could've been told in just over an hour. For the most part, this is a well-told drama with some very interesting sequences and solid character development, amplified by multiple unique perspectives on the building of the factory, but I didn't feel like anything more was being accomplished after a certain point, and the runtime became a bit of an endurance run. Still, not really a waste of time, but the best I can say is I look forward to watching more of Kieslowski's later films. 6.5

Name: Where Is the Friend's House?
Review: Where Is The Friend’s House, like most movies about kids, tells as plain and simple a story as any movie could have. Kids aren’t going to have the same knowledge and skillsets as a cast of adult characters would, not capable of taking on a challenge or adventure on a large scale, and are generally confined to either home or school. But it’s exactly these limitations that make this film so memorable. In addition to seeking the titular friend’s house, the main obstacle faced by main character Ahmad is simply overcoming the fact that he’s a kid. Every time he approaches an adult on his journey to ask any question, he’s rarely given much attention or an answer longer than a single-digit number of words. If they come across a kid who isn’t doing busy work for them or finishing school assignments, there’s no reason to make them a priority: they just see the kids as lesser versions of their younger selves. However, Ahmad is a completely respectful and kind soul, and isn’t phased by any of this; he’s determined to help out his best friend while still considering the needs of the adults around him. He may not go through any major character changes, but as Kiarostami contrasts him with his hostile environment in multiple different ways, the appreciation I had for him grew stronger with every passing scene. Kids are historically underappreciated by older generations, believed to get weaker and lazier with each passing year, and this film does a great job both conveying that message and providing a strong rebuttal to it from start to finish. From a technical standpoint, there’s nothing to criticize here. Given the scale of the story, it excels in basically every category. Most of the actors here had little-to-no prior experience in performing, but they do excellent work. The only comments I have to mention here are that I wasn’t super blown away or given a strong emotional reaction out of it all (especially in comparison to The Florida Project and The 400 Blows, both of which deal with the struggles of being a kid and established a stronger personal attachment), but the wholesome and effectively simplistic story make it impossible to dislike. And I only see myself liking this movie more in the future. 8.5 (right on the border of being a 9.0, couldn’t decide on what to rate it for the longest time)
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2022, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Ambulance
Review: After watching films by Kieslowski and Kiarostami yesterday, I'm continuing to enrich my cinematic palate. Martin Scorsese would be proud. This has the typical Michael Bay style you might expect from the undiagnosed ADHD camerawork to the annoying dialogue, good music being badly misused and obviously giant budget, but one thing I didn't expect was only being half-engaged for the most part. This should be gripping from start to finish, but using all the same tricks on repeat for a 90 minute chase get old surprisingly quickly here. There's no reason for this to be over 2 hours long. Bay's movies, no matter how stupid, usually succeed in keeping me interested from start to finish, whether it be due to solid buildup, engaging character chemistry or just pushing the bar on how absurd a story can possibly be. With Ambulance, all the cards are laid out on the table in the first 30 minutes, but it just works off of this for the entire chase until the ending switches things up a bit. To its credit, the characters are some of Bay's strongest, but given how interesting they are at the beginning and ending, I wish they did more than just screaming at each other nonstop for the 90 minutes in the middle. I'll be sure not to get involved with my estranged brother in crime for money; it's happened before, but this really reminded me of the worst case scenario. Imagine if this same concept was written and directed by George Miller or Edgar Wright.

Name: Cronos
Review: I love Guillermo del Toro a lot. Pan’s Labyrinth and The Shape of Water are fantastic movies, and while Nightmare Alley isn’t really my vibe, I can’t deny it’s very well made. Even outside of his body of work, he’s just a really interesting guy to hear talk and he’s got an amazing creative brain. Watching his directorial debut only increases my love for him, and reminds me why he’s a filmmaking idol. Cronos may not have the same scope and scale or sheer wonder of his later hits, but it’s still great body horror with an interesting take on themes of mortality and greed. The effects, production design and cinematography are excellent, all the performances were entertaining (Ron Perlman plays a great villain in particular), the musical score is surprisingly fantastic and always used well, and it’s just a simple but effective story that subverts expectations well. It’s a little too weird at times to the point of taking me out of the immersion, and some minor aspects of the plot I saw coming from a while away, but this is an early del Toro effort and I know from his more recent work that he vastly improved with these aspects. I was thoroughly engaged by the path things took, and had a great time. This is a story that Guillermo first wrote when he was in college, so it’s inspiring to see such an early idea from him take form. 8.0

Name: Three Thousand Years of Longing
Review: Despite some moments of real magic, Three Thousand Years of Longing is all around a disappointment. It feels devoid of interest, rushed, and all around messy. It almost feels like George Miller only took this project on as something to distract him from the stress of making Furiosa. As a regular movie, it's a mixed bag... as a follow-up to Fury Road, it's a cry for help. The biggest issue for me is how the film leans so heavily into the drama genre, and really lacks in the fantasy. There are certainly interesting themes at play, namely fate, the way we interpret world history, and making sense of our personal pasts, but despite a strong and thought-provoking start with all of these, it didn't last. The narrative is almost entirely exposition as the genie gives us every tiny detail in his 3,000 year journey. As basic as this sounds, part of every great movie is a strong narrative, which this only is half the time. If you took away the spectacle, the movie would essentially just be a bone-dry version of Good Luck to You, Leo Grande. Compared to all of my favorite fantasy films, this is missing a lot of potential wonder. The movie is certainly visually interesting, and some of the production design and effects were stunning, but compared to what Miller has shown us what he's capable of, most of it is more akin to recent Marvel movies; believable enough, but hardly distinct and at times very transparent. Worse yet, the central romance is wholly forgettable. We spend hardly any time getting to know Tilda Swinton's character, more often than not just seeing how she responds to the genie's stories. We briefly get a feel for the kind of person she is, understanding enough that she's a reserved and solidate person, but there are no layers to this. At one point early on, I felt an incredibly strong attachment to her character, and I was aching for more of that. The only romantic background we get is that she's content with being single: after an hour of the genie essentially trauma dumping on her, she decides her first wish is to start a relationship with him. The remainder of the film is a fragmented romance that feels like it has multiple endings, which I give the benefit of the doubt because of how it somehow managed to emotionally engage me, but on paper it could've used a lot of restructuring. This is a movie full of potential, one I really wish I enjoyed more. But despite the strong points, no aspects to it feel complete and it's a shell of what this concept could've been with more passion and/or a different creative backing. In the end, I'll only really look back at this film scratching at my head and thinking of better fantasy dramas.

Name: La Haine
Review:
This film has such a strange balance between being angry and sympathetic that doesn't really reveal itself until the final 30 minutes, but when it hits, to say it leaves its mark is an understatement. In specific, there's something genius in the way Vinz carries the most ambition of the three whilst also being the most nihilistic that really pierces me, connects to me, and leaves me feeling like a different person (not to undercut Hubert and Saïd with this; all three are some of the most electric and memorable personalities in any movie).

It's a film that leaves no loose ends and ties every little detail together at some point, the sign of an expert screenplay; this is the kind of satisfaction I crave when watching any movie, and Kassovitz managed to naturally pull this off in a hyper-realistic narrative. Every shot tells a story in its composition. The music is used sparsely but effectively. 95 minutes felt like 60. Bumping this up to a 10 felt impulsive as the end credits rolled, but ultimately it's the right decision for me.

Name: Don't Worry Darling
Review: More aptly titled The Mid-trix Visually interesting from a production design and at times cinematography standpoint (Matthew Libatique <3), and Florence Pugh is on fire as always (though everyone else around her being so bad certainly helps her case), but wow this movie is long. The first two thirds are obnoxiously stiff and repetitive; I unironically started dozing off after a certain point. The last third is pretty out of left field, but it justifies the movie’s existence, is surprisingly fun, and offers some interesting ideas (it’s a unique twist on the living-in-a-simulation trope), even if it’s kinda rushed. I have more to say, but at the end of the day I think this would be a good movie if: - the first two thirds were majorly cut down and more motive was given for the ending. - it didn’t take itself so seriously. Hot Fuzz and The Lego Movie are effective false reality movies because, aside from being much better written and structured, they turn the ridiculous parts of the story into satire. Here, it’s all just dramatic. I still liked it more than Men.
Tags: amc river east 21, dolby, w/ rhys

Name: Jerrod Carmichael: Rothaniel
Review: Love can tell a million stories

Name: The Seventh Continent
Review: … I don’t know dude. This was well made, I had an awful time. Thanks Michael.

Name: Sound of Noise
Review: Average band kids moment This was really funny, great sound design and tons of super creative scenes. It’s not amazing and the story is kinda bland but I really enjoyed it for the most part. We need more movies like this that use sound to the fullest potential, just with better screenwriters. I’m tired so I can’t reform this review into anything better or cleaner, I’m just jotting down thoughts. High 7.5
Tags: vimeo, for class, w/ braden, w/ jack s, in the dorm

Name: Good Night, and Good Luck.
Review:
This movie is a page in a textbook. It's harmless and doesn't do anything glaringly wrong, teaching you some history, but in the end it's just words on paper. Everyone does their job correctly as the movie is coherent and not badly made, but, save for some occasionally intriguing dialogue, this isn't a memorable experience.

I admire this movie for its historical accuracy and don't really take issue with what it's saying, but given how this movie was made during such a major era in US news and politics, I wish the writers tackled all the same themes in a story of more relevancy and importance. I get that the intent was to make a story about these TV executives, but it's not the side of this historical event I really care about watching, especially under the direction of George Clooney. Between that and the lack of any sort of impressive filmmaking, it reeks of awards bait.
Tags: hoopla, for class, in the dorm

Name: Pearl
Review: Minor plot spoilers: Every performance is great (Mia Goth shouldn't have been as incredible as she was) and it's never a bad looking movie, but it only justifies its existence half the time. I've already forgotten a lot of the first 2/3 of the story on the walk back from the theater, all the horror is effectively unsettling but kinda predictable, and the ending was almost great but ended up being a buzzkill. There's a one-take monologue that has to be at least 6 minutes long near the end where Pearl completely breaks down and it's genuinely an fantastic scene, like a mini Hunger. It gives new perspective on the entire preceding story and is thoroughly gripping, but unfortunately the screenplay decided not to stick with the effective character drama and retreated back into the typical horror shit. I see this growing on me and improving on rewatch the same way X did, but on first impression it's an entertaining mixed bag.

Name: Eraserhead
Review: “In heaven, everything is fine”

Name: In the Mood for Love
Review:
The camerawork of every conversation in this movie begins from a moderate distance away from the characters, as if you’re in the same location and happen to be listening in on an intriguing discussion between two strangers, then every shot gets closer and closer to Tony Leung and Maggie Cheung as we’re drawn in to their relationship. Even when we maybe shouldn’t, we can’t help but eavesdrop on every audible conversation around us.

This is a beautiful film, deeply human and understanding of several human experiences. I don’t have any more to say as of now, I’m still soaking it all in, but seeing it on the big screen was breathtaking. I lost myself for almost the entire runtime and loved it. If this romance really sticks with me days out, I’ll likely bump the score up to a 10.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, me time!, for class

Name: Joint Security Area
Review:
The second 2000 film from Asia that I watched a second time and bumped the score up from an 8 to a very high 9 this weekend.

This hit me though. A story that’s conceptually political but is really just about basic humanity. Aside from some pacing that took me out at the end of Act 2, I have nothing to critique. A brilliant procedural movie on individual value, being truthful, breaking boundaries, and friendship. I love Park Chan-wook so much.
Tags: dvd rental, in the dorm, library

Name: Bros
Review:
Dear straight people who are worried about seeing this film,

My name is Josh! I identify as pansexual which, if you don't know, is basically the same thing as bisexual (/j). In other words, I like women and I still really enjoyed this movie! You will still be attracted to the same people and still have a good time if you go see this in theaters, so what's holding you back?


In all seriousness: this movie is a really good time. Well made, very well acted, and quite funny, even it a little overlong. Some parts feel written by studio executives and the humor doesn't land 100% of the time, but in the end I felt emotionally attached to this story.

I hate to see it bomb in theaters because, while it isn't some huge landmark in film, it is enjoyable and deserves far better. Don't need to say anything more about the representation (though I was seriously aching for better bisexual characters and ANY pansexual character; I'm on my knees begging for just one in any movie). It's right on the border of being a 7 and 8.

Name: The Living End
Review: Imagine if Wild at Heart was combined with Happy Together, written and directed by Harmony Korine. I fucking dug this from the visual style to the screenplay and everything in between, I think I'm gonna be a really big Gregg Araki fan.

Name: Triangle of Sadness
Review:
I almost loved it. Great performances, loved the filmmaking, it’s genuinely so funny in both the writing and physical gags and the first 90 minutes are genius, but unfortunately the remaining hour slowly lost me. I can’t say this was a BIG disappointment but I wish the third act didn’t feel like such a step down. I'm glad Ostlund was able to get his entire vision on the screen but compared to the first two acts presenting a top 5 film of the year, I think the movie would've benefit from a shorter runtime.

I'm pretty divided on the rating but I hope this grows on me in the future so I’ll give it the benefit of the doubt for now. It's definitely unforgettable and never boring in spite of some inconsistency and, months out, I see myself remembering this more for the hilarity above everything else.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ rhys, early screener

Name: Girl
Review: You're gross if you like this movie outside of the first 30 minutes. (Lukas) Dhon't waste your time.

Name: Citizen Kane
Review: I’m in film school, this was inevitable at some point.

Name: House
Review: I’m down for hyperrealism from time to time but this just reminds me too much of my everyday life. Would it have killed them to try something different? Be a little creative?
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, w/ jack s

Name: Pan's Labyrinth
Review:
I want to make a joke but, this was... a truly euphoric experience. I’m so confused how this didn’t floor me the first time I saw it, but hey. Things change!

This has to be one of the best paced movies of all time; I either had a huge smile or huge frown plastered to my face through every scene and none of the transitions felt clunky. I think I felt every emotion in the book at some point during this and I couldn’t look away if you paid me. It’s a perfect blend of realism and genre, a brilliant story of childhood and losing innocence, a technical wonder, etc. etc. I’ll leave it here for now. Masterpiece. I love life.
Tags: gene siskel film center, 35mm, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Blood Theatre
Review:
It's certainly a series of moving pictures



lmfao I'm having a rough night and I thought something bad to make me laugh would help. This did that half the time, but the other half was barely even a horror movie, mostly being a shitty dramedy. I got bored real fast, even when it kinda went back to being goofy at the end. A masterclass in how not to make campy fun.

I still liked it more than some Disney live action remakes.

Name: Fantastic Mr. Fox
Review:
I hope one day my dorm building gets uprooted by three farmers and I have to be the renegade leader for everyone to rebel and start a new life.


Everyone wish a happy birthday to Jack and Marjorie!
Tags: hulu, w/ jack s, w/ braden, w/ marjorie, w/ elle, w/ linnah, w/ kate, w/ robyn, w/ joelle

Name: Halloween
Review: I actually thought the first 45 minutes of this were decent. Not great, but I felt somewhat attached to the story and I felt like it had its own creative voice. Then the remaining hour was basically Halloween Kills all over again with worse kills and the exact same story as the 1978 original; what an impressive feat to completely forget both how people talk and what makes a horror movie effective halfway through the writing process. I was expecting some really over the top shit from Rob Zombie’s Halloween, and instead I was more preoccupied with working on a midterm after a certain point because this shit gets boring. This franchise hasn’t had a single good writer / director since John Carpenter. SPOILERS BELOW 3/1/2023 -- the s**cide scene around the 40 minute mark (?) has really left its mark in my mind. This is a piercing portrayal of the opposite end of the spectrum from catharsis: re-living a memory that makes you feel absolutely terrible because of the context. I genuinely do think this scene is terrifying and psychologically effective. I don't remember the rest of the movie well enough to evaluate it as a whole, but this scene in particular is brilliant.
Tags: youtube, w/ braden, in the dorm

Name: Moonage Daydream
Review:
A technically dazzling movie that didn’t hit me as much as I wished. I love Bowie’s music but don’t know very much about him as a person so I was hoping this movie would teach me more about him, but this movie was more of a 2 hour montage of Bowie interviews and footage of him at concerts and in public; a spectating public view, when I was craving getting up close and personal with him.

I’m really glad that this movie touched so many people, including the friend I saw it with, but I think I needed to know more about Bowie as a person before going into it. I’ll rewatch once I do this and hopefully it’ll improve.

Name: Sick
Review: 2022 CIFF HAS STARTED I’M SO HAPPY!! This was fun! Kinda reminded me of Funny Games but with the pandemic instead of horror meta. Very well shot and acted, great pacing, and thoroughly entertaining. There’s an epic long take knife fight right at the beginning before the title card that’s genuinely heartstopping and I LOVED it, but it also kinda peaks there. The rest of the movie is solid and well made, but the kills become a bit more predictable (even if still very entertaining) and the story gets a little goofy (leans into COVID satire a bit much). Overall though, this is enjoyable campy fun. Check it out. 7.0
Tags: music box theater, ciff 2022, w/ aech, w/ rowan, w/ maddie

Name: Blood & Donuts
Review:
Why is this on criterion channel?
Why is David Cronenberg in this?
Why is the werewolf guy also a vampire at one point?
I’m not mad at all that this movie exists, but I have so many ‘why’ questions lmfao. What a bizarre motion picture this is.

Name: Plan 75
Review: A delicate and occasionally distressing debut from a protege of Hirokazu Kore-eda, Plan 75 certainly feels like a directorial debut in a few ways but the heart and passion dominate in the end. The character dynamics are touching and engaging (even if a few are underdeveloped), the cinematography is purposeful and effective, and every performance is understated but impressive. The concept is timely and fascinating, and it takes after a lot of Japanese classics from years past. It certainly has some pacing issues and sections of it definitely feel like they lack focus, but in all it’s a delightful slow burn. It was also very wonderful to meet director Chie Hayakawa; seeing the timid delight on her face as I told her that her story really touched me was amazing. She is a filmmaker to watch.
Tags: amc river east 21, ciff 2022, me time!, q&a!

Name: A Human Position
Review: A romance that you’d never find coming out of Hollywood, A Human Position is hyperrealistic and tame but portrays a relationship without any drama. These two women have been dating for quite some time and the movie choses to portray their everyday lives instead them getting together or breaking up. On top of that, it’s beautifully shot with lots of open space around the characters and great use of a singular location. The ending is wonderful too. There are a notable number of dull sections that make the 72 minute runtime feel more like 90, it doesn’t really bring anything new to the table and it doesn’t compare to the works of slow cinema masters like Belá Tarr, Hirokazu Koreeda or Kelly Reichardt, but it’s an all around personal and pleasant film, hard to dislike.
Tags: amc river east 21, ciff 2022, w/ dad, w/ mom, q&a!

Name: How to Blow Up a Pipeline
Review: The biggest issues cannot be accomplished with peace. Even if the narrative doesn’t all come together smoothly, How to Blow Up a Pipeline is loud and passionate, bold in its genre writing and tenacious in its advocacy. I love the concept, character dynamics + individual development, and the climax. The screenplay could’ve used some work as far as some obvious dialogue and occasionally irregular pacing, but I was always engaged and provoked. Kinda wanna go commit some eco-terrorism now (I apologize in advance)
Tags: ciff 2022, w/ braden, w/ dad, w/ mom, q&a!, gene siskel film center

Name: Close
Review: I’m emotionally vanquished. I don't remember the last time a movie was triggering like this. Gonna think some happy thoughts, sleep it off, and write more on this tomorrow. Very likely the saddest movie of the year so far.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ dad, w/ mom, ciff 2022, cried or teared up

Name: No Bears
Review: Meta to a level of mystification, No Bears is a fascinating look (and for me, a fascinating introduction) through Jafar Panahi’s view on Iranian society. I absolutely loved the pseudo-docu style that somehow never manages to feel fully real or fully staged, it’s maybe the most impressive use of this style I’ve seen in any film. I was fully on board with the story and ideas at play for the most part, but admittedly the ending left me scratching my head a little, albeit it is chilling. Watching this film quickly inspired me to search for interviews with Panahi to try and figure out more of what he’s saying since I’m just not sure on a number of things for the time being, but I loved this movie and only see it growing on me in the future. I’ll really be sure to watch more of Panahi’s work as soon as I can
Tags: gene siskel film center, ciff 2022, me time!

Name: Paloma
Review: Good movie for representation! I liked the cinematography and performances. The screenplay wasn’t bad but the story didn’t really hook me as much as I wanted it to and some plot points just feel really unnecessary. It still got some emotion out of me so I’ll give it the benefit of the doubt for now, but this was a fairly surface level exploration of being trans (it’s way better than bad representation though, lmfao Girl moment)

Name: Glass Onion
Review: A superior Triangle of Sadness. I had a total blast.

Name: Breathless

LOVE the score, cinematography is great, very well written, and it’s really well paced. Parts of it are boring and I don’t think anyone here is a morally good person but it’s self aware, almost like a satire of classic American film noir, and all story ends satisfyingly tie themselves up. I’d be lying if I said I didn’t have a good time. 8.0

Name: Women Talking
Review: A stellar movie from start to finish. I loved almost every aspect of this and felt some seriously strong emotions at a number of points; phenomenal screenplay and performances. As of now, it has the ending shot of the year. I'm Insane movie day 1/3

Name: Godland
Review: This absolutely shattered me, my GOD. The most masterfully crafted film (from a technical standpoint) I've seen all year, maybe of the decade thus far… it’s breathtaking. 2.5 hours long that barely felt like 2. Fascinating internal conflicts and character chemistry. A piercing finale. I can't wait to see it again and process it fully.

Name: Jennifer's Body
Review: Fun! Great concept, solid execution. Could've done without the random ableism, but this is a good movie overall. If I was less tired from today, I'd probably have enjoyed it even more. I'm Insane movie day 3/3

Name: All Jacked Up and Full of Worms
