Name: The Inspection
Review: Forgot to log this last night, but it's great! Great performances, solid screenplay, solid cinematography... nothing to complain about with the filmmaking really. It doesn't break any new ground but it's emotionally effective, very down to earth and not overdone at all. Don't really have much more to say on it, it's a very good film but it didn't leave a massive impression on me. Right on the border of 7 and 8/10.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ dad, w/ mom, ciff 2022

Name: She Said
Review: I have some conflicting thoughts on the production of this film (ironic that a movie about sexual harassment in Hollywood is produced by Universal and Plan B), but that aside this is a good movie. The two excellent lead performances really take over the film, but the cinematography is good, the directing is good, and the screenplay is very solid. It doesn’t explore much new territory on the real story or the themes and I didn’t really feel like I had any new major takeaways, but it’s an admirable film overall.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ dad, w/ mom, ciff 2022

Name: White Noise
Review: A delightful, if occasionally dodgy dramedy that almost always worked for me in some capacity! The comedic bits definitely hit harder than the serious, but all around I was very won over by this family and their chemistry, feeling all the necessary emotional beats. Adam Driver is fantastic as expected, Greta Gerwig and Don Cheadle are excellent as well, the screenplay is awesome and really echoes a lot of offbeat comedy masters effectively, the cinematography and production design are beautiful, Danny Elfman’s score slays… it’s amazingly well made. Despite being unclear in exactly what it’s saying and feeling a bit long at times, I really loved it and am surprised to see the mixed reception so far.
Tags: music box theater, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ alek, ciff 2022

Name: Night of the Living Dead
Review: I was really tired when I watched this, but I still loved it. The low-budget filmmaking is excellent and inspiring, the screenplay is really solid and very progressive for its time, the world-building and sense of immersion are both super impressive, and I was just enamored (as much as I possibly could in my mindset) from start to finish. Would love to give it a second try sometime soon since I feel like I’ll love it even more.
Tags: hbo max, w/ braden, w/ jack s, in the dorm

Name: The Banshees of Inisherin
Review: It’s honestly fascinating how much I can fully love a movie for 85% of the runtime only for the final 15 minutes to completely lose me. I’m still trying to figure out what exactly rubbed me the wrong way, I think a lot of it has to do with how Barry Keoghan’s character was handled and tonal shifts that caught me off guard. It gets a good rating still because I did love it for a long while, and I absolutely want to sit on it some more to see how my views change, but for now I don’t feel compelled to rate it any higher.
Tags: landmark century centre, w/ rhys, early screener

Name: Decision to Leave
Review: I’m not sure yet if this is actually a 10, but GODDAMN the experience was and the film itself is right on the border. With my first Parasite watch being the catalyst in my film obsession, getting to watch the next big Korean film in theaters was genuinely such an affirming, borderline religious experience. I fucking loved it and I can’t wait to see it again so I can talk more about the incredible movie itself.

Name: Monty Python and the Holy Grail
Review: This was my first time watching in at least 5.5 years, been since middle school for me. I'm not close to the same person I was then, but I still love this movie. Funny as fuck, probably always will be for me.

Name: Dead Ringers
Review: I didn’t know very much about this movie going in, but I certainly wasn’t expecting to be as BORED as I was… this did close to nothing for me. I think there are some interesting ideas about being a twin at play and this definitely isn’t a badly made movie, but I never found any sort of real personal connection. David Cronenberg is a fascinating filmmaker to me, one who I haven’t gone very far with yet so I’ll keep giving him the benefit of the doubt. But I have yet to enjoy, let alone love his work outside of The Fly.

Name: The Cornshukker
Review: I had the fucking time of my life. Both stupid and awesome at the same time, but that’s to be expected from dumb cult horror movies.

Name: Funnel
Review: Painfully relatable lmfao I love this

Name: An American Werewolf in London
Review: I really liked this on first impression, but I think with a rewatch I’ll love it. I cared about the characters but didn’t really connect to them strongly, the ending felt rushed and I didn’t love the editing, but outside of that it’s still really well made, surprisingly very funny, mostly well acted, and pure campy fun. I have a few ideas for what the movie means (I think it’s either about trauma or personal identity), and with a rewatch I think I’ll have a more concrete opinion.

Name: Halloween
Review: Watching this on the big screen absolutely helped, but it was unsurprisingly much better on rewatch. It was a big deal for the horror genre, mostly holds up today as far as being both entertaining and frightening, and does a great job capturing similar vibes to my hometown (I’m biased being from Illinois, but I have a big soft spot for Midwestern horror). I love this movie now and would talk about it way more if I wasn’t about to fall asleep.

Name: Lot 36
Review: Solidly crafted with a fun ending but I didn't really feel strongly for the narrative at any point.

Name: Graveyard Rats
Review: The story is like an Indiana Jones villain origin story that never finds a good balance between being silly and scary. It would make a great video game (it looks like one), but unfortunately it tries to be horror anthology. There’s some potential in the concept and the performances here aren’t bad, but the execution is just… not good.

Name: Spirited Away
Review: My eyes are teary and I’m not ashamed to admit it.

Name: TÁR
Review:
The visual style of this movie is as basic / realistic as can be, but within it is an oddly hypnotizing experience featuring a truly unbelievable lead performance from Cate Blanchett, a rich and perplexing screenplay, and an overall clear vision from a director who succeeded in playing tricks on the mind.

The pacing of this movie is so odd; at times I was fascinated, others had me dozing off, and a lot of the final 30 minutes had me scratching my head while captivated. But by the time the end credits rolled, the tedious experience suddenly felt REALLY fast. I didn’t walk away feeling like I had gained anything initially… in fact I was rather annoyed. But the trip back to my dorm quickly changed my opinion and I kept thinking about what I just saw.

I’m not going to go into any thematic analysis since I really am not confident in that right now, but this was an enchanting first watch and I loved it. An hour later, it’s somehow both so fresh and a total blur. I really have zero clue how this will resonate with me months out, and it’s good that way.

Name: The Maltese Falcon
Review: I feel like a lot of film noirs will fit this mold for me -- excellent filmmaking and admiration for that reason alone, with a story and characters that I only give a shit about half the time.

Name: North by Northwest
Review: I just ate a really good piece of toast. Nothing to do with the movie I watched (it was awesome), I just wanted to say that.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Paris Is Burning
Review: I often reflect on a lot of shortcomings and troubles I've experienced being queer and thinking about how I'm perceived, and it's easy to think I've had a rough time coming to terms with my identity. Not to say it HASN’T been rough at times, but then I watch a movie like this, and not only do I feel comfortable knowing that people have experienced similar things before I was even born, but I see how bad they really had it and I'm able to both be sympathetic for them and gain a new perspective on my own experiences. Paris Is Burning is 77 minutes of perfection. It's emotional across the spectrum, pretty much perfectly crafted, and completely encompasses an absolutely wonderful lifestyle in the best, most effective manner possible. The first time I saw this I definitely wasn't hit as hard, and I'm guessing I was either falling asleep when I saw it, or I was still figuring out my identity on the most basic of levels. Regardless, this was an incredible rewatch that fucked with my heart in so many different ways and I almost can't believe it exists. To say I love this movie is an understatement, I fucking adore it. I could go on and on and on but I think I'll just leave it here for the time being.
Tags: criterion channel, w/ braden, for class, in the dorm

Name: BARDO, False Chronicle of a Handful of Truths
Review: It’s bizarrely structured, sometimes confusing, sometimes a bit self-indulgent, and you do kinda feel the runtime… but I really, really loved it. I would describe it as the Hand of God of 2022. I admit I have some bias as a devout Iñárritu fan, but he’s saying a ton with this movie and I was always gladly listening. Above all, this is that kind of overwhelming experience I crave with a first watch; there’s so much going on yet it still finds a way to seriously connect to me at several moments, the filmmaking is so insanely impressive as expected, and I feel every emotion at some point. ‘Accessible’ is one of the last words I’d use to describe Bardo, and as such I understand why it’s been a bit divisive, but I’m thrilled to say it genuinely worked for me in almost every scene.

Name: RRR
Review: How do I even review this lmfao All I can say is I made the greatest decision in a long time by waiting for theaters to watch this for the first time. I’m speechless, my mind has been blown. It’s a masterpiece… this is what cinema should do to an audience, and this upholds many purpose I believe more movies should serve. I’ll go into more detail on rewatch. If it wasn’t clear already, I’m officially on the RRR train.
Tags: music box theater, q&a!, w/ will, w/ maddie, w/ alek, w/ josh z, w/ sadie, w/ rowan, w/ aech

Name: The Nice Guys
Review: An interesting double feature with RRR earlier today. It feels heavily inspired by so many other movies and filmmakers (most notably Tarantino, PTA, and the Coens) that I didn't feel much personality or unique style at all, and I think I'd even call it safe in some regards, but it's saved by the entertainment factor; lots of fun action and some really good comedy. And it is pretty cool that a major studio gave a sizeable budget to a concept like this. Even if I never really felt very connected to it, I can't deny that it's well acted (impressive child acting especially) and very well crafted both visually and technically. Side note -- I've seen Altman's name thrown around too but I can't make that connection for the time being... I promise I'll get to his work soon.

Name: Dark Horse
Review: I started watching thinking about the Katy Perry song. I finished watching really sad. - - - - - Dark Horse fundamentally feels like a dark rom-com, but it's mostly just a dark romance. It begins like any rom-com would, showcasing everyday life and the beginning of a relationship, but the genre conventions are made interesting primarily by the fact that the two leads are incredibly depressed. The story plays out like a cautionary tale for finding love when you aren't in a mentally good place; I've personally been there and really felt those emotions while watching this movie. The term 'dark horse' is brought up at a number of points and refers to different things, but it generally refers to Abe, the second of two sons who held a lot of potential but ultimately failed to live up to his parents' expectations. As a second child who worries that I could be doing a lot more with myself (and also as a film major), I felt some painful relatability to Abe; the ending in particular came out of left field and gave me a surprising physical reaction. This movie is very structurally offbeat, but I prefer it this way and felt a lot of truth breaking through in the writing. I actually felt like I was being spoken to by Todd Solondz and that he was creating this world based on his own personal anxieties. Stylistically, it's all very formulaic, but this definitely isn't a badly made film and the lack of flair or unique visuals adds a lot to the overarching mood. I can honestly say I wasn't expecting much from Dark Horse after I stumbled upon it, but I was met with an incredibly honest portrayal of depression and familial anxiety through the framework of rom-com genre conventions that hit me really hard. Every performance is excellent, the screenplay never really misses, it brilliantly toys with expectations, and aside from a couple small issues I hold with minor character treatment, I have zero complaints. This is an excellent movie.

Name: Trevor Thursday
Review: My good friend Joi made this short, it's really creative and awesome. You should check it out! Link here

Name: Chicago Transit Anguish
Review: Hey everyone! This is my first short film I've fully written, directed, shot and edited myself, and it would mean a lot if you could watch and review it (It's only 3 minutes long!). This is a pretty big milestone for me, and I'm so excited to finally get some of my work online! It's out on YouTube now, watch it here!

Name: Bones and All
Review: Bones and All is yet another excellent 2022 genre film, serving as a great road trip movie, crime movie, body horror, and character drama. It's a dark yet wholly aesthetic look at rural America in the 1980s through the perspective of pariahs and cleverly provides a deep-cutting look at a number of struggles the country faced during this era, most notably poor mental healthcare and queer identity, through cannibalism. I was considerably more emotionally moved by this than I expected and, while I didn't directly connect to the characters, was able to relate to many of their emotions; Timothee and Taylor do an incredible job portraying layered guilt. It's beautifully shot, phenomenally scored, offers amazing performances from the entire cast (shoutout to Mark Rylance being flat out brilliant and Chloe Sevigny blowing my mind for her measely three minutes of screen time), has very focused directing, is grimy and gross when it needs to be, and overall does an exceptional job immersing you into this world. My only critiques lie in some of the editing and occasional rough pacing, both briefly taking me out, but I really loved this otherwise. I need to re-watch Call Me by Your Name and Suspiria, but as of now, I'd say this is my new favorite from Luca Guadagnino.

Name: Black Adam
Review:
The worst rich Hollywood mistake of the year goes to casting Dwayne Johnson as a Middle Eastern character. Everything else is pretty damn bad too.


For the record — I didn’t pay for this. I have Regal Unlimited and was looking for some ironic fun, but good god this couldn’t even deliver that more than half the time.

Name: Thumbsucker
Review: Mike Mills once again proves that he really knows how to direct actors and tell a story that appeals to me, though this didn't hit me quite as hard as I was hoping. It does a good job portraying the struggle of fixing old habits from your childhood and how difficult finishing senior year can be, and as such I personally related to it at a number of points, but I also just feel like I've seen this story before and I didn't get a lot of new insight or anything. Strangely, it also feels longer than 90 minutes. I really enjoyed it, it's well made, but I don't think it offers much more. If I want a good end-of-high-school dramedy, I'll definitely pick Lady Bird instead.

Name: Lady Snowblood
Review: This has been sitting on my watchlist, always been in the back of my mind for the last couple years, and finally getting to watch it was just amazing. I’m a sucker for genre movies that double as period pieces since they always get me interested in learning about history, and this absolutely accomplished that. It’s also a layered, unforgettable tale of revenge that delivers its emotional beats and plot twists in a way that few revenge films have matched. Every performance is great, the CINEMATOGRAPHY my GOD, the music is beautiful, the nonlinear structure, this ticks so many of my boxes that I can’t believe it took me this long to see it. Aside from a couple structural issues and plot points that felt unnecessary, this is a wonderful film.

Name: Orlando
Review: A stunning movie from a visual standpoint that honestly stands its ground against even the best-looking period pieces, though I wish it hooked me more often and didn't feel longer than the actual runtime (I partially blame my ADHD pills wearing off halfway through, but this wasn't exactly easy to get through). Tilda Swinton is really good here and usually makes it interesting though, she's one of my favorite actresses and it's cool to see her in an early role. There are some interesting discussions of gender and identity at play that I really liked, but I wish they had more prominence in the story or found a greater connection to me. 7.7

Name: Freak Orlando
Review: As a disabled individual, I've always been interested in carnival settings with art. Any present freak is basically guaranteed to be disability representation, whether that be good or bad, and it's something I always strive to critically engage with. Freak Orlando features an entire cast of disabled people, and I don't know if I would necessarily call it positive representation, but the story presents many fascinating ideas about how "freaks" are mistreated and made a spectacle of. I have yet to watch a carnival / sideshow / "freaks" movie that really emotionally grips me, but this hit that mark at a few moments. I'm unclear on exactly what it's saying in a number of scenes, but I was expecting that from the start; it's surreal almost to a fault the entire way through (as any art film is) and, as such, I'm interested to see how it'll grow on me in the near future. On top of that, it's a stunning movie with gorgeous use of color, brilliant cinematography and production design, just a really artistic and purposeful visual style overall, and concise direction. The aesthetic is really endearing and always kept the movie interesting for me, even when the characters didn't. It has such a perplexing structure that I really can't describe, but I kinda loved it. I'm not familiar at all with New German Cinema bar this and The Bitter Tears of Petra von Kant, but it's slowly becoming a movement I want to thoroughly explore. 8.8

Name: Mad Max
Review: This is such a ferocious directorial debut from start to finish that I knew early on I would love it, and… yeah it’s awesome. I had a blast. The practical effects, cinematography and production design all slay, it’s written well from story and pacing standpoints, and fully emits a rebellious, punky, and loud aesthetic that leaves me really inspired. The dialogue isn’t always great and it’s not something I largely connected to, but I still love it for all its accomplishments and creative wonder. It’s fire. 8.7

Name: Knives Out
Review: The last time I saw this was in the very early days of my film obsession, so it's interesting to see how it holds up almost 3 years later. The biggest takeaway for me is that Ana de Armas gives a fantastic performance, I forgot just how impressively she fit and brought her character to life. I'm not sure how I missed this the first couple of times, but she has a lot more layers to her character than I remember. It's still an amazing directorial feat as well; Rian Johnson has a gift for writing a satisfying puzzle that few writer/directors hold (this is the type of film he should continue doing, keep him away from sci-fi please). Really, the only thing that was worse on this watch was some cinematography that seemed out of place, but that only goes for a few shots and a small nitpick in general. I've considered Glass Onion the successor since I saw it last month, but I now think they're equal. I think this has the perfect runtime that really doesn't feel like it overstays its welcome, and there's something about the two clearly-defined categories of characters (1. the Thrombleys, 2. everyone else) that just really does it for me. That being said, I wish some characters in this one got more attention or had more story prominence, specifically the other two detectives and Fran, and I think Glass Onion improves on that by having a smaller cast where everyone plays a role (even if the individual characters don't quite match those of the original). Right now, I'm not sure if I prefer the more down-to-earth mystery of this or the grander scale of Glass Onion, but I'll figure that out after I see it a second time in a couple of days. 9.0
Tags: blu-ray rental, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, library, at home

Name: Glass Onion
Review: It holds up outside of the festival. Knowing what was ultimately coming but forgetting a lot of the little details on how things get there made for an awesome experience as I still retained some surprise while still thinking about and admiring Johnson’s writing and direction. His skill in both simultaneously inspires me / gets me excited about filmmaking and makes me feel intimidated, a little uneasy (it's a compliment to him). The original and this are equal for me for different reasons; I love them both so much. 9.0
Tags: cinemark seven bridges, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Band of Outsiders
Review: This was solid but if you asked me to watch it again, I'd probably laugh at you. It feels like Godard wanted to make Breathless again in some ways, but this is more serious in tone (the only time I felt any quirk or fun was the dance scene) and less memorable overall. I fuck with the cinematography and overall aesthetic of the film, as I do with every Godard, but the story and characters didn't really do it for me. 7.0

Name: Exit Through the Gift Shop
Review: On paper, this is completely my thing and having watched it, I love it. I think the shifts in perspective and who's telling this story work against the final product at times, but it's as turbulent, badass, and 2010 as I was hoping it to be. I've always been fascinated with street art and graffiti, having dabbled in it myself and am interested in doing more (for legal reasons this is a joke), and this aligned perfectly with that. I got more out of Thierry's story than I initially expected as well; I see a lot of myself in him, both good and bad, so I was able to view him and his journey from two sides that both really stuck with me. Overall, it's really inspiring and thoughtful on first impression, and I only see it growing on me over time. Awesome fuckin' movie. 8.8
Tags: dvd, w/ mom, w/ dad, at home

Name: Pinocchio
Review: Big flashbacks to my childhood through watching this; reminded me that I'm not a kid anymore and I'm kinda sad about that. Watched it on my family's old VHS. Good movie, I love it when kids movies get dark. I definitely should be a donkey by this point. 7.9

Name: Guillermo del Toro's Pinocchio
Review: Guillermo del Toro + stop motion animation is a match made in heaven. It’s far and away one of the most beautiful looking and concisely directed films of the year, reminds me why I love creativity and imaginative filmmaking so much, and explores the character of Pinocchio on a lot more layers that are thought-provoking and even made me tear up a few times. And in true del Toro fashion, the narrative is placed in a deep historical context; icing on the brilliant cake. It effectively subverts expectations at a number of moments, to the point that for a brief period of time I feared this would be another Nightmare Alley situation (too close to the original, not enough personality), but every end meets and it more than pays off. I normally roll my eyes when directors self-insert themselves in their film titles, but this truly is Guillermo del Toro’s Pinocchio. I mean that with high fucking praise. 9.3
Tags: kendall 11, w/ dad, w/ mom, cried or teared up

Name: Beyond the Infinite Two Minutes
Review: I wrote a TV show pitch creepily similar to this movie’s concept a couple months back after getting high one night. This used the ideas to a far more creative and endearing potential than I possibly could’ve, however… In other words, it was really fantastic creative fuel and served as the first movie in quite some time that really gave me an urge to make a new short film of my own, even after I made my first one. I really loved the story and characters, it was hilarious, I’ve never seen iPhone filmmaking with so many genres before, and I was smiling almost the entire time. I had a blast! 9.0
Tags: amazon prime, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: The Menu
Review: The Menu certainly joins the 2022 film canon of rich people on islands (D**th on the N*le, Triangle of Sadness, Glass Onion), but to me this is far more a movie about the tortured artist, and it does a damn good job at getting this across in a unique and multifaceted manner. It's not an uncommon theme in film, but the genres it's framed within make this a really uncomfortable and memorable experience. It's darkly playful in the manner character dynamics are established and the meal courses are introduced, supported by a stellar ensemble and the tight screenplay. Overall, the movie is more of a fascinating puzzle than something I really connect to and it didn't win me over until a certain point, but once it did, it becomes a delectable slow burn that I'll be reflecting on for the next few days, reeling in its most intense sequences. I'm not sure this will stick with me months out, but for the time being I think it's pretty badass and awesome. 8.3

Name: M*A*S*H
Review: I tried my best, but my ADHD pill wore off hours ago and this was really difficult to watch because of that. It was well made and I could follow enough what was going on, but that's all I got for now. It sucks that this was my first Robert Altman movie and I could barely even pay attention; I was looking forward to being able to have an opinion on this movie! I'll watch it again at some point for sure.

Name: The Fabelmans
Review: It's impossible to draw a line between family and art.
Tags: regal cantera 17, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Whiplash
Review: I watched this for the first time when I was 16, and it came into my life at the exact right time. During sophomore year, I was in the top jazz band at my high school and, both with playing trumpet and everything I was doing, I was full of ambition and energy to be the best. I put too much on my plate, and when my grades fell, I found less joy in everything and I felt like a failure, I re-evaluated myself completely and burnt out. Seeing Andrew tear his life apart to get to the top just two months later burned its way into my brain forever; I saw a character that seriously reminded me of who I was at that time, and gave me some perspective on why I needed change (the entire anecdote is mostly depressing, but watching this for the first time was a good thing). The end of sophomore year was marked by COVID and quarantine, both of which did anything but help me; I was isolated from my previous life completely, left with my invasive thoughts and depression over self-esteem for a long time. I never reached the same level I was before 2020 again. Things eventually got better at the end of the year, when it was safe to go back to in-person school and jazz band; it felt like a breath of fresh air. This fresh air lasted through senior year and into now, but I was forever a changed person. This isn't to say I miss being 16 at all; I am so proud of how far I've come and who I am today, but I'd be lying if I said I don't miss some of the excitement of being at the top of my game (relative to that time). I'm now in college, an aspiring filmmaker, no longer in a jazz band; things are completely different. It's a bittersweet feeling, but I contribute both strong emotions to the impact this movie has left, through all its incredible filmmaking and the personal connection it has with me. Watching this again is a big reminder of who I used to be early on in high school, and has given me some new perspective on how I have evolved as a person over the last few years. I will forever love Whiplash.

Name: Lucifer Rising
Review: This didn’t really get any emotions out of me, but it’s surprisingly kinda camp and visually stunning. I wanna smoke the Kenneth Anger strain.

Name: Funny Pages
Review:
It's the next plotless, hyper-realistic slice-of-life movie and, as such, it's successful based on the colorful characters and intense honesty. It's a strong directorial debut, written super well and has great pacing, and as someone with a passion for the arts who thinks a lot about meeting their heroes, this was like a horror movie. I really didn't think much of it on first impression, but it's growing on me like no one's business. That ending was really upsetting and will stick in my brain for the rest of natural history. I wonder how much further it'll go.

Also I regret watching this with my parents. In addition to discomfort with select scenes, they both hated it.
Tags: tivoli theater, after hours film society, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: La La Land
Review:
I like the moviefilm!

I don’t have much else to say right now… my mind is weirdly drawing a blank. My decision to watch this felt very forced, the circumstances were just too dull and I reeeally wish I chose to watch it in a setting that came up more naturally. It's fucking beautiful though!

Name: Final Destination
Review: I think it's really fascinating that a true story, intended to be adapted for X-Files, was turned into a shitty teen horror movie. It does a decent job at building suspense sometimes and I think the beginning is unironically really solid, but then it just gets boring. At the very least, I've been having a really rough evening and this made me feel better -- thanks for the fun cheer-up, James Wong!

Name: The Phantom of the Opera
Review:
That one unemployed friend at 3 PM on a Wednesday:


Serious review — It’s more fun than anything else in my opinion, but it’s a really great spectacle and not too long of a watch! Universal has a huge legacy in horror, and this was the first horror film they ever distributed. Its influence can be felt across the entire genre in the 97 years since, especially in the shot composition and mise-en-scene.
Tags: bored at work, hoopla, watch for screenplay

Name: Dracula
Review:
A movie that I watched today.

Visually pleasing and well acted but that's about it for me.
Tags: criterion channel, watch for screenplay, at home

Name: White Noise
Review: It didn’t hit me quite the same the second time around, but it’s still a darkly playful, wholly entertaining, and visually pleasing story of nihilism that hits a solid amount of personal notes for me. It’s not the most original thing ever but it’s comforting in a way that I didn’t fully realize at the festival. I kinda really love it! I’m glad I saw it in theaters again! Side note — I saw this in the theater I worked at for all of 2021 and it was very weird being back. Had some bad memories from that time of my life come back, but also a very strange feeling of missing work there.
Tags: hollywood palms, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Shadows
Review: A tale of colorful '50s New York characters portrayed in B&W, Shadows is an entertaining, well paced, and very intriguing debut from someone whose work I should have discovered at least a year ago. I felt like I was watching a time capsule, and I was always interested in spectating these lives. The amount of shots that highlight only the upper body vastly outnumber any other framing, and it's apparent to me that Cassavetes is focusing on emotional and instinctual reactions to drive the story more than anything physical. This is the earliest film with an aimless plot I've seen, and it's also one of the best examples I've seen of this. It's a very objectively made movie, simply mirroring Cassavetes' own view of his city; if he is making a biased statement on these people, it's cryptic to me. I can completely see why this was such an influential film for the industry, and I'm so excited to have begun my John Cassavetes journey.

Name: Tangerine
Review: “Los Angeles is a beautifully wrapped lie.” My favorite Christmas movie is also the perfect anti-Christmas movie, and a movie that has inspired me more than almost any other. Learning about and admiring the iPhone filmmaking, as I’ve said in the past, completely opened my mind up to the possibilities of low budget film and I wouldn’t be here without it. It goes completely against the stereotypical rich family characters and “saving the holiday” plot everyone is used to, doing so while also setting the story in a part of Los Angeles rarely seen in the movies and telling respectful and authentic stories of characters rarely seen in the movies, of which are both hilarious and painful in portraying the reality of their lives. In addition to the filmmaking, this was also a crucial film in inspiring me to learn more about all the real-world issues portrayed here and how to respectfully tell stories like this. As I’ve said before, it’s brilliant and seriously left its mark on me. I’m so grateful to have seen it on the big screen.

Name: All About My Mother
Review:
Tragedy does not mark the end.


I’m still soaking it all in, but this was a stunningly beautiful, layered, and thought-provoking study of sex work, intersectionality, the unpredictability of life, and, above all, identity. I was left confused by a decent chunk of it for a while, but then it ends and I’m like “holy shit!” Can’t wait to see it a second time.

Name: The Piano Teacher
Review: "I'm a pianist, not a poet." I have so many thoughts that I'm not sure where to start, but this was... fascinating. I've watched a lot of movies about tortured artists, harsh instructors and the like recently, but this one flips the idea of these characters around in the way that Michael Haneke does with any genre trope. As expected, the filmmaking is nothing short of brilliant, the performances are insane, every shot speaks volumes, and the pacing is rewarding. Haneke's method of portraying graphic content is fascinating, in how he loves his long takes and unflinching portrayals but is very specific with what exactly gets portrayed; it's really inspiring. It's a tragic story really, one that left me in a negative headspace as I anticipated. At the bottom of everything, I view this as a story of self-sabotage of someone who I initially perceived as a typical harsh instructor, but then her character is broken down in glorious Haneke fashion and I'm left thinking about too much to handle at once. Is it her overly sheltered upbringing? Is it her narcissism? Is her mother over-protective for a good reason? What caused this to happen?! It's not exactly something that personally related to me very much, especially as I started to have more concrete interpretations, but I was still engrossed from start to finish. I have way more thoughts, but this is the basis and I'll leave it here for now. Kind of an incredible film. 8.8
Tags: criterion channel, watch for screenplay, at home

Name: Avatar
Review: After the strain of The Piano Teacher, this was a good cool down. The screenplay isn't great, but it's an easier watch than I remember with some obviously great visuals and stellar direction; the action is always exciting and certain scenes play out like an orchestra in how well paced they are. It moves pretty quickly too, especially given the runtime. Zoe Saldana's performance is also way better than I remember though, before today, I hadn't seen this since I was in middle school. I admire the ambition that went into telling this story, but at times it feels like James Cameron got a bit ahead of himself in thinking it's more thematically serious than it really is. To me, it's more fun entertainment than anything else but doesn't embrace this as much as it should.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Batman
Review: It’s cheesier than I remember, a little too long, and there aren’t many stakes that I actually cared about, but it’s entertaining as hell. Jack Nicholson is so much better than I remember, Danny Elfman’s score is incredible, and Tim Burton has such a way with immersion. The cinematography is always great and the practical effects always slay.
Tags: dvd, w/ quinn, w/ dad, at home

Name: Batman Returns
Review: This was sooooo fun! All the set pieces, cinematography, and practical effects are either just as good or improved upon from the first! The performances are awesome! Danny Elfman’s score still rocks! It’s really funny! The action all kicks ass! This is what I want out of my comic book superhero movies! Yeah it’s not like an all time favorite movie and it still runs a little long but it’s the biggest blast I’ve had with a movie in quite some time and would gladly rewatch it any time. Future comfort movie mayhaps? 8.7

Name: Kimi
Review: This was cool! Well shot, well acted, a solid amount of interesting ideas, and it feels like everyone on set actually cared. The use of an apartment as the central location is nothing new, but the tech job + city of Seattle is a great combo to pair this with and I think it worked well. I was never in love with anything going on, but I enjoyed it and had fun. The first Soderbergh movie I can speak highly of (I haven't seen any of his older stuff yet I'm sorry). 7.3

Name: Barbarian
Review: This was solid but not a whole lot more. It's certainly well made and well acted (I loved the editing in particular), I admire the way the story is told structurally and I think there are interesting ideas at play, but I felt at a number of points the story could've gone in a different direction to push the themes further or explore territory that would've hit me more. For example, and this is oddly specific, I felt some moral dilemmas would've done the plot a great deal, adding both needed tension and further character development. I cared enough about the characters, but they were a bit one-dimensional. Horror tropes are bent at times and it works in favor of the story when they appear, but I still found it predictable overall and not that scary. The fear factor comes from a cross between Sinister and The Black Phone but right between those two in terms of quality (more unique for sure though). Fun movie and definitely not forgettable, but I'm a little disappointed by how unmoved I was left.

Name: The Happening
Review: This was not good but not unwatchable. The screenplay is not sure what it's saying, nor does it ever expand on anything deeply, but it's at least consistent and I was never bored. It's just kinda... interesting but without much of a reason for existing. I dunno, I'm gonna keep giving M. Night Shyamalan the benefit of the doubt until I see something from him that I truly hate, but I'll probably forget about this soon.

Name: Empire of Light
Review: This really isn't a bad movie, but I can name movies that do everything this does better. Olivia Colman is great, but this felt like a weaker version of her Lost Daughter performance. Roger Deakins did a wonderful job as always, but watch literally any of his films for visual splendor. Bones & All is still in theaters and has a better Reznor + Ross score. Everything in Steve McQueen's Small Axe does a FAR more potent and effective job at tackling racism and other UK political issues in the 80s. I could go on, but you get the picture. This got a little emotional reaction out of me and I thought the classic movie theater stuff was cool, but if you take that away, this would be close to nothing for me. Meh. I have a much longer review for this planned for Instagram with far more thoughts, so I'll keep it here for now. Generous 6.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Fargo
Review: This gets better every time. I seem to have a bigger blast every time I rewatch this; it just hits hard in increasingly different ways. An excellent, hilarious dark comedy that genuinely subverts many different genre tropes, doesn't overstay its welcome, has a truly authentic atmosphere and wonderful level of immersion, and has proven itself to be one of the biggest influences on my next short film. The last 15 minutes are perfect. I could listen to Carter Burwell’s score forever. Awesome movie.
Tags: blu-ray, w/ mom, w/ dad, watch for screenplay, at home

Name: Point Break
Review: It's the adventure of a lifetime. Point Break captures the excitement of letting loose and taking adrenaline-fueled risks, mixing that with the crime-thriller genre in a tidal wave of robberies, beach nights, ambitious pursuit, embracing oneself, and moral dilemmas. It's the most exciting piece of media I've seen in a long time, and easily a new favorite action movie, reminding me of why it's a beautiful thing to put on a movie solely for fun. Radical.

Name: The Whale
Review: After I saw Close at the Chicago Film Festival, I did not think any other movie of 2022 could make me more emotional, but damn was I wrong. The Whale prides itself in being such a devastating story that I would not blame anyone for watching it and being turnt off. It does a great job at invoking a strong emotional reaction, boasting a portrayal of depression that cut me to the bone backed up by impressive filmmaking and truly heart-stopping performances, but do not go into it expecting any beauty or much positivity at all. It will hurt you, yet it’s surprisingly rewarding. Read more here!
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ quinn, w/ rhys, w/ matt g

Name: Athena
Review: Imagine if La Haine was remade as an action thriller and directed by Iñárritu. God do I wish I watched this in theaters or with surround sound and not on the plane… it’s so, so impressive technically. One of the best made films I’ve seen all year for sure. Maybe not one of the best screenplays, and at times it does feel style over substance, but at the end of the day I still felt moved by the story and how it’s conveyed through this epic, punky, rebellious tone. This is a movie I wanna make. It’s so awesome, I LOVED it.

Name: The Banshees of Inisherin
Review: This definitely improved on re-watch. My uncle is a huge art fanatic and analyzes everything which made for a really fascinating convo for the ride home and opened my eyes to a lot of new ideas, in addition to the theories I was already forming myself. It's fascinating and close to perfectly made, I just still struggle to find a strong personal connection despite loving it for what it is. The only criticism I have still lies in Barry Keoghan's character treatment at the end but this is more personal preference than anything wrong with the movie itself. Pretty close to a 9.
Tags: tacoma grand cinema, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, w/ grandparents, w/ uncle joel, washington, on vacation

Name: Little Women
Review: Somehow I forgot this was a Christmas movie. I love it more every time I see it. It's absolutely stunning and so emotional.

Name: National Lampoon's Christmas Vacation
Review: It’s still fun but it grew off me some in the two years since I last saw it. An enjoyable watch that makes me laugh pretty consistently, but I feel like I found it much funnier when I was younger. I’m interested to see how 1983 Vacation and European Vacation hold up now, it’s been years since I’ve seen either of those as well.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ quinn, w/ gideon, washington, on vacation

Name: Avatar: The Way of Water
Review: James Cameron spent so long making the movie look great he forgot to write a compelling story. It’s visually amazing yes but so is the first Avatar. I had a fun time seeing this in IMAX with my extended family, but I didn’t feel any strong emotions until the final 10 minutes of this 3 hour movie. I found this to be at its best when it was an action movie, but it didn’t do this enough. More or less the same plot and conflict as the first with a little extra worldbuilding and some new characters.
Tags: galaxy theaters gig harbor, 3d, w/ quinn, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ grandparents, w/ uncle joel, w/ aunt katie, w/ jonah, washington, imax, on vacation

Name: Black Mirror: The Entire History of You
Review: A surprisingly potent portrayal of dying love. It reminded me of my past relationships and kept me hooked the whole time. I’m glad I’m in a good relationship right now, otherwise this would’ve been a horror movie for me. I’m not sure I would take this concept in this direction storywise if I made it myself, but the execution here is great and made me think otherwise. Good shit.

Name: Breaking
Review: I honestly kinda loved this; a great surprise! It’s kinda bland stylistically and feels like a Wikipedia page at times, but within that page is some amazing performances, a solid screenplay, good cinematography, and great pacing. I was left very moved.

Name: The Handmaiden
Review:
What an incredible, wonderful, unforgettable, perplexing, and joyful theater experience this was. I am speechless.
This movie is a perfect reminder of why I love to learn history and historical context of certain eras, particularly when it comes to movies and period pieces.
I don’t think I’ll ever fully figure this movie out, but it is a masterpiece.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: The Apartment
Review: This movie made me feel so happy. I can’t remember the last time a movie impressed me this much by how good it made me feel.
