Name: Unhappy Customer
Review: My newest short film is out! Enjoy! Link here!

Name: When Harry Met Sally...
Review: When I was but a wee lad, my parents and I watched a trailer for Monsters Inc. on YouTube (link here) where Mike and Sully play charades, and one of the jokes made is when Mike guesses “When Harry Met Sully.” My parents died laughing at this when they heard it, but elementary school me couldn’t have been more confused. Fast forward some 10-ish years later, probably more, I have finally watched When Harry Met Sally and can laugh at that joke with ma and pop. Great flick, this one!
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: To Be or Not to Be
Review:
This was awesome! Great filmmaking, tight screenplay, funny performances, excellent pacing, and I just really enjoyed it all around. It's one of those old films that feels like it has had a lot of influence on the industry, specifically from the dialogue, cinematography, and comedic timing.

I watched it in two sittings since I moved back to school today and was hanging out with some friends in between both, but I think if I had better watching conditions then this would be a 9/10 for me. Awesome way to start the new year in film! (RIP my streak of ultra-long media on January 1 though, it was a good run </3)
Tags: hbo max, criterion challenge 2023, in the dorm

Name: Aftersun
Review: Really liked, didn’t love. I wish I didn’t go in with super high expectations. The aesthetic was awesome and very well developed, the filmmaking has nothing to complain about and I always was engaged with what was happening, but I never felt really emotionally moved by anything. I dunno… I’ve beginning to avoid reading reviews on films I haven’t seen and it’s working out really well. This unfortunately was not one of those.

Name: Babylon
Review:
This happened to me when I made my first two short films.

I loved the moviefilm! A total blast and even better with a group of friends! Not a masterpiece but oh my god it was so much fun I loved it.
Tags: regal webster place, w/ alek, w/ sadie, w/ will, w/ maddie

Name: Witness
Review: If you think about it, this is a lot like Chicken Run.

Name: All the Beauty and the Bloodshed
Review: I can’t think of words right now. I’m truly floored. My emotions are everywhere and I just want to sit in how this affected me for a while.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: The Bridges of Madison County
Review: The first film directed by Clint Eastwood AND the first performance by Meryl Streep I’ve truly loved. Beautiful movie.

Name: Synecdoche, New York
Review:
2.5 years later, I’m still deeply impacted by this in so many different ways. Funnier than I remember, equally as upsetting, some details feel more profound while others don’t hit quite the same… but it is still incredible overall. This has so many more similarities to the cinematic styles that have influenced me that I remember and I was wowed by almost every creative decision made once again. I’m still soaking it in, but seeing it on the big screen was… unreal.


Side note — the theater was nice overall but the crew must not have much experience in playing film stock. The video and audio cut out briefly at two different points, then the entire projector shut down with 15-20 minutes left, remaining this way for another 5-10 minutes until it kicked back in… this almost added to the meta immersion in a way lmao
Tags: doc films, 35mm, w/ hazel, cried or teared up

Name: Total Recall
Review: My first midnight movie! I loved it! Arnold is hilarious and the action fucking rules!
Tags: music box theater, w/ alek, w/ maddie, w/ will, w/ josh z, w/ peter, w/ nick

Name: Broker
Review: A little overlong and it didn’t immediately hit, but on reflection it’s only grown on me. Awesome movie. I love effective kid / family dramedies so much and that’s just Kore-eda’s bread and butter.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: This Place Rules
Review: It’s pretty bland stylistically and didn’t really teach me anything new on the subject, but it’s a great documentary in the sense that it does a really impressive job at balancing Andrew’s perspective and feeling subjective without serious manipulation and just portraying both sides. If you know me or read my reviews, you know I obviously sway to one side personally, but Andrew is very patient with his interviewees and allows you to really give both sides a chance. Someone like Dinesh D’Souza could never.
Tags: hbo max, w/ jack s, w/ davis, in the dorm

Name: Daughters of Darkness
Review:
European horror and the queers both stay winning with this movie and I'm in love with it.

This struck me as a big genre experiment with liberation subtext and, for the most part, it succeeds. It's visually excellent with stellar mise-en-scene, striking colors/lighting and several unforgettable sequences (feels very much like a Fassbinder or Bergman film), the performances are all really great, the pacing and structure are both super effective, and, best of all, it's both explicitly and implicitly queer coded! It gets a bit muddled with the messages it wants to convey in the third act and the ending left me a bit puzzled, but overall this is very ahead of its time, engaging, and totally bizarre. A superb time.

It clearly has influenced a lot of different filmmakers over the years, but I was getting the strongest vibes of Ari Aster, Julia Ducournau, Yorgos Lanthimos, Park Chan-wook, and Alex Garland.

Very high 8/10.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2023, in the dorm

Name: M3GAN
Review: Something I really didn’t expect with M3GAN is that it’s more effective in its character drama than the horror. It’s a mixed bag in many regards, but my expectations were exceeded and I had fun! Full review HERE!

Name: Plane
Review: If you like shaky close-ups for 2 hours and a super predictable story, this will be your favorite movie. It’s passable and the action is occasionally fun though.
Tags: amc river east 21, early screener, w/ alek, w/ peter, w/ nick

Name: Puss in Boots
Review:
I've been feeling pretty anxious and existential this week so rewatching a movie I loved as a kid was a pretty bittersweet decision.

Low 7
Tags: dvd rental, in the dorm, library

Name: Grease
Review: Watching this made me hungry for pizza, ice cream, and young John Travolta. GOod movie!
Tags: netflix, date movie, in the dorm

Name: Saint Omer
Review: A superbly well-crafted and powerful movie about motherhood, colonialism, and the France legal system. It builds itself up incredibly slowly and deliberately until the final 30-45 minutes which are just stellar. The final monologue seriously left me with so many chills. I'll be thinking about this at least the next few days, if not longer.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: Holy Spider
Review: There’s really good filmmaking and powerful performances here but I think I was hoping to get a little more out of it all. From the very little Iranian cinema I’ve seen, one pattern I’ve noticed in each movie really takes its time to get intimate with the main characters, and not only was this true here, but it was interesting to see that applied to a serial killer procedural movie. This isn’t as good as movies like Memories of Murder or Zodiac, but it approaches the subject matter and tells the story in a very different manner from both of these that I really did like… I just wish I walked away thinking about what messages I got out of it more. I also didn’t think the music was used very well but this is more of a nitpick. Low 8 for now, teetering into high 7 territory

Name: Fire of Love
Review: A great movie, one that does justice to its subjects. It starts off pretty rough with some weak editing and overbearing narration, but gets so much better as it progresses. It feels like a movie I'd see at a science museum growing up that would leave me stunned and inspired, and I mean that as high praise. It didn't hit me quite as hard as All the Beauty and the Bloodshed, but it's definitely my second favorite documentary of 2022.
Tags: hulu, in the dorm, watched while sick

Name: Dragon Inn
Review: While Dragon Inn may not as meaningful or impactful as a Kurosawa film (this movie feels very inspired by his work), it is definitely as visually stunning and entertaining as they are. I was admiring the film more than emotionally connecting to it, but there's still a lot to love here: the film makes the most out of its stunning locations to immerse you into this time period and story, boasting some incredible cinematography and production design, and King Hu really knows how to direct his actors in both line delivery and action choreography. I just wish the impressive story had more thematic punch to it.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2023, in the dorm, watched while sick

Name: Thief
Review: The first half was solid enough to keep me engaged without really impressing me, but the second half completely turned it around for me and the last few scenes are all just amazing. A really great character study with incredible filmmaking, especially for a debut. I’m still very afraid of the American prison system. I only see this getting better on rewatches.
Tags: criterion blu-ray, w/ alek, w/ peter, watched while sick

Name: Skinamarink
Review: I had sleep paralysis when I was 11 and after a certain point, this movie seriously replicated that feeling for me… to an uncomfortable degree.
Tags: music box theater, w/ josh z, w/ maddie, w/ will, w/ davis

Name: Dope
Review: It doesn’t look spectacular and it’s not super well edited, but I like the screenplay. Honest and heartfelt with solid performances, just not always with the clearest focus. For example, there’s a really great and unforgettable ending scene that just comes up a little too early. A good twist on the typical American high school movie, I enjoyed it overall.
Tags: netflix, w/ jack s, in the dorm

Name: Minding the Gap
Review: I don’t know what to say. This is a devastatingly personal movie, I needed this right now. It’s only 1 PM on a Saturday and I’m almost on the verge of tears. As someone who also grew up in rural Illinois and is very anxious about their future, I got hit in so many ways… even through the many parts I couldn’t directly connect to. It may be perfect.
Tags: hulu, in the dorm, cried or teared up

Name: River of Grass
Review: It starts off like a Chantal Akerman movie and ends up being something more in the vein of 90s David Lynch and Gregg Araki. I didn't expect a movie like this to be Kelly Reichardt's debut, but I could still see traits of her style throughout the story and filmmaking. She definitely went on to do better things, but it's a well made and interesting watch.

Name: Women Talking
Review: It's apparent that Sarah Polley commanded and pushed her cast and crew to do their best, and this confidence shines through the heartstopping performances and how the camerawork establishes every group dynamic. Seriously, the way she makes the most out of a single central location is commendable and just shy of the very best (12 Angry Men, Mass, Scenes from a Marriage, etc.). It helps that the writing is consistently mesmerizing too! It's a little overedited with its flashbacks and the music is overbearing in a couple scenes, but overall is captivating and piercing and the emotional power reigns far supreme. My second watch thoroughly moved me just the same... maybe even a little more after a few months of reflection.
Tags: landmark century centre, me time!, cried or teared up

Name: Puss in Boots: The Last Wish
Review: Okay yeah the rumors are true… this is awesome. Such a fun and beautifully animated adventure with the heart and personality that made me fall for this franchise when I was a kid; this movie genuinely made me so happy. I truly loved it and am very tempted to bump the rating up to a 9.

Name: WALL·E
Review: This is a part of my earliest movie theater memory. It was July 2008, on a family vacation and my aunt took me to the movie theater. We accidentally walked into a show for The Dark Knight at first; I remember that theater having a particularly confusing layout. I don’t remember much about the movie itself since I was only 5 at the time, other than I really loved it of course, but I will cherish this memory forever. I love this movie endlessly and I cannot be objective here. It’s not perfect but it’s always a 10/10 in my heart. I can’t believe it’s been a part of my life for 14.5 years now.
Tags: in class, cried or teared up

Name: Mamacruz
Review: Writer/director Patricia Ortega clearly loves Pedro Almodóvar. Sexual awakenings are usually only found in dramas and comedies about teens and young adults, but Mamacruz spins this into a story about the elderly and how it is never too late to try new things. While nothing is amazing about how this story is written, and you might not walk away feel like you have gained anything, there is a lot of charm and this is a difficult film to dislike. The pacing and direction of the plot are so light, there are hardly any stakes to the story, and every character is realistically written. Not to mention the cinematography is shockingly gorgeous!
Tags: sundance 2023, virtual cinema, in the dorm

Name: Beautiful Missing People
Review: “What a shitty way to start summer.”

Name: And the King Said, What a Fantastic Machine
Review: There was some good editing and cool stock footage shown in the first half, but after a certain point it just becomes a viral video extravaganza. Really disappointing. It's cool this movie speaks to some people, but as someone who is interested in and knows a decent amount about the history of cameras and has seen many viral videos in their lifetime, this did close to nothing for me and after a certain point I just found it annoying. Ugh.
Tags: sundance 2023, virtual cinema, in the dorm

Name: Blonde
Review: Curiosity finally got the best of me with this and I watched it. I hate movies like this that have great performances and impressive cinematography at the hands of someone like Andrew Dominik thinking he was the right person for this job (I still don’t understand the point). What a waste of creative talent. I only see myself disliking this more over time.

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
Review: 9 months ago this movie played a considerable part in saving me from one of the darkest times of my life. Today it reminds me of my past pain but comforts me and makes me feel happy with where I am and who I am. I am happy.
Tags: amc newcity 14, cried or teared up

Name: Animalia
Review: Oh I loved this! A confident and committed debut with tight writing, great filmmaking, and no rush to answer every question, complete with some genre infusion. It’s engaging from start to finish and difficult to find flaws in. Easy pick for final shot of the year so far as well; it’s stunning! I need some more cultural context to be sure of an analysis, but on first impression I found this to be a unique and occasionally surreal look at religious delusion. I really hope it gets a good US distributor.
Tags: sundance 2023, virtual cinema, w/ hazel, at a friend's place

Name: Kokomo City
Review: So powerful with such a concise direction and meticulous usage of stock footage for emotional effect, music that feels complementary instead of overbearing, and such an endearing aesthetic. Despite the emotionality, there are some cutting choices that feel strangely comedic and took me out of things, but the last 20 minutes are perfect. I wanna see this again when I’m not tired since it’s probably better than I’m currently giving it credit for (aka a 9/10) and I didn’t get the biggest emotional effect I could have.
Tags: sundance 2023, virtual cinema, w/ hazel, at a friend's place

Name: The Sixth Sense
Review: Horror is subjective, and the thrill I get when I watch a horror movie that makes me feel true emotions is a wonderful feeling. The Sixth Sense did just that for me. In addition to the great filmmaking and performances, this movie brought back a lot of memories from when I was Cole's age. No, I couldn't and still can't see dead people, but I went to my fair share of behavioral therapists to help me and my parents figure out my ADHD and autism. While not quite perfect, M. Night Shyamalan did a truly commendable job of capturing this struggle from all perspectives and extracting genuine feelings from every actor. I really love this movie and I cannot believe it took me this long to see it.

Name: The Killing of a Sacred Deer
Review:
I FUCKING LOVE EUROPEAN HORROR

I WANT TO BE DISTURBED AND CONFUSED AND FASCINATED AND FLOORED BY EVERY MINUTE OF THEIR RUNTIME
Tags: hoopla, in the dorm, w/ lucie

Name: Moonstruck
Review: Every romcom needs to be bursting with personality like this does. A great first watch indeed, I loved it; it's just so endearing. The performances are really solid, the screenplay is excellent, the cinematography is never bad, every location feels very lived in, and there's never a slow moment. At one point I was reminded a lot of The Banshees of Inisherin which I really wasn't expecting, but it made me laugh.
Tags: alamo drafthouse wrigleyville, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Incredibles
Review: “Remember the bad guys on the shows you used to watch on Saturday mornings? Well, these guys aren’t like those guys. They won’t exercise restraint because you’re children. They will kill you if they get the chance.” Michael Giacchino's score is an all-timer. It's so well structured, the dialogue is awesome, and the characters are iconic. Somehow even more layered than I remembered too. And the entire second half ?? The 3D animation hasn't aged that well but aside from that, whatever. I will forever love this movie.

Name: Breaking the Waves
Review:
Was expecting this to be a movie about surfing but it turns out most of the waves being broken here are metaphorical :/

In all seriousness… holy shit! Given multiple circumstances, this was a little bit too heavy for me tonight and I don’t have a lot of analyzing to do, but I was really stunned by this. It is beautiful, complex, depressing, and weirdly captivating. Emily Watson is just incredible of course and Stellan Skarsgård gives one of the best subtle performances I’ve seen in a while.

This also might be one of the most effective portrayals of religious delusion I’ve ever seen; as someone who was raised Christian and felt very controlled by my faith for a good portion of my life, I always love to see this done well and this done did it! Lars von Trier’s writing and directing is so confident but also loose in the sense that he just shows everything for what it is with very little manipulation; I love seeing filmmakers put faith in the audience like this.

I didn’t always love the editing, I don’t think the runtime is 100% necessary, and a few sequences just kinda lost me emotionally, but overall this will stick with me for a while and I only see it growing on me.

VERY high 8

Name: Infinity Pool
Review: When the function got doppelgängers, Winding Refn lighting, and Gaspar Noe characters + sex scenes: (serious review: I really loved the first half and was genuinely disturbed. Very much my shit. But the second half felt long, like it went way overboard on style over substance and really lost its grip on me to the point that I occasionally found it boring. But then something clicked with the final 5 minutes. Between this and The Lost Daughter, I’ve learned that vacations in Eastern Europe are a trip in more ways than one. Mia Goth is one of the most exciting actors working today. Overall, I like this movie)

Name: Moonlight
Review: “Nah man, it’s a life. I ain’t ever had one of those before.” Today was maybe the best watch of Moonlight I’ve had yet. Almost three years ago I saw this for the first time, and I was cishet as far as I knew but it still crushed me and became an instant favorite. A movie that made me want to make movies. Now I am much more familiar with who I am, and am much more knowledgeable on filmmaking and what I look for in art, and I was emotionally destroyed all over again. I think Moonlight will always hold a place in my life. (Note to self: pay extra close attention to how this movie uses music on the fourth watch)
Tags: in class, cried or teared up

Name: Chi-Raq
Review: Need to let this sink in a little more before I write on it… but I definitely loved it. Spike Lee really does have a film industry of his own, from his artistic style to how fast he can crank out his projects. Even if his movies don’t always succeed, I can’t help but admire the hell out of his dedication.
Tags: dvd rental, video vortex, in the dorm

Name: Lady in the Lake
Review: I discovered this film because Gaspar Noe credits it with giving him the idea to film Enter the Void entirely through POV shots. Suffice it to say, I admired this film more than I enjoyed it. There's nothing wrong with the filmmaking really (aside from some laughable killings thanks to the Hays Code) and the usage of POV shots is so impressive even if thematically light, but I only found myself invested every so often. The movie felt longer than it actually was, and I was mainly engaged by the technical side, only strongly caring about the characters during certain scenes. One day I will find a film noir that finds an emotional in point for me, but today is not that day. Low 7

Name: Knock at the Cabin
Review: The Killing of a Sacred Queer M. Night Shyamalan is gonna keep making movies about how shitty he thinks Pennsylvania is and I’m gonna keep enjoying them. A relatively simple battle of wills on the surface, but it’s well shot, well acted, and has some weight to it. It explains too much, the ending could've gone through another draft in the writing room and sometimes isn’t edited that well, but I genuinely liked it. 2/21/2023 — This is a campy version of Funny Games. M. Night Shyamalan makes camp on Hollywood-level budgets and honestly, I think it’s fun. I just wish he didn't waste all his genuine potential after his first few movies.
Tags: regal webster place, w/ jack s

Name: As Tears Go By
Review:
Wong Kar-wai, one of the most romantic filmmakers of our times, started off his career with a dark, gritty crime thriller that takes a lot after the New Hollywood Era... and it's badass and awesome. I want to make a debut like this. Even in his first film, Wong left his very apparent mark all over, from the editing to the cinematography to the character dynamics.

He was still finding his footing in a few areas with his writing (specifically the romance, which doesn't work nearly as well as later films of his), but there’s really not much I’d change with this movie. Every action scene is so raw and the ending had my jaw hanging open. It’s an invigorating experience, still packing a lot of emotions that you'd expect from a Wong film, and one that REALLY exceeded my expectations. I loved it.

Name: Chungking Express
Review: I still don’t know what exactly I take away from this film but maybe I’m not supposed to, at least not right now. I want to be confused by this masterpiece film and just feel every minute of its wonder. That’s the beauty of the eccentricity of life and love.

Name: The Fabelmans
Review:
The first scene where Mitzi helps Sam make a movie with his trains to help process his anxiety is a perfect representation of parents supporting kids with autism.

The last conversation that Sam has with his dad is so relatable and feels so honest to me; it left me even more speechless tonight.

I can’t decide which of these two scenes is my favorite out of this whole amazing movie.


If this wasn't already obvious... it's even better the second time; a very high 9. I am so incredibly happy I got to see it in theaters again. I might never be Spielberg, but watching this movie motivates me to try my best to reach that standard.

Name: Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban
Review:
Meant to just watch the first 30 minutes of this because of a scene I’m using as inspiration for my next screenplay but I ended up sitting down and watching the whole thing since it was moving so fast and I was having so much fun.

I love how half of it is borderline a screwball comedy and the other half is a far better version of the first two Harry Potter movies — as the old folks say, what a picture this is!

Name: Wild Strawberries
Review: I’m still letting it sink in, but I loved it! It already feels like a film that'll grow on me with time and age. Bergman is always great, of course.
Tags: blu-ray rental, video vortex, in the dorm

Name: How Do You Measure a Year?
Review: Very simple, but so beautiful and touching. A great foundation and concept for something grander and better.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ cam

Name: The Elephant Whisperers
Review: Despite a bit of overbearing music, the passion for the subject matter shines through and the cinematography is beautiful. I’ve always loved nature documentaries and this is no different. My personal pick for Best Documentary Short.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ cam

Name: Stranger at the Gate
Review: Challenging and it feels important, but it definitely could’ve improved in its editing and directing. Hmmmmmmmm
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ cam

Name: Haulout
Review: I admire the mission of this one, but it’s on the opposite end of the spectrum from Elephant Whisperers where it doesn’t do quite enough with its filmmaking and directing.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ cam

Name: The Martha Mitchell Effect
Review: There really isn’t anything wrong with this, but I am really not in the mood for history lessons on US politics today so this left no impression on me.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ cam

Name: Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon
Review: I can’t think of many other movies that I would describe with this word, but Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon is wise. A beautiful, intelligent, purposeful, and wholly entertaining labor of love for the martial arts action movie (specifically wuxia in this case). This is a wonderful film in its own right, and watching it on the big screen was nothing short of unforgettable and mesmerizing. The only thing holding it back from a perfect score is the romance flashback which really breaks the pace for a bit. From the stunning cinematography and production design to the beautiful music, perfect sound design, and balletic screenplay, I truly adore it. (and seriously regret only giving it a 7/10 the first time) Perfect scenes: - Jen is confronted by Li Mu Bai and Jade Fox back to back, foreshadowing her inevitable dilemma later on. These confrontations feature some of the best choreography, camerawork, and dialogue out of the entire film. - Jen challenges Yu Shu to a duel with the Green Destiny after calling off their sisterhood. It’s a beautiful match with a perfect combination of thrilling action and emotional tension. - The finale. Goddamn.

Name: Meshes of the Afternoon
Review: This reminded me of a nightmare I had the night after I took my first edible, and I mean that as high praise.

Name: Cocaine Bear
Review:
Met the bear in a post-film Q&A. Cool guy!

Feels very inspired by Spielberg’s monster movies in its character dynamics and story structure, but much looser (this is less good) and with a lot more over-the-top pulp and splatter (this is a good thing!). It’s obviously not high art but such a blast as you might expect, one that tests limits you don’t see tested much anymore, that I don’t care. I can’t wait to see this at a midnight cult screening at the Music Box some number of years from now.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ rhys, early screener

Name: The Royal Tenenbaums
Review: Oh my god YES

Name: Kids
Review: Most people don’t watch this movie twice, but I’ve consistently thought about it ever since my first watch. From the impact it had on me, I really felt like it was time for a revisit. It’s a brilliant, pessimistic, scathing indictment of teenage life that pierced my mind and influenced every screenplay I've written since. It felt like someone finally made a movie that scarily accurately portrayed the kids that bullied me and I hated growing up. It's mostly portrayed ethically, but my one big issue lies here that somehow slipped my mind over time; I can’t overlook or get behind the gross and pointless rape of Jenny. Aside from that, this is a 9/10 movie and there are very few teen slices of life that are more realistic and unforgettable (off the top of my head, I can only think of Fish Tank, Pariah, and City of God). Controversial subject matters are crucial in film for breaking boundaries and expanding the medium and, for the most part, this is a huge success.

Name: An Irish Goodbye
Review: My favorite of this year’s live action Oscar shorts. Great cinematography, excellent chemistry, humor that always landed for me, an effective structure, and beautiful disability representation. Please pick this!
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ cam

Name: Night Ride
Review: So after I saw Letterboxd tearing this to shreds, I figured I'd throw my hat into the mix and talk about why I enjoyed Night Ride as much as I did. If you're reading this and feel like I missed something then please comment below, I will willingly listen, but I did not find this offensive. I thought it was well made, funny, and fairly effective in what it was trying to get across (it could definitely be worse). As someone who is queer and lives in Chicago, I always have a fear of something like this happening to me in the back of my mind, and this short did a great job tapping into that anxiety. The story goes in directions I didn't expect, I don't necessarily agree with how it ended and wish there was more nuance to the transphobia and a stronger focus on the trans character, but I didn't find it upsetting or repugnant either. This felt like a very real situation to me and, despite some questionable character dynamics, I think it's better than a lot of people are giving it credit for.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ cam

Name: The Last Detail
Review: I loved this!! Three awesome central performances with an endearing, emotional group dynamic that I connected to way more than I expected (reminded me a lot of George and Lennie from Of Mice and Men but more touching), tons of memorable dialogue, an effective loose structure, stunning mise-en-scene (DP Michael Chapman never misses) with a lot of red, white and blue, and consistent laughs. It’s really hard to pick a favorite scene, they’re genuinely all great. This was my first Hal Ashby film and I think I’m going to be a big fan of his.
Tags: dvd rental, video vortex, in the dorm

Name: Godland
Review: Lucas is as unfit a leader as the island is unfit for a church. Despite being primarily about religion, Godland comments on a copious amount of human behaviors and captures the anxiety of reflecting life’s choices + an ensuing descent into madness perfectly for me. I'm shattered once again. Also included is some of the most unforgettable filmmaking I've ever seen to complement the piercing screenplay! This is one of those mystifying movies that makes me rethink everything in a span of 140 minutes but still somehow reminds me to keep moving forward… and it’s an Icelandic 19th-century period piece?? In other words… it’s a camera bag that I might carry by my side forever.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: The Pact
Review:
Was feeling retro earlier this week so I made a blind grab from Video Vortex (local video store) and ended up with this. Video Vortex just opened a couple months ago and I couldn't be happier -- I finally know the joy of video store serendipity for myself!

I liked the practical effects, the mise-en-scene is interesting, Haley Hudson is the one actor here who actually tries, and the runtime went by pretty quickly. But otherwise, this thing is LIFELESS! The story has like two layers, the dialogue is completely bland, and the exact same outcome would’ve happened if there were no other characters except the main girl and one of her friends. Why is there a full ensemble?

It’s also not scary at all: one of the jumpscares is immediately followed up with a slo-mo close-up of the main girl’s foot as she jumps in the air and I laugh every time I think of it. Tarantino is in shambles. It's only saved by a decently fun ending that feels reminiscent of John Carpenter.

And what the hell is the pact? Why is it called that?


UPDATE -- I just watched the proof-of-concept short film from 2011 and it was genuinely unsettling in its visual approach to the story and actually felt like it had personality. What happened here ??
Tags: dvd rental, video vortex, in the dorm

Name: All Quiet on the Western Front
Review: All Queer on the Western Front (they’re gay) Some of the best editing and character development I’ve seen recently. The opening lecture scene is perfection. And every war scene still kept me on edge and unnerved even though this movie is almost 93 years old, holy shit. Some parts of this haven’t aged well (sound design, some performances, etc.) but I found this engrossing and effective from start to finish. Awesome movie.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ hazel, in the dorm

Name: All Quiet on the Western Front
Review: Where the 1930 All Quiet has a relatively straightforward structure, complementing the phenomenal character development well even if a bit standard, the 2022 remake feels much more loose and poetic in its pacing, with clear influence from Kurosawa, Schrader, Malick, and Spielberg. I’ll be debating in my head which one I think is better for a while, but despite the two different styles of storytelling on display, I found both to just be really affecting. I’m really glad I waited until the big screen for this one because it really deserved it — this is war genre filmmaking done in a way that gets a strong reaction outta me. I love it.

Name: Deep Impact
Review: All things considered, I enjoyed Deep Impact more than I thought I would. It’s obviously not 2001 or Star Wars, but I’ve dreamed of making a disaster movie ever since I was young and I always find some level of entertainment out of them, especially on grand scales like this one, so it did the job for me. It’s technically impressive with surprisingly good cinematography (the DP worked with Rainer Werner Fassbinder in the 60s and 70s), solid production design, the music is mostly used effectively, and a healthy mix of CGI with the practical. A lot of the CGI doesn’t hold up, but I was convinced enough for a movie from 1998. The screenplay is a really mixed bag though, and none of the performances really impressed me. The plot is actually very strong on paper, and some moments really do live up to that potential, but the execution leaves a lot to be desired. The movie worked best for me when it focused on collective terror rather than individual characters, which is both good and bad. I felt like there wasn’t enough thought put into the families we were spending the most time with and during a couple scenes, they’re flat out annoying. I felt most connected to the astronaut team by far, but they couldn’t have had more than 20 minutes of screen time. Even if the movie is narratively mixed, I admire the strong moments and overall scope of the project too much to dislike it. I don’t ever really see myself loving this movie, but I had fun watching it for the first time.
Tags: dvd rental, video vortex, in the dorm

Name: Burning
Review:
This was beautifully shot, well acted, and is clearly really meticulous in what it’s saying, but I spent most of the movie trying to find an emotional in-point and I didn’t really get that. I wanted nothing more today than to fall in love with this movie but alas… goddammit.

I know it’s one of those movies that’ll go up for me on rewatch once something clicks. I’m in the middle of getting destroyed by finals so I'm blaming school for my lack of excitement here. And please enlighten me if you love this more than I do! (You probably do, and I envy you) But this was a disappointing first impression.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: Return to Seoul
Review: Full review here !! I loved this movie. It’s the first time in a while that a film heavy on subtext and unspoken nuance worked so well for me on first impression (my world is healing?). I don’t feel it fully justifies the 2 hour runtime as of now, but it builds up to something that is so powerful and satisfying that I'm starting to believe this less with time, and it’s only grown on me further overnight.

Name: TÁR
Review: A nice and gentle palate cleanser after a rough week.

Name: The Meyerowitz Stories (New and Selected)
Review: A movie I needed right now; absolutely wonderful. Brilliant screenwriting and performances, and the family dynamic kept me emotionally engaged nearly the whole time. I truly loved it. Between Moonstruck, The Royal Tenenbaums, and now this, my biggest trend of 2023 so far is heartwarming and hilarious movies about chaotic New York families.

Name: Scream VI
Review: I’ve been feeling really optimistic in general with life recently and I hoped that would fall into this movie… but I’m so tired of the franchise at this point and I have such little hope that any future entries will be a worthy follow-up. I wish I didn’t convince myself that this would be any sort of turning point, because it isn’t. Nothing in this movie left any lasting impression on me. I’m not in the mood to rant right now so I’ll just leave it at this and hope the movie only grows on me from here.
Tags: regal bolingbrook, w/ dad, w/ quinn

Name: Nocturnal Animals
Review: My brain is still processing the last 2 hours, and I mean that in the sense of the movie was great. The full impact crept up on me so well and a lot of the creative decisions made just worked really well for me. But I need a hug.

Name: The Diving Bell and the Butterfly
Review: This movie is intensely moving and captivating with several perfect sequences. The performances, cinematography, sound design, and multiple monologues are just incredible. But the editing, while mostly great, makes some weird transitions, and I just wish I was able to watch it all in one sitting. (I love my parents but taking a break halfway through happens 75% of the time when we watch movies at home) I can't wait to see it again! Follow-up recs from Roger Ebert -- Johnny Got His Gun (story) and My Left Foot (Amalric's performance)
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Shazam!
Review: I hadn’t seen this since it first came out but it aged well. All the comic book and superhero stuff is really forgettable, but it feels like the writers prioritized writing solid characters instead which really paid off… why is this not the priority way more often? It’s not great in any respect, but it’s purely fun and I actually care about who I’m watching. I’m in the car on the way to see the sequel so hopefully it keeps all this going.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, at home

Name: Shazam! Fury of the Gods
Review: I have never liked Skittles as long as I've lived. This movie unexpectedly helped my case. There's still a pulsating heart behind the main group of characters, and I cared enough about them the entire time because I enjoyed the first Shazam, but the movie is just a step down from the first in nearly every way. It looks uglier (half the shots in this could be memed for bad green screens and color grading), it's less funny, the character arcs all feel less human, the runtime feels less warranted, and it's just... your average superhero movie. That's not to say any of the above is a total misfire per se (except 75% of the VFX), but all the emotional arcs that made the first Shazam compelling have been replaced by bland villains. As I said, there's still some heart, and there was just enough around the corner of each plot point that made me hope for something to change my mind to keep me from being bored, but in the end I'll forget about this after a few days. High 4, maybe a 5?
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Mississippi Burning
Review: This was recommended by a professor a couple weeks back, but I forgot to watch it until now. An incredibly well shot and acted movie with powerful structuring and good editing. It got real anger and sadness out of me, and aside from some weirdly used music I have no big complaints.

Name: Scott Pilgrim vs. the World
Review: I didn't enjoy this as much as I possibly could have since my life and mental health are both hellscapes right now, but I'll never have a bad time watching this awesome movie. It provided a surprisingly nice gateway to open up about all my awful life shit to my parents.
Tags: netflix, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: John Wick
Review: I was thoroughly entertained by this! Great subtitles. And I would do the same in his situation. I think I still like the first Kingsman more as far as mid-2010s-over-the-top-pulpy-action-movies-that-started-franchises-and-also-have-dogs-in-peril go (my favorite subgenre, of course), but I forgot until today that movies like this are my guilty pleasure. So I don't mean that as a detractor at all.

Name: John Wick: Chapter 2
Review: "GUH!... UHH!... NUH!... UGH!..." - powerful dialogue delivered poetically by Keanu Reeves and Common In all seriousness, this was great. It's great as an action movie, a sequel, and a movie in general. The mythology is weaker than that of the first, but it has less prominence in the overall plot in favor of improved cinematography and action choreo, and holy shit does it pay off! Much like the first, it takes a bit of time to really get into things, but when it goes from 0-60... damn! Every action scene in New York is directed so incredibly well, and I truly lost myself in most of the movie's second hour. Even despite weak mythology, there are still enough small story decisions along the way and confidence in the action leading the exposition that really prove to be effective and warrant the 2 hour runtime! So yeah... I had a blast, and the filmmaking is seriously impressive too. It's not quite Fury Road or Skyfall, but it's still one of the best 2010s action movies. I would put it roughly on par with the first Kingsman and Hardcore Henry... just for very different reasons, haha. I'm a bit worried that Parabellum and the fourth entry will start to feel tedious after a certain point, but given the jump in quality between the first two, I'll be going in with optimism.

Name: John Wick: Chapter 3 – Parabellum
Review: Chapter 2 is my favorite of the John Wick movies so far, but this was still very good. Arguably the best screenwriting of the first three (not saying a whole lot), but I thought the cinematography and set pieces were a bit of a step down from the previous entry, even if still really impressive. That could have to do with my bias for effective use of big cities as someone who goes to school in Chicago (and might be moving there full-time in August), but the final fight in this one really won me over and was about on the same standard on all the action in the second one. Two things I noticed about the trilogy from this one:  — they do a really good job at making 2 hours go by quickly, there’s genuine power in the pacing of these movies! — getting hit by a car is the equivalent of stubbing a toe for them

Name: John Wick: Chapter 4
Review: Maybe my least favorite of the four, but it still has two truly awesome action sequences and some of the best cinematography of the franchise. It was super fun in EMX too, I’m glad I finally saw one of these in theaters.

Name: Invictus
Review:
Morgan Freeman teaches Matt Damon about racism!

Another movie recommended by a professor. I remembered like the first 30 minutes of this from 7th grade language arts but nothing else after so I'm not gonna mark this as a rewatch. It's not offensively bad in any way and Morgan Freeman gives a great performance but outside of that, this is just adequate. Matt Damon literally just plays himself with an accent, the cinematography and editing are fine (some of the slo-mos were unintentionally really funny though), the writing and dialogue are solid, but after taking an apartheid class last quarter, I didn't get any new knowledge or enlightenment out of this.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Warrendale
Review: I need to go for a walk and decompress. This is an incredible documentary with amazing directing and editing, and it left me with maybe a little too much to think about for right now. I will absolutely be thinking about this movie for a lot of the near future. I am feeling every emotion right now and it's a bit painful, but it'll be hard for any movie to top this as my discovery of the year. I seriously loved it so much.
Tags: criterion channel, in the dorm, criterion challenge 2023, cried or teared up

Name: Night Mail
Review: The second half carried. It was like The Wrong Trousers!

Name: Child's Play
Review: I watched this for the first time 9.5 years ago (here’s the link to my full story!) and this thing still scares me. It always will. I will say, that on this rewatch I felt some of its novelty wearing off, but a lot of images and scenes that stuck in my brain when I was 10 years old STILL make me panic at 19! It’s also a movie that uses the city of Chicago very creatively, which is something I never would have picked up on had I not gone to school here. So I'm still finding new reasons to love this movie at different stages of my life! And the filmmaking, while not mindblowing, is still incredibly solid and has clear influences from both Carpenter’s horror and Alien. I’m super biased, but I love this movie.

Name: Exit Through the Gift Shop
Review: "They say art is dead. Sweetie, art is all around us." This is both a cautionary tale and an inspiring true story for ambitious artists, with a badass style and fascinating commentary with a razor-sharp direction that always keeps you questioning what will come next. The editing especially is fantastic, with the Disneyland sequence in particular being breathtaking and perfect. I see more of myself in Thierry than I'm willing to admit, but after this rewatch, I'm coming to realize that this connection isn't such a bad thing at all. I may not pursue any field of art as a full-time career, but as I have become more artistic in my daily life over the last few months, I have come to realize more of how art plays a strong role in my life. And watching this movie again reminded me that having dreams is a wonderful thing and pursuing what you're passionate about will take you places you'd never expect. I really, really love this movie.
Tags: youtube, for class, in the dorm

Name: Child's Play 2
Review: I have less of a history with this one but still some good memories from when I was younger. The pacing is weaker, the city of Chicago isn’t a character anymore, and it’s not quite as scary. BUT, it's definitely still well-made overall and it still serves up some good creativity in its horror as the kills are even better, the character drama is still effective enough to be engaging (Kyle will always be awesome), and the finale in the factory is just brilliant. Overall, I love this one too.

Name: Child's Play 3
Review: I'm pretty certain I had only seen this once all the way through before today, so it almost felt like a first watch. I was expecting it to be the dud of the series, and while it definitely isn't as strong as the first two, I still enjoyed myself. Child's Play 3 is just as technically well-made as the first two, and the supporting characters are memorable and you come to care about them, making for a consistently engaging movie. But I was most surprised to find that this is a weirdly easy watch; it doesn't feel much like a horror movie at all. The kills are all really standard, some even being really boring, there's not much tension or build-up, and the story functions far more as a drama rather than something scary. You see everything coming, and the meat (literal and metaphorical) that makes the first two entries as memorable and unnerving as they are is absent here. If any Chucky movie were to be rated PG-13, it's this one. As I said, the drama is still solid enough to be entertaining, at least as someone who has a personal connection to the franchise. That being said, there's a big detractor in that Andy feels weirdly one-dimensional: the military school setting was a great choice for the sequel, but the care you have for Andy's character relies entirely on having watched the first two Child's Play films. As with the previous entries, the final act is strongest, as Don Mancini shows again he knows how to write a good ending. This movie is fun, but it's missing a lot of narrative groundwork, as a lot of ends are left untied and there isn't much reward for the viewer.

Name: The Favourite
Review:
T h i s   m o v i e
i  s    g  r  e  a  t   !

(I was genuinely so torn on the rating. I literally tossed a coin for whether I would give this an 8 or 9, so it could easily get a bump up at some point in the future)

Name: Beau Is Afraid
Review: THAT WAS NOT THE MIDSOMMAR DIRECTOR’S CUT !!!! My brain is a milkshake. My legs are numb. I’m buying a ticket for this again as soon as I can.
Tags: alamo drafthouse wrigleyville, early screener, w/ hazel, cried or teared up

Name: Bride of Chucky
Review: The biggest opinion change of the franchise will probably lie here for me. I thought it was pretty mid in years past, but now I really appreciate it for what it is. It has a REALLY good campy setup with funny characters, some great dialogue and great vibes, complete with one of the most cringe-worthy kills in any horror movie (as someone with multiple piercings, I physically tensed up for 2 minutes straight. Piercings needs to be in horror more !!). But unfortunately, despite remaining decently fun, it loses a lot of its uniqueness and feels more formulaic as it goes, including a kinda sweet but fairly anticlimactic ending. I still really enjoyed this overall, it's on par with Child's Play 2, but if the second half kept the camp factor going strong, I'd probably really love this and put it on the same level as the original.

Name: Dressed to Kill
Review:
For the most part this is a really tense and very well directed noir-thriller, especially in the music, cinematography (I normally don’t like split diopter shots, but this movie has some of the best I’ve ever seen), and the awesome use of the city of New York. (the art museum and taxi chase scenes are captivating) And even if there really isn't a mystery in the story, I still found it thoroughly exciting.

It's also a rare film from this era of Hollywood to tackle sex work and transsexuality, but while the former holds up decently well, the latter is super outdated and unfortunately takes up almost the entire last 15 minutes. It didn't ruin the entire experience for me, I still think the first 75 minutes make for a great watch, but the solid ending was dampened a lot for me thanks to being entirely pitted against the already weak trans character. I don't even say that solely for representation's sake, I also just found it to be lackluster.

Name: Seed of Chucky
Review: This might be my favorite movie in the franchise, for better or worse. I unlocked a new piece of the mystery of how discovering this franchise at too young an age affected me (and how I’ve grown as a person) while watching this. I’ll talk about it more another day <3

Name: I Am Not Your Negro
Review: An important watch for anyone who lives in the United States. James Baldwin was one hell of a powerful speaker / writer.
Tags: criterion channel, in the dorm, for class

Name: Hi Stranger
Review: This reminded me of every relationship I've had and I'm sad now... thanks, Kirsten. Imma go see Dungeons and Dragons and cheer up.

Name: Dungeons & Dragons: Honor Among Thieves
Review: This movie isn't great, but after a certain point it just won me over. It felt like a movie I would love as a kid and that sense of nostalgia, which I haven’t felt with a new movie in a while, stuck with me through the end credits. The screenplay and performances were pretty weak, but the cinematography and practical effects (when present) were impressive and I liked the finale in the maze.

Name: The Super Mario Bros. Movie
Review: I would also do all that for Anya Taylor-Joy

Name: Diary of a Wimpy Kid
Review: After watching this movie for the first time and not understanding the message, I wanted to be like Greg which was a really bad thing for me as a kid with major social issues and I kinda hate this movie for that. But I also respect it as a historical artifact from 2010 so I have no idea on the rating. Either way, Rodrick Rules wipes this.

Name: Badlands
Review: “You’re quite an individual, Kit.” “You think they’ll take that into consideration?” Very good movie. Thought about a lot of others that were influenced by this while watching, and pretty quickly realized that Badlands is held in such high regard with good reason. Who could've guessed? I’d be lying if I said I fell completely head over heels for it, but I was definitely never bored and don’t have anything to complain about. In particular I love the cinematography, screenplay, use of geography, and finale. Malick's poetic style is completely up my alley. Also the main theme sounded so annoyingly familiar for the entire movie, and when I made the connection I felt so relieved. Update — I woke up the next morning already thinking about this movie a lot, so I’m giving it a rating bump!
Tags: dvd rental, library, in the dorm

Name: Cameraperson
Review: It’s movies like this that remind me why I want to pursue filmmaking. Parts of the way this was structured left me scratching my head, but the editing is pretty fantastic overall and there are tons of individual moments that got a real reaction out of me. This is also one of the best exercises of semiology I've ever seen in a film. All things considered... it’s just beautiful.

Name: Super Mario Bros.
Review:
When the assignment is to make a Mario Bros. game adaptation but you take an edible and instead write a surrealist dystopian sci-fi fantasy:




As cuckoo bananas bonkers as this movie is, I unironically enjoyed this a lot more than the new one. Give me a weird fucking auteur vision over a boring ass Illumination screenplay any day.
Tags: dvd, w/ hazel, at a friend's place

Name: Body Parts
Review: An easy pick for my favorite documentary of 2023 so far and second favorite of 2023 overall. An uncomfortable and depressing yet also hilarious and entertaining study of not just sex and its portrayal in Hollywood, but also body image and gender politics in all phases of production. There's a heavy emphasis on how individual actresses and their body doubles are affected, but also a strong element of how all of the above speaks to and educates the audience. It’s phenomenally edited, deeply insightful, and so confident in how it’s directed. I truly learned a lot from this and would recommend it to everyone. Amazing.

Name: The Big Lebowski
Review: I don’t think I was paying as much attention as I should’ve the first time I saw this, or it’s just that I’ve just changed a lot as a person since then. Either way, this was SO much better than I remembered and it’s probably a top 5 Coen movie for me now. I’ve had a particularly great week, but I still feel like this is a movie I needed right now. It starts off with some rough footing, but quickly turns into a nearly-flawlessly paced and hilarious stoner odyssey with one of the best acting ensembles I’ve seen in a while. It’s awesome and I see myself easily watching this much more often in the future.

Name: Mary and Max
Review: “The reason I forgive you is because you are not perfect. You are imperfect. And so am I.” I’ve been meaning to watch this movie for a long time now, but I either never feel like I’m in the right mood or it just slips my mind. Tonight, my friend suggested it and I decided today would be that day… …and I was absolutely floored. I haven’t watched a movie that made me shed real tears in ages… oh my GOD!! I’m a mess right now, and for that I’m imperfect. But that’s a beautiful thing. I can’t physically add more right now, but words literally can’t do this experience justice. This is a movie I’ve needed my whole life. Autism is beautiful, and no movie has made me feel more seen as an autistic individual than this one (the only other movie that has come close is Punch-Drunk Love). This was life-affirming and beautiful and masterful. :,)
Tags: internet archive, w/ hazel, in the dorm, cried or teared up

Name: Lessons of Darkness
Review: Beautifully shot and edited, but I didn’t learn or take away as much as I was hoping. I get that the point of the movie is to be low on interviews and human experiences, and to its credit it is an interesting antithesis on traditional documentaries, but I wasn’t really moved by this execution of that idea.

Name: Sisu
Review: Working title: Inglour-Sisu-s Basterds It’s kinda like Godland or The Revenant directed by Michael Bay but it’s good stupid fun. One of the girls at the end looked like Björk and at the end of the day, that's what cinema's all about.
Tags: amc river east 21, early screener, w/ jadyn, w/ claire s

Name: The Fly
Review:
This is the first time in a while that I rewatched a movie in theaters and truly felt like it can only be seen on the big screen. This was the most unexpectedly great movie to watch while high — I had an absolute ball of a time laughing, crying, and being scared shitless for 90 minutes.

The witty and snappy screenplay, the animalistic performances, the mindblowing practical effects, Howard Shore’s music, stunning editing and camerawork, the fascination of sci-fi combined with the frights of body horror, the way it made me think about what I could be doing better in my own life and having me reevaluate what I love in movies… yeah fuck it this is a 10.
Tags: doc films, 35mm, w/ jadyn, cried or teared up

Name: Being John Malkovich
Review: The fact that this weird ass movie got nominated for Oscars in 1999 gives me hope that one day mainstream cinema can be this bizarre and queer again.
Tags: doc films, w/ izzy, w/ jadyn

Name: We're All Going to the World's Fair
Review: It’s kinda like Enter the Void for chronically online Gen Z but I really liked it. It was kinda predictable and not that scary for the most part despite me really loving the filmmaking, but the final act surprised me and genuinely hit me emotionally, kudos for that. It reminded me of COVID depression and other previous memories from middle and high school. I’ll be thinking about it for a bit. I started this for the first time in April 2021 at a virtual film festival but my rental period expired and it took me two years to finally finish it lmfao, it was worth it though @ Christian — thanks for the rec, you should rewatch this
Tags: hoopla, in the dorm, sad liminal vibes, lived in locations

Name: Paris Is Burning
Review: “If you shoot an arrow and it goes real high, hooray for you.” The phrase ‘slice-of-life’ gets thrown around a lot but this literally is a portal into 1980s New York. It’s a beautiful, intimate, and heartbreaking guide to queer life and I genuinely think I will always adore it. I can’t wait to be a documentary filmmaker. I also really want to learn how to vogue.

Name: Polite Society
Review: This reminded me a lot of Edgar Wright on a sitcom screenplay with a campy version of Get Out thrown in(?). It needed some re-writes, but I definitely had fun! Personally I would not do what Salim did.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ ross, early screener

Name: Curse of Chucky
Review: The 2010s-horror-fication of Chucky… isn’t great. Bride and Seed are flawed, but they still have substance to offer along with being products of their eras, especially the latter being a hidden gem of a movie about exposure to horror at a young age. The message of this movie is a mix of eating the rich and family ties, and while I respect how dark this one gets, there is almost no comedy to create a good tonal contrast and the character writing is just not up to par. It has an original musical score which is very good, some really striking practical effects and camera framing, and many of the most brutal kills in the franchise. But as I said, the character treatments are really not that good and the lore, while interesting, only serves this individual entry. I remember actually caring about the characters when I was younger but in retrospect, Nica is the only one really to care about and she’s written poorly. At its best, this is an operatic slasher movie, and I appreciate the gothic nature of the setup. But at its worst, and in the end, it’s more of a hodgepodge of loose ends, giving me the impression that Don Mancini was getting tired at this point. I still like this movie as I do every Chucky entry as even when I don’t connect with the vision, I always admire Mancini’s ambition and this franchise holds a lot of nostalgia. But this was the disappointment of the binge for me, unfortunately. Side-Note: I unintentionally watched the R-rated cut and it didn’t really impact the movie that much aside from some missing gore, but for some reason it doesn’t have the post-credits scene of the NC-17 cut. It’s a difference that only fans will notice, but I still felt it was worth including here.

Name: Blissfully Yours
Review: และแม่ของคุณด้วย (That means Y Tu Mamá También in Thai! This was a really beautiful movie)

Name: Zombieland
Review: I was in the clouds but I think this is the best movie ever. Citizen Kane doesn’t end with a shootout in a theme park, now does it?
Tags: netflix, w/ izzy, w/ jadyn, w/ claire s, at a friend's place

Name: Syndromes and a Century
Review:
We think too hard and too much.


(I asked Apichatpong a question and got a photo with him after the movie and I will never forget those moments)

Name: Star Wars
Review:
Luke really mourned Obi-Wan more than his own aunt and uncle.

My ass got a DVD from the library for the original theatrical cut and I accidentally put on the 1997 special edition disc… but I love this movie too much to care.
Practical effects, genuine stakes, emotional characters, intelligent plot structuring, a completely justified 2 hour runtime, the most iconic score in cinema history, hundreds of childhood memories… it’s not only everything I miss from Hollywood genre movies today, it's everything I adore in movies in general!
I will never not love revisiting lifetime favorites years after I’ve seen them, it’s such a joy every single time. It’s not a 10 in my critical brain, but it is in my heart so it gets the bump.
Tags: dvd rental, library, in the dorm, w/ hazel

Name: Days of Heaven
Review: I didn’t have the best of watching conditions for this but I still understood everything that happened. The story just wasn’t working for me for a while, but the ending started to change my mind so I’m still unsure how I feel about the movie as a whole. I definitely prefer Badlands and Tree of Life. Beautiful cinematography and Morricone music for sure. I liked all the dogs.

Name: The Empire Strikes Back
Review:
The bravest thing Luke did in this movie was eating Yoda’s musty ass food.


(I can’t bring myself to just leave it at a joke review, this movie will always mean SO much to me and be a lifetime favorite. Fucking awesome)
Tags: dvd rental, library, in the dorm, w/ hazel

Name: Return of the Jedi
Review: I have vivid memories of the first time I watched the Star Wars trilogy. I remember the first time I saw the opening crawl of A New Hope, the first time I heard that Vader is Luke’s father, and a particularly strong memory of the first time I saw Vader throw Palpatine down the Death Star. I was 6, and these were the greatest movies I had seen since I was first introduced to Pixar. I was blown away, my life was changed. I have been wanting to see one of the original Star Wars movies in theaters since then. Tonight, at the crisp age of 19, that wish was finally granted. Sure, it was the 1997 special edition and not the original cut, but I saw this damn movie and I saw it in IMAX at that rate so I really don’t care at all. I had an absolute blast with a smile plastered to my face and my eyes glued to the monstrous screen. This isn’t a perfect movie, none of the Star Wars movies are, but this is one of those 10/10 experiences. Some things in life are worth waiting multiple years for, and this was just that.
Tags: showplace icon roosevelt, w/ hazel, cried or teared up, whatever the large format here was called

Name: Waltz with Bashir
Review:
I was engaged from start to finish but only processing the story progression about 2/3 of the time, and I think that honestly added to the immersion of how this movie tackles and portrays memories, specifically images that scar us for life. It’s exhausting and devastating. A perfect movie to show anyone who thinks animation is a medium for kids, and something that everyone should watch in general because it's just phenomenal.

Edit - removed the rating as I have become far more aware of what the IDF has done, both during the time period this takes place in and in the modern day.

Name: The Celebration
Review: Ain’t no party like a Dogme 95 party cause a Dogme 95 party is uncomfortable and challenging as it uses unconventional and inspiring filmmaking to highlight the strangest parts of humanity! I need to decompress before I go to sleep because this was insane! Sorry to use two modern American examples, but it reminded me a lot of the anxiety of Shiva Baby combined with the structure and pacing of The Meyerowitz Stories. The Celebration didn't move me *quite* as much as those two, but I respect the hell out of how this laid the groundwork for so much future cinema, and overall I loved it… I think.

Name: Sibyl
Review: A lot of moody, powerful, and personal moments that hit VERY well in a movie that needed a stronger structure. The performances (my first film with Adele Exarchopoulos and she was phenomenal), gorgeous cinematography, bold questioning of ethics, and overall atmosphere of this movie are awesome, but it’s a little overstuffed and oddly paced so it leaves me questioning more than I want to. I still really liked this overall though — it’s certainly memorable and won me over. Can’t wait for Anatomy of a Fall.

Name: Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse

I was ultimately won over by the final emotional core, visual clusterfuckery (in a good way), and impressive plot structuring / revealing of information.

Name: Bama Rush
Review: Weird movie. Not as good as The Act of Killing. I think this is a story that needed to be told in documentary form, but the filmmakers take a strangely objective stance on Greek life and fail to bring up a lot of other aspects that surround it. The director explicitly states that she doesn't want to turn people off of rushing after segments on bullying, racism, and her own alopecia (which she thought was relevant here) ?? She also put herself on an equal pedestal as her subjects, and in the end there isn't much actual rushing on screen at all, so what was the point? A lot of individual moments work cohesively and emotionally, but as a final product it really feels like a movie that didn't have enough of a plan or much research when they set out to make it. The lack of focus made me forget what the whole point of the documentary was on numerous occasions and the more I think about it, the more I dislike it. At the end of the day, as an aspiring documentarian, I learned more of what NOT to do from watching this.
Tags: hbo max (courtesy of friends), in the dorm

Name: Pitch Perfect
Review: Unexpectedly really liked this ?? New guilty pleasure unlocked! I wish I saw this like 4 years earlier.
