Name: The General
Review:
Buster Keaton never fails to make me laugh. This is also such a great movie from a shot composition standpoint, I always love me some beautiful landscapes and stunning usage of foreground/background! Don’t really need to say anything more about how good the stuntwork is.

The ending lost me a bit though, it made me realize this is far more of an entertaining movie than one I genuinely connect to. Not the worst thing ever, it just didn’t keep the same energy going. Still really liked this overall, it gave me the idea to add a chase to my current screenplay!

Name: Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse
Review: Reasons why I bumped this up to a 9/10: 1. Akira motorbike shot 2. I felt even more strongly that this is a superhero movie filled to the brim with genuine emotion and struggles where all ends meet and no conflict is forgotten about or sidelined, made arguably more impressive by the fact that this is a Part 1 movie. I've said it before, I'll say it again -- the best superhero movies are those where there are more villains than just the bad guy who needs to be defeated and this is such an incredible example of that; Miles' struggle to prove his worth to everyone including himself, Rico's struggle to be satisfied with himself as both a cop and father, Gwen's struggle to make amends with her dad, and hell, even Miguel's struggle to hold everything together. It's such a great screenplay, emotionally honest in ways that almost all new studio movies just aren't. It could've very well ended up being a therapy session for the screenwriters, but that's how you know a film is well written; genuine emotion masked by a broader, more accessible and cinematic story. And of course I don’t need to say any more about how impressive this is visually. My small criticisms from the first review stand, but don't be mistaken... I really do love this movie. A lot.
Tags: regal webster place, w/ jadyn, w/ izzy, w/ claire s

Name: Creature Comforts
Review:
One of my favorite short films, ever since I was a kid. Thought-provoking, funny, and serious all in 5 minutes. Maybe my favorite mockumentary, I love Nick Park and Aardman so much.

I relate to this because I would also hate living in captivity in England. The polar bears' faces look like a weird hybrid between both Wallace and Gromit.
Tags: youtube, w/ hazel, on the quad

Name: The Flash
Review: The complete emotional arc is really predictable from the get-go, it’s not visually pleasing to put it nicely, the characters are weak and none of the performances are very good, and it introduces a ton of new ideas only to expand upon none of them. All the superhero movie sins. The budget was at least $200 million for this, there's no excuse for it to be as frustratingly immature as Barry Allen's character. If you’ve never seen 1 (one) time travel or multiverse movie, then the story and stakes will be impressive to you. I would’ve enjoyed it more if they stuck with a more grounded and fun story since I didn’t hate it for a bit, but of course they just HAD to try being profound and large-scale and very weakly tackle the multiverse like we didn’t see it coming… only to have almost none of the emotional ends meet. It’s not powerful, it’s pointless emotional whiplash. There are definitely some heroes in smaller roles on the crew who tried really hard on this film so they deserve credit, especially since they were probably underpaid in favor of pointless cameos. The soundtrack was good too. Across the Spiderverse has been making me rethink the limits and capabilities of superhero movies. This did the opposite. Make the right choice if you're going to see one in theaters.
Tags: amc newcity 14, early screener, w/ jadyn, w/ claire s, w/ izzy

Name: Jubilee
Review: HAPPY PRIDE MONTH! Finally finished the school year and am able to start my queer film watching! And nothing to kick off pride month like a bizarre movie critiquing the 70s British punk scene with a ton of political, artistic, and music industry-related discussions against a Clockwork Orange-esque vision of the country, especially visually. This isn't a good film to watch after the ADHD pill wears off and it definitely isn't one to fully understand on one watch, but one thing I do know for certain is that the sets, costumes, lighting, and cinematography were all KILLER! It didn't speak to me on a lot of personal levels and I feel it didn't warrant this long of a runtime, but a lot of individual scenes in this are excellent and it left me thinking a good bit about hypocrisy and activism. This would be one of Britta Perry's favorite movies. I'm in the mood for some Siouxsie and the Banshees now.
Tags: criterion channel, pride month '23, at home

Name: Blue Velvet
Review:
Hopefully this doesn’t happen to me over the summer.


Still good but less good than I remembered. Re-watching your favorites can be dangerous. I adore the art direction and cinematography as much as I always have and the meshing of classic family TV tropes with the darkness of crime and noir is still a pretty brilliant genre blend, but I found myself caring less about a lot of what was going on, even if I was engaged the whole time. One of the best opening sequences in history, hands down.

Name: Wild Things
Review: A big shitpost of a movie that takes itself too seriously to be fun or campy, but isn’t nearly well written enough to compel me or keep me from not giving a shit; I almost fell asleep towards the end. It was just annoying to me and felt completely forced in all the wrong ways. I’ve had plenty of bad first watches that grow on me a lot, but I honestly think I would’ve disliked this either way. Points for the potential of me liking it more in the future + great cinematography and aesthetics, I want to live in Neve Campbell’s doll head house. Update — I also had a frustrating theater crowd which isn’t the movie’s fault. Note to self: Don’t see re-release movies in theaters unless you’ve already seen them.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, me time!, pride month '23

Name: Bringing Up Baby
Review: 100 minute long episode of Looney Toons where everyone is rich and annoying... Fun movie! Great filmmaking and comedic timing with brilliant natural-flowing dialogue and performances, I just wish the story and stakes offered more for me to care about and connect to. Normalize having goofy misadventures with your partner.

Name: Kiki's Delivery Service
Review: "Then I stop drawing. I take walks, look at the scenery, take naps, do nothing. Then after a while, all of a sudden I get the urge to draw again." I started this back in January but only made it halfway before getting really depressed. I regret not finishing it then because I've needed a movie like this to help me through the last 6 months, but better late than never. EASILY the best first watch of the summer so far and one of the best of the year in general. Everything you could possibly want from a Miyazaki film on a smaller scale than something grander like Spirited Away. I had no notes as I left the theater so I'll leave it at this for now, but I was genuinely so close to giving it a 10.
Tags: amc naperville 16, ghibli fest, w/ quinn

Name: American Graffiti
Review: "It doesn't make sense to leave home to look for home, to give up a life to find a new life, to say goodbye to friends you love just to find new friends." So, so, so good. We don’t say “bitchin’” or hang dice above our car mirrors nearly enough anymore. So bittersweet for me emotionally when thinking about memories of high school and how life changes in the blink of an eye but in a strangely comforting way, and it’s SO well made in all aspects. These characters represent so many things for me, and it helps that they're entertaining as hell. The dialogue is so good and the story structure is perfect in how information is revealed and how twists just naturally find their way into the story, for both the highest realism and emotional impact. If I was a little more awake for this watch I genuinely think I'd give it full marks, but I had a great time either way so the rating doesn't mean much to me. I'll probably bump it up at some point soon anyways. Love this movie.
Tags: netflix, virtual watch party, w/ izzy, at home

Name: Desert Hearts
Review: I love it when romance movies have enough faith in the screenplay and performances to feel natural, real, and soft enough to leave a lasting impression thanks to emotional honesty. No shade at all to romance that’s loud and operatic in its presentation, a lot of my favorite romance movies function that way! But the ability to make an effective and lasting toned-down romance feels more directly relatable to my own past relationships, and this hasn’t seemed to get appreciated enough as of late (hopefully that changes with Past Lives as it gets more popular). This was a really great movie that slowly crept up on me, and I can’t wait to talk about it more in the future.
Tags: criterion channel, pride month '23, at home

Name: Bumblebee
Review: I loved Bumblebee when it came out thanks to a strong connection with Charlie's struggling aimless character combined with the thrill of all the action, and I'm glad to say I still found some enjoyment out of it today. For a recent comparison, I'd put it in the same league as Dungeons and Dragons in that it's an unmistakable IP studio movie, but it feels like actual human care was put into the production. It's an obvious setup for a new universe and 80s nostalgia bait above all else, but a solid version of that. I still relate to many parts of Charlie, especially in the first half when the film is at its best, focused more on a good story and less on action. The second half falters not just because of a lesser focus on story and a predictable finale, but also because it falls victim to sci-fi elements fulfilling emotional arcs in place of human connection, which I’ve grown really tired of. In Bumblebee's case, I would have far preferred an ending where Charlie finds solace in Memo or her brother after all is said and done. I can see how the ending we got / the emotional arc between Charlie and Bumblebee would satisfy those who had a strong connection to the Transformers universe prior, since those people are undoubtedly the target audience here. Maybe I’m jaded from AI, or I don’t connect to having a Transformer solve my father wound, but it just leaves me feeling unfulfilled after genuinely caring about Charlie's journey leading up to this. It's also worth noting the CGI is surprisingly good here, none of the shots stood out to me as bad, and it makes a clever use of a standard 80s soundtrack. I also have a soft spot for movies that use amusement parks as effective backdrops, but it's hard not to call it surface level here when Jordan Peele's Us came out just three months later and even used the exact same park. (Santa Cruz Beach Boardwalk) I'm tempted to bump the rating up to a 7/10, but I'll leave it here for now. Hopefully the solid blockbuster quality continues over to the new Transformers movie, which I'll probably be seeing this weekend with my family.
Tags: paramount+, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, at home

Name: Monsters, Inc.
Review: I had to revisit another Pixar movie before Elemental tomorrow, especially after realizing how long it's been since I've seen most of them. I almost cried after my mom reminded me of a memory of this movie from when I was growing up, and if / when I have kids I can't wait to share a similar experience with them. The movie is great too. Endlessly creative in all aspects, super funny, amazing music, and Marxism for kids! Jennifer Tilly my beloved <3
Tags: disney+, w/ dad, w/ mom, cried or teared up, at home

Name: Elemental
Review: Pixar’s Clash of Clans There's neat worldbuilding (even if the world’s logic doesn’t make a ton of sense), and on paper there’s a solid central metaphor about family roles. But most of the emotional beats felt rushed and unrewarding, and at that rate there are a few too many of them. I never actively disliked it, but almost everything in this movie has been done by Disney somewhere before. The Kernkraft 400 needle drop made me laugh.

Name: Transformers: Rise of the Beasts
Review: Rise of the Beasts is fun and inoffensive, albeit nothing amazing or something I’ll still be thinking about weeks out from now. It’s Citizen Kane compared to the Michael Bay movies (I will never take this for granted), but I’m also impatiently waiting for Past Lives to expand so everyone can finally see what they’ve been missing from their local multiplex.
Tags: amc naperville 16, 3d, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: The Champion
Review: Charlie Chaplin wears a gay outfit at the end (happy pride!), the bulldog was adorable, visual storytelling is used super well, and the physical comedy is hilarious... it's super campy and kind of a perfect short film. Chaplin could do Fast X but John Cena and The Rock couldn't do The Champion. Every wrestler-turned-actor should be required to make a silent film.

Name: Bottle Rocket
Review:
I'm not adding anything new to the discussion, but this is a great example of a directorial debut that has such a strong and distinct style to it from the get-go. Every shot doesn't have to be symmetrical and dollhouse-core for me to recognize that this was made by Wes, it just FEELS like it.
The offbeat mannerisms of the characters with their eccentric conflicts and desires, the striking color palates and coverage of every location (this is how you make desaturated colors look pretty, Disney!), consistent humor, and well-executed plot twists.

It's easily the simplest Wes movie and one of the less memorable ones at that, as I expected, but it's not my least favorite and I still had a super fun and great time with it! Right on the border of a 7 and 8.


TL;DR -- I greatly enjoy the work of Wesley Wales Anderson
Tags: hulu, virtual watch party, w/ izzy, at home

Name: Mission: Impossible
Review: I thought I had seen this entire movie when I was younger, but so much of it felt new to me that I think my parents only showed me certain scenes. Regardless, it was interesting to finally see the original all the way through after loving every 2010s Mission: Impossible movie growing up. I'm excited to rewatch all of those before Dead Reckoning so I can give some retrospective thoughts. Anyways, there are moments of spy-thriller brilliance with the great opening scene and all the heists along the way, but the screenplay did just enough with the characters and plot to keep me engaged without really captivating me or finding a strong emotional in-point. It's a strong and fun start to the franchise, but I feel they were prioritizing spectacle a little too much at the expense of a better narrative. This unintentionally made a great double feature with Bottle Rocket last night, in a way that made me appreciate Wes Anderson's debut a bit more for being loose and incorporating genre as more of a step two.

Name: Past Lives
Review: I hate all other movies.
Tags: amc oakbrook center 12, w/ mom, w/ dad, w/ quinn, cried or teared up

Name: Asteroid City
Review: It REALLY pains me to say this, but Asteroid City was just Wes Anderson white noise for me. It looks beautiful of course and I really fuck with this aesthetic, but that’s a given for his movies. It didn’t focus enough on the individual characters I had attachment to and the story moved too fast for me to have really latched onto anything emotionally. I was unmoved almost the whole time, I’m genuinely sad this didn’t work better for me.

Name: Black Mirror: Be Right Back
Review:
Got really sad after looking at Hayley Atwell’s Letterboxd page and realizing how few film opportunities she’s had to reach her full potential. She’s a good fucking actress and deserves better.

This one started off insanely strong, but once the doll came in I predicted the rest and was a tad unsatisfied. But I thought it was really good nonetheless. Maybe a 7.

Name: Raiders of the Lost Ark
Review: Great feature length version of that one short film Sam made in The Fablemans! This combined my fascinations for history, geography, and movie VFX when I was 7 or 8, and stuck around as a favorite for many years after my first time. It definitely was a springboard for a lot of what got me to this point. (in tandem with The Last Crusade, of course) I don't *quite* love the movie like an all-time favorite today, the romance in particular stood out as especially cheesy. But the production value is still amazing even by today's standards, and Spielberg knows scene coverage like a master and can shoot an incredibly engrossing genre film. He always will. I might bump the rating up eventually for those reasons alone.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, at home

Name: Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom
Review: The first and last 20 minutes are a ton of fun, and the production value is still amazing. It's really well made, the set pieces especially, and the fact that it commits to the bit is super admirable on a technical level. But the screenplay is just trivial. The stakes are insignificant since it's a prequel and you know Indiana survives, and none of the story elements are rooted in anything I can feel anything towards so it ends up just being fairly boring and annoying. Even as a kid, I wasn't scared or laughing at all. It doesn't help that the supporting characters aren't strong enough for me to give a shit either. I never liked this one much as a kid, and I still don't like it much today. I don't hate it, but it leaves no lasting impression except for being excited that The Last Crusade is next.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ quinn, at home

Name: Talk to Me
Review:
I got nothing else to say right now other than this was genuinely horrifying and I loved it. I’m kinda speechless, this movie isn’t perfect but it’s the first horror movie in a while that’s left me in a bad, nervous headspace from how well it worked on me. There were some buried memories and nightmares that got dug up as I watched this.

I say this both with and without the bias I have for RackaRacka… this is a really great debut. Absolutely go see it when it comes out.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ hazel, early screener

Name: Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade
Review: George Lucas loves to end his trilogies with stories about parent issues, and bless him for it. The search for the father is the search for the holy grail. This was the first Indiana Jones film I saw all the way through when I was 7, and I had never seen something so exciting and funny that also appealed to my interest for history. Film and its capabilities were so new, and little did I know that was barely even the tip of the iceberg. I miss that time of my life. Anyways, this is still my definite favorite of the series.
Tags: paramount+, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull
Review: This is not a very good movie but it made me feel nostalgic and, as I've been saying in the last few reviews, took me back to when I was 7 and the world was new. I will always personally appreciate this mid movie for that reason alone.

Name: Stranger Than Paradise
Review: This is the boring America that I feel I’ve experienced most of my life, which is admirable from a cinematic standpoint in how it captures the desolate aesthetic. But also because of this I was turned off at a good number of points. Strange is an apt adjective here, I‘ll probably be thinking about this movie on and off for a while. It felt like a directorial debut not just in its obvious and intentional simplicity, but in how every film it reminded me of is also a debut -- Je Tu Il Elle, The Seventh Continent, Buffalo '66, Breathless, etc... But I definitely see it growing on me, like Seventh Continent in particular did.
Tags: criterion channel, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Hard Boiled
Review:
He was saved by the little piss-pot.

And a bunch of other really awesome action (Hong Kong does it so insanely well), mise-en-scene (John Woo loves his insert shots), powerful performances (Tony Leung!), and intense use of freeze frames and slo-mo. Reeeally great stuff, I wish I remembered to take my Adderall today so I could’ve focused a touch better.
Tags: amazon prime, w/ cam, at a friend's place

Name: Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny
Review:
The Dial of Disney.

It's never offensively bad, and there are a handful of entertaining action sequences and promising setups. But the lack of Spielberg's concise direction and creative choices in favor of overstylized Disney shit sticks out like a sore thumb, and the movie just isn't fun or enjoyable like the first four are… there are more emotional beats here, only a few of them effective, and at that rate it takes itself much more seriously than all the previous entries. I just found it a really frustrating experience, definitely my new least favorite of the franchise.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Mission: Impossible II
Review:
John Woo's direction shines a lot in the second half as most of the story drama is forgotten for a great series of chases and twists to close things out; practical effects, hand-to-hand combat, slo-mo and all.
But the drama leading up to it is pretty bad, and unfortunately difficult to overlook. The first half is 50% fine spy movie, 50% everyone wanting to fuck Thandie Newton (PSA: that’s not a character trait). The second half reigns supreme for me so I'd call it a decent movie overall, but the characters and stakes are lazy and prevent it from being any better. Robert Towne, what happened?
Tags: paramount+, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Mission: Impossible III
Review:
It's a solid 2006 action movie, and it’s admirable in a camp way how J.J. leaned so heavily into the late 90s/early 00s aesthetic for his directorial debut. But this entry mostly forgot that it’s part of a spy-mystery franchise, as there isn't really any intrigue to be found or a real enthusing plot for that matter. It's weirdly straightforward from start to finish. Enjoyable enough and somewhat of an improvement over the previous film, but not particularly great.


Two funny things I noticed:
1. The supporting cast really outshines Cruise here, as Simon Pegg was given a silly introduction and Philip Seymour Hoffman was surprisingly camp. (this entire movie kinda is)
2. The War on Terror metaphors are kinda hilariously obvious.
Tags: paramount+, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Happy Together
Review:
I spent all day yesterday outside and today I had a stressful shift. I’m really tired and watching a movie was definitely not the move tonight, but this was still good.
I genuinely did not plan on rewatching it exactly one year after my first watch but it’s cool as fuck that I did.

Name: Dark Star
Review: John Carpenter’s debut feels like a debut. On one hand, it has some solid production design and editing, but the screenplay just isn’t that interesting and at times had me wondering if Carpenter meant to write something satirical or if it ended up being funny unintentionally. Was Ed Wood’s Plan 9 a light influence? On the other, the final 15 minutes are genuinely well orchestrated and thought provoking - Carpenter finally proves himself as a director with potential. Here you can tell he really likes Kubrick and Twilight Zone. (not as good as either of those but you can see the parallels) The mediocrity of everything leading up to it does make me wonder if this needed to be a feature. But I’ll give it a small benefit of the doubt for now.
Tags: criterion channel, car dvd / on a roadtrip

Name: 24 Days
Review: I had never heard of this until Hoopla recommended it to me, and I went in completely blind which wasn’t my brightest idea for a movie on vacation since this was really depressing… whoops! It was great though - powerful, important, very well acted, and mostly tightly made. It doesn’t blow the roof off the crime-drama genre, but it’s comparable to recent acclaimed efforts such as Holy Spider. And this is a case where simplicity in parts of the presentation work effectively for maximum emotional impact as opposed to something more stylized. There were a few scenes that felt edited like a TV show and that took me out, specifically during a pivotal emotional moment. Part of me also feels this would’ve worked better as a documentary, but I definitely felt its impact at the end and would recommend it if you’re in the mood for something real and devastating.
Tags: hoopla, car dvd / on a roadtrip

Name: Barbarian
Review: Barbarian has been growing on me since I first saw it, but I never thought to actually sit down and rewatch it until tonight when I switched to HBO at the exact right moment when the opening logos were rolling. I grew up watching movies on cable TV with my dad whenever something was playing he wanted to show me, so I felt really nostalgic when I got the chance to do it again, on a family vacation no less. Anyways, watching it late after a day of driving through three states wasn’t my brightest idea, but I can still say with certainty that I was too harsh the first time and that Barbarian is indeed a very good movie. In particular, I have been thinking about the film’s editing the most in the last 7 months, especially the blunt jump cuts between narratives. They are truly bold choices, especially from a directorial debut, and on first impression lost me, as I understood why they were used but failed to impress me on a thematic level. I didn’t gain an emotional connection from them. On watch 2, I still don’t have a strong emotional connection to the movie, but my admiration for the bold choices is even stronger now. The first act is certainly my favorite, as Cregger introduces what is clearly a horror setting with a consistent sense of unnerve and tension without actively trying to scare the audience every few seconds, the mark of any good horror beginning. The two leads are actively interesting, and all their conversations are really impressive in how genuine they feel. What impresses me most about Barbarian is how it uses geography. The location of Brightmoor in Detroit isn’t shown until 25 minutes in, making for a reveal and unnerving aesthetic that will gain the attention from any viewer who has been told to avoid areas like this their whole life. The following introduction of Los Angeles in the second chapter sets up an interesting contrast that develops AJ’s character just as much as his angering conversations do. The third act still falls short for me, as the interesting characters serve no further purpose than to fulfill horror genre tropes and the monster isn’t given a ton more development than just the disgusting monster, especially when it goes 100% horror after developing a commentary on how the USA has failed neighborhoods like Brightmoor over the years. I will admit though I was able to have more fun with the pulpy finale this time around. TL;DR — really good horror movie. The setup is excellent and enough to warrant a watch, even if it gets weaker as it goes along.
Tags: on tv, in a hotel, w/ dad

Name: Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind
Review: I was too tired tonight to follow every last plot detail (I got most of it though!). But it made me like Avatar a little bit less — the mark of any good Ghibli movie.

Name: Night at the Museum: Secret of the Tomb
Review: Guilty pleasure / nostalgic fun. The Escher scene is genuinely good. This trilogy is the only culturally relevant thing Shawn Levy has ever done.
Tags: on tv, in a hotel, w/ dad, w/ quinn, on vacation, colorado

Name: Mission: Impossible – Ghost Protocol
Review: It was Steven Spielberg who once said that all directors should be animators first, and I thought about this quote a decent amount while watching Ghost Protocol. Brad Bird is an incredible director of animated movies, and while his first live action venture was bound to be a bit weaker than his previous works, his stylistic and thematic traits only really shine every so often here. That aside, this is still a good movie. The action is stellar here, every sequence in Dubai and Mumbai is just great. The screenplay is pretty forgettable and never goes above and beyond what it has to do, but the standard it had to meet still made it an entertaining watch that, despite lacking in character, understands the mission better than the last two sequels did. I think De Palma’s franchise starter was still the strongest entry up to this point, but McQuarrie was sure to give him some tough competition.* * = I ran out of time before the vacation I’m currently on to rewatch Rogue Nation and Fallout and I’ll be going into Dead Reckoning only up to this point… so I’ll figure that last statement out for sure another day.

Name: Mission: Impossible – Dead Reckoning

Name: Stagecoach
Review: I methodically waited until I was with my grandpa who introduced me to John Ford years ago to watch my first John Ford movie, and I have zero regrets. He finally got to show me a movie from his childhood and it’s a really great one at that! The action was amazing and the group dynamic was compelling… but best of all, the horizon was always at the top and bottom where it was interesting and never in the middle where it would be BORING AS SHIT.

Name: Berlin: Symphony of a Great City

Name: Oppenheimer
Review: A delightful coming-of-age comedy about a small-town schoolboy who moves to the big city and makes a name for himself.
Tags: amc river east 21, early screener, w/ hazel, 70mm

Name: Barbie
Review: Barbie combines all the childlike wonder of a young kid playing with the title brand on the surface but deep down figuring out who they want to be, with the drive and emotional intelligence of an adult who has experienced the lowpoints of adult life but refuses to lose their optimism. Director Barbie / Greta Gerwig proves that she knows how to make an excellent movie even within the fantasy genre and the confines of studio interference. I have my share of criticisms, but all that be damned until another time because tonight I had the most fun I’ve had in a movie theater in ages.
Tags: charlestowne 18, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, early screener

Name: Moonlight
Review: — I meant to only watch the first chapter for visual inspiration as I was color grading my upcoming short film, but ended up sitting down with my parents and watching the whole thing again. — this is still a near-perfect, cathartic movie even with my parents asking questions every 10 minutes. What more do I need in cinema aside from poetic filmmaking and a good cry? — I thought a lot about my last watch of this which demolished me when I was severely depressed in February, and how no other movie harnesses the power of positive memories as effectively as this does for me. Watching this with my parents started the first convo we’ve had about my mental health in a couple months. — there’s a lot of emotional relatability in Chiron for me, but the mental and physical freedom he feels from the beach and water is an immortal symbol for me. And this peace contrasted with the feelings of shame and confusion about who he is and how to help himself is one of the most human narrative elements ever put on the silver screen. “Cause one step ahead is a step too far away from you…”
Tags: blu-ray, w/ dad, w/ mom, cried or teared up, at home

Name: The Only Son
Review: Ozu is definitely the blueprint for a lot of my favorite movies and I'm sure he has a future all-timer or two for me somewhere in his repertoire. Can't wait to find out which ones they are. This was really good even if it didn't floor me. Could definitely grow on me though. I've been meaning to dive into Ozu’s work ever since I found out he was the main inspiration behind Columbus, but better late than never. 7.5

Name: Gran Turismo
Review: Decent enough fun but we need to stop making racing movies longer than 2 hours 6.0

Name: m.A.A.d
Review: A really exuberant and artful visualization of one of my favorite rap albums. I genuinely don't know how I hadn't heard of this until today. Beautiful and inspiring.

Name: Carne
Review: What I can only assume is the proof-of-concept short for I Stand Alone is more watchable than the feature purely based on the shorter runtime, but feels just as style-over-substance unfortunately. I think I will always love Gaspar Noe’s approach to technical filmmaking, if not just because Enter the Void and Climax changed the way I view the medium, but his stories remain very hit-or-miss for me. I’m more open-minded now than I was a year ago when I binged the remaining movies of Noe’s I had to see before Vortex, but even when giving Carne a genuine chance, I was left mostly unmoved. The first 15 minutes are great as they slowly and arguably meditatively develop the Butcher and his psyche, but once the story reaches its highest point the remainder of the film just switches to a frustratingly surface level portrayal of nihilism complete with dialogue difficult to take seriously and several unintentionally goofy match cuts. Unconventional and provocative for sure, but ultimately unsatisfying. I can really only recommend this to people who are already Gaspar Noe fans.

Name: Prisoners
Review: Everyone in this movie sucks at driving, and I wouldn’t have been able to figure that out without the impeccable work of Roger Deakins. A great movie that didn’t quite floor me but I definitely need to let it sit. There are multiple phenomenally crafted sequences, but the scene of Jake Gyllenhaal speeding down the highway to the hospital will especially stick with me for quite a while. 8.0

Name: STRANGER
Review: Thought this was gonna be a Jhené Aiko biopic but it’s actually my friend’s short film!

Name: Runner
Review: She ran! This is in the same league as A Love Song for 2022 directorial debuts that feel very inspired by Kelly Reichardt and Bela Tarr with a bit of Malick sprinkled in. A lot of really good scenes with gorgeous blocking and cinematography, I just feel like I've seen this story done a lot in film and this one didn't add anything particularly unique to the narrative. (albeit this was a consistently watchable and enjoyable version of said story, so good movie overall) Thanks to CIFF for sending a free screener of this to members! 7.5

Name: Circus Maximus

All around, amazing experience and really good movie. I’m so stoked to hear Utopia in full, from what I heard in the movie it’ll possibly end up my new favorite album of his. 8.0

Name: New Chapter
Review: My third movie has arrived!! I hope New Chapter connects with others struggling with the same reasons why I made this in the first place, autism / neurodivergence, and that all audiences find something to connect to while watching. Here tis!

Name: Landscape in the Mist
Review: Good things come to those who wait.* * = those who wait meaning me finally finishing this incredibly slow but beautiful movie. I think with time this could grow to be one of my favorites. 8.5

Name: The Grandmother
Review: Amazing short film. This is the earliest of Lynch's work that I find genuinely scary. There's so much here of what would come in Lynch's later work with Eraserhead family horror, combining animation with live action, color palates with bizarre contrast, Kafka-esque motifs, nightmare sound design, etc.

Name: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles
Review: I wasn't sure what to expect, given I haven't seen this in 10 years, but I was entertained enough. It starts off really strong, with playful filmmaking, solid choreography and some funny dialogue that reminded me of some of my favorite 80s genre films, even if not as impressive. But unfortunately it peaks in the first act. April deserved more to work with as a character all around, the second act is pretty boring and a bit all over the place, and I felt myself slowly forgetting why I enjoyed the show as a kid. Thankfully the final act goes back to being more fun and engaging, though the stakes still feel too kids-movie-ish to bring the same energy. It's just... forgettable. Still fun, but not a great movie. 6.5

Name: Theater Camp
Review: This movie is a joy. Anyone who went to any kind of summer camp or day camp growing up will find something to latch onto here, but it’s a great little movie no matter how you look at it.
Tags: cinemark tinseltown north aurora, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Shortcomings
Review:
It took me a bit of time get into this story, but then the movie did that thing where it developed the characters who felt real a lot and found multiple emotional in-points and I finished out the movie with a big bittersweet smile.

Sure it feels derivative of other filmmakers and too structured at times, and it could be me-watching-this-at-the-exact-right-time syndrome (that name isn’t set in stone) but I genuinely had a really great time with this.


There’s really no feeling like realizing the year for film is getting really damn good. There’s so much great shit in theaters right now.

Name: Lola
Review: Movies about how love and romantic sensibilities flourish with age have been a pattern for me lately, and they seem to have become oddly comforting over time. Given the pessimistic nature of early French New Wave (Breathless and The 400 Blows), I suppose I should’ve expected something as dry as this, though I was thrown off for a bit at the start. But much like the two films I mentioned, it built little by little into something I really came to dig. My favorite story aspect was the friendship between Cécile and Frankie, but the final 10 minutes are pretty great too. My favorite technical aspect was the use of Beethoven’s 7th.
Tags: criterion channel, w/ dad, at home

Name: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles
Review: "She's so hot, I can feel my shell tightening." I could've gone my whole life without remembering that fucking line. Some of the action was mildly enjoyable (still a worse version of every great action movie) and it made me feel the smallest bit nostalgic, but those are the only nice things I can say here.

Name: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles: Mutant Mayhem
