This is, however, McQuarrie's first outing in the franchise and I love that he channeled more of De Palma than any of the other directors that took the helm before him.

Name: Shiva Baby
Review:
Whoops, somehow I forgot for a while that I adore this movie! But I watched it again and it's all coming back to me, don't worry!


Maybe a little less personal now than when I felt trapped in the world of my senior year, but it's still hilarious and painful and makes me feel seen in the most darkly comforting way. Amazing and inspiring movie.
Tags: hoopla, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Fast Times at Ridgemont High
Review: I loved it!! This may be my favorite movie of the mini teen movie binge I’m on right now. Beyond having great characters, great filmmaking, tons of laughs and being consistently engaging, this is the first movie in a while where I genuinely was able to turn my brain off of everything else going on. Thank you, Amy Heckerling and everyone involved with this movie.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: The Perks of Being a Wallflower
Review:
Okay so... that really was insanely good! I definitely didn't expect to love this as much as I did, but... wow!

I say a lot that I wish I saw teenage movies when I was in high school, but this one made me feel truly robbed of an experience during those years. I genuinely loved this movie a lot, and I’m sorry I don’t have more specifics to give right now but I was so moved by the experience and I just wanted to get initial thoughts out!

Name: The Wind Rises
Review: As beautiful as I was hoping, especially the ethereal animation. Not quite a favorite Ghibli on first impression, as the story structure lost me a couple times, but it still was a great experience and really made my heart heavy. The best moments are really masterful moments of emotion and portrayals of the artistic process, and I see the whole movie growing on me as I age.
Tags: amc naperville 16, ghibli fest, w/ cam

Name: Welcome to the Dollhouse
Review: I first heard of this movie when I was 14 and watched a few clips of it at that time but never saw the whole thing. Granted, I don’t blame myself - during that time, my taste in film was mainly geared towards comfort movies since that’s what really got me through middle school. For the first time in this binge, I watched a movie that I’m glad I hadn’t watched until now. This is the most relentlessly pessimistic portrayal I’ve seen of how teenagers really are since Kids. It has some heavy Fish Tank vibes too. It’s a burning experience that might stick with me in the same way, but on first impression is something that I’m still figuring out how to fully feel about and I wouldn’t quite put on the same level. Thank God I’m following this up with a Ghibli movie.

Name: WALL·E
Review:
A favorite of mine for 15 years now. The story may feel less impressive as I get older, but otherwise how does it always seem to hit me at the right time?


There’s a lot I could say but I’m tired and sick so I’ll leave this for now… watching this was a perfect reminder that movies are here for me in my life. And in this life, I’m here for movies.
Tags: virtual watch party, disney+, w/ aaliyah, w/ kelly, at home

Name: Casablanca
Review: Film noir has never been my favorite genre: while I’ve found every one I’ve seen to be watchable, I haven’t fallen in love with any of them yet. Casablanca didn’t immediately strike me as a favorite movie, though I can say with relative ease that it’s my favorite classic noir I’ve seen thus far. I see the appeal for its classic status (basically flawless filmmaking) and was also thoroughly entertained by it on a personal note. The whole thing is good, but the final 15-20 minutes are awesome and where I really felt the punch. This was a great pick for National Cinema Day. Aside from a loud breather sitting next to me, the theater experience was really fun.

Name: Rebel Without a Cause
Review: Great commentary on a) the treatment of teenage mental health and b) what it means to live for yourself, with a bit of the meaning of masculinity thrown in. I just don’t think on first impression that these always meshed effectively. I liked this movie overall though, the first hour is fantastic.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, at home

Name: Past Lives
Review: Another summer has come and gone and I couldn’t have asked for a better experience to commemorate that… I really want to rest my head on a shoulder right now. Past Lives is so comfortable in every way — I was genuinely sad when this ended and walked out of the theater unsure of how to approach the rest of the night. Every filmmaking choice feels like the smoothest brush stroke ever, the dialogue is just incredible, and the performances bring all the emotion to the table in both body language and vocals. It’s such a beautifully confident work and I wish I had delved into this level of detail sooner. I didn’t actually cry at the end this time, maybe because the pacing felt a bit different, but I did cry during the bed convo between Nora and Arthur the night after Nora and Jae Sung meet. The end made my heart really heavy, of course, but rather than being the clímax of all my feelings, it felt like more like a moment for reflection on why I cried at that specific moment and all the thoughts I had during the movie in general. This is such a special movie. It hit me less than last time, that’s just what happens with movies, but I still had a wonderful night.
Tags: cried or teared up, tivoli theater, after hours film society, me time!

Name: Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban
Review: My parents were like 2 minutes in when I got home from work and I had such a good time when I watched it back in February that I figured I'd give it another go. Juno can wait until later tonight or tomorrow. This is a wonderful and fun movie. Cuaron's directing is so playful in how he goes about adapting the novel into the exciting, twisty franchise entry it deserved to be, especially the time travel aspect. Great supporting cast too; slightly unrelated but I have a lack of talent for being able to identify Gary Oldman in all of his roles. More than that, this is the latest movie I've seen that reminded me how much fun filmmaking can and should be, the best example since Barbie - I had a blast making movies when I was a kid, so why should I ever sacrifice that same adrenaline and joy today? I think the fact that I enjoyed this movie so much growing up helps as well.
Tags: peacock, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire
Review:
Put this on in the background while I pack for moving back to Chicago tomorrow.

This movie feels long and probably should’ve been cut down (I watched the extended edition so that probably didn’t help) but it’s ultimately better than I remember. All the triwizard stuff is entertaining and the last 30 minutes are still a scorcher. It’s not really remarkable outside of the above scenes, but still good fun and nostalgic for me nonetheless.

Name: Empire Records
Review: Kinda great, to the point where I wish I didn’t watch this when I was so distracted by packing for my move back to Chicago. Reminded me a fair bit of Ridgemont High and Clerks in a record store, and while I don’t think it’s quite on the same level as those on first impression (especially the former) I still mean that comparison as high praise. Warren and Debra are my favorite characters for sure. Izzy, assuming you’re reading this, I’ll leave it up to you to find a time for a rewatch.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, w/ quinn, at home

Name: Caché
Review: Yesterday I moved into my first apartment, and today I took my first walk around the area. There’s a corner just a couple blocks from me that looks eerily similar to the first shot of Cachē, and I eventually took it as a sign to finally rewatch this movie. I’m glad I did because this felt incredibly different from my first watch 2.5 years ago. The mise-en-scene, performances, and overall craft are as deadpan yet incredible as I've come to expect from Michael Haneke; the visual storytelling here might just be perfect in how he reveals all the necessary clues and evidence. The best example of this is Majid's son, who briefly appears in Majid's doorway about 2/3 of the way through. Later, as part of an excellent suspenseful twist, he re-appears at Georges' place of work without saying a word. Haneke's trust in the audience more than pays off. Haneke keeps the film engrossing at all times without overstaying any welcome. As he puts it, “I enter [my stories] as late as possible and exit as early as I can.” Cachē captivates you on the edge of your seat at all times and the slow burn is executed beautifully, qualifying the movie as a thriller in my book, but this really is a domestic drama. The story is a seemingly picture-perfect nuclear family grappling with the father’s past as they reap the negative effects of the father maintaining his own image before everything else in life. There’s something to be said here about surveillance and how we respond to seeing ourselves on a screen, but at the end I had concluded something else entirely. Georges goes to the police early on, but gains nothing as they refuse to step in as the situation is "harmless." Nonetheless, Georges continues his hot pursuit and turns his family every which way to figure out why he is being tortured. Cachē is about how the guilt from painful regrets tucked in the back of our brains manifest in unpredictable ways until we have no choice but to confront the issue again. As great as the movie is on the technical level, how much am I emotionally impacted? On my immediate impression, a good bit but not to an extent that I see myself thinking about the film a lot in the future. Hopefully I’m proven wrong.

Name: The Incredibles
Review:
TALK ABOUT FULL BODY CHILLS IN A MOVIE THEATER


Nostalgia and everything, yes, but also this is an insanely well written movie with great animation even if some of it has aged. The structure is crazy and keeps going STRONG for almost 2 straight hours, and it's packed full of iconic characters, moments, music, comedy, heartfelt shit, etc. Aside from maybe a couple tiny emotional beats that don't fully hit, I can't critique anything here. This is a masterful powerhouse and I'm just SO EXCITED I wanna get all my thoughts out!
Tags: amc village crossing 18, w/ claire s, w/ jadyn, w/ izzy, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Bottoms
Review: Bottoms is a blast; the kind of pep rally adventure I wish I had in high school. It's an erratic ode to dark teenage movies (with a number of Heathers and Spring Breakers vibes) as well as a wonderfully queer entry to Emma Seligman's and Rachel Sennott's future legendary bodies of work. Talented comedic performances (Edebiri MVP but everyone is great), killer editing (those montages!), snappy writing, and adrenaline-fueled direction launch this up to the ranks of the movies that Seligman clearly grew up loving.
Tags: alamo drafthouse wrigleyville, w/ jadyn, w/ claire s

Name: Perfect Blue
Review: This happened to me when I was 12 and deleted my YouTube channel to focus more on vlogging. (none of which exist anymore for reasons similar to this movie) Perfect Blue is sleek and brutal and captivating; also emotionally confusing and intellectually challenging. It's a great movie that left me breathless, and I know for sure it has amazing animation, sound design, voice acting, and visual motifs. If nothing else, I adore a good movie puzzle, and this was exactly that! But a rewatch really is in order for me to piece everything together.
Tags: music box theater, me time!, spooky season 2023

Name: Pink Flamingos
Review:
I couldn’t have asked for a more memorable first watch of this than in a fully packed theater.


John Waters’ seminal (literally) movie contains what has to be some of the most unique and influential verité filmmaking in history to create one of the most difficult to watch, yet funniest movies I think I’ve ever seen. It’s so difficult that I can’t consciously give it a higher rating than this for now, but it’s so inspiring in how much it really shocked me in both the standard and unconventional ways, especially for a 1972 movie. The story is a straightforward slice of life, but tested every limit of representation, content, and morality it could have just by portraying an extreme real-life example of ‘be gay do crime.’ I loved it, everyone should see this.
Tags: gene siskel film center, 35mm, w/ ross

Name: Spring Breakers
Review: It didn’t work quite as well for me tonight as it did in high school since I knew what to expect, but this remains a really great movie. A ridiculous satire of MTV and tabloids (Harmony Korine’s America?) with genuinely great filmmaking and dark, liminal undertones to the atmosphere that garner startling physical and emotional reactions. Hearing Scary Monsters and Nice Sprites blaring in the theater was life-affirming, especially 10 after midnight when I knew enduring a long day was worth it for that experience.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, w/ jadyn, w/ izzy, w/ claire s, w/ carmen

Name: Cocaine Cougar
Review: The abyss of the cinematic medium (what else would I expect from a movie called Cocaine Cougar?). It’s not even ironically funny, it’s just fucking boring and worthless. The best parts were the Tubi ad breaks.
Tags: tubi, baby's first apartment, spooky season 2023

Name: Dumb Money
Review:
God forbid men have hobbies.


A solid time! I’m not a finance guy at all and was only into stocks a bit for a class in high school, but I understood all the terminology and was able to follow along just fine. Craig Gillespie’s style is never really favorite my thing, and this did have some unnecessary montages and overbearing music to it, but he does know how to direct a competent crowd-pleaser and especially good performances. Dano obviously shines here, but Davidson was surprisingly good and no one on the ensemble was bad. Don’t expect a major classic, but it’s a funny movie and does what it needs to, semi-often rising above the studio biopic mold. I may bump the rating down as I sit on it.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ claire s, w/ dylan, early screener

Name: The Cutting Edge: The Magic of Movie Editing
Review: It's literally just a compilation of filmmakers and editors talking about their work for an hour and a half, but as an aspiring filmmaker and editor I enjoyed it and learned quite a bit.
Tags: youtube, for class, baby's first apartment

Name: Legally Blonde
Review:
UPS guy is so me (I went as him tonight!)


Also this is genuinely such a funny and well-crafted comedy and the theater experience was a blast as expected. Amazing comedic cutting and line delivery all the way through, complete with everyone laughing and booing in unison at all the right moments.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, drag show, w/ izzy, w/ claire s, w/ jadyn

Name: A Haunting in Venice
Review: Not as stupid and bad as Death on the Nile, but still pretty mediocre. It’s just charming enough with some creative choices that make it not a waste of time, but nothing about the movie was exceptional either - I forgot all the characters’ names and motivations halfway through. Moreover, this is not a scary film at all, with a handful of moments that just had me laughing unintentionally. My favorite was when Michelle Yeoh was in her chair, flapping her arms and screaming to emulate a possession. I didn’t see her as a character at all, so I just saw Michelle Yeoh being goofy. (Not her fault given how talented she is in everything else, I’m guessing this was Branagh direction issue)
Tags: cinemark seven bridges, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: New Jack City
Review: No cameras, no cell phones, just people living in the moment.

Name: M
Review:
One of those classic tales of Brendan Fraser lookalike vs Orson Welles lookalike.


There’s so much tact to the mise-en-scene and cutting, multiple great monologues with multiple great performances, and a brilliant directorial eye for Chekhov’s gun and details big & small. Awesome ending too. It felt longer than the runtime and the characters aren’t the most interesting ever, but dammit if the filmmaking isn’t brilliant. There are an endless amount of newer movies that feel inspired by this and I only see it growing on me from here.

Yesterday for my editing class, I watched an interview that talked about how editors watch eyes for the best visual cues from actors, and I can’t think of a better example of that than Peter Lorre’s giant baby blues here.
Tags: criterion channel, for class, baby's first apartment

Name: Duel
Review: American cinema has been infatuated with the chase scene from the very beginning and clearly Sam Fabelman has too, given that his debut movie tells the tale of an all-time worst morning commute. Duel presents itself from the opening radio monologue as a loose commentary on American masculinity but, while the dialogue is serviceable, don't be mistaken. This is a movie purely memorable for visual storytelling through and through. I also wish the screenplay had a better feel for when to place the highest energy moments, but I was still enamored the whole time.

Name: The Rocky Horror Picture Show
Review: My dad told me the only way to watch this movie is in a crowded theater so I did just that for my first time, but I definitely needed to have seen and understood the movie first to be even close to the same wavelength as everyone else yelling over every line of dialogue. I still had fun despite feeling lost half the time.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, w/ izzy, w/ claire s, w/ shelby, w/ jadyn, spooky season 2023

Name: Waiting for the Light to Change
Review: Just as good on rewatch. It captures the hypnotizing essence of the best slow cinema in that the performances and technical filmmaking feel incredibly real and there’s just the right amount of space in between moments to let you think about your own life reflects what you're watching. The screenplay asks and gives personal answers on questions friends are often too afraid to ask each other, and it really hits me at this point in my life. So many shots look like Monet paintings. I love it so much.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ cam, q&a!

Name: Johnny Guitar
Review: I forgot until a couple months ago that I love a good western, and that should’ve been enough to convince me that I’d love this movie. Being surprised was just as fun though! A super well directed and thoroughly engaging movie with everything from satisfying gunslinger action to thoughtful narrative conflict! Not to mention kickass female leads in 1954! There’s so much great dialogue, not a single bad performance with a particularly stellar Crawford, and wonderful use of geography in both how locations are used for the story and how they’re blocked in the cinematography (the high angles!). It sometimes feels a little long and not every plot point is super memorable, but I had a genuinely great time here. A hot take, maybe, but I think this is on the same level as Rebel Without a Cause.
Tags: panopto (school), for class, baby's first apartment

Name: The Creator
Review: The Creator is an original studio sci-fi movie in 2023 so the fact that it exists is enough of a reason to see it. I really wanted to love this movie but, unfortunately, I would say go in with alleviated expectations because it’s not a new classic or anything. I liked the movie more than not thanks to a great Hans Zimmer score, super cool and really impressive VFX and production designs, great visual worldbuilding and an exciting finale, all keeping me interested when certain narrative aspects failed. The cinematography utilizes a handheld style in a lot of places where it feels out of place, and this undermines the beautiful visual landscape this movie is more often than it should, but I’ll still take this color grading and these amazing set pieces with fleshed out textures over whatever the fuck Warner Bros. was giving us in The Flash. In the same movie, however, the road to get to that finale is predictable and never really captivates you, none of the characters or performances are particularly great (although the main kid exceeded expectations), the dialogue is all just forgettable, and the Vietnam metaphor for AI is quite odd and I was never fully on this movie’s emotional wavelength; that’s where it really suffers. This is a movie with a story that will age either really well or really terribly with no in between.

Name: I Vitelloni
Review: At long last… my first Fellini! I went in pretty blind, and I’m glad I did! There were a couple moments I found particularly amazing and very moving (Alberto and Monaldo after the carnival + Leopoldo’s convo with the famous actor on the street), and while I didn’t quite fall in love with the movie the same way outside of those, the whole thing is a really well directed and thoroughly engaging affair. I’ve been a sucker for aimless plot movies for a while now, and this is a welcome addition to that canon. Can’t wait to check out more Fellini in the future (particularly La Strada, La Dolce Vita, and 8 1/2)

Name: Rope
Review: Awesome movie. The dialogue, pacing, and mise-en-scene were incredible and reminded me why I love movies. I would also murder a classmate if it meant James Stewart would investigate my apartment during a dinner party.
Tags: internet archive, for class, baby's first apartment

Name: Psycho
Review: I just watched THE Psycho for the first time so don’t expect any grand immediate thoughts. One of those movies I’ve seen clips from and been hearing about since I was 10 so it was crazy to finally see the whole thing. I’m still soaking it all in but I definitely do think this is a great movie. Crazy good directing and mysticism as to be expected from any Hitchcock; the vibes are so correct here and they will stick with me for quite some time. I just didn’t have any other immediate reaction or emotional connection than “wow, this is so cinema!”
Tags: for class, w/ mary, spooky season 2023, at a friend's place

Name: Mulholland Drive
Review: I had two opportunities in the past to see Mulholland Drive in theaters but wasn’t able to for different reasons, and it’s bugged me ever since. As soon as I saw it on another schedule in the Chicago area, I knew I wasn’t going to be asking myself “what if?” any longer. I have no regrets jumping at this chance because it was the amazing experience I was hoping for. As cliche as it is to say, having all your senses overwhelmed in a large auditorium is an unmatched experience, especially with a long time favorite. The first hour and 45 minutes of this were everything I wanted and more; complete captivation, losing myself in the world of Betty and Rita while having a whole new appreciation for Lynch’s excellent direction. I fell in love all over again, it’s everything I want out of a movie. That being said, I still don’t quite think this movie is perfect, though that is subject to change as it did when I was 17. The final 45 minutes were good but left me in a weird headspace, and whether this was confusion or sadness from the ending I’m still not sure. Nonetheless, I can once again look forward to structuring together what I think it all means in all its brilliance. I love this movie, and I think I always will.

Name: Vertigo
Review:
Not my favorite Hitchcock but I enjoyed it more than when I was 16. A striking commentary on how every damn Hitchcock leading lady looks more or less the same.


(Also some profound message about how love isn’t always the answer to your problems and all that good shit)
Tags: for class, w/ jadyn, w/ izzy, w/ claire s, at izzy's place

Name: Near Dark
Review: So bold and bloody and full of personality, like all vampire westerns should be. Not that I’ve seen any others off the top of my head, but this set a high bar.
Tags: music box theater, w/ hazel, w/ jake, w/ benji, w/ gus, 35mm, spooky season 2023

Name: Cléo from 5 to 7
Review: What an awesome little movie! Love the filmmaking, especially Varda's blocking, the production design, the mini silent film, and the token French New Wave jump cuts. I recognized Agnes's cameo out of the corner of my eye and I'm really proud of myself. The blend of being funny and tragic is seriously really impressive here, and I was always thoroughly engaged with what was happening in Cleo's life. Every character is just so iconic in their own right. This was a little light on first impression to rate any higher, but certain moments really took me to my memories and feels in a similar way to something like The 400 Blows and for that reason I think it'll only grow on me from here.
Tags: criterion channel, w/ jadyn, baby's first apartment

Name: Who's That Knocking at My Door
Review: Scorsese’s debut mob flick is quite solid! It definitely doesn’t hold a candle to his best work, but there’s still a lot of Scorsese’s personality + great individual moments here and, even if it feels like an overextended short film at times, it's still strong enough to convince me that this guy deserves a shot at directing more mob flicks (had I seen it upon release, of course)

Name: The Trouble with Harry
Review: Definitely the most offbeat Hitchcock I’ve seen yet, which made for a really interesting watch. Beautiful cinematography and aesthetics in this. The first hour left me feeling pretty distant overall, but the last 40 minutes were great. Even then, I admired this more than I personally enjoyed it, but it's still a good movie. Respect for the dead doesn’t matter to these people until it poses a threat.
Tags: internet archive, w/ jadyn, baby's first apartment

Name: Faces
Review:
A great movie for sure - I’m convinced Cassavetes can direct an engrossing conversation with any actor in any location, this was filled to the brim with that, and I can already tell he’s great at making emotionally challenging stuff. But I don’t want to rate this yet until I’ve had time to decompress since this was a LOT for me right now.


Unpopular opinion, MVP was Lynn Carlin. But there are a lot of great performances here.

Name: Bride of Chucky
Review: I’ve gone into detail with my Chucky experiences in the past so I don’t need to expand here, but finally seeing the silly little guy who gave me nightmares as a kid in a THEATER (on 35mm no less) was incredible. All drag show movies at Music Box are 10/10 experiences idgaf.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, drag show, w/ paloma, w/ megan, w/ phia, spooky season 2023

Name: The Taste of Things
Review: The Taste of Things is a really delicate and assured story about the forever blurred line between artistic and romantic passion. Magimel and Binoche are excellent, the directing and visual look are gorgeous, and the pace moves as smoothly as the slicing of veal. It feels its runtime at moments and I was occasionally spacing out during the second act, but this one really crept up on me emotionally at the end. Tran Anh Hung gave one of my favorite Q&As I’ve attended afterwards, and I had the pleasure of meeting him too!
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2023, q&a!, w/ chris, w/ gus

Name: The Descent
Review:
There's not a whole lot of strong character work in this one, but I like the concept and it thrives with its Raimi inspiration - relentless violence and a sense of erratic survival. Solid performances, some creative camerawork, and genuinely great practical effects make this a good, nearly great horror movie. And despite its imperfections, you can tell by watching that Neil Marshall has a huge passion for the horror genre and that he really put effort into making this.


It's one of the more odd watches I've had for a film class (you don't see much contemporary horror in school), but I liked it so I don't mind. It was pretty depressing though, so it's time to turn on a sitcom for a bit.
Tags: amazon prime (rent), for class, baby's first apartment, spooky season 2023

Name: The Boy and the Heron
Review:
As visceral and dreamlike as every Miyazaki movie is visually, now with added dreamlike confusion to the storyline which... was something. I have a few theories on what it means, but I’m still feeling pretty lost. All my friends and parents were lost too. I still enjoyed it though.


Update, 10/16 -- initially it played like a convoluted Miyazaki highlights reel (not a negative) but after a day to sit on it, I've come to see it as a more haunting experiment about fulfilling family roles and the horror of growing up. Can't wait to see it again.
Tags: music box theater, ciff 2023, w/ mary, w/ cam, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ dylan, w/ ashar, w/ alek

Name: Only the River Flows
Review: Some really great filmmaking and vibes on display here that makes it all the more frustrating that I was tired and couldn't focus more on the story, though to be fair I did think it was lacking regardless. I’m being a bit generous with the rating but I'm willing to watch it again down the line for sure.
Tags: gene siskel film center, ciff 2023, w/ cam, w/ gus

Name: The Killer
Review: The Killer is incredibly slick and a total rush. After a bunch of heavy dramas, this was the perfect burst of energy I needed. It’s not a movie that’ll win any awards for story or screenplay, but with David Fincher’s expectedly brilliant filmmaking and sense of humor, kickass action sequences and hard-nosed pacing, it’s a brutally entertaining and interesting story that kept me on the edge of my seat the entire time, as any great crime thriller should. This one takes after everything from Godard to the Coens to Oldboy. While not really new territory for Fincher, it’s quickly become what I think is one of my favorites of his just for giving me everything I was expecting and not having any extra bullshit. I really loved it for that and had an blast.
Tags: music box theater, ciff 2023, w/ cam, w/ peter, w/ ross

Name: La Pointe Courte
Review: I will always love French New Wave vibes. I will not always love French New Wave stories but this was a solid one. Agnes Varda has the most random grossout moments in her movies and I think it’s really funny.
Tags: hbo max (courtesy of friends), w/ jadyn, w/ carmen, at jadyn/claire's place

Name: The Zone of Interest
Review: I went into The Zone of Interest very blind, having seen no trailers or images prior and only having read the IMDb synopsis. I have also never seen a Jonathan Glazer movie prior, bar a couple clips of Under the Skin that gave me the vaguest idea of what this might be. I was mostly wrong. This is an amazing movie. I don’t feel the need to rate it higher on first impression just because of the movie’s unassuming nature, but the technical craft is gorgeous and the narrative craft is numbing. Once I finally put two and two together on the construction of the story, my brain went “Oh…” and I just sat processing everything I had seen prior. The silence and grace here are devastating.
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2023, w/ dad, w/ quinn, w/ cam, q&a!

Name: All of Us Strangers
Review:
CIFF staff,

Thank you so much for letting my friends and I sit in the theater for 15 minutes after the movie ended to gather our emotions.


A perfect ensemble, an incredible screenplay, beautiful and subversive direction + visual style, and multiple emotional in-points and memories I was reminded of that just made me sob. I don’t want to get into any more detail this soon, but this may be my movie of the year.
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2023, cried or teared up, w/ erin, w/ simone, w/ hope, w/ claire m, film fatales

Name: Saltburn
Review: Saltburn, as the title suggests, is a bold and liquid movie. Emerald Fennell’s direction is awesome with great performance and a visual style that is probably one of my favorites of the year. The vibes are great and there are some really cool ideas to back them up, though it did leave me feeling emotionally distant at times and some reveals weren’t as shocking as I think Emerald intended because of occasionally dodgy pacing. Nonetheless, I really enjoyed getting lost in this crazy gothic world, so I’ll give it the benefit of the doubt for now and bump the rating up to an 8. It’s a unique and uncompromised vision that deserves support.
Tags: college theater, ciff 2023, q&a!, w/ erin, w/ simone, w/ claire m

Name: Evil Does Not Exist
Review: The movie equivalent of your family forcing you to go on a hike when you’re a kid, but the hike ends up being really pleasant and meaningful. It’s minor Hamaguchi and no Drive My Car, but still a sweet and cozy little movie that’s hard to dislike. There are multiple really funny scenes and a surprisingly powerful ending here.
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2023, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ hazel, w/ cam, w/ jihan

Name: Killers of the Flower Moon
Review: I’m giddy. I can’t believe this movie actually lived up to the hype. Such a rich, powerful, epic monument to both filmmaking and decolonizing how American history is portrayed in cinema, all handled with the precision and genuine care Scorsese is known for. I have a few nitpicks but I absolutely loved it and would gladly see it again, provided I have the time.
Tags: amc newcity 14, w/ quinn, w/ cam, w/ hazel

Name: Poor Things
Review: Glorious and exhausting and fucking awesome, Poor Things is an intensely maudlin and graphic retelling of Frankenstein that only Lanthimos could pull off. It’s SO funny, the expressionistic visual style and worldbuilding are genuinely incredible, the performances are insane, the screenplay is towering in the best way (even if a bit too long), and there’s so much more to say about the themes and character dynamics that I’ll save for another watch when I’m not about to sleep. But I LOVED this! Talk about ending the festival with a bang.
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2023, w/ megan, q&a!

Name: Apocalypse Now
Review:
I needed something to ease my mind before starting a new week so obviously I went for this first.

(1979 theatrical cut)
Tags: blu-ray, for class, baby's first apartment

Name: Persona
Review: 'Existential lesbians exploring the art of film' is one of the best genres we have. An artist's only audience should be themselves, so why do we throw ourselves at film, or any kind of art, or any person, with a preconceived idea of a reaction we'll have? "I always thought great artists felt this great compassion for other people, that they created art out of great compassion and a need to help. That was silly of me." Anyways, this movie is great but after everything I've seen in the past week, I am fucking itching for something fun.
Tags: criterion blu-ray, for class, baby's first apartment

Name: The Brood
Review: Cronenberg is one of the most hit-or-miss directors for me, but thankfully this one ended up hitting. The first two acts had some interesting ideas at times and some cool imagery with nothing to really write home about, but the final act really turned things around for me. Not just because it’s way more upbeat and graphic, but it’s one of those final acts that ties things up well and makes you like the rest of the movie more. A really bizarre story about work-life balance and loyal relationships.
Tags: hbo max (courtesy of friends), w/ benji, spooky season 2023, at benji's place

Name: The Hills Have Eyes
Review:
Western Craven


Wild and pulpy fun. The picture-perfect TV family meets some really goofy killers. It's not that scary or amazing, but there are actual stakes and it's never boring. Some great practical effects and hilarious overacting too, it's all part of the charm.
Tags: hoopla, baby's first apartment, spooky season 2023

Name: Anatomy of a Fall
Review: Anatomy of a Fall is really great. The screenwriting, performances, and editing are brilliant and dry, some of the best of the year in each respective category. Some of the cinematography choices were odd, but it’s a well shot movie overall. It all gives the feeling of an uncompromised vision. It’s unsurprisingly an emotionally difficult watch at times thanks to its content (never bad enough for me to leave the theater but it did give some anxiety), but it stops at nothing to be a hyperreal, in some ways uncinematic, portrayal of a court procedural. All around, it’s not a hugely personal movie to me, but definitely a towering experience that I’ll be thinking about for a while. (very high 8, could go up to a 9)

Name: Citizen Ruth
Review: Citizen Ruth is a really enjoyable comedy, boldly poking fun at political extremists and only advocating for Ruth's individuality as a human (so it’s pro choice lol). It's a bit overlong, I picked up on what was going on less than an hour in, but it's mostly made up for by how ridiculous the scale of the movie gets, and overall I found it a really confident and well done work. This was my first Alexander Payne movie and I think I'm going to be a big fan.

Name: Election
Review: I really loved Election. It’s funny and thought-provoking in its commentary on destiny, has amazing character writing, and is really inspiring as an unassuming comedy. I love how unhinged movies about sexual liberation were in 1999, everyone really wanted to get shit off their chest before the robopocalypse. If I saw this in high school, I would’ve related a little too much to Tammy.
Tags: dvd rental, library, baby's first apartment

Name: Toy Story
Review:
The last time I watched this, I was 15 with pneumonia. I didn't intend to make this my sick movie again, I've had a cold for a week and a half + pink eye the last two days, but damn it if this doesn't always makes me feel better.

I think this deserves a higher rating but watching it reminded me again that I'm not a kid anymore and I'm sad now :/ still a really great movie though. The liminal world of these toys, almost a suburban nightmare, is so well done, and the screenplay is really tight. I forgot most of the adult jokes here too, they all made me laugh.
Tags: baby's first apartment, watched while sick

Name: Boyz n the Hood
Review: Still an amazingly well made movie and a really powerful emotional experience. Perfect editing and wonderfully realized characters.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ hazel, for class, black harvest film festival

Name: Catch Me If You Can
Review: It’s a bit too long and ends up feeling a little like pro-FBI propaganda, but dammit if I didn’t love the ride. I love the way Spielberg + Kaminski capture the 60s, the casting is so on point, and Spielberg’s exciting directorial sensibility is so apparent here.
Tags: netflix, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Gladiator
Review: I enjoyed Gladiator more than I expected. Yes it's too long, but the spectacle and action are really big, well directed, and brutally enjoyable. The central conflict of Maximus vs Commodus, when it's the main focus and doesn't try to bite off more thematically than it can chew, does just enough to keep you invested in the story without losing sight of what it really is - a bloody, entertaining action-adventure movie.
Tags: paramount+, for class, baby's first apartment

Name: The Hunger Games
Review:
According to this movie, it doesn't matter if capitalism wins as long as you find a lover through the system!

Not as good as CaptainSparklez' Minecraft Hunger Games series, but a fun enough nostalgia watch. Production design is great, Katniss is iconic, the handheld camera and editing are hilarious. Could go up to a 7.

Name: The Hunger Games: The Ballad of Songbirds & Snakes
Review: There are 20 minutes of Hunger Games in this 150 minute Hunger Games movie, and I didn't despise the rest but it doesn't warrant the runtime and is just really boring. Tom Blyth had blonde hair and got a buzz cut in the third act, I was waiting for him to sing Real Slim Shady.
Tags: regal city north, early screener, w/ carmen, w/ jadyn, w/ claire s, imax

Name: The War of the Worlds
Review: One of the longest 85 minute movies I've seen. Maybe a 6/10 since the effects were cool and some scenes were well directed, but by the time it was over I was tired. Didn't strike me so much as a commentary on war as much as a basic story of human survival. Hopefully Spielberg's is better.

Name: War of the Worlds
Review: I really wasn't sure what to expect from Spielberg's War of the Worlds before I watched it, but I was definitely surprised. It's an anxiety-inducing ride complete with great sound design, solid VFX, and some good performances (why was Dakota Fanning the best actor on the ensemble). Still deciding how I feel about the War on Terror commentary, which is why I watched this. The ending is annoying and left a weird amount of loose ends open, but overall it's an engaging experience that, until the finale, reminded me of why I found Spielberg's movies so engrossing when I was younger. Could go up to an 8.
Tags: netflix, baby's first apartment, for class

Name: The Hurt Locker
Review: I've had The Hurt Locker on my watchlist for roughly 3-4 years now, so finally sitting down and watching it was definitely satisfying. As for the movie itself, I finished it a couple hours ago and I'm still not fully sure what to think. It's certainly a technical achievement, especially in the sound design, editing, and practical effects, which are even more effective thanks to the handheld cinematography. Combine that with hard-nosed direction, great performances, and a breakneck pace, it all makes for a truly intense and engrossing experience. The substance is a lot harder to talk about though. It's obviously a depressing movie, and it should be that way given it's about Iraq in 2004. The movie is neutral in its portrayal of USA's Iraq occupation which I don't personally agree with but admire from a filmic standpoint, and it fits with the fly-on-the-wall storytelling. All the main characters are very watchable but not THAT fleshed out or easy to care about, which I believe was writer Mark Boal's goal. But this does mean there are certain scenes that just feel shallow to me, not helped any by some cheesy / unnecessary dialogue. Overall, it feels like a slice of War on Terror life, but I admire the approach as it grounds the events more than a movie with a more basic Hollywood approach would even if I don't love every part of it. This movie sticking pretty hard to me and I really do appreciate how it's still making me question both its content and my own opinions, and in the end I can call it great.
Tags: dvd, baby's first apartment, for class

Name: The Avengers
Review: This Avengers rewatch hit the spot. I have very particular feelings about the MCU at this point as I'm sure many of you know, but I haven't seen this specific movie in a few years. I have bias for it as my parents let me see a PG-13 movie in theaters for the first time here and I just really loved it as a kid, and finally seeing it again took me back to that love at times. I also think this is just a really good action movie. It blends practical effects and CGI very well, has good editing and great sound design, has a decent amount of visual storytelling, and stays engaging for most of its 140 minute runtime, all things I wish I could say about more Marvel movies lately but don't. It's not perfect, there's a lot of bad dialogue, some bad edits, and nothing that really blew me away, but I needed something fun to break from all the more depressing movies I've watched for this history of cinema final and this was just that.

Name: Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace
Review: The first time I logged this in January 2020 was my honest reaction. My April 2020 rating was the result of me being too exposed to film YouTube that hated it (mainly YMS). 3.5 years later, I'm back to an honest reaction, and man I don’t know how to feel anymore. This is such a weird movie. At this point, to me, it just feels like George Lucas was fucking around with random ideas he had on the backburner from the 70s, mixed with some political ideology that doesn’t really speak volumes since, to be frank, this movie is pretty boring. Jar Jar in particular felt more annoying to me tonight than ever before. I love the high-energy sequences, both for their craft and nostalgia, but outside of those I don’t have much of a connection or a care for the movie. John Williams’ score is always good at least, but here it really doesn’t compare to what’s coming in the next two. I will say: I know it’s not just me being jaded on the franchise. I’ve watched parts of Clones and Sith the last couple days as I’ve been working on a final essay (which does have a paragraph about Sith in it) and have really enjoyed that, I think Phantom Menace just speaks to me less right now.
Tags: dvd, w/ hazel, baby's first apartment

Name: Marie Antoinette
Review: I think this would've worked a lot better for me if Kirsten Dunst wasn't in the title role. I love Dunst as an actress, but I didn't realize until after the movie that Marie Antoinette became dauphine when she was only 14... and Dunst was 22 (and didn't look 14) when the movie was filmed. So maybe that's on me for not knowing enough of the real history, but I read the movie wrong for that reason. Gotta rewatch at some point.
Tags: dvd rental, library, baby's first apartment

Name: Priscilla
Review:
There’s a shot in this where Priscilla and Elvis kiss in the foreground and fireworks go off in the background. In pretty much any other movie, this setting would be a moment of perfection in a relationship. But here, the kiss is brief and the fireworks fizzle out within seconds. This shot is delightful but imperfect and fleeting, and works as a wonderfully subtle thesis for the entire movie.

Yeah, I loved this.

Name: Dream Scenario
Review: Life is less what actually happens to you and more how you react to it. Whether or not that was a message Kristoffer Borgli intentionally wrote into Dream Scenario, it’s what I kept thinking about. This is a challenging, Kaufman-esque feel-bad comedy with so many possible interpretations, and I wouldn’t have it any other way. I love being put against myself to decide whether or not to laugh at certain things, and this was chock full of that. Though I’m still figuring out what exactly I took away from it, I couldn’t help but be captivated by it because of how overwhelming and damn confident it is. I’d say we need more movies that take bold swings like this, but then we’d risk them feeling less special.

Name: Saving Private Ryan
Review: I farted loudly during the emotional final scene and still feel bad about that, but this was a really intense and memorable experience in theaters nonetheless. And of course I thought the movie itself was really good. Spielberg’s direction here is unbelievable and made it worthwhile even if it feels the runtime, I especially loved the more intimate moments in between all the stress and amazing battle scenes.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Ran
Review: Fucking amazing. Seamless blend of story structure and visual storytelling with incredible performances. Akira Kurosawa make an ugly-looking or bad movie challenge (IMPOSSIBLE)

Name: Sideways
Review: Seriously amazing dramedy, Alexander Payne understands people so well. Miles is very Josh-coded in a lot of ways: we both have art-related hyperfixations, we’re both anxious about the future, and we just wanna enjoy our downtime with friends. Hopefully I get to drive around my asshat friend for a weeklong road trip when I’m older.

Name: The Holdovers
Review: My parents and sibling are seeing The Marvels right now. I love them, but when they asked if I wanted to see that with them you can imagine what my response was. Loved this! It’s so cozy and likeable, I had a smile and pretty easily cared about all the main characters the entire time; that’s just what Payne is so good at. And how about the vibes?? That’s all for now, I’m gonna sit with it for the rest of the drive home.

Name: Prince of Broadway
Review: I admired this more than I personally enjoyed it, but it’s a really entertaining and well made movie. A really interesting watch as an early Sean Baker work not only because he’s one of my biggest creative influences, but also because this is a story about parenthood 9 years before The Florida Project with undertones of Bush-era United States. I wonder where Aiden Noesi is today. Neorealist cinema forever.

Name: Godzilla Minus One
Review: This movie was so fun! My friend told me before this that the director calls Star Wars a lifelong influence, and I totally saw its DNA here. I’d be lying if I said I always cared about the character drama, but this really satisfied my need for some great monster action and I was certainly never disappointed or bored.

Name: Tangerine
Review: YEARLY REWATCH! I’ve changed a lot in the last 12 months but one thing that hasn’t changed is I still love this movie!

Name: Poison
Review: Fucking great. I love me a bold, maudlin, artful piece about how all humans are inherently selfish and there’s no hope in the world (better yet, this one was oddly comforting). I can’t wait to see more from Todd Haynes.

Name: Carol
Review: I was hoping this wouldn't be my take, though I gotta be honest. This was a really well made and acted movie, but I felt like I was on the outside looking in almost the entire time and never really got a major emotional in-point from what I was watching. Hopefully it grows on me, but as of now I honestly prefer Poison.

Name: Some Like It Hot

Name: The Abyss
Review: Amazing experience and great movie, I don’t think I actually saw this when I was 12 because I definitely would’ve remembered that ending. My second favorite James Cameron joint I think and maybe a 9/10.

Name: May December
Review: I'm more than a little stressed after this. Some of the best and bravest performances I've seen of the year, along with beautiful direction and a really great screenplay. Todd Haynes has a gift for intimacy both through the camera and between his actors that is matched by few other filmmakers. I have some mixed feelings on the ending but that's my only minor criticism here, if I can even call it a criticism - it just didn't move me quite as much as everything before it did. Really great movie overall.

Name: Die Hard
Review:
It beats dying flaccid!


(translation -- visual storytelling for the win. I've seen this movie a number of times but still forgot a lot of the smaller details. Part of me always wishes I was a part of the movie's big fanbase, but it was still fun to re-acquaint myself, in theaters too at that rate!)
Tags: amc naperville 16, dolby, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ cam

Name: Killers of the Flower Moon
Review:
A rewatch hit very different since the “OMG new movie” mindset wasn’t set in. I get why Paramount and Apple advertised the movie the way they did, to draw in as much mainstream interest as possible to recoup the $200 mil budget, but regardless I think it went against the point of the movie. Not that I ever thought this was a ‘fun’ movie or anything like that, but I regret starting my October review with “I’m giddy.”

On that note, this rewatch was completely necessary. I felt a much stronger and more upset emotionality during the experience and I still was able to admire the craft, scale, and commitment to this kind of portrayal (can’t think of the right word off the top of my head) of a dark history that the world doesn’t know enough about. As someone who went through American public schooling and was severely undertaught about the history of native Americans, this movie hits really hard. And how about that finale? Really amazing movie.
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ dad, w/ mom, cried or teared up

Name: The Night of the Hunter
Review: Directing one movie in your life but making it European-feeling + critical of religion in Hollywood during the Hays Code era is so inspirational. I also just loved this in general.

Name: Days of Being Wild
Review: This did surprisingly little for me. I never really found a reason to get invested in these characters, and it seemed to me that’s what the entire movie hinged on - it barely embraced the medium compared to Chungking and In the Mood for Love. I got SO into Wong Kar-wai a year ago that I might be burnt out, and I’m willing to give this a rewatch at some point, but I was left frustratingly unmoved. Good cinematography and performances though.

Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ quinn, cried or teared up, imax

Name: Tokyo Godfathers
Review: A very different movie from what I remember but I had a great time. Feels a lot like an anime Tangerine in a lot of ways, most notably because of how funny and tragic this is. Completely wacky but it blends this really well with good emotional moments, even though some pass too quickly. Go in with your disbelief suspended. I think it might deserve a higher rating, but I'm still soaking it in. Or maybe I'm just tired from life.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Manhunter
Review:
There are some really great character moments, one in particular that takes place in a cereal aisle, but I wish there was more intimacy overall to keep me more invested.

Every scene where Graham is working as a detective really made me feel like I was on the outside looking in, and I suppose there’s an argument to be made that this is a purposeful contrast on Michael Mann's part to show how people change between their work and personal lives. But it wasn’t as much my cup of tea.

Still a very well made movie overall, as expected. Really great production design, blocking, and performances to keep things visually interesting when the story and screenplay sometimes failed. A high 7.

Name: Chicken Run
Review: This movie made my childhood happy, it’s currently making my adulthood happy. I can’t believe I’m at an age where I can actually make movies now. Nick Park, I hope one day I make a movie half as good as this. Mrs. Tweedy is a bitch and I hope she goes to hell. That is all.

Name: Chicken Run: Dawn of the Nugget
Review: …so I’m never getting excited about anything ever again

Name: Wonka
Review:
Not a particularly good movie in any way and inoffensive to a bit of a fault, but it still kinda won me over at the end. I felt like there was actual life in the VFX, maybe more life than any other aspect. The rest of the movie never commits to something memorable, the performances feel lacking, the story is ridiculously cliche and the attempts at political messages are forgettable, but its got enough kids movie charm that I don’t really hate it as much as I normally would. It’s not a movie I have enough energy or care for to really tear to shreds.

I think part of it honestly is that I had a really good night and I just happened to watch this movie during that timeframe, so we’ll see how I feel in the morning. VERY generous 6/10.
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ aaliyah, w/ kelly

Name: Eyes Wide Shut
Review: Better than Wonka. 35mm looked gorgeous, especially with the Manhattan exteriors. The crowd was a blast to be with. Great movie, much more memorable and striking than when I was 16. Close to a 9.
Tags: music box theater, w/ alex k, 35mm

Name: A Christmas Story
Review: A personal time capsule that reminds me what it was like to be a kid. The scene where Ralph finally beats up Scut Farkus is so liberating.

Name: The Bridge on the River Kwai

Name: Home Alone
Review:
I wanted to do all this when I was a kid and I still kinda wanna do it today. (Referring to both making a movie and also setting up a bunch of traps in my house)


He may have been a mass murderer in 1492 but I guess I can forgive Chris Columbus for directing this movie. Love me some good screwball physical humor, spot-on casting for characters who aren’t stupid, John Williams, and a formula for a good Christmas movie that many have tried (and failed) to rip off.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: In the Mood for Love
Review:
I think unfortunately I’m not as enthralled by Wong Kar-wai as I once was, I’m sad about that. But this is still a great movie, that won’t change. I’m still very fascinated by the screenplay, specifically this time about how love is binding of certain freedoms. And of course Maggie’s performance is excellent.

I sorta talked about this last time, but I really love how Wong and Doyle cover every location here. The way they use focal length is intimate with the characters and details of the production design without being placed directly next to what’s important to each shot.
Tags: criterion channel, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Elf
Review: Haven’t seen this since before I got Letterboxd, it holds up really well. Great comedy. Better than when I was younger tbh, though I got tired of it really fast since it was overplayed in special ed. I only mention that because it’s the memory I associate with the movie, and it’s a lot easier to admire Buddy as an autistic icon in retrospect.
Tags: hulu, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, at home

Review:
I need to sit on this one some more, but WOW I really loved it and it reminded me why I fell in love with Wong Kar-wai’s body of work in the first place. Very layered narrative, visually rich, eccentric without feeling like it’s moving too terribly fast, power in the individual moments… all things that Wong is known for, yet it still feels like such a big movie without really losing sight of its core!

Awesome.

Name: Ferrari

Also this was my 500th movie I’ve seen in theaters!
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Showing Up
Review: Showing Up tells a story about how individuals struggle and pave their unique paths in the United States. Very down to earth, intimate with its characters, and quite likeable. In other words, this is a Kelly Reichardt movie, but it stands out as a new step for her in its cleaner and more vibrant approach while still maintaining a solid story, even if not her best. In particular, there’s a line drawn here between whimsy and seriousness in creating art that I found really interesting. Overall this one’s a bit minor compared to Reichardt’s more memorable and hard-hitting screenplays, but still good stuff in its own right. I'm glad I finally caught up with it.

Review: "I'm very happy when I'm here"

Review: Good last movie of the year. Anyone wanna hide under a bunch of cones and cross the street sometime?

Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: The Conformist
Review: I'd be lying if I said I was always engaged in the characters and their drama, but the directing always kept me interested. Really cool of Bertolucci to make this when he was only 29. The look of the movie is always beautiful and purposeful, the performances from Trintignant and Sanda are both great, and the screenplay, despite not always engaging me, is ultimately really rewarding and got me interested in learning more about this time period. The more universal theme of, well, conforming vs living as yourself is delivered really well. A really great final 30 minutes too. High 7 or low 8.

Name: American Fiction
Review: Enjoyed this! Maybe a bit too much of a crowd pleaser to be really spectacular, it is predictable at times, but it's confident (which feels redundant given that I enjoyed it) and I was entertained and really liked how the story unfolded. The balance between both the satire and the actual messages on how everyone responds to art differently was well done, and I cared about the characters (though I think some of them deserved more complexity). Good performances too.

Name: All of Us Strangers
Review: A movie that uses music, lights, colors, individual lines of dialogue, editing of all different sorts, and performances to create a breathtaking, visceral, ultimately comforting experience. Everything falls into place. Hard to rate and compare to my first watch having known what was coming, I almost gave it a 9, but I again sobbed during the diner scene and my breath was taken by that final shot. It makes me think about everything, but especially my loving parents who I am so lucky to still have and be so close to. This is a VERY cathartic movie for that and all my anxieties that go with it. God… this is a movie. “The power of love…”
Tags: amc river east 21, cried or teared up, w/ hazel, w/ cam

Name: Casablanca
Review: I missed the middle 15 minutes since I had a bloody nose which worried me since obviously my professor wouldn't pause it just for me, but I came back into class and was able to pick up exactly on what was happening. Part of me wonders if the runtime isn’t completely justified because of that, and the ending is predictable, sure. But the movie was a lot more entertaining and easy to appreciate this time around, and the story is told so well and engagingly that I was able to just pick up where I left off again, which justified the runtime in its own way. Great movie.

Name: The Spirit of the Beehive
Review:
A really good movie, very poetic in that there's equal power in the individual moments and the entire story as you think about it more. There are some really awesome scenes of the kids being kids in very thoughtful and surreal ways complete with beautiful cinematography, and I like how the Francoist-era metaphors were subtly integrated. The movie didn't hold back with either of these, functioning well as both cinema of history and personality.

I found myself caring a lot more about the kids than the adults and it felt like it went on for a little too long at some points, but I definitely enjoyed this. I need to let it sit before a rating.

(Criterion Challenge ‘24, week 1/52)
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2024, baby's first apartment

Name: Star Wars: Episode II – Attack of the Clones
Review: I was catching up with a friend during the movie so I was only half-watching but I don’t know why I hated on this so much when I was 16. Like yeah the romance is laughable but the physical production design and cinematography and battle scenes (and there's a lot of them!) are all literally good, idc. Maybe better than Phantom Menace but I’d need to be more focused on rewatch to decide.
Tags: disney+, w/ esther, at esther's place

Name: Jackass: The Movie
Review: Golf Course Airhorn will never not be funny to me. The entire movie never fails to make me smile though.
Tags: paramount+, w/ carmen, w/ claire s, w/ jadyn, at carmen's place

Name: Asteroid City
Review: I enjoyed this more going in knowing what to expect. I really admire Asteroid City for taking the narrative swings it does, for owning the fact that it isn’t fully confident in what it really is and cleverly writing this into each of the two storylines. It’s a really unique movie about the creative process in that sense, though even on rewatch I’m still feeling conflicted about how well this works emotionally. It works beautifully for the first two acts and has a lot of entertaining individual moments and, for whatever reason, I found a lot more joy in watching this community of characters. The final act loses me a bit emotionally despite being consistent with the rest of the movie, though I’m still figuring out why exactly that is. Nonetheless, I walked away more satisfied than I did on first watch and am thinking a lot more coherently about what it means. And it hopefully goes without saying that Wes’s skill and command over every part of his style is unparalleled, as usual. (These are very fresh thoughts so I still need time to sit on the movie again, I just wanted to write it all down while still in mind.)

Name: Soul
Review:
It goes without saying that December 2020 was an incredibly different time in my life compared to now, but this really was better than I remembered. So much care and imagination is in the details and visuals, it’s much funnier than I remember, I love most of the structure, and just having lived more life in the last 3 years I resonated a lot more with what was said about life being worth living.

It peaks around the 3/4 mark for me (the piano montage! <3) and the final 1/4 is a little weaker than everything before it, but it’s not enough to take that much away from the experience. Very worth venturing in 25 degree weather to see this in theaters. High 8.
Tags: showplace icon roosevelt, w/ quinn, cried or teared up

Name: The Zone of Interest
Review: Still have a lot of thoughts swirling in my head. Gonna attempt a full review in the morning. Update - that second sentence didn't happen but I did write a full paragraph that I'm proud of: The Zone of Interest has arguably the most evil protagonist of the year, if you can even describe Rudolf Hoss as a protagonist. Jonathan Glazer forces the audience to sit with the Hoss family for 100 minutes as they live serenely with the Holocaust not only happening around them, but also being perpetuated by them. It's a very bold narrative, and something of a cinematic endurance test. Certainly, Glazer made this to simulate being a bystander towards crimes against humanity, but I don't believe he made the movie to invoke guilt in the audience for not taking action of their own. What makes it so uncomfortable is that it's an experience emulating the guilt we feel for not physically being able to do anything about it.

Name: Origin
Review: Origin, predictably, is built around the central idea of the book it's based on. But as someone who hasn't read that book, it's an important idea I found very thought-provoking and well delivered through the movie, a very personal story about the author's journey. It has a share of questionable choices, but the many powerful moments outweigh the negatives. The ensemble is especially good, and I really liked Ava’s blocking here.
Tags: amc newcity 14, early screener, w/ carmen

Name: Star Wars: Episode III – Revenge of the Sith
Review:
It’s always hard to go back to normal life after watching this movie. (the cure today for me was literally switching to the other disc for special features)

I will continue to think the dialogue didn’t need to be so stilted, and after this rewatch I wish there was more buildup to the deaths of Dooku and Grievous (neither feels that fulfilling), but honestly those are my only real gripes. Great camerawork and stunts, interesting visuals on every world, John Williams continues to go ham, and I always love the structure + editing even if the stakes are only really in Anakin’s arc.

This franchise reminds me why I love movies.
Tags: dvd, baby's first apartment, w/ esther

Name: Happy-Go-Lucky
Review: This was awesome!! My first Mike Leigh and I love his approach, and this is a movie where it’s obvious how much care was put in. As with any realist film, it feels so raw, like you’re standing in on-screen - dialogue and performances so natural they could be improv at times. Both Sally Hawkins and Eddie Marsan are amazing. And even outside how well it’s directed, I just really enjoyed living in this world! Some of the best complicated characters I’ve seen in a while (that ending!), and it’s really funny too. I felt the runtime at a few weaker points, but otherwise this is a great movie that really moved me. 9.0 Side note: Interesting double feature today. Revenge of the Sith reminded me why I love movies because of how it provides me with unmatched escapism and imagination. Conversely, Happy-Go-Lucky reminded me of why I love movies with how wonderfully down-to-earth and human it is.
Tags: dvd rental, video vortex, baby's first apartment

Name: The Virgin Suicides
Review: “We knew that they knew everything about us, and that we couldn’t fathom them at all.” Rating is very subject to change.
Tags: hoopla, w/ jadyn, at jadyn's place

Name: Letter Never Sent
Review:
The cinematography and blocking here are stunning and, though these are the standouts, there isn’t much to complain about with the rest of how the movie was directed and is presented either, aside from maybe a couple nitpicks. Unfortunately I never found the characters to be super compelling emotionally and the story didn’t really leave me thinking about anything, though at least I can say I was never bored. I have no doubt Kalatazov is a great director, this just didn’t blow me away or anything. Very good movie still, hopefully this’ll grow on me.

High 7, possibly a low 8
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2024, baby's first apartment

Name: Yi Yi
Review: Pretty quickly realized I was not in the right mindset for this and it became evident when I was fighting falling asleep the entire first half of the movie (not the movie’s fault). I still teared up at the end so I’m sure this’ll hold up another time, but this was a pretty frustrating experience.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ christian

Name: Crimes and Misdemeanors
Review:
As someone who's a huge fan of American indie cinema, there really isn't an excuse for having not seen my first Woody Allen movie until today. But, as you might've guessed from reading this review, I finally sat down and watched one and I don't have to worry about that any longer.


I really liked this! Allen's skill for writing dialogue is everything I was hoping for and there are interesting conversations being had in seemingly every scene, I love the movie's visual style (nerd moment but these color palates + 35mm = gorgeous images) and how Allen blocks his actors, and the way the crime genre is used was surprising and interesting. I really liked the themes of religious guilt especially, this combined with how the movie portrays memory. Great soundtrack too.

The performances didn't always hit for me in terms of line delivery, but I love the way body language is used here - Martin Landau was my favorite out of the ensemble. And I was kinda underwhelmed by the final message of the movie which is basically "it's beautiful how humans continue to persevere" (I already know this lol), but it wasn't quite enough to take away from how entertaining everything before that was.

Name: Superbad
Review: I liked the beginning and ending 30 minutes, but the middle hour feels so long and has major “we get it” vibes with almost every joke. Decent movie overall, though some random high schooler is probably gonna put a hit out on me for this take. Jonah Hill’s character deserved both car hits, but I like how his arc was completed. Maybe a 7 as I sit on it.

Name: The Underdoggs
Review:
For a Snoop Dogg-produced movie starring Snoop Dogg most likely made to plug his youth football league… I didn’t dislike it and maybe even had fun!

Has the most predictable story ever and for some reason has an adult target audience (I think there’s some intentional irony to be fair), but the characters are likeable and there are some funny bits. I liked how much it leans into the fact that middle schoolers actually swear. I didn’t expect or need anything more than that, first movie of 2024 done.

Also funny that they showed this in the IMAX equivalent theater for some reason lol
Tags: showplace icon roosevelt, w/ ross, early screener, whatever the large format here was called

Name: Secrets & Lies
Review:
A movie that leaves me especially grateful of my relationship with my parents. Gotta soak in some more but this was really great and impactful. The performances in particular left me speechless, holy shit.


Update - I really respect how much this captivated me in the moment. Part of me thinks there’s too much exposition before the story starts, but it’s made up for by having amazing performances and really getting to know the family’s dynamics. Great movie.
Tags: dvd rental, video vortex, baby's first apartment, lived in locations

Name: Harakiri
Review: Really really good. First 90 minutes are phenomenal and a nearly perfect emotional experience, last 45 minutes aren’t on the same level and lost me a little but still very well done. I think the #1 on Letterboxd hype hindered my experience a little bit, but I’m glad to have finally seen this. It is great. Criterion challenge week 3/52
Tags: criterion dvd, criterion challenge 2024, baby's first apartment

Name: Maestro
Review: Maestro is better than I was led to believe by some, but I didn’t fall in love either. There are really good performances and art direction and occasionally great cinematography but, in standard awards bait fashion, I wasn’t terribly moved or impressed by the story. It did what it needed to, but I was never blown away by the substance. I would not call it bad overall, the good does outweigh the bad for me! But it doesn’t commit enough to an overall idea or visual style (is it French New Wave or classic Hollywood?) to be a really great or memorable movie for me. High 6.

Name: Parasite
Review: Happened to be watching for class today so this is my yearly rewatch, just a few days after the 4-year anniversary of my first time. <3 It’s almost redundant to say at this point, but there are genuinely new things I picked up on this time, even despite this being my seventh watch. And as time goes by, I only appreciate that first watch more. It really was a pivotal moment for me, on the same level as something like Toy Story or Star Wars when I was a kid. New things I got this time: - I watched this for my film structure class, and I had never picked up before on how the three acts of the movie are actually rather clear. It’s inspiring to be reminded that you can still make a masterpiece even without hiding your story beats. - Cho Yeo-jeong (Mrs. Park) and Lee Jeong-eun (Moon-gwang) are my favorite performances in the movie. They have the most impressive use of body language and line delivery IMO, genuinely so consistent and memorable in their ranges and emotionality. - Smell plays a much bigger role in the story than I had ever thought. I never realized that the symbolism behind smell is what sent Mr. Kim over the edge at the end. - This, no doubt in my mind, has one of the best scores ever composed for film.
Tags: in class, cried or teared up

Name: Perfect Days
Review: The best use of House of the Rising Sun since 2016’s Suicide Squad. (seriously though, this might be the best I’ve heard it used in a movie) A great case for enjoying and holding onto the little things and saving your energy for the people that really deserve it, among other things. Just a lovely movie with a lot of lovely individual moments, and it looks stunning. Any movie that convinces me to enjoy my surroundings and not zone out to music on the walk back is a great one. “Next time is next time. Now is now.”
Tags: amc river east 21, early screener, me time!

Name: The Holdovers
Review: “I prefer to be alone. I’ve always been drawn to the aesthetic.” In all honesty this didn’t hit *quite* as hard as the first time did having gone in knowing what to expect, but I was much more impressed this time by Paul, both the character and the actor. I was moved by him the first time, no doubt, but revisiting this story knowing his character arc was really rewarding to be reminded of all he is (an asshole, an encyclopedia, a reminder to be grateful, etc etc). He’s amazing. And even if the first watch was better, this movie is still so thoroughly enjoyable. Really great blocking, wonderful performances, and a truly impressive screenplay in how reassuring and smart it is despite being predictable at times.

Name: The Teachers' Lounge
Review: Oh this would so happen in Oswego, IL. The best part of this movie is how airtight it is. The already short runtime feels completely apt, and whether this can be most credited to the screenplay, direction, or editing, only the movie’s crew knows, but they created a really hard-nosed drama as a result. The performances here are amazing too. The woman who played Carla did a great job, but the middle schoolers here acted like it was nothing for them. They really exceeded my expectations. As for the screenplay itself, I love this story and how it plays out. Coming from a school district growing up where it was impossible to avoid parents and teachers who were overly passionate about their beliefs and politics, this seems exactly like something that would’ve happened in my hometown. You can tell a lot of effort was put into making an accurate portrayal of how a real school would’ve reacted, I love it, it's frustrating in a very good way. The cinematography, score, and sound design are all also really great and work wonders. For my complaints, I’d say two things. 1. the movie just took a while to really grip me and I didn't get much of a reaction towards the start. 2. I didn't love how the movie ended, both the shot that plays over the end credits and the Rubik’s Cube. I think the Rubik's Cube works as a gotcha moment, but I would've preferred a more still, thoughtful ending (Hitchcock’s Rope comes to mind for a great ambiguous ending to a character thriller)

Name: The Promised Land
Review: “It appears your land ownership is not what you claim, Mr. Bond Schenckel” Good movie! I’m not quite sure what it was going for tonally, it was serious sometimes and campy at others, but I enjoyed the ride a lot. It is a little long at the end, but Mads and the practical effects + stunt teams made it worth it in the end.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ claire s, w/ shelby

Name: On the Waterfront
Review:
Really great. Brando's performance works wonders for Terry and the emotional core of his character, who really struck a chord in me. I think he's my favorite part of this movie. I also loved the obvious care put into making an accurate portrayal of this world, the story structure, supporting performances, and cinematography. Just a very engrossing experience.

There were a few scenes that felt too theatrical bordering on cheesy for my liking, both in dialogue and line delivery, and it took me out. But that's my only real complaint. This feels like a movie I'll revisit every few years and get something new out of, so I'm content leaving the score where it is for now. Though I was tempted to give it a bump.
Tags: blu-ray rental, video vortex, criterion challenge 2024, baby's first apartment

Name: The Woman King
Review: No idea how historically accurate any of this is, but it has good action and Nanisca + Nawi were really well written and performed. The rest of the story was interesting and well made but a little overlong and didn’t grip me like the chemistry between the two leads. I liked this though. Either a 7 or 8.

Name: Signs
Review:
Good shit. It's cheesy and awkward at a few points, but this really worked for me overall. I loved how Shyamalan dramatized media coverage more than anything else, there are some really emotionally effective moments with the family (especially during the buildup to the finale), I loved the visual look* and production design, the sound design was consistently killer, and I was immersed in the suspense + story to a level that everything else in life fell out of my mind while I was watching, the most I’ve really been immersed in a movie in a while which was awesome.


* = I'm not educated enough in film stock to know if the cinematography was close to the raw footage or heavily color corrected but I loved how it looked
Tags: dvd rental, library, baby's first apartment

Name: Lisa Frankenstein
Review: It made me laugh, the story is campy and entertaining, and it has good vibes - I never found anything really bad in it, and I didn’t need anything more to have a good time. Cinema!
Tags: amc newcity 14, early screener, w/ quinn, w/ megan

Name: Clueless
Review: I watched this at a full ass house party so I had some trouble staying on track, lmao. But it was a great movie, funnier than I remember, I dunno why I didn’t vibe with it more the first time.
Tags: w/ phia, youtube, at phia's place

Name: The Assassination of Jesse James by the Coward Robert Ford
Review:
Obsession is a theme that fits so well with the Western genre, but I don’t think I’ve ever seen it take center stage like it does here. If anyone has any recommendations, please let me know!
