Great directorial command here with insanely good cinematography, editing, performances, and production value, with the first two chapters and the final 30-ish minutes being just incredible. The screenplay is very good too but my only issues with the movie lie here - it does feel too long at times and the narration took me out. Great movie overall, I’ll be thinking about it for a while.

Q&A after with Roger Deakins was really cool too.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, w/ chris, q&a!

Name: Paprika
Review:
Because the internet.


Completely worth the theater experience for my first watch for the visuals alone, even if I’m mixed on the story. Almost gave it a 7 because there’s too much plot and I felt like I was on the outside looking in for a while, but eventually I did emotionally grasp onto the lead guy’s story and really like it in retrospect. I also just really admire how big the scope is, given the story. And while it was a lot to process in the moment (maybe adding to my confusion), this movie is visually SO full of life that it genuinely feels unbelievable, good lord.

Name: Whiplash
Review:
Here’s to the fools who dream.


I don't think I'll ever beat that first watch when I was 16, but of course this is still an amazing movie. Tragic and triumphant and human and, do I even need to say, very stressful. It always gets me thinking about both filmmaking and playing instruments again, I mean that as very high praise.
Tags: for class, panopto (school), baby's first apartment

Name: Blast of Silence
Review: A fun ride. The action is all pretty tame and cheesy and the music is a overbearing at times, but it was really well made otherwise and I liked the consistent nihilistic tone - it worked well here and really came full circle. The voiceover narration took me out sometimes, but more often than not it actually really helped to further flesh out the story's events and Frankie’s arc without being too much. I also thought it was funny when the narrator called me racist.
Tags: dvd rental, library, criterion challenge 2024, baby's first apartment

Name: NYAD
Review: When Nyad said she would “follow the yellow brick road to the Taj Mahal” to motivate herself, I didn't think the directors would ACTUALLY show both of those things (in cartoony Spy Kids style CGI no less), but sure!

Name: Dune
Review:
The biggest reaction this got out of me was when David Lynch got eaten by a sandworm, very sad :(


Some really great practical effects and non-green screen visuals though. It's slightly endearing in its own cheesy 80s way, but otherwise this made no noise for me. Some emotional payoffs and reveals were hilarious.
Tags: showplace icon roosevelt, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: CODA
Review: Was expecting this to not hold up on rewatch since it's hard to view outside the Oscars lens and my movie taste has changed drastically since January 2021, but despite that it actually hit fairly similarly. The boyfriend subplot was more half-baked than I remember and it doesn't really have a directorial voice. But it’s won over so much by great performances, good writing and filmmaking, and the family dynamic that just hits the spot for me. Really good movie, right between 3.5 and 4.
Tags: apple tv+, for class, baby's first apartment

Name: All My Friends Are Nineteen
Review:
Great performance from Salem the Cat!


And my friends too, I suppose…

Name: Pulp Fiction
Review: Haven’t seen this since I was 16 and… okay yeah, it holds up for me. It’s so easy to pinpoint this as the ultimate filmbro movie and shit talk it for that reason, I’m very guilty of that myself... but dammit if I don’t lose myself in this world, this story, these characters every time. It’s so entertaining and memorable in the moment, and it actually has things to say about crime, identity, destiny, and foot massages. That more than wins it over for me; great movie. Should also say I didn’t watch this under the best conditions either, right in the midst of a super stressful week lol. Hopefully it doesn’t take me another 4 years to watch it again. “I wanna dance.”

Name: Burn After Reading
Review: Didn’t surprise me *quite* as much as it did the first time (even with forgetting most of the story), but it’s still really funny and surprisingly smart and very well made! Brad Pitt is seriously underrated as a comedic actor, but the whole ensemble is awesome. And I honestly love that this is an easier Coen movie that’s always a good time and a mood booster, but still feels like they put a ton of effort and care into it. “YOU HIT ME!”
Tags: amazon prime, w/ hazel, baby's first apartment

Name: The Babadook
Review:
This really struck some chords with me. It has its flaws, but I love when a horror movie challenges me emotionally and ends up being a satisfying, coherent experience and overall, and this was just that. A really piercing statement on caregiving and acknowledging scars from your past.

Horror movies are hard for me to gauge sometimes because different anxieties hit me at different times and in retrospect something that might not seem so special really hit me in the moment (I’m sure other people have this problem too), so I cherish the watches that do hit while I can,

Name: Dune: Part Two
Review: Was missing a David Lynch cameo, but DAMN I really had a great time with this otherwise. I’m still soaking it in, I’m so overwhelmed by how rich of a visual and aural experience this was. (Not that I was expecting any less) Even as someone who isn’t that invested in the Dune universe, I enjoyed myself a lot and became envious of those who are. I found myself genuinely curious to learn more about it throughout most of the movie, and I mean that as high praise.
Tags: regal city north, w/ claire s, early screener, w/ paloma, imax

Name: The Lighthouse
Review: Not having subtitles proved to be a bigger crutch on the experience than I expected, there really are a lot of scenes that rely on dialogue. Combine that with my being extra tired today, this wasn’t the best theater experience ever. But, if nothing else, every shot looked amazing on the big screen and I really enjoyed that. Also the farts in surround sound were incredible.

Name: The Piano Teacher
Review:
I remembered multiple individual images and a few full scenes, but not the way everything actually plays out. But the partial clarity without a full picture honestly made this watch even more distressing than the first, and ultimately made me appreciate the movie even more.

Every bit of shock value in this movie is earned - it’s not “look at me, I’m so edgy,” it’s a story with fully fleshed out characters and everything shocking makes sense given who they are. Haneke’s incredible writing brings it to life, and the way he directs makes you feel every painful moment right alongside Erika. To quote a lady leaving my theater, “it’s so gross but so good still.”

I’ll be thinking about this movie for a while and still have some things to process so I’ll leave it here for now, but I’m in awe that this movie managed to surprise and move me just as much if not more the second time. A case of “admired more than enjoyed,” but dammit if didn’t do both INCREDIBLY well.

I need a sitcom now.

Name: Little Miss Sunshine
Review: Really good movie all around, but Dwayne (Paul Dano) especially did it for me here. The range of his silence to his voice, caricature to real emotional being, hating then loving his family but still hating everything else, like yes this was me when I was 17 you hit it right on the head!

Name: Kung Fu Panda
Review:
Good shit, it really holds up even as an almost-adult. A really simple hero's journey saved by genuinely good and emotional character writing with interesting visuals, the second act of this movie in particular is so good. High 7, maybe an 8.

How far would you go for a dumpling?

Name: Kung Fu Panda 2
Review: I will always have a good memory of this movie since I had a visceral reaction when I saw it in theaters both times as a kid. I wasn't able to give this my full attention today just because of life and anxiety, but it still hit hard enough to make me shed a couple tears. This is also just a really fun and beautifully animated movie in general.
Tags: peacock, baby's first apartment, cried or teared up

Name: Kung Fu Panda 3
Review:
I wish I spaced these out more, but I was considering going to an advance screener tonight (ended up not going) and wanted to be prepared. At least I finished the trilogy.

This one’s pretty good - the weakest of the three but not by very much. Po goes through the same arc with a different message and the villain isn’t that memorable, but it’s still entertaining and of course the animation is always great.

Also, on rewatch of all three, I’ve realized that everyone else in the Furious Five is weirdly one-dimensional. No one ever really gets any development other than the fact that they’re Shifu’s students and that they service Po.

Name: Anatomy of a Fall
Review: “You must be prepared. But it won’t be the end of your life.” Still amazing, just as good the second time. Moments that especially stuck out to me: - The transition between the initial procedurals and the start of the courtroom drama is so damn clean, specifically in the contrast between the two. - Sandra describes how she doesn’t view Daniel as a handicap and why she was mad at Samuel for taking that view. - Antoine Reinartz is incredible as the prosecutor, he embodies such a frustrating man so well I wanted to yell at him through the theater screen. - Somehow I forgot about the Snoop aspirin scene so... that was a fun surprise to endure again! (and I forgot about how powerful the payoff is for it) And my favorite… - During their convo while driving, Samuel describes his suicidal ideations to Daniel by saying Snoop won’t be around forever, and the last scene of the movie is of Snoop lying in bed next to Sandra. It’s a heartbreakingly perfect final shot.

Name: Portrait of a Lady on Fire
Review: Wasn’t in the right mindset for this slow of a movie but I still definitely think it’s great. Insanely good cinematography and art direction.

Name: My Old Ass
Review: I really dug it! After a weeklong break from movies (for various reasons), an emotionally investing and lovable dramedy with great messages was a great way to ease me back into things. Megan Park has a gift for writing and directing Gen Z teenagers. Made me feel like I can make movies and also made me feel single (a complement to the movie)
Tags: amc newcity 14, early screener, w/ claire s, w/ ross

Name: A Brighter Summer Day
Review: Holy shit… one of those movies where it ends and you want to write something but your brain is just left in a weird, overwhelmed state...
Tags: criterion blu-ray, criterion challenge 2024, baby's first apartment

Name: Badlands
Review:
You live and you learn.

Kit (Martin Sheen) is who inspired the real events, but this is Holly’s story, which I didn’t fully realize the first time. From the very first monologue, it’s clear that she needs nothing more than another human to connect with. Watching her navigate everything with Kit, it’s jarring and a little frustrating to see how nonchalant she is on the surface with the murder and sociopathy. But she fell in love AND found a friend for the first time maybe ever in Kit. And after her dad is killed, there is quite literally no one else in her life except Kit. By default, he made himself Holly’s only friend, lover, and main provider.

The movie presents all this so serenely, as the contrast between Kit’s actions and the location in the Sticks speaks for itself, and this is also probably how Holly perceived everything in the moment. And judging by the tone of her narration, she probably still wasn’t sure how to process everything some time (at least 6 months) later. Then there’s a lot more to be said about Kit… for another rewatch.

A beautifully wrapped but thoroughly sad tale of emptiness, control, and how you never really know what someone is up to. Great movie, high 8 / low 9.
Tags: criterion blu-ray, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Days of Heaven
Review: I’m still not *fully* sold on how the story is told from Linda’s perspective, the whole thing feels a bit inconsequential in hindsight, but I wouldn’t change the approach either given that this really  moved me in the moment. Having gone in knowing what to expect, I admired the life-goes-on themes more - it’s powerful in ways I really didn’t expect. I do prefer the directness and more challenging character writing of Badlands to the chapters of the love triangle in this, but both improved on rewatch today. I love Malick’s work. And holy shit did this look STUNNING on 35mm!
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Office Space
Review: Favorite part was the attention to detail in world building. Made me laugh sometimes too and had pretty well written characters. It's funny that the exact same outcome would've happened if the three main guys didn't exist.
Tags: hbo max, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Before Sunrise
Review:
Delightful, yet again.

A new thing that stood out to me this time was Ethan Hawke’s physical performance. I was so impressed by how he uses his whole body to fall into the character of Jesse, especially with how he uses his hands. It’s fantastic.

The picnic scene ended abruptly and, as a result, I didn't feel much for it compared to the rest of the movie, and the ending probably hit harder in 1995-2003. Like now that we have two sequels, there really isn't any ambiguity, and that sense of questioning felt missing. But those are the only less than great things I have to say here, everything else in this movie is awesome.

Name: Música
Review: The Everything Everywhere-izing of studio movies has arrived. Starts out awkward and quirky in a fun and endearing way, but unfortunately descends to awkward and quirky in annoying and tropey ways and then it just ends abruptly. Overall was just alright, a lot more interesting stylistically than narratively, but I respect the ambition given that Rudy wrote, directed, acted, and composed his own movie.
Tags: alamo drafthouse wrigleyville, early screener, w/ jack l, w/ jadyn, w/ claire s

Name: The Godfather
Review:
Don Corleone was probably the best high school treasurer ever.


This was more of a refresher on the story than an appreciation of new things to pick up on, but I still had a great time of course. I did, however, appreciate the structure even more, especially after the baptism once I put together how everything plays out from the wedding at the very start. And the more objective, non-spectacled portrayal of each scene makes them more shocking. I can’t imagine seeing this for the first time in 1972.
Tags: dvd, w/ jadyn, baby's first apartment

Name: Hugo
Review: I never got to see this in theaters during its initial run since my kid self wanted to see Tintin twice. I don’t have any huge regrets there to be honest, but, having watched the movie later on, I always wondered and eventually felt a little robbed. So this was a special experience for that reason . And WOW… my heart is heavy now. A great movie for so many reasons, as I’ve known since I was a kid. But even more than that is this was the first time in a while that I was really moved by a reminder of the importance and joy of movies in the world. I love movies.
Tags: music box theater, 3d, cried or teared up, w/ cam, w/ quinn, w/ mom, w/ dad

Name: Monkey Man
Review: Forgot to log in the moment so some of my thoughts have left me, but I really enjoyed this! The first two acts are decent but nothing amazing, with some solid action but nothing to really memorable and a couple things that took me out (sound mixing covering dialogue mostly). Halfway through, though, the lore is fully revealed and sweeps the rug out from under you in a way that really worked for me. I don’t know if this next part was intentional or not, but the movie also had a surprising boost in quality after this point, with better action and filmmaking, on par with the first two John Wicks for me. Very interesting and well executed pacing, making the first half intentionally messy and second half more taut. Overall, not a mindblower but a well done and thrilling action movie that convinced me Dev Patel is an actor-turned-director that actually earned his status.

Name: Before Sunset
Review:
Oh my good God

Everything about this worked so well for me, the first thing in a while to feel like it hit me at the exact right time in my life… but the fight in the taxi and the final 5 minutes obliterated me. Holy shit.
Tags: dvd rental, video vortex, cried or teared up, baby's first apartment, criterion challenge 2024

Name: Children of Men
Review:
The way I was clutching myself and struggling to breathe during multiple scenes… what an amazing theater experience.

A couple parts didn’t hit *quite* as hard the first time when I went in blind, but most of it did, and honestly I credit that more to the fact that this damn movie is still lingering in my mind 3.5 years after my first watch.

Aside from a couple screenplay nitpicks (still a great screenplay overall - history is always bound to repeat itself), I can think of very few movies that balance spectacle and realism this well, especially in that this is a sci-fi movie that draws from the present and feels so familiar. And the pacing between scenes is something else - this time in particular I noticed there’s no music during any of the long takes, and it pays the hell off. Alfonso Cuaron really is an unbelievable director, and this movie is just fantastic.
Tags: gene siskel film center, 35mm, me time!

Name: Punch-Drunk Love
Review:
I don’t have anything to criticize with the movie, but I’ve seen it + rewatched clips of it enough that the surprise is mostly gone for now. I need to wait a few years to refresh my brain before I watch it again.

Still, rewatching it tonight, just feeling things alongside the story and delighting in the joy of the film’s visual and aural language was more than enough for me.

And the writing + performance of Barry still makes for one of the best on-screen representations of autism, warts and all. The ways he runs in fear at sudden noises, his internal feelings manifesting physically seemingly at random, the stark contrast between split second decisions and laborious overthinking… It’s always so oddly comforting for me.

I love this movie.
Tags: criterion blu-ray, w/ jadyn, baby's first apartment

Name: Civil War
Review: Boiled my thoughts down to… - Better than Men but no Children of Men - Technically impressive - Central story is mostly solid (Kirsten Dunst goes Sarah Connor mode. Cailee Spaeny! Somehow they made Jesse Plemons terrifying) but falters at the underwhelming end - Works as a feel-bad, frightening movie but didn’t actually do much in takeaways other than giving me a stress headache and just simply reminding me that people suck, warfare is bad, and that war would forever change the dynamic of the United States (which I already knew) - It’s still funny to me that Texas and California formed an alliance.
Tags: cinemark melrose park, me time!, cinemark xd

Name: Just a Home Movie
Review: Can't rate right now because I have bias (friends with Dustin), but this is pretty great stuff. Intentional editing, effective emotional payoff, and a lot of purely interesting scenes. It reminded me of family reunions I've had in the past, maybe moreso now that I haven't been to one since before COVID, and I was genuinely invested in watching his extended family. Moreover, congrats Dustin on your first feature! In a matter of a month you put together a well-made documentary that I really enjoyed: don't lose that grit and dedication, and I can't wait to see what you do next. Everyone else... watch it here!

Name: Redes
Review: More admirable based on its place in history than something I really got a lot out of, but still a mostly good movie nonetheless. It’s like the bridge between Eisenstein and Rossellini. I haven’t seen Forrest Gump yet but I have seen Redes (1936).
Tags: dvd rental, video vortex, criterion challenge 2024, baby's first apartment

Name: Sing Sing
Review: Just a really well directed and written movie with a great ensemble. Not super groundbreaking or anything but it doesn’t need to be - I was consistently moved, engaged, and impressed. One of the best movie reminders I’ve seen recently about the importance of art.
Tags: college theater, w/ jadyn, early screener

Name: Alice in the Cities
Review:
Delightful.

I have a particular affinity for movies that put noticeable care into the locations they feature, especially road movies (it’s the geography nerd in me). From the very first scene this was exactly that, and this intimacy with the world is only furthered by the performances and Wim Wenders’ writing. Every choice made by Philip and Alice is believable and/or endearing (my favorite scene probably being when he tells her a story) and I was constantly wondering “what would I do in this situation,” more than any movie I can think of recently. Not to mention some fantastic cinematography and editing as well, on par with any New Wave movie from this time period and equally inspiring.

It loses some points because I’m feeling a bit mixed on the end and there were a couple times when I lost interest in the story, but even then it was quickly renewed.

This is equal to the other Wim Wenders joints for me, which is to say I really loved it (if that wasn’t already clear). Currently teetering between 4 and 4.5 on the rating.
Tags: blu-ray rental, video vortex, baby's first apartment, criterion challenge 2024

Name: Challengers
Review:
We had to sit three rows from the very front since we forgot the small step of buying tickets beforehand, but!… kudos to the movie for filling up a huge auditorium and also to the audience for being super fun and respectful.

I had a really good time with this! Phenomenal editing throughout, tons of incredibly well directed scenes that made things thrilling even when they didn’t need to be, really good performances, the nonlinear structure added to the excitement and really justified its existence, and the music was killer. It felt overlong and took me out at times when it held for too long on the obvious flaws of the relationships without adding anything, though I do appreciate the movie not taking it that seriously - the whole thing is campy in a way befitting a movie about a fantasy tennis love triangle.

Overall though, I had a really great time getting into this story and will be thinking about both the movie and theater experience for at least the rest of the night, if not longer.
Tags: amc newcity 14, w/ jack l, w/ hazel

Name: Alien
Review: I already love Alien, and seeing it in theaters was awesome. The claustrophobic horror is SO entertaining and achieved so well through Ridley’s direction and blocking, the insane production design in all regards, some of THE best special effects in movie history (including some images that’ll probably visit my dreams tonight), the inescapable and increasing stakes of the story (I fully forgot about Mother as well as the mission change plot twist, that was a fun surprise), and jumpscares that still got me on my third watch. When it gets going in the third act, it gets GOING. I admit I wasn't as engaged with the characters as I remember being in the past, but they're entertaining and keep things light without taking away from the thrill. It’s not perfect, I admit the movie is a little too slow at the start and takes a while to get into things, and some of the sound mixing took me out (could’ve just been my theater)… but I doubt I will ever not have a good time watching this. It’s timeless. On the border of an 8 and 9 (if you're new here, I'm incredibly indecisive with ratings)

Name: Spirited Away
Review: Amazing as usual, though I fear my making this a yearly tradition has slightly taken the sting out of the movie. I might wait two years before my next watch. Nonetheless, still a deeply emotional and breathtaking experience to see this in theaters again. I needed it. Three things I noticed: 1 - Much funnier than I remember. Just the pure fact that this is a movie where no human, animal, or object serves its normal everyday purpose. The three bumbling green heads? The toads smoking cigarettes? The anthropomorphic slices of paper? It’s so funny and I’m surprised I haven’t laughed more at it in the past! 2 - Despite all the visceral and bizarre imagery in the movie, the shots that moved me the most this time were the shots of normalcy in between. The hill in the title sequence, Chihiro and Lin staring out off the balcony after her first full day of work, and the train ride are so wonderful and hit me more than any of the weird shit. 3 - The Name of Life is still one of my favorite movie themes of all time, and I realized that my favorite part of the song is only played at four points during changes in the plot. Something to think about for my next watch. Once again, I love this movie.
Tags: amc newcity 14, me time!, ghibli fest

Name: I Saw the TV Glow
Review: “Isn’t that a show for girls?” This was… kinda incredible. An indescribable and endlessly bizarre ode to the media and memories we never really outgrow, that take years to figure out why we reacted the way we did. Watching this after a week of wondering about my gender more than usual was… interesting. But I loved the movie and the crazy theater experience, and had a couple real visceral reactions watching.
Tags: music box theater, chicago critics festival, w/ hazel, w/ cam, w/ alek, q&a!

Name: Planet of the Apes
Review: The closest I’ve seen to a feature length Twilight Zone episode, and I mean that as a compliment. Played on the sci-fi genre tropes of the time so effectively that I didn’t see the ending coming until it already happened. I love it when a director subtly and meaningfully trolls me for an entire runtime.

Name: Rise of the Planet of the Apes
Review: I’m so sure there was a level of Chicken Run inspiration to this. Has a strong enough emotional core (solid main character writing + digestible themes of animal testing, nature v. nurture, violence spawning violence, etc.) for the campiness of the premise to be enjoyable. It’s also just a well made movie overall, my only real complaints are some poorly aged CGI and a bit of overediting. Lastly, it’s a bit darker than I remember, but as per usual with Hollywood movies I admire the darkness: it makes the payoff more satisfying. Good stuff. Random side note: I always get a weird excitement watching movies from 2011 that aren't kids movies. It was the year I became obsessed with film for the first time despite not being old enough to watch most of what came out, so going back to what I missed out on is really fun, especially since I remember when this was in theaters and my parents said no.

Name: Dawn of the Planet of the Apes
Review: This didn’t actually happen in 2021 but otherwise the rumors are true - this is actually a great movie. I really enjoyed the ride, more than I was expecting. There’s too much going on for me right now to really get into it analytically, but it’s all I’ve heard. Great filmmaking and ape VFX with clear references to some of the best in sci-fi and action, a very strong emotional story with really effective plot twists, and some really badass action scenes. It's relentless yet touching in its presentation and pays the hell off, I was so invested in this. In retrospect, I already have some mixed feelings on the movie feeling too much like a bridge between the first and third in the trilogy, but that’s my only major complaint. I loved it.

Name: Kingdom of the Planet of the Apes
Review: Kingdom has a strong concept on paper, using Caesar as a biblical figure for the Apes to build warring religious groups around. I wish I liked it as much as everyone else seems to, but in the end it didn't live up to its potential for me. Wes Ball is a solid director here, but it doesn't reach Matt Reeves heights to really elevate it to something great in its filmmaking. The CGI is as good as we've come to expect and there is just as much creativity to the ape world, but otherwise the screenplay is another step down from Dawn, mostly just being serviceable and often flat out boring (the second act especially REALLY dragged for me). I will say that I found the ending oddly endearing despite being pretty sudden and unrewarding, but for the most part the movie feels its runtime and the new set of lead characters are like cookie-cutter adventure genre character arcs, missing the emotional core of the other recent entries. It's just... kinda emotionless. What we have is fine, but both in general and after loving Dawn earlier today, I was pretty disappointed. But maybe the comparisons are making me judge this one too harshly, hopefully it'll grow on me.

Name: San Andreas
Review: Not enough Paul Giamatti or hot coffee but, all things considered, better than I thought.
Tags: hbo max, w/ ross, at ross's place

Name: AGGRO DR1FT
Review: Despite reviews claiming that this is where Harmony Korine lost his mind (as if he hadn’t already), this felt like the next step he would be taking in his career. Maybe the interview where he stated he’s more interested in video games is still fresh in my mind, but even despite the obvious experimenting in visuals and storytelling, Aggro Dr1ft maintains a fair chunk of Korine’s aggressive poetic style while emulating a video game: voiceover narration, loose plotting, lingering camera movement, and rattling cutting, to name a few. If you’re new around here, then it should be known I love a lot of Korine’s filmmaking style. I admire the experiment and even enjoyed the movie a little bit, despite a handful of issues and question marks. The only issue I’ll go into right now is the AI visuals, which are shockingly constant and added nothing. I’m not for AI in movies at all, though I should clarify that here, it’s not a case of AI replacing human artists, as all the human-made visuals are present. If anything, it makes the AI visuals even more ugly and distracting by comparison. The movie suffers because of its inclusion.

Name: Husbands
Review: Alternately titled: John Cassavetes and His Philandering Asshole Friends Good movie! It took me longer to get into this and didn't rattle or captivate me as much as Faces did, but there are still some really fantastic scenes: everything in London especially compelled me. And despite the lesser emotional reaction and tougher focus, this still has all the meticulous direction, hyperreal performances + dialogue delivery, and aimless characterization that makes Cassavetes great. Also, a little tip since I think it'll help future watchers... I wasn't able to tell the three apart until I took note of their sideburns. Gus's are stubs, Archie's are curly, and Harry's are thick.

Name: There Will Be Blood
Review: Caught this on 35mm, first time with 16-year-old Josh’s favorite movie since… well, since I was 16, and I’m so happy it held up as well as it did. Even more funny and tragic than I remember and the filmmaking is virtually perfect. Left the theater shaking.
Tags: doc films, 35mm, w/ jadyn, w/ claire s

Name: Wildcat
Review: Wildcat got me thinking about its themes of religion and creativity, but I wish it came together more in the moment. The way her stories are portrayed here is interesting, though the story of Flannery herself doesn't really break a standard biopic mold or correlate with her stories in a way that goes beyond "stay dedicated, keep making art." Nonetheless, it has a great ensemble and is technically well directed. (followed by a Q&A with Ethan Hawke himself!)
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!, q&a!

Name: The Fall Guy
Review: Liked, didn't love. The stunts are all really fun, both the hand-to-hand stuff and everything on the film sets is genuinely really impressive, and I was on board just enough with Colt and Jody to be entertained by their journeys, though the romance is super bland and the movie never got more than a little chuckle out of me. I was honestly fairly bored with the first 30-40% of this, but once the crime subplot kicked in I found the rest of it surprisingly endearing.

Name: Mad Max 2
Review: I put this on as soon as I got home from work, I’m too tired to have appreciated this fully or given it a lot of thought. But I really liked it still, really good action movie. I will say, from what I remember, I love the gritty realism of the original and was surprised by how much less serious this one felt; it threw me off at times. But still a very entertaining ride with a lot of great stunts and effects (even if some specific effects haven’t aged super well lol)

Name: The Incredibles
Review: A biopic about Brad Bird in directors’ jail
Tags: baby's first apartment, watched while sick

Name: Paperboys
Review:
Have had such a busy couple weeks that I picked the shortest feature on my watchlist just to finally log something again... and damn I picked something good!

I really haven't been that into documentaries in a while, but this was a great way to get back into them. A time capsule from teenage life at the turn of the 21st century was always going to intrigue me, but I was fascinated by everyone's life in this. It’s nothing spectacular, but did all it needed to for me to enjoy it and be inspired by it. Really good editing, a great soundtrack, and very honest interviews about pop culture, local community, and where the future is going with a strong sense that Mike Mills really knew how to communicate with these boys.

Name: Tetsuo: The Iron Man
Review: Happy pride month! Was honestly not sure what to expect going in, and… it definitely wasn’t this, lmao holy shit. I love it when movies are nothing like I think they are, though I admit I was a bit tired for a movie where there’s nothing to distinguish between real and fake, or even what the hell anything is about. But, if nothing else, holy shit the effects and editing were insane, and I was either grossed out or entertained the entire time. The fact that there was some coherent story that made me feel things out of any of this is impressive. I could lightly compare it to Persona, Eraserhead, House, etc. but it’s really in its own league. Also - I watched it with my friend Skyler who I’ve known online for 7-8 years but finally met in person tonight. It was awesome.

Name: Mad Max Beyond Thunderdome
Review: Honestly kinda loved. Ranges from silly fun (rarely taking itself too seriously even if some of it is a bit ridiculous) to awesome (great cinematography and production design, and holy shit the final act!), and stays entertaining through all of it. In some ways maybe a bit too derivative of The Road Warrior story-wise, but no less enjoyable in my opinion. From the North by Northwest homage at the beginning to the David Lean-esque cinematography and the High and Low homage on the train, it feels kinda like a love letter to action-adventure movies without being too obvious and I really liked that about it. See also - Tina Turner in THAT outfit.
Tags: dvd rental, library, baby's first apartment

Name: Mad Max: Fury Road
Review: I was worried that I had overhyped this for myself in my 16-year-old mind, but I'm so happy to report that this movie is still an absolute banger and one of my favorite action movies. I've got so much energy.

Name: Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga
Review: I do think this suffered a bit from prequel syndrome where I know Furiosa makes it out of every situation alive, taking away some suspense. Plus it’s more focused on worldbuilding than action (not a bad thing) and some of the subplots aren’t the most engaging things in the world. All that being said, I still had a really good time and there’s a lot to appreciate here. Best part is that it gets better as it goes along, all in terms of more exciting chase scenes, more interesting story stakes, and deft plotting (every reveal was awesome). Also I noticed a little more CGI here than in Fury Road, but it wasn’t much of an issue at all. By the time the awesome third act kicked into gear with the 40-day war and everything, the fire under my ass was, indeed, lit. Good movie, deserves a second watch and better box office performance.

Name: Babes
Review:
Claude acted in a damn Martin Scorsese movie, and after a whole month of Eden trying to find his last name, she didn’t think to check… idk, maybe IMDb??


Good comedy, had a fun time.

Name: The Breakfast Club
Review:
Really great way to kick off the summer.

I’m surprised I didn’t vibe with this more a couple years ago given that I was IN high school at the time, although maybe it just felt too familiar and didn’t hit for that reason. Especially since a number of teachers and counselors at my high school really made me feel shitty in similar ways to the characters.

But now that I’m out of it, I really do love how brutally honest it is (chef’s kiss for the sharing circle scene) the entire time, it reminds me that unfortunately not much changed on that front between 1985 and my high school years. And it’s also much funnier than I remember. Rating could go up.
Tags: netflix, w/ jack l, at jack's place

Name: Inside Out
Review:
This was a life-changing masterpiece for me when it first came out, it wasn’t quite that today but damn it’s still really great.

It goes for funny more often than I’d like, a little more cringey at age 20, and the sheer amount of ideas makes it feel a little overstuffed at just 90 minutes.

But god, I really do love the worldbuilding and how it represents inner thoughts, it’s really funny, Giacchino doesn’t miss, and there are a few scenes that still really hit — the first island falling, Bing Bong, and the final conversation.

And one thing in common between both eras: I was left lying on the couch after each watch.
Tags: disney+, cried or teared up, w/ mom, at home

Name: Inside Out 2
Review: Inside Out 2 didn’t do a ton for me, but it’s inoffensive and fun. It has a fairly surface level exploration of anxiety with the same base message and plot as the original, though it’s at least fun, and Riley and her stakes (separate from her emotions) are still endearing. The cast is solid: I miss Bill Hader and Mindy Kaling, though the new emotions are all voiced by talented people. I really liked the addition of the belief system to the mind world, which also has some interesting new animation styles; the anxiety attack sequence was visually great in particular, even if what follows is underwhelming both in general and compared to the truck hit of the original’s ending.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, w/ cam

Name: Knife in the Water
Review: More a movie I was interested by than one I really cared about, but it was well made and kept me engaged. Definitely feels like a feature length version of Polanski’s student shorts, it has a similar examining of human impulses with a touch of expressionist influence. The scene in the boat was my favorite, it was like the boat scenes in Jaws in that the trio gets a chance to have a single intimate night before the rest of the story takes things downhill. It’s also still weird to watch adult characters in movies only to find out they’re as old as you are. I'm 21 in a few months bro.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, w/ mom, criterion challenge 2024, at home

Name: The Thin Red Line
Review:
Crazy that Terrence Malick invented the stereotypical Hans Zimmer score.


I’ve been looking forward to this for a few years now, even moreso the past year as I’ve become a much bigger Malick fan. It was lot to take in on first watch (duh), in particular the total rejection of a singular character driven narrative being unexpected. But I really do understand and admire the fact that, as a historical war movie especially, this isn’t any one person’s story. I did feel the runtime a bit by the end, but dammit if this isn’t still as impressive of an achievement as any Malick movie I’ve seen thus far.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: The Grand Budapest Hotel
Review: An absolutely delectable movie and a really special theater experience. This hit me wayyy harder on a second watch after a few years and has (finally!) joined my Wes Anderson podium. So much to unpack but I’ll give myself the night off and just sit in the emotion.
Tags: music box theater, w/ dad, w/ mom, q&a!, cried or teared up

Name: Inside Out 2
Review: Rewatch because a friend hadn’t seen it yet. Enjoyed it a bit more the second time. Some other random thoughts: - as someone with an anxiety disorder, I honestly do appreciate that this is a no frills portrayal of what it can be like, even if the portrayal doesn’t blow me away. It’s good, and super visually impressive. - as someone with an eyebrow and nose piercing, between this and the last Spiderverse, it’s cool to see them becoming more commonplace in animated movies. We need to indoctrinate kids with the idea that piercings are cool! - one of the hornier Pixar movies, which doesn’t say much but funny that it includes a weird innuendo between Riley’s parents and Fear saying he can fix Anxiety. - Everything with the emotions seems to pass in real time, but the scenes in Riley’s world have some pretty drastic time jumps. Maybe the magic of editing, but it’s not like the emotions stay in the same place for hours lol - Riley has to be suppressing bisexuality, right?? @Disney you bitch
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ aaliyah, w/ kelly

Name: Raiders of the Lost Ark
Review: My mental health saver for the day… Been thinking recently about how this came into my life at the same time as Star Wars when I was a kid and also played a pretty major role in the aughts of my film love. It doesn’t quite get the same emotional reaction out of me today as Star Wars does, but dammit since this still hits in its own entertaining way. I’m most impressed this time by how the movie keeps itself going for all 117 minutes without much of any break. Writing, cinematography, and editing are all stellar in this regard, as are the production design and practical effects (obviously). I also realized this time how humanized Indiana’s character is - a hero that’s regularly stumbling, missing jumps, or turning the wrong way, and his fear of snakes is conveyed so well to the point that I found myself more afraid of them. And the opening of the ark is still an all-timer ending.
Tags: disney+, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: The Departed
Review: Not much to be said that hasn’t been said already, an awesome movie and a total rush. Killer filmmaking (the EDITING!) and performances with a surprisingly impactful message about trust and morality. One of the best crime movies maybe? Can’t imagine watching this in 2006 during the paranoid ass Bush era. I wanna watch it again knowing what to expect and not when I’m being jostled by a bunch of other people on a flight.

Name: Idiocracy
Review: I liked it. Not the absolute best thing ever but I found it regularly funny with creatively stupid worldbuilding and a couple gags aged really well. Between this and the MLK episode of The Boondocks, comedy filmmakers in the mid-2000s clearly really hated pop culture of that time lmao
Tags: hulu, w/ dad, w/ uncle joel, washington

Name: The Killer
Review:
Surprisingly good movie to watch with the family. My aunt even said at one point “this is really good, I can’t believe I’m kinda rooting for this guy.”

Obviously watching again didn’t match the electric energy of the CIFF screening and I was a bit less enthralled going in knowing what to expect, but I maintain that this is a good movie. Technically amazing still with satisfying and focused pacing and violence, though it works best as an action comedy to be honest. The story is decent, but ultimately I found myself wishing it was more fun than self-serious, especially in the final two chapters. Still had a lot of fun overall, rating might go back up.
Tags: netflix, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ uncle joel, w/ aunt katie, w/ gideon, washington, on vacation

Name: Kubo and the Two Strings
Review: I went to the LAIKA exhibit at MoPop in Seattle earlier in the day so of course I had to rewatch one of their movies. To be honest this wasn’t quite as great as I remembered but maybe that’s just a credit to the quality of this movie? Everything technically and aesthetically speaking is mindblowing and masterful of course, so damn immersive and rich with detail, and the narrative is solid but my problems lie here. For being the main villain, the grandpa character is actually pretty underwhelming and not mysterious enough to justify how little screentime he actually gets. The ending overall is a bit anticlimactic, although I can forgive this more because it’s a satisfying kind of anticlimactic that feels truer to real life and differs from typical hero vs villain kids movie arc, and it also works super well with the detail of Kubo waiting for an ending to his stories to come naturally. Overall a really good movie still, not my favorite LAIKA (hiii ParaNorman) but up to the company’s standards and definitely one of the better animated movies to come from the last 10 years. Low 8. Random side note — I’ve grown so tired of Pluto TV, it’s free but ad breaks every 10 minutes is borderline unbearable. You know what else is free and without ads ?? DVDs from the library.
Tags: pluto tv, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ uncle joel, washington

Name: The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
Review: A few years on, I'm still annoyed by some of the plot conveniences but am maybe even more won over by the beauty of the movie and Walter's arc with Sean. It's well made, entertaining, funny, and I still think about it fairly often for how it captures the world and the joy of an adventurous spirit. The scene in the Himalayas is sooo good and the TSA scene that follows is one of the funniest things I've seen in a while. Awesome soundtrack, even despite my wish for more Of Monsters and Men. Great way to end a family vacation and a great distraction from endless fireworks.
Tags: amazon prime (rent), w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ uncle joel, w/ aunt katie, washington

Name: MaXXXine
Review: Uses too many horror cliches (discordant violins for jumpscares, corny buildup, etc.) to be scary and I forgot most of the story on the ride home, but by and large it’s inoffensive. There are some good moments here and there and the climax is quite fun, but the rest feels too self-serious and predictable to be fun and Mia Goth’s performance is a step down from her work in Pearl. Great production design, costumes, and makeup in this one at least, although I wish the editing worked to make it all something more flashy or overwhelming when it had the potential.

Name: Close Encounters of the Third Kind
Review: One of those movies where I go “damn I gotta return to normal life after this.” Only reason the rating isn’t higher is with Roy’s arc, the ending left me questioning more than I wish, but that could be because this is my first watch in 7 years and could definitely change at some point. Everything else is compelling, entertaining, incredibly well made, and an amazing summer movie with some of the best familial drama and wonder in any Spielberg.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Taste of Cherry
Review:
I’ve had a lot of similar conversations on suicide and life’s worth with others and myself before. The feeling of seeing conversations you’ve had replicated on screen is always an interesting watch, although I never quite fell in love with this in a way that I was hoping for; I definitely went into this with an expectation that built up over the years that I wish I didn't have.

Very well directed and acted movie though - some super great long takes, wide shots, and effective cuts are on display here and make for an engrossing experience of people talking. Using construction sites as a backdrop for depression was a great choice.

Final 5 minutes completely swept the rug from under me and I’m still not sure how to feel.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2024, at home

Name: Kinds of Kindness
Review: First story — starring Willem Dafoe as my body dysmorphia and anxiety Second story (my favorite) — alternate title: Jesse Plemons Is Afraid Third story — uhhhhhh
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ matt g, w/ rhys

Name: Symbiopsychotaxiplasm: Take One
Review: As expected, incredibly cool stuff. The secret crew meetings were my favorite scenes, but watching the progression of the performances was awesome too - reminded me a lot of my own set experiences. I'll be revisiting this more than a few times. Great pregame to a late-night Longlegs showing.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2024, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Longlegs
Review: I saw the Anora trailer for the first time on a massive screen with great speakers and that was amazing… Neon really knows how to market a movie. Seriously though… aside from 1 (one) good performance, an interesting visual style with some eerie blocking to boot, and a promising first act, what are y’all on? Underwritten characters with an overexplained story that takes itself so seriously. There’s never enough grounding in reality to elevate the horror to a memorable level past a couple gruesome kills, or establishing of an emotional connection with Maika’s thinly-developed character to make me feel anything but detached. It’s also completely reliant on typical cueing sound effects and jarring cuts used in 385295 other horror movies to the point that I was taken out every time, what else ya got?? The sting of disappointment doesn’t help since I was very excited for this, but every time I felt something watching the movie, it was immediately followed by something that fully turnt me off.
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ cam, cinemark xd

Name: Flushed Away
Review: Silly fun time, I always like Aardman's worldbuilding and sense of humor even if the story isn't anything great
Tags: netflix, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: The Searchers
Review: I don’t remember how I first heard of this movie, but it entered my radar more between its high placement on the 2022 Sight & Sound poll and me visiting my grandpa who loves John Ford movies, and my interest has really been growing in the year since. And regardless, this is one of the more popular movie classics that I haven’t seen yet, so I went in excited… …and it was an interesting experience! One of those that is incredibly well made with gorgeous colors, beautiful framing (no horizons in the middle that I noticed!), and complete immersion in the time period, although also one where I didn’t find a notable personal connection to it beyond being engaged with the story (which is very consistently engaging either way). I also do think it’s less a study of racism and more a product of its time in that sense, although it made for an interesting watch in that regard. I look forward to letting it sit and having a more concrete opinion in the future. As of now, though, my favorite John Ford movie remains Stagecoach. Seeing it on 35mm was awesome for my first watch as well. The grain and wear of the print, combined with the movie looking and sounding how it does being from 1956, totally added to my immersion.
Tags: gene siskel film center, 35mm, me time!

Name: Brick
Review: The directing and style here are genuinely awesome, although whether or not the screenplay is too frenetic and stuffed for its own good remains to be seen on another watch when I’m less sleepy (had a big day). Until that rewatch, it was a little hard to follow and I never felt that emotionally invested, but I was consistently entertained and I hope I get the chance to make or produce something this cool one day.
Tags: dvd, w/ hazel, at hazel's place

Name: Hot Fuzz
Review: Oh damn yeah, this holds up incredibly well… possibly my favorite pure action comedy?? It’s both hilarious and badass thanks to Edgar Wright’s insane comedic talent and sheer understanding of how to write and direct something that garners a laugh. But it also has a fully fleshed out world with genuinely amazing Chekhov’s gun — every character and every little detail has their ends met at some point later on, from lines of dialogue (“Hag!”) to physical comedy (the filing cabinet bit will never not get me) and it’s somehow all satisfying in the end. Through all the cutting and details to watch for and words spoken in British accents to figure out what’s being said, it’s never very confusing to follow along with. The only critical thing I have to say is that the gunplay at the end goes on a little too long without enough variety in the action, but that’s all. Edgar Wright, with this movie in particular, reminds me of the joys of film. A comfort movie.
Tags: dvd, w/ jack l, at home

Name: Videodrome
Review: “I think it’s what’s next.” “Then God help us.” Good movie. Fascinating and uncomfortable and kinda funny, all in ways that never felt unearned despite the confusion I felt at times. Feels like a precursor to The Fly in its body horror and themes of how overconsumed modern technology, when it’s too late, turns humans into visceral, disgusting amalgamations of the images you want to get out of your head. It’s super confident and all works: even when I didn’t know what was going on, I was locked in and highly unsettled.

Name: Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom
Review: The divorced man’s Bridge on the River Kwai finally clicked. Yes, parts of the story still feel too superficial and too long, but the sheer commitment and clear fun being had by the two guys who got me into film really does elevate the story and stakes of the characters a lot. I’ll think less of the story again with time, I’m sure. But I’ve been feeling a bit rough this week and I really did enjoy watching this tonight for the sheer fun and nostalgia of these movies. Having some really sick action directing, practical effects, and well-built tension combined with the intricate set pieces and the coverage of it all helps a lot too - it’s Indiana Jones.
Tags: dvd, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Lemonade Mouth
Review: UPDATE - a couple things I marked as watched got removed(?) so this isn't even my 2000th anymore lmfao This is my 2000th watched film on Letterboxd, lmao. My original plan was to make it Jeanne Dielman, but because of my energy level, mood, and schedule recently, I never found the right time and I wanted to hang out with friends. I do plan to watch it sometime soon though. Even then, this isn’t my 2000th watched film ever. I’ve seen plenty of short films online, at festivals, made by friends, etc. that aren’t even on here, so I probably passed 2000 at least like 20-30 films ago. So, you know, whatever. As for the movie itself? It was pretty fun. I don’t really have much of a childhood connection to Disney Channel, but I’m a part of that generation and see the appeal of this movie. I actually kinda cared about the characters despite some obvious flaws and unintentionally hilarious moments that took me out. I wonder where Principal Brannigan was on January 6th. There are worse movies, my 2000th could’ve been Longlegs!
Tags: disney+, w/ aaliyah, w/ kelly, at home

Name: Twisters
Review: I genuinely really wanted to like this more, but I unfortunately found it comparable to The Way of Water in that it felt too wrapped up in the jargon and scientific accuracy of storm chasing / meteorology that it has the exact same story and character arcs as the original. It’s saved by some very intense tornado scenes and the charisma of the actors when the narrative and directing of non-tornado scenes (often) didn’t make any noise for me. I suppose the simple acknowledgement of people who are negatively affected or impoverished by tornadoes and the subhuman cunts who profit off of that tragedy gives it a little more weight than the original, but otherwise it pains me to say this largely felt like a cash grab. Happy birthday and keep resting easy, Philip Seymour Hoffman. Dusty is a more memorable character in the first than anyone in this.
Tags: cinemark seven bridges, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, imax

Name: The Treasure of the Sierra Madre
Review:
The two hours of this movie are marked by very different qualities. The first hour is classic Hollywood at its best: razor-sharp dialogue and performances, captivating filmmaking, an exciting premise with entertaining characters that start from the bottom and chase the possibility of fortune, and never a dead scene. The second hour fizzles out in its excitement as things get muddled and the plot becomes more complicated, but it becomes no less well-made or engaging as the characters become more primal and unpredictable. The looser plot sweeps the rug out from under you, but it mostly maintains the same emotions as you have no idea where things are going.

All around, a really great movie that balances exciting, sharp filmmaking with loose character studies. So confident and a great example of a film that you could easily learn filmmaking from, and it’s still really enjoyable. I loved it - the only reason the rating isn’t higher, and a lot of initial ratings for first-time watches, is because it still feels to fresh to have a more concrete emotional connection to it.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: Life, and Nothing More…
Review: Got too tired to fully pick up on everything that happened in the second half (I’m getting tired of saying this), but this is a really good movie still. Kiarostami’s tenderness finds a place here against a backdrop of tragedy that doesn’t take the effect on the characters / real people you might expect, he really knows how to write and pace conversations in memorable and touching ways. Not to mention the camerawork, editing, and use of geography are just as smooth here as you might expect. I didn’t fall in love with this like I did the other two Kiarostami movies I’ve seen, and I need to rewatch at least the last 30 minutes, but would recommend it nonetheless.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2024, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: X-Men Origins: Wolverine
Review: I don’t have any hard plans to see the new movie, but there’s still a part of me that misses when I was younger and enjoyed Marvel movies more. That took over and curiosity got the best of me. Although, prep watches, especially for franchise movies, don’t feel as genuine and for that reason I can’t judge them like I would if I was watching them as their own thing. So we’ll see how far I get. This one isn’t great but it also isn’t The Flash. I went in with an expectation and this was exactly that. For every interesting story idea or fight scene, there’s something that deems it forgettable or an ugly shot to follow. Didn’t hate it but wasn’t particularly moved by any of it, whatever!
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, w/ quinn, at home

Name: The Village
Review: Equally effective and goofy, I’m conflicted here in ways that I wasn’t with the first few M. Night features. At the end of the day I do still respect Shyamalan’s commitment to his craft enough that I was won over more than not, but if I’m being honest with myself I don’t think I liked this as much as I wanted to. I will say, though, this is the most visually impressive Shyamalan film I’ve seen thus far by a good margin; movies shot by Deakins continue to make me go "damn this looks good" without even knowing it was shot by Deakins.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ quinn, at home

Name: Trap
Review: A silly premise with too many plot conveniences, plenty of weird character decisions, and a sense of “why does this exist?”... made weirdly engaging and fun with some effective individual scenes thanks to M. Night’s occasional creative humanity, along with the fun being had and commitment across the board from a talented cast and crew. A true Shyamalan 2.5 or 3 star movie with a heart, filed alongside The Village, The Happening, and Old.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ quinn, w/ cam

Name: The 400 Blows
Review:
I just spent the last few days on a short film set and it was wonderful to be back at it. After having a notable increase in anxiety lately, it was a great break from everything. But the day after finishing anything particularly special, whether it be a film set, a trip, a hangout, etc. is always gloomy and a weird adjustment.

Suffice it to say, this movie, in all its reflections on childhood and the desire to just grow up already only to find out that it’s not everything it’s cracked up to be, seriously hit the spot today. The entire thing is well written, directed, and acted, but in particular the moments of bliss in between all Antoine’s struggles (the Rotor ride and the theater trip with the parents) are executed to perfection and the final 10-15 minutes really got me - the way the entire thing builds up to Antoine finding the shore for just a brief moment before life crashes in again. Great stuff.

Name: Cuckoo
Review: Honestly very comparable to Men in its mostly solid cinematography and sound design, secluded vacation spot turning against the main character, the outlandish antagonists that end up feeling too caricatured, and the random nature of the horror that really isn’t scary. But I prefer this by a little bit because Hunter Schafer’s character was fleshed out and engaging enough to win me over slightly more than not. Overall though I’m a bit disappointed with the theme of 2024 summer movies leaving me feeling indifferent, I’m being a bit generous with this rating. Also, Neon loves horror movies that fully explain their horror through dialogue between the second and third acts lmao why is the visual storytelling not servicing this more…
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ jess, w/ sid

Name: PlayTime
Review:
A movie for workers and players alike - there’s fun to be had everywhere. Be sure to tip and thank the door on the way out.

I wasn’t fully in the right mood for this but the fact that I still enjoyed this as much as I did probably speaks to its sheer level of quality. An absolutely unbelievable achievement in filmmaking with plenty of laughs all along the way. Rating could (probably will) go up.


Many thanks to Music Box for screening this on the only 70mm print of the movie in North America. Also my last time in the main Music Box theater before renovations begin, a very special experience!
Tags: music box theater, 70mm, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: The Wolverine
Review: It’s better than Origins: Wolverine but it’s also a 2010s superhero movie in nearly every way. Exact same plotting, character dynamics, and weak romance as almost all of ‘em, although I did have some fun with the action and there’s inspired cinematography in small amounts. I wanna see more of James Mangold’s earlier indie stuff, I’ve only seen his studio work so far
Tags: disney+, at home, w/ mom, w/ dad, w/ quinn

Name: Pecker
Review: Had some free time tonight on my last night of a short vacation, decided a John Waters movie was the best way to get me back into the swing of things. Silly fun time! Well made visually and technically, very funny, and never boring. This is only my second John Waters after Pink Flamingos (which has grown to be one of my favorite first watches of the last 12 months) and the first time I’ve seen him direct in a more cinematic style, at least as "cinematic" as Waters can be - the overly stilted performances and didactic dialogue threw me off, but I grew into it, and the humor wouldn’t land the same way without it. The cast is very strong too, no particularly amazing performances but a lot of actors I really like (Furlong, Ricci, Plimpton, Sexton) who were clearly facing fun. The message about selling out was also fairly obvious to me, but the movie mostly gets away with it thanks to Waters’ awkward approach. A few of my favorite lines: “Shelley! I love you more than Kodak!” “We’re all famous… just like the Jackson family!” “Your child has attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder. She needs… *dramatic music* Ritalin.”

Name: Purple Noon
Review: One of my favorite first watches of the summer. There’s so much playfulness to the performances, how the movie reveals all the story details, and how it captures the serene energy of every location that I couldn’t help but be engaged with this all the way to the excellent ending. A great example of how to use motifs, how to balance confident direction with a deft and fun touch, and how to draw you in with a mystery that reveals itself early on and continues to evolve. Not a whole lot of huge personal takeaways, just highly enjoyable. Loved it.

Name: Deadpool
Review: A fun enough time, though I can see myself getting tired of these fairly easily. I’ll just keep spacing them out like I have been and hope that I finish before I go back to school. I’m preaching to the choirs by saying entire thing is just another typical Marvel movie with extra gore, swearing, and nudity, but the first half does at least have some creative cinematography and VFX work that isn’t found in most of the others. If that continued, and the story didn’t descend into the 582581482nd basic Marvel hero arc, this could’ve been one of, if not the best 21st century Marvel movie.* * I haven’t seen Logan yet, that’ll be next.

Name: Glengarry Glen Ross
Review: Went from 30 Rock to this... Alec Baldwin plays the same character in everything lol One of those that was super well made with a great screenplay and captivating acting but didn't leave a whole lot of takeaways for me. Crime doesn't pay, toxic workplaces breed bad behavior... true but I already know! Even then, it was still a really engaging watch. I dunno what else to say, just a good movie that I don't have a whole lot of unique thoughts to contribute. Very strong 7.
Tags: netflix, at home, virtual watch party, w/ jadyn

Name: Dìdi (弟弟)
Review:
Really good stuff. If this came out 2 years ago, I'd relate a little too much to Wang Wang / Chris. A very honest feeling and well made movie.

The energy of being a teenager, both the triumphant and the awkward, is captured so well through Sean Wang's direction, the lead performances are great, and the screenplay is full of good dialogue with an ending that really crept up on me. A lot of scenes brought back specific memories of my teenage years, the last convo Chris has with his mom nearly made me cry, and I was not expecting the ending to have that particular message but I'm so glad it did.

It feels too structured and staged at a few points when I felt the plot and performances needed to be more free-flowing and natural, and some of the supporting performances were less than stellar, but this was a great watch that scratched my summer movie itch and really hit close to home for me.

Name: Blink Twice
Review: Blink Twice has an easy story to predict from the very beginning and it lost me a fair amount in its buildup, but it successfully gets you on board with the ride once the reveal eventually happens. And as others have been saying, Kravitz has potential in her visual direction. Despite its blatant obviousness, the fact that it follows through and manages to have fun with the thriller genre makes it a decent movie overall.

Name: Workweek
Review: I also love Eraserhead. Great work though, everyone… a really interesting short film idea executed with just a few great shots and editing techniques. Super tight and to the point.

Name: Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets
Review:
The WOKE LEFT wants to DESTROY Lucius Malfoy! Here’s how you can save the Wizarding World.



Oh this was wonderful in theaters, my first Harry Potter on the big screen! The flying car, the quidditch, the basilisk, the character drama, “Dobby is freed”… so nostalgic and so fun. The movie was also funnier than I remember tonight, maybe I was just excited from the experience idk. Even the flaws with this movie! I like to dog on them, but this is my childhood… it’s all in good fun.

Oh, and Leaving Hogwarts by John Williams… one of my favorite pieces of film score. Got chills hearing it in theaters for the first time.

Name: Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban
Review: With apologies to Chamber of Secrets, I was delighted to be reminded that this is in fact the best one. The filmmaking really is up a notch or two here, the cinematography and editing are honestly killer. And the way the story builds up to every big reveal and uses time travel is genuinely so smart and satisfying. I also think the emotional buildup to Harry learning the Patronus charm is one of the best plotlines in the franchise, I really clicked with the depression symbolism of the dementors this time. I wish there was more of this in the other movies, but then this might feel less special. Alfonso Cuaron is my Patronus animal. And just the pure joy I felt watching this in theaters made this one of my new favorite Harry Potter experiences. Months from now, I’m going to be nostalgic for this specific night.
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Pee-wee's Big Adventure
Review: Unironically really good. Paul Reubens was a treasure. Bring back unhinged kids movies with satire of America and Hollywood.
Tags: dvd rental, library, at home, w/ dad

Name: Shaun of the Dead
Review:
Awesome.

A great final theater experience of the summer.

Name: Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire
Review: I wish I caught this in theaters like the last two, but that's life... and they'll come back sometime. To re-word my last two reviews again, there are pacing issues in the first half and I think it takes itself too seriously at times, but I continue to still enjoy it a lot and be super impressed by how the movie drops subtle hints to the finale and the Barty Crouch Jr. arc and builds to the great final 30 minutes. See also: Brendan Gleeson is a treasure. And I miss when franchise films had creative freedom to build a good mystery and have creative shots longer than 3 seconds (back when these things were still shot on film). Lastly, I realized a new reason why I love Prisoner of Azkaban and possibly why so many others consider it the best, because it's the delightful calm before the storm that is this and the rest of the franchise's brooding. Not to say it isn't good, of course, but Azkaban's individuality, the difference in tone, and pure greatness hit me after this rewatch.
Tags: peacock, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Stand by Me
Review:
I read The Body, which this is based on, over the course of this summer, so it only seemed natural to close out the summer with watching one of my favorite movies again.

Honestly, it didn’t scratch the itch I was hoping for. Having read the book, I think the movie is too short and doesn’t give the scenes as much time to breathe as they should. I was expecting a visceral reaction since I’ve had that recently thinking about my memory of the movie but, nonetheless, I still enjoyed it and fell for the characters again. It still made me feel some things. A lot of scenes do capture the nostalgic and fleeting but still joyful essence of hanging out and being with your friends that they go for, and the time period is more than translated through production design, language, soundtrack, etc.
Tags: dvd, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Donnie Darko
Review:
I’m taking a break from depressing movies for a while


Update - despite watching it while feeling rough, I have to say this is a pretty great movie. Glad to have finally seen it like 10 years after I heard the name for the first time, it didn't disappoint.

Giving it an 8/10 as a default, it's at least that for me! Could possibly get bumped up in the future.

Name: Alien: Romulus
Review: Really enjoyed it a lot! From what I’ve seen, definitely the summer genre movie of 2024, lots of fun and I left very satisfied. It doesn’t quite have the edge to the characters or frights that the original does, but the story is solid and gets increasingly engaging, and Rain and Andy have an interesting dynamic. Even despite some dumb character decisions and moments that I felt were too expository, the filmmaking to follow made up for it. The production design and visual effects are just stellar through and through, and, similarly to the original, the horror and stakes just keep ramping up as it goes. Also AI Ian Holm real bad, I hope that goes without saying but it can’t be said enough.
Tags: landmark century centre, w/ hazel, spooky season 2024

Name: Beetlejuice
Review: Will we ever get another PG-rated movie with an F bomb? Obviously a ton of care was put into making the movie look great and stand out as its own thing, and it does. But I had forgotten, before this and Pee-wee last week, that Tim Burton’s sense of humor really is his backbone, both in how he’s able to direct a laugh visually and how he can get such impressive comedic performances. Hopefully the legacy sequel can live up to that. What a fun movie.
Tags: hbo max, baby's first apartment (year 2), spooky season 2024

Name: City Lights
Review: First movie as a legal adult! This was cute and pretty funny and well made. I didn’t fall in love with it or have that huge emotional connection like a lot of others have, maybe just my mood today, but I still liked it and admired it.

Name: Jerry Maguire
Review: “I was 35, and I’d started my life.” So effortlessly charming and witty that I really didn’t mind the Cameron Crowe cheesiness as much as I thought I might. Performances are stellar, dialogue is great, it’s engaging and fun the whole way through, and I forgot about other things going on in my life while watching. The 90s were a peak decade for talky business dramas.
Tags: dvd rental, library, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Beetlejuice Beetlejuice
Review:
I liked it when it was a Tim Burton movie, I liked it much less when it was a family drama. The family drama is totally bland (I could’ve lived my entire life without Justin Theroux’s character) and studio-manufactured, and it takes what feels like a long time to become entertaining. But when it finally gets there it’s mostly made up for, actually kinda feeling like Burton is back with edgy humor, practical effects, and all. Hopefully this makes enough money that he can do whatever he wants for his next movie.

I will say though, despite having rough pacing, points to the movie for looking good the entire time, with a lot of great production design and no bad VFX.
Tags: amc newcity 14, w/ quinn, w/ mom, w/ dad, w/ jadyn, w/ carmen, spooky season 2024

Name: High Noon
Review: Great editing, story structure, and direction in this. The dialogue strikes that old Hollywood balance of feeling both snappy and realistic, and there’s a perfect amount of intrigue/things left unsaid behind the (very well-written) characters and the lore of the town that really adds to the immersion and keeps you interested. The recurring use of the ballad was great as well. I was a little too tired to fully appreciate this, but it’s really good stuff. Loved it.

Name: Dementia 13
Review: Pretty good for what I was expecting. Even in his debut, Coppola had a really good grasp on how to create something engaging - there are some really striking images (there’s a genuinely great focus pull in the final act, to mention one), transitions, and uses of music in this. The story isn’t incredible, and at times in the second half I was wondering why I cared, but does its job in terms of developing an interesting mystery and throwing you off at times. This was my first Roger Corman-produced movie as well, a good start.
Tags: amazon prime, baby's first apartment (year 2), spooky season 2024

Name: The Master
Review:
At first I felt I didn't love this quite as much as I did in 2020, but it’s starting to come back to me as I sit on it. I forgot just how dense this story really is, it was a lot to take in again and will be lingering in my mind. Freddie’s whole arc is so interesting, but the first time Dodd goes through “processing” with Freddie had me speechless. I think that was my favorite scene this time around.

Seeing it on the big screen and on 70mm reminded me of just how well directed and shot this thing is… not even to mention the performances.
Tags: music box theater, 70mm, w/ jadyn, w/ izzy, w/ hazel

Name: The Element of Crime
Review: Not bad, but on the lower end of LvT for me. The setup of the investigation and the dynamic between Fisher and Osbourne were both really interesting in the first act, and there are some really striking images and transitions to be found here. As for the investigation itself, there were a couple cool scenes sprinkled in, but overall it lost me little by little until I was able to semi-predict the ending and wasn't very moved by it. There are plenty of good ideas here, and the ingredients of a great director are present, though von Trier was understandably still finding his footing in execution. It's super interesting how this and The House That Jack Built have a lot in common as an intro and (spiritual) outro to his career respectively. Though in terms of what they're going for and my overall watching experiences, I prefer the latter. High 6, maybe a low 7 as I sit on it.

Name: Child's Play
Review: Look, on this rewatch I acknowledge that this movie totally has some stupid parts to it. In fact it was kinda fun to laugh at it, for example the dude who slammed open the door and shot his damn stove like the stove had a gun. But even despite that, just like on my last watch, some of the murder scenes still got to me, and the images of charred Chucky at the end still made me feel like I'd have nightmares tonight. It remains effective for me, warts and all.
Tags: hoopla, w/ esther, baby's first apartment (year 2), spooky season 2024

Name: Jane in Peril
Review: I helped produce and was an extra in this, the latest from my dear talented friend! It’s quite good and it’s available now, for free! Watch it!

Name: The Substance
Review:
I need 1 million cute dog videos and a nice blankie.

I honestly can’t think of a more disgusting movie off the top of my head, this might be #1 which is an insane achievement in itself. And the way it completely earns the title with how well rounded, emotionally engaging, nonstop visceral, and insanely well crafted the whole thing is… it's so singular and genuinely unbelievable.

I was SO taken by the editing, cinematography, and humor in the first act, the second act I was just there for the ride enjoying as the story developed and I came to understand the characters better, then the final act combined both of these and kept building upon itself more and MORE... myself and the rest of the theater made a whole plethora of sounds and yelps at every turn.

We’ll see how the substance (haha) holds up in my memory against the style, but right now I have almost no complaints. What an absolutely killer theater experience (almost a completely sold out show, no less) and mind-blowing first watch this was. Fucking sick.
Tags: amc newcity 14, me time!, spooky season 2024

Name: Child's Play 2
Review: Much like the first one, it starts off okay but gets much better as it goes along and, if it weren't for my nostalgia with the first one, then Kyle and the final act would push this over the edge for me as the superior entry. Honestly, they're equal after this rewatch. That final act is always awesome, like Monsters Inc. meets The Substance.
Tags: netflix, w/ esther, baby's first apartment (year 2), spooky season 2024

Name: The Conversation
Review: A lot of things have been spoiled for me in film school.
