But I am so very glad no one told me about what happens in the final act or how much of a ride it is, especially compared to the meticulous character study the first two acts are, because what a fun surprise it was! A super intriguing story that’s just as well directed as I’d have expected from FFC. Felt a tad long at times, but part of that came with watching this after a busy day. My initial reaction didn’t hit me with the same immediacy I felt with The Godfather and Apocalypse Now, but it makes total sense given that the final scene here is much more open ended, and I really didn’t see the ending coming in general. Even then, because the central story wraps itself up just as nicely as the two aforementiomed movies, the mystery of the final scene hits that right balance of narrative satisfaction and thrilling intrigue that I haven’t felt in a while. Great movie. I’m excited to see how it sits with me in the near future.
Tags: dvd rental, library, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: The Wild Robot
Review:
Cute and endearing! It’s fairly predictable and nothing you haven’t seen before in kids movies storywise, but it hits a good amount of the right buttons and has a lot of fun with the worldbuilding. And the animation is so visually pleasing and textured that you start to take it for granted.

This was also my first 4DX experience and honestly? It’s really fun. The air blowing directly in my ear was annoying but everything else worked for me and was a nice extra thrill. I might see more 4DX stuff every once in a while.

Name: Megalopolis
Review: Many are saying this, but, shit, Megalopolis is just massive. The scope + scale needed for a movie about a big city’s politics and the stories of its most outspoken figures is fully felt and aptly overwhelming. Whether or not the story or the characters or dialogue were working for me at any given point, Coppola’s direction successfully immerses you in the minds of these men who carry the weight of the world on their shoulders. What a hectic ride this movie is… an uncompromised fever dream vision from a legendary filmmaker who’s been in the game for over 60 years, 40 of which this has been in the making. There were definitely some dead zones for me (I was pretty zoned out for a good chunk of the second half), as well as lines of dialogue and performances that took me out, but it is endlessly intriguing in its overall commentary on how history repeats itself and the menial nature of petty competition. Coppola is clearly fed up with American politics. And aside from a few questionable green screens and VFX, it’s gorgeous looking with countless striking images, and is generally a stunning filmmaking achievement. The Madison Square Ben-Hur scene was my personal favorite. Also Shia LeBeouf was not acting… he definitely just walked on set and played himself.
Tags: regal city north, w/ hazel, w/ cam

Name: Weekend
Review: Andrew Haigh is two for two with movies with unassuming scenes that make my inner thoughts go: “Oh that was really sweet… sorta reminds me of-- wait… oh my go-“ Then I start sobbing. This reeeally tapped into an inner dialogue that I’ve regularly had with myself lately about my romantic side and my sexuality (that I won’t go into detail with online). I didn’t really fall in love with the movie until this aspect really kicked in, but I was always engaged. The conversations and performances of these characters are so real, vulnerable, and honest that I felt simultaneously entertained and painfully seen. It’s always a weird combo but when it works like it does here, it creeps up on you and ends up flooring you. Haigh is a special filmmaker for me in my life right now.
Tags: criterion blu-ray, baby's first apartment (year 2), cried or teared up

Name: THX 1138
Review: 14.5 years ago, I watched Star Wars for the first time. Tonight, after all these years, I have finally finished George Lucas’s feature filmography. Can’t believe the logo that gave me nightmares as a kid was named after Robert Duvall. I’m not gonna pretend to understand every part of this story or this world on first watch, especially while feeling under the weather. But this is certainly a confident, intriguing, and entertaining ride. An incredibly bold debut in Lucas’s command over the world building that certainly has a lot to say (might have to write about this in my business ethics class), as well as the excellent visual style (production design and cinematography went hard), with characters that are solidly written and performed. I think Lucas was still finding his footing in pacing and plotting, both in terms of a couple emotional beats that fell a bit flat and plot points that happened because of dumb character decisions (why did THX and LUH have sex in a detention chamber with total surveillance?). But by the time I got to that kickass final chase, I was locked in until the end and finished very satisfied with the experience. Really cool stuff, would love to see in a theater one day. Teetering between a 7 and 8 on the rating.
Tags: internet archive, baby's first apartment (year 2), watched while sick

Name: Benny's Video
Review: 90s Arno Frisch has a very punchable face and whoever cast him in both this and Funny Games deserves a raise. Watching two cold, clinical dramas in a row during the busiest time of the school year thus far is definitely one of the decisions I’ve ever made. I didn’t know anything about this going in other than it was directed by Michael Haneke, and the concept of the movie is actually pretty fascinating. Haneke kinda predicted incel culture, or at least conceived of some early evidence of it through making this movie. And early on, he was already a master at regularly putting audiences in effectively uncomfortable positions with his static camera long takes. This isn’t technically considered a horror movie, but the psychological torment that came with it might convince me to put my spooky season tag on this. I was engaged the whole time and regularly asking questions of what I’d do in this situation if I was in Benny’s family or one of his friends, but with all that being said, this gave me the least strong reaction out of all the Haneke joints I’ve seen thus far. I can’t find any glaring flaws in the movie, yet I felt emotionally alienated by this in a way I wasn’t by his other work. I can’t put my finger on why, but I have felt this way about his work before and it’s all grown on me. Hopefully this will too, or at least I’ll have some more clarity in the near future.
Tags: criterion channel, baby's first apartment (year 2), watched while sick

Name: Carrie
Review:
Oh, that fucking rocked!! Sold out theater with a drag show beforehand for my first time watching one of the last horror classics I hadn’t seen yet?? Yes please!

So much to love here but I think my favorite part is Sissy Spacek’s performance. She’s so perfectly cast as a sensitive, quiet high schooler, and so emotionally engaging in nearly every scene. Every expression, every mannerism during the prom sequence… just amazing. Not even to mention literally everything else (I’m tired and don’t want to do a fuller analysis right now, sorry!)

Even outside of the amazing first experience I totally had a blast with this and unlocked a new horror love tonight. Keeping the rating where it is because I’m still soaking it in, but it could very easily go up at some point in the near future.
Tags: music box theater, drag show, w/ hazel, spooky season 2024

Name: Anora
Review:
One of the most consistently entertaining, intimate, and immersive movies of the year, never running out of steam and balancing the joy and heartbreak nearly immaculately, all the way up to that very last scene which took my breath away… was thinking about that ending the entire ride home.

Really great movie. Can’t believe after anticipating it ever since production was announced that I’ve finally seen it, and damn what an exciting theater experience it was… already thinking about when I can see it again and process.
Tags: music box theater, early screener, w/ cam, w/ alek, w/ peter

Name: Temporary
Review:
Very happy to announce that, after a year since I began writing it, my fourth film is here! I want to let the movie speak for itself when it drops, but I’m confident in saying I’m proud of this and it might be my best, most personal work yet, as I have done with each subsequent short.

I have begun submitting it to festivals, and I plan to release it publicly early in 2025 (currently looking at March). Wish me luck at the fests and get hyped!

Name: Memoir of a Snail
Review: “Childhood is like a season. It goes by quickly, but everyone deserves to have a good one.” The first new movie in a long time to render me nothing but a ball of total excitement and joy over film, storytelling, art, family, and being alive. I am totally floored. What an amazing, mesmerizing, beautiful, special film this was. The animation is incredible, the voice acting is so full of life, the story is so full of quirks and personality, the plot direction completely catches you at every turn, and I felt so many emotions (including crying really hard at the end). Made me think about so much life as it went along, then knew completely how to use those feelings for total catharsis. Could not have asked for or honestly even imagined a better start to this year’s CIFF. Phenomenal experience.
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2024, q&a!, cried or teared up, w/ hazel

Name: An Almost Christmas Story
Review: Cute, and very creatively/well animated! Good fun for kids and super special to watch this in the same room as David Lowery during a panel discussion with him! I love listening to directors talk when their obvious and long-standing love for the medium comes through in all their answers, and this might be one of my favorites.

Name: Nobody Knows
Review: It’s been quite a while since I’ve seen a Kore-eda film and they’ve mostly faded in my mind, so I was a bit thrown off when I remembered while watching just how structureless his screenplays are. The main plot point of this movie happens, and the rest is the characters going about their lives and dealing with it in their own ways. It’s very novel-like, where you’re presented with these characters and follow them around their daily lives, and typical film structure is forgotten, but there are still some hard hitting emotional beats. It reminded me most of Edward Yang’s work and Kiarostami’s Life… and Nothing More. I mean this all as a positive. Kore-eda is totally a strong enough writer that this hyperrealistic approach works. I was interested in these kids and their lives, and felt a lot of emotions along the way. Kore-eda admit in the Q&A after that he thought the child actors were horrible, but his direction and editing had me convinced otherwise, as I believed them as their characters feeling like real people. I also really love that this was shot on super 16mm and as if it were a documentary about these kids, the cinematography was very immersive and had a nostalgic feeling to it. I had a really nice time with this movie.
Tags: gene siskel film center, ciff 2024, w/ dad, w/ mom, q&a!, 35mm

Name: Two to One
Review: Bonus movie watched while volunteering at the festival! I could use a crash course in the history of the 1990 German unification to fully understand some things in the movie, but I still honestly enjoyed this! A fun and endearing comedy with a lot of visual playfulness, a likeable set of characters (including Sandra Hüller!) and some solid emotional weight. The ending was pretty sudden against the rest of the emotional experience before it and the love triangle was a bit odd, but otherwise a good time. And maybe these will be cleared up with more context.
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2024, w/ other volunteers

Name: Okie
Review:
As someone born and raised on the edge of Chicago suburbs and rural Illinois, this hit VERY close to home and it’s so rare to see places I visited and lived nearby growing up portrayed in film… at all! And the way the geography of the area is used here is done really well!

What starts off as a hangout dramedy about a guy who reconnects with old friends in his home town quickly turns into something I wasn’t expecting, with a surprising message that honestly shook me up a bit taking me down a rabbit hole I haven’t gone down very often, but I really admire the swings taken and the reaction it got out of me. I was conflicted on the overall story arc at first, and there are a couple story points that were a bit cliche for my taste. But after sitting on it, the overall arc is really growing on me. Not to mention some really gorgeous visuals + effective editing, lots of laughs, and super engaging and lived-in performances and characters. I’ll be thinking about it all for a good bit.

See this one when it drops on VOD on November 8th, especially if you’re an Illinoisan!
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2024, q&a!, me time!

Name: Emilia Pérez
Review:
1/2025 update:
I'll leave the original review up for posterity (as always) but this hasn't aged very well for me. Granted, there is some universal emotionality to be found in the story that redeems the movie from some of its flaws, as well as some impressive camerawork & choreography and a great Saldana performance. But I've already forgotten all the music (this is a musical), and I do think, after time to sit on it, that there are elements of the transition process and trans identity that are mishandled (particularly between Emilia and her wife) as well as the glorification of the cartel lifestyle, on top of a third act that didn't even do it for me on first watch. This is coming from someone who was assigned male at birth and male presenting but gender non-conforming, it's complicated for me right now.


Original review:
Still thinking about the experience, had to get over to AMC NEWCITY for another movie so I didn’t really have time to walk and process.

Immediate reaction is I liked it a lot but didn’t quite love it. The filmmaking is spectacular with some really great choreography, and these characters are interesting and entertaining to follow along with, not to mention some very touching individual moments, but some of the directions the story went in worked against this and didn’t feel earned. I didn’t get a huge emotional connection or reaction which admittedly was a bit disappointing against how excited I was for this, but I enjoyed it for what it was.
Tags: music box theater, ciff 2024, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ cam

Name: The Seed of the Sacred Fig
Review: Wasn’t even sure what to say when the credits rolled, just a truly incredible movie. Had me locked in nearly all 167 minutes with insanely good filmmaking, brilliant performances with layered characterization across the board (Mahsa Rostami is top 5 of the year for me), a masterful screenplay with consistently deft and unpredictable plotting, thought-provoking social commentary on religion, family, and trust that struck a number of personal chords and always balances well with the domestic drama, and far and away the most tense finale of the year. I barely breathed the last 15 minutes. A must see.

Name: The Brutalist
Review:
It's not about the journey... it's about the destination.


Need some time to process this followed by another rewatch, on account of this just having so damn much to it... towering is an understatement, I keep remembering things as I type this. But WOW, yeah this is a phenomenal, special movie. I commented to a friend after that this feels like it was made in 2007 (a complement to its quality). A total labor of love and creativity in all regards, I still cannot believe we have a new 215-minute American indie film with complicated, dense storytelling with several big narrative swings and breathtaking filmmaking like this.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, ciff 2024, q&a!, w/ ross, w/ cam

Name: Bird
Review: I liked it a lot! I was moved by the story overall and felt that Andrea Arnold nailed much of the intimate touch she’s known for with her deeply layered characters, and there’s a fair number of individual scenes that hit the very specific, very right personal spot, which she’s done so well in the past. The handheld camera was pretty constant and took me out at a few points, but it works 80% of the time and there are some really great aesthetic choices that come with it. Great performances, especially from the newcomers, and the pacing and structure are both pretty stellar too. Those 2 hours really flew by. All that being said, there are some… very odd story choices that I’m still not sure how to feel about. Not odd bad, but I was definitely left visibly confused, and I won’t be able to articulate it or know how I really feel any better for at least a day or two. I'm very excited to see other's reactions to this. No matter, this is another win for Miss Andrea. “I’m an adult. You’re a kid.”

Name: Hard Truths
Review: I've been in a weird mental space today and I'm still soaking in and figuring out how I feel about this. There are still some really piercingly personal Leigh moments to be found throughout the film, both funny and heartbreaking, though they build up to a conflicting and surprising third act + ending, different from the other two Mike Leigh films I've seen. A very good movie overall with stellar performances however, the good certainly outweighs the mixed feelings. This might be the movie with the most flexible rating out of everything I've seen at this year's CIFF, it's very subject to change.
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2024, w/ cam, w/ blaine, w/ cam e

Name: The Fog
Review: A good Monday night watch, nothing too serious and a movie with strong enough visual storytelling that I always knew what was happening even if I was tired. Not very scary at all, but the central metaphor is cool, Carpenter's score is awesome, the characters and story pace are entertaining, and the cinematography + G&E teams ATE. Dean Cundey, more like Dean Cunty!
Tags: criterion channel, baby's first apartment (year 2), spooky season 2024

Name: The Texas Chain Saw Massacre
Review: The second half of this is so insane and amazing. I love that a lot of horror that’s come out since this has failed to realize the reason THIS works so well is because the kills are part of the buildup, not the climax itself. The fact that the climax here is a brutal tale of human survival instead of more kills keeps it fresh and unpredictable even after 50 years.
Tags: peacock, baby's first apartment (year 2), spooky season 2024

Name: The Rocky Horror Picture Show
Review: I understood 33% more of the movie on second watch. I wonder if I’ll ever watch this outside of an interactive theater experience, and if I don’t then I wonder how long until I understand the whole thing. Had a better and more fun time tonight though. I went dressed as Brad and slayed. Happy Halloween y’all.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, w/ izzy, w/ jadyn, w/ carmen, spooky season 2024

Name: Juror #2
Review: I love a good courtroom drama. I love the deep analyses of human behavior; the way that technical details of the cases, the emotions and psychology, the mannerisms and actions are all highlighted and revealed throughout a story, they never fail to get me thinking and entertained. I’m happy to report that Juror #2 largely delivered on this! It’s a really engrossing experience, thanks to a fascinating central story (an original story no less, as far as my research has gone!), very good technical direction, Justin’s unpredictable journey to balance truth and justice, and the great characterization of the rest of the jury. The performances are mostly really good across the board as well. I left the theater unsure of what to say and I credit that to the movie's effect on me. A couple supporting performances didn’t quite do it for me, I think the first act could’ve used another draft in the writing room as it took me a while to lock into the story and I felt the initial development of the characters was rather bland, and there’s a major story detail at the end that felt too cleanly revealed, but none of these were enough to ruin the overall very good movie. On the border of a 7 and an 8. I had a good time with this, and it’s a damn shame that Zaslav is burying it. Everyone who’s seen it seems to be enjoying it as well, and Eastwood is Warner Bros. and Hollywood legacy… not to mention this might be his final film.

Name: Seeking Mavis Beacon
Review: Jazmin mentioned in the Q&A (in more elegant wording than I'm about to write) that special interests are often pushed to the side by the social conditions of being a teenager, then you revisit them when you’re a young adult and realize that you had it going for you. That resonated with me and sums up why I enjoyed going on this journey.

Name: How a Mosquito Operates
Review:
Watched because it’s on Mike Leigh’s S&S top 10.

Incredibly vivid animated motion and character design for its time, and I actually got kinda invested in this. Good stuff.

Name: Day for Night
Review: “Cinema is KING.”
Tags: dvd rental, library, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Witness for the Prosecution
Review: Thank you to Francois Truffaut and Billy Wilder for helping me to get through the last couple days. I totally loved this, awesome movie that expertly balances tone and has incredibly well written characters all the way through as I’ve come to expect from ol’ Billy. I was so enthralled by this that even some of the cheese of the ending didn’t really bother me (it was so shocking that I was distracted!), this was too fantastic of an experience with nearly pitch-perfect filmmaking that kept me on the edge of my seat all the way through.

Name: Widows
Review: So much with the screenplay here to still unpack after my first watch, but the directional changes and density of the entire narrative are handled so damn well. The first half is an effective character drama with several character storylines intertwined with ease, growing into the gripping labyrinth plotting and heartstopping crime thriller of the second half, and it all feels earned. I was locked into this from start to finish. The entire ensemble is excellent, not sure who my favorite performance was right off. I don't often hear this one talked about as being on the same level of quality as McQueen's first three features, but even though I'm not gonna rank it as highly on first impression since I was a bit more taken by the filmmaking than the story, I think this is just as captivating and well made (Sean Bobbitt's cinematography, holy shit), and if you've seen Shame or Hunger then you know what HIGH praise that is. Really great stuff.
Tags: blu-ray, w/ hazel, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Deadpan
Review: Buster Keaton if he made Steamboat Bill Jr. in modern Hollywood (they need 12 angles of the same 5-second action):

Name: Clodagh
Review: First time at the new annual film festival in my hometown!! I wanted to go to the inaugural fest last year, but the dates didn’t work out. Pretty light storywise, but the emotions are there, and the technical direction and performances are really well done and engaging. As far as movies from the UK about religion go, here’s baby’s first Breaking the Waves.
Tags: city of lights film festival, the venue aurora, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Contagious Swagger
Review: Definitely technically proficient for a student film and it was funny at times, but the cookie-cutter meet the parents story and fact that it ended up basically being a Nike ad kinda ruined it.
Tags: city of lights film festival, the venue aurora, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Then Comes the Body
Review: Definitely the best short I saw here. Commercial-style documentary filmmaking (meaning studio-esque, not advertising) done right, a consistently engaging story with really gorgeous cinematography and editing all 15 minutes of it. No idea if this qualifies for the documentary short Oscar, but if it does, I would certainly be happy if this got nominated.
Tags: city of lights film festival, the venue aurora, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Blitz
Review:
Unsure of why this has been getting such mixed reception from my following. It’s not quite a life-defining masterpiece, but I was fully immersed with George’s journey and the way Steve McQueen portrayed his world. All the surprises caught me off guard, I was constantly worried for him and his mom, and the conflict all was believable, maintaining a healthy balance between being cinematic and realistic. It’s similar to Widows in that it’s more commercial than McQueen’s earlier work, but he’s proven here again that he’s really skilled at commercial filmmaking. The ensemble is expectedly great as well, especially the excellent newcomer Elliott Heffernan.

There’s some cheesiness to be found (the dream sequence for example) and certain emotional moments that felt a bit alienating or underdeveloped to me, but for the latter I still respect McQueen’s dedication to an atypical film structure to mirror the unpredictable nature of living in a country during war. We’ll see how my thoughts on the flaws age in the future, but in the moment I’m more taken by how moved and engaged I was by everything.

On the border of a 7 and 8.

Name: The Boat
Review: He forgot to relax his triangle of sadness

Name: A Real Pain
Review: A really good drama with some truly wonderful character work and very personal moments that cut deeply for me, all of which I want to experience again without a couple assclowns next to me talking over half the movie. Had to move to a different seat eventually just to enjoy the last 1/3 of this in relative peace.

Name: The Godfather Part II
Review:
Okay, before you all go flaming and unfollowing me because of the rating, let me emphasize that this was an awesome movie and experience for my first watch. But with a movie as dense as this with 3-4 simultaneous storylines at all times (a lot like the first), it was just a ton to take in. Based on how I feel about the first, I believe this will grow on me with time as I process and remember things, and I’m almost certain the rating will go up in the future.

Don’t need to say anything more about how unbelievable the filmmaking and performances are, was completely enamored by all of it. My favorite aspect of the directing was how Coppola contrasts visuals and sound with certain shots, for example when Michael and Kay are screaming at each other in the background (off-screen) of a shot where their kids running around. There’s innocence with what’s shown but violence in what’s heard, and it was incredibly effective.

I loved both timelines, but gun to my head on first impression, I think I actually prefer the 1917 Vito story. I was so taken and fascinated by the details put into the period and just loved his origin story, not even to mention the rooftop sequence.
Tags: alamo drafthouse wrigleyville, w/ jadyn, w/ dad

Name: A Woman Under the Influence
Review:
Walked into this with a weird mood (unrelated to the movie) and of course this is quite the upsetting experience on top of that, so I'm gonna let my thoughts sit overnight before I update with more details and a possible rating.

Will say though that this is indeed a captivating experience with some incredible performances. Cassavetes has such a way with writing and directing complicated characters that I can't articulate just yet, the way this progressed (especially in the final 45 minutes) really got to me, but I'm not sure what to make of how exactly it got to me until I sit on it some more.


1 day later... can't put a number to it yet, but I haven't stopped thinking about it.

Name: The Navigator
Review: Some of the stunts were amazing and giving Sherlock Jr. a run for its money during the first 2/3, then the story dropped the ball with the unrewarding copout ending; it's like they ran out of money to make another reel and had to compromise. Despite all that, I was in the right mood for a goofy silent film and satisfied on a sheer entertainment level.
Tags: dvd rental, video vortex, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Conclave
Review:
Went in expecting to really like this, but left feeling pretty mixed. I was interested in the story itself, which has a very gripping structure (after a slow start) and a few great individual moments that rose above simply following along with the events taking place. But as someone raised Christian who still struggles with faith, I've heard all these discussions about the progressivism, corruption, and morality of religion on several occasions, and nothing about the substance did much for me. The final twist also left me feeling quite conflicted, and on first impression I'm leaning more towards dislike. It feels like it only exists to push a button, and undid the impact of prior discussions highlighting the divides among the Cardinals.

The performances were pretty solid, the production design was excellent, a few shots had very striking framing (though others had odd framing that took me out), and my attention was definitely held. But in the end, this felt too standard in its messages and themes, and I wasn't wowed by any one aspect. Again, was really hoping I would like this more but, while a decent movie overall, this is the first 2024 movie I've seen that I'd classify as Oscar bait.

Name: Collateral
Review:
After the first scene, I was reminded that I could maybe be an Uber driver. Then that thought very quickly faded away.

I love anything where the city is effectively a character, and this happened to use that in a thoroughly intense, thoughtful, genuinely unpredictable, and overall very well made crime thriller. Only thing is it's hard to tell how much it'll stick with me in the long term, but no matter... in the moment, this is very good shit, maybe my favorite from Mann so far.

Name: All We Imagine as Light
Review: I admit I was more nodding along than finding a lot of big emotional in-points (there are a few great individual scenes where I did find a connection, however), but this is so formally concise and visually striking with just enough being said to stay both engaging and mystifying that I enjoyed the ride. The kind of film I could see growing on me in the future as life changes and I find myself relating more to certain situations in the story, similar to an Apichatpong film for example (who I believe Kapadia said was an influence?). And damn that ending is a beauty.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: Back to the Future
Review: One of our most fun movies, might partially just be nostalgia talking since there are a few flaws here but I had a smile on my face through almost all of this. Production design, directing of minute details, comedy, immersion and vibes are all immaculate or incredibly close to it. Showing a friend this for her first time was icing on the cake.
Tags: peacock, baby's first apartment (year 2), w/ esther

Name: Gladiator II
Review: Had a really good time with this! Ridley captures the sword-and-sandals scale and excitement on a surprisingly close level to the original with every battle scene, excellent production and costume design, and a committed ensemble (Denzel!) with only a couple moments that felt a bit overacted to me. There's some rough CGI here and there, but he really knows what he's doing with the spectacle, both in terms of how fun this is and how visually pleasing it is for digital period-piece cinematography (the Seventh Seal-esque dream scenes were a really cool surprise). The story could've been better if the central conflict was more confined, as it moves a little too fast for just how many lead characters there are and some of the big moments don't hit as hard as they could, and I felt the ending was a bit rushed too, but I was able to follow and remained interested with what's here. Sure, there's not a ton of substance to chew on outside the fact that this simply tells a story about a fight against colonization and politicians' abuses of power on the surface, but it did enough to succeed on an entertainment level for me, and I wasn't expecting much more. Most surprising to me on first watch... I've been ambivalent on Paul Mescal's casting from the start. But, and I'm not sure how hot this take is, he really exceeded my expectations here. For the most part, I did buy him as the son of Russell Crowe's Maximus and was impressed by his range between the grit and grime of a talented fighter and the restrained emotion he brought to the story drama. Same rating as the first, high 3.5 bordering on a low 4.
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: The Freshman
Review: Was looking for something to kill time at work and I wish I picked something less emotionally investing to pause a few times, I actually really liked this and wish I watched it all in one sitting. I know Safety Last is the famous Lloyd film and everything because of that iconic clock shot, but I definitely thought this had the better story and I prefer this for that reason. Can’t believe no one in that big ass stadium at the end saw any of the interference happening. And every silent film leading lady looks so similar to Lillian Gish that I just assume that’s who they all are.

Name: American Honey
Review:
Wow. My only immediate thought is that the way the movie immersed me was incredibly impressive. When the movie suddenly ended and the credits started rolling, I was genuinely taken aback because it felt more like 120 minutes had passed than 163 and I was actually hoping for more. Adjusting to reality again after this has felt weird, I really can’t stop thinking about it. Oh, also the cinematography is gorgeous.

Gotta think about the rest for a while, a good amount of this was different from what I was expecting. Lots of emotions were felt during this unsurprisingly great movie by Andrea Arnold.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: Cloudy with a Chance of Meatballs
Review: I like how everything that happens in this movie eventually goes wrong. More depressing than when I was a kid now that I’m able to pick up on what it’s saying, but it still made me laugh and is very creative. My favorite part was when Flint broke into a house, headshot a family of four with ice cream snowballs, then ran out like nothing happened. Great voice cast, worldbuilding, and character design.

Name: To Die For
Review: Phenomenal editing, writing, and directing as far as revealing details and creating an emotionally engaging and challenging mystery go. And I really see why people call Nicole Kidman’s performance in this one of her best. Still a bit mixed on how I feel about the mystery itself, specifically a major plot point near the end, but this certainly left me with a ton of emotions and lots to think about. Devastating and conflicting, I love me an unrelenting neo-noir.
Tags: dvd rental, library, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Jackie
Review: "Only crass, self-indulgent people kill themselves. No, I was just hoping... if I walked down the street next to Jack's body then someone would be kind enough to do it for me." Just as with Spencer, nearly everything here that went into making Jackie an emotionally engaging character worked. The writing and pacing of the conversations she has and how they tie into the direction of the story, Portman's amazing Port-formance, the way the cinematography reveals her state of mind... it's excellent. Only real critique right off the bat is that there are like four different endings and it felt a bit repetitive, but even then this could grow on me with time. I was really moved and taken by this.

Name: Queer
Review: Last Tango in Mexico A lot still to think about, but I certainly think this is a very good movie and I left the theater satisfied. Because of the mysterious, wandering structure this has, the rise of the central relationship was a bit slow and didn’t emotionally grasp me until a while in, but I like that the structure seems to acknowledge how people in love drift together by happenstance, and how this reality is rarely as structured as a lot of the romance genre in film makes it out to be. I remained engaged because Kuritzkes really balances the aimlessness with a sense of excitement around the unpredictability of love with ease, and it becomes a very fulfilling adventure by the end. And when my favorite moments came around (the beach scenes and the Ecuador high, both incredible), they were that much more surprising and joyful. I have some mixed feelings on the epilogue and less to say about it, specifically how it concludes, but I will say the very last couple scenes ruined the pace a bit for me and left me with more questions. Perhaps the original 3-hour cut does it more justice, or maybe it’ll just grow on me with time. But all things considered, I was thoroughly taken by the story and the lead performances, and I’m glad it takes the narrative risks it does. And from a craft standpoint, this is honestly quite astounding! The cinematography is some of the year’s best (has Sayombhu Mukdeeprom ever missed??) with plenty of gorgeous lighting and eye-popping blocking, every cut feels so clean (even if they didn’t all work for me emotionally), the production design is excellent, and the effects are shockingly great. I was informed beforehand that this is movie gets surreal, but I was still surprised by how in love I was with how Luca accomplished much of it. It hangs its influences on its sleeves at several points (WKW, Bertolucci, Dali, Spielberg, Treasure of the Sierra Madre, Y Tu Mama Tambien to name a few) but still feels wholly unique.

Name: Wicked
Review: Pleasantly surprised! A charming and fun adaptation and I honestly didn’t feel the runtime, which is an accomplishment given that this is a 160-minute version of a 75-minute first act that I was already somewhat familiar with (my parents took me to see it live when I was much younger but my memory of it is pretty weak). I agree with the detractors that some of the cinematography, CGI, and color grades had room for improvement, but that's my only complaint to be honest; I had too much fun to notice anything else.
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ dad, w/ mom, cinemark xd

Name: Light Sleeper
Review:
Contemporary and dry and maudlin, this feels very neo while still retaining the spirit of noir. I liked it, feels super confident and fleshed out on Paul Schrader's part in his writing and how he directed the whole thing; I genuinely felt immersed in this world and in these performances, even if I wasn't overly moved by it.

The romance felt a bit hollow to me and the dryness took me a bit to get in the right mood for, but after that I was interested in these characters and was taken by how the story played out. I felt the formula a bit when LeTour first hinted at pulling off another violent grand gesture/typical Schrader ending, but this ended up being a pretty effective version of that.

Great colors and cinematography too, I want one of my films to have this kind of visual cinematic realism with the exact right color palate when I'm skilled enough to direct it. A strong 7 on first impression, feels like something that could grow to be an 8 with time.

Name: The Order
Review: Very intense and depressing, and certainly well made. The action and violence are grounded and gripping while still feeling cinematic, comparable to No Country or Three Billboards. And I admire Justin Kurzel’s commitment to immersing us in Bob’s character (performed scarily well by Nicolas Hoult) and the horrible world he lives in, consistently reminding us that these characters are twisted, infuriating, real humans. The story struggles with genre formula at times and a couple scenes feel a little too sensationalized for my liking, but all around the experience is a challenging pill to swallow, worth seeing. Also of note, I thought it was interesting that the final title cards talked about what happened to the real life neo-Nazis but not what happened to Jude Law’s character.
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ aaliyah, w/ kelly

Name: Y2K
Review: Enjoyed it enough for what it was. Had a solid amount of fun moments along the way and I really liked the effects of the monsters, but the story is pretty unfocused with a decent amount of jokes that don’t land and it ends up being a predictable getting the girl story that didn’t do that much for me. I do like that it doesn’t go super overboard on references to the time period for the most part however, it just feels like a regular movie set in this time period having fun with it and not total nostalgia bait. At least, it didn't annoy me as much as other nostalgia bait media has. If you liked this, go watch Funny Pages.

Name: Tie Me Up! Tie Me Down!
Review: The most out-there romance film I've seen in recent memory (slightly edging out Queer), but I suppose I should've expected this wild of a story from early Almodóvar, lmao. It's justified though, as I felt the characters and story are super well written given what the movie is about, with my only question marks here being parts of the final act that might alter how I view the movie preceding it. Overall, yeah I really liked this. I was engaged the whole time and definitely felt like I was on the right emotional wavelengths for the most part, genuinely being surprised and feeling things at every turn. My favorite aspect was how well it got me to question whether I should be laughing at certain things or not, it certainly works as a dark comedy and satire of both romance genre character stereotypes and the concept of romantic destiny. As I mentioned though, the final act is certainly a risky one, and I gotta let it sit some more. Genuinely amazing cinematography, art direction, and colors though, and the performances are great.

Name: Christmas Eve in Miller's Point
Review: Lots of memories of holiday gatherings past came with this watch. Lovely atmosphere building and execution of a loose structure, funny performances from every single actor, exceptional editing and transitions, and just super confident in all regards. Really good portrayal of teenage life in that it was endearing while also making me depressed thinking about specific teenage experiences I missed out on. Very possibly something I’ll watch again next year and get even more out of. A good watch for after the ADHD pill wears off and it’s easiest to just take in the vibes without being able to catch a ton of little details (although I would like to watch again to be fully engaged and more sure of how I feel).
Tags: amc+, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: National Lampoon's Christmas Vacation
Review: Good fun seeing this in theaters. It peaks in the first few scenes for me, but there are a lot of good bits along the way. As a huge fan of The Middle, it’s cool to see how this movie shaped the show in different ways, from the casting to the character archetypes and small details that come with living in the Midwest (a complement to the movie)
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Nosferatu
Review:
Watched at 11:15 PM which was not ideal since I was falling asleep at a couple points, but the only time I was able to fit this in. Aside from the distraction of Santa on my roof halfway through, I was at least able to watch this uninterrupted.

Didn’t really work for me as a horror film, except for the bug shots which actually got to me and really worked as a metaphor for Count Orlok, but it is of course a very admirable technical achievement in worldbuilding, cinematography, and editing. And it was very cool to discover in real time that this adaptation is actually a metaphor for the Spanish Flu. Very excited to see Eggers’ version, of course.

Name: A Christmas Story
Review: I yearn for the day that Music Box plays this in theaters so I can once again watch my favorite Christmas movie uninterrupted and without my family talking over it (love them a ton, I just wish they’d quietly watch the movie!). Still hits though. Some of the best child performances, hilarious in every scene, so perfectly captures the essence of being a kid around the holidays, filmmaking that emulates Golden Age and Hays Code-era Hollywood, yada yada yada great movie
Tags: dvd, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Home Alone
Review: This one grew on me sorta out of the blue this year, but this is the most I've enjoyed it since I was a kid and I absolutely love that I did. I dunno, it just brought back memories this year that I cherish more for whatever reason to the point that I'm able to look past more of the plot conveniences and just enjoy the ride. There's just something about well-done big-family Christmas movies. Also one of the best movies that center on a kid, like Hughes' and Columbus's 400 Blows. Also I know he had to defend his house and everything, if I lived in Winnetka I’d probably do the same, but I can’t be the only one who thinks that mac ‘n cheese dinner looked insanely good.
Tags: dvd, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Nosferatu
Review:
Good! If a little underwhelming narratively. Really stunned by the craft of it all, as expected; gorgeous cinematography and compositions, thrilling sound design, full immersion into the world of 1838 Germany, plus a committed and highly watchable, entertaining ensemble. And Eggers still really knows how to create a genuinely unnerving scene (that poor bird, Lily’s vision of rats, Lily getting possessed after the argument, the climax, etc.), even if the movie errs too much on the side of jumpscares at a couple points.

But to be honest, outside of a few compelling individual scenes, I just wasn’t really on board with the screenplay like I was hoping for. It certainly didn’t make a point to deeply flesh out its characters before jumping into plot, and it was giving me that outside-looking-in feeling, albeit an entertaining version of that. I just don’t have much else to say here. At 142 minutes long, there were times where it remained engaging almost exclusively because of the filmmaking.

Still, it remained engaging and enjoyable. And the story deserves at least some credit for that, if not just because of me waiting for the potential moment when I’d find a stronger connection to the new Robert Eggers movie. Given my history with Eggers’ work, I’m sure I’ll watch this again in a year or two and hopefully get something more out of it.
Tags: amc naperville 16, dolby, w/ cam, w/ dad, w/ quinn

Name: Petite Maman
Review: Beautifully quiet and understated in a way that works wonders for a movie about both childhood and life at all stages. Still really moving and endearing on a second watch (and it probably would've hit even harder if my ADHD pill didn't wear off while watching), a really special and inspiring movie. This is mom's favorite movie of the 2020s so far and I really don't blame her, I feel a special connection to this movie as well that I can't quite articulate in words but makes me smile and feel a lot of things while watching. Can't wait to watch it again in a more focused environment. "Marion." "Nelly."
Tags: criterion blu-ray, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Walk the Line
Review: A fairly standard Hollywood biopic but definitely one of the best to fit that mold. It's didactic in its messages and doesn't have a very tight story structure which keep it from being great, but the ensemble is excellent and I always liked the look of the film, which elevate the positive elements of the screenplay a good amount. There are a number of moving individual scenes about his family, relationships, and tumultuous journey that still move me nearly 5 years after my first watch and have lingered on my mind every now and then in the time since. Not amazing or a favorite, but an interesting movie I've always thought fairly highly of. Hopefully A Complete Unknown ends up being more like this and not Mangold's mediocre previous biopic.
Tags: dvd, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Singin' in the Rain
Review: Just discovered this pretty unknown old movie. Fun time. Great filmmaking and choreography.
Tags: dvd, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Dahomey
Review: I wish I caught this in theaters instead of at home where it was harder for me to focus, but I still really admired this. As many others have said, a very tightly crafted documentary; the better of the two I’ve seen from 2024 thus far.
Tags: mubi, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Ratatouille
Review: I’ve been itching for a Ratatouille rewatch ever since I re-introduced The Incredibles to my all-time favorites list in 2023, and what better timing than to rewatch it as my final film of 2024? So wonderfully fun and intelligent and moving, leaving me on a curious, inspired, and film-loving, but pragmatic and open-minded note going into the new year, in… one minute. Why did Brad Bird ever leave animation? “Change is nature, dad. The part that we can influence. When we decide.”
Tags: disney+, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Flow
Review: I’m so glad I waited for theaters to see this, because what a vibrant and thrilling experience this was! The animation is incredibly impressive as expected, with insanely detailed and gorgeous settings and animal designs that strike a great balance between realistic and fantastical. The animals are also highly entertaining to watch, with all the right mannerisms and body language to make a good story without any dialogue. And I totally fell in love with the worldbuilding, easily my favorite aspect; there's clearly so much care put into every little detail of the animal world among the ruins of humanity, hugely inspiring and endearing. Half the movie is the animals in peril, and the way it maintained a beautiful look with the sheer excitement of the story is really impressive. On that story note, I’m still not 100% sure how to feel about the screenplay (although I am more on positive side). It’s a very plot-heavy movie with only a handful of times where the animals share intimate moments, which more accurately reflects how real animals would behave and the survive-or-meet-your-impending-doom nature of the situations they’re in, not allowing for much of touchy-feeliness that you might expect from a Disney movie. I appreciate that it doesn’t go overboard on the dramatic beats, although it was a bit alienating at times because of this as well. The movie certainly remains engaging the whole way through as you root for the animals, but there are moments where more character intimacy would’ve benefit the story and given me stronger emotional reactions, such as at the beginning where the story kicks in super quickly without a ton to go off of or really care for until later on. And at the end, where the finale does feel structured in a traditional story sense, I certainly felt some things, but not as much as if there was more breathing room in the pacing. Nonetheless, this is very good stuff overall. You could read the story as being biblical, about the environment, about displacement and adapting in survival situations, but the fact that the movie fully embraces the visual medium and doesn’t have any dialogue means you could easily make an argument for any of these. And it’s such an accessible film for that, too. I had a really good time watching this, and I would be surprised to hear of someone who didn’t. Side note - to add on to the note about accessibility, I was really happy to see just how full my theater was, both in terms of the quantity of people and how all the kids stayed quiet and engaged.
Tags: amc streets of woodfield 20, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: A Complete Unknown
Review: This accomplished what it set out to do, although it’s not something that ever really had me captivated. Still pretty good though. Chalamet and the ensemble are definitely really good in their roles, the time-traveling production design and attention to period detail are both excellent, and individual scenes are great; my personal favorite was the electric performance at Newport. It just felt too much to me like a movie made for longtime Dylan fans and never found a strong emotional grip. A generous 7/10, but a 7 nonetheless. (In case anyone asks, I do like Dylan’s music a lot from what I’ve heard, and my parents were raised during the peak of his popularity, so I wasn’t going in completely blind but I’m not an expert)
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Weekend
Review: Feeling surprisingly indifferent on first impression, despite how impressive and engulfing some of the scenes in this are (the one-take of the traffic jam at the start went insanely hard, and I thought the garbage collectors' monologue was interesting and well shot). I was able to follow all of what was being said and going on for a while, but there came a point when there was just too much happening and being said that I stopped caring and I numbly endured the rest. It's admirable in its Godardian audacity, with some gorgeous colors and stellar editing to boot, but ultimately too alienating and numbing for me to rate it right now. Maybe it's just my mood today, maybe this'll grow on me like his other films have.
Tags: criterion channel, criterion challenge 2025, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Wallace & Gromit: Vengeance Most Fowl
Review: As a lifelong Wallace & Gromit fan, I ABSOLUTELY APPROVE!! This was SO good!! These movies have always been about the good and bad sides of technology, and the way Nick Park tackles AI and smart technology as a whole here is simple but effective, as is the standard. I mean, these are movies for all ages after all, but it succeeds in doing this in a way that never feels like it's talking down to anyone; it's always in on the joke and stays true to who each of the characters have become over the last 35 years. As for the new things added, this feels exactly like the other Wallace & Gromit entries tonally (which I was hoping for, Nick Park’s still got it!), and the new characters fit that perfectly. The Norbots are both hilarious and creepy as they should be, and Mukherjee is an excellent addition to the story, never feeling like she overstayed her welcome or was overbearing. Mackintosh was a bit sillier and dumber here than he was in Curse of the Were-Rabbit so I wish he was written to be a bit more intelligent, but aside from that it still feels like the same entertaining character in a new story. It's absolutely hilarious in its physical and spoken gags as expected, I can't tell you how many times I laughed out loud. Also as with all the others, it draws from so much of the best action and comedy films. And with Feathers McGraw back in the picture, it draws a lot from the best spy and heist films as well, all in its own, singular Nick Park/Wallace & Gromit vision, and it doesn’t feel like one giant homage without any heart. On top of that, I am so impressed by how cinematic this felt. What stuck out most to me, just look at the fact that there are several dolly zooms in this... and this is a hand-crafted claymation movie! It's absolutely the most fine-tuned and well-crafted of the franchise as expected, and it accomplishes all this while still leaving all the fingerprints and textures that gives it an authentic, nostalgic feeling, like this could have easily been made at any point between A Matter of Loaf and Death and now. If there's one thing I will criticize, I do think that the movie feels just a little bit too reminiscent of The Wrong Trousers and A Close Shave plotwise. Whereas Were-Rabbit felt fresh and inventive storywise while still retaining the charm, I found this to be a bit predictable and nothing super amazing as far as the story structure goes. Still, all that being said, it remains so entertaining and hilarious that this only feels like a minor nitpick. All the beats hit just the same, from the meticulous buildup and revealing of the details of Feathers' plan, all the way up to the kickass final chase scene. And most of all, I'm willing to forgive this because THAT moment during the climax actually made me tear up(!), and I had a giant cheesy smile from then all the way until the end. Bravo, Nick Park and Merlin Crossingham for making a legacy sequel that fulfills longtime fan wishes with heavy emotions that doesn't feel forced, truly impressive. So yes, overall I am a huge fan of this movie and am elated that it lived up to the hype. I still remember watching Loaf and Death for the first time as soon as it released and waiting patiently these last 15 years for the follow-up. I especially became hesitant after they moved forward with the project following Peter Sallis' passing and after being underwhelmed by Dawn of the Nugget just last year. But with Nick Park and other original Aardman creatives back at the helm, there's nothing to worry about. My inner child is satisfied and delighted. Lastly, any other Aardman fans here who lost their shit at the few cameos from Were-Rabbit and Shaun the Sheep?? I felt like Marvel fan all over again.
Tags: netflix, baby's first apartment (year 2), cried or teared up

Name: Nickel Boys
Review:
Really great stuff. As experimental and avant-garde as I’ve been hearing, and somehow also as powerful as I’ve been hearing. The fact that this had a strong emotional effect on me despite how committed it is to this style and its various methods of visual storytelling, from the rare use of standard techniques to crosscutting between stock footage to the constant POV shots, genuinely is incredible.

There were a few moments where I felt the runtime, or that the visual style was a bit overpowering and took away a bit of the impact, but as I’ve sat on it I’ve been thinking a lot more about how confidently executed the vision is and how the slow buildup to the incredible final scenes is largely earned. Not even to mention the excellent ensemble, all of whom were clearly on the same page with how to deliver the intimacy and emotions their characters at the right moments.

Rating could go up very soon, I’m still letting it wash over me.

Name: The Tin Drum
Review:
An overwhelming journey that I certainly found myself lost in (with some suspension of disbelief) and changing with the characters, and I find it an admirable movie for that reason and how it was able to transport me. The second half struggled to grip me the same way the amazing first half did, and some of the movie's stranger story directions have me feeling unsure of a concrete summation of how I feel. But overall my reaction is positive and I do love a movie that manages to blend conflicting scenes into a story that moves me in the end. This was a very emotional ride.

Slightly generous 8/10.
Tags: criterion channel, baby's first apartment (year 2), criterion challenge 2025, virtual watch party, w/ cam, w/ skyler

Name: The Brutalist
Review:
This rewatch was outside the film festival, no Brady Corbet Q&A afterwards, I went in knowing what major story details to expect, and in the back of my mind I was curious what my family was thinking for their first watch. But despite all that, I’m still shocked by how differently I reacted to this. By the time it finished, I drew a blank on what I got out of it after the first watch… and I still can’t fully remember. But so it goes with long, dense movies like this; one watch you're so taken by the craft, the next you're so taken by the meat of the story, and by the third hopefully I'll be able to fully appreciate it altogether.

Even then, this is still a great movie and huge accomplishment. And the 3 hours 35 minutes felt even quicker this time! I had a lot of conflicting feelings on the way as the dots I’d connected in my head the last few months were ruptured as I experienced the parts of the story again that I had forgotten about, but my favorite part was how the epilogue so beautifully tied it all together. Again, I’m struggling to remember what exactly I got out of the epilogue the first time, but today I watched it in a completely new light and I think it’s my favorite part of the movie now.

***(Minor thematic spoilers)***

The way it reframes Toth’s work as immortalizing his personal history and carrying deeply emotional weight that isn’t ever discussed prior really took me by surprise and cut deep. It made me reflect on the prior 3 hours in a fully new light and a lot of Corbet’s creative decisions made more sense. It certainly helps that I believe immortalizing aspects of your personal history is the best and most meaningful aspect of creating art in any medium.

Holding off on a rating until I get my thoughts more straight.
Tags: music box theater, 70mm, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Killing Eleanor
Review:
One of my professors produced this! First time I've had to watch something a professor worked on for a class, but I honestly like that it was assigned. It's much more in line with the scale of film I'll likely be working on after I graduate.

No matter, I really enjoyed this. It's clear to me that whatever low budget this had was put to good use, as it's always visually appealing and concisely directed with some particularly notable editing, the performances are all great by a cast with impressive repertoires, and there's never really a dull moment, even if I don't agree with every single creative decision.

I wish the soundtrack was used more sparingly or that there was an original score since the songs used turned a couple big emotional scenes into schmaltz, and there are a couple sound and continuity errors that took me out (more nitpicks and not really much impact but still what I noticed).

Overall though, portrayals of addiction, mental health, and family hit me personally and never felt like it was mistreating the characters while still portraying them as deeply flawed humans all the while balancing both the comedy and drama effectively and sensitively, and I'm really impressed by how this was all accomplished on this scale.
Tags: crackle, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Annie Hall
Review: Really annoyingly wasn’t in the right mood for this (and once I got to a certain point I just wanted to finish my first watch) but still had fun going on this journey. I want to watch it again at some point because there’s definitely a lot I missed and/or wasn’t able to process in the moment.

Name: Kill Bill: Vol. 1
Review: "THIS... IS WHAT... YOU GET... FOR FUCKING... AROUND WITH… YAKUZAS!" Feels weird to say this was a mood booster today given what it is, but returning to and losing myself in old favorites always seems to get me back on my feet. Still so epic and emotionally gripping 4.5 years after my last watch. Every story beat in the first half hits, every action sequence (especially in Japan) is still breathtaking, and when it finished I still couldn't think about much else other than when I can rewatch Vol. 2. And my gosh this thing is just so visually insane, everything from the visceral O'Ren anime down to the gorgeous shot of The Bride catching her breath on a bench in the Japanese garden. There was a sense of fleeting after I finished this, both because I have to go back to normal life now and that this admittedly wasn't quite the masterpiece it was when I was 16 (I've changed as a person and film lover since then and I'm happy with it), but it always reminds me of how far you can go with the medium in a very specific way that Tarantino always does; he fucking loves movies and that infectious nature shines through in his work. It just gets the blood, excitement, and creativity rushing because it's so much fun, Jan! Two other things I noticed that I somehow didn't before: - Yuen Woo-Ping did the stunts in this. - Zoe Bell was Uma Thurman's stunt double.

Name: Blue Velvet
Review: A compelling cautionary tale about doing a favor for a family friend. (wanted to wait until a few days after Lynch passed to watch this as I normally would’ve otherwise, it’s what the movie deserved <3 also this is definitely the most moved I’ve been by this, will possibly bump the rating up soon)
Tags: hbo max, w/ claire s, w/ carmen, w/ jadyn, at claire/jadyn/izzy's place

Name: The Protagonists
Review:
Didn't make much noise for me, mostly admirable in the sense that this is Luca's silly little debut. For being a satire of true crime, which is a cool concept that is well executed in some areas, this ironically falls victim at other times to what can make documentaries in general annoying, when the director (Tilda) inserts themselves at random moments, and the way the story is told occasionally reveals too much too quickly before later moments where they would’ve hit harder, although it generally matches the structural style. I'm also kinda over movies that portray pent-up gayness as violence for shock value, different time period I guess.

Credit where credit is due, I was engaged mostly the whole time, the ensemble’s not bad, and there are echoes of Luca's future talent in the great lighting and blocking of the reenactment scenes and the interview with the fake wife. Maybe I'm being too nice with the rating, but that's how I usually am with directorial debuts.

Name: The Room Next Door
Review: First movie of 2025 to make me cry. Edit: actually it’s the second but this was really gorgeous and delicate and moving, all in spite of the occasionally rocky road to get there. Don’t want to write much more right now since I’m still reeling in the perfect final scene. Loved.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!, cried or teared up

Name: Under the Skin
Review: Holy shit… yeah I really haven’t seen much, if anything like this. Can’t think of a better word for this than entrancing. I rented a Blu-Ray of this and had the option to watch the making-of featurettes, but decided I didn’t want to learn how they did this yet because of how engulfing the world of this is. It’s horrifying and so confidently made in every regard which is so impressive given how insane this is. Still feeling conflicted by the ending and the loose story ends that aren’t tied up, as well as figuring out my personal connection (which does exist but not in a coherent form yet), but I do think this is pretty amazing overall.
Tags: blu-ray rental, video vortex, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Dog Man
Review: I mainly went to this because a free early screener is a free early screener but I’d be lying if I said I didn’t actually enjoy this. Not much you haven’t seen before in kids movies, but it’s well animated, clever and full of heart. Some really good gags and decent character work too, I had fun!
Tags: amc river east 21, early screener, w/ hazel

Name: I'm Still Here
Review: Really good! Great ensemble with an emotionally engaging family dynamic, really solid technical filmmaking, and a well-written, unassuming structure with mostly effective emotional beats that covers a lot more of the story than I initially expected. The way it covers several time periods put a lot of the themes into new perspectives and was very well executed. Surprisingly kinda similar to Seed of the Sacred Fig in the through-line between the political story elements and how they create family drama (not quite at the same level as Sacred Fig, but not many are) There were a few slower sections that didn’t hold my attention as well and it took me a bit of time to really get into the story, but once I was in I was locked in. And I wasn’t wowed by it like some other people were, but I was certainly moved and almost teared up a couple times. My favorite scenes were the prison sequence, the family tensions, and the end of the 1971 chapter. Slightly generous 8.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ cam, w/ alek, w/ peter

Name: Parasite
Review: “I won’t hire anyone unless they’re recommended by people I absolutely, positively trust.” By the time you get to the eighth watch of any movie, it loses a lot of surprise. On top of that, in the last two weeks, I’ve now read the screenplay twice for a class, including thoroughly breaking it down during that second time. So I REALLY felt like I knew what to expect. But with all that being said, no screenplay is ever perfectly relayed in the final film! Yeah, this obviously didn’t feel like my first watch 5 years ago from yesterday(!)… but somehow, on this eighth go around, there were still things in Parasite that surprised me. It’s easy to just say this was mainly because I had become familiar with the screenplay and noticed when Bong and/or the actors changed something on set, but there honestly were a few things that still would’ve surprised me had I not done any of this prior to watching. Still, it is ridiculously faithful. This remains one of the most confidently executed movies I’ve ever seen, a firing-on-all-cylinders if there ever was one. Things that stood out to me this time: - Choi Woo-shik (Kevin / Ki-woo) is a great comedic actor and Cho Yeo-jong (Mrs. Park / Yon-kyo) gave maybe my favorite performance this time around. - Kun-Sae’s line “RESPECT” that he shouts at Mr. Park’s poster then later at Mr. Park himself was not written in the screenplay. - There’s something so overwhelming about watching Kun-sae press the buttons and learning that the automatic lights aren’t actually automatic. It’s such an insanely good reveal and really washes you over with the terrifying feeling that you’re never truly in control of your life. - The scenes of Ki-tek driving Mr. Park are perfect and reveal more about the Park’s true family dynamic than anything else in the movie. In one of the scenes, there’s a particular shot that’s so immaculately framed and lit by the golden hour sun that I teared up a little. - Sound design plays a more important role than I’d previously thought and is also nearly spotless, between all the subtle uses of music and silences in between, the blunt sound effects of everyone whose heads gets bashed, the doorbell, and even small things like Kevin getting shocked by the water. - Reading the screenplay caused me to think about certain images and body + facial expressions that really stood out to me as vivid but are either quickly breezed by or not present in the final cut. Not that I’m holding it against the film, since it’s had such a profound impact on my life without them and I never wouldn’t noticed otherwise, although I am curious why Bong didn’t include them since I can’t imagine they would’ve broken the pace or added much more than 5 minutes to the final runtime.
Tags: criterion dvd, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: The Hangover
Review: Seeing Ken Jeong’s bush wasn’t on my bingo card for today but life is full of surprises. I think I like this more than Joker.
