Name: The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari
Review: Enjoyed this more than I expected after liking but not loving the original Nosferatu. Genuinely has a well-written and engaging story structure that has the right amount of plot twists to keep an interesting mystery going, I actually found myself caring about what was going on, and I really liked the production design a lot (as expected from this movement). Not a deeply emotional experience, but a really good movie. Would probably be an 8 had I watched this with more energy to focus.
Tags: hoopla, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Star Wars
Review: I finally saw A New Hope in theaters after nearly 15 years of being a Star Wars fan. No notes :)

Name: The Empire Strikes Back
Review: “R2, you know better than to trust a strange computer!” Watching again just 45 minutes after finishing the first really reminded me of what a brain switch this is, from the continuously ramping-up adventure and regular action of A New Hope to the much more character-focused, meditative storytelling of the sequel, especially given the movie begins on Hoth with a large-scale battle then slows to a crawl in its pacing. It usually takes me a bit of time to adjust and fully get on board (hence the 0.5 stars off, but I’m tempted to give it the bump given how much I’ve written), but once the plot starts thickening and details start getting revealed, I got progressively more and more locked in, just as the plot keeps ramping up as well, all the way up until the bombshell endings. And, of course, the constant spectacle always keeps things interesting without being overbearing. Cloud City is one of my favorite locations in the Star Wars universe, and seeing it on the big screen reminded me of how insanely well put together the set pieces are and what it represents in the story. Even as a kid, it was always a shock seeing such a utopian, beautiful city suddenly turn on the characters, and that feeling was replicated again tonight with great excitement from my 21 year old self. On a semi-related note to all the above, I think my favorite new thing I picked up on, which no doubt has come with more age and experience in film, is that this is such an insane G&E achievement; the lighting schemes and color grades in this are so good and have out-aged most, if not all CGI. It looked even more impressive on the big screen of course, but one moment in particular is when Luke enters the carbon-freezing chamber and the entire thing just lights up as Vader’s beckoning voice starts booming. Such an unbelievable moment. I’m not sure if I’m just really late to the game here, but I remember there being a lot more one-sided banter between Han and Leia before their first kiss. Maybe it’s also with age, but I got the impression this time that Han and Leia had maybe kissed or even had a fling prior to the movie’s start. I mean, there’s a full year in between the two movies, and I can’t imagine Han would be putting in THAT much effort unless it had already happened once before. I’ll have to let this one sit before I have a more concrete stance. Last thing, a very oddly specific thing to praise, but the way The Imperial March is used over the end credits is absolutely perfect. It helps that I have such nostalgia for the song, but it has such a beckoning, badass presence following the beckoning, badass ending as the audience leaves, as to remind everyone that the Empire won this round and has full capability to win in the end. Definitely the best reminder I’ve had in a while about the power of music in film. That sums it up well. This was never my favorite Star Wars film growing up, but I’ve really come to appreciate it more over the years with time and life lived. I still think I prefer its predecessor, but the competition has become much more stiff. Time to get sat for Return of the Jedi!
Tags: tivoli theater, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Return of the Jedi
Review:
Marathon complete, and I’m so fucking happy I was able to make it. Such a huge bucket list thing for me has officially been crossed off, and I had so much fun doing it. Will be cherishing this day for a very long time :)

Not many thoughts on the movie, other than it’s still awesome and emotional despite the corniness and CGI sticking out to me a bit more than it did the last time. Marquand, Lucas, and everyone creatively involved put their entire nutsacks into everything with Vader, Luke, and the Emperor, those are so easily my favorite sequences in this. The production design, the score, the lightsaber duel, Vader’s arc, Vader’s death scene still being I think the only scene in this franchise to make me tear up… so good.

Having seen this in theaters a couple years prior, there wasn’t as much surprise with the experience as the first two, but it more than succeeded at immersing me and reminding me why this whole experience was worth the time and energy.
Tags: tivoli theater, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Possession
Review: Super well made and so layered without feeling too overbearing, and also a lot to take in after a full week of me nearly always being busy and having not slept very well the last few nights. It allowed me to just take the movie as it was without thinking too hard which was cool, but also I started getting drowsy at the end and need time to think about this. Insane movie. Darling, I by Tyler is a fitting song to follow this up with, no? (I saw Tyler live a couple days ago so that's probably why)
Tags: hoopla, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: No Other Land
Review:
Completely devastating but completely essential. Even as someone who’s kept themselves informed of what’s been happening in Palestine over the last year and few months, there were still moments in this that were shocking and shed new light on the subject.

I cannot rate or rank the realities of human suffering so soon after watching, but know that I think everyone should see this when they get the chance, whether in theaters or however else possible. Reviews that state how vital this movie is right now have not been exaggerating at all.

To quote the review of a mutual… if you aren’t enraged, you aren’t paying enough attention. Free Palestine.

Name: Mickey 17
Review:
I’ll need a second watch to process everything (not a complaint at all), but AAAH what fantastic FUN this was! Leave it to Bong to create a completely immersive sci-fi adventure that balances the thrill of classic blockbusters, from an expectedly amazing blend of comedic and dramatic beats to a genuinely unpredictable story with a real sense of curiosity, immaculately with social commentary without either one overpowering the other.

The worldbuilding is wondrous, the cinematography is delectable, Jung Jae-il is back with another excellent score of classical music, the ensemble is so very entertaining and fully committed to the gonzo vision across the board, and what is EASILY the best Wilhelm Scream in at least a decade is icing on the cake. What Robert Pattinson and his titular character bring to the screen is not only absolutely hilarious, but the range of emotion hits so many notes on identity, mortality, and empathy that this is an instant contender for my favorite performance of his. This is such an insanely polished filmmaking feat that is going to be super hard to beat for anything coming out later in the year!

I’m a bit more conflicted than I was hoping on how the commentary is delivered in certain ways and the ending ties up some ends in ways that I’m not sure about, but much of what is here on the subjects of environmentalism and worth of the individual is still very moving and right where it should be for big budget Hollywood. And the sheer joy of witnessing a genuinely great, creative, singular new blockbuster in 2025 is really going to stick with me.

A VERY high 8 that I might bump up in the morning, LOVED!
Tags: amc newcity 14, early screener, w/ claire s

Name: Memoir of a Snail
Review:
Even going in knowing what to expect, having not stopped thinking about the film since I first saw it, I still cried twice. There was a little less surprise, but the animation and creativity on screen are still so phenomenal and the screenplay and story have such an unparalleled appreciation for life and all the small details and quirks that add so much more to it than they ever get credit for... sorta like the process of making an animated film.

In the moment, some of the critiques that it leans too heavily into the miserablism crossed my mind, but the emotional journey and progression of Gracie’s story render those critiques completely moot for me and all the positives reign far supreme. Phenomenal and beautiful, might just bump it back up to full marks since this was such a special experience.

Second time with an Adam Elliot Q&A as well :)
Tags: college theater, me time!, q&a!, cried or teared up

Name: Uncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past Lives
Review:
Hmm

First Apichatpong that didn’t really move me in any particular way to be honest, and I’ve liked or loved everything else I’ve seen from him. I admired parts of the story, and there are some beautiful shots, but it just didn’t culminate into anything that got a reaction out of me, at least on my initial reaction.

I will say, in my experience, that Apichatpong’s movies are movies where the best part of the experience is after you’ve watched them, when they start to resonate with you some time later. So I’m open to the idea of this growing on me, although I was still more moved in the moment by his others I’ve seen. Disappointing :/
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: The Big Gundown
Review:
I read in the synopsis that there was a train but turns out there aren’t actually any on-screen trains :/ still really good though.

Loved the editing, framing, and the way the mystery unfolds. Cuchillo is a great character, love how Tomas Millian performed him. And I know it’s obvious to say but I love a western that makes me go “damn imagine doing all this without modern technology.” Some really fire gun and knife play as well. Probably would’ve loved it more if I was more awake (not the movie’s fault)

Name: Lawrence of Arabia
Review: “You saved him, and you killed him. That was written.“ I’m already turning this over so much in my head and this is the most I’ve been astounded by a film’s craft in quite some time. Peter O’Toole’s performance is legitimately as incredible as I’ve heard, as are the mindblowing cinematography and editing. The story’s structure and several storylines are certainly a lot to take in, but somehow it still comes together to sweep the rug out from under your unassuming body in a coherent, moving fashion, amazing and timeless. And seeing it in theaters for my first watch was otherworldly, a true experience to be cherished and one of those theater trips that somehow all came together as I’d hoped. Just an awesome night all around, all of it reminding me that I’m exactly where I should be :)

Name: Vertigo
Review:
So I’ve said enough on here that I have ADHD severe enough that it really affects my movie-watching at times. My previous two watches of this were good to varying degrees, but I couldn’t focus enough that I had a great grasp on everything. Tonight, the stars aligned and I was finally able to focus on this the entire time without any major distraction! I would’ve been happy with this at home, but getting this experience in theaters was just fantastic.

All that being said, I’m definitely still processing since this is the most personally affected I’ve been by the movie! This was one of those rewatches where, now that I was able to fully lock in, the story was re-contextualized and hit me in such different ways that I’ve honestly blanked on what I got from it the last time I watched. And I’m thinking about things from my own life just as much as I am about the movie itself, I was honestly a little stressed walking out.

But all that’s to say/re-iterate that this is a great movie that just gets better with each watch. The cinematography and production design are to die for, Jimmy Stewart and Kim Novak give wonderful performances, Hermann’s score is beautiful, the end is devastating, and the story progression and use of motifs are so amazing and hit all the right notes of discomfort, humor, mystery, and stress without ever being disinteresting, I mean do I need to say much more?? Maybe my favorite Hitchcock now, definitely top 3. I love it.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Great Silence
Review: Sort of a bummer!! Gorgeous filmmaking and great performances with a very nice Morricone score (that I honestly would’ve liked more of) of course. I’m definitely not the first person to say this, but this is unfortunately still fairly relevant as a US resident in the year of our lord 2025 and the emotional experience hits very close to home, so I admittedly am not fully sure of how I feel just yet other than this is a really good, well made movie. You genuinely do feel the ruthlessness of how lawless this world is. Great theater experience too. Edit - holy shit this was my 600th in theaters let's go

Name: Temporary
Review: My latest film is OUT NOW!! Click the links below and enjoy! YouTube Vimeo

Name: CLICKBAIT
Review: One of my favorite DePaul films I’ve seen. Can’t believe that Jackson was able to get a full cast and crew on board with a story dealing with this niche of a corner of the internet, one that hit me surprisingly personally, and it’s just a blast to watch. Can’t wait for it to release online, I loved it!!
Tags: college theater, me time!, q&a!, early screener

Name: A Different Man
Review: I've been wanting to see this for months now, both because of how intriguing the movie is and the fact that it was produced by Killer Films (one of my dream companies to work for), but unfortunately the theatrical release was both very short and at a time when I was increasingly busy and stressed with school. I definitely would've preferred the theater experience to the 6 ad breaks on Max, but I'm glad to have finally seen it, and the movie is engrossing and memorable enough that I was left thinking about it through the ads. The emotional resonance I felt from how this portrays regret, repressed self-hatred, and how jealousy can reveal itself in harmful ways made this pretty difficult for me at times. And because of the stress I felt and connections I made while watching, I honestly didn't find it very funny, although I understand why so many others do. I really love the concept and how the story is told in the first half, and the second half is just as confident and bizarre but a bit more conflicting. In the moment, I wished that the self-hatred and jealousy threads weren't revealed until later on, and it felt a little like an endurance run for how long Guy / Edward would put up with everything without the story saying a whole lot more, although I'm already thinking about how the story progresses and keeps these characters connected in a mostly unpredictable fashion; it certainly carries itself until the end. Overall though, this is a really well made, confident, and powerful movie. The cinematography is great (love the decision to shoot on 16mm, it adds an odd sort of desolate emotional texture to the visual style), the editing is awesome, all the performances are great (Adam Pearson MVP for me), I love how the music is used (oddly reminded me of how May December uses music), and there are just so many stark images. Still thinking about exactly how I feel, but I'm glad this exists. Slightly generous 8

Name: Germany, Year Zero
Review: I obviously wasn’t expecting a movie about post-war Berlin to be happy, but good lord this really got upsetting as it went on. Despite the obvious future innovations in film that date this, a lot of the emotional impact is still timeless. I’m still thinking about some story elements and figuring out my personal connection to the story, but the filmmaking is really good of course. I haven’t been acquainted with Italian Neorealism in a while, but I forgot how powerful these movies can be in all their non-superficiality and lack of spectacle, even in scenes that would be sensationalized in other movies (the final scene and the chase in the train station comes to mind). Not even to mention the fact that this literally takes place in Berlin just 2 years after the fall of the Reich; it’s an artifact. And this is a classic where I could really see its influence on future movies, from those that take place in WWII (The Tin Drum, The Pianist, etc.) to movies from future significant movements (The 400 Blows, Satantango, etc.). Pretty great stuff.
Tags: criterion channel, for class, in a school lounge

Name: Once Upon a Time in the West
Review:
I remember close to nothing from my first watch back in 2020 so tonight was basically first watch #2 of this.

To be honest, I wasn’t as captivated as I was hoping in the moment compared to how amazing a lot of people think this is, and it is a long movie to be feeling that way about, but it’s come together more as I’ve thought about it on the ride home. The cinematography and editing are amazing, some of these shots and scenes (especially the final duel) on the big screen were just chilling. And I like how there aren’t any obvious heroes or villains in the story, with a good amount of interesting standalone character subplots that push the story along little by little… it unfortunately just falls into the category of admired more than enjoyed. But my admiration here runs pretty deep. Maybe it’ll grow on me in the future. I had the harmonica and piano theme in my head on the ride home.
Tags: music box theater, w/ esther, w/ robin, w/ peter

Name: Good Bye, Lenin!
Review: Wasn't fully sure what to expect going in, since the preview I was shown in class led me to believe this was a more typical comedy (probably to appeal to more American audiences in 2003). This definitely is a comedy at its core, but it turns out this is really also a lovely ode to both the absurd lengths we sometimes go to for family and how we struggle to adapt to life-altering change. Watching this taught me a surprising amount of Germany's history from the 80s to the reunification, and the balance that had with thoroughly engaging me and entertaining me at the same time is really impressive. The filmmaking is tight, the performances are great, the screenplay is smart, fast, and unpredictable, and it left me with a surprisingly strong bittersweet feeling by the end. A great time all around, and a wonderful palate cleanser after yesterday's much darker (but still great) classic German watch. I've still got a wide smile thinking about the movie as I write this. Can't wait to actually go to Berlin in a couple weeks! :) Oh and shoutout Yann Tiersen for re-using Comptine d'un autre été (theme from Amelie) in a different context that actually works lmao, was very surprised to hear it at first but slowly realized it fit very well
Tags: alexander street, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: A Woman Is a Woman
Review: I’m still catching up from getting almost no sleep a couple nights ago, and mid-afternoon drowsiness hit me like 15 minutes into this and didn’t really let up until the end (the comfy, dark theater didn't help)… the movie was interesting from what I saw, with some good moments here and there, although I definitely wasn’t awake enough to feel confident in an opinion. Really annoying since I was actually looking forward to this.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ alek, w/ cam

Name: Big Deal on Madonna Street
Review: Really fun! A chain is only as strong as its weakest link, and when they're all the weakest link, it makes for a good comedy. Everything before the big finale is solid enough, if a little forgettable and slow at times despite some good scenes, but that big finale is genuinely hilarious. Solidly crafted, good ensemble, very enjoyable all around. Can't believe this was co-written by the same woman who co-wrote Bicycle Thieves. My first movie in 5 days that I stayed awake the whole way through watching, nature is healing! Rating could definitely go up.

Name: The Electric State
Review: TL;DR - ...blugh I'll be the first to admit I have an unwillingness to shit on movies where I can avoid it. I respect the fact that humans who were in the same shoes as me at some point are working on a big budget sci-fi action movie, a genre where so many people first found their film passion, and I want to acknowledge that. But in an increasingly high number of big-budget Hollywood cases, this being no exception, it's just as soul-crushing to witness firsthand that the 13TH HIGHEST BUDGET in cinema history is, with very little surprise, a largely boring and predictable ride that really doesn't have anything new to say. Honestly, just watch the first two Terminator movies again. I could write a laundry list of things I noticed during the movie, but my biggest takeaway is that this is a great example of a movie lacking in human spirit. There's not a single line of dialogue that felt like it wasn't just copied and pasted from a similar scene in something else, there's so little (if any??) personality. I hate comparing it to the process of AI-formed answers, just entirely gathering it from previous material, but come on... I was reminded of the adage that you can't just watch a lot of movies to make something good, that you have to live life and gain experiences to really bring something to the table. That's a fairly common saying in film, but I REALLY felt it here as the first bad example I've seen in a long time, like the screenwriters literally just watch movies and take online classes about 3-act structure and basic screenplay format in their basements. The two main positive things I will say is that it honestly doesn't look that bad visually thanks to the hard workers at ILM, and some of the actors feel like they're doing their best with the material. It's at least a competent story that isn't hard or annoying to follow, doing the bare minimum which places it above The Flash. It wasn't offensively bad in the moment, but the fact that it all contributes towards a flavorless $320 million vision is arguably an offensively bad oversight. I miss when the Russos did Community, and the only thing I'll be excited for from them is if they direct the Community movie... but I also fear they've lost that touch, and I care so much about the show that actually I take that back. Maybe I'm being too harsh and I just needed to get it out of my system, like will I think it's THAT bad in the morning? But I'll keeping the rating where it is for now.
Tags: amc river east 21, early screener, me time!

Name: Olympia Part One: Festival of the Nations
Review: The experience of watching the film can be summed up like this: The opening 17 minutes contain incredibly striking cinematography and editing, far more impressive than what I was expecting. I've been told so much about how Leni Riefenstahl innovated editing, but I was shocked by how gorgeous many of the images are as well. However, at roughly the 17 minute mark, there are close-ups of a Nazi flag and Hitler's face, reminding you that this was unfortunately produced for and under the Nazi regime. A fascinating watch from a historical perspective, given both the obvious significance of the subject matter and how Leni's direction innovated the look of several movies to come (I thought of The Seventh Seal and Beau Travail in those opening minutes), as well as live TV sports broadcasts before TV broadcasting was even a thing. The Jesse Owens footage was dope.
Tags: criterion channel, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Olympia Part Two: Festival of Beauty
Review: What a towering achievement in documentary filmmaking this is (the whole entity, both parts 1 and 2). Even if I didn’t get an all-timer level emotional connection, the craft and history behind it all is so fascinating to me and made this an engrossing combined 4 hour watch. Documentary filmmaking, live TV, sports photography, and even narrative filmmaking really wouldn’t be the same without this. In particular, I’m kinda obsessed with how Leni Riefenstahl shoots the human body here, especially the male athletes. Very curious to look more into the female gaze in film since I seriously noticed it here. With a few days between watching Part One and now this, I’ve thought more about what I wrote initially and how these films were in fact commissioned by the Nazis, but they certainly aren’t propaganda. After Part One, I read a review on this with a sentence that stuck with me; where Triumph of the Will makes a heroic spectacle out of Adolf Hitler, Olympia makes a heroic spectacle out of the bodies of the athletes. Leni and her film(s) portray every participating country and their athletes with the dignity and visual splendor they deserve, regardless of what country they’re from. It is important to engage with historical media in this context, of course, but I see no reason why this celebration of sports and the human body shouldn’t be praised for the filmmaking achievement it is, despite how it came to be. Rating applies to both parts as one complete film.
Tags: criterion channel, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Janet Planet
Review:
Really liked this a lot and it’s only growing on me, although maybe not the best time to watch this on an overstimulating plane to Europe. Really great character work and beautiful long takes that really elevate the story; seeing this story told from mainly the perspective of a kid added a great touch of personality to help it stand out and connect with me in unique ways. Wonderful overall.

I read a play by Annie Baker (The Flick) for a class a couple weeks ago and really enjoyed it. One of the most distinctive qualities about it was just how many pauses she wrote into it, and I was surprised to see she wrote and directed the same amount of pauses into this as well. There’s a lot of breathing room in every convo and I really liked that about it.

Name: Wings of Desire
Review: Great rewatch, so awesome to be watching a movie that takes place so prominently in Berlin IN THEATERS IN BERLIN! Even despite a few points when I wasn’t as into the story, the moments that worked for me hit so well and rang so true to life that I’m thinking about those moments a lot more. The way it uses inner dialogue feels so organic and honest, it might even outdo the regular dialogue?? Gorgeous cinematography too. Keeping the rating here for now since not everything worked for me, but lowkey the movie’s got me looking up at the sky as I walk and comparing it to the Chicago sky so it might go up.
Tags: babylon berlin, in germany, w/ carmen

Name: Monty Python and the Holy Grail
Review:
Lowkey didn’t make me laugh as much as it did in the past, but still really good and a lot of bits still hold up. Maybe I need to wait a few years to watch again so it feels a little more fresh, but it is already growing on me again, rating could go back up.

Sobbed my eyes out laughing at the opening credits, genuinely might be the funniest scene in any movie I’ve seen and it’s not even a scene technically. The commitment is still super impressive in every scene and elevates a lot of it, even if some of the bits don’t hit like they used to. I like the implication that all of Britain is just roleplaying medieval times. Love the blending of animation and the modern timeline.
Tags: babylon berlin, w/ kel, w/ lena, w/ allen, w/ lily, w/ maddie c, in germany

Name: Groundhog Day
Review: Great studio comedy and a very good plane watch, although I hadn’t seen this in 7 years so maybe the plane was too distracting a setting. Conceptually this is fantastic and executes its ideas in a ton of intelligent ways, although I do wonder what other endings were considered; this is such a specific and unpredictable story that there had to have been a ton of possibilities, but I suppose it makes sense they ended it in a pretty typical way for this genre, and it’s emotionally satisfying enough even if a bit standard for my liking. Shoutout Illinois film!!!

Name: Black Bag
Review:
God, I miss Europe already…

Pretty great stuff all around here. Really cool to me that Soderbergh is able to direct AND be DP on AND edit his own independently-produced thrillers with large casts, and this proved that he’s worthy of all of it. Not the most innovative or risk-taking affair or one that left me feeling like a changed person, but it’s so sturdily and confidently made in a way that it’s really engrossing and entertaining in and of itself; the kind of movie that rebukes a claim like “they don’t make this kind of movie anymore.” Not a bad scene in it with fully realized classical filmmaking!

Definitely my favorite score and probably my favorite editing of the year so far as well, and I’ve only seen four 2025 releases (not as many as I’d like at this point but life is life and I’ve been doing some awesome shit), though it’ll be difficult to overtake those titles. Love it when I spend money on a Good Movie in theaters.

Name: Shadows
Review: Squint your eyes and tell me Lelia Goldoni doesn't look at least a little like Mikey Madison. I wish I watched this more awake so I could've been a little more present, but this was still a really good revisit. I only remembered a few stills so it felt really new anyway. Didn't impress me *quite* as much 2.5 years later, now that I'm much more well versed in Cassavetes, but it was still interesting to watch in retrospect and see where he began. Like any good Cassavetes, a lot of this feels uncomfortably real and relatable, especially the sequence in the outdoor museum and a lot of the relationship discussions, although this being an improvised, mostly plotless story kept it from having the same emotional through line that the other three (especially Faces) had for me. Still really well made and groundbreaking overall; awesome that this is really where American indie cinema began.

Name: Brief Encounter
Review: First watches suck ass when you’re able to follow the story but too drowsy to get emotionally invested or feel any of it beyond being able to tell what’s going on.

Name: Mannerisms
Review: Haha Mann-erisms... cuz it's a heist film Good job to all involved! That shot of the mallard amidst all the chaos was my favorite part, immaculately placed.

Name: Get Out
Review:
Chris breaking free and going on his rampage really is one of the most satisfying finales of any horror movie (or maybe any movie in general) from the last 10 years.

I haven't read the screenplay yet (although I really want to now), but from a producing standpoint, it's amazing how well the vision was executed with all its intricacies and minute details in every scene, especially for a debut. Such an excellent, fine-tuned film in all regards that still surprised me and had a lot of moments where I went "Ohhhh right," even on my third watch.
Tags: blu-ray rental, video vortex, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Sinners
Review:
Ryan Coogler’s first original movie since making it big in the studios is really entertaining and really well made! It’s been a while since we’ve seen a vampire action movie out of Hollywood, and a high standard is set here.

An awesome-looking movie from the production design to the copious practical effects to the 70mm cinematography, a great ensemble including Michael B. Jordan playing two characters and the amazing newcomer Miles Caton, and an amazing new score from Ludwig Goransson my beloved (who I MET AFTER!!), among a lot else. The main action centerpiece is pretty awesome too with its heavy Raimi influence, even if it wrapped itself up too cleanly and quickly to have a bigger punch. There’s never a slow moment and always something to appreciate.

I admit I didn’t find as strong an emotional connection to the characters as I think the movie wanted me to, as I admired the major beats on a technical story level more than I was moved by them, but the character work is very solid and supported well by the rock solid story structure. I’m also a bit unsure about what the vampires represent, as there seem to be a few very different threads without tight ends, but hopefully with time it’ll make more sense. I did really like how music was interwoven into the story and how it represents unity in several circumstances; there’s one shot in particular that I won’t spoil, but startlingly ties it all together very well.

Will the $90 million pay off? An R-rated period vampire movie is definitely a risky gamble, but I love that the risk was taken. Really hoping it gets a nice breadth of audiences when it goes wide, go support this one!
Tags: regal city north, w/ hazel, q&a!, early screener, imax

Name: Slacker
Review: This story was based on fact. Any similarity with fictional events or characters is entirely coincidental. Dangerously good propaganda for movies comprised entirely of conversations, as is any great early Linklater film. I got so enamored after a certain point, then some scenes started reminding me of old memories of home that quickly turned into screenplay ideas. Thinking about how I'd write them ended up distracting me a little bit from the remainder of the film, that and I slowly started to feel my social battery drain as I was watching (a credit to how real these conversations feel), but I love how confident and entertaining this film is all the way through. So many young aspiring filmmakers (myself included) don't feel ready to make a feature yet, but it is undoubtedly nice in a way to be reminded again that all Linklater needed was some bodies for actors, a cameraperson, a sound person, an art director, a band of grips, and a dream.
Tags: criterion channel, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Drop
Review: Trap if it was a how-do-you-do-fellow-kids high concept directed by someone who does a bunch of random, out of place camerawork and editing “just because it looks cool” (but it's annoying): This already had the worst trailer I’ve seen all year which set expectations low, though there actually were a couple individual moments, either tense or comedic, where it had me convinced that the humans behind this actually executed an okay vision. These and the admittedly cool graphic designs for the texts are the saving grace. But in the end, they all just feel out of place and muddled. If other individual scenes work better on paper, I wouldn't know since the movie never felt like it was committing to a tone for a long enough time at any point to actually get any sort of connection or lasting impression out of me. I don't think almost every line of dialogue being forgettable or copied + pasted was helping either. Even if Trap was laughably dumb at points, at least Shyamalan was consistent enough with his vision and able to direct a few individual standout moments that felt in line with the story. Drop isn’t offensively bad (actually a couple scenes might be), but it is just regularly annoying and boring, even despite its sometimes good tension and couple of interesting moments. Oh and another half star off because it’s a contender for all-time worst Chicago representation in how unconvincing it is. I’m obviously biased, but there are shots in this where they literally just CGI’d a single iconic Chicago skyscraper against some other downtown city that very obviously wasn’t the rest of the Loop; it’s kinda hilarious in retrospect. Not even to mention the worst LED wall I’ve seen in a movie, and lmfao at the fact that she drove from the Loop to her house in either North or West side in like 2 minutes, coincidentally JUST in time to save her child. Gimme a break…
Tags: amc river east 21, early screener, me time!

Name: Saint Frances
Review: “I can’t wait for next summer...” Another Illinois film W. Sometimes a heartfelt indie dramedy with great characters is just what you need in the moment. This is just what I needed today. If I had a nickel for every time I watched an indie film about a struggling young adult woman who finds new direction in life by bonding with a woman at a very different age, I'd have three nickels now. Very interesting trend between this, Starlet, and Killing Eleanor, but I think this is my favorite of those three.

Name: Meditation, Creativity, Peace
Review: “Wise is he who enjoys the show offered by the world.” Definitely not the kind of thing I had expected to see in theaters, but an hour and 10 minutes of hearing David Lynch talk about everything the title suggests on the big screen was a really cool and meaningful experience for me, super well worth my time. Watching this and then going straight to therapy worked out so nicely, haha. I’ve always been someone who loves to hear the artists I admire talk about their work and offer snippets of advice and wisdom (taking it with a grain of salt of course), and even if this got a tad repetitive in what Lynch talked about in the clips the editors chose to use, any Lynch fan knows that he’s got a ton of gems to offer. The best moments of this were as refreshing and enlightening as I was hoping; sweeter than donuts, as he’d say. The screening was preceded by a few of his short films: Memory Film, Pierre and Sonny Jim, and Dream #7 (as it’s titled on Letterboxd)
Tags: music box theater, me time!, david lynch retrospective

Name: The Elephant Man
Review:
Between the abrupt final beat and how I had been lead to believe by many that this was going to make me cry and it didn’t, I didn’t get the impact I was expecting and I’m still processing what I ended up getting. But, no matter, that’s not really a complaint; I still really liked this. Two great performances, a lot of really wonderful individual scenes (especially between John and Frederick), and some of Lynch’s best cinematography, like oh my GOD there isn’t a remotely bad shot in this; some scenes just look completely unreal.

I’m still thinking about how Lynch portrayed the more insidious motives behind why people wanted to know John and how far certain scenes went, but on first impression I’m definitely leaning towards a more positive response. Not my favorite Lynch or a movie that really floored me, but definitely a really good one. Feeling very lucky that I got to see this in theaters for my first time.
Tags: music box theater, w/ claire s, david lynch retrospective

Name: Habit
Review:
I admire the swings here as a micro-budget feature based on a film school thesis, and I was genuinely impressed by the first half of this. A horror film that starts as a quirky, messy Cassavetes-and-French-inspired dramedy. I was hooked, there was personality to the conversations and it was well shot and edited.

In hindsight, though, I’ve realized that I don’t actually know any more about these characters than where they work and who they’re fucking, and the horror element was really unintentionally(?) silly; the screenplay really dropped the ball once the genre was introduced in the neo-realist mix. Combine that with the runtime that felt long, this was alright with all things considered, but I was a little disappointed that it ended up kinda boring and frustrating by the end.

Rating might go down, but there’s just enough here to respect that I’ll keep it where it is.

Name: Warfare
Review: Really well made; the tension is incredibly prevalent from the moment the first major explosion happens all the way until the end, the performances are all pretty fantastic, and the verite style mostly worked well for me (aside from some distracting cuts). I also like how the story began with the brotherly, happy Call On Me scene and only got worse from there, it worked in really allowing the impact of how bad war eventually gets to sink in. Maybe not the most novel approach to the genre ever, but it’s airtight and incredibly solid filmmaking, not hard to lose yourself in at all and feeling shorter than it actually is. All that being said, though, that’s kinda where it ends, thematically and emotionally. Alex Garland really succeeds with feel-bad war movies, even if they don’t say much beyond the extents of how graphic and stressful war can get. This at least excuses itself more of having to make a larger statement than Civil War did, in its pursuit of being a real-time, historically-accurate movie about this specific attack. Only time will tell how much I’ll be thinking about this months out from now, but I felt enough for the injured soldiers in the moment and walked out of the theater feeling uneasy and on edge, which definitely speaks to its immediate effectiveness. I’m glad I was able to catch this in theaters. Also — I wouldn’t call this propaganda or pro-war, but the inclusion of the final few minutes took away from the tickle of how brutal everything preceding it was. Was working very well at showing the horrors of war, then cuts to a montage showing one of the real soldiers visiting the set accompanied by BTS footage, giving it a slightly preachy final note. There’s a very good reason why montages like that are saved for the special features and not part of the actual movie; I would’ve much rather the movie just ended at the actual end of the story. A strong 7.

Name: Julien Donkey-Boy
Review: Not every decision in this worked for me, but as per usual with Harmony, I did like the execution of this very weird concept overall and there were some really impactful individual moments (the slapping scene in particular hit me very emotionally in a similar distressing way to Cassavetes). Really great performances from the three leads, a fascinating tone that really challenges whether or not you should be laughing at a lot of points, and a good number of very striking compositions even despite the low quality video, wild color grading, and Dogme style. And one of my favorite aspects to Harmony's work is how he dives into the communities of his leads and explores the environment they live in, and I loved how all the side characters were written and performed in this. Definitely a movie where individual moments really got a reaction out of me more than the progression of the entire story, although I'm still processing. What the movie says about talent and potential is something I want to ponder more. Not up to Kids, Spring Breakers, or Gummo for me, but I'd rank it fourth after those. Interesting, good stuff. A distressing watch on 4/20 for sure, but not one that I regret.
Tags: criterion channel, virtual watch party, w/ matt o, baby's first apartment (year 2), w/ annalise

Name: Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace
Review: Despite all its flaws, I still enjoy this overall with each rewatch. I think this has more sauce than some people realize, and I’m biased just since it’s Star Wars, but I think everything on Tatooine is pretty fun and of course the final set of battles is all good and thrilling; it actually does capture some of that original feeling even if it isn’t as consistent or well made. The big thing that stuck out to me this time though is just how dull the first 30 minutes and some of the Coruscant stuff are. It’s mostly just exposition with these two, and it feels like George Lucas was just as bored with some of this as we are. The lesser effort does dampen the fun of the experience, but even then… it does improve at it goes and the good bits make up for it, all things considered. Someone please get my man Jar Jar an ADHD diagnosis and some Adderall. Can’t believe Revenge of the Sith is almost back in theaters, I’m so excited!

Name: The Legend of Ochi
Review: I dug this, it was cute! I thought it looked pretty great visually (love how it blends Romania’s natural beauty with the more fantastical elements), the original score is surprisingly awesome, the ensemble was clearly having fun, and I was entertained. There are echoes of Wes Anderson, E.T., Daniels, and more, but it still feels pretty singular which is definitely the mark of a successful debut. The tone and emotional beats of this are pretty off-kilter, sometimes in surprisingly effective ways and sometimes in ways that left my head scratching (notably the ending), but it has an endearing quality to it all throughout that more than won me over. Hopefully I’m not alone on this when it goes wide this weekend. Strong 7!

Name: Star Wars: Episode II – Attack of the Clones
Review: Okay yeah. Despite its flaws, I do like this movie. Long review incoming! The biggest new thing I got from this rewatch is realizing that I think Anakin has a genuinely emotionally engaging arc. I noticed this time around especially the discussions about how strict life as a Jedi is, and how he has no freedom to do basically anything outside of this. If there's one thing I appreciate more about The Phantom Menace on this rewatch, it's how it shows Anakin as a genuinely talented and passionate kid, with his abilities to fix anything electronic and to build and win a damn podrace as a 9-year-old. When he gets taken away to start his Jedi training, I think it's safe to assume that he completely abandoned all of that, despite how much he clearly loved it. As soon as he arrives back to Padme after retrieving his mother's body and killing all the Tusken Raiders, the first thing he does is return to fixing electronics; I think this reminds him of his roots and makes him feel like an actual human being in a way that being a Jedi never does. He has no time for anything else he loves outside of this, and the constant pressure and back-breaking work to become a Jedi Knight manifests in anger and violence (and lines of dialogue about how dictatorships might not be so bad after all). These manifestations very likely could’ve been prevented at some point in the 10 years between movies, were the Jedi code not so damn strict. All that being said, as much as I've come to appreciate Anakin's arc here on its own, I still do think that the movie's shortcomings do undermine this overall. Of course, Padme has to fall in love with Anakin by the end of the movie for the story to connect how George Lucas planned it to be, but it still does feel like he really phoned it in here and did Padme dirty. He really played up Anakin as a sexless, nervous Jedi-in-training with how much he stumbles and sometimes comes across as creepy when he tries to flirt with Padme, and it feels like she was believably uncomfortable for a while. By the time Padme admits her feelings, it feels hollow and the only explanation for a sane human in this situation is that she just settled (and enabled his red flags in the process), perhaps because of how equally sexless the politicians in her life are (even if that's not actually the case). At least Anakin is making some effort, lmfao Before then, though, I have to credit George for cutting between the Anakin and Obi-Wan subplots well enough that the movie stays engaging despite everything I mentioned with the romance. Despite the Stilted Dialogue Syndrome that more noticeably kicks in whenever a character lands on Coruscant (must be the air quality), the politics are definitely more interesting here than in the previous entry as Obi-Wan discovers the clones. Even if the motivation from before the movie is never actually explained in great detail from what I remember, it actually feels like something of weight is happening that affects the rest of the franchise, and the vulnerability that the Senate and Jedi Council feel make for much more interesting stakes against the increasingly threatening Sith. I definitely see this being a confusing movie for anyone who isn't super familiar with the entire universe, since there are a lot of super brief nods to characters and subplots that are only mentioned once or twice (I never picked up on Dooku being Tyrannus until I was much older), but now that I've been a Star Wars fan for close to 15 years, I appreciate how George reveals key information here. And the way it all culminates on Geonosis... still so entertaining. Watching all the battles at the end still turns me back to that 6-7 y/o watching these movies with fresh eyes, I'm locked in and on the edge of my seat. I admit nostalgia bias comes into play here, but dammit if I don't still enjoy it after all these years. Say what you want about George Lucas's directing or the effects of the movie (some of which are definitely outdated; Coruscant from space in the opening scene and the greenscreens behind Anakin on the speeder and on Geonosis both stuck out to me), but the man knows what he's doing with large-scale blockbuster action. Not even to mention the sheer power of his world-building and how lost I get in it. So yeah. Despite how Anakin's sole arc gets undermined by the romance, certain lines of dialogue, and the most up-to-date VFX technology in 2002, it shines through enough and bolsters well with the mystery of the clone army and action at the end to create what I would overall call a good movie. This is a review I never would have written at any younger age watching this movie, but I honestly love how differently I'm able to interpret everything here while still having a fun time. Being an adult is awesome sometimes. Oh, last thing -- Across the Stars is still a contender for all-time best Star Wars song.

Name: Tiger Tail in Blue
Review:
On paper, this totally reads as a Woody Allen-inspired film taking place in the western suburbs of Chicago(!), and honestly it pulls it off more than not! There are a number of really well written and acted moments that felt genuine and hit me with a reminder of how fun relationships can be, especially once I really got to know the characters. Some of the editing goes really hard too.

On the downside, this was also a good reminder of how poor sound mixing can hinder a film, as it was seriously hard to understand what people were saying in a handful of scenes and that really took me out. Some of the cinematography looked a bit cheap as well, although more scenes than not were really well shot and reminded me of home (with a few locations that I've been to many times over the years!).

Not the most novel indie film by any means, but I enjoyed myself and the passion is translated on screen. It looks and feels like what my first feature might be, assuming my budget and resources will be similar. But honestly, all things considered (except the sound mixing), this is close to what I'm shooting for on my debut, whenever that is.
Tags: vimeo, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Star Wars: Episode III – Revenge of the Sith
Review: Highly emotional and religious this theater experience was… I am so fucking happy I got over my sickness enough to catch this in theaters before it left :) Same as the original trilogy marathon I did a few months back, the emotions ran high from the damn beginning... I got a lump in my throat and a little teary when 20th Century Fox played, "A long time ago" came on screen, and the title crawl rolled up... and I was locked in from then on out. No one has ever said this before about any movie, but there were so many new things to notice watching this in theaters. This (and the other prequels?) honestly has one of the best soundscapes in any movie ever, being locked in allowed me to realize how over-the-top the comic relief is at times (sometimes hilarious in ways that weirdly work, while other times definitely felt distracting from the emotional experience), and I got another big ol' reminder that this movie still rocks as a 21 year old, even despite actually understanding the story and political elements now and not just being enamored by how cool the many lightsaber duels (still) are. Much like Attack of the Clones, Anakin's arc resonates a lot more clearly with me, being able to lock in at this age and stage of my life. I remembered more than well enough how conflicted he is between the Chancellor and the Jedi Council during the entire first half, but what really hit me on this watch is just how close he gets to remaining on the light side at so many points, notably at the fault of his fellow Jedi. The most pivotal moment is when Windu attempts to kill Palpatine once and for all: despite Windu's good intentions, he goes against the Jedi way, and Anakin even nearly convinces him of this. But he continues, and the image of Palpatine about to be killed is the moment where Anakin is fully convinced of what lies his friend/mentor has been telling him, that the Jedi are planning to take over without due process. And then, there are the two extremes between him and Obi-Wan. Earlier on, right before Obi-Wan leaves for Utapau, Anakin apologizes for his arrogance due to all the stress and the weight of the movie's story, and Obi-Wan finally expresses just how proud he is of his apprentice despite it all. And at the very end, when Anakin gives Obi-Wan that vulnerable, helpless, hurt animal look lying next to the lava, I was convinced in the moment that if Obi-Wan had helped him, his direction could have changed. Yes, Anakin had been so manipulated by this point that the chances of this happening were unlikely, but the fact that Obi-Wan would have taken the high road and helped him before Palpatine did means that the likelihood was nonzero. If there's one thing with all this I have to critique, however, I think Hayden's performance doesn't have enough range. He has his explosive emotional moments that hit, of course, but there are certain scenes (such as when he assists in Windu's death) where his performance honestly feels a little too static and muted in the moment. The only other critique I have for the movie at large comes down to the edit, or perhaps the direction... there are a number of shots that are over really quickly and deserved to be felt for another moment or two. The lava exploding behind Obi-Wan and Anakin comes to mind; I had full ass body chills when that hit the theater screen, but it's over after just a second. The movie already is pretty long, so maybe this was a producer's note, but moments like these deserve to be felt a little longer, especially when the propulsion of the edit and emotional journey have already gotten us this far. Other than that, aside from some occasional prequel dialogue and delivery that doesn't fit, this remains such an immaculately paced tragedy with Anakin's arc and emotions, along with the fate of the galaxy, so well communicated that the massive scale of the story and trilogy is fully earned and always hits. Order 66 is always hard to watch, every single battle scene and lightsaber duel is phenomenal, and John Williams' damn score, man. Even despite some of the 2005 CGI, there are also just so many rich and emotionally-charged images in this that are so well composed and executed that they almost (almost!) make me forget about my dialogue complaints. God, how I hope we one day in the future get another big-budget auteur-driven blockbuster event trilogy like this... Great movie.
Tags: amc evanston 12, cried or teared up, me time!

Name: Minority Report
Review:
Is there a shot more definitive of the 2000s film aesthetic than the over-saturated Dutch angle?

That was awesome, and I said as much out loud to myself when the credits rolled. This is an incredibly good argument for Spielberg as one of the best Hollywood directors in how damn well it blends the elements that make it accessible and entertaining with the many layers of the story and its real world commentary, neither ever really seemed to detract much from the other. I’m sure he’s had his reasons, but I wish Spielberg worked with more stories with this level of complexity and emotional challenge.

It’s such a polished and refined movie that I was thinking about it from a filmmaking mechanics standpoint really frequently while watching (although this might be because I just spent all weekend on set), and I almost wish it felt a little looser in its plotting just so I was feeling more than thinking, but also I had such a good time with this that I honestly didn’t mind that much. I think every beat hits, even in the misdirects, and this has some of my favorite Kaminski cinematography.

Very to learn more about real-world inspirations that motivated both the original PKD story and the decision to make this movie in 2002, obviously lots to say about what we see and how we're watched that I didn't think too much on since I was more just enjoying the ride. Totally loved this.
Tags: dvd rental, library, for class, baby's first apartment (year 2)

Name: Sorry, Baby
Review:
Really good. Not sure just yet if it’s my favorite of the year thus far, but it’s certainly strong competition… it’s the most touching and human new film I’ve seen this year for sure though.

At its consistent best, it’s really powerfully moving, oozing with personality and honesty, and incredibly endearing. Certain scenes, especially in the last two chapters, were insanely well executed and gave me actual physical reactions, both out of stress and joyful catharsis; definitely my favorite individual moments of 2025 at this point. There’s a surprising amount of comic relief as well; some of it felt too caricatured and out of place for my liking (not always helped by a typical Music Box crowd), but all the humor after a certain point hits just the right spot and blends beautifully with the story as it continues and wraps itself up. Great writing, directing, and performances overall.

Really loved the Eva Victor Q&A after. Listening to a director talk about her movie who spoke as informally as she did complete with random tangents made me feel really seen, lmao, but it was still awesome and she said a lot of really interesting shit.
Tags: music box theater, chicago critics festival, w/ blaine, q&a!

Name: The Florida Project
Review:
My first watch in over 4 years, and I only had a strong memory of a few of the most iconic scenes.

For all the ethical questions this movie asks, it does a remarkable job at maintaining a strong emotional core. Nearly every scene hits the right spot, every character is memorable and has a striking screen presence while still feeling genuine, and of course the cinematography and editing are beautiful. Really great to remember just how fantastic the ensemble here is as well, Brooklynn Prince's performance is so good.

For some reason, I remember there being a more clear message from this, but I respect how open-ended and honestly every situation is portrayed because there are definitely too many themes to dissect for there to be one clear statement; no information is shown or told less than clearly without ever being didactic, and the loose plotting works excellently to the movie's effect and its emotional journey.

I had the final scene in mind while watching and was wondering every once in a while how it would get there. And to be honest, its inclusion makes sense to me on paper, even if takes a little tickle away just because of how radically different it is from everything before it. The way the childlike perspective is achieved is really well done overall though.

Shoutout iPhone filmmaking as well!

Name: Targets
Review: Well made and effective movie that was definitely not a fun Friday night watch! I definitely went in with the wrong expectations and misread my mood but certainly still appreciated its impact on me, even if it only added to my anxiety from other things. I’m just gonna leave it here for now since I’ll remember this, for better or worse.

Name: Jeanne Dielman, 23, quai du Commerce, 1080 Bruxelles
Review: Can confirm, everyone in the theater absolutely lost their shit when she started peeling potatoes. Didn’t totally love this on first impression, could chalk that up to either anxiety that still had a hold on my brain from the night before or just how hyped this movie was for me (both of these together made for a frustrating combo), although I would definitely call this a good movie and one that’s growing on me a little already. And hell, it’s still subject to change thanks to how much this was to take in while in this mood. I honestly felt more like I was engaged with the movie more based on the fact that I was finally watching Jeanne Dielman than anything happening on screen, although I eventually settled after a certain point. There are only a handful of recurring locations, but still enough that specific story and set decoration details get easily forgotten. Even when the ending hit, I had forgotten in the moment what her job was. It's just a lot. My favorite moment was the dinner after she peeled the potatoes. Her conversations with Sylvain (her son) up to this point had felt very formal and rigid, but this dinner is where we first see her show a genuine interest in Sylvain’s life, and it was a very touching mother-son scene. That’s where I really felt emotionally hooked for the first time, and the rest of the movie was a little easier as a result. Even beyond all that, I see the vision behind it and why it resonates with so many people, so for that, along with the fact that Chantal clearly had love and respect for this character to be able to dedicate her life to making a 200 minute movie about her, I do like this. But I definitely was still hoping for a stronger reaction and I’d be lying if I said this wasn’t tough at times even regardless of extenuating circumstances, so I’m gonna keep the rating here for now.

Name: The Life of Chuck
Review:
Good fun! A little basic in its messaging about living life to the fullest and unapologetically schmaltzy, but this does a good job of being that; we all need that every once in a while.

The third chapter was definitely my favorite, as that’s when I really found an emotional hook and thought the best individual moments were all there; it’s moving stuff. The first two were decent and certainly had their own moments, they’re just a bit hollow and forgettable in comparison to how strong the last third is, and I felt the supporting characters in the first two were largely just okay.

Still, the ensemble is incredibly solid all around and this is always a visually pleasing movie, and it’s ultimately touching enough that I still had some feels leaving the theater. Could maybe go up to an 8 if I’m still thinking about it as other new movies come out, but it’s a firm 7 for now. Thumbs up overall.

Name: Kids Go Free To Fun Fun Time
Review: Test screening (in class with a director Q&A after, really cool experience). Embargoed.

Name: Bring Her Back
Review:
This is the kind of horror movie I fucking live for. I’m here in the moment processing how purely visceral and shocking my initial emotional reaction was, and tomorrow I’ll be processing the weight of how terrifying it is to be taken advantage of when you're at your lowest and most mentally vulnerable… fuck. I was so locked in and emotionally engaged from 5 minutes on, this thing is so uncomfortable at times but man it MOVES when it moves.

Go in blind, see this on the biggest screen possible, and have the damn ride of your life. The first movie in quite some time that had me vocally, passionately cheering for the protagonists; I think I’m obsessed. As hard as this shit was sometimes, it was so adrenaline-fueled that I really want to see this again.

Name: Radio Days
Review:
Beautifully shot, wonderfully acted, and it just feels timeless in both the themes of its characters as well as how it portrays and discusses radio as both a crux and a necessity of life, just like all forms of media that have come since. The attention to detail is fantastic, and no character with lines feels left behind or lacking. Nostalgia bait is pretty hit or miss, but this one hit it just right for me. It never feels overbearing or inauthentic, blending both memories of childhood and acknowledgement of the flaws of this time period expertly.

It reminds me of stories my grandparents tell from their own youth, and I mean that as high praise. Sometimes hilarious, upsetting, weird, thought-provoking, but it’s consistently moving and nearly every scene hits the spot. So many to mention, but my favorite sequences are probably Bea’s date with the blonde guy, Joe on the beach alone, everything with Polly Phelps, and the last 10 minutes. Definitely my favorite Woody Allen movie I’ve seen thus far. Was almost tempted to give it a reactionary 9, but I’m gonna keep it at a strong 8 as I sit with it.

Name: Ran
Review: Kudos to StudioCanal for such a gorgeous 4K restoration of this. Still love this movie, though it would’ve been great to see this in a theater with stadium seating (Siskel would’ve been a great choice) so I could’ve read the subtitles. Also without asshat guys behind us laughing at literally every wrong moment… so it goes sometimes :/
Tags: music box theater, w/ alex b, w/ aden

Name: Jaws
Review: I don’t care that I still have a week and a half of school to go, this was such a perfect summer 2025 kickoff. 50th anniversary screening of Jaws on a pristine-ass 35mm print (it looked SO GOOD) with a sold out screening of fans all completely into the movie made for such a great accompanying atmosphere to what would have been my favorite watch of this yet, even if I had watched it alone in my apartment. Laughed at loud at several points, the jumpscares always hit, and the widespread cheers at the big moments were icing on the cake. I’ve always *liked* this movie to a certain degree, but it’s honestly never been one I think about much or one I’ve considered a favorite Spielberg. But something about watching it tonight, at this exact time, just completely clicked for me. This movie is so funny and intense and well paced with such a memorable and on-point atmosphere so full of personality, and it’s genuinely insane how well the effects in this hold up. It’s just such an entertaining and engaging experience all the way through that I’m surprised I’ve never enjoyed it like this before, but maybe finally going in remembering what to expect, combined with everything in the air tonight, just did the trick. I jump at any opportunity to see Spielberg on the big screen, but this in particular was such a joyful experience that, for lack of a better phrase, really just reminded me of the emotional power movies can have on you. Absolutely loved it.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, w/ alex b, w/ aden

Name: I Saw the TV Glow
Review: A whole different kind of existential emotional experience on second watch, less gender dysphoric (which I was expecting) and more generally existential for me, but it’s still dissociative and it still scared me in a number of ways. The atmosphere, character, and world building in this are so eviscerating (extremely positive), sometimes different from my own suburban upbringing but still consistently hitting all the shockingly personal emotional spots, like something I’ve buried away for years. Watching this on my TV alone in my apartment with all the lights off except my Christmas lights had me completely losing myself in this world all over again, in a very different way from seeing it in a sold out Music Box. Gotta sit on it some more before I add anything concrete beyond descriptions of the watching experience. Kinda insane how many lines of dialogue in this hit such specific personal notes for me, I can’t decide on just one to use in this review. “I’m leaving this city one day, you know. I’m gonna die if I stay here.” “THIS ISN’T MY HOME.” “Sorry about earlier… it must be the new meds I’m on.”
Tags: blu-ray, baby's first apartment (year 2), for class, sad liminal vibes, lived in locations

Name: Grand Theft Hamlet
Review:
Really good stuff. Beyond the accomplishment of making a coherent, emotionally engaging film in GTA V, this really got me thinking about who I was during that time. I obviously related to the sections about loneliness and depression, but it reminded me of how I never really did much with all the time I had, mainly thanks to that consistent bad mental state I was in.

Between watching this and having just wrapped shooting my next short film which is also about COVID loneliness, I've been doing a lot of reflecting on how the difference between who I was then versus who I am now is complete night and day. The pipeline between then and now is so weird to think about still, but I'm glad I've been living way more up to my potential as of late, and I'm glad that this movie effortlessly contrasts the real characters' issues and struggles with the comic relief of GTA V logic and physics, since it reminded me of some uncomfortable memories at times.

I struggled with the Shakespeare diction at times, and just the fact that I was taking in how a film was made in GTA V took away from some of the emotional punch of a couple-few moments, but I genuinely loved nearly all of this. A genuinely impactful, unique film, my new #2 of 2025 thus far.
Tags: mubi, w/ hazel, at hazel's place

Name: Mission: Impossible – Fallout
Review: Fun movie, crazy that this is my first time watching since it came out in theaters. I still miss (parts of) Europe. The plot armor is pretty funny at times, and at one point it becomes the Community episode where Jeff, Annie, and the Dean are constantly plotting against each other, but of course the action and stunts are more than good enough to keep things interesting and engaging despite all that. Whoever cast Simon Pegg and Ving Rhames in the first place deserves a raise as well, their chemistry with Tom is great and it elevates the story for me.
Tags: dvd rental, library, at home, w/ dad

Name: Mission: Impossible – The Final Reckoning
Review: It was a divine movie-watching experience seeing this in full-frame IMAX, so I’m just gonna write a summary review at first so I can keep letting the memory soak in. And thankfully, I can summarize my thoughts on this pretty easily… TL;DR — The movie goes like this at a few different points. You have an absolutely incredible extended sequence of action and suspense; I’m talking completely locked in, losing feeling in my legs, trying not to think too hard about the fact that I might be witnessing film history in the making. The abandoned submarine and the dual storylines making up the climax are both just insane. Then, after it’s all over, the story wraps it up with either no explanation or surprisingly anticlimactic plot armor. It’s never enough to really ruin how good the sequences before them are, though they are hard to overlook. Alright, writing that gave me adequate time to sit. Some more extended thoughts… McQuarrie’s direction in this is so fascinating to me, in just how often it feels like different directors were attached to different scenes. That first action scene is edited way too fast (kinda like Quantum of Solace), then the main scene right before the credits and most of the scenes after feel just like the same McQuarrie who directed Fallout intermixed with J.J. Abrams’ dutch angles and Di Palma’s cutting, then everything with the submarine was like James Cameron lite (there’s no way it isn’t a blatant Abyss homage at times, though it is amazing), and everything at the end is a miraculous combo of everything the spy thriller genre can and should be. Not even to mention that nearly all the CGI looks excellent. From a technical standpoint, somehow, it all works. And from a screenwriting perspective, combined with everything mentioned above, by all means, this had all the makings of brilliance. Even despite some of the cornier moments, there is so much propulsion and story and character development that’s the highest form of serviceable blockbuster writing possible. It feels like the Endgame of this franchise, with all the threads pointing to an earth-shattering series finale with plenty of fan service and callbacks that feel equally earned, servicing the story’s continuation and keeping the emotional journey going strong. SPOILERS FROM HERE UNTIL MENTIONED AGAIN But then, Ethan and Bengi survive, and reunite in the final scene in a way that points all signs to continuing the franchise further. Based on how strong everything was up to this point, I fully believe McQuarrie and Cruise had intended it to be the finale I had mentioned, but then Cruise got cold feet (relative to this) and decided that he wanted to keep doing these movies into his 90s, and of course that just means ending a franchise that could so easily bring Paramount and everyone involved even more money. Obviously I don’t know the inside scoop for sure, but I bring it up because it is a very jarring transition from finale to not that took some tickle out of everything that preceded it and has been sitting less well with me as I’ve thought about it. Having all these incredible scenes and technical accomplishments servicing a story that changes at the last minute is pretty frustrating. If this commit to being a finale, it would be a 9/10. SPOILERS OVER Still, I had so much fun in the moment that I think this rating is deserved for right now, all things considered, though it might drop. It’ll be interesting to see how it ages with me.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, imax

Name: The Delinquents
Review: Damn, Robert Altman really started with a PSA for the Kansas City PD. Wasn't actually that bad of a movie either; aside from a couple of bad acting moments and a sense of superficiality at certain points, the dialogue and character & story foundations are pretty solid, it's not badly shot, and I was decently invested for a while. But then the superficiality of the climax is amplified by a narrator at both the start and very end indirectly saying that this is a PSA, giving it an extra superficial feel... Either way, I do appreciate the positives here and the fact that Altman was able to prove some of his early talent in this form definitely shows his potential for what would come later. A better indie 50s debut than something like Fear & Desire. Maybe it was the only way for him to make his first movie, I wanna learn more about this.

Name: Isle of Dogs
Review:
This is the last non-new Wes Anderson movie I haven’t seen yet… and I really think I saved one of the best for last. I was apprehensive when this first came to theaters since I’ve always been extra sensitive about stories with dogs in peril, but I’m so glad to say that this lived up to the hype 7 years later!

Don’t really need to say anything about the animation or direction about this, since I’m sure all of you already know that this just looks absolutely stellar in all regards, easily one of the best looking Wes films which is no small feat. The voice cast is great too, Desplat’s score is unsurprisingly excellent, and, as expected, it’s so full of Wes Anderson’s personality and deadpan delivery, which never felt misplaced here like it does in other cases. And I’m reminded that it works even more cleverly with anthropomorphic animals.

The only criticisms I have are a couple moments where effective dramatic moments are undercut by a bit by comedic dialogue or by a use of music, and the movie perhaps ended a little too cleanly and felt a bit anticlimactic, but even then I still found the end to be pretty moving there as I did the whole way through; the reward for every character was so fun to see, even if sudden. I honestly really loved this world and almost everything about this, was locked in the whole time and the rating will possibly go up another half star in the near future; definitely an upper half Wes for me! Unfortunately very relevant in 2025 America as well; was not expecting that going in, haha.

I think I teared up at one point? I felt an emotional hit, but also I was yawning from the day and I’m still getting over some allergies, so I can’t say for sure. Either way, the hit was there, and that’s why I love Wes’s work.

Name: The Phoenician Scheme
Review: On first impression… really fun! Not a story that really deeply moved me like my favorites from him, but it looks fucking great (duh, it’s Wes), I was thoroughly engaged (duh, it’s Wes), and I appreciated the lighter approach. The overall emotional arc was a little underwhelming for me in any circumstance, hitting me a lot more like “oh that’s nice” and not as a deeply cathartic journey, although in retrospect I don’t see this as a story that really aims for big emotional punches like Budapest or Tenenbaums. It's pure entertainment and really funny above everything else, and it thrives more in its individual character moments than it does as an entire story which is definitely different for Wes (the moment that I keep going back to is a great interaction in a helicopter between Richard Ayoade and Mia Threapleton). I’m sure a rewatch would be more rewarding now that my expectations are a little different. MVP for me is definitely Michael Cera(!!), followed by Richard Ayoade. This is also possibly the most fun Wes has had with his own style; normally when you have a style as defined as his, it generally just comes to the small details that you notice. But I’m pleased to say that there were a number of moments in this where the new things he was trying rose above simply saying “oh, that was different,” and were actually pretty jarring! Every visual decision worked for me, and I would definitely say he switches things up more here than he did in Asteroid City (this is positive; I wanna see him try new things!). So yeah, even if this isn’t the next peak in his career, I still had a lot of fun with this and am glad to see him changing it up a bit in both its overall tone and certain stylistic shifts. Hopefully he builds on this with his next movie, with a story that is just as fun and hits the spot a little harder. Strong 7! Other notes that didn't fit: - Basketball scene is hilarious. Bryan Cranston dribbling and Tom Hanks' random "AH!" both really got me. - There's moment near the end that really reminded me of The Brutalist that I thought was awesome. - Was the very short scene in the desert shot on a re-used set from Asteroid City? - A lot of the major classic American and British movies came to mind, from Citizen Kane (Korda's character) to Casablanca (the club) to It's a Wonderful Life (the heaven cutaways, which also reminded me of Wings of Desire) to David Lean (the jungle reminded me of River Kwai and a lot of discussions and visual nods reminded me of Lawrence)
Tags: music box theater, w/ claire s, w/ izzy

Name: Punch-Drunk Love
Review:
The best, most insane story of a man in disarray who actively wants to feel like a human again and finally finds the support in his life to do it, both from others and within himself. Barry’s arc from unrestrained and self-destructive to confident and proactive is a total joy and seriously has to be one of my favorite arcs in any movie. A hodgepodge of tone and structure that miraculously hits every right note and perfectly encapsulates how accepting love in your life turns chaos into order and puts so much strength in you, you have no idea.

Obviously, this movie centers on a budding romantic relationship and most directly applies there. But on rewatch, just letting the emotional journey take over, I realized that this story applies to any kind of emotional bond you might share when you need it the most, and it hit me like a truck. Watching this movie is what genuine, life-affirming love feels like, and there are few greater feelings.

The sound design and cinematography are perfection, the celebration of small moments is wonderful, the graphics are to die for, the final scene made me cry through the credits and I love this movie so, so much. Everything about it.
Tags: criterion blu-ray, baby's first apartment (year 2), cried or teared up, for class

Name: The Phoenician Scheme
Review: Already rewatched it lmao, the vibes were just right after Premiere (DePaul’s student film festival).
