Not a whole lot more to add, but going in with different expectations did in fact help me enjoy the ride more. It’s the closest thing to a Wes Anderson Indiana Jones film in its story, genres, and no-frills nostalgic feel. I both picked up on more hints that Wes left throughout the story and processed more of the actual story, of course, and laughed even harder at Tom Hanks yelling “YOW!” I will say, though, I still think the final chapter feels a bit lacking in oomf and I still wish there were more emotional in-points with the leads, but I was a little more touched by Korda’s overall arc. Keeping the rating where it is, but I’m definitely more open to the idea of it bumping up in the future.

Name: The Adventures of Tintin
Review:
Some of the most cinema ever cinema'd. Incredibly faithfully adapted, and genuinely insane how good the visual storytelling is in this. Computer animation as a medium firing on all damn cylinders; every scene is a joy, reminds me why I love this shit (movies). Every time, it takes me back to when I saw this in theaters when I was 8, eyes glazed to the screen and jaw on the floor, realizing that this is the best damn thing I had ever seen. Really, all that’s changed since then is I have piercings now.

The mo-cap animation sticks out just a little more in 2025, but aside from that, seriously... whatever studio executives have been blocking the sequel from getting made, you aren't seeing heaven. I love my parents for so many reasons, but raising me on Spielberg was one of their best parental decisions ever.
Tags: youtube (rent), baby's first apartment (year 2), w/ hazel

Name: Raiders of the Lost Ark
Review: I’ve been doing a lot of rewatching recently, but it’s just easier and more fun to rewatch sometimes, especially during the final stretch of school. Also, tonight being the first night of summer, gotta kick it off with what’s become a yearly tradition for me. Still awesome, of course, even if just a little bit of the tickle is taken out with yearly rewatches. Most stricken this time around by Belloq’s character, I was thinking about him more than I ever have in the past. The most “human” of the Nazi ensemble here, through all his smooth-talking, romancing, and all his thoroughly entertaining back & forth and comparisons with Indiana, but he’s still irrevocably a Nazi. I also admired every hand-to-hand combat scene here even more; I’ve said it before, I’ll say it again, Spielberg is just so good at action coverage. The screenplay is honestly very strong in ways that I hadn’t picked up in the past as well. The game of cat ‘n mouse between Indiana and the Nazis is always a total blast to follow along with, and you’re never quite sure who the cat and who the mouse are in any given situation. And, plain and simply, no one part of the story and no character ever felt lacking, and the satisfaction felt watching every end eventually cleanly meeting is really awesome, from the obviously divine climax to the poetic irony of the very last scene. My only complaints with the movie are that it’s a little didactic at times, with certain lines of dialogue that spell things out a little too clearly (could also just be the delivery of those lines) and I found it funny when Indiana spelt out the entire movie near the beginning on the chalkboard for the FBI guys (edit — in retrospect this is interesting foreshadowing to show how much smarter they are than the Nazis from the start, even if it reveals a ton), and there are a couple moments where the editing prioritizes the comedy at the expense of a better flow. But otherwise, it’s just minor continuity errors that I noticed. Having just written one of my final papers on Temple of Doom, I’m excited to see my updated thoughts on that since, even just watching this, I noticed some ways that the edit and aspects of the writing get tighter as the series progresses. Still, this was my official summer kickoff and it was awesome as always, even if the thrill of certain scenes starts to wear off after yearly watches. This is never less than a great time, and there’s always something new to pick up on and appreciate. Like damn, I just wrote this whole review without even mentioning the production design, cinematography, or score!!
Tags: paramount+, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Blood of a Poet
Review:
What a cool discovery; stumbled upon this earlier today and decided to watch it like an hour before I hit play. Totally feels like something that could only have been made by someone whose background and education came from different artistic mediums delving into film for the first time, and also something that should be considered a learning point in both avant-garde and experimental film.

Definitely confusing at times in a ways that took me out, the first two chapters make for a phenomenal short film and would arguably be better on their own than the second two, but the effects are really good for 1932 and the imagery and editing are timeless and stellar. Experimental film isn't typically my favorite, even if I admire what it does, but in the moment this was just a very cool and surprising watch from start to finish.

Name: nosebleed
Review: Relatable (I also have frequent nosebleeds every time it gets even a little dry. Living in an apartment built in the 50s with no humidity control… every day, man). Jake, I hope you didn’t let your nose bleed just for the film, but it’s an interesting visual exercise either way. The camera zoom was cool, bro is becoming Michael Paneke

Name: Materialists
Review: Loved it. Celine is still so good at character writing and directing that elevates the story so much. The scientific, exacting nature of Lucy’s character and how she approaches love made for a super interesting and watchable lead without ever feeling emotionally distancing, and she’s a great vessel into the rest. Chris Evans and Pedro’s characters are a bit more cliche, but they still bring enough personality and resonant qualities to those cliches that I didn’t mind and I impressively didn’t see them as the actors in the moment. There are a ton of great convos and very specific personal in-points here, not unlike how Past Lives hit me even if not quite as hard and in an (obviously) different story. Nearly every shot and cut looked great as well. Really, the only criticism I have is that the story falls into romcom tropes and these characters deserve a little better. The story is paving its own path for the most part and this does wonders for these characters going on their unique journeys, then they enter into more predictable plotting for the genre and it delves into the territory of “oh, that’s nice” when I was hoping for bigger emotional punches. Still, pretty great overall. All three leads are great in their own way, but Zoe Winters… kinda stole the show at a couple points! There’s a crucial line delivery from her that didn’t fully work, but if not for that she would be the standout.

Name: Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom
Review:
Currently somewhere in between where I was last year and where I was in years past on this, and I think this is something close to a final stance.

Certainly has all the makings of another great entry in the series, and delivers on a lot of those promises still. Obviously a lot of fantastic set pieces and scenes of combat and moments of Williams going ham. And after writing a final paper on this, I’ve come to appreciate that Indiana has more of an actual character improvement arc in this, going from “fortune and glory” to witnessing how truly dire the situation is and prioritizing the well-being of the village.

Still, there’s a stench of phoniness that comes from how the Thuggees are portrayed here. I know the excessiveness of it is tongue-in-cheek and all, but it still gets to a point of eye-rolling after a certain point and it lacks the emotional grounding that the Nazis have as villains in the first and third. The stakes just aren’t as interesting to me, not helped by some of the ridiculous plot armor in the final act.

Nonetheless, it captures the Spielberg excitement and beating heart of the series that I’ve come to appreciate more with age and I would call this a fun movie overall. My description of this from last year as divorced man’s David Lean still feels apt.
Tags: paramount+, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Friday the 13th
Review: Great summer movie. Stupid sometimes but also kinda ripped at times too. Gets away with more than other similar movies might since slashers weren’t tropey or worn too thin just yet, this is just having fun with a budding trend and wearing its Halloween and Psycho and Texas Chainsaw influences on its sleeves. I respected it for that and had a good time. Very fun ensemble of characters here, they were very watchable until they had to act scared, surprisingly that’s when they were least convincing and kept some of their own deaths from hitting harder. Movie never feels too long, builds atmosphere well, and has some genuinely great jumpscares (in theaters too so some of them REALLY got me). Interesting to me that 70s horror classics (I know this was 1980 but was obviously made during the same era) and the birth of the slasher all revolved around teenagers being killed.

Name: Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade
Review: Fathom Events knew what they were doing showing this today… such a fucking treat seeing this in theaters with my parents over Father’s Day weekend. Final act was divine on the big screen, I forgot just how emotional and well executed it is as the closer to both this movie and the entire trilogy. Best ending in the series by far.
Tags: hollywood blvd cinema, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Ballerina
Review: I mean, it’s just totally made for middle schoolers, from how mind-numbing the violence gets to the Evanescence song(??) over the end credits. The action is mostly awesome, the screenplay and performances are even weaker than I expected... like damn, when it focuses solely on the story after the major action centerpiece of the second act until the finale, it’s a total slog. Somehow though, despite that, the fun of the action did end up leaving a *slightly* stronger impression on me. There’s so much violence it’s funny. So I’ll give it a bump, I mean I can’t say I didn’t get what I signed up for. I’m starting to crave a serious character driven movie after all my recent watches though. Oh also it’s only June, but this is the 2025 winner of the Award for Most Climate Change by a Movie
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ kelly, w/ aaliyah

Name: 28 Days Later
Review: Pretty phenomenal imagery (even if some of the MTV editing didn’t work for me) and directing throughout, a great ensemble, a slow but endearing build to a breakneck, shocking, and emotionally charged final act, and it’s pretty damn scary. Aptly a movie that feels like it’s being held together at the seams by medical tape and safety pins; somehow the exact right mix of cheap and professional. Great stuff, really happy to have finally seen this.

Name: 28 Weeks Later
Review:
Look, watching this and all its commentary on the utilitarianism of the United States military and government on June 20, 2025 obviously brought up some emotions. And to its credit, it actually is quite effective. It pretty deftly mixes splatter gore zombie action with its military survival paranoia with some strong moments, and I certainly had it at a higher rating for the most part. But damn, it really just fumbles the ending, not only leaving the central plot thread untied but also resorting to a corny, laughable teaser for a threequel that wouldn’t even come until now, 18 years on.

It’s certainly a solidly crafted and well acted film, but I only feel like being so generous with a rating when the emotional payoff is underwhelming like this. Enjoyed it enough though that it could certainly get an extra half star in the future.

Keeping it at 3 though because — lol at the entire movie happening just because no one on the US military noticed two kids cross a well-surveilled bridge and enter a restricted area.

Name: 28 Years Later
Review:
Admired the experimentation in structure and directing more than I personally enjoyed the movie. I think Spike has a solid enough foundation even if he’s a bit of a typical adventure genre boy leaving home for the first time, and I do really like the worldbuilding of this, around how humans might return to a more primitive state in this situation; the production design and practical effects are both pretty fantastic and I was certainly immersed in this regard.

But it really just feels way too much like a Part 1 in how it ties everything up without feeling like a complete experience and lacks in strong emotional payoff, as sweet as everything with Spike and his mom and Dr. Ralph was. And while it’s very cool as an iPhone filmmaker to see iPhones be used and experimented with at this scale, the sheer amount of trying-to-be-different here distracted me enough from ever really being scared or feeling the most intense and emotional moments, even if the character development was working for me enough that I was engaged in the story. It added a distance, even if it was really sick at times.

All things considered, it did enough that I’m intrigued by where the sequel will go and how it’ll finish this story which says something (and might have me bump up a half star, idk), but as an individual experience this just made me feel frustratingly alienated. Certainly not bad, but definitely had more potential to live up to.

Name: The Apprentice
Review:
Good movie. Obviously can never know how exact this is unless I know the man personally or do a lot more research into his life during this era, but the movie effectively played into public perception and my own knowledge of Trump and turned that into a digestible and well done business drama. Great performances for sure, as I’ve been hearing for a full year now, and I’m so interested in the visual style of the film. The edit is honestly pretty fantastic, and I love how they shot on film and emulated a vintage TV look, even if the increased static in the final act was a bit distracting. Good needle drops for the most part as well. A great plane movie, definitely one that kept me engrossed and thinking and forgetting I’m in the air with very little legroom.

Strong 7.
Tags: dvd rental, library, on a plane, portable dvd player

Name: Crooklyn
Review:
Didn’t have internet for a few days on vacation in the mountains (it was amazing and I hate that I have access to it again) so that definitely took away my immediate thoughts after watching this, but I liked it initially and it’s held up well in my head.

The ensemble is fantastic here and I was shocked at how good the child actors are, it’s so clear that Spike Lee had everyone on the same page throughout the whole shooting process. Every character on screen is fleshed out and memorable, crucial given that this is a mostly plotless movie. Fantastic production design (love how much you learn about the characters from this) and editing as well. I didn’t find this to be the most emotional watch in the moment, but it’s always engaging, doesn’t really miss a beat, and I’ve grown to love how Troy’s character progresses even just 3 days after watching.
Tags: dvd, portable dvd player, on a damn boat, w/ jonah, washington

Name: Twin Peaks
Review: I forgive myself for every other time I dropped the ball on finishing Season 2 because I ended up binging the last four episodes in a damn mountain village IN WASHINGTON. Went for a walk after finishing and was instantly struck by just how immaculately the general vibe of the Pacific Northwest is captured in it, like… I was fucking there. Just a sick ass experience and I wouldn’t have it any other way.

Name: L.A. Confidential
Review:
Okay yeah, I get it. I was actually kinda just blown away by this.

I’ve said enough here that I always love stories about moral and ethical quandries that are written with intelligence and emotion, and this is exactly that while still being a thoroughly engaging and unpredictable neo-noir that had me at nearly every step of the way. The screenwriting is just top notch, from the plotting to the single lines of dialogue that single-handedly unlock plot points to the witty lines of dialogue that are immaculately placed.

On top of that, this is living proof that Hollywood is fully capable of updating classic genres and styles without chipping away at their core. This has all the deft plotting, deep, heartful character development, and “cool factor” of classic Hollywood noir and mysteries, and not once is it ever compromised by the high amount of action sequences (the FINALE!) or Scorsese-inspired directing + editing to satisfy modern audiences just the same.

I completely love Bud White’s character arc and what it says about masculinity and repressed emotions. When he started opening up and vocally communicating with people, it was a genuine surprise but that was the moment I just knew. All the characters and performances in this world are incredible, but Russell Crowe is absolutely the standout here for me.

There’s a part of me that’s a bit conflicted with the epilogue, wishing that it just ended on Exley smiling and laughing in the interrogation room, as the addition of the monologue is just a bit cut and dry without leaving lingering thoughts for my taste, but otherwise… no real complaints.

Admittedly, there’s also just enough to think about and keep track of while watching that I’m not fully confident in my opinion and feelings on it yet, but the fact that this was such an exhilarating ride that really surprisingly moved me inclines me to give it the half star bump. Loved it.


Edit - also forgot to include the whole celebrity element which I thought was surprisingly poignant even if not the standout aspect for me, but that just goes to show how much there is to this. It’s so good.

Name: F1
Review: “What’s the most you’ve ever lost on a coin toss?”
Tags: cinemark cantera, w/ sid, w/ jess

Name: light blossom
Review: Couldn't relate to the characters :/

Name: 8½
Review: Gotta ponder this one, but from Point A to Point B I ended up really falling for it. Such a richly textured, weird, beautifully shot film that never had me fully confident in my predictions for where it would go next. In all its ruminating on honesty, genuine vs forced emotional feeling, the commercialization of art, and the cycle of losing and finding your passion, I certainly had a few moments of confusion and alienation, but in stronger measure I found it fascinating, wondrous, and ultimately very moving by the time it reached its final few scenes. All around, loved.

Name: Natural Born Killers
Review: Not bad, but not Badlands. My parents and I chose this knowing its premise, but assuming it would feel a little more in touch with its humanity given the names involved. Needless to say, we were all a little thrown off by how abrasive and ridiculous this gets at a lot of points. I do realize that that’s the point of this all, and I certainly do respect the audacity to a certain extent (especially in how fucking wild some of the filmmaking here is) as it definitely spins the nonstop violence into an effectively feel-bad experience, but it does so to a numbing degree. It’s so consistently graphic that it’s hard to feel any kind of shock or strong emotion towards it, and there are moments that do stand out with the story (notably the interview itself, where we finally find some humanity in this story for the first time in a while) but as a whole there isn’t anything to really latch on to. As a result of both of these, at times it’s almost kinda… boring? There’s certainly always something to watch, and props to the technical crew and performances for always giving it their all, but on my initial impression it did feel frustratingly empty for how much it has going for it. Could see the rating go up to a 7 at some point though, once the initial reaction subsides.
Tags: dvd rental, library, w/ dad, w/ mom, at home

Name: No Country for Old Men
Review:
Was completely locked in and not thinking about anything else for nearly all of it, and I couldn’t have asked for a better headspace or theater audience to accompany the experience. Much like Tommy Lee, I didn’t wake up from this until the very end.

Don’t wanna think too terribly hard about technical specifics since I’m still thinking about how damn good that theater experience was, but I do wanna say I’m completely in love with how this so consistently uses silence so effectively. It’s captivating and distracts completely from any story structure or beats you might notice in the moment, which is exactly what movies should do and works SO well in this case.

Also Javier’s performance and character obviously makes for such a good psychopath that you’re genuinely fascinated by and scared of, but Tommy Lee does SO much with the screentime and conversations he has, to the point that I might slightly prefer him.

And to briefly address my first watch from 4.5 years ago, I honestly just went in with the wrong expectations and I realized that maybe a few months later, like… if you had asked me what I thought of this movie in June 2021, I would’ve already said I liked it and it grew on me. Really glad to finally update my opinion on this in an actual review and not just an increased star rating. Fuckin awesome movie.
Tags: music box theater, w/ claire s, lived in locations

Name: Fallen Leaves
Review:
One of my 2023 blindspots I’ve had lurking in the back of my mind for a couple years now, and I liked it quite a bit! Not something that really blew me away on first watch, but it’s thoroughly just very enjoyable and engaging. Very relevant to my life right now as I search for a new job and have been dabbling in online dating for the first time.

A lot of great and beautiful individual moments, and I really love how this is paced on a scene-to-scene basis even if some parts of the story didn’t hit me as much as others. Very confident direction and writing overall, it really shows in how such direct and inexpressive performances are able to evoke the emotions and reactions they do; it’s thoroughly and unapologetically Finnish and I really appreciated that about it.

Great cinematography too, wasn’t talked about enough in the 2023-24 conversation.


UPDATE 7/16 -- bumped up to an 8 since I'm still thinking about the film, and I think the inclusion of Ansa's frustration with the radio only playing Ukraine news and not music is surprisingly poignant. She starts by listening in full and acknowledging what is going on, and continually does this to stay up to date, but when the news continues in place of music she wants to hear, it gets understandably incredibly frustrating for her. It's one of the more contemporary portrayals I've seen of how art is equally crucial when the world goes to shit (for lack of a better term) and has continued to resonate with me even a couple days later. Also it's just a really touching romance film about how accepting love into your life is a basic part of human happiness; we're social creatures, in tandem with the work we do for ourselves.
Tags: mubi, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
Review: "But I tried, didn't I? Goddamnit, at least I did that."
Tags: cinemark seven bridges, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Out of Sight
Review: “Most bank robbers are fucking morons.” This was really awesome, pretty easily my favorite Soderbergh so far. First two acts had me looking for a strong emotional in point, but make up for it just by being a hell of a lot of fun and having a grand ol’ time with nonlinear structure and riffing on heist genre tropes. But then I got to the final act, and all this continues through to the end as Clooney, Rhames, and Lopez’s characters all reveal their depths and the depth of the story, simultaneously justifying how surprisingly goofy the movie is. Clooney and Lopez’s intimate conversation was where it all started unlocking for me, and up until the end I was fully locked in. I didn’t fully buy into the chemistry between Clooney and Lopez for a bit, and that combined with some of the absurdity of the story is what kept me a little disconnected through the first half despite my overall enjoyment, but as I already said it makes up for it later, and aside from that this movie rips. I only mention that since that’s what’s keeping the movie at a strong 8 and not a 9 on first impression. Also hot damn, we used to make movies that LOOK like this visually and are SEXY like this! Even when I didn’t fully find the romance believable on paper, Soderbergh really knew here how to direct the right chemistry and vibes to achieve that sexy mood.
Tags: criterion channel, car dvd / on a roadtrip, on vacation

Name: The Monkey
Review:
Watched this in two sittings and unplugged my earbuds for the last 15 or so minutes in the second sitting because I genuinely felt it was better for my own mental health to finish it that way. I can’t put it into words in a coherent fashion yet without writing a multi-paragraph rant why I hated this but I did. I’m gonna brainswitch before going to bed since this genuinely almost ruined my mood for a while on my first day of a family vacation.

I’ve said before that I hate shitting on movies but this really is my honest reaction. This mostly felt like it was written by an edgy, misanthropic 14 year old who wants to shoot up his school until it FINALLY reveals some depth at the end, only to revert back into failed comedic territory and making the depth feel like the butt of the joke, too afraid to actually make a serious, emotional movie. And that’s the recurring pattern for the majority of this; offensively frustrating emotional whiplash, hinting at any sort of actual weight only to completely fumble the bag every time and end up being a miserable pile of dung. I disassociated at one point from it. Never wanna think about this shit again.
Tags: dvd rental, library, in a hotel, on vacation, colorado

Name: The Naked Gun: From the Files of Police Squad!
Review: Really fun. Car chase bit was hilarious and probably my favorite gag.
Tags: paramount+, w/ dad, w/ mom, in a hotel, on vacation, colorado

Name: Sweat
Review: Interesting one for me. I totally love the concept of this on paper and it didn't really misstep for me, but it also hit all the notes I was expecting. The performances and filmmaking are great and there are certainly some very poignant moments, surprisingly great sound design too, although the fact that it was largely what I was expecting with minimal surprises in the end meant that my emotional reaction was surprisingly muted. Definitely a good movie, but one that I need to gauge and process a little bit more before I give a rating.
Tags: mubi, in a hotel, on vacation, colorado

Name: Superman
Review: So, Superman definitely fits the mould of a studio superhero movie in 2025. James Gunn is certainly among the best to work within that mould, although this still feels manufactured, you know all along the good guys win, there’s a lot of expository dialogue and a long section with a tiring amount of one-liners, sometimes feels like it’s too catered to kids, etc. But, all that being said, the Gunn emotional core here is genuinely good, and it has enough presence among all the spectacle and exposition to elevate the movie! The themes of how your actions define you is basic but is refreshing for Superman and fits with the genre and this particular story. The plotting once the movie shifts into gear is solid and engaging and makes up for more of the story flaws than I expected as well. There’s actually some tension to be found, and elements to the story that felt more human than pretty much anything else superhero from this decade aside from Spiderverse. And he did in fact manage to successfully write in real world commentary in a digestible, even if very surface level way. There’s also some very good VFX to balance out the less good VFX (lmao at that enough being a positive change). If you hadn’t figured it out yet, that’s the overall theme of my opinion on this - there is a pulsating heart behind the highlights of this movie that I really did appreciate, it doesn’t embrace or commit to those changes enough to fully be the radical superhero 180 that some people claim it to be. Hopefully Gunn’s DCU can push that further with future entries. On the border of a 6 and 7 for me, I’ll give it the edge since I did have fun in the end despite my annoyances. Fun is fun.
Tags: amc chapel hills 13, w/ quinn, w/ mom, w/ dad, w/ nathan, w/ uncle mark, w/ aunt shannon, w/ grandparents, on vacation, colorado

Name: Winchester '73
Review:
Continuing my new tradition to watch an old western when I visit my Colorado grandparents…

I’m a little worn out from the week and overwhelmed by heading back to normal life later today, so I didn’t watch this in the absolute best headspace but I still really liked this. Hollywood Golden Age is an era I definitely want to watch more from since they always scream “this is how you frame and edit,” and this was no different in how well it was directed. The first act is really strong, and the second act lulled a little bit as a result as it meanders with minor plot points for a while, but once the crucial information is revealed that kickstarts the final act I became more interested again.
Tags: dvd rental, library, on vacation, colorado, w/ mom, w/ dad, w/ quinn, w/ grandparents

Name: Megalopolis
Review:
Only made it for the second half since the train I took out of the suburbs got into an accident and delayed me by 2 hours, but I persisted and made it in time for as much as I possibly could. I walked in right before the fabulous shot of Cesar and Julia kissing on the steel beams in the sky, and it was such a welcoming moment given how late I was.

As for what I saw, I might’ve missed “so go back to the cluuub” and the whole Madison Square sequence, but watching just the second half honestly put into clearer perspective for me the point that the movie is making. Sure, the messages of how we’re all human beings of the same family and how we cannot move forward without questioning the present aren’t marvel ideas, but the way this movie just plays by its own rules remains fascinating and captivating to me. And honestly, just watching a movie where there’s such an emphasis on the importance of how the younger generations are who we’re making a world for was enough to resonate with me and my life right now. It doesn’t all come fully together and the story resolution is a bit simple for my tastes, but it’s a blast to watch.

As for the real reason why I was here, Francis Ford Coppola didn’t just give a Q&A about the movie, he had a full on discussion about… EVERYTHING. He actually wanted to have an intelligent conversation and it was a huge surprise for sure, but dude it was so awesome getting so much deeper with him. He talked about such a huge array of the biggest aspects of human life and shared what he’s learned about each over the years, and it truly felt like I was actually getting to know this guy on a personal level as he discussed time, money, work, politics, education, etc. He talked about film and the industry through several topics of course, but hearing him talk about so much else had me rethinking how I view the above topics in my everyday life, which was just a treat through and through.

Name: Panic
Review: Aptly a frustrating watch as it explores both the humanity of a person who's never received the love he's needed and how this humanity can never be discovered when people are manipulated to believe that same person doesn't have any. There's a scene early on involving bumper cars that works as the surprisingly perfect metaphor for this lack of critical thinking. Certainly made as a reflection on how Europe was groomed to vilify Jewish people in the 40s, and not a surprise to say it's still relevant in certain ways today. Also this is another one of those lesser known old movies that feels like a highly overlooked bridgepoint between eras of film. It’s definitely got elements of Russian and German film that came before it, but it also reminded me a lot of Germany Year Zero (which came out 2 years later) and Hitchcock, with direct visual references to Rear Window and Vertigo (which came out 8-11 years later). Certain scenes and shots are so uncanny to the point that this had to be an influence.

Name: Eddington
Review: The message of Eddington can be most frankly summed up by the name of the city this was filmed in; Truth or Consequences. But despite the infinite ways that statement can be interpreted, this film about the people of a small town during one of the most heated political moments of the decade ends up surprisingly thin. Ari absolutely hasn’t lost his audacity with what I’ve gathered on first impression is a morbid dramedy about running for and challenging the political status quo in 2020s USA, but it brings me no joy as a massive Beau Is Afraid fan to say he did lose a notable amount of his emotional resonance with me. There is a cleverness to admire in how the film uses the lying, misinformation, and performative nature of the characters to shape just how insane the story gets and how everything collapses in on itself, but for me it’s really only something to admire that I didn’t actually have any personal connection to. It’s thoroughly engaging as we watch everything unfold, but for how exhausting it is I was really craving some kind of emotional in-point to go along with it and I never actually got that from any of the characters; they all just felt like they servicing the story and overall message without any deeper digging into who they are. And when I did feel for characters, they’re either vastly overshadowed by the plotting or close out their arc by feeling too caricatured to drive home a more serious point. To be honest, the best way I can describe it off the top of my head is Ari Aster’s Civil War (2024). This is certainly the more interesting movie and I do prefer it overall, but it strikes that same underwhelming emotional discord by successfully hitting all of the stress and frustration associated with Trump-era political divide without leaving a real lasting impression or reframing what it brings to the table beyond “that was exhausting.” These are emotions we live with every day in 2025 America, and for all this movie throws at you, it irritatingly doesn’t do much else with them. In equal measure, there are some fantastic sequences in this, and I’m almost tempted to bump the rating up to a 7 just because of how definitively Ari Aster this all is and how it’s impossible to look away from it; it is a very memorable experience in the moment. but it unfortunately added up to the closest Ari’s come to a swing and a miss for me and the lack of stronger resonance inclines me to keep it at a (perhaps reactionary) 6. Oh also — no bearing on the film overall, but lmao at Ted Garcia having Movie Magic Budgeting on his computer for some reason. Did no one working on this notice that? Is he actually a secret movie producer? Is this whole thing a commentary on Hollywood and the struggle one goes through to get an original idea out into the world???

Name: The Naked Gun
Review: “She had a face, hair, head, shoulders, knees and toes… knees and toes… and a butt that would make a toilet beg for the brown.” Really fun and airtight, as it should be. No extra meat to cut, just super funny all the way through. Love how they were able to adapt the Zucker comedy feel in 2025 Hollywood, very impressive blend of the two. Stay for the end credits!

Name: High and Low
Review: Still really good, but didn't quite scratch the same itch as the first time. I think it was a combo of my tiredness and also the occasional frustration I felt while watching realizing that I wasn't feeling the same connection. Maybe I just wasn't in the right mood. But I at least can still acknowledge that it's a really well made film and there still are a number of fantastic individual moments that hit the same way even if the whole thing didn't. Will hold off on a rating.
Tags: criterion blu-ray, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Cloudy with a Chance of Meatballs
Review:
The first time I rewatch a movie I haven't seen for years, it always hits a little differently and throws me off. This fell victim to that last December. I still remember when this movie first dropped and my parents bought it on iTunes, I watched it SO many times.

But rewatching it this morning, I'm so much more enthralled by it. Really gorgeous and creative animation of course, I think the screenplay strikes a balance of humor and serious commentary much better, the voice acting is mostly excellent, and I really love all the motifs. And that ending hit the spot!
Tags: vod, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Weapons
Review: Matthew is 9 years old and he already has a Fury Road poster in his room. Thanos is raising him well! This is definitely a movie that I was looking forward to, and I’m happy to say it delivers a good bit more than not! The first two acts (everything until the Alex chapter) are genuinely pretty fantastic and I was confident at times that it had the makings of the best of the year so far. The final act then reminded me that, like Barbarian, Zach Cregger is better at developing a mystery than he is at explaining and wrapping one up, but thankfully not enough to sour its many strengths. There’s absolutely a lot to chew on with what the whole story means. Cregger doesn’t make it easy to think too much about the subtext while you watch thanks to how quickly this moves, and I really appreciate that about it. The way this plays with the ethics of each situation and fully understanding each main character through the nonlinear structure without any unnecessary explanation in the first two acts is genuinely to die for. It’s consistently emotionally investing watching everything unfold, every character is believable, and it’s spooky as hell. There’s such effortlessly effective horror and mystery writing and directing all the way through, and it goes without saying that the ensemble is incredibly strong as well. It would be a captivating watch anywhere, but in a huge theater with a packed crowd it was just fantastic and highlighted all of Cregger’s brilliant decisions even more. The final act does have genuine emotional payoff to complement everything up to that point and some very effective thrills to boot, which is what’s keeping me firm on my strong rating. But the final act is additionally where all the mystery is explained, which revealed two big plot holes that are a bit hard to overlook, and (also like Barbarian) it quickly descends into genre territory at the expense of a stronger emotional finale. It’s fun to watch and all, and the closure is there, but I already feel myself craving a more emotional conclusion given just how invested I was in everything. No matter, being just shy of an absolute killer for me still means this is good shit overall, extremely worth seeing in theaters with a big, invested crowd. On the border of a 7 and 8; I could genuinely see it going either way in the future, but right now I’m gonna give it the edge just because I appreciate how enthralling of a theater experience this was.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ alek, w/ peter, prime at amc

Name: Blow-Up
Review: Good movie, wish I saw it in better conditions for my first watch (watched late last night and was falling asleep during the second half). One of the best looking movies I’ve seen in recent memory and it’s a lot of fun to watch these characters, although I think the runtime had me wishing that a little more was going on storywise. Still liked it though.
Tags: criterion dvd, paulina apartment, w/ peter, w/ david

Name: It's a Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad World
Review: “I said it before, I’ll say it again… I never wanted to move to California.” This whole thing is a wonderfully constructed joy, but that second half… I think I just witnessed one of the most perfect second halves in cinema history. Enjoyed it all the way through, but had a little bit of unrelated anxiety at the start, and by the end I was hollering at every bit; exactly the movie I needed today to take my mind off things for a few hours. Watched on 70mm at the Music Box, which looked just as beautiful as that sounds. And with the exact right amount of grain and cigarette burns to give it a feel of 35mm, to say I was transported is an understatement. Combine all that with an audience that was appropriately loud and almost never got into obnoxious territory… one of the most euphoric theater experiences I think I’ve ever had.
Tags: music box theater, 70mm, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Ocean's Eleven
Review: Okay yes I’m familiar with Soderbergh’s game now. This screenplay is such a plant-and-payoff merchant, the editing and score are so sleek and cool (it’s Soderbergh!), and I could rave about so much of the cast in their own ways. Matt Damon’s boyish nature is so shockingly good, Casey Affleck is always so watchable, Don Cheadle is hilarious, I could go on. On the same level as Out of Sight for me, they both have their own strengths and they’re both pretty equally awesome. Maybe not the most deeply emotional thing, but the entertainment factor here really makes up for so much of that. I had a really great time.

Name: Ne Zha
Review:
Good movie! By American standards, very weird and unhinged for a kids movie in some scenes (what the fuck was that with the pants??), but the emotional core and metaphors are there and come across well. The animation is mostly great as well, and all the fight scenes are actually pretty incredible!

I think the final scenes get a little too mixed up in both the plot and the message simultaneously in a way that neither came across as strongly as they intended, but I had a good time despite that. I appreciate how the movie makes it easy to sympathize with Ne Zha early on, even despite all his flaws and the literal demonization from those around him. If I saw this when I was a kid, I probably would’ve fallen head over heels for his character.

Strong 7!

Name: Ne Zha 2
Review: Wow holy shit!! Have been anticipating seeing this since February (was too busy during its initial release to see it), and I'm so happy I got an incredible IMAX experience to cap off all the hype. Would be lying if I said I caught everything on first watch, there's a ton of exposition during the first half and it was a little much to follow on first impression, but it genuinely does build up to and result in one fucking hell of a second half, as well as a pretty amazing movie overall once everything is said and done. There's so much going on visually and it looks so, so good that it's genuinely overwhelming. Couldn't tell you how many scenes had my mouth hanging open; the animation is just shy of Disney's and Pixar's quality level, and WAY more epic than anything they've put out in several years. The emotional beats are really strong here too, I'd argue stronger than the first; if my struggle to follow all the plot threads hadn't distracted me as much as it did at times, I likely would've cried at one scene in particular that still had me welling up despite everything. I've commented to friends on multiple occasions at this point how there just isn't the same excitement with Hollywood movies anymore that we got with Lord of the Rings, Star Wars, Harry Potter, and Endgame, but movies like this, Spiderverse, and, to an extent, Dune remind me that it still exists, just not as commonly as it did at the beginning of the century. If anyone you know shares a similar sentiment, by all means, send them in the direction of this movie. The fact that these franchises exist and continue to excite and move me give me hope that they'll blaze a trail for more to come in the future, I just know it. Big ups to middle chapters of trilogies released in the 2020s that throw traditional three-act structure out the window at one point or another! (this along with Dune and Spiderverse)
Tags: regal city north, imax, early screener, w/ peter

Name: 2001: A Space Odyssey
Review:
Realized after a certain point that I was too tired to lock into this the way I always like to with movie-watching, so combine that with having not seen this in over 5 years… my brain is fried. Every conclusion I thought I was able to draw ended up being changed when the movie went in a different direction from what I had remembered, so I have very few confident thoughts to write. An exhausting watch, I need to lay down for a bit.

After some time to think — there are a ton of things here to appreciate, of course, and I absolutely still think this is a great movie. And if nothing else, maybe this’ll be a fun story to tell in the future, how 2001 in 70mm while tired fucked my brain up lmao. I can’t think of another movie that so overwhelmingly immerses you into a different universe like this one does, the immersion factor here is absolutely second to none.

Name: Jimi Plays Monterey
Review:
Blind watch on Criterion 24/7, knew nothing about it ahead of time either. My first concert film since becoming a concert person, and it was the perfect watch for tonight. Still catching up on sleep, in the homestretch of getting over some heavy anxiety I had today, parents were in and out of the living room… but I really enjoyed this in its entirety.

From the opening scenes, I thought there would be more voiceover, but the concert footage is pretty spectacular. Hendrix was an awesome performer and he’s done justice by the direction, loved the cutting back to the audience as a concert person to get brief glimpses into how people enjoyed them in the 60s, and the film photography is pretty sweet. There were some moments where I thought they held on the same angle for too long without cutting to something more interesting, just in that it felt a bit monotonous, but I wasn’t in the editing room watching all the dailies so who knows?

Going back to the film comment… we don’t capture colors like this anymore, and any time I see a hair caught in a shot, I’m reminded that movies shot on film come from a simpler time where human error was much more evident on camera. There’s a real beauty in that which is much harder to come by on digital.

Obviously very sad that Hendrix’s life was cut so short, and it makes me wonder what other dead artists might have a concert film, or at the very least the footage to make a concert film somewhere in existence. As a big rap fan, and mentioning rap in particular since I can’t think of any major concert films for the genre, I would kill to see a concert film for Tupac, Biggie, Mac Miller, or MF Doom.

Name: War of the Worlds
Review: A cautionary tale about hiring filmmakers with no sense of geography, or who just don't give a shit about obvious details: --- he clicked on a Chicago camera, on a regular house in a residential area of Pilsen, and what popped up was a New York city block with actual buildings. Also, even though there's no water to be seen, he exits the camera footage, opens the map again, and is hovering over the Chicago River. --- at one point, he clicked a camera in Maryland, it opened on a shot that looked like Nebraska or Kansas but he labelled it as Memphis, then he closed it and the map appeared over Nevada. --- later, he clicked on another Chicago camera and it opened at Georgetown University (near DC). Stupid in a lot of ways, but my expectations were already so low that I didn't actually hate this like I was led to believe by all my mutuals. I rate my movies based on my emotional reaction, so my rating reflects this. I'm not going to defend it, it is a bad movie and has a lot of flaws to it, but I went into this assuming it would make me want to kill myself and it only got THAT bad a few specific times. It's all just boring and lazy, falling victim to and continuing the theme of my least favorite movies from this year that only ever hit notes we've seen in other movies before but even worse. I'd barely rank it over The Electric State and The Monkey, at least this is tonally consistent. Also this completely feels like it was made to cater to quarantine (originally filmed in 2020, at least according to the sometimes reliable World of Reel), so I genuinely wonder why it was put off for 5 years lmao. I liked when the president actually called it the War of the Worlds.
Tags: amazon prime, at home, w/ dad

Name: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly
Review: Beautiful, mesmerizing, badass, iconic cinema on the big screen 😍😍😍 Yeah I’m totally blown away again. Walked out of the theater with a huge smile as my dad and I talked about how good it was. A perfect example of how when you see a movie at two different ages, you see two different movies. There’s sooo much I could say after this rewatch, but I’m most immediately fascinated by how this subverts expectations for its violence. Aside from the final few glorious standoffs, there’s still tons of violence and killing to be found, but really, this is a story revolving around the idea of fates worse than death. The meat of the story lies in leaving you wondering whether or not the violence you expect from scene to scene will even occur at all, or if characters have to continue suffering. It’s brutal, but it makes for such an enthralling, endlessly surprising watch, somehow all feeling completely earned and genuinely moving me as it progressed. There’s so much more to say, but I truly loved it. Genuinely might be a 10, there isn’t anything that I would change about it on this watch… For the first time since I declared this, The Assassination of Jesse James has competition for the title of my favorite western.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ dad

Name: Together
Review:
NEON horror movies have a good final act challenge (IMPOSSIBLE)

First two acts were really good! Genuinely interesting portrayal of codependency and the dangers of not committing to yourself enough while in a relationship that actually got some reactions out of me. The imagery is really interesting as the mystery slowly builds as well, remaining strong despite some of the dialogue that I felt was undercutting it a bit along the way. Then the final act… it becomes a comedy ?? Obviously this is a goofy concept on paper, but the movie was actually succeeding at getting some interesting depth out of it, at least until it suddenly becomes self-aware and ironic and leans so far into this that it abandons the genuinely good emotional threads in favor of a series of rushed moments that, at best, really just do the bare minimum and tie up the story. I was really underwhelmed after how I was moved up until then.

I feel bad saying this, since I was genuinely rooting for this movie. Love Alison Brie, love seeing original horror thrive, and love seeing genuinely good and bizarre effects in the genre… but why did Michael Shanks throw the weight of it all out the window in the final act :/ I liked this more than not because of the emotions it got out of me in the first 2/3 and the potential here, but it becomes a great argument for movies in the 2020s becoming too ironic and failing to commit to real emotions in favor of something annoyingly and unnecessarily comedic

Name: McCabe & Mrs. Miller
Review:
My first watch of an essential piece of the Peter movie canon.

Kind of miraculous! I watched this without subtitles and after my ADHD pill wore off, which at first I figured might be a deadly combo, but the way Altman writes and directs conversations with so much overlapping dialogue while the exact right important lines both stick out and feel natural is something I honestly don’t think I’ve seen before. Between that and just how much information and storytelling is packed into each shot, I was genuinely able to follow along fine! On that note, atmosphere building and cinematography in this… completely gorgeous. Tries so many things that you wouldn’t expect to fit into a Pacific North-Western, but again, somehow it all works here. Altman was smoking the maverick strain while making this, and we love that here.

That being said, the fact that I still feel like I missed some important pieces while watching (despite following along fine) keeps me from rating this any higher, at least on first impression, as I feel there’s a greater connection to this movie that I haven’t had yet. But don’t be mistaken, I thought this was great. A movie that you don’t fully grasp on just one watch, but one that still is concerned with offering you a full emotional experience / not making you feel stupid without two watches that you’d be happy to watch again and grasp more of without pushback.

Also 70s movie hair continues to make its way into time periods and locations where it doesn’t fit… love a good movie starring Warren Beatles.
Tags: peter's criterion blu-ray, paulina apartment, w/ peter

Name: Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone
Review: HARRY POTTER IN THEATERS YAYYY Haven’t seen this in 5 years but still remembered almost all of it beat for beat. First half was a bit more of a sit than I remember, but the Quidditch and Wizard’s Chess matches were totally captivating on the big screen and many emotions were felt as I let all the nostalgia wash over me. 2001 CGI Voldemort's face is so uncanny valley and that’s scarier than the whole body amalgamation, I’m so glad they eventually got Ralph Fiennes. Good shit. “TROOOLLLL IN THE DUNGEON”

Name: Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets
Review: No matter how heavy this is on plot conveniences, expository dialogue, or the corniness of certain plot elements, this one still gets me emotional like the rest of the series does. Harry’s arm after Quidditch and Hermione’s potion are still two of the funniest scenes in the entire franchise. Also, despite the occasional outdated CGI, it can never be understated how immersive all these movies are, and I think this one has underrated cinematography. It’s consistently visually interesting and does a genuinely great job at revealing details. The whole screenplay has really good plant and payoff as well, it’s a fun mystery. I love da movies. “It’s not our abilities, it’s our choices.”

Name: Highest 2 Lowest
Review: Really good as it is, even if it would’ve been even better if it separated itself from Kurosawa’s original more. I do really like Spike’s reinterpretation through the use of the music industry, social media, and how A$AP Rocky’s character was poised as an aspiring major artist disgruntled by how hard it is to make it as a creative in 2025, to the degree that I certainly think this is one of the more timely and era-defining movies of the year, but the story still follows High and Low very closely to the point that Spike’s new ideas feel a bit held back and I was taken out of the immersion comparing the two in my head and knowing where things would ultimately go. It’s not helped by distracting self-insert easter eggs sprinkled throughout either (an apartment’s number is A24, at the center of a frame in clear focus no less, and at one point a girl says the name of her song is the movie’s title), although there are some interesting homages as well. (Denzel’s rival company is called Stray Dog) Still, a good movie worth seeing in theaters with a lot to praise about it. The ensemble is genuinely fantastic throughout, Spike Lee direction + Matthew Libatique cinematography makes for a deadly and delicious visual combo, the original score is excellent and one of the best of the year so far when it’s used well, and even it I wasn’t necessarily on board with every visual or aural swing on set or during post-production, seeing Spike Lee continue to stand out as one of the more singular creative voices in Hollywood is a treat. On first impression, a strong 7.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ dad

Name: Gallipoli
Review: Some very beautiful cinematography and great performances in this. Love the attention to detail with historical context here as well, everyone was dressed like a gay stripper. I just wish there was a more coherent emotional throughline than just these two guys going off to war, I wasn’t as moved by the end as I expected and thought the final act felt too much like a typical war movie final act, even though that final shot was an admirable swing. The main duo is very watchable for sure, as is the whole thing, but I wasn’t very gripped by them. Good movie, but would rather rewatch Paths of Glory or Lawrence of Arabia.
Tags: paulina apartment, w/ peter, w/ david

Name: Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban
Review: Jadyn and I going on our adventures is just like Harry and Hermione doing all their time traveling shenanigans. Still not a single bad scene in the movie, and seeing this in a big theater was as wonderful as I had expected. Not a single bad scene in the movie, still with so many beautiful individual moments and Cuaron’s genuinely great directing; this is the movie that most embraces the cross between gothic horror and screwball comedy that the first couple only seemed to toy around with. The way Cuaron plays with camera movement seems to succeed every single time, the images of landscapes and nature are all great touches, and some of these shots just look like damn paintings. Additionally, it’s such a good encapsulation of not only all the positive themes that the franchise is about, such as identity, fate vs choice, what truly makes someone evil, etc. etc. but it’s easily the best of the bunch about looking at situations through multiple lens with how Sirius’s character arc goes from mysterious villain to actually being Harry’s new family; it’s an amazing twist and one of my favorite arcs in the series. I love it. Aside from a couple moments and performances that still read as a little goofy to me, there are no other flaws. The only Harry Potter movie in my forever changing top 100 favorites.

Name: Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire
Review:
“Everything’s going to change now, isn’t it?”

Holy shit, I’m… kinda speechless. Watching this in theaters helped, but even without that, I was so much more taken with this. The second-hand embarrassment of watching Harry after his name is announced and the awkwardness of the Yule Ball still very much exists, but those are my only complaints here. I found it funnier, more engaging, I felt more invested in all the minor threads along the way, and I didn’t catch any of the occasional boredom that has marked my more recent watches of this.

As soon as we cut to the stadium before the final trial begins, my heart never fails to skip a beat knowing what’s to come. I’m completely locked in from there to the end, and that feeling was even more elevated in a theater late at night. And the way it continues to build and get more terrifying until the climax is brilliant.

When that main thing happens at the end, I cried at it when I was younger, around 13, but I haven’t found myself crying at it these last few watches. But, being in a theater with other people and hearing their sniffles echo… I actually came close to it for the first time in a while. It was a powerful feeling, and made for a genuinely divine experience.

This might not hit the same joy or nostalgia as the previous entry, but the level of emotional charge is a HUGE step up from the previous entries and it completely works. I now fully think this is a great movie, perhaps now in my Top 3 Potters but I will decide for sure after I finish them all once again.

Strong 8.

Name: Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix
Review: Fun to see this in theaters for the first time, as I lost myself in the world like I do with all the movies, but even despite my renewed love for the first four movies this one still felt kinda… sauceless. Kinda like the Community Season 4 of the franchise. It’s so plot heavy and the more intimate character moments are mostly all just passable, save for the moments between Sirius and Harry. All the stuff in the Room of Requirement is still lots of fun, but the Ministry battles at the end didn’t live up like I hoped they might, and why is Sirius’s death edited to be so corny :/ not a bad movie, but that might just be nostalgia talking since I did like this more as a kid. Wish I had more positive to say about it, but it’s still my least favorite. And at least David Yates improves with each entry, from what I remember.
Tags: regal city north, w/ mary b

Name: Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince
Review:
So easily the weirdest one, lmao. I still kinda like it though. Everyone being on drugs (after being taught how to break bad by Slughorn) and not having any idea how to communicate romantic feelings are both played up way more than I remember. And, to be honest, the movie isn’t fantastic at blending tones, sometimes feeling too corny as a contrast from all the dramatic stuff to be taken seriously, but I’d be lying if I said I wasn’t at least a little entertained by it all. And the main Harry and Draco storylines are still effective in their own rights. The actual Half Blood Prince storyline is barely a part of this though which is kinda funny.

Also, I stand by this being a well shot film, courtesy of Bruno Delbonnel, although I was a little more put off by some of the color grading and green screens than I remember. It’s more inconsistent on these fronts, but it hits when it hits. The horcrux cave scene looks fantastic, and I love the contrast in the bathroom fight between Draco and Harry. The blood is the only thing in color, and it makes for some super striking images.

All around, I might be a bit generous here, but I still don’t think it’s as bad as some people say. Pretty good movie despite its more glaring flaws.
Tags: regal city north, w/ mary b

Name: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows: Part 1
Review: 15 years ago, I was first getting into Harry Potter when the previews for this came out, and I was definitely disappointed when my parents wouldn’t take 7 y/o Josh to see it in theaters. Tonight, in the same amount of time between Harry’s parents’ deaths and the start of Half-Blood Prince, I finally got my long awaited theatrical experience of this movie. It was glorious and a wonderful experience for that reason alone. Even with all that aside, this is a really good movie. After these rewatches, this flip-flopped with HBP as what I believe to be the most underrated. It feels almost painfully like a Part 1 in the end, I can’t imagine being edged like that in 2010 then waiting several months for Part 2, but up until the abrupt ending I genuinely don’t think there’s a bad scene in this. In particular, the air battle at the start is wicked fun, everything at Godric’s Hollow stands out, and that damn animated sequence is brilliant. And I honestly do think the slowness of all the traveling works well for its intentions and place in the story, I was never bored with it. On that note, I was struck by just how much of this I had forgotten. I remembered everything through the Ministry infiltration beat for beat, and how could I forget the final scenes from Malfoy Manor to the end, but everything in between after they escape had genuinely just left my mind. It was a real treat rediscovering it all, not just realizing that it’s actually quite good but also getting to have that experience in a theater! Also has really good cinematography (those landscape shots!) and solid VFX (except for a couple green screens), the character moments are all effective, it’s more tonally coherent than the previous entry, and overall I honestly do appreciate how much this separates itself from the rest of the franchise. It’s the darkest one, and really commits to it while still remaining engaging and not straying away from who these characters are. Doesn’t quite reach the same nostalgic joy or emotional charge as the first four, but it’s really not that far off and in the same tier for me. Still deciding how I feel about the abrupt ending before rating again, but it’s either a strong 7 or low 8 (most likely the latter).
Tags: regal city north, w/ mary b

Name: Meantime
Review: Really expertly made as all Mike Leigh films are, there’s just something about how he writes and directs such specifically personal conversations. Hard to rate and compare though, since this one ended up making me feel worse even despite having his usual catharsis at the end; it tapped into some very topical emotions for me that I usually feel more comfortable avoiding unless they’re really pressing me. Ended up being a bit of a weird watch, but the craft on display delivers for the majority of it at least.

Name: Lurker
Review:
Pretty phenomenal actually, until the final act I thought. I don’t even think the final act is bad, and it’s at least tonally consistent which is more than many recent horror movies can say, it just felt a little rushed in how it conveys its ideas and lets the comedy take over too much for my liking when I was hoping for a more emotionally truthful catharsis.

Still, what it says about celebrity culture, the need for validation, and self-image cut deeply for me, especially with how pervasive celebrity culture and obsession still is and how it finds its way into all our lives. And the way Alex Russell created such an uncomfortable, psychologically torturous environment with almost nothing scary explicitly happening on screen is an accomplishment; the screenplay, performances, and camerawork do wonders. Could’ve just used some screenplay polishing at the end is all.

Name: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows: Part 2
Review: All the money and effort I put into seeing the entire damn franchise in theaters this week was worth it for the cathartic cry I had watching the final moment on the big screen. Not even seeing this at Regal Webster could ruin it. An absolutely wonderful, unforgettable moment.
Tags: regal webster place, me time!, cried or teared up

Name: Caught Stealing
Review:
I’m getting just a little tired of 2025 movies with final scenes that weasel out into something either funny or easy to end with instead of having a more emotionally or thematically satisfying conclusion; why put us through all that if the final thought is just “damn that sucks”? Especially since this felt like it was building to that and had me interested literally until the final 5 minutes, making the ending feel all the more abrupt and like there was still more story to be told.

Nonetheless, this is well directed, thoroughly engaging, and a lot of fun that’s entertainingly confident in itself. All the comedic and dramatic beats worked and never felt like they were unnecessarily overpowering the other, the whole ensemble is having a lot of fun while still feeling confident in their story, and I do think interesting things are being said here about self-destructiveness, forgiving and separating yourself from your past mistakes, and the dangers of letting your strong emotions take over; the humanity that exists in the characters and how these themes are tackled was enough to almost convince me to be an 8. I just wish they felt a little less surface level and had a better ending to tie them all together.

Matthew Libatique is 2/2 on serving as DP for 2025 films set in New York that I’ve rated a strong 7.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Incredibles
Review:
Turned 22 years old today and this is still very possibly my favorite movie of all time. A few things I appreciated in particular this time around:
- The scene of Dash spitting water and Frozone freezing it and catching it mid air is somehow one of the hardest things ever put to the silver screen
- The callback to Bob playing catch with Dash when he throws the remote at the end is so simple but touching and perfect
- The themes of refusing to develop and struggling to accept where you are in life hit hard as someone about to embark on their senior year of college thinking a lot about how I’m gonna be an independent adult a year from now. There’s always something emotionally engaging going on here no matter how old you are and it’s fucking awesome
- Watching this with my cinematographer roommate really allowed me to appreciate just how immaculate every shot in this is and just how good the small details are, given that they’d been invisible to me for so long. It’s all about the camera effects and cuts that you DON’T see!!
- Still quite possibly the most emotionally mature and resonant family for me in any media ever. Perfect movie 20/10 #1 of all time etc. etc.

Name: The English Patient
Review:
Has charming individual moments for sure and I was somewhat moved in the moment, as well as some stunning cinematography, but it’s a long movie for my main complement to be "charming individual moments". And while I respect the fact that the movie did get some emotions out of me, I already am questioning if they'll resonate since this felt like two different movies were going on at the same time, with certain plot threads cutting between each other and distracting from the impacts of others.

The Fiennes-Thomas romance was interesting and watchable, definitely my favorite part of this story, but it could've been much better if this took center stage. Willem Dafoe's character barely had any reason to be here, Binoche's character didn't need this much screentime; 80% of the time we cut back to these two, I was just itching to go back. Not horrible, but its lack of focus frustratingly affects the emotional experience and overall I wish I liked this more than I actually do.

Name: Splitsville
Review: I think this is a good movie but part of me thinks I either went in with the wrong expectations, or this isn't as much my cup of tea (the couple in my row blabbering during almost all of the first act didn't help). I was expecting (and, to be honest, hoping for) stronger emotional beats and payoff, but in reality this is much more focused on satire and laughs while only having genuine emotional beats a few select times. It's certainly a funny and entertaining movie and the comedy does feel intelligent as it explores what it means to have multiple serious emotional commitments, though I'd be lying if I said I didn't feel a little alienated by it as well for how much I felt like a spectator at times, especially during the first half before most of the depth is revealed. Good stuff overall though, could maybe see this growing on me.

Name: A Bug's Life
Review: “You’re weird. But I like you.” Must a Pixar movie be life changing?? Can it not just be a simple narrative that’s extremely likable with a lot of gorgeous shots and world building as good as any of their others?? This actually was just a total joy to watch in theaters though. Hadn’t seen this in at least 10 years and that was only ever on VHS, so this felt like a first watch all over again. Not the most amazing story or anything, but this movie knows exactly what it is and owns that, and watching it just made me really happy. I had a smile on my face the whole time and that’s just what I wanted, and enough for me to bump this up to 4 stars. Also the animated bloopers on the big screen were so awesome.

Name: Finding Nemo
Review:
Maybe deserves a higher rating since this really is a wonderful movie, but as with my other Pixar rewatches of the last 2-3 years for the first time since childhood, I was doing a lot of soaking in and remembering exactly how everything comes together. It’s largely still beautifully animated (some things definitely aged better than others), it’s hilarious (seagulls might be the funniest side characters in Pixar history still), the emotions run high and it flows wonderfully from scene to scene, the father son stuff got to me… maybe it is a 9.

But who really cares about the rating? I had a great time rewatching this, that’s all there is to it.

Name: Toy Story
Review: I’m stuck on a Pixar kick, lmao. It's awesome though, it's been hitting hard with where my head's been at and has helped a lot as I've transitioned back into school mode. New things I recognized or appreciated: - when the Pizza Planet driver arrives back at the restaurant, he takes a handicapped spot. - Rex is hilarious. "Great! Now I have guilt..." - Sid’s room is insane. This dude who’s probably in late elementary school or early middle school has barbed wire on his bedframe, unlimited access to whatever tools and explosives his parents will buy for him, and is a huge metalhead who wants to ride the pony. On that last note, I kinda love how little detail we get about his own life and family life, even if I wish I knew a little bit more of his past since he’s obviously a really complicated kid, but I suppose we get just enough (especially in his dad’s room) to effectively use imagination. - kinda just the perfect screenplay. Like, they could not have written this thing more to a perfect T in every way. Obviously with this being maybe the most formative movie to my childhood, it doesn’t really have the element of surprise, but it’s SO refined and smart and well made still with new things to appreciate that I might bump it up at some point. But, again, ratings don’t actually matter. This is a fantastic movie and it was an absolute joy to experience in theaters again.
Tags: amc newcity 14, w/ alek, w/ peter, w/ jake p

Name: Sunrise: A Song of Two Humans
Review:
Ravishing!! Took me some time to process certain character and story decisions earlier on, but after the 1/3 mark the complexities started to come together once they slowly make up and from there to the end, this is just such an engaging and joyful portrayal of the feeling of re-affirming the love in your life after you’ve been questioning it, it’s super dreamlike and not always realistic but that just added to it for me, representing how this feeling is so strong that you don’t care to question any other details. I was genuinely so taken by it just on pure emotional attachment.

Nearly every emotional beat hit even on first watch, the cinematography and editing are gorgeous, the production design of the amusement park was mindblowing, and that final scene… just a beauty. I don’t wanna get too ahead of myself and make big proclamations that make me question my own initial reaction, but this is probably the most moved I’ve ever been by a silent film and something I could definitely see sticking with me for a while. Loved.
Tags: youtube, paulina apartment, w/ peter, w/ alek

Name: Malcolm X
Review:
Very formal as a biopic in a storytelling sense, but it's never boring despite the runtime and the heavier emphasis on and intimacy with Malcolm's character growth was really well done and emotionally engaging; I loved how this has more prominence than the dramatization of the famous historical events and prioritizes making Malcolm feel fully realized.

Denzel and the entire ensemble are as good as I've heard, the pacing is crazy, and it looks great visually. There were a few directing choices that took some of the impact out of the final couple scenes for me, but aside from that I don't have any major complaints. High 8 on first impression, could go up to a 9; this was an overwhelming first watch with all there is to the movie and I feel like there's more still to be processed, but it is fantastic.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ alex b

Name: All the President's Men
Review:
The way we paused the movie so I could get water, thinking we were about to enter the final act, but it turned out there was only 5 minutes left… I could’ve watched at least another 30 minutes of this movie, if not longer.

Fantastic representation of scatterbrained people who have to write everything down on paper, I heavily related to that. Loved when Robert Redford (RIP) said he was from Wheaton, IL. Also phenomenal directing, cinematography, sound, and editing all the way through; my first Pakula and I can already tell he’s the kind of great filmmaker that knows how to use the smallest of touches, like a slight zoom or a cut to black, to draw you in more than anything else, sometimes in ways I’ve never seen before. The whole ensemble is excellent as well, of course.

Lots of fascinating looks at how people behave when they’re confronted with confessing the truth they presumed to always be hidden, definitely something here that I wanna think about. Didn’t personally connect to me as much as I was observing everyone’s actions as a spectator, but I was still pretty transfixed; pretty brilliant unfolding of the mystery and tons of great lines in the screenplay. All around great movie, no rating yet but it’s gonna be high.
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, w/ alek, w/ peter

Name: The Baltimorons
Review: I feel so blessed to live in a time when we get yearly indie Christmas bangers (The Holdovers, Miller’s Point, and now this) This is much more than the stereotype associated with Christmas movies can offer though. A rare example of a predictable movie that I fell head over heels for. I could see where it would likely end up at the end (and it did), but of course it’s the reasons why they end up there that make the movie great, and I can think of few other movies in recent memory that emotionally resonated like this did. This might be THAT indie movie of the year for me that isn’t overtly new territory, but just worked so well for me. I could say things like the characters are so phenomenal and memorable and well performed here, the balance of hilarity and heart is so tender and always worked for me with some moments in particular that cut really deeply (the mental health then and now comparisons in particular), the way the movie feels so lived in its titular city and creates such a cozy, welcoming environment really caught me by surprise, the 16mm cinematography was GREAT, and I was just smiling or tenderly listening the entire time. And these are all incredibly true, but they’ve been said about every other acclaimed indie movie. You gotta get a little personal to describe its effect on you. I’m happy to say even online to whoever the fuck gets here that I’m at a point where I’m really fucking grateful for where my life is, I’ve had an amazing run the last few months and I’m stoked for everything in my future, but it hasn’t always been this way of course and 4-5 years ago I was in a way worse spot. I think about this every now and again, wondering if I’m feeling solid about all this or if I actually have an Achilles heel that could make it all come crashing down. Still, getting in my head just as an individual action is usually the worst it gets, and having largely overcome my sensitivity to this has really done more than enough to convince me I'm both solid and that I'm more resilient than I give myself credit for when things DO happen and I don't let it get me down. And, that’s kinda exactly what this movie is about! Combine that with just how much fun it is to follow along with these characters, and tons of smaller mannerisms and lines that reflect all the above similarly to how I do… yeah it’s an incredibly topical banger. I obviously can’t guarantee this experience for everyone, and who knows if this was just an amazing first watch given where my head's at right now? But if you have any inkling for an indie romcom with genuine, memorable personality, the full range of emotions, a lived-in Americana setting, and probably the most heart out of anything I’ve seen this year… it is incredibly likable if nothing else. My heart feels a little fuller with this now in my life.

Name: Megadoc
