Review: Coppola: “The cinematic medium is the death of the very thing it’s trying to preserve.” A well made documentary of fascinating glimpses into the pre-production and shooting of Megalopolis, which admittedly feels a bit limited by its 100 minute runtime but has some seriously compelling moments. For the most part, however, as someone passionate about filmmaking and the very existence of this movie, the footage here is priceless and does a great job creating a compelling series of vignettes from pre-production and shooting. It offers everything from entertaining footage of people both having fun and arguing on set (and there’s a lot of that!), sure to please anyone here for celebrity gossip, to interviews and candid footage of Coppola and several actors peeling back the layers and discussing what this film means to them and what wisdom they’ve gained over the course of their careers, both related to film and not, more than satisfying all the film nerds; I know I was geeking out at a number of points. My favorite part of the film, in fact, is actually how Figgis covers everything involving Shia LeBeouf and Coppola. In the hands of a lot of other people, these countless heated arguments (there's many more than you expect of Shia picking fights, I cannot imagine working with this fucking guy) would simply just be celebrity gossip/tabloids footage. But Figgis uses these fights to elevate the story in such a way that's both uncomfortably entertaining and shockingly deep as it peels back the layers of Coppola and forces him to defend both his vision for the film itself and what it all means to him, in poignant, thought-provoking remarks, many of the best moments of this documentary. It's an expert blend of comedy and heart. The main issue comes at the very end. After the last day of production, it cuts to a very brief montage of the Cannes premiere that really is just an underwhelming ending, both in that it doesn’t really offer anything beyond “the movie was finished and released” (no shit) and completely skips over everything in between. I’m not gonna hold this against Figgis as much as I can, because who knows if his footage of the editing process didn’t warrant a section about it, or it was pulled for legal reasons, or something else entirely, but without an answer or explanation it’s pretty jarring and a lame final note. Nonetheless, what we have up to that point really is very good. And despite the less than stellar final note, I’d definitely recommend this to all my fellow film people. Was originally a 7, and probably would be if I had less interest in the subject matter, given the flaws, but I've bumped it up to an 8 because the way Figgis utilizes the Shia-Coppola arguments has only grown on me.

Name: Phantom Thread
Review:
First watch was over 5 years ago on my computer late at night and I remembered basically nothing about it.

Ravishing, with a touch of wanting to think about and process the story a little further. Love a movie that delivers on all the emotions while also getting you to think about it all and not feel fully confident thanks to surprising character behavior, as PTA does so well. Maybe not the most emotionally resonant ending ever for me, but it certainly fit the story and that response could just be where I am in life right now talking. Love seeing an autistic-coded guy who’s really good at what he does reluctantly experience changes in his life as he eventually grows and embraces love in his life. (remind you of anything else?)

Jonny Greenwood’s score is honestly in contention with TWBB for his best work; I completely forgot just how transportive the music is here, as are the performances and every technical filmmaking aspect of course. Some of the shots, especially near the beginning, were framed in a way and had camera movements that hearken so clearly back to Golden Age Hollywood that, were it shot on film stock contemporary to that time period, I probably would’ve been fooled; that delighted me. The 35mm print this was shown on looked fantastic nonetheless.

A strong 8 on this first impression, who knows how that'll change as I grow and have a more experienced love life.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Jackass 3D
Review: Shoutout to the two older ladies who joined me in seeing Jackass 3D in theaters at 8:00 PM on a Monday night. I’ve really grown to love these movies with time and I honestly can’t explain why, and seeing this in theaters in 3D for my first time only made that even stronger. By all means it’s brainless entertainment, but I just eat this shit up. Film is all about showing you things you’d never see otherwise, and this is the one of the most down to earth yet completely insane examples of that. Seriously, the physical reactions I had watching this rival any body horror I’ve seen and I haven’t laughed this hard in a theater (or at any movie) in months, despite having seen some very funny movies recently. And this unironically might have some of the best stunts in movie history. Easily the best one I’ve seen so far and just an absolute blast from start to finish, left with a huge smile that I still have as I’m writing this. Why I don’t kick off every school week by doing this is beyond me.

Name: The Battle of Algiers
Review: Not a particularly fun morning watch but undeniably a great and powerful film. Some really fantastic editing here and a gripping story in how un-cinematic the beats and the way it unfolds both are. The whole thing is very neorealist inspired, from all the above to how grounded and intimate all the imagery and performances are, and I wouldn’t have had it any other way given the strength of my initial reaction. Love the way it uses music as well. Tons of really hard-hitting moments, both in battle and in conversation; I sound like a broken record watching old political movies and saying “tHiS iS rElEvAnT tOdAy” but I was genuinely struck by how many lines of dialogue are close to verbatim in the conversation surrounding the Palestinian genocide and any example of modern colonialism. Interesting how the film almost exclusively takes place in Algiers itself with nothing in France; everything we learn here is in some way revealed through the battle and the living conditions of the city. On that note, this is less a movie about educating the audience on the real events (outside of contextualizing the setting and characters which is of course a necessity) and more a devastating portrayal of violence as the only means of pushing forward. And it’s all effective, and certainly has left its lasting impression. I thought a good bit about City of God and La Haine in particular while watching, although I know countless other filmmakers have mentioned it. It doesn't have the same personal hook as the two films I mentioned above do, and as a result this feels more concerned with dramatizing the events rather than offering something more to latch onto. For that reason it's not 100% my cup of tea, but this is just personal preference and not a knock. Plus, as far as dramatizations of real events go, it's such an achievement in how it utilizes real locations and non-actors to portray a more authentic story, and it’s a gripping experience outside of that. Great stuff overall.

Name: Riefenstahl
Review: Feels like a museum documentary, but I was definitely engaged from start to finish. I don’t even mean that as a criticism, more just a general note of how I’d describe it. I admit that I have bias in that I’ve written two papers for school where Leni and her work held some central focus, and I’ve grown to find her one of the most fascinating figures in film history. But regardless, the direction and edit definitely make some really interesting points about Leni’s honesty as a person, her compliance with and eventual departure from her work with the Nazis, her views on art vs politics, and all her squabbles with interviewers over the years. Certainly strikes me as a complete enough portrait that could’ve been accomplished in a sub-2 hour runtime. Great final scene. And bonus points for allowing me the opportunity to see even a little bit of Olympia in a theater, that was a cool treat.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: One Battle After Another
Review:
You can't spell Mo Bamba without OBAA

Pretty awesome stuff. It’s overwhelming and left me with enough to think about at every turn that my brain is kinda just tired of deep critical thinking for the day (circumstantial and not a critique of the movie), but it's so sheerly, unmistakably PTA's own unique world that I mostly don't mind as I continue to unwrap this in my head. And I wasn't unmoved by the end! Could also just be exhaustion from political movies and shit that makes me say "this is relevant today," I've been saying that with a lot of movies recently and I think I need to start using them as more of an escape.

But anyways, yeah PTA is probably one of the most unpredictable storytellers working in film that we have, and this absolutely kept that promise going. This is definitely a movie that made me feel more like a spectator than intimately connected with the characters, which I usually don't jive with as much (just personal preference), but it's so thoroughly entertaining, looks brilliant visually, and has enough human heart shining through each scene that it's one of the best possible outcomes of a movie that makes me feel like a spectator. I had a lot of fun with it.

I’m very tired right now from a lot of things so I don’t feel like getting any deeper right now, but I genuinely look forward to continuing to process it all in the coming days/weeks. And right now, that’s the most telling sign that I think this is great and something pretty special.
Tags: music box theater, 70mm, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ cam

Name: Crash
Review: Coming within inches of collision and surviving actual crashes as the thrill of living dangerously, car crashes as a form of power and domination, utilizing cars, their features, and their appearances as representative character traits to the most intimate degree, predicted the future of people obsessed with the grotesque photos they don’t want you to see, the use of smoke and windows and hoods to shroud the sex and keep you wondering how much you’re going to see, the whole damn movie immersing you in a world with the startling excitement of watching something you feel like you shouldn’t be seeing. Somehow just coasts through the entire runtime on the immersion and sexy atmosphere alone; any more story or plot or logical explanation would get you to think about it too much and ruin the feeling. Everyone is so immaculately cast, Howard Shore’s score kills, it looks visually insane and uses technology and architecture to in such a unique way, and I was genuinely locked in and feeling like I was seeing something new. It’s not as emotionally devastating or personal as something like The Fly, but it might be just as good for how shocking and fascinating and well executed it all is. Somehow haven't even talked about the James Dean scenes yet either, among all the above. Absolutely loved this. Unrelated -- thinking a lot again about how popular Crash is in the Chicago film scene. In the last 2-3 years, it's shown at both Facets and the Siskel, and it sold out completely both times before I was able to get tickets. I get it now, I get the appeal of seeing this in a sold out theater. I can't wait for it to come back.
Tags: criterion blu-ray, paulina apartment, w/ peter, w/ alek

Name: The Long Walk
Review: David Jonsson pulled out The Long Cock in that one scene hehe It’s good! Of course, the King short story is a great metaphor for a number of different things, and those came across pretty effectively here. Having gotten just a little worn out from all the overtly political movies recently, it was a nice change in pace for me to watch something still political in undertones where human relationships and a thrilling journey of what-would-I-do-in-that-situation take center stage instead. A lot of very watchable and endearing conversations between the characters, only with the occasional line of dialogue that felt weirdly delivered or out of place; some really good performances certainly help. The way American geography was visually portrayed here was pretty great as well. I didn’t necessarily deeply connect with this on any level, and a number of moments feel overly cinematic and/or overly gory, but it just works well for what it is, the emotional experience is largely consistent (I actually cared about these character dynamics), and overall I’d call it a worthwhile time at the movies. I liked it!
Tags: amc newcity 14, w/ carmen, spooky season 2025

Name: The Tree of Life
Review: The whole thing is still as heady and emotional as I remembered (complementary), probably even moreso after 3.5 years of living and growing since my first watch, but... words really can't do justice for how strongly I reacted to everything from the beginning until the first few minutes of the childhood memories. The emotional synthesis of everything up to the montage of Jack as a baby representing the joys of the beginning of life against all the cosmic, existential, overwhelming ruminations on life and death... it hit the exact right spot and already had me choking and tearing up. Really incredible stuff. I can't put into coherent words yet how I feel about everything after that point or the movie at large, but it's already got me thinking existentially about my own life and the many ways that I related to Jack when I was his ages as he grew up... just like all the Malick movies, it's something you soak up in the moment and let it hit you harder after you've experienced the whole story. Still a really amazing movie and just completely beautiful in theaters. I love my everlasting journey of growth and my family and movies and art. This really is one of the best film portrayals of all the above. Only reason why a half star is off - I wish there was more emotional resolution between the end of the childhood memories and the end of the entire film; it immediately jumps from the end of the flashback into the final cosmic montage, and I think there was room for a stronger, more unexpected emotional hit. But even then, the ending is really beautiful as is, and talking and thinking about it on the ride home has already gotten me to reevaluate and appreciate it for what it is. Either way, I have now seen the theatrical cut twice, so I wanna watch the director's cut next to see if this was changed at all between the two (and just to have that experience in general). Absolutely love the theatrical cut still. "The only way to be happy is to love."
Tags: gene siskel film center, cried or teared up, w/ peter, w/ matt r

Name: The Beyond
Review: Hilariously dumb with randomly amazing special effects and a killer opening scene (movie peaks there). Also convinced that this was a blank check for the makeup artists and no one else involved. At a whole 86 minutes, this felt longer than One Battle After Another; very cool of you to lay out the plot twist the whole story revolves around in the first 5 minutes then dance around the same point for the rest of the runtime. Stupid fun though, I had some good laughs. The dog is the best actor here.
Tags: tubi, paulina apartment, w/ peter, w/ alek, spooky season 2025

Name: Tron
Review: Tron: Legacy had a decently notable presence in my family growing up and I still look back at it pretty fondly, and I’m just as shocked that it’s taken me this long to finally watch the original, hehe There’s an extended sequence around the middle of this that’s a total blast. After a slower but thoroughly watchable buildup, this sequence completely embraces why this world is so much fun to watch with a lightcycle battle, a long chase scene after they escape, and even has some effective moments of joyful peace after all the chaos. There’s even some really funny moments, like the guy who was just a human insurance ad, or how the movie plays around with visual comedy. It really hearkens back to the fun of the best 80s movies in this sequence. Unfortunately, this is where the movie peaks, as the remainder of the movie reverts back to the dramatic story more than the fun of what I mentioned above, and… I honestly just don’t care about the dramatic story as much as I wish I did. I know a lot of these characters are computer avatars, but damn I did not care about some of these arcs. I didn’t particularly care at the start either, but at least I could feel that we were building to something. That last third is just kinda frictionless when there isn’t any physical action. The supreme leader just being the computer from Super Best Friends (South Park) was pretty funny though. All that being said, the movie is still having fun with itself when it leans heavily into the dramatic elements, and I enjoyed this more than not. It reminded me a lot of THX-1138 and the original Star Wars (hi George) with a little bit of PlayTime weirdly? But honestly I think this is still shockingly singular with its visuals, worldbuilding, and special effects. Yes, these effects are very dated, but this is a movie about a damn computer software; it fits perfectly in with the time period. And the immersion in the world here is genuinely well executed! Even when I didn’t care about the characters, I still felt very in this universe, and Kurt Russell was clearly having a lot of fun. Just the fact that the movie commits to the bit to the degree it does goes quite a long way given how slow this feels at times. I had fun! Lower 3.5 stars but 3.5 nonetheless.

Name: The Smashing Machine
Review:
Captivating movie about Mark Kerr’s transformation into Dwayne Johnson.

I did like this though! And I mean the above comment as major props to the performances across the board as well as the makeup department. Feels surprisingly structured and not always with the hardest hitting beats at times, the ending in particular didn’t work incredibly well for me, but the raw emotional Safdie power is still prevalent in the characters throughout, making up for it more than not, and the movie certainly is never boring. Pretty fantastic cinematography and editing as well, was surprised to see Benny edited this himself.
Tags: amc newcity 14, w/ aden, w/ alex b

Name: Strange Days
Review: Kathryn Bigelow’s Megalopolis. Was not the movie I expected going in so I wanna sit on it, but I’m a little mixed on first impression (apologies to the many fans). I admire the audacity to tackle all the themes it does and it’s technically well made, but I think the idea works better on paper than in execution, and the noir caricature nature of the characters didn’t really blend with the real-world commentary in a way that hooked me like I was hoping. I was, however, very impressed by how the MiniDisc technology was portrayed and how well all the implications of that have aged, and the POV shots were excellent.

Name: TRON: Legacy
Review:
This isn't a bad movie, but I had a sorta unfortunate realization while watching, that... this becomes a kids movie after a certain point. Been thinking recently about how I still feel a kid in certain ways and why that still is, and it hit me while watching when I realized how obvious this movie is with some of its dialogue and ultimately predictable story in a way that bothered me in a way I wasn't expecting. I think it comes down to having watched a lot of kid or family oriented movies the last month and a half or so, and honestly I think the heavy load of them has made me realize that I'm maybe not acting my age as much as I want to.

And weirdly enough, looking back after it's all over, I don't think the movie has this problem until the main plot actually starts halfway through. It's weird that the movie all of a sudden starts talking down to its audience like a kids movie after not doing so for a while, but at least the first half was actually quite good in this area. And as a result, I let myself start zoning out and the second half didn't hit quite the same, despite some good moments along the way. Still, I'm not sure just how much I want to hold this against the movie itself since a good amount of this comes down to personal reasoning beyond whatever Joseph Kosinski and company intended.

And granted, the whole thing does at least look and sound fantastic. Joseph Kosinski is definitely a director who's good at elevating a basic screenplay into something cooler and more fun than it should be just based on his skills as a director, even if he's too confined to basic screenplays. A lot of the CGI here is just absolutely unreal even in 2025. Even uncanny valley Jeff Bridges was something I got used to after a while. Like the original, the action scenes are the highlights, exciting and tons of fun no matter how old you are. I also do think the story brings up some genuinely interesting ideas about AI and overreliance on technology and all that shit, and the creative worldbuilding is here to back them up... I just wish they didn't fall into the hands of Disney only to have a story that fails to reach the levels of complexity they deserve.

Name: Down by Law
Review: “We are good eggs.” The way everything in this movie unfolds, especially near the end… funny how life works out like that. A little too dry in the first act for my taste perhaps despite still being solid, but once things kick into gear this gets really awesome and is just a delight. Been a while since I’ve seen something this good that I would describe as delightful, this fed my soul. Slightly unrelated, it’s crazy just how much crossover Jarmusch and Wenders have stylistically, probably thanks to Robby Müller as a mutual DP as well as just having similar visions and approaches to how they make their shit. Also funny that Claire Denis was somehow a 1st AD for both of them, lmao. Also: Roberto Benigni is one of my spirit animals and an international treasure. Strong 8.
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, w/ peter

Name: TRON: Ares
Review: The electric state Morbidly perfect representation of Disney blockbusters in 2025, in that this is an insane showboat of what talented visual effects artists, sound mixers, and nails that are nine inches long are capable of, then literally everything else relating to writing and any sort of human emotion feels AI generated. Might be a little generous with my rating, I did really love the first five minutes that were completely driven by visuals and sound before any human opened their mouth, and just the excitement of watching such a spectacle in theaters again took me a moderate distance here; I was honestly rooting for the movie just based on how fucking good it looks and sounds, only with the blink-and-you’ll-miss-it rough greenscreen or obvious ADR. But it’s amazing how quickly all that can become boring when the characters are this thin and the story is this flavorless. The contrast between the visuals and the substance here is just downright baffling… very cool to have your protagonist be a pro-AI tech billionaire with no character traits except a dead sister who teams up and bonds with an AI-generated human (Jared Leto, no less) to fight off the antagonist, her rival pro-AI tech billionaire who’s such an idiot from the get go that there are no stakes, and with an equal lack of any actual character; everyone’s unlikeable and not how they intended! I’m happy for the post-production team and everything, but really fucking cool of Disney to drop $180 million on what feels like a middle finger of a screenplay, because who really cares about humanity and emotions when it looks good! We did what you wanted and made the movie look good!! Very generous 4, mostly gauging the fact that a lower rating could just be initial disappointment speaking but in the heat of the moment I’m feeling a 3… at least I have a greater appreciation for the movies I love that do some aspect of this way, way better.
Tags: amc river east 21, dolby, 3d, me time!

Name: Clue
Review: Unlike Tron: Ares, this was NOT poop from a butt. Haven’t seen since I was 8 and it was a super fun time still. Goes without saying that this is very clever in a very charming screwball sorta way and has tons of fun visual comedy from front to back, and the whole McCarthy element and all the endings were genuinely such good ideas for this adaptation. The positions gag was probably my favorite, but the whole thing is super well realized and joyful of course. Great ensemble too, Tim Curry looks scarily like Elon Musk in certain scenes of this.
Tags: w/ claire s, w/ jadyn, w/ izzy, w/ carmen, spooky season 2025, at claire/jadyn/izzy's place

Name: A House of Dynamite
Review:
I went in expecting an intense, real-time thriller, and this definitely delivered on that and I can’t say I was disappointed; my legs were getting numb and my heart was poundin! The sound design and editing are pretty stellar (as can/should be said for all effective thrillers), and it got me invested in the story to boot, despite the (understandably) loose characterizations. It does get you a little too invested in the characters to have THAT ending, and it’s not the most emotionally satisfying experience after all that stress, making it all feel a bit like hollow fear-mongering, but the physical experience of watching this is at least worthwhile. So for that reason my final verdict is very good, short of great.

Bonus points for the nuclear target being CHICAGO! There’s a moment when this cuts to black and you just hear audio of planes flying overhead and people wondering what’s going on, and… watching that in Chicago after all that happened definitely left me on edge.

Name: Roofman
Review: I fucking cried at the Channing Tatum Toys ‘R Us movie. Getting thoughts out while they’re fresh… A delightful romcom, a fascinating biopic, an intricate crime movie, and all in all one of the absolute best movies I’ve ever seen about how it’s inherently possible to turn a new leaf until you stop running from your past. It’s so, so funny, these characters are all so insanely likable and true to people I’ve met all throughout my life, the underlying dread of knowing what’s to come is always prevalent but never gets in the way of what you should be focusing on… the poster of Channing Tatum balancing is a perfect poster for this because the movie is an unbelievable balancing act of tones and genres. As ridiculous as the concept for this is, the humanity that Derek Cianfrance found in the story and this character made for an experience that had me fully hooked and on board from the first scene to the last and emotionally resonated more than I ever could have imagined. I can’t explain it any better than that right now, I’m still reeling in the reaction I had; this just so fully hit the spot in every damn scene. Aside from one character that I felt was a little underdeveloped for her presence in the story, this is beautiful, hilarious, wonderful stuff. I don’t expect many others to reciprocate my love for this, and that’s fine. I’m owning how much I ate this shit up. The power of film is to completely subvert your expectations of what something can be or even has the potential to be, and this is easily the best example of that for me in a minute.
Tags: amc newcity 14, w/ hazel, cried or teared up

Name: La Promesse
Review:
The Dardennes sure are good at that hyperrealist humanist thing they do!

Seriously though, this was quite good. Works very well as a coming-of-age movie and balances all its social commentary and blunt portrayals of the character flaws of Igor and his dad. All the emotional beats hit, all the performances are really good, I was never bothered by all the handheld camera and the editing all worked, it's just a really solid and investing debut all around. Perhaps a bit too predictable and cinematic in its story structure by the end, but this definitely hit more than not and got a real reaction out of me.

Name: Drugstore Cowboy
Review: Surprisingly Malick coded, which is more positive than not here. Very wistful and drifting in ways that were sometimes alienating and turned me off (Nadine’s character arc in particular rubbed me the wrong way), but ultimately poetic and quietly impactful both in others and overall. Very confident and fittingly uncomfortable (at times) atmosphere and worldbuilding. It’s very on the nose with what it’s saying in some moments, but somehow Van Sant made a world and characters here where it made sense and fit effectively, which is honestly quite impressive. Great music supervision, casting, and cinematography too.
Tags: tubi, paulina apartment, watched while sick

Name: Sirāt
Review:
Fuuuuuck…

This is 100% a film you should go in knowing as little as possible (and with a crowd!!), so I’ll keep my thoughts vague. This is an amazing movie, in that it’s completely unassuming and unpredictable from front to back. There’s an underlying sense of the unknown from the moment the plot kicks in that had me on edge even when nothing bad was happening, and there’s a ton that goes unsaid or seemingly forgotten about, but the fact that the film is able to recoup itself and leave both the emotional and physical impression it does by the end is a real achievement. Cinematography, sound design, the SCORE, and ensemble are all excellent and some of the very best of the year. There’s just enough here that goes unsaid that I’m already questioning if *every* moment of the film is earned, just given how much we aren’t informed of in some way, but the initial reaction I’ve had is so visceral that I can’t give it any lower of a rating than this. A strong 8 on first impression, could definitely go up to a 9 once I process a little further, likely one of the year’s best.

All I wanna do is dance…
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2025, w/ joey, w/ kenny

Name: Sound of Falling
Review: This was… …actually I’m not even sure where to start. What a towering, head-spinning movie. I've been anticipating this ever since it was first announced as The Doctor Says I'll Be Alright, But I'm Feelin' Blue, and I can't say that I was let down. A heavily Terrence Malick inspired movie with a bit of Sofia Coppola but in a much colder European style (I think Tree of Life is Mascha’s favorite movie and God bless her for it) with a screenplay that’s as cerebral as it is, in equal measure, seemingly aimless in direction. A coming of age story spanning three generations about grappling with death and mortality at different young ages through superstition, processing others' sudden unexplainable actions, and imagining what could be if I did this and ended it all right here. But that doesn’t properly explain how I felt about the movie myself. I walked out of the theater with an empty brain of conflicted emotions, but as I’ve pondered the movie on the trip home, one thing that’s stayed consistent is I’ve been staring at the ground and out of CTA windows thinking about the meaning of existence, so the movie absolutely did its job. This is the slowest of burns I’ve seen all year, yet I was transfixed from start to finish, thinking about what here is going on and never once bored; you wanna talk about a film that just completely transports you, this is it. Going back to its seemingly aimless direction, this never once takes an easy way out to reveal its own messages or story logic, but every scene is so rich with intimate moments and humanity that I couldn’t help being moved and getting emotional at several points without ever thinking about or questioning why I felt that way until the movie finished. The attention to minute detail with character behaviors and mannerisms here is truly something to behold, the ensemble is fantastic, and it’s all shown via what I’m so sure is the best cinematography of 2025. While I’m still not sure yet exactly how I feel, I can confidently say that Sound of Falling is something special and a truly groundbreaking breakout feature. While the movie has left me with a lot of questions to ponder, one thing’s for certain, and that’s Mascha Schilinski is an incredibly talented and fearless new voice in cinema. I don’t say this often about movies, but, like a Terrence Malick movie, I actually want a second watch to fully process all the mysteries of the story. (I don’t mean to discredit Mascha’s singularity here with bringing up that comparison again, I just feel comparisons in general make most sense with breakouts and debuts)
Tags: gene siskel film center, ciff 2025, me time!

Name: Ivan's Childhood
Review: A really good 7/10 Soviet war movie! Almost as good of a 7/10 Soviet war movie as Letter Never Sent. In all seriousness, this was good. I think Tarkovsky was still figuring out how to pace his movies in sections, as a number of beats didn’t quite hit like I was hoping, but there are an equal number that do hit effectively, and every performance feels like a fully realized character in spite of that. Not to mention it looks stunning from front to back.
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, w/ peter

Name: Adult Children
Review: “Connective tissue.” Really nice stuff. I love any indie movie with a real-feeling, well-developed, and endearing family chemistry, and this achieved that. While there have been a lot of indie coming of age dramas in this style that I’ve seen at this point, this at least matched the standard and was very fun and heartwarming in the moment. Very proud of all involved, and I am in no way biased because I know one of the producers like why would you ever think that? Also, having watched Okie, Killing Eleanor, Saint Frances, and now this over the last 12 months, I feel compelled to make my own Illinois-set adult coming-of-age film of my own. This is a niche I think I could comfortably fit into, and the fact that there’s an actual niche for Illinois indie films now makes me so happy and hopeful for the future. Strong 7.
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2025, w/ carmen, q&a!, sensationalized realism, illinois production!

Name: Train Dreams
Review:
This rocks. Despite my ADHD getting the best of me at a couple points, I was really taken by this and love a lot about it. Joel Edgerton’s transformation is amazing, what the story says about aging, struggling to find meaning, what causes life events to happen the way they do, and just how tied we are to nature struck a number of chords with me, and the cinematography, sound design, and editing are just divine… I’m always a sucker for films with an emphasis on having the most immersive sound design possible, especially when it comes to movies as in touch with nature and the world like this, so the sensory experience here is to DIE for.

It’s just a little held back by some mixed feelings I have on the narration here, which sometimes really adds to the story but sometimes feels a little too hand-holding, as well as me personally hoping for a bigger gut punch of an ending rather than the “oh that’s nice” that I felt was here (would probably be a 9 if the ending hit me harder), but overall this is much more moving than not and beautifully conceived overall.

Strong 8, but honestly don’t think too hard on it. I loved it and even if this wasn’t like IMMEDIATELY life-changing on first watch, comparison and fixating on small flaws feel like the thieves of joy in this instance and I had a really great time with this.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, ciff 2025, q&a!, w/ blaine, w/ jack l, w/ cam

Name: Palestine 36
Review: Surprisingly accessible and cinematic in its portrayal and dramatization of all its events, occasionally a bit too much so at the expense of a greater impact, but by and large this makes for a thoroughly moving and well crafted experience. Similar to Battle of Algiers in how there isn't one specific main character, it's really about the community. Also does a very good job at clearly developing and explaining the British colonial agenda, which definitely helped add extra weight to the pivotal scenes near the end. This is Palestine's official Oscar submission for 2025, coming to theaters in the States in winter; would definitely recommend seeking it out!
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2025, q&a!, me time!

Name: The Love That Remains
Review: Really beautiful and touching with some very… interesting choices in the second half that confused me at first, but slowly unlocked and revealed their meaning as the film kept going. The sentiment of it all is very surprising by the end, especially given how everything in the film is presented up to that point, but I can very safely say that I loved this. Need to sit on it a bit before I give it a firm rating or ranking, just because of how it sweeps the rug out at times, but this movie is really great in spite of that. One of the absolute best and most endearing ensembles you’ll see all year is in this, working so incredibly well with the screenplay to make for one of the best movies of the year about family, and it’s almost, if not just as stunningly shot as Godland. Palmason is so good, and if he stays on track, he could very well be one of my all-time favorite filmmakers one day.

Name: Spilt Milk
Review:
So I went into this expecting it would be a fun movie. It has an intriguing premise that convinced me to take a chance on it, about a boy in the 80s who takes after his favorite TV detective to find his brother. Sounds like simple fun on paper, right? Well…

I’m blown away and so unbelievably moved by this. This is the sleeper hit of CIFF 2025 for me. An adult fairy tale about a boy who plays detective not just because he loves to, but because he wants to learn what his parents aren’t telling him and to just be talked to like an equal. This so deeply cuts into the emotions of watching a family member descend into something you’ve never seen before and knowing that you might lose them, but it’s such an insanely empathetic, beautifully human portrayal of a family that, despite all the shit and painful back and forth, truly loves and wants to help each other through it all.

The use of a child’s perspective here is absolutely second to none, the performances are excellent, the mystery of it all is truly unpredictable and emotional in all the most unexpected ways, and it hit such a comforting, heartwarming note at the end, as all the best adult fairytales do. There’s a side characters and a couple moments that I felt were a bit underdeveloped, even after finishing the film, but those are my only critiques for what is otherwise one of the most emotional movies of 2025 for me. I absolutely loved it and, while I have a very specifically personal connection to the movie from my past, everyone should give this a chance.
Tags: gene siskel film center, ciff 2025, q&a!, cried or teared up, me time!

Name: Wake Up Dead Man
Review: Very good entertainment while still packing in some genuine surprises, as Knives Out always does well! As I’ve grown, I’ve begun to think less of the emotional weight of the franchise compared to how taken I’ve been by it in years past, namely the side characters feeling too caricatured for how prominent they are in the story. But while this does have moments of leaning too much in the caricatures, it also has some of the best individual character moments of the whole franchise as well, really recouping itself by the end, and that was probably my favorite part. It ends up being legitimately moving to a certain extent, AND it surprises you with the plot and presentation all the same! In particular, there's a notable religion element that the story revolves around, and it makes for some genuinely unique creative story decisions, especially for a whodunit. Not even to mention Rian Johnson has such a talent for casting actors against type in a way that completely fits them; my favorite here was Andrew Scott as David Mamet. So yeah, even if I don’t love this franchise as much as I used to, Rian still knows how to use the wrought nature of the whodunit and expectations of it to make something incredibly entertaining and engrossing, and reminding me why I enjoyed these as much as I did in years past. I had the fun I was hoping, and I think a lot of people will too :)
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2025, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ blaine

Name: Cover-Up
Review: An amazing documentary that I hate exists, just given everything covered in this. It paints such a vivid, polished, and complete-feeling picture for a 2-hour documentary, and it doesn’t waste a single minute being an incendiary, angering watch about the darkest side of recent US history, still managing to paint a genuinely interesting portrait of its main human subject. Admittedly, I was hoping for a more personal portrayal of Hersh to balance out how much history there is here, and that might keep it from lingering any more strongly with me, but I also do realize that this can be chocked down to Hersh’s own limits with sharing. Nonetheless, an excellent documentary and likely the best one I’ve seen all year.
Tags: gene siskel film center, ciff 2025, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ cam

Name: Jay Kelly
Review: More wrought and structured than my favorite Baumbach movies, there’s some stuff in here that goes nowhere or doesn’t hit as I was hoping, but that doesn't keep it from still being thoroughly entertaining with a number of great individual moments, enough heart with most of the central relationships to drive it through to the end, and a cast as committed and endearing as this that really does goes a long way. Maybe not up to expectations and won’t sit quite as well with me over time, but it made me happy in the moment. I had fun.
Tags: gene siskel film center, ciff 2025, w/ alek, w/ cam

Name: Father Mother Sister Brother
Review: Absolutely wonderful. With each chapter, you have a meaningful moment that will likely be taken for granted for the foreseeable future, the joy of simply being with your family despite seemingly minor circumstances, and embracing those you love while you’re with them, each leaving me with a new kind of smile as they transitioned into the next. The performances are stellar in their realism and character, the directing and editing make for perfectly paced conversations all throughout, the motifs are all cheekily and effectively placed in each, and it’s just a heartwarming lowkey film without ever being in your face about it, just appreciating the beauty of what family means. I love it a lot already, and I could totally see this growing on me with age.
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2025, w/ mom, w/ dad, w/ alek

Name: Frankenstein
Review: As I texted my parents soon after I walked out of the theater… Frankenstein is AWESOME!! This is Guillermo del Toro’s lifelong passion project, and it absolutely shows. This is just completely firing on all cylinders, from the divine production and costume design and makeup effects, to the genuine passion and commitment and pure joy of watching every actor on screen (Jacob Elordi gives one of the best performances of the year), to just how RICH this screenplay is… after seeing some mixed reactions, I had slightly tempered expectations for how invested I would get in the story, but I am here and delighted to say that this effectively tackles so much more than I was led to believe, from the basic ethics of playing God to the conditions that cause someone to become violent and how it becomes cyclical to what calls for grace and forgiveness, and it’s all right up to par with what I’ve come to expect from GDT. Put it all together, and you’ve got a massive 2+ hour adventure that genuinely feels and overwhelms you like a novel come to life; the weight of the most intense moments is truly, powerfully felt. It’s crushing in its best moments, and I can’t think of a better thing to praise this film for. One little critique - the final emotional moment is just a tad underwhelming, as while it makes sense in the context and progression of the story, is just a little slight for how layered and captivating the movie is up to this point. It’s at least moving in its own, simplistic way, I was just hoping for a bit more of a head-turner. Aside from that and a couple brief moments of clear CGI that I noticed, these are minor critiques for how immense and accomplished this is overall. A magical, transportive experience. If you aren’t able to catch it in theaters, find a setting at home that most closely resembles that experience; shut the blinds, turn off the lights, turn up the speakers, and lose yourself in this.

Name: No Other Choice
Review:
Damn I really ended CIFF on a bleak note with this one... but it was very good. The base synopsis of this movie is obviously very topical, being about a man who kills his potential competition after being laid off from his job, but what it amounts to (that I obviously won't spoil here) is even more topical and even more nihilistic. That being said, I wouldn't emphasize it that much if it wasn't effective in delivering what it intended. It's a captivating ride through and through, it's visually insane as expected from Park, the ensemble is quite great, and it is a genuinely unpredictable story with several fascinating scenes, especially the small mannerisms and conversations that have just the right action or dialogue to unlock so much more than you anticipate; there's a monologue in here where the main guy tells a story about his own dad to his son, and in my opinion this brief exchange is one of the keys to the whole damn thing.

Only negative is that the film feels tonally compromised in a handful of scenes, particularly when the plot first starts to kick into motion and it tries heavily and jarringly enough to be a comedy to an extent that I actually saw the characters as too cartoony and became detached for a bit. But it does recoups itself and becomes a fascinating and head-spinning Park Chan-wook puzzle which is a real treat as always; pretty great, depressing stuff all around.
Tags: amc newcity 14, ciff 2025, w/ alek, w/ cam

Name: Scream
Review:
Was really missing having a proper spooky season earlier this week, but I finally had a free day today and there’s no other horror movie that I’d rather watch to get me back in the swing of things. Haven't seen this since high school, and... it's pretty damn close to just as good, if not equally so!

Some of the references still get a little annoying, but this also still works scarily well as a fusion of a seriously likeable and hilarious high school comedy (Matthew Lillard especially was so much funnier after forgetting most of his lines) and a slasher film with a serious, dark underbelly. Even on this fifth watch, watching this evokes a genuine emotion of fear after seeing people you know and love kill or be killed at a moment's glance, it's a tight hour 50 from start to finish, the bloodbath at the end is somehow both gangbusters and terrifying, and the overall sense of survival and triumph still hits. I can think of few other scream queens that have even come close to Sidney in this, she's such a good character (as is everyone else).

A couple things I noticed in particular:
- the opening scene is perfect. The references somehow all feel natural, the sense of "wtf is going on" is so well executed and stays horrifying until the movie actually begins, Drew Barrymore's performance is way better than it needs to be (the weak "mom" unironically gets me every time), and the progression of funny and cute to horrifying and gory could not have been a better thesis.
- Wes Craven knew exactly how to pace this movie effectively, and I noticed that especially today through the score. Even in moments that are super on-the-nose, the score genuinely hits the right emotional note of every scene and makes you forget any doubt you had that you're in the hands of a confident filmmaker.
Tags: paramount+, paulina apartment, w/ peter, spooky season 2025

Name: It Was Just an Accident
Review: Very well made and acted and I hope it goes without saying that I respect the hell out of Jafar Panahi’s bravery as an artist, I just… didn’t quite feel that visceral emotion or as much urgency out of the story as I was hoping, given all that. The performances are excellent and I was compelled by the characters as they went along their journey and revealed their pasts, but I didn’t think the points being made about revenge and justice were saying anything particularly new on the subject except for when we dug into the lives in the characters, and I thought there wasn’t enough of that personality against the back-and-forth debate. The sociopolitical context certainly elevates it, I just think the story just as a story for a film hits a weird mix of being hyperrealist and plotless while still having an overarching plot that is surprisingly predictable and undercuts the power of the realism. Personally I thought No Bears had a more investing personal touch and more surprise to all its social commentary. I’d be lying if I said I wasn’t a little underwhelmed, but this is definitely a good movie all around that leaves more impact than not, and the best moments are quite great. And maybe this’ll grow on me, who knows.

Name: Halloween H20: 20 Years Later
Review: This movie is okay and all, but it’s left me asking why did Laurie never just move out of the country?? There’s no way Michael would ever be able to get a passport or sneak onto any sort of boat or plane without someone noticing, and Laurie is clearly wealthy in this; I’m sure that could’ve been arranged and solved all her problems. I mean c’mon you idiot, think of your son!
Tags: paulina apartment, w/ alek, w/ peter, spooky season 2025

Name: Aguirre, the Wrath of God
Review:
Always pretty amazing to see a movie that naturally finds a way to balance this many themes. Colonialism, environmentalism, nationalism, and authoritarianism all make their way into this, and it’s more generally just a very fascinating look at human behaviors and values within this time period and world. Really liked how it never felt too structured or too clear on what it was saying either (the ending in particular impressed me, I might even almost call it a positive ending from a certain perspective?), really engaged me as a result. The approach feels very novel as well, combining documentary/verite techniques with what very much still is an historical epic.

Felt a little too anthropological at times to really forge a stronger emotional connection for me (just personal preference), and every once in a while there was an individual creative decision that briefly took me out (although the dedication to the craft and painterly visual style far outweigh the negatives and will stick with me far more), but this is pretty great stuff overall.

Name: Bugonia
Review:
I was actually excited for this, but Yorgos spends 2 hours making a movie with the most common and obvious themes in film this decade (capitalism is bad and will kill us all) but overwhelmingly misanthropic, and impressively does so in a way that’s neither thought-provoking or funny.

Yorgos does as good a job as any of his previous movies here developing caricatures that reveal genuinely interesting ideas and emotionality, some of which were particularly impressively achieved given how unlikeable everyone here is, but after 30-45 minutes, not only does it make its main message obvious before act 2 even kicks in, I truly felt that any depth or emotional resonance you feel towards them just becomes the butt of the joke (I really can’t do suicide played for superficial shock value after you actually come to care about a character, and there’s a lot more upsetting shit that you have to endure only to reach an obvious ass message) or a frustratingly simple plot device towards a movie and its overall message that really aren’t saying anything worthwhile about conspiracy theories, class differences, or the mental health through line between all of it. And as a result of how frustratingly little it commits and follows through on its genuine emotions, this really isn’t that funny either and mostly just came across as empty or mean.

Points for the film looking visually excellent and having a cast as committed as any other Yorgos film, as well as the first 30 minutes which I actually thought were pretty great. Also, if nothing else, I take it with pride that I supported the movie being shown on 35mm.
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, w/ alex b

Name: The Mastermind
Review:
Didn’t always tonally or emotionally cohere into something impactful for me, but it was certainly fun to see Kelly Reichardt’s highly entertaining minimalist take on a heist film with heavy shades of early New Hollywood. And as per usual with her, it does present some very effectively thought-provoking moments on who this guy is and why he acts like this, as well as a very well-developed world and environment for him.

It’s certainly helped by the great performances, strikingly lush cinematography and editing, and awesome music too, I just wish it ended up being something more than an “oh… well that happened,” both thematically and with James’ arc. It’s especially underwhelming considering how investing the story gets at its best moments (particularly with how exhausting it is to sympathize for James at times as well), but credit where credit is due, what’s here is still an interesting enough story and well crafted.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: Seeds
Review:
Wow, yeah. Definitely one of the best 2025 documentaries and among the better 2025 films period I’ve seen thus far. Any documentary that is successfully able to use the form in a way that allows you to really get to know its human subjects and be as immersed in its atmosphere as possible is one for me, and this did a really great job at that without being needlessly demanding or over-explanatory; everything you come to learn while watching this is given exclusively through Shyne’s wordless visuals and the natural conversations she has with her subjects. Particularly as someone with ADHD, I thought the flow of the entire movie was brilliantly conceived; even at the times when I wasn’t able to catch every word or detail of a scene, there was something being said or shown at some point that caught me up to speed and worked wonderfully as a subtle thesis.

At 2 hours, I was missing more of an overall gut punch (which I'll even admit is more personal preference since I thought the ending was still fitting for this style of doc), but really, aside from that this covers a perfect amount on family, both across generations and in the now, the US government’s failure to support all its farmers equally, and just what it means to be a farmer in the 2020s to feel complete for its runtime. I was thoroughly moved, learned a lot, and deeply impressed with the directing and editing on display here; this one is excellent.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!, black harvest film festival

Name: Die My Love
Review: Messy in ways I both liked (how the second half reframes the first) and didn’t like (the first half), but it ultimately does present some pretty fascinating ideas about mental health and the extremes on either side of independence and codependency in a relationship. I wish it offered something a little more to chew on, by the end it felt a little too driven by its style and emotions at the expense of a harder-hitting overall idea to ground it more effectively, but the style and emotions of it all still do have me liking it more than not… Woman Under the Influence x Antichrist x mother! probably wasn’t my best choice for a movie to watch after a long week, but it’s a good one nonetheless. Very interested to see how this will sit with me, it’s conflicting and challenging enough on first impression that I already foresee some shifting views after time to sit on it.
Tags: amc newcity 14, w/ gracie, w/ aden, w/ alex b

Name: His Girl Friday
Review:
I’m currently the busiest I’ve been in months, and it was nice to watch an extremely accurate depiction of what having ADHD during a busy week is like.

In all seriousness though, as much fun as screwball comedies can be, I’ve always found the artifice of them to be a little alienating when it comes to them sticking with me in the long run or having a deeper emotional connection. While there is a bit of that artifice here as well, I’m really impressed with how this managed to use the constant wise-cracking and wrought plotting in a way that really works and makes a lot of sense for a story about the insanity of trying to have a work-life balance, the performative nature of journalism and politics, and, in a weird way, generally just giving yourself up to the moment when you’re doing what you love; it's really entertaining and fun with an extra layer all the way through as a result. This movie is too busy dealing with all its own insanity to be overtly sentimental or have more conventional emotional beats, but it still finds ways to be emotional amidst the chaos.

All that’s to say, this was really engrossing and surprisingly thought-provoking; it moves quickly enough that I definitely didn’t catch everything, but, in a hypnotic and endearing sort of way as a result of watching this late at night and losing myself in the chaos of it all. I really enjoyed it a lot.

Name: Men in Black
Review: Love me some good ol' Hollywood fun, this is still a blast. So sharply written and directed for a goofy sci-fi comedy, all the little details and callbacks like the Elvis thing and the sheer amount of push-ins are so iconic and actually work somehow. All 21st century movie comic relief fears just how effortlessly funny Will Smith in this, the casting across the board here is just so on point. Can't believe Spielberg and Lucas are actually aliens. Peak anti-cockroach cinema.

Name: Monsieur Hulot's Holiday
Review: Wasn’t in the biggest mood for a bits and gags movie but this is a really good bits and gags movie nonetheless. Probably a 7 based on my mood now but also probably an 8 if I was in the right mood and will likely be an 8 later on… idk man who gives a shit lol. Fun movie though, made me laugh a bunch. Mr. Hulot is a really good autist

Name: Nouvelle Vague
Review: The whole movie revolves around the gag of “haha wasn’t this Godard guy crazy making this movie??” which certainly made for some very good individual moments, but it’s so focused on that one gag to tell the story that it starts to feel tepid after a certain point, in one ear out the other. There’s a lot more emotions and experiences of filmmaking to be had and I find it hard to believe there was nothing of more substance to include here. Nonetheless, still an okay time, looks visually dazzling and the cast is committed at least, just kinda empty. I think there’s a single cutaway shot of Godard driving down the exact road from Weekend which is cool, and the guy they cast as Truffaut is truly the spitting image of him. Also lmfao at them ending with a title card saying Breathless went on to be an international success like… yeah no shit

Name: Optimism
Review: One of the best short films I’ve seen in recent memory. Love when experimental filmmaking gets an actual reaction out of me, actually gave me some ideas of how to more creatively approach a film idea I’ve had in the back of my head. Definitely wanna see more from this director, who is apparently another Chicago local!
Tags: in class, w/ davis, w/ tabitha

Name: The Running Man
Review: Cornball 80s fun, which I was able to get behind tonight. Good production design and some fun action sequences, and a committed cast helps too. I know we used to make Movies with a capital M and all that, but I’m glad we left unearned final kisses and actors saying their own lines from different movies in the past; Arnold saying “I’ll be back” fully took me out. Still, there’s soul to this that kept it going until the end for me, despite all its flaws. Going with a low 7 because I’m feeling nice, could be a high 6 though.

Name: The Running Man
Review:
Kinda funny (more disappointing in retrospect) just how barely passable this is. For every charming moment and solid action scene, there’s another moment of studio-interfered blandness. After a fairly tedious first half, Edgar has a couple scenes to really shine and almost convince me the movie would redeem itself, but the second half and final act get so self-serious to a point of feeling hollow, and only getting me to think about how ironic it is to be watching this movie from Paramount of all companies (when you put something like “from Edgar Wright” in all your marketing campaigns and people expect an Edgar Wright movie, the studio involvement becomes all the more obvious).

The Arnold version has its flaws and is a pretty simple telling of this story, but at least it embraced what it was without any extra bullshit and effectively got its messages across in the end. There's just barely enough to appreciate here that it might be 3 stars, but I left feeling bummed and mixed and not in the way the movie wanted me to (the empty box-ticking political messages mostly) that 2.5 definitely feels more apt.

Edgar Wright, if you happen to be reading this, please go ultra-low budget on your next film. Cleanse yourself of the studio system and re-discover your filmmaking voice with exponentially more creative control.

Name: Midnight Run
Review: Oh this was awesome. This screenplay is so meticulous, intelligent, and entertaining from start to finish with a number of emotional themes that related to my life at the present moment way more than I anticipated, expertly balancing the thrill of the chase with genuinely coming to understand and care about both the main characters, all the while remaining so sincerely directed and presented, and on top of that I have a new contender for my favorite De Niro performance. There’s a questionable plot convenience every now and again, and I thought the final scene leaned just a little too hard into being schmaltzy, but even with that I was simultaneously moved and entertained in equal measure. And the fact that I was surprised by so many different and unique ways that the movie achieved both of these was a real treat and is convincing me to give it the edge with my rating. This ruled. On a broader note, I think this is a pretty fascinating link in between two niches of movies. I was reminded a lot of cross-country chase movies from the 60s like Mad Mad World and Good Bad Ugly (also could throw North by Northwest in here even though that was 1959) with echoes of the more serious Coppola and Di Palma mob movies of the time, and it simultaneously really felt foundational to crime movies to come soon after that had more contemporary approaches to various elements of the genre like LA Confidential and Collateral.

Name: The Railfan
Review: Watch the teaser trailer HERE for my upcoming fifth short film :)

Name: Wicked: For Good
Review:
Overall a rockier movie than the first just because it’s so twist heavy, although the emotional beats and story progression of the first two acts were still enjoyable and had me on board close to as much as the first; good stuff!

But as much as I hate to say it… the final act just didn’t do it for me. There were brief windows of a working finale to the saga, where you understand certain twists and where they’re coming from, but the story just reframes itself too many times and structures so many of these as THE ENDING only to continue, and frankly it’s numbing. I don’t remember if the original musical does this too, but the way the movie goes about all this isn't effective and brought my overarching opinion of it down from good to just okay. And how did they end up on Arrakis??

That being said, I don’t think it’s that bad as a whole. When the story hits, this is a world I genuinely find myself getting lost in, and after a certain point this stayed true for me through the end, even despite my qualms. The production and costume designs are still excellent, the cast is as committed and watchable as before (story aside), and I really think people still are hating too much on the cinematography. Until the very last scene, which did admittedly leave a sour taste in my mouth, this is largely a very good looking movie. I just wish it was all used towards a better screenplay and stronger direction for the last 30 or so minutes.

Name: It Was Just an Accident
Review:
First time I was so focused on the plot that it took me a while to really settle into how much more character-driven this is. But going in expecting that on the second watch… this honestly hit way harder! Like, I was shocked! I was locked into and thinking about all the characters and their own journeys from minute 1, and I noticed so many tiny details that I never would have on just one watch; this really does benefit from a second watch for all its intricacies. The conversations and intersectionality of the personal drama with the discussions of revenge are that much more layered than I initially led on, the plot twists and moments of silence remain stunning and incredibly tense (ending hit even harder), and I was just that much more enamored by the human element of it all.

I’m saying the same thing over and over again at this point, this really is a great movie even despite knowing what happens going in and I had an excellent time with it. Not even to mention the Jafar Panahi Q&A afterwards, which was as fascinating as it was entertaining.
Tags: music box theater, q&a!, w/ jihan

Name: Jay Kelly
Review:
Despite there still being a vanity to this that it can’t quite escape in certain scenes, Baumbach overall is still just so damn good at making me feel things in his best moments.

All the hype going into the first watch being absent gave this revisit much lower stakes, and I was able to just kick back and let myself enjoy it with no pretext. As a result, I was more able to appreciate how Baumbach really hasn’t lost his edge for wonderfully constructed stories that focus on humans as purely emotional beings; really loved and was more struck by the overarching theme of chasing your individual dreams vs. being there for your family in particular, probably given that I’ve been feeling a little more existential about my future as winter’s begun to draw in. The first flashback scene to when he was 23, in the context of looking back on a brighter time, nearly made me cry and might be one of my favorite scenes of the year. It’s just so charming.

And maybe catching this on 35 helped, but I now fully think this has some of the better cinematography in a year chock full of great looking movies.

Name: The Sugarland Express
Review: This is an interesting one. The first two acts are quite wonderful, as Spielberg effortlessly and entertainingly sets the scene and builds the main trio of characters; it's a surprisingly delightful and heartfelt road movie, given what the story is about, and it's genuinely moving. But while I think I knew from the beginning that the movie perhaps wouldn't end the happiest, just based on what New Hollywood is known for, the movie makes a surprising switch at around the 2/3 mark from being really intimate with the three leads, while still giving context and keeping the story moving, to focusing more heavily on the rest of the law enforcement in a much less personal manner. It still hits on some very real and surprising emotions as well as having some very striking images, as Spielberg has of course become known for, but the jarring transition between perspectives and decreased intimacy took away some of the tickle for me and almost sort of made this feel like two different movies. Nonetheless, this is really strong work all things considered. It's wonderfully directed from a technical standpoint and has some really striking visuals to back up the performances and where they take the story, and even despite the qualms I was moved and engaged through to the end. I just think the way it meshes different approaches doesn't always work, perhaps signifying Spielberg still figuring out how to effectively tell a feature length character-driven story, while still having a truly strong foundation. Or, given what he's known for now, I wonder if he just didn't work as well with the more depressing traits of New Hollywood and found he was much better at telling stories with some kind of more positive outcome or final note. And the vast majority of audiences clearly felt the same way. Also -- has me thinking about not only why true crime was so popular in New Hollywood, but how there is a genuinely strong future in true crime if those filmmakers are able to hit similar, more humane/less exploitative notes as Bonnie and Clyde, Targets (debatably), Badlands, this, and Dog Day Afternoon. Strong 7, riiiiight on the border though.

Name: Rental Family
Review: Surprisingly slight for a feel-good drama but I liked it. While a number of moments definitely feel structured in a typical way for the subgenre and the music makes itself very obvious at times, the movie ultimately ends up subverting expectations a little more than not by being a quieter affirmation of all the values and ideas it puts up throughout the story, and I actually appreciated that about it! I maybe was expecting and hoping for some kind of emotional wallop that never came, and certain details of the screenplay and story definitely could’ve gone through another draft, but I cared about the characters and was engaged, and the bulk of this is confidently realized, emotionally mature, and rarely taking itself in a worn-out or overbearing direction, even if it sometimes takes an easy way out. Good stuff.

Name: The Social Network
Review:
A little tired to write more right now, but very predictably this movie hit much differently as a college student with big dreams and 5 more years of life lived, and the bottom line is I'm kind of so in love with how Sorkin reframes Zuckerberg's rise as a story on how "processing" your emotions by distracting yourself will catch up to you eventually, no matter how much you accomplish.

Every scene in this is so quippy but in a way that always makes sense and either plants or pays off some really satisfying or intelligent detail. Even if it's a little overwrought at times, this is ridiculous writing and directing, as locked in and on top of things as Zuckerberg was while innovating social media; captivating from start to finish, and all the main characters are just really well realized.

And my favorite shot, despite just being a small cutaway, is in the first coding montage when he types 'Facebook' and it's underlined as a misspelled word. Such a simple but perfect little detail that I think has unlocked more of the movie for me than I initially thought.
Tags: criterion channel, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: La Jetée
Review: Watching this at 1:30 AM was the perfect experience of being tired without being too tired to follow along with it all. This is kinda so sick, I loved it and can’t believe the still photography with narrations worked this effectively in a short film format.

Name: Sentimental Value
Review:
This is pretty great. While I'm someone who wavers more towards the big cathartic moments both in real life and in movies, and was perhaps thinking that might eventually happen here, this isn't one that prides itself in that. The moment it sank in for me that this is a movie about wandering and avoidance rather than a more clear-cut journey, I was genuinely struck by a different personal connection to Nora's character and started to appreciate it more from then on out. It ultimately ends up being a very realistic and true-to-life portrayal of depression that took a bit of time to get into, but really moved me in an unexpected way by the end and has been sitting really well with me since.

Strong 8
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: The Insider
Review: On a snowy Illinois day, nothing else to do but get comfortable with your parents and settle into a longer movie. Sublime and topical and maybe too dense to emotionally satisfy every major thread, but also every single scene hit to some degree and overwhelmed me with the weight of its stakes. Kinda blown away by how well this worked and how much it touched on and actually resonated with me. Maybe my favorite Mann so far but I do also love Collateral.
Tags: hulu, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Marty Supreme
Review: One Paddle After Another (no spoilers below, just vague thematic stuff) Okay wow… they got lightning in a bottle with this one. A sublime and electrifying blend of screwball comedy, Safdie thriller, and constant ethical quandaries, constantly testing the waters but simply just impossible to look away from and an absolute blast from start to finish. Dives straight into the belly of the beast with how far people are willing to go with their own ambitions and flips the switch to gonzo mode, but somehow remains fascinating and never feels overly caricatured in a way that kills the immersion. I’m not 100% sure all the threads or character treatments come fully together on first watch, but it’s also already begun to reveal its many intricacies and is just pretty fucking awesome. I have a lot to say and think about with this one. More to come, potentially a full review to be posted somewhere else.
Tags: amc river east 21, dolby, w/ alek, early screener, q&a!

Name: High School
Review: Pretty fascinating look at how uniformity, complicity, and respect for authority are the basic building blocks of character in high school. Really great editing and camerawork that highlights so much of what's come to be associated with a stereotypical high school experience, full of genuinely interesting conversations, a number of surprisingly intimate character moments, and broader commentary on how the American high school system sets up its students for their adult American lives to come. Verite filmmaking remains my favorite style of documentaries, great stuff.

Name: The Voice of Hind Rajab
Review:
…

I honestly can’t think of how to write a normal review in a way that doesn’t undercut how I’m feeling in the immediate aftermath.

If my saying this means anything at all, Free Palestine.
Tags: gene siskel film center, early screener, cried or teared up, me time!

Name: Neighboring Sounds
Review: It's always interesting to see movies that patiently take on a whole community, this sort of reminded me of Edward Yang in that regard (though not to that level). This certainly has some really good character moments, but I think it's a little too specifically personal to this particular community in a way that alienated me from a stronger emotional impression, which I always hope for out of these slower movies about communities. Well made but felt longer than it actually was, the final act in particular felt a little dragged out. Maybe a 3.5 but right now I'm just feeling a little too underwhelmed to give it the edge.

Name: Kill Bill: The Whole Bloody Affair
Review:
The last few years, I really haven’t been able to explain why this movie left such an impression on me. And granted, I really couldn’t give any sort of deeper explanation why I loved this as much as I did even back when it was still fresh in my mind, and this lack of concise explanation led me to assume that I had moved past the part of my life where this was a favorite.

Well, at long last I’ve fulfilled my wish of several years to finally watch The Whole Bloody Affair, and… good fucking God it’s just as good as it was then.

And I finally realized while watching this, the reason why I could never explain myself better is because… this has never been a movie where that was necessary. I watch it, and I’m swept up from start to finish, not thinking about anything else, literally just feeling out the emotions of this massive story. The discomfort, excitement, triumph, shock, sadness, overwhelming thought-provoking… it’s all just so visceral and the kind of epic, extremely emotional storytelling I just absolutely crave from start to finish.

This shit reminds me why I love movies as much as I do, easily the most affirming watch of this in a long time for me, as well as just the power of storytelling and it completely makes me forget everything else in the world. And catching all 240 glorious minutes of it in theaters was a damn treat.

Name: The Sting
Review: If I was a critic in 1973 I’d say something like “The Sting, as a movie, is as much of a beautifully well-oiled machine as the sting in the movie itself. A stunning new picture!” Bonus points for my first watch of this, set in Joliet and Chicago, being a 4K restoration in a Chicago movie theater!! Stunning in all regards really, I honestly can’t pinpoint if I was most taken by the ensemble (Redford the golden boy!! Everyone here is so clever!!), the sound design (the uses of silence!!), the story structure (the twists!!), cinematography (those colors!! That blocking!! Those painted backgrounds!!)… I really, truly fell for this by the end. Maybe not the most deeply emotionally resonant, but it’s just such a full meal of a film, and that in and of itself combined with the strong emotions already present here makes this such a satisfying watch. Fantastic stuff.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!, illinois production!

Name: Spielberg
Review: Damn this thing flew by for being 2.5 hours, fully did not realize the runtime at all until near the end. Don't even really care that it's a basic talking heads documentary, maybe didn't blow me away but this is a really great portrait of Spielberg's life and career. Alek and I were both actually tearing up during the E.T. and Schindler's List sections and I really want to watch Munich now. Should've had more Tintin and maybe some more Indiana Jones but whatever, I liked this a lot.

Name: Unknown Pleasures
Review: Liked it. Felt a little slight, but this story about minor distractions and struggling to deal with a changing world being told in this very loose fashion worked more than not and definitely had some relatability to where my head's at right now. Solid all throughout with some great individual moments and some real great imagery, I just personally wish I felt a stronger connection to the characters beyond what I got through relating to their larger situation. Strong 7.
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, w/ alek, w/ jake p

Name: Late Spring
Review: Really excellent overall. I haven’t fully decided how I feel about the final twist yet, but otherwise this was a truly beautiful work of humanism. Not only the first time I’ve truly felt on the same wavelength as an Ozu movie, but the most strikingly relatable movie that I've seen to where I see myself right now… the ending is just a little jarring after what I came to be invested in up to that point, even if it generally makes sense in the progression of the story. This is seriously so good though; very strong 8, borderline 9.
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, cried or teared up

Name: Blue Moon
Review:
Oh my god this is so achingly wonderful. It’s so fast-talking that I feel like I’m not done processing it all yet, but this is really emotionally true and endearing and just feels like an instant classic for this kind of meticulous, classical, stage-esque chamber piece.

Ethan Hawke does in fact give one of the best performances of the year, as he carries a whole 100-minute movie going against type as an autistic guy who continuously rides the line of being too much but somehow always comes up as charming, charismatic, and just so delightful to watch. Perhaps also a more relatable character than I’m willing to admit, but no matter, easily one of my favorite turns ever from him.
