This movie is a true celebration of how underrated real passion for something is, even if Lorenz was passionate to a damaging degree, but Linklater here is so largely concerned with the wonder of passion in the moment while we’re still alive and kicking, no matter what else is happening. And just that sentiment, which remains prevalent at nearly all times while just letting the characters do their thing, is truly something special.

At times I wondered if the heavily-staged nature of this was perhaps not always working for me, as it can feel a little calculated in retrospect which isn’t always my personal cup of tea even if incredibly impressive to watch, but the movie does end up being very witty and emotional in spite of that.

I don’t want to deliberate over the rating too much since it can obviously ruin the fun, but no matter if this is an 8 or 9 (I think I need to let it sit before confidently giving it the edge), I loved it. Very likely one of my 2025 favorites.

Name: Shivers
Review:
Do NOT go to the 1st floor of Starliner Apartments, WORST MISTAKE OF MY LIFE!!!

First 40 minutes actually were very well done and presented a very interesting world, but the last 45 minutes unfortunately were really only held up by some unnerving imagery. I think the metaphor would’ve worked better if the cast and story were on a smaller scale as well, it all culminates in an ending that I found surprisingly goofy and fully took me out. On one hand it kinda feels like a satire? But the first half was definitely going for something more serious and the story deals with some very serious and upsetting themes, so my initial verdict is that it ends up very tonally flawed.

Credit where credit is due, some of the imagery here is truly striking and I had a number of physical reactions, the ensemble was giving it their all, and the overall atmosphere of the world is developed well, which kept me more engaged than not despite all the flaws. It’s just very much David Cronenberg in the midst of putting all his signature pieces together and only succeeding 50-60% of the time, but the pieces are here at least. There’s a monologue from one of the main characters near the end where she goes into how “everything is erotic” and I cannot think of a better thesis statement for what would eventually come from Cronenberg’s body… of work.

Name: Avatar: Fire and Ash
Review: When someone says “bitch” before a power move in a Jim Cameron film… you feel it. Will update with more thoughts in a little bit. Still just reeling from it all and loving it. I think I have been re-converted to an Avatar fan :) “Don’t shoot anyone you love.”
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ jake p, w/ alek, early screener, dolby, 3d

Name: Caught by the Tides
Review: Very cool film with a lot of big ideas! Formally speaking this is incredibly fascinating, there's some stuff in here that I've never seen in a feature film before, most notably the intertitles for me. This got me to do a lot of thinking, wish it got me feeling a little more strongly than it did but I'm still very impressed nonetheless. Wanna sleep on it and ponder some more before giving a rating.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ alek

Name: Hamnet
Review:
Started off a little slow for me and I wasn’t a huge fan of the ending, but everything in the middle (from the first childbirth scene through Hamnet’s death scene) is really strong and emotionally resonant. The portrayals here of superstitions, the trauma and reasoning associated with those, and just generally the emotional connection you feel for the family is truly great. I did cry real tears at the height of it… I just wish that wasn’t the peak for me.

The final act was super interesting in how it reframed the Hamlet play in the context of this story, and it had some very touching moments… I just wish it didn’t lean so heavily into “the healing power of art” as its final, overarching message. It honestly kinda rubbed me the wrong way when the grief elements were so strong. It felt too nicely wrapped up.

Still, this is good stuff overall. The ensemble is really great, the cinematography is lush and gorgeous, and much of the editing is really solid (although there were a handful of cuts that took me out and I felt could’ve been edited better). My issues with the screenplay left me underwhelmed enough that I’m confident in this rating on first impression, but there is enough to love here that it could go up in the future, who knows.
Tags: amc newcity 14, me time!, cried or teared up

Name: The Secret Agent
Review: Just absolutely incredible. The way the impact of each scene and story thread creeps up on you is SO subtle and surprising, somehow hitting a surprising mark with every detail revealed even when you know there’s so much more to the story. It’s captivating and emotional the whole way through. And it does it to such a degree that I still feel like I haven’t fully processed it yet, but at that same rate it happened SO often that I already know this is an instant favorite. I’m blown away by not only how effortlessly compelling and fascinating this screenplay is with all its themes and handling of structure, I found some kind of highly specific emotional in-point in nearly every scene, some of which cut far deeper than I expected but I absolutely loved that about it. That ending… oh man. It’s such a dense screenplay and, again, so subtle at times that I’m still processing, but this is a truly fantastic film. My new favorite of 2025. “You know more about my dad than I do.”

Name: Diabolique
Review:
Great classic mystery movie. Has Hitchcock, film noir, and German expressionism written all over it and very much wears its influences on its sleeves at times, but I really liked this nonetheless and that doesn't take away from the fact that this is incredibly strong stuff. The mystery of it all ends up being really engrossing and intelligent once it gets going, I really loved the school and teacher-student elements of it as well, particularly that scene where the boys are talking about how powerful men in history had many women at the same time, and it has a stunner of an ending that ties both of these elements together in a surprising, very smart way. Funnier than I expected while still maintaining a strong dramatic core. Rating could go up, but this feels right on first pass.


Random note, unrelated to my opinion -- I don't understand why this is considered a horror movie in the traditional sense. There's the finale that certainly counts as horror, but everything up to that point is pure mystery thriller. It's just that one scene.

Name: Stans
Review:
Pretty standard as a documentary, but interest in the subject matter obviously helped with that. Sometimes feels like it's platforming parasocial relationships a little too much, but the sentiment of art being a genuine part of people's growth as humans takes up more space than not which I appreciated. Getting Eminem's own perspective on his relationship to his fans helped too, even if I wish there was more of that. Slightly generous 7, there's a scene where one of the fans actually starts to break down while telling a story that really resonated with me so it gets the edge for that.

I've been an on and off Eminem fan for 12 years, and I do credit him for being one of the first rappers I ever really got into even if I don't consider him a favorite today, but I'd also be lying if I said I wasn't singing along to some of these songs as I watched. There are a few of his songs that I keep on my regular repeat even today, and I was reminded that Not Afraid did actually help me get through some shit when I was 18 which was cool.

Name: Resurrection
Review:
I have truly never seen anything like this. Which will forever feel so hyperbolic to say because everything draws from something else, but I have truly never seen a film that combines this seemingly endless amount of different stylistic, genre, and story elements and emotions to create a movie about movies that felt so divine and religious, especially as someone whose life is foundational with film.

I became genuinely sad when it hit me that I was watching the final scene, and now that it’s over I genuinely don’t know what else to say. I got so insanely comfortable and tuned into this world that I didn’t realize how deeply I was connecting to it until I realized it was coming to an end and that in and of itself is something that I never want to take for granted.

All that being said, movies about movies by their very nature will forever be held just a little bit back by the fact that their emotional messaging about how life-affirming movies and storytelling are is simply just that after a certain point, and I did feel that take just a little bit of the tickle out of the immersion, hence the mere half star off. But the fact that this transported me as much as it did, and the fact that it turned something as seemingly emotionally predictable as a “love letter to movies” into something I’ve never seen before is such a reminder that there will never be limits to what film (or any art) can achieve.

This is pretty magnificent stuff.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: Avatar: Fire and Ash
Review:
Realized on second watch that a really considerable amount of the story tension relies on the question of “will they make it out alive??” It’s well done enough that I was obviously taken by it on first watch, but on rewatch that and a couple other screenplay issues were definitely more see-through and felt more Hollywood-manufactured, as it goes for most big studio releases I suppose.

Still a lot of fun though, even without the surprise and picking up on some more flaws. Could’ve done without the worst theater crowd in recent memory though… there were two loud dropping of objects, two ringtones, a few phone usages, and the people behind me talking at a regular volume every now and again. Didn’t help that I was tired and under the weather either, but I comfortably lost myself in this and still remained engaged until the end.

Not sure if it’s a 3.5 now or still a 4, gotta sit on it.
Tags: amc naperville 16, imax, 3d, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Home Alone
Review: Have a fever and was tired but still a fun movie of course. Christmas movies like this will always hold such a special place in my heart for how they liberated elementary school me and made me realize I could do anything.
Tags: hulu, at home, watched while sick, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn, illinois production!

Name: Black Christmas
Review:
Incredibly glad I didn't watch this with my family.


Definitely not a merry vibe, in fact this is actually quite depraved, but credit where credit is due this really succeeds at what it sets out to do. Legitimately scary, and for some reason I didn't have that expectation going in; very frequently had me tensing up and at one point even bracing myself as I looked slightly away from the screen. I'm sure watching the literal opposite of a cozy Christmas movie when I wasn't expecting that certainly helped, but this is the most a horror film has scared me in months. For that reason alone, and its many other strengths, I'm gonna give it the rating edge.
Tags: amazon prime, at home, watched while sick

Name: A Christmas Story
Review: With each passing year I become a little more appreciative for just how emblematic this movie is of what made Christmas such a special time for me growing up. It's all here, and it's all a perfect delight. There's also some new filmmaking detail I find to appreciate each year and this year it's the casting, how did they do it??? Finally bumping it up to that coveted 10/10 that it probably deserved sometime before now but never too late!
Tags: hulu, watched while sick, w/ dad, at home, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Home Alone 2: Lost in New York
Review: Good movie overall, fun with some great individual moments. The pigeon lady made for a great subplot. Too tired to really lock into this but I did my best. Sequels that came out before sequels became all the rage have a sense of genuine care to them that I don't think exists anymore with how saturated they've become.
Tags: hulu, at home, watched while sick, w/ dad, w/ mom, illinois production!

Name: National Lampoon's Christmas Vacation
Review:
Line delivery perfection. And I have such a weak spot for just how funny I find Chevy Chase.


Also I don't care how stressful or gross family holidays get, this is what I want when I'm older.
Tags: hulu, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom, illinois production!

Name: Tampopo
Review: They did the thing! Cute movie. Glad my dad enjoyed his Christmas present from me.
Tags: criterion blu-ray, at home, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Sharing Heat
Review: This was a very solid approach to this idea, my only note would've been to lean into either extreme even more. REALLY heighten the senses of the slo-mo sensual stuff, REALLY heighten the senses of the hyperactive, experimental cutscenes, just really make that contrast more prominent for a stronger reaction and a more memorable short film. This was good though, good job Dustin!

Name: Resurrection
Review: As expected, the second watch opened my eyes to so many more details that unlocked each story before they even finished, and then they each went on to unlock greater meaning just as I watched them with a fresh pair of eyes. This time I fully picked up on what the first and fourth ones meant, was a tad more confused but still emotional over the second one, and loved the third one even more and actually even cried a little at the end of it. I absolutely love a good puzzle. I’m still just a little bit put off by how on the nose some of the voiceover is to give it the full marks right away, but it’s such a moving and rich experience across two watches that it feels like a movie I’ll bump up to a 10 in retrospect. And still, it left me with such a sweet lump in my throat in that final scene that I’m tempted. Also - surprisingly fantastic family movie! My sibling picked up on as much as I did while offering some new insights as well and really loved it, which was awesome, and both my parents had a consensus of “I liked what I saw but I have no idea what I just saw.” Made for an excellent post-film discussion. I love this shit. Still probably my movie of the year and I feel so grateful to have seen it in theaters twice now.
Tags: gene siskel film center, w/ quinn, w/ dad, w/ mom, cried or teared up

Name: You Can Count on Me
Review: Really got to me, great lump-in-my-throat cinema with lots of feeling and less thinking. Laura Linney was fantastic in this role as a mom who copes with her own problems by playing mom for everyone in her life, really fascinating look at quiet self-destruction and projecting insecurities. Really good uses of classical music too. A little by-the-numbers for this kind of feelgood drama at times, but it always kept me achingly on the hook and caring about this family when it easily could've fallen too heavily into stereotypical or sensationalized territory, and for that reason I think it's a great movie. "No matter where I am, you'll still be here, rooting for me."
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, good needle drops, lived in locations, non-sensationalized realism

Name: Is This Thing On?
Review: Watching some good friends scream, “LET ME OUT!” I’m actually… really impressed by this! Really likable and engaging and genuinely funny comedy with a great amount of heart, kept me hooked the whole way through with its truly great blend of emotional honesty and studio comedy; it’s kind of a blast honestly! Made me feel like I had a real understanding of all these characters, and also like I’m capable of doing great things in my middle age without shoving anything overly sappy down my throat, aka effective feelgood filmmaking. I’m happy to be alive. Despite an ending that ended up being a little too rushed and clean-cut, I had a great time with this and I hope Bradley does more earnest, personal, lower-stakes stuff like this in the future instead of all that Maestro Oscar-baity fare. This was way more my jam.
Tags: amc river east 21, me time!, trains in this one

Name: Anaconda
Review: The final act was actually kinda decent and I appreciate some of the sentiments this has with filmmaking, but it’s a fucking drag to get there. At one point around the halfway mark I was thinking to myself that this was terrible, and if they didn’t have the fun they did in the final act to make up for just how unfunny and “self-aware” and obnoxious the first hour or so was (like, it actually just feels like it’s for 9-year-olds half the time), this would be a complete wash. Might be a little generous with the rating, but… idk I guess I wouldn’t go so far to say I truly disliked this. Maybe I just feel bad since my family liked it more than I did.
Tags: amc naperville 16, w/ dad, w/ mom, w/ quinn

Name: Zootopia 2
Review: Fun movie. Actually a really funny comedy, I was laughing a lot throughout. Story is very standard for a kids movie, but it’s having fun and touching on some real emotions in solid ways at least. I liked when they went to a desert rave very briefly and wished they stayed longer, would’ve loved to see what Sirat might’ve looked like in this universe but oh well. Also at one point early on they took the Moleholland Tunnel, and in my brain this suggests the existence of a Moleholland Drive directed by David Lynx… your move, Disney.

Name: Before Sunrise
Review: Just an absolutely perfect experience. Today was my first day back at school, and finishing it out with a trip to theaters where I could just kick back and let myself enjoy one of the most easygoing, welcoming, lovable, and relatable movies I’ve ever seen in a big crowd of other passionate movie fans was a complete and utter delight. It felt like a summer movie in just how communal and laid back and low stakes the whole thing was, and it made for an absolutely wonderful night out at the movies. I love moviegoing and I love the moviegoing community and I love Before Sunrise (1995) directed by Richard Linklater.
Tags: doc films, w/ alex b, trains in this one

Name: Claire's Knee
Review: Very great. On first watch I think my impression of the overall story is just a bit too slight to give it an extra half star, but the dialogue, performances, cinematography, and editing are all so on point. Really just love the brutal honesty and introspection of all the characters, makes for such an engrossing community to follow along with. The transitions between every day were sick as well. Strong 8, very impressed by what was finally my first from Eric Rohmer.
Tags: criterion channel, w/ peter, criterion challenge 2026, paulina apartment

Name: Bill & Ted's Excellent Adventure
Review: “No, I’m okay dude, I just have a minor oedipal complex.” Just so much fun. Perfect amount of stupid while actually reminding me of how much I love history lmao. I love that every time period they visit is just the movie stereotype of each time period, and the filmmakers already recognized and made fun of how stupid 80s pop culture was before the 80s were even over. Socrates best character.
Tags: dvd, w/ syd, at syd's place

Name: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
Review: One of those movies that was so emotionally intense to the point that, as I left the theater and went home, it made me more observational and grateful for the simple things and comforts in my life. Also one of those that’s kinda frustrating because it’s definitely a great movie but also did a good job reminding me of all my own anxieties and left me thinking about that on the entire ride back.

Name: The Bread and Alley
Review:
Every director should be required to start their career with a banger opening titles sequence.

Actually really loved this though; as a debut short film, I wouldn't change a thing. The plot is literally just a boy who gets so lost in kicking a can that he gets lost, and he has to figure out his way home, and I was already falling for it. Could probably watched a short feature of this. I'm not sure what Kiarostami's process of working with actors was (definitely wanna learn more about it), but even early on it seems like he had a really good grasp on how to just let his actors be themselves, while still getting something engrossing out of them.
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, good needle drops, good opening credits

Name: Uncle Yanco
Review: Viva Varda!

Name: A History of Violence
Review:
Love how it started out with kind of a western vibe (mid-westerns are always so good and this so accurately built the vibe of rural farm town), like all these guys are outlaws returning to settle a score with the retired outlaw trying to live a new life, then progressively became more of a mob movie.

Howard Shore is so good at what he does, the whole ensemble is excellent, and despite my skepticism ahead of time after reading the synopsis, I should never have doubted Cronenberg's ability to turn any story in existence into swirling, bloody pool of body horror and eroticism. Also loved how he struck such a balance of making Viggo this badass hitman while never letting you forget that there are highly depressing undertones to it all; shockingly unsensational and never fully embraces the cool.

Not sure what this was missing, if anything, to keep it from a higher rating, this just feels right. Maybe I'm still processing. But it's definitely a great movie.

Name: 28 Years Later: The Bone Temple
Review: “I want my mama…” Kinda fucking amazing!! Was not at all prepared for just how rich of a text this is on religion and one’s own belief systems + values (both exclusively and in tandem), as well as the many ways people process their own long-term trauma, but it does all this and more to emotionally move you while being so goddamn terrifying and viscerally gory. It’s really just damn good storytelling. Nia DaCosta’s direction feels so much more confident in this visual approach and style than the sometimes triumphant, sometimes muddled experimentation in Danny Boyle’s predecessor, and the result is not only the best-looking iPhone movie to date (edit: doesn't change my opinion but apparently not shot on iPhone), but a fucking visual feast in general. Loved the music and sound design too. I didn’t realize how on edge and tense I was until the end credits hit and I finally started catching my breath. There’s certain weird-isms to the story that I’m a bit mixed on and just so much more to unpack than I expected that it’s just shy of being a 9, but I’m genuinely so blown away. Maybe in the future it’ll bump, hell I’m right on the border already.

Name: HIM
Review:
Still had enough lingering interest to put this on tonight, but unfortunately it’s not good. I actually liked the football training stuff at the start, but it abandons all that shockingly quickly and just descends into straight cult shit with no more of the actual game played (and no more of what I cared about). The metaphors of it all were effective enough to keep me hoping until the end, but it ultimately just got obnoxious and derivative… at least it was visually interesting for the most part, but that can only go so far when you abandon your most interesting idea before the halfway point.

I want to see what football training is actually like now, because I genuinely really enjoy watching the sport and I already know there are flaws in the process, but I wanna know more about what keeps players in the game year after year and what their actual motivations are, not the surface-level and tired reasons shown here.
Tags: peacock, paulina apartment, w/ jack l

Name: Eyes Without a Face
Review: What’s already here is legitimately fantastic, I honestly just wish it went on longer. I could’ve easily watched another 30 minutes, not just because I was so into this world, but also because I was hoping for some bigger wallop of an ending. I really felt like it was building to a bigger punch so I’m just a little underwhelmed by that, but I loved nearly everything else about this. Fantastic plant-and-payoffs, a gorgeously tender score, genuine emotional charge to all the horror and ideas here, imagery and effects that still really haunted me, and a truly creepy antagonist all worked together to create what could in the future be a real horror favorite for me. Strong 8, could be a 9. Oh also... extremely cool to watch this for the first time as I'm in the midst of getting pre-production started on my next film, a body horror. Without spoiling anything, this tackles some similar emotional ideas in a very different way, and the way this made me feel so strongly in its best moments has me all the more excited to keep working on my own. Also cool is that the very first scene has shades of what I was trying to do in this scene of Unhappy Customer. Love watching older films that would've given me so many ideas for something I made if I had I seen them sooner.
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, criterion challenge 2026, trains in this one

Name: The Testament of Ann Lee
Review:
I liked it. Worth supporting in theaters just on the basis of this being one of the most inventive movies of 2025; I honestly don’t think I’ve seen a movie musical like this before, I just can’t clearly articulate why yet. Even if it had some trouble really finding its footing in the first half, the second half really hits that synthesis of musical and patient drama that I was hoping for, and it definitely ended up being more moving than not for me.

When it’s at its best, it has some of the best editing, camerawork, and acting of 2025, and the choreography and physical performances are incredible. And I love what it says about the community aspect of religion, I just wished it was highlighting this and other individual character moments in a more consistently affecting manner; its best moments hit very specific elements of faith right on the head from my own experiences and I wanted more. Would love to see again at some point to pick up on more of its subtleties (maybe with subtitles next time).
Tags: music box theater, 70mm, w/ izzy, w/ claire s

Name: Ratcatcher
Review:
Those last few shots :(((

It really is a minor miracle when kids make it to adulthood and turn out okay. Really love how Ramsay builds such an endearing world here and saves all the wallop for the end; it’s already more damaging to think about in retrospect, and I just finished the damn thing. Already curious to see how it lingers further out.
Tags: peter's criterion blu-ray, paulina apartment, w/ alek

Name: Slums of Beverly Hills
Review:
Oh this was so my shit and I loved it. Always love a coming of age movie that knows exactly what its world is and embraces the fuck out of that to great effect. So funny and full of personality and cleverly crafted, not even to mention how stacked this ensemble. Really fucked with the soundtrack too. As always, glad to have caught this on 35mm. Strong 8.


Interesting that the girl giving the speech before the movie warned that there were things in this movie that “might feel out of place for a coming of age movie” when everything here made complete sense in context. I don’t think anything is out of bounds for coming of age movies as long as it’s tactfully written into the story, that’s what makes it coming of age! Anything goes!

Name: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
Review: Having finally now seen the extended edition, I do prefer the theatrical cut (it’s more succinct) but this was still great of course. Those final 20 minutes are so good and would make the end of any runtime worth the journey.

Name: Totally F***ed Up
Review: Cool shit. Feels like a time capsule (complimentary). Really fucked with the style and a number of the conversations being had, both in dialogue and delivery some of them really hit. I just wish it expanded on that more because it hit kind of a platitude for me at one point, but this was very good overall.

Name: Cutting Through Rocks
Review: Haven’t caught an Oscar-nominated documentary in a few years, but I’m happy to report that this was great. Not the most inventive or mind-blowing documentary but it was very emotionally investing and did an excellent job at crafting its most impactful moments into scenes that truly sunk in. A simple but powerful movie reminder of the value of doing the most with what you’re given and appreciating the small steps even if there’s still progress to be made. Especially topical and worth supporting with everything going on in Iran right now.
Tags: gene siskel film center, me time!

Name: Videodrome
Review:
Caught an 11:00 PM screening and was falling asleep but then that one fucking scene happened… and then that other fucking scene happened… and then that other fucking scene happened… and then, yeah, I stayed awake.

This is fucking awesome though. Has only sat increasingly well with me since my first watch and this theater trip affirmed that. This story is insane and still somehow feels ahead of its time in 2026, and I forgot just how ridiculous the body horror in this gets as it goes on. Could be a 9.

Name: Star Wars
Review: Watched 4K77. Great movie. Way better than Bugonia.
Tags: paulina apartment, w/ alek, w/ peter

Name: Sound of Falling
Review:
Unbelievable and amazing how in tune this movie, about four generations of young girls in Germany, is with me and my introspective, existential brain, both now and throughout my life up to this point. That’s MY brain, get outta there Mascha!!

My gut feeling is telling me this isn’t *quite* a 5 star movie, can’t quite put the why into words yet, but this remains absolutely major for me and I love it so deeply.
Tags: music box theater, w/ alex b

Name: Black Narcissus
Review: Didn’t actually know much about this going in except for it being about nuns and the iconic lipstick scene… the lipstick scene was so cool. Love this look at being too strictly bound to a religion and the damaging effects of that, both to yourself and anyone who disagrees with you. Feels like a definite Godland inspiration in that way, which is a big plus for me if you know me. Didn’t have the oomph on first impression to warrant a higher rating, but this was very good.
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, criterion challenge 2026, watched while sick

Name: F1
Review: I watched this because it got nominated, sure, but I honestly don’t feel like thinking about the Oscars and comparing it right now because that would just undercut the fun I had. Might be the Bears fan in me talking for part of this, given all that’s happened since last June, but this fully immersed me and made me believe in the power of the underdog and that’s a good ass feeling! For all its cliches and story beats that might normally bug me more, this film really just has its finger on the pulse of a fun summer blockbuster. It’s focused in its story, slick in its filmmaking, funny and thrilling, and aside from a few moments when Joseph Kosinski reminded me he isn’t as strong when it comes to the more serious story beats, does all it needs to satisfy me. “Does all it needs to” might not make a Best Picture of 2025, but it most definitely made for a good time that I really enjoyed in the moment. Strong 7. Joseph Kosinski do you have Cars on your Sight & Sound top 10?
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ alek, best picture showcase

Name: One Battle After Another
Review:
Watching this in IMAX is certainly one of the best ways to wake yourself up in the morning.


Also: I’m so happy I finally got to see this in IMAX, what a fucking great experience. I love the Music Box and I’m glad I saw it on 70mm there, but I honestly think you gotta go as loud and big as possible with large format shit like this. So stoked for that IMAX 70mm to open in Illinois later this year.


Also also: this is a truly great movie. I’m definitely glad to have had some distance from my first watch and all the hype surrounding it, since that definitely had an effect on my initial impression (still didn’t have a ton of distance this time with it being awards season, but it was certainly less pronounced while watching). Also just because PTA films are so easy to take at face value the first time that I think you need some patience to sit with them. With time to sit with my own thoughts, I really haven’t stopped thinking about this movie and have really come to form some strong personal meaning from it. My only real gripe with it is I think the final couple scenes in their home are a little too slight after such a massive movie that takes on so many different things. But it’s still a solid ending, and everything up to that point is legitimately incredible.
Tags: amc roosevelt, imax, w/ hazel, best picture showcase

Name: Mad Max 2
Review: Second time I’ve watched this while tired and overwhelmed and not able to lock in like I normally prefer… that being said, sometimes I just want to watch a film and enjoy the gut reaction of badass action, and this film certainly delivers in its best moments while still having a really solid story. Love how much this commits to its gonzo world and worldbuilding too, just a really consistent and confident movie all around. George Miller always reminds me that movies are, and should be, visually driven more than most other filmmakers.
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, w/ peter

Name: Ten Meter Tower
Review: How do you go from making something as fascinating as this to Fantastic Machine

Name: Send Help
Review:
If she had to take her chances with a man or bear… she chose the boar.


95 minutes of legitimately fucking great followed by the most what-the-fuck, rug swept out from under you final 15 minutes I’ve seen in a long time…

Initially I was really not rocking with those final minutes actually, like I felt my rating dropping in real time, but there was still a sliver of feeling that Raimi might actually be onto something… and now that I’ve arrived home, I think I would still have preferred it wrap itself up around that 95 minute mark…

But I already feel myself coming around to it. I think it’s fully helped by the fact that Raimi is just a damn wizard at balancing tones, and this movie has a perfect amount of goofy while still fully committing to the bit and never feeling any less than genuine with any of its emotions. None of those final 15 minutes are meant to be taken THAT seriously, and I’m already feeling myself respecting it more.

All that’s to say, what a fucking blast. Sam Raimi has announced his return to cinema in his full, firing-on-all-cylinders delirious glory. I laughed hard, I jumped hard, I cringed hard, my jaw dropped multiple times… this is truly the most fun I’ve had at the movies in a while.

I don’t know whether this is a good omen for the greatest year in cinema history or a bad omen for a misleading start, but it’s only the start of February and we’ve already gotten two fucking bangers in wide release. A very, very intriguing glitch in the matrix…
Tags: amc river east 21, dolby, w/ branden, w/ andrew, w/ tabitha, w/ josh m

Name: The Moment
Review:
If Charli had legs, she’d kick you.


Interesting timing for me since I have genuinely been getting into Charli the last 12 months, particularly this calendar year so far as she’s become a new favorite. My overall verdict is I really liked the setup and first half, but as it went on it started leaning too much into The Absurdity of it all at the expense of more interesting story and character development, ending up leaving me feeling like “okay we get it” more than it should’ve. I think I’m getting a little tired of the Safdie-inspired/ripoff approach (I realized while watching that this is a consistent thing now) when it’s done in a way that stops presenting itself in a unique way and just descends into stressor upon stressor upon stressor to further the story instead of better character development. And then the ending, while interesting with its sentiment, just relied too much on dialogue.

Definitely not a bad movie, I thought it was well made on a technical level with solid performances and genuinely interesting ideas throughout, but the screenplay could’ve gone through another draft or two and some of the execution in the second half just didn’t work well for me. Strong 6 on first impression, maybe a 7 in the future just since I think the concept on paper is novel enough and it does hit at fascinating emotions in the process.
Tags: amc river east 21, prime at amc, w/ alek

Name: THX 1138
Review:
35mm at the Gene Siskel, a real treat.

This is just such a cool movie, let alone being one of the most insane and visionary debuts. Even if I think the dry nature of the pacing and performances hits a point of diminishing returns in the final act and the final scene leaves some loose ends, the whole thing is just so impressively made and hits enough of the right notes that this rating still feels warranted. People don’t talk enough about how good Robert Duvall’s physical performance in this is.
Tags: gene siskel film center, 35mm, me time!, trains in this one

Name: Popstar: Never Stop Never Stopping
Review: Akiva Schaffer is so good at this type of thing. “All I’m saying is, don’t be an asshole to your friends.”
Tags: amazon prime, paulina apartment, w/ brenden, w/ blair

Name: Kill Bill: The Whole Bloody Affair
Review: I have too much wrapped into this movie to cover in a heat of the moment review, even on my second Whole Bloody Affair watch in 2 months, but I’m so happy I caught this on 70mm before it left because I would’ve forever been kicking myself if I didn’t go. Probably my eighth or ninth time seeing this and I’m still picking up on new shit on every subsequent viewing, this movie is never anything less than a huge treat for me. Not even to mention just how needed it was in the midst of how stressful life’s been lately to remind me why I’m in the life I’m in, always hits that spot. Gonna listen to The Grand Duel for the millionth time on the bus ride home now.
Tags: music box theater, 70mm, w/ alex b, w/ aden

Name: Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die
Review: Ugh… I know I’m gonna be in the minority on this one and I truly wanted to enjoy this after being on board with the first 10 minutes, but it was SO fucking obvious with its themes from minute 1, and I found the way it continued to just be so tonally inconsistent and one-note and mishandling of every interesting idea. The humor almost never landed for me, the action scenes are all sauceless, and the personality here all just feels like Gore Verbinski dating himself 10 years in the past. Everything I thought was interesting about this movie only lasted 5 minutes then went away for the rest of it and/or was undercut before becoming incredibly boring again… and this movie was 140 minutes long. Dare I say it... The Electric State came early this year?? (at least this has ideas, but at that rate it fails more on its own promises which makes for a more disappointing watch) Sam Rockwell, Haley Lu Richardson, VFX artists innocent.
Tags: amc newcity 14, w/ andrew, w/ harrison, w/ josh m, w/ tabitha

Name: The President's Cake
Review: A truly excellent film. For my money, easily the best child performances of the year anchor a film heartfelt and heartwrenching in equal measure, very evidently inspired by neorealist and social realist classics and occasionally feeling a tad derivative, but the emotional experience is every bit their equal. The buildup is solid, but once the plot really gets going it just gets progressively better and never lets up with one hard hitting moment after another; I absolutely love the way everything here unfolds, not even to mention how good the edit, camerawork, and sound design are. The final 30 minutes are pretty close to flawless and had me welling up at least three different times, was on the border of 8 or 9 for the rating but those last handful of scenes really just did it for me. Don’t let this one fly under your radar.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie
Review:
Love how this movie is basically saying “you could never make a Robert Zemeckis classic anymore unless you’re an indie rat” and it’s also completely its own glorious thing as an insane buddy comedy, an ambitious sci-fi idea, and some kind of love letter to the city of Toronto.

I had such a blast with this!!! Rating might be a bit reactionary, but this was such an awesome movie and I totally get it, I was fully on this movie’s wavelength in the moment. Maybe I can make my OWN Robert Zemeckis movie to show him what he’s lost one day.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ brenden, w/ blair

Name: Serengeti
Review:
Skipped my first class today to go to MSI instead because mental health matters.

Really fascinated by the dome theater here, always have been since I was a kid, and realized while watching that I have no idea how it works and would love to learn more. I always loved and was fascinated by this brand of science museum documentary growing up, and I would honestly not be mad if I ever found myself working on one!

Name: Forrest Gump
Review: Monumental moment… look what I finally watched for the first time at the ripe age of 22 and a half! The timing finally felt right after a professor showed our class a VFX breakdown and after watching Nirvanna. Not gonna lie, I was kinda clowning on it for a bit but I progressively started clowning on it less and got into it after a certain point. I liked it, good movie. Even my roommates who said they were gonna go out stayed in to watch with me! Obviously very corny at times and has plenty of flaws but it came up in the end making me feel good and I fucked with the underlying sentiment of what kindness can do. Said many times prior and I'll say it again, I really get into feelgood movies in the right mood and this was certainly that mood for me; interestingly reminded me more of Train Dreams than anything else. Also was impressed with a lot of how this looks visually and how well a lot of the CGI has aged. Anyways, yeah, glad I got around to it today when I did. Strong 7 on first impression.
Tags: paramount+, paulina apartment, w/ alek, w/ peter, trains in this one

Name: sex, lies, and videotape
Review: "You've had an effect on my life." Cold, clinical, grounded Soderbergh working with a screenplay full of truths that feel like a summation of what he learned his first 26 years of life. Love it when a movie feels like it was made by someone who's lived life and had actual, fascinating human relationships. Loved the performances, all the dialogue, the Cliff Martinez score, the keys, garbage, and videotape motifs, and how the visual language is so reliant on, for lack of a sexier or less technical term, the floor plans of every room. Can't put it into clearer terms right off, but the visual language here really feels lived-in and like these characters are truly moving around in every space like humans do; there's something about that feels significant, reminded me of A Woman Under the Influence to use a comparison. Anyways, might be my new favorite of his, still taking it all in. Strong 8, felt so piercingly relatable and human that I feel like it might be higher at some point, but this feels right in the immediate aftermath.

Name: Sinners
Review:
Whole thing came together so much better for me on second watch, but damn, when Sammy started quoting the Lord’s prayer…

… yeah, that did it in for me.

I don’t blame myself for not loving it to this extent on first impression, narratively speaking it really is a risk-taker in a lot of ways and threw me off as I detailed in my first review, but I’m truly so happy I was able to catch it in theaters again a whole 10 months later. This thing fucking rocks.
Tags: amc newcity 14, me time!, best picture showcase

Name: Gugusse and the Automaton
Review: This is so goofy but so cool and I never thought I'd see a "new" Melies film on the day it dropped, how surreal indeed. Props to everyone at the Library of Congress for discovering and restoring it to its former glory!

Name: Four Daughters
Review:
Amazing documentary. Didn't even intend to watch this today specifically because of world events, it's something that I've been having to watch for a class and just happened to time it with right now.

Really one that got me thinking deeply about a ton of things that I'm still processing, so my review will just be a broad recap of the emotional experience... for as intensely as this pulls out the rug from under you in the final 25 minutes (at least as someone who had no familiarity with the subject matter beforehand, trust me when I say go in blind to this one if you still possibly can), it made complete sense given how much a sense of relative normalcy is poured into the first 80 minutes, and how this contrasts with the ways the real events affected the family.

And for what I most directly resonated with emotionally, it's a really fascinating look at how many different ways people cope, and how many different headspaces people go through in the process. As much as I fall head over heels for how this has been portrayed in fiction filmmaking, this documentary is the best portrayal of this I've seen in at least a couple months.

Name: Enter the Void
Review:
Big exhale + a full 30 seconds to just stare at the Blu-Ray menu screen and contemplate my life after this one, even on second watch.

Been close to 17 years since this world premiered, 5 years since my first watch completely fucked with and expanded my brain, and about 4 years since my Gaspar Noe deep dive, and this still feels so ahead of its time. Say what you want about him and this movie, but Gaspar really was doing it like no one else when he made this.

And yeah I still think it’s pretty fucking incredible too. Strong 9.

Name: Eephus
Review: “Is this a DELI? Am I in a MEAT MARKET? What the SHIT is happening on the field?!” Realized recently that I give a lot of movies an 8/10 lol, but I think this one deserves it on first pass. I was fully immersed in this very comfy and subtly existential autumn world, and I absolutely love how it was produced and made. I’m always really impressed by movies that are able to pull off this very specific kind of offbeat humor, I mean the performances, cinematography, and editing here feel so confident and unified (Christmas Eve in Miller’s Point also came to mind, no surprise then that Tyler Taormina was attached to both), and I always appreciate a movie that tackles over-the-top dedication to seemingly meaningless things, doing so in ways both hilarious and quietly thoughtful; applying this to an amateur baseball league was so creative and feels surprisingly universal. I’d be lying if I said I didn’t zone out a couple times or that I was ever blown away by this, but I was always enjoying what I was watching and don’t have any real complaints aside from that. Good stuff. “Where do we go, Joe DiMaggio?”
Tags: music box theater, w/ quinn k

Name: 21 Jump Street
Review: Four immediate thoughts - ‘movies where Channing Tatum plays an alter ego and gets too attached to his double life’ is becoming a great subgenre (shoutout Roofman I need to watch that again) - Dave Franco is really great here - I wonder what it was about this movie that made Kanye declare he was no longer antisemitic a few years ago. Maybe he heard Ice Cube’s jab at Jonah Hill for being really good with money and saw it as a mirror - …I actually forgot what the fourth thing was, I’m tired and gotta go to bed. But this was a lot of fun and surprisingly really well performed and written. Strong 7.

Name: Sirāt
Review: Choose your fighter: Chekhov’s sound effect edition. It Was Just an Accident leg squeak vs. Sirat handbrake Obviously having had 5 months to not stop thinking about the movie since my first watch helped this case (it hasn’t left my mind to an insane degree), but I remain baffled by how many people think this is just empty shock value. Do you need everything explained to you? Even on second watch knowing what to expect, there was SO much more crossing my mind thematically-speaking, mostly relating to some form of what we have control and power over and how people react to external circumstances, and even if I still don’t have the most well-worded analysis yet, that doesn’t change the fact that this remains a great and effective movie for me from front to back. I truly take no issue with how vague the screenplay is in certain aspects; I think it all works perfectly in line with who these characters are, their development in passing, and the fact that these are strangers to Luis and Esteban. I will admit, it does prevent the movie from having an even stronger emotional hook with the characters here, but I think the fact that the story hits on such specific ideas of people who live in their own bubble in such a fascinating way does most, if not all the heavy lifting here. So I digress for the most part, we’ll see how that ages after this watch. And just the fact that it still got me thinking as much as it did on second watch just with what little is said and what is shown is really something to behold; I absolutely love when a movie gives you just enough to keep you thinking without losing sight of its badass, unforgettable, and emotionally charged atmosphere and the physical reactions it gives you. I already love any movie that has such a heavy focus on the elements and sheer human survival and, such as with cases like this, the visceral physical reaction I get watching this speaks just as much, if not more than any other element to the themes of the movie! It’s a fucking fantastic blend of both aspects!! One little thing I will say that I’m still thinking about… those final few minutes are so weird. After such an unbelievable visceral experience for nearly 2 hours straight, the movie ends on a very solemn note and seems to skip over a pretty major emotional beat! If you know you know, but it’s like they don’t even care anymore and… well, maybe that’s the point, and ultimately I do think it works, although it’d be interesting to see what one last visceral note to end things off here might’ve looked like. Not even a critique, was just not the reaction I expected and I’m still not sure how to feel yet. If nothing else, I’m fascinated by the fact that this was able to pull off such a calm final note the way it did, really cool effect and I can’t think of any close comparisons to that. Still, as my roommate said, I feel the opposite way about Bugonia (I hate it) when most people would write something like what I did above for it. After a certain point it’s pointless to say anything more than “to each their own.” But my Sirat opinion is my own, I love this movie.  In all honesty, still not sure if this is an 8 or 9 but just the fact that I’m still able to write as much as I just did after endlessly thinking about it ever since my first watch in October, I think this is something special and really just one for me. So it gets the bump. “Before my dad died, he said… ‘Fuck, this is serious.’”
Tags: music box theater, 35mm, me time!, trains in this one

Name: Opening Night
Review: This might not make sense, especially when I say I mean this all complementarily (is that a word?), but watching a Cassavetes film is like being constipated, except the constipation is the discomfort of feeling painfully seen in a character that represents something you’re embarrassed about. But this was a great movie; as with any Cassavetes, it was an emotional constipation worth it for the more wonderful and cathartic moments, and just how innately human and unforgettable the story ends up being; Gena Rowlands and the cinematography, oh my god. Really loved this overall, strong 8 with potential to move up. “I seem to have lost the reality of, the… reality!”
Tags: criterion blu-ray, paulina apartment, w/ alek

Name: The Bride!
Review:
A lot of things happen in this movie


Some notes
- every time the characters changed location, the Bride’s cheek splatter took on a different shape. At first it was just distracting, but in retrospect it kinda contributes to the overall clustered feel to the whole thing.
- I did honestly want to like this more, and I liked a good amount of the first half actually but after it peaks with the dance number, it progressively just hits a mediocre platitude of repeating the same major beats and ideas on repeat and never fully recovered from that. By the end I sadly did not really care and I can confirm that this really isn’t sure what it is or what tone to take at most given moments. The sheer audacity did a lot of heavy lifting.
- ending on a more positive note, great costumes and makeup here. Also not a bad ensemble all things considered (despite some being obnoxiously wasted, justice for Penelope Cruz’s vapid character). Glad Matthew Maher is continuing to get work.

Name: Song Sung Blue
Review: First half of this is frustratingly just a well-acted, competently made Wikipedia biopic, moving from one event to the next in a didactic structure without emotional oomph, but the last 45 minutes were much better and made the journey more worth it than not. Overall a nice movie, I didn’t dislike it despite having a lot more potential on the way. I don’t think Kate Hudson was Oscar nomination worthy here but she is still good, as are Hugh Jackman and their kids. Could maybe be a 7 in all honesty, call it a guilty pleasure.

Name: The Secret Agent
Review: What a fucking treat to finish out both a great Chicago Friday and also my Oscar watching for this year with a rewatch of this in theaters. It’s the Best Picture winner in my heart <3 Really surprised but equally really excited to say I got something completely different out of the ending this time. As the final few scenes went by, it was actually very… bittersweet? I was expecting to cry at the final reveal after choking up the first time, but I was overcome with an uncontrollable sense of comfort instead. It’s hard to discuss without spoiling the story, but the sentiment of Fernando having finally received a hard drive to recover his memories was just so wonderful, despite all the circumstances that led to it. The way Kleber was able to tie in such an emotionally rich and dense throughline on family scars, the processing of real life fears through art and community, the connection to the locations that formed us in childhood, our lack of control over memory, and the fear of being forgotten/lost to history into a nearly 3 hour crime saga (that just completely flew by on this rewatch) that hits so many different chords for me is truly one of THE film achievements of the year, and the fact that it was able to hit two drastically different reactions for me between both watches is just icing on the cake. This blend of inventiveness and emotional resonance is what film is all about, this is why The Secret Agent is a masterpiece, this is why The Secret Agent is likely going to end up being an all-time favorite for me.
Tags: amc river east 21, w/ ed, w/ alex b, best picture showcase

Name: Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me
Review:
That was really fucking affecting.

Too stunned to have any real analysis off the bat, but there’s one thing in particular that stood out to me from the first moment Laura walked on screen. I’m so used to seeing depressed high schoolers portrayed solely as aggressive and with their defenses up at all time, which isn’t to say Laura doesn’t have those qualities… but this movie taps into how SCARED you are of everything when you’re severely depressed, more accurately than maybe anything else I can think of.

That’s about the extent of how I relate to Laura’s character here, and I admittedly have my minor caveats here and there with the movie, but that one set of emotions within her was so overwhelming and strong that I knew I was watching a great movie. And it was enough of an emotional in-point, in tandem with all the horrifying stuff that happens, to really mess me up while watching.

Anyways, Ray Wise is giving a Q&A so I’m wrapping it up here.

Name: Hoppers
Review: I don’t think it’s hyperbole to call this For the Birds feature-length adaptation (not actually, they just cameo) Pixar’s most creative idea since Soul, and I’m happy to report it’s also likely their best since Soul! Mabel is a truly great lead, and I love how they were able to write her as equally complex and digestible, I really appreciate that Pixar created a movie about young activism and even if it came from the Disney corporate machine, it’s nice to see a kids movie treating the subject well and encouraging kids to fight for their futures. She even kinda reminded me of my 19-year-old self in the movie’s best moments. And for the most part it never feels too overly preachy or like it’s seriously compromising the complexity of the main characters, backed up by plotting that’s actually really solid; it’s definitely a regular 3-act structure with some obvious beats, but when you have a story and characters that are this interesting and this mature for the most part, it’s really easy to not care as much. That being said, it does have the modern Pixar achilles heel in that it’s too over-reliant on gags that just take you out if you aren’t an easily excited kid, and the final few scenes are a little too everyone-gets-along-in-the-end utopian, even for a kids movie since the character’s layers all made the story what it was, and the ‘happy ending’ of it all felt a little too forced. It’s nice and all to think there’s a future where kids won’t cuss out movies like this for misleading them into believing in a better world when they grow up, but realistically this just won’t happen. Overall though, I really did like this. It might not have the lasting maturity or resonance of the Golden Age Pixar I grew up with, especially as a 22 year old watching this for the first time, but I’d be lying if I said I wasn’t genuinely invested and entertained. Strong 7!

Name: Rebuilding
Review: Really nice movie, another good entry into the canons of 2025 American indie movies about family connection (Father Mother Sister Brother, Adult Children) and living in nature (Train Dreams). The ensemble here is really good and the cinematography is quite spectacular. Narratively it was a little too simple to get a bigger reaction out of me and I wish the ending was able to match the stronger emotional promise of the best individual scenes along the way, but it's just so likeable front to back that it's still a strong 7 for me, not far off an 8. See you down the road.

Name: The Fugitive Kind
Review:
Not 100% sure what to make of this right off. On one hand, it never really felt like it took off and stayed that way for a long period of time (like a bird without wings), but at the same time I was always really enraptured by and transported to the world of this movie. Definitely a good one overall, lots to think about here and I really was interested in all that was happening despite how loose the plot is.

Been reading Sidney Lumet's Making Movies book recently, both for fun and because I'm deep in pre-production on my next film, so I put this on figuring it was high time I finally watch one of the movies he talks about. Really interesting to watch this after reading his chapter on actors; he mentions that the most rewarding part of directing actors is when they finally feel confident in themselves and how they express themselves, and the whole arc of this movie starts with the main characters meeting each other and progresses into them forming deep, expressive relationships to each other and their county. A cool connection.

Gonna let it sit before rating.
Tags: amazon prime, paulina apartment, w/ peter

Name: The Railfan
Review: IT'S ON YOUTUBE!! In November 2024, on my last day of that quarter, I was in a class when inspiration randomly struck me. My professor was lecturing us, and completely out of the blue, a ton of threads just came together in my head and I had an idea for a short film about two guys duking it out at a train station. After Temporary, I knew I wanted to make something more light-hearted and fun, and this was that. I wrote a one-page treatment, shared it with some friends (who would go on to work on this film), and we agreed that it was our next project. Over the course of just a month, we created everything we needed and organized the whole shoot together. Despite the blistering cold temperatures, all our planning paid off as we had a hell of a lot of fun together making this and feeling equally confident in the process. And despite the low stakes of it all, how it was more or less an excuse for all of us to hang out together while also making a short film in the process, I really look back on that day as being one of my favorite filmmaking days of my life thus far. Through the editing process, I realized that, even though this was essentially just a film made via my friends and I fucking around together, this would end up being the project that I would feel the most confident in my own direction of. Once all the pieces came together in post, I realized that I was really proud of what we had made and that there's still a ton of my DNA and serious effort put in this short film that I made with friends over the course of just 8 hours. With time, since actually finishing the film, it's continued to linger all the same. The score is constantly stuck in my head, I look back at certain scenes and continue to feel proud of how I executed them and how my friends executed their roles, and I truly feel like this is my biggest accomplishment to date, despite the low stakes of it all. This is my biggest progression as both a cinematic storyteller and filmmaker, and it still feels incredibly personal and like it could only come from my own mind. I can't wait to keep going from here. I hope you all enjoy :)

Name: Project Hail Mary
Review: Some great individual ideas here that are executed well and visually it truly is a triumph, but when SO much of your main character’s personality is just shitty Marvel humor to undercut serious moments and at the expense of better storytelling then yes, it pains me to say that your movie suffers greatly. The stuff on earth at the start was the most interesting part of this but I think that might’ve set a wrong expectation for me. Still thought it was decent overall just because of the overarching threads that kept me more interested than not, but it’s truly frustrating just how much the humor here and the insufferable commitment to it keeps the great elements of it down; not everything is funny nor does everything need comic relief. Not bad but disappointing.
Tags: music box theater, 70mm, w/ dad, w/ mom

Name: Reprise
Review:
Almost great, a strong 7, it just lost me a little too much on the way and in the final couple scenes (but I could see it growing on me).

Overall this is one of the best case scenarios for a messy directorial debut, influences worn on its sleeves and tons of individual moments that contribute to an emotionally investing story more than not. It just had room to go further and be more concise in what it strived to achieve, but the personality, specificity, and deep-cutting emotional honesty are here and made this thoroughly watchable. Was really interested in what this ends up saying about personal ambitions vs sharing your life with others and what it means to be connected to others as an aspiring artist, feels very much like Joachim Trier reflecting on a past version of himself and friends he had surrounded himself during this era of his life.
Tags: criterion channel, paulina apartment, w/ peter

Name: Man on Wire
Review:
Turned this on while at work thinking it was something I'd enjoy but be fine with pausing when I had to help guests. Ended up being an extremely beautiful, life-affirming, perfectly edited documentary that is hilarious, fascinating, heart-wrenching, heartwarming, heart-pounding, and somehow ultimately and wonderfully emblematic of exactly where I am in life right now (chasing your ambitions being your main lifeforce and the community involved in that with you), even despite some of Phillipe's character flaws… suffice to say, I instead got so invested that I felt myself PHYSICALLY being pulled into hitting play again as soon as I could every time I had to pause. Really wish I saved it for a time when it would be uninterrupted but oh well.

Maybe a reactionary rating, but it’s no lower than a 9 because THIS is what falling completely in love with a movie feels like for me. I fully feel that every emotional beat of this movie hit the proper mark, the creativity in the filmmaking is all so fitting and inspiring, and the overarching human thread of it all is translated with no false note. I absolutely loved it, an instant all-time favorite documentary.
Tags: hoopla, bored at work, cried or teared up

Name: The Devils
Review:
Yeah this kinda really fucking rocked lol. I don’t know if I fully grasped enough of it to give it a 9 but I’m also feeling inclined to give it the rating edge just on how impressed and taken by it as I was; honestly just a matter of how reactionary I want to be.

This movie is so ALIVE!! Really badass production design and effects, and anything that’s eviscerating the veil of religion like this is always fascinating and invigorating to me, both given my upbringing and also just who I am and what I’m drawn to in art. Incredible turn from Vanessa Redgrave here. Love it when I watch a movie and fully understand why a director has dedicated years of his life to a specific vision. Could totally see this becoming a favorite in the future.
Tags: at a friend's place, w/ jake p, w/ peter, w/ alek

Name: Raw
Review:
That scene… the waxing followed by the moment of realization… still fucking gets to me.

I’m deep in pre-production on my next film, a body horror short of my own, and looking back this was probably the first body horror film that I truly found a strong personal connection with and overall the first one I truly fell in love with. I owe a tremendous debt of gratitude to Julia Ducournau.

I was a senior in high school when I first watched this, now I’m a senior in college… glad to say my college experience was a little better overall. Even if some of the surprise factor was obviously gone, and I’ve also just become far more well-versed in body horror since then, I still got a lot of different and new things out of this on rewatch that I really appreciate. And even then, so many scenes of this still manage to get SO far under my skin, and the overarching push-and-pull of embracing a new you while still being terrified of breaking out of your shell continues to seriously hit the spot; so many coming-of-age tropes here are completely subverted to fit the most grotesque images and emotions, but the coming-of-age of it all never fails to keep shining through. Not even to mention how great Garance Marillier, the original score, the SFX, and editing of this all are.

Great movie.

Name: Ready or Not
Review: Samara Weaving is great, I was on board with the story at times, and it's competently made with good cinematography, but tonally it never stuck the landing in either direction for me and I'm left kinda just like "okay whatever." Its interesting commentary on the family dynamics here ended up just simply being brought up in passing, I never really found it THAT funny in either its humor or how ridiculous some of the kills are, and the final couple of scenes left an underwhelming taste in my mouth overall. At least the characters and dynamics on a surface level are believable enough for this to be a decent ride with some fun deaths, when they landed well.

Name: Ready or Not 2: Here I Come
Review: More or less thought this was equal quality to the first, maybe didn't develop as interesting of a personal story but I thought the larger overarching stakes helped and I felt less disappointed by this one, so I think I had more fun as a result despite its narrative flaws. Disappointingly little David Cronenberg screentime, both in general and for how high he was billed… I don’t even know if she’s an actress but Julia Ducournau should’ve been cast as his daughter.

Name: Titane
Review:
As fucking sublime and electrifying and weird as this film is, it also completely reminded me on this rewatch of how surprisingly heartwarming and cathartic it is to find exactly the right kind of love you've been looking for that you didn't even know you needed… I think I needed that today.

Took me over 4 years to finally watch again and realize how incredible of a balancing act this is, but seriously, I'm so glad that I can fully say I click with this now. Rating could lowkey go up in the future.
Tags: blu-ray rental, library, paulina apartment, cried or teared up

Name: Alpha
Review: Julia Ducournau’s movies remind me that, if nothing else, I’ll die happy as long as someone else loves me enough to stay by my side as it happens. The weakest Ducournau film thus far, but even her weakest is really good. The central family dynamic is truly strong and emotionally resonant throughout, and Alpha is a truly well-written and performed teenage lead; I welled up and felt a lot of real emotions for both her and her family numerous times, in spite of this being the most straightforward and accessible thing she’s made; still emotionally effective for sure, although it lacks the more fascinating revelations of Raw and Titane. Not every directorial and editing choice worked for me on the way either, in particular some of the time jumps took me out more than I would’ve liked, although in the same breath there are a lot of remarkable images too. The body horror metaphor and worldbuilding definitely struck me as weird at first, but I ultimately adjusted as I became more attached to the story and saw the vision more than not. Oh, also Ducournau and her music supervisors continue to have incredible music taste. Could be an 8 as I sit on it, but a strong 7 feels right for now.

Name: Wandering Ginza Butterfly
Review: Wooooah okay that was pretty awesome. Meiko Kaji is really good here and her songs are fucking perfect as expected. Surprisingly funny and not much of an action movie until the end, mostly a light noir and just Nami exploiting the crooked system of the Ginza crime network to make amends and help out other people which was pretty sick and surprisingly entertaining and just really likeable, even if it's a little too light on story at times. Great billiards scene in this too. Have been feeling indecisive on ratings and even considered just taking a break from rating things for a while because my brain has a tendency to stress out over it so I won't but I'm feeling 4 stars on this right now.

Name: Oldboy
Review:
Fifth watch (third time in theaters) and I’m still picking up on several new details and connections, and I just got something entirely different out of it even though it’s only been 3 years since my last two watches; it’s so insanely rewarding on multiple viewings. And the fact that I jumped at the chance to see it again in theaters just on instinct, despite how repugnant it ends up being in the end, says a lot about how addicting this movie is on a sheer emotional and physical level; few other movies have been able to match this power. Also helps to have top 10 all-time cinematography, lead performances, and original music.

Some other notes I have:
- despite all the insane camerawork on display, my favorite shot in the entire film remains the close up on Lee Woo-jin’s face, mid-stretch, when a single tear rolls down his cheek.
- the cut from seeing Lee Woo-jin’s cross tattoo just above his ass to Mido praying is so unhinged but in the most fitting way possible.
- earlier today I was auditioning an actress for my next film and we were reading a scene that’s just two characters gossiping and laughing about people they used to know. Following that up with this movie and all its musings on the lasting impact that gossiping can cause gave me some fun ideas for a potential sequel.

Name: Bound
Review: To all involved, from the characters to the actors to the Wachowskis and every crew member… well played!

Name: Wandering Ginza Butterfly: She-Cat Gambler
Review: A fun time at the movies still, though a definite weaker sequel to the original. It's funnier in sections, but sometimes at the expense of stronger story. The overarching Nami arc is interesting enough, but for a film still called Wandering Ginza Butterfly, there's surprisingly long sections here without any Nami and heavier focus on the less interesting supporting characters. It all comes together in the end, and that final showdown was truly aces in a theater, although the journey to get there is a bit lacking. Still a decent movie though, slightly generous 7. Saw with a Meiko Kaji Q&A after!
Tags: music box theater, me time!, q&a!, beyond fest chicago

Name: Speed Racer
Review:
Was hitting a very consistent platitude of ‘liking it’ for the most part, but that final act… was CINEMA and finally pushed me into loving it.

Watched with a Lilly Wachowski Q&A(!!) preceding this, the world premiere of the 4K restoration(!!) with a sold out crowd(!!), and when she mentioned that this was a movie for ADHD kids and that Michael Giacchino did the score, I was like… hm okay this might be extremely Josh-core… and I’m so happy to say it ended up being that, regardless of how unbelievably perfect the conditions for my FIRST WATCH were. What a delirious and wonderful time at the movies.

Rating it 4 stars on first impression both just because it just feels right and also I feel like there’s still greater meaning and emotional resonance to come from this that I haven’t unlocked yet. Gotta let it sit but let it be known that I loved it.
Tags: music box theater, q&a!, beyond fest chicago, w/ alek, w/ jake p, w/ josh m, w/ andrew, w/ tabitha, w/ patrick

Name: Faces of Death
Review:
Bought this on Blu-Ray yesterday because it was a fun thing to do at Beyond Fest and I’m seeing the new one tomorrow, but having now seen the movie I’ll probably sell because I don’t need to ever watch it again.

The idea of a pathologist narrating and speculating over a movie about various kinds of death has real potential on paper, and at times there was some actual thought being provoked complete with obviously great SFX… but in the same vein this movie is quite literally just nonstop horrific imagery and I’m not sure yet if there’s enough substance to justify all the negative emotions this brings up. Had to purposefully distract myself for my mental health’s sake at a couple points just because it was so excessive… not sure what to make of this yet as a whole but I didn’t like it either so I’ll keep the rating in the middle on first pass.

Would be interesting in learning more about how the film was received in 1978 and getting a glimpse at what cultural mindsets surrounding the film’s themes looked like during this time.
Tags: blu-ray, paulina apartment, w/ jake p

Name: Imposters
Review: I went in blind and I think this should be experienced that way, so I’ll just say that this is the most a film has rattled me in a hot minute, and with what I’ve been watching recently that’s actually saying something. Skin-peeling psychological torture at times with some truly unpredictable plotting that sometimes feels like it’s too close to bursting at the seams, but I felt it ultimately came out strong, helped by having so much emotional honesty to it. You can never predict how a horror film will play with anyone else, but I definitely just discovered one for me. if everyone hates this when it eventually comes out and tells me I’m stupid for loving it or whatever, I’ll sleep well at night knowing that I truly got a kick out of this (and that you’d all be wrong). My top 5 films of the year so far are now 4 horror movies and also Hoppers.
Tags: music box theater, me time!, q&a!, beyond fest chicago

Name: Faces of Death
Review:
There are truly interesting things being said about internet culture and the desensitization to graphic content here that might not be the most profound in the world but work really effectively in the context of this story, and the main characters are watchable and entertaining enough, combined with effectively gruesome violence, to make this a good, well-crafted horror movie.

It’s just held back by the fact that it's too reliant on typical horror conventions when it comes to stupid character decisions, plotting that feels overly manufactured for specific things to occur, and over-explanatory dialogue in some of its most crucial dramatic moments, and the meta aspect was cool although there were strides where it got too on the nose. But despite all that, the threads come together and I still had a lot of fun with what this ended up being and left more satisfied than not thanks to how singular this specific approach to internet desensitization is. Strong 7.

Extremely cool to be at the world premiere with Daniel and Isa in attendance. A really fantastic experience to close out Chicago’s inaugural Beyond Fest.
